file: Nayak et al. - 2023 - Learning to compose soft prompts for compositional zero-shot learning.md
title: Learning to Compose Soft Prompts for Compositional Zero-Shot Learning (CSP)
one_line: CSP is a parameter-efficient soft-prompting method that turns the attributes and objects defining classes into learnable vocabulary tokens, tuning them on seen compositions and recomposing them at test time so a CLIP-style VLM can recognize unseen attribute-object combinations.

ideas.idea: Treat primitive concepts (attribute, object) as independently learnable embedding tokens that are recomposed into novel class prompts at inference, rather than learning one monolithic prompt per class.
ideas.rationale: The compositional insight maps loosely onto Memoria's typed-relation/aspect tagging: a paper-aspect or tag classifier could represent labels as composable primitives (e.g. method x domain) so unseen combinations are scorable. But this is vision-CLIP machinery requiring gradient training of embeddings, which Memoria's MCP-only, deterministic-ingest, local-model pipeline does not run; only the conceptual framing is transferable.

ideas.idea: Feasibility calibration: filter out infeasible compositions (e.g. 'eroded cat') using cheap word-embedding similarity before scoring the full combinatorial label space.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and tag-candidate generation face the same combinatorial-explosion problem when proposing relations/tags; a cheap embedding-feasibility prefilter to prune nonsensical (concept, concept) pairs before any LLM scoring fits the 'engines filter, agents judge' cost discipline.

ideas.idea: Parameter-efficient soft prompting / prefix tuning (CoOp) to specialize a frozen large model without full fine-tuning.
ideas.rationale: Memoria mandates frozen local open models (qwen2.5:7b) with profiles compiled as prompts, no gradient training in the agent sandbox; learned continuous embeddings are not deployable or governable here.

ideas.idea: Generalized zero-shot evaluation with a tunable seen/unseen bias scalar swept to trace a seen-vs-unseen accuracy curve (AUC).
ideas.rationale: This is a vision-classification benchmarking protocol with no analog in Memoria's single-researcher, faithfulness-over-flash retrieval/triage tasks.

file: Neekhra et al. - 2026 - RAGScholar & DBLP-QA explainable retrieval augmented scientific QA on dblp with source-attributed a.md
title: RAGScholar & DBLP-QA: Explainable Retrieval-Augmented Scientific QA on dblp with Source-Attributed Answers
one_line: A scholarly RAG system over dblp/Semantic-Scholar abstracts that pairs a Lucene BM25 retriever with mid-sized instruction-tuned LLMs, plus a 50-item manually-curated DBLP-QA benchmark, systematically comparing context-construction strategies.

ideas.idea: BM25/Lucene lexical retrieval is highly effective for scholarly QA (Recall@1 0.88, perfect Recall@3, MRR@3 0.93) over abstracts, with answer quality driven more by retrieval+evidence packaging than by model size.
ideas.rationale: Directly validates Memoria's local-and-cheap stance: a deterministic BM25 index over the Zotero/abstract backbone is a strong, transparent, source-attributable retriever for the PI's corpus, reducing reliance on heavyweight dense models and matching the faithfulness-over-flash constraint.

ideas.idea: Context-construction is the central design lever: concatenating top-3 abstracts helps, but top-5 often adds noise that smaller models cannot exploit (a coverage-vs-noise trade-off).
ideas.rationale: Concrete, model-size-aware guidance for Memoria's retriever/scout profile and discovery loop: cap aggregated evidence at ~3 documents when using qwen2.5:7b-class local models rather than naively stuffing more context, since the default model sits in exactly their 'sensitive to redundancy' regime.

ideas.idea: Two-step answer-concatenation (answer each doc separately, then fuse answers) does NOT beat concatenating raw abstracts before generation.
ideas.rationale: A useful negative result: it tells Memoria not to over-engineer multi-pass per-document summarize-then-fuse pipelines for QA-style synthesis; direct aggregated-evidence conditioning is simpler and stronger, saving local-compute. Borrow because it is corpus-specific (abstracts only, 50 items) and may not hold for full-text claim synthesis.

ideas.idea: Source-attributed answers: always return the generated answer together with the supporting source documents (key/DOI/title) so claims are verifiable.
ideas.rationale: Aligns with Memoria's atomic-claim provenance, citekey backbone, and faithfulness requirement; every synthesized answer or claim should carry its supporting citekeys, and this paper confirms the UI pattern of presenting answer+sources together.

ideas.idea: Lexical retrievers are misled by high-frequency surface keyword matches (e.g. 'CtRL-Sim' vs 'CtRL-C'), a known BM25 failure mode addressable via dense/hybrid (DPR/ColBERT) + RRF fusion + MonoT5 reranking + MMR diversification.
ideas.rationale: Flags a concrete failure to guard against in Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and discovery scoring; a lightweight hybrid+rerank stage is worth a deferred-ADR analysis, but full dense retrieval/ColBERT indexing is heavier than the single-researcher scale strictly needs, so borrow rather than adopt wholesale.

ideas.idea: A small, manually-curated benchmark (50 QA pairs grounded in abstracts, each tied to a dblp key) plus a 3-point manual rubric (2 correct+complete / 1 correct+incomplete / 0 wrong), with ROUGE-L/BERTScore as cheap proxies that correlate weakly with human judgment.
ideas.rationale: Memoria can build an analogous tiny gold-set tied to citekeys to regression-test retrieval/claim-extraction quality, fitting the L5 quality-test tier; but the paper's own caution that ROUGE/BERTScore only weakly track human judgment means Memoria should keep human/agent-judged scoring, not trust automatic metrics.

file: Neupane et al. - 2025 - Towards a HIPAA compliant agentic AI system in healthcare.md
title: Towards a HIPAA Compliant Agentic AI System in Healthcare
one_line: A work-in-progress agentic framework that enforces HIPAA on clinical LLM workflows via Attribute-Based Access Control, a hybrid regex+BERT PHI sanitization pipeline (dual pre/post-inference redaction), and immutable cryptographically-hashed audit trails.

ideas.idea: Immutable, cryptographically-hashed audit trails logging every access decision (allow/deny), policy match, and both raw and sanitized model outputs for retrospective verification.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's structural review gate ('engines write, agents judge, PI approves') already wants a tamper-evident record of what each agent proposed and why a gate allowed/denied a note-write; a decision-log + interaction-log pattern (raw engine output kept alongside the judged/approved version) is a clean way to make the gate auditable, though Memoria needs no cryptographic ledger at single-PI scale.

ideas.idea: Hybrid regex + fine-tuned BERT entity pipeline for detecting structured vs unstructured sensitive spans (deterministic regex for formatted identifiers, model for contextual entities), reporting 99.4% precision / 97.6% recall combined.
ideas.rationale: The deterministic-regex-plus-model split mirrors Memoria's deterministic-ingest-plus-judgment philosophy and is directly reusable for entity extraction / record-linkage dedup (catch citekey/DOI/author-id patterns with regex, contextual entities with the local model). The PHI/clinical purpose itself is off-domain.

ideas.idea: Cumulative session risk scoring (sum of sensitivity x access count) with per-role thresholds that terminate a session before incremental over-disclosure.
ideas.rationale: This guards multi-user, role-segmented PHI access against incremental leakage; Memoria is single-PI with no PHI and no need to gate the owner's own access. The MCP-only sandbox already hard-denies the dangerous capabilities (file/terminal/code-exec) at the boundary, so per-session risk accumulation adds no value.

ideas.idea: Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC) with XACML-like policies over subject/resource/action/environment attributes, replacing static RBAC.
ideas.rationale: ABAC solves multi-user, role-and-clearance enterprise authorization; Memoria's single-researcher model has one principal and instead governs by capability sandboxing and profile compilation, not per-attribute access policies. Adopting ABAC would contradict the single-user constraint.

ideas.idea: Dual-stage (pre- and post-inference) redaction wrapping the LLM call.
ideas.rationale: Redaction of PHI before/after inference has no analog in a personal research vault where the PI owns all content; nothing needs masking from the user.

file: Newell - 1982 - The knowledge level.md
title: The Knowledge Level
one_line: Newell's foundational AAAI address proposing a distinct computer-system level above the symbol level — the knowledge level — where an agent is described purely by its knowledge, goals, and the principle of rationality (it takes actions its knowledge says will attain its goals), independent of representation.

ideas.idea: The knowledge level as an abstraction separate from the symbol/representation level: characterize a system by WHAT it knows and its goals, decoupled from HOW that knowledge is encoded.
ideas.rationale: This is the conceptual spine for separating Memoria's content layer (atomic claims, typed relations, argument graph = the PI's knowledge) from its symbol/storage layer (markdown, vault files, indices). Designing the knowledge model independently of the Obsidian markdown encoding (which is just one projection) is exactly Memoria's disposable-projection-engine principle, and gives a principled justification for it.

ideas.idea: Principle of rationality: an agent selects actions that, according to its knowledge, will achieve its goals; behavior is predictable from knowledge+goals without simulating the mechanism.
ideas.rationale: Useful framing for Memoria's autonomous loops (nightly discovery, triage, scout profile): define each agent's behavior by the knowledge it has access to and the goal it pursues, and judge it at the knowledge level (did it act on what the vault knows toward the PI's goal) rather than only inspecting prompt mechanics. It motivates the 'agents judge' altitude in the review gate.

ideas.idea: Knowledge is defined as the competence-level closure of what an agent can be ascribed (radical incompleteness: knowledge is not a finite stored structure but everything derivable for goal-attainment).
ideas.rationale: Cautions Memoria against treating the vault's stored claims as the totality of 'what is known' — entailments, contradictions, and supersessions are part of the PI's knowledge even when not yet materialized as notes. Justifies the contradictions dashboard and claim-supersession tracking as surfacing latent knowledge-level facts, while keeping it a borrow since the full closure is not computable at the symbol level.

file: Nye et al. - 2021 - Show your work scratchpads for intermediate computation with language models.md
title: Show Your Work: Scratchpads for Intermediate Computation with Language Models
one_line: Shows that letting a Transformer emit explicit intermediate computation steps into a 'scratchpad' before the final answer dramatically improves multi-step tasks (long addition, polynomial evaluation, Python execution tracing), giving the model adaptive compute and an externalized, inspectable working state.

ideas.idea: Emit explicit intermediate reasoning/computation steps (a scratchpad) before the final answer instead of forcing a single forward pass; this externalizes state and gives adaptive computation time.
ideas.rationale: The proto-chain-of-thought insight applies to Memoria's local models when doing claim extraction, claim-sentence classification, or contradiction judgment: prompting qwen2.5:7b to lay out evidence/intermediate steps before emitting the structured verdict should improve faithfulness on multi-step judgments. Borrow (not adopt) because Memoria needs the FINAL structured output validated at the gate, and the scratchpad is a prompting tactic, not new infrastructure; the paper's fine-tuning results don't transfer to frozen local models.

ideas.idea: Inspecting the scratchpad output lets you diagnose and correct common errors by revising the intermediate-step format.
ideas.rationale: Supports making engine/agent reasoning traces inspectable in Memoria's read-only inspector and audit log, so the PI can see WHY a claim or relation was proposed before approving it at the gate — improving the judgeability the review-gate depends on.

ideas.idea: Self-augmentation: use the model itself to generate candidate programs, then execute them to create additional, correctly-labeled training traces (MBPP-aug), which helps even when some generated programs are wrong.
ideas.rationale: This is a fine-tuning data-augmentation loop requiring code execution and gradient training; Memoria's MCP-only sandbox forbids code execution for agents and uses frozen local models, so this pipeline is non-deployable.

ideas.idea: Quantizing intermediate state to emitted tokens reduces compounding error versus carrying state in recurrent activations.
ideas.rationale: An architectural/training observation about RNN-vs-scratchpad numeric error propagation with no actionable mapping to Memoria's retrieval/extraction/governance tasks.

file: Liu et al. - 2022 - What makes good In-context examples for GPT-3.md
title: What Makes Good In-Context Examples for GPT-3?
one_line: Introduces KATE, a kNN retrieval over sentence embeddings to select semantically-similar in-context examples for an LLM, which sharply reduces prompt-choice variance and improves both classification and generation, and shows that encoders fine-tuned on a task-similar dataset retrieve better examples.

ideas.idea: Retrieve in-context examples by kNN over a sentence-encoder embedding space (cosine/Euclidean) instead of random sampling, ordered by similarity.
ideas.rationale: Directly applicable to Memoria's local extraction LLM (qwen2.5:7b). For atomic-claim extraction, claim-sentence classification, and MASSW aspect tagging, a deterministic kNN retriever over already-computed note/claim embeddings can pull task-matched few-shot exemplars from prior approved notes, raising quality without fine-tuning — fits the local + cheap + deterministic-ingest constraints.

ideas.idea: Encoders fine-tuned on a task-SIMILAR dataset retrieve more useful exemplars; fine-tuning on a dissimilar task HURTS (KATEsst-2 beat KATEnli for sentiment).
ideas.rationale: Insight, not a build: Memoria should match its exemplar-retrieval embedding to the extraction task (e.g. claim-similarity encoder for claim work) rather than a single generic encoder for everything; informs the retriever/scout profile and pre-file similarity gate's embedding choice.

ideas.idea: Retrieved relevant exemplars reduce hallucination (case study: random examples made GPT-3 invent 'senior year'/'University of Texas' absent from the table).
ideas.rationale: Memoria prizes faithfulness over flash and flags uncertainty; grounding extraction prompts in similar real exemplars is a low-cost faithfulness lever for the engines before the structural review gate.

ideas.idea: Example ORDER has only minor, data-dependent effect on KATE results.
ideas.rationale: Tells Memoria not to over-engineer exemplar ordering in prompt assembly — keep it deterministic (most-similar-first) and spend effort elsewhere.

ideas.idea: Larger retrieval pool improves KATE while random stays flat; more examples help but gains saturate (k as low as 5 already beats random).
ideas.rationale: Justifies keeping a growing vault of approved notes as an exemplar store and using a small k to stay within local context/token budgets.

ideas.idea: kNN-only baseline (predict via neighbor's label/majority vote) performs near-random; the LLM is essential and complementary to retrieval.
ideas.rationale: A negative result confirming retrieval alone is not a classifier here; Memoria's record-linkage dedup already uses similarity scoring, so nothing new to build from this.

file: Liu et al. - 2023 - GPT understands, too.md
title: GPT Understands, Too (P-Tuning)
one_line: Proposes P-Tuning, trainable continuous prompt embeddings concatenated with discrete prompts (with an LSTM/MLP prompt encoder) that stabilize and improve LLM adaptation on NLU/knowledge-probing tasks under frozen or tuned models.

ideas.idea: Train continuous (soft) prompt embeddings to replace/augment hand-written discrete prompts, optimized by backprop.
ideas.rationale: Requires gradient access and a training loop over the model's embedding layer; Memoria uses off-the-shelf local models with no per-task fine-tuning and a deterministic ingest. Soft-prompt training is operationally out of scope for a single-researcher local setup.

ideas.idea: Manual discrete prompts are highly unstable — changing one word can drop accuracy ~20 points; prompt choice is a real variance source.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces that Memoria's extraction prompts must be version-pinned and regression-tested (profiles compiled), since prompt wording materially shifts engine output before the review gate.

ideas.idea: Prompt-encoder (LSTM/MLP) to model dependencies between continuous prompt tokens; placement should not segment sentences.
ideas.rationale: Architectural detail of soft-prompt training, irrelevant to a no-fine-tuning, MCP-only-agent, local-model design.

file: Liu et al. - 2025 - SciLinker a large-scale text mining framework for mapping associations among biological entities.md
title: SciLinker: a large-scale text mining framework for mapping associations among biological entities
one_line: A modular deterministic NLP pipeline (NER -> normalization to UMLS -> co-occurrence scoring with statistical significance -> fine-tuned PubMedBERT relation typing) that mines PubMed abstracts into a weighted, significance-filtered entity-association network.

ideas.idea: Background-corrected co-occurrence association score (STRING-style: corrects raw counts by each entity's overall frequency, weighting factor a=0.6) so heavily-studied entities don't dominate.
ideas.rationale: Directly buildable for Memoria's typed-relation/argument graph: when surfacing 'which claims/notes co-occur,' raw counts over-rank popular topics. A frequency-corrected score gives better relation-edge weights and discovery relevance scoring at single-researcher scale — purely deterministic, no model needed.

ideas.idea: Hypergeometric test + Benjamini-Hochberg FDR correction to assign a p-value/significance to each co-occurrence association.
ideas.rationale: Memoria can attach a statistical-significance flag to inferred relations and use an adjusted-p threshold to gate which edges are written, complementing the structural review gate ('engines compute significance, agents judge'). Cheap, deterministic, and matches the uncertainty-flagging ethos.

ideas.idea: Significance-filtered, score-weighted network construction: drop edges below an adjusted-p threshold, weight survivors by association score, surfacing high-score/low-count novel links.
ideas.rationale: Maps onto Memoria's contradictions/relations dashboards and projected canvas: prune noisy edges, keep statistically robust ones, and explicitly surface 'few mentions but high score' as discovery candidates (the FTCDNL1 case) — useful for the proactive discovery loop and triage.

ideas.idea: Modular NER -> normalize-to-ontology (UMLS via char-3-gram + ANN) -> typed relation extraction, kept separable so each stage can be swapped/expanded.
ideas.rationale: Validates Memoria's deterministic-ingest + record-linkage entity dedup design: normalize entity mentions to a canonical ID (citekey/entity registry) via approximate-nearest-neighbor over char n-grams before relation work. The modular staging mirrors 'engines write, agents judge.'

ideas.idea: A defined relation typology (Target->Cause, Modulator->Increase/Decrease, Biomarker, Plays-a-role, No-explicit-relationship) for a fine-tuned classifier.
ideas.rationale: The domain labels are biomedical, but the pattern — a closed, directional, polarity-aware relation vocabulary with an explicit 'no relationship' class — is exactly what Memoria's typed-relation and argument-graph (support/contradict/warrant) schema needs; borrow the schema-design discipline, not the labels.

ideas.idea: Treat statistical significance (not journal/citation quality) as the truth filter, deferring quality weighting because corpus is huge; suggests journal-IF/citation weighting for smaller corpora.
ideas.rationale: Memoria operates on a SMALL single-researcher corpus, so the paper's own caveat applies: Memoria should weight by source quality/recency rather than rely on large-N significance. Adopt the caveat's prescription, reject the large-corpus assumption.

ideas.idea: Fine-tuning PubMedBERT for relation typing is cheaper at inference than in-context/prompting when processing 39M+ abstracts.
ideas.rationale: Argument is driven by web-scale corpus economics; Memoria's single-researcher vault is tiny, so a local LLM with in-context prompting is preferable to maintaining a fine-tuned domain BERT.

file: Liu et al. - 2026 - AutoResearchClaw self-reinforcing autonomous research with human-AI collaboration.md
title: AutoResearchClaw: Self-Reinforcing Autonomous Research with Human-AI Collaboration
one_line: A 23-stage multi-agent autonomous-research pipeline combining role-based debate, self-healing execution, a verified-numeric registry + 4-layer citation verification, seven human-in-the-loop intervention modes, and time-decayed cross-run lessons.

ideas.idea: Verified numeric registry: a whitelist of every value produced by actual runs; at draft time only registry-populated tables are injected, and a post-hoc verifier rejects any number in strict sections that cannot be matched to a registry entry (writer can read but not modify it).
ideas.rationale: This is the engineering form of Memoria's faithfulness-over-flash + structural-review-gate principle. Memoria should anchor every extracted claim/number/quote to a provenance registry (citekey + source span) and have the gate hard-reject notes asserting values absent from the registry — 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' made enforceable.

ideas.idea: Four-layer citation verification (DOI via CrossRef -> fuzzy title via OpenAlex -> arXiv ID -> Semantic Scholar) plus an LLM relevance check classifying refs as Verified/Suspicious/Hallucinated.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's Zotero/citekey backbone can run an analogous deterministic resolver chain at ingest to reject hallucinated or unresolvable references before a note is written, and flag 'suspicious' ones for the gate. Single-researcher-scale and directly buildable.

ideas.idea: Targeted human-in-the-loop at a few high-leverage decision points (CoPilot, 6 gates) beats both full autonomy and step-by-step approval; SmartPause routes to the human only when system uncertainty is high.
ideas.rationale: Empirically grounds Memoria's gate philosophy: the PI should be asked to approve only at high-leverage/high-uncertainty points, not every note. SmartPause = an uncertainty-gated escalation policy for the structural review gate and learning-to-rank triage, avoiding approval fatigue for a single PI.

ideas.idea: Multi-agent debate with complementary epistemic roles (Innovator/Pragmatist/Contrarian for hypotheses; Optimist/Skeptic/Methodologist for results) and a synthesizer producing one structured artifact.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's 'agents judge' layer can adopt a contrarian/skeptic critique pass over engine-extracted claims and proposed relations (especially for the contradictions dashboard and argument warrants), but a single-PI local setup should keep it to one cheap critique role rather than a K=3 panel per the cost ablation.

ideas.idea: Cross-run evolution: persist structured lessons from past failures, retrieve by category, inject as natural-language overlays ranked by a time-decayed half-life weight (default 30 days) — no retraining.
ideas.rationale: A lightweight persistent-lesson store with recency decay fits Memoria's nightly discovery loop and skill governance: record gate rejections/PI overrides as decaying guidance injected into future engine prompts. Borrow the time-decay-overlay mechanism; the 30-day half-life is a tunable.

ideas.idea: Self-healing executor with Pivot/Refine loop that treats failures as diagnostic information and preserves partial artifacts instead of aborting.
ideas.rationale: Applicable to Memoria's deterministic ingest pipeline: on parse/extraction failure, capture the failure signature, retry with a targeted fix, and keep recoverable partial extractions rather than dropping the paper. Useful robustness pattern even without autonomous experiment execution.

ideas.idea: Per-stage formal input/output contracts (JSON schema, acceptance criteria, error-code namespaces) with checkpoint-based resumption across a 23-stage pipeline.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's compiled-profiles + deterministic-ingest design: each engine stage should declare a typed contract and acceptance criteria the gate can check mechanically. Memoria's pipeline is far shorter, so borrow the contract discipline, not 23 stages.

ideas.idea: Sandboxed Docker execution of generated code with three-phase network isolation, read-only metric harness, AST security checks, and module/builtin blacklists.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's agents are MCP-only and get NO file/terminal/code-execution ever (ADR-32/46; PR #364). The whole point is that nothing runs untrusted code; a code sandbox is unnecessary and contradicts the hard-deny architecture.

ideas.idea: Full autonomous idea->experiment->paper generation with anti-fabrication safeguards positioned as a 'research amplifier'.
ideas.rationale: Memoria deliberately does NOT autonomously generate papers/experiments; it organizes the PI's knowledge with the PI making final claims. End-to-end paper generation is out of scope and against the faithfulness/PI-authority stance.

file: Liu et al. - 2026 - Omni-SimpleMem autoresearch-guided discovery of lifelong multimodal agent memory.md
title: Omni-SimpleMem: Autoresearch-Guided Discovery of Lifelong Multimodal Agent Memory
one_line: An autonomously-discovered lifelong multimodal agent-memory framework built on selective novelty-gated ingestion, unified Multimodal Atomic Units (hot metadata / cold raw), hybrid dense+sparse+graph retrieval with set-union merging, and budgeted pyramid (summary->full->raw) expansion.

ideas.idea: Multimodal Atomic Units (MAU): atomize inputs into units carrying a summary + embedding + pointer-to-raw + timestamp + modality + structural links, with two-tier hot (searchable metadata) / cold (raw) storage.
ideas.rationale: Strongly parallels Memoria's atomic-claim model. Adopt the hot/cold split: store the claim summary, embedding, citekey-anchored pointer to the source span, and typed links in fast storage, with the full PDF/page in cold storage — fast retrieval over compact metadata while preserving full-content provenance on demand.

ideas.idea: Hybrid dense (FAISS) + sparse (BM25) retrieval combined by set-union merging — keep dense ordering, append BM25-only hits — because score-based re-ranking empirically disrupts semantic ordering and hurts.
ideas.rationale: Concrete, buildable upgrade to Memoria's retriever/scout and pre-file similarity gate: lexical BM25 catches exact-term matches (citekeys, gene names, rare terms) that dense embeddings miss. The set-union (not score-fusion) detail is a directly applicable, deterministic, local recipe.

ideas.idea: Progressive 'pyramid' retrieval: expand candidates in stages (summaries -> full text -> raw evidence) under an explicit token budget, gated by deterministic similarity thresholds rather than LLM judgment.
ideas.rationale: Fits Memoria's local/cheap-model constraint: feed the extraction/answer LLM compact summaries first and only load full claim text or the raw source span when similarity warrants, controlling token cost on qwen2.5:7b. Deterministic gating keeps it inspectable for the read-only inspector.

ideas.idea: Selective ingestion via lightweight novelty detectors (Jaccard overlap vs recent summaries for text; CLIP frame deltas / VAD for media) to discard near-duplicate content before storage.
ideas.rationale: Text novelty-gating is exactly Memoria's pre-file similarity gate / record-linkage dedup: cheap Jaccard/embedding overlap against recent notes before writing avoids duplicate claims. Adopt the text path; the media detectors are lower priority for a paper-centric vault.

ideas.idea: Knowledge-graph-augmented retrieval: extract typed entities (7 types) + directed relations per unit, resolve duplicate entities via combined cosine(name-embedding)+Jaro-Winkler over a threshold, and answer multi-hop queries via distance-decayed h-hop neighborhood expansion.
ideas.rationale: Maps onto Memoria's typed-relations + entity-dedup directly. The hybrid embedding+Jaro-Winkler entity-resolution rule is a concrete record-linkage formula to adopt, and distance-decayed h-hop expansion is a buildable way to answer cross-note questions over the argument graph.

ideas.idea: Empirical finding: returning full original text instead of LLM summaries can sharply raise token-overlap F1 (counter to the efficiency-driven summary default).
ideas.rationale: A caution for Memoria's projection/answer surfaces: when faithfulness to exact source wording matters (quotes, claim text), prefer verbatim spans over generated summaries — consistent with faithfulness-over-flash. Borrow as a design caveat for when to summarize vs. quote.

ideas.idea: Read-only thread-safe FAISS/BM25 indices enabling parallel query workers for 3.5x throughput.
ideas.rationale: Minor: Memoria's nightly discovery loop can use read-only indices for concurrent similarity lookups, but single-researcher scale makes the throughput win marginal — note the read-only-index pattern, don't prioritize parallelism.

ideas.idea: Use of an autonomous research pipeline (AutoResearchClaw) to discover the architecture itself; claim that bug-fixes/architecture/prompt changes beat hyperparameter tuning.
ideas.rationale: The auto-discovery methodology is out of scope for Memoria (no autonomous experiment execution; MCP-only agents). The valuable output is the resulting memory architecture, which is captured in the adopt items above; the discovery process itself contributes nothing buildable here.

file: Weld - 1999 - Recent advances in AI planning.md
title: Recent Advances in AI Planning
one_line: A 1999 survey of classical AI planning algorithms (Graphplan, compilation of planning to propositional SAT, and interleaved planning/execution for spacecraft control).

ideas.idea: Graphplan planning-graph with binary mutex (mutual-exclusion) relations between actions/propositions, computed recursively (inconsistent effects, interference, competing needs, inconsistent support)
ideas.rationale: This is STRIPS action-planning over world states; Memoria does not synthesize action sequences. The mutex idea has no clean analogue to claim contradictions (which are semantic, not precondition/effect logic), so adopting it would be forced.

ideas.idea: Solution extraction reformulated as a dynamic CSP with forward checking, dynamic variable ordering (fewest-remaining-values), and memoized nogoods
ideas.rationale: Memoria's structural review gate and triage are scoring/judgment tasks, not constraint-satisfaction search over a combinatorial plan space; CSP machinery is off-domain and far heavier than needed at single-researcher scale.

ideas.idea: Type analysis to eliminate static (timeless) predicates and instantiate only plausibly-typed ground actions, shrinking the search space before solving
ideas.rationale: The general insight — precompute domain invariants to prune candidates cheaply before expensive reasoning — loosely echoes Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and discovery pre-filters. Worth keeping as a design principle (filter early, deterministically), though no specific mechanism transfers.

ideas.idea: Compilation of a problem into propositional CNF and solving with a separate fast solver (separation of compile/simplify/solve/decode stages)
ideas.rationale: Memoria's reasoning is LLM-plus-deterministic-engine, not SAT solving; there is no planning formula to compile. Architecturally irrelevant.

ideas.idea: Interleaved planning and execution with real-time monitoring (Deep Space One), where deliberation is bounded so the agent can act within deadlines
ideas.rationale: Memoria is a batch/nightly knowledge system with a human approval gate, not a real-time reactive controller; bounded-deliberation-under-deadline is not a constraint it operates under.

file: Weng et al. - 2025 - CycleResearcher improving automated research via automated review.md
title: CycleResearcher: Improving Automated Research via Automated Review
one_line: An iterative preference-training framework where a policy LLM (CycleResearcher) writes full ML papers and a generative reward LLM (CycleReviewer) simulates multi-reviewer peer review, with the review scores driving SimPO refinement.

ideas.idea: CycleReviewer: a generative reward model that simulates multiple reviewers with varying perspectives (lowest-to-highest rating) plus a senior reviewer who synthesizes a final decision, scoring Soundness/Presentation/Contribution/Overall
ideas.rationale: Directly relevant to Memoria's structural review gate ('agents judge'). The multi-perspective-then-synthesize review structure, and per-aspect rubric scoring, is a usable pattern for the gate's automated critique of candidate notes/claims — though Memoria would run it as MCP-only judgment, not as an RL reward signal.

ideas.idea: Proxy MAE/MSE evaluation: with no ground-truth quality, treat the mean of the OTHER n-1 reviewers' scores as an unbiased estimator and measure each reviewer (human or model) against it
ideas.rationale: A concrete, cheap way to evaluate Memoria's review-gate/triage scorers and even the PI's own judgments without a gold label — measure consistency against held-out peer scores. Buildable into the evaluation/benchmark plane for the gate.

ideas.idea: Rejection sampling: generate N candidate outputs and keep the highest-reward one; quality rises monotonically with N (5.36 to 7.02 at N=100) because generation is cheap relative to evaluation
ideas.rationale: Applicable to Memoria's extraction/projection engines: sample several candidate claim-extractions or note drafts and let the review gate pick the best. But cost-sensitive at single-researcher local-model scale, so use small N and only where the judge is reliable.

ideas.idea: Structured outline-then-section generation (motivation, methods, experimental setup, results extracted as explicit outline fields) to plan before writing
ideas.rationale: Mirrors Memoria's MASSW-style paper-aspect extraction; reinforces that paper structure should be captured as explicit typed fields, and that planning-before-writing improves long structured output. Reuse the aspect schema idea, not the generation pipeline.

ideas.idea: Fast-DetectGPT watermarking / AI-generated-content detection as an integrity safeguard on outputs
ideas.rationale: Memoria is single-user with a PI approval gate; provenance is already known (engines write, PI approves). LLM-text detection solves a multi-author submission-integrity problem Memoria does not have.

ideas.idea: Full end-to-end autonomous paper generation with FABRICATED experimental results as the RL objective
ideas.rationale: Contradicts Memoria's core value of faithfulness over flash; generating papers with fabricated results is the opposite of a faithful claim/evidence store. Explicitly not a goal.

ideas.idea: Iterative SimPO preference training over a trainable open-source policy model to internalize evolving review standards
ideas.rationale: Memoria uses cheap local off-the-shelf models with no in-house RL training pipeline; maintaining a custom-trained 12B-123B policy/reward pair is out of scope for single-researcher local deployment.

file: West et al. - 2022 - GOMS, distributed cognition, and the knowledge structures of organizations.md
title: GOMS, Distributed Cognition, and the Knowledge Structures of Organizations
one_line: A Cognitive Science Society proceedings paper (1998) on applying GOMS and distributed cognition to organizational knowledge structures — but the extracted markdown contains only title/author/permalink metadata with no body text.

ideas.idea: No extractable content: the markdown file is metadata-only (title, authors, eScholarship permalink, empty section headers); the paper body did not parse
ideas.rationale: There is no usable content to judge. The title topic (GOMS task models, distributed cognition across an organization) is also off-domain for a single-researcher knowledge vault — Memoria explicitly is NOT multi-user/organizational, so even the implied subject offers nothing to adopt.

file: Whelton et al. - 2018 - 2017 ACCAHAAAPAABCACPMAGSAPhAASHASPCNMAPCNA guideline for the prevention, detection, evalu.md
title: 2017 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Prevention, Detection, Evaluation, and Management of High Blood Pressure in Adults (Executive Summary)
one_line: A clinical practice guideline for hypertension management, notable for its methodology of graded evidence-based recommendations and a 'modular knowledge chunk' document format.

ideas.idea: Modular knowledge-chunk format: each recommendation packaged as a self-contained chunk (recommendation table + synopsis + recommendation-specific supportive text + local references), enabling focused updates and content-tagged electronic retrieval
ideas.rationale: A strong analogue for Memoria's atomic-claim-plus-evidence note unit: keep each claim with its supporting text and citations co-located so it can be superseded/updated in place and retrieved by tag. Reinforces the supersession + content-tagging design at the unit-of-knowledge level.

ideas.idea: Class of Recommendation (strength) and Level of Evidence (quality) as orthogonal graded labels attached to every recommendation
ideas.rationale: Maps cleanly onto Memoria's atomic-claim uncertainty flags: separate the strength/confidence of a claim from the quality/type of its supporting evidence as two distinct typed fields. A concrete, buildable schema refinement for the claim model.

ideas.idea: Commissioned systematic-review triggers (criteria for when a formal evidence review committee is convened) and ~6-year revision cycles with event-triggered focused updates
ideas.rationale: Echoes Memoria's deferred-ADR cadence-review model and proactive-discovery loop: define explicit triggers (new practice-changing evidence) that flag a claim cluster for re-review rather than continuous churn. Borrow the trigger-plus-cadence governance pattern.

ideas.idea: The clinical content itself (BP thresholds, drug therapy, comorbidity management)
ideas.rationale: Off-domain payload for a system designed around CS/ML research papers; the hypertension recommendations are not knowledge Memoria's design needs to encode.

file: White et al. - 2023 - A prompt pattern catalog to enhance prompt engineering with ChatGPT.md
title: A Prompt Pattern Catalog to Enhance Prompt Engineering with ChatGPT
one_line: A catalog of 16 reusable prompt patterns (documented in software-pattern form with intent/structure/example/consequences) for structuring LLM interactions, grouped into Input Semantics, Output Customization, Error Identification, Prompt Improvement, Interaction, and Context Control.

ideas.idea: Template / Output Automater patterns: instruct the LLM to fit output into a fixed template with named placeholders so output structure is application-controlled
ideas.rationale: Directly applicable to Memoria's deterministic-ingest and structured-capture engines: extraction prompts (atomic claims, MASSW aspects, claim-sentence classification) should pin output to an explicit template/schema. This is exactly how engines should constrain LLM output before the gate.

ideas.idea: Fact Check List pattern: have the LLM emit the list of facts/assumptions its output depends on, separately, so they can be verified
ideas.rationale: Strongly aligned with Memoria's faithfulness-over-flash and uncertainty-flagging: when an engine extracts or summarizes, requiring an explicit dependent-facts list gives the review gate and PI concrete checkable items, supporting the contradictions/warrant tracking.

ideas.idea: Persona pattern: assign the LLM a role to scope what it attends to (e.g., 'security reviewer')
ideas.rationale: Underpins Memoria's compiled agent profiles (retriever/scout, reviewer, judge). The persona-as-scoping insight is sound; Memoria already operationalizes it as governed profiles rather than ad-hoc inline prompts, so borrow the principle, not the loose prompt form.

ideas.idea: Cognitive Verifier pattern: subdivide a question into sub-questions, answer each, then combine — improving reasoning accuracy
ideas.rationale: Useful decomposition tactic for the review gate's harder judgments (e.g., is claim A a contradiction or a refinement of B). Adapt as an internal multi-step judging prompt; the human-answers-the-subquestions variant does not fit a batch pipeline.

ideas.idea: Flipped Interaction / Question Refinement patterns: LLM asks the user clarifying questions or rewrites the user's query before answering
ideas.rationale: Relevant to Memoria's PI-facing structured capture and triage forms — eliciting missing metadata interactively. Use sparingly: the system favors deterministic batch engines over chatty interactive flows, so this fits only the human-in-the-loop capture surfaces.

ideas.idea: Meta Language Creation pattern: teach the LLM a custom shorthand notation for the conversation
ideas.rationale: Adds ambiguity risk (the paper itself flags this) and fragility; Memoria prefers explicit typed schemas and deterministic parsing over ad-hoc per-session notations. Not worth the unpredictability in a faithfulness-first pipeline.

ideas.idea: Refusal Breaker / Infinite Generation / Game Play patterns
ideas.rationale: Refusal Breaker is adversarial (works against guardrails — counter to Memoria's MCP-only safety posture); Game Play is irrelevant; Infinite Generation is just a loop better handled by Memoria's deterministic batch orchestration than by a self-continuing prompt.

ideas.idea: Documenting prompts as reusable named patterns (intent/structure/example/consequences) for knowledge transfer
ideas.rationale: Meta-level: Memoria's skill governance and compiled profiles benefit from cataloging vetted prompt patterns with consequences/caveats rather than scattering ad-hoc prompts. Adopt the cataloging discipline for the skill/profile library.

file: Brooks - 1990 - Elephants don't play chess.md
title: Elephants Don't Play Chess
one_line: Rodney Brooks's manifesto for behavior-based ('nouvelle') AI arguing intelligence emerges from situated sensor-action coupling rather than explicit symbolic world models — but the extraction is empty (scanned image, no recoverable text).

ideas.idea: The PDF extraction contains no recoverable body text (only empty headings and an omitted figure), so no concrete ideas can be assessed from the file itself.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's ingest is deterministic and faithfulness-first; an empty/OCR-failed extraction yields nothing to adopt. The known thesis (representation-free situated AI) is in any case orthogonal to a markdown-vault knowledge system. This file should route to the failed/re-OCR queue, not inform design.

file: Brooks - 1991 - Intelligence without representation.md
title: Intelligence Without Representation
one_line: Brooks's foundational argument that explicit symbolic representation is unnecessary for intelligent behavior (the world as its own model) — but the extraction is empty (scanned image, no recoverable text).

ideas.idea: The extraction contains only empty headings and two omitted figures; no body text was recovered, so the paper's actual claims (subsumption architecture, 'the world is its own best model') are not present in the file.
ideas.rationale: Nothing extractable to adopt or borrow. Even thematically, an anti-representation stance contradicts Memoria's entire premise — a typed claim/argument graph IS an explicit symbolic representation built deliberately for a single researcher's knowledge. Route to re-OCR; do not feed into design.

file: Brown - 1992 - Design experiments theoretical and methodological challenges in creating complex interventions in c.md
title: Design Experiments: Theoretical and Methodological Challenges in Creating Complex Interventions in Classroom Settings
one_line: Ann Brown's seminal articulation of 'design experiments' — iteratively engineering and studying complex interventions in real (classroom) settings — but the extraction is essentially empty (title page, abstract metadata, and a single contrast table).

ideas.idea: Design-experiment methodology: iteratively co-engineer an intervention and the theory explaining it in a real-use setting, treating deployment-in-context as the unit of study rather than a controlled lab.
ideas.rationale: Only the title/abstract and one table survived extraction, but the recoverable methodological stance maps onto how Memoria itself should be evolved: treat each ingest/triage/gate feature as an intervention studied against the single PI's real workflow (deferred-ADR 'assumes + cadence review' model), not validated in the abstract. A weak, partial borrow — the empty body means no concrete mechanism is available.

file: Brown et al. - 2007 - Locating family values a field trial of the whereabouts clock (nominated for the best paper award).md
title: Locating Family Values: A Field Trial of the Whereabouts Clock
one_line: A six-month field trial of a coarse-grained family location display showing that deliberately reduced fidelity ('less is more') and information families 'already share' delivered more value (reassurance, connectedness) than precise tracking.

ideas.idea: 'Less is more' / deliberate coarse granularity: a system that intentionally shows only four privacy-preserving categories outperformed higher-accuracy designs because coarse, ambiguous information fit users' practices and avoided overwhelming detail.
ideas.rationale: Directly applicable to Memoria's surfaces: dashboards, the contradictions view, and projected canvases should present coarse, glanceable, at-a-glance status (e.g. claim-status buckets, 'needs-review' counts) rather than dumping precise scores. Reinforces the design instinct to keep gate/space surfaces low-resolution and reassuring rather than exhaustive.

ideas.idea: Situated, always-on, periphery-of-vision display located where routine happens (the 'clock in the kitchen'), read at-a-glance with no interaction cost.
ideas.rationale: Maps to Memoria's Obsidian-native dashboards and disposable projection engine: the PI should get ambient, zero-setup status inside the vault UI they already inhabit, not a separate tool requiring action. Supports the PI-direct-access pillar.

ideas.idea: Privacy-by-architecture: access control achieved structurally (the display is only visible inside the home) and by limiting resolution, rather than by policy/configuration.
ideas.rationale: Echoes Memoria's MCP-only sandbox philosophy — security/safety enforced by architecture (agents physically cannot touch files) rather than by configuration or trust. The 'structural, not configured' pattern is the transferable insight; the family/multi-user content itself is off-domain.

ideas.idea: Location-in-interaction / value comes from supporting what users 'already do' rather than automating or predicting for them ('let people be smart', not 'smart homes').
ideas.rationale: Strong alignment with Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' governance: the system should resource the PI's existing research judgment, not automate decisions away. Argues against over-automation of the structural review gate.

ideas.idea: Family coordination semantics, social-touch messaging, emotional/moral connotations of shared location, multi-user awareness fieldwork.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is explicitly single-researcher, not multi-user/family-awareness; the affective/social-group substance has no analogue in a personal knowledge vault.

file: Brown et al. - 2020 - Language models are few-shot learners.md
title: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners (GPT-3)
one_line: Introduces GPT-3 (175B params) and demonstrates that scale enables in-context (zero/one/few-shot) learning where tasks are specified purely via natural-language prompts and demonstrations with no gradient updates.

ideas.idea: In-context / few-shot learning: specify a task to a frozen model via a natural-language instruction plus K demonstrations in the prompt, with no fine-tuning or weight updates.
ideas.rationale: This is the foundational mechanism Memoria's entire local-LLM pipeline relies on — claim extraction, claim-sentence classification, MASSW aspect tagging, keyphrase candidates, and relevance scoring are all prompt-plus-exemplar tasks on a frozen qwen2.5:7b, not fine-tuned models. The few-shot framing (give the small local model a handful of in-context exemplars to lift accuracy) is directly the right pattern for Memoria's no-training, cheap/open-model constraint.

ideas.idea: Task framing changes output distribution (e.g. the LAMBADA fill-in-the-blank reframing forced single-word completions and jumped accuracy 18%).
ideas.rationale: Validates investing in prompt/output-format engineering for Memoria's extraction engines: constraining the task shape (e.g. 'output one atomic claim per line', structured-capture schemas) materially improves a small model's reliability — cheaper than scaling the model.

ideas.idea: Fuzzy document-level deduplication of the training/reference corpus to prevent redundancy and protect a held-out set's integrity (and the systematic N-gram overlap 'contamination' detection with exact Spark collisions, false-positive handling for common phrases, and an 8-13 word minimum gram length).
ideas.rationale: Memoria already has a pre-file similarity gate and record-linkage entity dedup; the N-gram overlap methodology (variable gram length set to the 5th-percentile example length, ignoring grams that match >10 docs as boilerplate) is a concrete, deterministic, no-model technique to borrow for the similarity/dedup gate and for flagging near-duplicate incoming papers.

ideas.idea: Smooth power-law scaling of capability with model size; larger models are markedly better few-shot learners.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is constrained to small local/open models (qwen2.5:7b default, qwen3:14b fallback) on a single 16GB GPU; the headline lesson 'just make it 175B' contradicts the local+cheap pillar. Useful only as a caution that few-shot reliability will be weaker at this scale — handled via the structural review gate and PI approval rather than scaling up.

ideas.idea: Models are not well-calibrated on novel inputs, show high output variance, can contradict themselves over long passages, and inherit data biases.
ideas.rationale: Direct justification for Memoria's core governance: the structural review gate ('engines write, agents judge, PI approves'), atomic-claim uncertainty flags, and the contradictions dashboard exist precisely because LLM extraction is uncalibrated and self-contradicting. This paper is the canonical evidence that LLM outputs must be gated and faithfulness-checked, not trusted directly.

ideas.idea: Human-feedback / learning the objective from humans as a fix for the un-grounded, equal-weight pretraining objective; treating useful systems as goal-directed rather than pure predictors.
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's learning-to-rank triage and discovery-relevance scoring being trained on the PI's accept/reject signals — a lightweight human-feedback loop to steer a frozen model's outputs toward the single researcher's actual interests, without retraining the LLM.

ideas.idea: Massive-scale pretraining infrastructure, model parallelism across GPUs, energy/compute accounting, synthetic-news misuse and threat-actor analysis.
ideas.rationale: Enterprise/cloud-scale training and societal-misuse threat modeling are outside a single-researcher local vault's scope; Memoria consumes a pretrained model, it does not train one.

file: Morris et al. - 2011 - Emerging input technologies for always-available mobile interaction.md
title: Emerging Input Technologies for Always-Available Mobile Interaction
one_line: A 2011 Foundations & Trends survey of wearable/body-worn input and output sensors (inertial, touch, vision, sEMG, BCI, haptics, glasses) aimed at 'always-available' mobile micro-interactions, plus cross-cutting challenges of ambiguity, sensor fusion, and gesture design.

ideas.idea: Systematically modeling input uncertainty as first-class state (Hudson & Newell probabilistic state machines; Schwartz et al. passing ambiguity up to the UI level so a form can place uncertain input in whichever field its model best fits)
ideas.rationale: Memoria already flags atomic-claim uncertainty and runs a structural review gate; the notion that ambiguity should be carried forward as explicit probabilistic state to higher layers (engines emit candidates with uncertainty, the gate/agent resolves) rather than collapsed early at the engine maps onto 'engines write, agents judge'. Borrow the design stance, not the sensor mechanics.

ideas.idea: Segmentation / 'Midas Touch' problem — distinguishing input genuinely directed at the system from ambient activity that merely resembles it
ideas.rationale: Analogous to Memoria's discovery loop and pre-file similarity gate having to separate genuinely relevant incoming papers from near-miss noise, and to claim-sentence classification separating asserted claims from background mentions. The framing (explicit on/off intent gestures, anti-coincidence design) is a useful lens; the eye/gesture specifics are off-domain.

ideas.idea: Sensor fusion: no single modality wins; combine multiple noisy modalities for robustness
ideas.rationale: Memoria's retrieval already blends signals (LTR triage, similarity gate, keyphrase tags), so the generic 'fuse modalities' insight adds nothing buildable; the paper's content is about physical sensors, not text/knowledge signals.

ideas.idea: Whole survey of wearable hardware (accelerometers, sEMG armbands, EEG/ECoG/fMRI BCIs, on-body projection, smart-glasses displays)
ideas.rationale: Entirely off-domain for a single-researcher markdown-vault knowledge system; no ingestion, claim, relation, or retrieval mechanism here. Local/deterministic constraints make body-sensor hardware irrelevant.

file: Mueller et al. - 2019 - Explanation in human-AI systems a literature meta-review, synopsis of key ideas and publications, a.md
title: Explanation in Human-AI Systems: A Literature Meta-Review (DARPA XAI)
one_line: A DARPA XAI integrative review synthesizing CS, philosophy, psychology, and human-factors literatures on what makes a good explanation of an AI system, with concrete taxonomies of explanation goodness, explainee 'trigger' questions, explanation-vs-justification, mental-model elicitation, and an evaluation framework (goodness / mental-model / trust / performance).

ideas.idea: Explanation-goodness feature set (soundness, appropriate detail, veridicality, usefulness, clarity, completeness, observability, dimensions-of-variation) as a coding rubric independent of the working system
ideas.rationale: Directly usable as the rubric Memoria's judging agents apply at the structural review gate when an engine emits a claim/relation/argument-warrant with its rationale. 'Veridicality / does-not-contradict-the-ideal-model' and 'completeness relative to an ideal model' formalize the faithfulness-over-flash constraint into checkable axes.

ideas.idea: Explanation vs. justification distinction: verbalizing rules ('why we did it this way') is justification, not explanation; over-reliance on local justification yields persuasion over knowledge-level understanding
ideas.rationale: Memoria's gate must show the PI WHY an engine proposed a note, not just restate the rule that fired. Adopt the rule that gate/inspector surfaces present grounded, contrastive explanations (why this claim, why-not the alternative) rather than mechanical rule traces — keeps the PI in genuine control, not just persuaded.

ideas.idea: Explainee 'triggers' table mapping user questions (What did it just do? What will it do next? Why didn't it do z? What if x were different?) to goals (understanding, trust, control, anticipation)
ideas.rationale: A ready-made spec for the read-only inspector and gate dashboards: each discovery/triage/dedup decision should answer the why / why-not / what-if questions for that one case. Single-researcher scale makes per-case local explanation tractable and is exactly what builds calibrated PI trust.

ideas.idea: Contrastive explanation (why X and not Y) is both more effective for mental-model formation and cheaper than a full causal analysis
ideas.rationale: Cheap to generate with the local qwen model and aligns with Memoria's contradictions dashboard, claim supersession, and argument graph: present a superseded/rejected claim alongside its competitor with the discriminating reason. Avoids the cost of full causal traces while improving PI understanding.

ideas.idea: Global vs. local explanation: users need 'how it works' during onboarding but 'why it did this on this case' during use; local case explanations alone don't instill global understanding
ideas.rationale: Tells Memoria to offer both a global static-HTML admin report of how an engine/profile works AND per-item local explanations in the gate. Borrow the two-tier split; the deep-net-specific framing is not Memoria's stack.

ideas.idea: Mental-model elicitation via prediction-then-explain (Muramatsu & Pratt: show test cases, have user predict the system's output, then explain why) and the 'illusion of explanatory depth' / knowledge-shield risk
ideas.rationale: A lightweight way to calibrate whether the PI actually understands what a profile/engine will do — show a few sample papers, let the PI predict the triage/relevance verdict before revealing it. Useful for tuning profiles without multi-user studies; borrow as an optional inspector affordance.

ideas.idea: Four-class XAI evaluation framework: explanation goodness (objective rubric), mental-model quality, trust/reliance, and human-machine WORK-SYSTEM performance (not AI performance alone)
ideas.rationale: Frames how to evaluate Memoria's gate/discovery: measure the PI+system joint outcome (correct files written, contradictions surfaced) not just engine precision. Borrow the work-system-performance principle; the experimental-psychology protocol (participants, IVs/DVs) is overkill for n=1.

ideas.idea: 'Moving Target Problem' — because the AI keeps learning, explanations must also explain how the system adapts, and exploratory/reversible debugging ('adjustable replay', revert-and-rerun-with-modified-features) builds the best mental models
ideas.rationale: Maps onto Memoria's nightly discovery loop, LTR triage retraining, and supersession over time: the disposable projection engine / projected canvas can act as reversible 'adjustable replay' letting the PI re-run a case with a tweaked profile and see the diff. Borrow the reversible-exploration pattern; honor the MCP-only sandbox (agents never execute code — the engine replays deterministically).

ideas.idea: Explanation is collaborative/co-adaptive and requires 'common ground' + Grice's maxims (don't explain what's already understood; minimum-necessary-information)
ideas.rationale: Single-PI common ground is learnable over time, so the gate can suppress already-understood rationale and show only the discriminating delta (minimum-necessary-info) — keeps the PI-facing surface terse. Borrow the maxim; multi-agent 'team player' rules are aimed at multi-actor sociotechnical systems Memoria deliberately avoids.

ideas.idea: Sensemaking models (Klein Data/Frame, Pirolli-Card information foraging) framing explanation as continuous active exploration / inquiry rather than a one-shot delivered artifact
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's spaces + projected-canvas as exploration surfaces rather than static reports, and the discovery loop as ongoing foraging. Conceptual backing, not a buildable mechanism, hence borrow.

file: Murdock and Goel - 2008 - Meta-case-based reasoning self-improvement through self-understanding.md
title: Meta-Case-Based Reasoning: Self-Improvement Through Self-Understanding
one_line: Presents TMKL (Task-Method-Knowledge Language) and the REM shell, in which an agent holds an explicit functional self-model and adapts its OWN reasoning process (e.g., turning a disassembly planner into an assembly planner via 'relation mapping') instead of just tweaking individual solutions.

ideas.idea: Explicit Task-Method-Knowledge self-model: tasks (function: given/makes conditions), methods (how, with incidental pre/post-conditions), knowledge (typed relations) as a structured, inspectable representation of an agent's composition and teleology
ideas.rationale: Echoes Memoria's 'compiled profiles' and skill governance: representing each agent/profile as an explicit task+method+knowledge spec with declared given/makes conditions would make profiles inspectable and governable at the gate. Borrow the structured-self-spec idea; the Lisp/Loom/HTN machinery is heavyweight and predates the LLM-profile approach Memoria uses.

ideas.idea: Separation of functional effects (on the task) from incidental effects (on the method), so a new method must satisfy the task's function but may carry new incidental requirements
ideas.rationale: A clean governance distinction: a profile's REQUIRED outcome (function) vs. its implementation-specific side requirements. Useful framing for validating that a re-implemented engine still meets the approved goal (the memory note on separating 're-implement approved goal' from 'net-new component' tracks). Borrow conceptually.

ideas.idea: Relation mapping: adapt a whole reasoning process by mapping its internal relations through a single known relation (inverse-of, generalization, specialization), only modifying internal-state relations and inserting optional mapping tasks where procedures can't be edited
ideas.rationale: An automated self-rewriting-agent technique that fundamentally conflicts with Memoria's MCP-only sandbox (agents get NO code execution and never modify their own machinery) and the 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' gate. Self-modifying agents are exactly what Memoria's governance forbids.

ideas.idea: Reinforcement/Q-learning to resolve the few decision points that model-based adaptation leaves unspecified, with verification by execution against a 'makes' condition
ideas.rationale: Online RL self-adaptation is off Memoria's deterministic-ingest, cheap-local-model path and would put unreviewed behavior into the pipeline; verification-by-self-execution bypasses the PI approval gate.

ideas.idea: Internal vs. external state-relation typing (what the agent can change by editing knowledge vs. what requires acting in the world)
ideas.rationale: Specific to a self-adapting physical-planning agent; Memoria's typed relations are about claims/notes, not an agent's manipulable action space, so the distinction doesn't transfer to a buildable feature.

file: Nadeem et al. - 2021 - StereoSet measuring stereotypical bias in pretrained language models.md
title: StereoSet: Measuring Stereotypical Bias in Pretrained Language Models
one_line: Introduces StereoSet, a crowdsourced natural-language benchmark and the Context Association Test (CAT) that jointly scores a language model's stereotypical bias AND its language-modeling ability, combined into a single Idealized CAT (icat) score, showing larger/better LMs are also more biased.

ideas.idea: Jointly measure a desirable capability and an undesirable property in one paired test, and combine them into a single composite metric (icat = lms * min(ss,100-ss)/50) so a model can't look good by being uniformly useless (a random model is unbiased but a bad LM)
ideas.rationale: The composite-metric design pattern transfers: Memoria's discovery relevance scoring / LTR triage should not be praised for low false-positives if it also retrieves nothing useful — pair a quality axis with a coverage axis into one score so a degenerate engine can't game it. Borrow the metric-design idea, not bias content.

ideas.idea: Meaningless 'distractor' option included alongside the two real choices to verify the model retains basic competence while the bias axis is measured
ideas.rationale: Maps onto Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and dedup: include an obvious-negative control item to confirm the local model still distinguishes meaningful from meaningless candidates before trusting its finer judgments. Cheap, deterministic sanity check; borrow.

ideas.idea: Crowdsourced bias dataset with multi-validator agreement (retain only items where >=3 of 5 validators agree) as a quality filter
ideas.rationale: Memoria is single-researcher (n=1) with no crowd workforce; multi-annotator agreement filtering is structurally inapplicable. The like-for-like (PI-as-sole-judge) is already the design.

ideas.idea: Measuring social/stereotypical bias of pretrained LMs (gender/race/profession/religion) as a deployment-fairness concern
ideas.rationale: Memoria serves one researcher over academic-paper content, not millions of users in fairness-sensitive decisions; the bias-fairness motivation is off-domain. The chosen local model (qwen2.5:7b) selection is driven by hardware/quality, not these social-bias benchmarks.

file: Nation - 2002 - Online how americans are expanding their use of the internet.md
title: A Nation Online: How Americans Are Expanding Their Use of the Internet
one_line: A 2002 U.S. Department of Commerce (NTIA/ESA) policy report using Sept-2001 Census Current Population Survey data to document internet/computer/broadband adoption across U.S. demographic groups, with chapters on the digital divide, youth, workplace, disability, and the unconnected.

ideas.idea: Tracking adoption/penetration over time and segmenting by demographic strata (income, age, education, race, rural/urban) with growth-rate tables
ideas.rationale: Pure population-statistics methodology for public policy; Memoria is a single-user system with no demographic population to segment. No ingestion, claim, relation, retrieval, or governance mechanism is present or transferable.

ideas.idea: Using a Gini coefficient to quantify inequality in technology access
ideas.rationale: An inequality metric for a population distribution; meaningless for n=1 and unrelated to any Memoria surface (claims, relations, triage, gate).

ideas.idea: 'Network effects' framing — value of a network rises with each added subscriber, driving S-curve adoption
ideas.rationale: Macro-economic adoption theory with no analog in a personal, local, single-researcher knowledge vault; explicitly contradicts Memoria's non-multi-user scope.

file: Berkowitz et al. - 2025 - Biomedical text normalization through generative modeling.md
title: Biomedical text normalization through generative modeling
one_line: Systematically compares LLM-based clinical text-normalization strategies and shows that a retrieve-then-generate pipeline (RAGnorm: embedding retrieval narrowing to ~sqrt(N) candidates, then GPT-4 selection) beats zero-shot recall, prompt-recall, pure semantic search, and TF-IDF for mapping free-text terms to a controlled vocabulary.

ideas.idea: RAGnorm: two-stage normalization = embedding retrieval of top candidates, then an LLM picks/synthesizes the best match from that shortlist
ideas.rationale: Directly maps to Memoria's record-linkage entity dedup and controlled-vocab tag/entity normalization: an embedding retriever proposes candidate canonical entities/keyphrases and a local LLM (qwen2.5:7b) under the structural review gate selects the canonical match. Retrieval-first keeps the LLM call small and deterministic-ish, fitting the cheap-local-model constraint.

ideas.idea: Retrieve ~sqrt(N) candidates as the shortlist size to balance recall vs. prompt cost
ideas.rationale: A cheap heuristic for the pre-file similarity gate / dedup shortlist: pass only sqrt(N) nearest candidates to the judging LLM, controlling token cost on a single-researcher machine. Memoria should validate the constant on its own vocab sizes rather than adopt blindly.

ideas.idea: Token-count / API-call accounting as the efficiency metric instead of wall-clock
ideas.rationale: Useful instrumentation idea for Memoria's diagnostics plane: report tokens-per-decision for engine runs, since local runtime timing is noisy. But Memoria runs local models, so cost framing should be compute/latency, not dollars.

ideas.idea: Mean-shortest-path-in-ontology as the eval metric (graded distance, not binary match)
ideas.rationale: For evaluating dedup/normalization quality against a hierarchy (e.g., MeSH/field taxonomy), a graded ontology-distance metric is more informative than exact-match accuracy. Adapt where a hierarchy exists; many Memoria entity spaces are flat, so it is partial.

ideas.idea: Prompt Recall (stuff the entire candidate list into the prompt)
ideas.rationale: Explicitly shown to be cost-prohibitive and to degrade as the candidate set grows (dropped at N=4747). Contradicts Memoria's local/cheap-model and deterministic-ingest constraints; the retrieval-first path dominates it.

ideas.idea: Zero-shot recall of codes from the LLM's parametric memory
ideas.rationale: Worst performer (path length ~3.8) and known to hallucinate codes; conflicts with Memoria's faithfulness-over-flash and verifiability requirements. Retrieval grounding is mandatory.

ideas.idea: GPT-4-generated synonym sets as evaluation data
ideas.rationale: A dataset-construction trick specific to their benchmark; not a Memoria system feature, and synthetic-synonym eval risks circularity. No system component to build here.

file: Bernstein et al. - 2010 - Soylent a word processor with a crowd inside.md
title: Soylent: A Word Processor with a Crowd Inside
one_line: Embeds paid crowd workers into Microsoft Word for shortening/proofreading/macros, contributing the Find-Fix-Verify pattern that splits an open-ended edit task into independent generation and review stages with voting to control unreliable workers.

ideas.idea: Find-Fix-Verify: decompose an open-ended generation task into a Find stage (locate patches needing work), a Fix stage (independent candidate edits), and a Verify stage (independent voters filter bad candidates)
ideas.rationale: This is a clean blueprint for Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' governance: an extraction engine proposes claims/relations (Find/Fix), a separate judging agent votes/flags faulty ones (Verify), PI approves. Splitting generation from verification with workers who cannot vote on their own output maps onto separating the writing engine from the structural review gate.

ideas.idea: Verifiers are more reliable than generators ('Turkers are much better at vetting than authoring')
ideas.rationale: Strong justification for Memoria's architecture where cheap local LLMs are used as judges/gates rather than trusted authors: route the model to flag/vote on engine output (high reliability) instead of free-generating notes (low reliability). Reinforces the structural review gate design.

ideas.idea: Independent-agreement thresholding (keep a patch only if >=20% independently flag it) to denoise
ideas.rationale: Memoria is single-agent, so literal multi-worker voting does not apply, but the insight transfers as multi-sample self-consistency / threshold-on-agreement for claim extraction and contradiction detection (run the judge N times, keep only consistently flagged items). Adapt the mechanism, not the crowd.

ideas.idea: Timeout-with-minimum-quorum to keep an async pipeline responsive
ideas.rationale: Maps to budget/timeout controls for Memoria's nightly proactive discovery and ingest engines so a slow model call cannot stall the loop. Useful operational pattern; the quorum part is crowd-specific.

ideas.idea: Human Macro: natural-language task spec with example input/output and a preview of what the worker sees
ideas.rationale: Informs Memoria's structured capture forms and skill/prompt authoring UX: show the PI a preview of exactly what the engine/agent will receive, and encourage example-driven task specs. Adopt the UX principle, not the crowd backend.

ideas.idea: Paid Mechanical Turk crowd as the execution substrate
ideas.rationale: Directly contradicts Memoria's MCP-only local sandbox, single-researcher privacy, and no-cloud-human-labor constraints. The paper itself flags privacy exposure of documents to anonymous workers, which is a non-starter for a private vault.

ideas.idea: Per-edit micropayment cost model
ideas.rationale: Irrelevant to a local single-user system running open models; there is no marketplace and no payment surface in Memoria.

file: Beth Kery and Myers - 2017 - Exploring exploratory programming.md
title: Exploring Exploratory Programming
one_line: A conceptual framework defining exploratory programming (designing the goal while coding) and characterizing it along needs, code-quality tradeoffs, ease/difficulty, process (backtracking, scale, duration, history), and group vs individual exploration.

ideas.idea: Exploratory history is experiment-centric, not code-centric ('When I tried RandomForest, how did that affect accuracy?'), and needs versions of code plus outputs, notes, parameters, and graphs kept together
ideas.rationale: Relevant to how Memoria records the PI's research process: a discovery/analysis run should be logged as an experiment (query + parameters + result + the note/claim it produced) so the PI can ask outcome-framed questions later. Memoria is a knowledge vault, not an IDE, so borrow the provenance-bundling idea for ingest/discovery audit trails, not full code versioning.

ideas.idea: Exploration happens at multiple scales (single parameter, a function, cross-function snippets, whole file) and conventional file-level versioning is too coarse
ideas.rationale: Argues for fine-grained, sub-document provenance in Memoria: track supersession/changes at the claim and relation level, not just at the note-file level, mirroring claim supersession over time. Conceptual support, not a concrete mechanism.

ideas.idea: Code-quality is deliberately deprioritized during exploration; polish comes only when a final solution is reached
ideas.rationale: Maps to Memoria's disposable projection engine / projected canvas: scratch exploratory surfaces should be cheap and throwaway, with structural review only enforced when content is promoted to durable notes. Validates the 'engines draft, gate before durable write' split.

ideas.idea: Cognitive-dimensions guidance: favor low viscosity and high closeness-of-mapping so changes/reverts are cheap
ideas.rationale: This is programming-language/IDE usability theory; Memoria builds no programming environment and its surfaces are Obsidian-based. No buildable component for a knowledge system, and it does not touch ingest/claims/retrieval.

ideas.idea: Group/team exploration coordination challenges
ideas.rationale: Explicitly multi-person collaboration; contradicts Memoria's single-researcher (single-PI) scale constraint. Out of scope.

file: Betz et al. - 2021 - Thinking aloud dynamic context generation improves zero-shot reasoning performance of GPT-2.md
title: Thinking Aloud: Dynamic Context Generation Improves Zero-Shot Reasoning Performance of GPT-2
one_line: Shows that having an LLM generate problem elaborations and feed them back into its own context (self-bootstrapped 'thinking aloud') improves zero-shot deductive/NLI accuracy, and that an elaboration's effectiveness is predicted by its semantic coherence (faithfulness/pertinence/verisimilitude) with the original problem.

ideas.idea: Piecemeal/structured elaboration that emits explicit proof-chain statements (fact -> intermediate conclusions -> final conclusion) rather than free-form text
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's argument graph with warrants: when an agent judges whether claims support/contradict, prompt it to emit explicit intermediate inference steps tied to premises, which the structural review gate can then inspect. Borrow the structured-elaboration prompt design; the model here is tiny GPT-2 so accuracy claims do not transfer.

ideas.idea: Faithfulness = embedding cosine similarity between generated elaboration and the source context; high-faithfulness elaborations boost accuracy most
ideas.rationale: A concrete, cheap faithfulness signal Memoria can compute for engine-generated content: score an extracted claim/summary against its source passage and route low-similarity outputs to the review gate. Aligns with faithfulness-over-flash. Note the paper's own caveat that embedding similarity fails under negation/contraposition, so use it as a gate signal, not ground truth.

ideas.idea: Embedding similarity does NOT capture deductive relations under negation/contraposition ('not' is a trouble-maker)
ideas.rationale: A direct warning for Memoria's contradiction detection and claim-supersession: pure embedding similarity will miss logically-negated relations, so the contradictions dashboard must use explicit logical/NLI checks, not cosine similarity alone. Concrete design constraint to bake in.

ideas.idea: Self-bootstrapping (same model generates context and answers) and that the gain comes from making a shallow syntactic heuristic succeed rather than real reasoning
ideas.rationale: Cautionary insight: context-expansion can inflate apparent reasoning while the model stays 'stupid'. Memoria should treat agent self-elaboration as a retrieval/coherence aid, not as trustworthy reasoning, keeping the PI-approval gate. Borrow the technique with skepticism.

ideas.idea: Free / unstructured context expansion can actively hurt accuracy (precision drops vs no expansion)
ideas.rationale: Tells Memoria what NOT to do: do not inject free-generated elaborations into claim-extraction prompts, since unfaithful expansion degrades output. Rejected as a usable mechanism; only structured, faithfulness-filtered expansion is worth keeping.

ideas.idea: ChainRuler synthetic deductive dataset and GPT-2-specific accuracy fingerprints
ideas.rationale: Benchmark-construction and model-specific (1.5B GPT-2, no fine-tuning) results; not portable to Memoria's models or domain and not a system component.

file: Beurer-Kellner et al. - 2023 - Prompting is programming a query language for large language models.md
title: Prompting Is Programming: A Query Language for Large Language Models (LMQL)
one_line: Introduces Language Model Programming via LMQL, a declarative query language that combines scripted prompting, control flow, and high-level output constraints, compiling them into token-level decoding masks (via final/follow semantics) that enforce constraints eagerly and cut inference cost by 26-85% while keeping or improving accuracy.

ideas.idea: Declarative high-level output constraints (e.g., len(words(X))<=3, X in [list], stops_at(X, '.')) expressed over words/phrases/entities rather than tokens
