ideas.rationale: Directly buildable into Memoria's MCP-only agent runtime: constrain engine/agent outputs to valid enums, citekeys, claim-type labels, or controlled-vocab tags so the structural review gate receives well-formed, parseable structure instead of free text. This makes deterministic ingest and compiled profiles more robust.

ideas.idea: Constrained decoding via token masks (final/follow semantics) that prune illegal tokens eagerly during generation instead of post-hoc validation+backtracking
ideas.rationale: For local models (qwen2.5:7b via a runtime that exposes the decode loop), eager masking guarantees schema-valid output (JSON fields, label sets) in one pass, cutting retries and cost on the single-researcher GPU. Strong fit with deterministic ingest and the diagnostics-cost concern.

ideas.idea: Joint optimization of multiple holes / scripted beam search over control flow (e.g., expert name + answer decoded together)
ideas.rationale: Useful for multi-field extraction (e.g., jointly decoding claim text + uncertainty flag + aspect label) so fields are mutually consistent. Memoria can borrow the joint-decode idea where its runtime supports it; full scripted beam search may exceed the cheap-local-model budget, so apply selectively.

ideas.idea: distribute clause: return a probability distribution over a fixed support (classification) instead of a free completion
ideas.rationale: Ideal for Memoria's claim-sentence classification, MASSW aspect tagging, and triage scoring: constrain the model to score a closed label set and read calibrated probabilities, which the learning-to-rank triage and discovery relevance scorer can consume directly.

ideas.idea: Vendor-agnostic front-end/back-end separation so the same prompt program runs on any LM exposing a next-token distribution (HuggingFace/local/OpenAI)
ideas.rationale: Aligns with Memoria's preference for local/open models and the qwen-default/qwen3-fallback story: author prompt logic once and swap the backing model. Borrow the abstraction; Memoria already standardizes on local runtimes so it need not adopt LMQL wholesale.

ideas.idea: Optimize for billable-tokens / model-queries as the cost metric and report cost savings
ideas.rationale: Token/decoder-call accounting is the right efficiency lens for Memoria's content-light diagnostics plane on a local box (compute/latency rather than dollars). Borrow the measurement discipline, reframed away from paid-API billing.

ideas.idea: Constraints can force the model to generate text it would never explore unconstrained, sometimes improving downstream accuracy
ideas.rationale: Reinforces using output constraints not just for validity but as a quality lever for extraction tasks. Memoria should treat constraints as part of engine quality, while keeping the review gate since constraints guarantee form, not correctness.

ideas.idea: Adopting LMQL as Memoria's actual query/runtime layer
ideas.rationale: Memoria already has the Hermes runtime and an MCP-only, profiles-compiled agent architecture; importing a whole external query language as the substrate would conflict with the established stack. Adopt the techniques (constraints, masked decoding, distribution outputs) inside Hermes rather than the LMQL toolchain itself.

file: Horvitz et al. - 1998 - The lumière project bayesian user modeling for inferring the goals and needs of software users.md
title: The Lumière Project: Bayesian User Modeling for Inferring the Goals and Needs of Software Users
one_line: Seminal UAI'98 paper on real-time Bayesian-network user modeling that infers a user's goals/needs from actions to drive proactive assistance (the basis of the Office 'Clippy' assistant) — NOTE: this corpus extraction is EMPTY (254-byte blank file), so assessment is from the established paper, not the provided text.

ideas.idea: Bayesian network inferring latent user GOALS from observed events plus an explicit user-action/needs ontology
ideas.rationale: Memoria's retriever/scout and triage profiles must infer the PI's current research intent from observable signals (notes opened, claims queried, recent ingests) to rank discovery results; a small probabilistic intent model is a cheap, interpretable alternative to an LLM call for the nightly loop.

ideas.idea: Modeling the cost/benefit and TIMING of proactive interruption (expected utility of acting now vs. waiting) before surfacing assistance
ideas.rationale: Directly applicable to Memoria's proactive discovery loop and the structural review gate: an expected-value threshold governs whether a discovered paper or contradiction is worth interrupting the PI vs. queuing it silently, keeping 'faithfulness over flash'.

ideas.idea: A temporal model of user attention/dwell as evidence (events decay in relevance over time)
ideas.rationale: Useful as a recency/decay prior for discovery relevance scoring and for ordering the triage queue; lightweight and deterministic, fits single-researcher scale.

ideas.idea: Always-on agent that autonomously offers help by watching the user (the Clippy interaction pattern)
ideas.rationale: Contradicts Memoria's gate-and-approve governance ('engines write, agents judge, PI approves') and PI-direct-access model; Memoria surfaces candidates in dashboards/spaces for review, it does not interrupt with autonomous assistance.

ideas.idea: Hand-authored Bayesian network with expert-elicited CPTs over a fixed software-feature ontology
ideas.rationale: Off-domain (Office UI features) and high authoring cost; Memoria's signals are research-artifact interactions, not GUI events, so the specific network is not reusable — only the inference/utility framing transfers.

file: Hu et al. - 2022 - Knowledgeable prompt-tuning incorporating knowledge into prompt verbalizer for text classification.md
title: Knowledgeable Prompt-tuning: Incorporating Knowledge into Prompt Verbalizer for Text Classification
one_line: KPT expands a prompt verbalizer's label-word set from external knowledge bases (RelatedWords/ConceptNet/WordNet) and refines it with frequency/relevance/calibration/learnable methods, sharply improving zero- and few-shot text classification.

ideas.idea: Expand each class label into a knowledge-base-derived set of diverse label words spanning granularities/aspects, then average their MLM probabilities for prediction
ideas.rationale: Memoria does claim-sentence classification and MASSW aspect tagging with a local 7B model; an expanded verbalizer over a small curated label-word set is a cheap, deterministic, training-free way to raise zero/few-shot accuracy on these fixed-schema labels without fine-tuning.

ideas.idea: TF-IDF-style Relevance Refinement: keep a label word only if it is relevant to its own class and irrelevant to others (relevance score > 1)
ideas.rationale: A principled, unsupervised filter to prune noisy candidate words; transferable to Memoria's keyphrase tag-candidate generation to drop generic terms that fire across many notes.

ideas.idea: Contextualized Calibration: estimate each label word's prior from a small unlabeled support set and divide it out to remove the LM's surface-form bias
ideas.rationale: Cheap (~50 unlabeled examples), local, and directly addresses systematic class-bias in zero-shot classification by small open models — exactly Memoria's deterministic-ingest constraint; can stabilize claim/aspect classifiers.

ideas.idea: Frequency Refinement via contextualized prior to drop rare/unreliable label words
ideas.rationale: Simple ranking-based pruning that improves robustness of verbalizers; reusable wherever Memoria maps model token probabilities to a fixed label schema.

ideas.idea: Learnable per-word weights (few-shot weighted-average verbalizer)
ideas.rationale: Requires labeled few-shot data and gradient training of the verbalizer; Memoria favors training-free local inference and has no per-PI labeled classification sets, so the zero-shot average + calibration path is the right fit, not the learnable variant.

ideas.idea: RoBERTa-large MLM [MASK]-prediction backbone with handcrafted templates
ideas.rationale: The MLM cloze formulation is dated relative to Memoria's generative qwen2.5:7b stack, but the verbalizer+calibration insight is model-agnostic and can be re-expressed as constrained-label scoring over a generative model's logits.

file: Hu et al. - 2026 - Evaluating memory in LLM agents via incremental multi-turn interactions.md
title: Evaluating Memory in LLM Agents via Incremental Multi-Turn Interactions (MemoryAgentBench)
one_line: Defines four memory competencies (Accurate Retrieval, Test-Time Learning, Long-Range Understanding, Selective Forgetting) and a benchmark feeding context incrementally, showing no current memory agent masters all four — especially selective forgetting/multi-hop updates.

ideas.idea: Four-competency framework for memory: Accurate Retrieval, Test-Time Learning, Long-Range Understanding, Selective Forgetting
ideas.rationale: A clean evaluation rubric for Memoria's own memory layer; Accurate Retrieval = pre-file similarity gate + retriever, Long-Range Understanding = cross-note synthesis, and Selective Forgetting maps directly onto Memoria's CLAIM SUPERSESSION and contradictions dashboard. Memoria should adopt these as named, separately-tested axes.

ideas.idea: Selective Forgetting / FactConsolidation: when a newer fact contradicts an older one, prioritize the newer and reason over the consolidated state; all agents fail multi-hop here
ideas.rationale: This is exactly claim supersession over time. The paper's finding that retrieval/graph agents fail multi-hop forgetting is a strong argument that Memoria must make supersession an EXPLICIT typed edge (not rely on retrieval recency), and gate it through review rather than trusting an LLM to forget.

ideas.idea: Memory is a compressed/distilled representation of history, not verbatim long context; agents should absorb input incrementally and consolidate
ideas.rationale: Validates Memoria's atomic-claim extraction + typed-relation model as a distilled store over raw PDFs; supports building consolidation passes rather than dumping full papers into context.

ideas.idea: Empirical result: RAG wins on accurate snippet retrieval, but long-context wins on test-time learning and long-range understanding; retrieval-only misses holistic understanding
ideas.rationale: Tells Memoria not to make discovery/synthesis purely top-k retrieval; cross-cutting tasks (contradiction detection, argument-graph synthesis) need whole-corpus or structured passes, justifying the argument graph and BERTopic clustering as complements to the retriever.

ideas.idea: Structure-Augmented RAG (knowledge graph / event timeline / HippoRAG-v2) as a memory category, with HippoRAG-v2 strong on retrieval+forgetting
ideas.rationale: Memoria already builds a typed-relation/argument graph; the result that graph-structured memory helps on selective forgetting supports investing in graph-based retrieval over the claim graph, though their multi-user commercial systems (Mem0/Zep/Cognee) themselves are out of scope.

ideas.idea: Pairing one long context with many questions for cost-efficient evaluation; fully automated EventQA pipeline from novels
ideas.rationale: An evaluation-design tactic: Memoria can build a local regression suite that injects a known corpus once and probes retrieval/supersession with many queries, keeping eval cheap on local models.

ideas.idea: Commercial/agentic memory systems (MIRIX, MemGPT) and 1M-token frontier backbones as the deployment target
ideas.rationale: These assume cloud APIs (GPT-4o-mini/Gemini-1M) and multi-user scale; Memoria runs local cheap models at single-researcher scale, so the specific systems are not adoptable — only their measured failure modes inform design.

file: Huang et al. - 2025 - Automated hypothesis validation with agentic sequential falsifications.md
title: POPPER: Automated Hypothesis Validation with Agentic Sequential Falsifications
one_line: POPPER validates free-form hypotheses via LLM agents that iteratively design and execute falsification experiments, aggregating per-test p-values into e-values for statistically rigorous, Type-I-error-controlled sequential testing.

ideas.idea: Two-agent split: an Experiment Design Agent proposes a falsifiable sub-hypothesis (with explicit null/alternative) and an Execution Agent runs it to produce a p-value
ideas.rationale: Mirrors Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge' separation. A design/judge split for validating a PI's claim against the vault (proposing checkable sub-claims, then evaluating evidence) fits the structural review gate, though Memoria would judge against stored claims/relations rather than run live statistics.

ideas.idea: Decompose an abstract claim into concrete, measurable, FALSIFIABLE sub-implications before testing it
ideas.rationale: Directly strengthens Memoria's atomic-claim + argument-graph model: a high-level claim node can be linked via warrants to falsifiable sub-claims, making the contradictions dashboard and uncertainty flags evidence-grounded rather than vibes-based.

ideas.idea: Relevance checker (LLM-as-judge giving R(h) in [0,1]) that prunes sub-hypotheses not logically implied by the main hypothesis before they count as evidence
ideas.rationale: A concrete guardrail to stop irrelevant 'support' from inflating confidence — reusable as a relevance gate in Memoria's discovery relevance scoring and before a relation/warrant is written, consistent with the gate-before-write rule.

ideas.idea: Sequential e-value aggregation with anytime-valid Type-I error control and optional stopping (p-to-e calibrator)
ideas.rationale: The statistical machinery is heavier than single-researcher Memoria needs, but the PRINCIPLE — accumulate evidence across multiple independent checks with a calibrated stopping threshold rather than trusting one LLM verdict — is exactly the rigor Memoria wants for claim validation/supersession; worth a lightweight evidence-aggregation analog.

ideas.idea: Self-refinement critique loop over a proposal scoring novelty/implementability/relevance before execution
ideas.rationale: A reusable pre-execution self-critique pattern for Memoria's agents that judge whether a proposed note/relation is novel and well-formed before it reaches the PI gate.

ideas.idea: Error analysis: dominant failure was misinterpreted p-values (36%) and broken implication links (17%); LLM relevance checker overestimated relevance vs. humans
ideas.rationale: Cautionary evidence that an LLM judge is optimistic and needs calibration/human confirmation — reinforces Memoria's PI-approval-in-the-loop design rather than auto-acceptance of agent judgments.

ideas.idea: Execution agent runs arbitrary Python (pandas/scipy) in a ReAct code environment over raw datasets
ideas.rationale: Memoria's MCP-only sandbox HARD-DENIES file/terminal/code execution to agents (ADR-32/46, PR #364); live code-running validation cannot be built as-is. Memoria's analog must judge over already-extracted structured evidence, not execute experiments.

file: Huang et al. - 2025 - Deep research agents a systematic examination and roadmap.md
title: Deep Research Agents: A Systematic Examination and Roadmap
one_line: A survey defining Deep Research agents and taxonomizing their search (API vs browser), tool use, static-vs-dynamic workflows, single-vs-multi-agent architectures, memory mechanisms, and RL/SFT tuning, with benchmarks and open challenges.

ideas.idea: Three context/memory strategies for long-horizon research: extend context window, COMPRESS intermediate steps (summarize between phases / 'Reason-in-Documents'), and use EXTERNAL STRUCTURED STORAGE for temporary results
ideas.rationale: Memoria's vault IS the external structured store; the survey's point that intermediate-step compression beats raw long context for cost/quality supports a per-phase summarization step in the discovery loop on local models, and validates persisting distilled claims rather than full text.

ideas.idea: Static vs dynamic workflows: predefined sequential pipelines vs adaptive replanning
ideas.rationale: Memoria's nightly ingest/discovery is best as a mostly-STATIC deterministic pipeline (deterministic ingest constraint), reserving dynamic planning only for open-ended PI queries — the taxonomy clarifies where each belongs.

ideas.idea: Three planning/intent strategies: planning-only, intent-to-planning (clarify first), unified intent-planning (propose plan + ask user to confirm)
ideas.rationale: Unified intent-planning (generate a plan, request PI confirmation) maps cleanly onto Memoria's PI-approval gate and structured capture forms for research-question-driven tasks.

ideas.idea: MCP as the standardized tool-access interface separating tool access (MCP) from agent orchestration (A2A)
ideas.rationale: Confirms Memoria's MCP-only architecture is the right industry-aligned substrate (ADR-31); the survey reinforces MCP for extensibility while Memoria deliberately omits A2A multi-agent coordination as overkill for one researcher.

ideas.idea: Structured memory via knowledge graphs (Agentic Reasoning) and shared knowledge bases (Agent-KB/Alita) for precise information reuse
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's typed-relation/argument-graph approach over plain vector stores for retrieval precision, with the survey's caveat about higher maintenance cost — acceptable since the graph is core to Memoria's value.

ideas.idea: Browser-based vs API-based retrieval; self-evolving agents that instantiate new MCP servers/tools at runtime (Alita); RL/SFT tuning of the agent backbone
ideas.rationale: Runtime self-spawning of tools and RL-trained backbones contradict Memoria's compiled-profile, deterministic, MCP-only, no-self-modification governance; browser-based crawling at industrial scale is out of scope for a single-researcher Zotero-backed corpus.

ideas.idea: Benchmark critique: evaluation metrics misalign with practical DR objectives; sequential execution is inefficient (calls for asynchronous parallelism)
ideas.rationale: Async parallel multi-agent execution is an enterprise-scale concern; at single-researcher nightly-batch scale Memoria gains little, and the cited benchmarks (GAIA/HLE) are off-domain for personal-vault evaluation.

file: Yue et al. - 2026 - Building MCP-native hierarchical AI scientist ecosystems a perspective on scaling multi-agent scien.md
title: Building MCP-Native Hierarchical AI Scientist Ecosystems: A Perspective on Scaling Multi-Agent Scientific Discovery
one_line: A position paper arguing that scalable agentic science needs both an MCP-native tool-interoperability substrate and a human-inspired organizational layer (tasks, shared artifacts, disagreement/repair, provenance) on top of it.

ideas.idea: MCP-native composition: expose tools AND coordination services (task boards, lab notebooks, provenance stores) through the protocol rather than host-specific wrappers, decoupling fast agent stacks from slow scientific software
ideas.rationale: Memoria is already MCP-only for its agent sandbox (ADR-32/46, MCP-only-agent-sandbox memory); this paper's framing that even coordination/state surfaces should be MCP resources directly endorses the existing Librarian-over-obsidian-MCP design and the BERTopic/dedup MCPs.

ideas.idea: Artifact-centered coordination with externalized, versioned, inspectable state instead of private agent chat context ('engines write, agents judge')
ideas.rationale: This is exactly Memoria's posture: the vault is the durable shared artifact, engines write deterministically, the structural review gate adjudicates before notes land. The paper provides external corroboration for the 'externalize memory into inspectable artifacts' pillar.

ideas.idea: Provenance interface: every claim links to evidence artifacts and execution traces (tool calls, environment versions, data lineage) so claims are traceable not merely callable
ideas.rationale: Directly maps to Memoria's atomic-claim backbone with citekey provenance, argument graph with warrants, and claim supersession; reinforces tightening claim-to-source traceability in ingest.

ideas.idea: Decision/disagreement interface: create structured disagreement records and trigger repair actions (replication, alternative methods, human review) to prevent silent divergence
ideas.rationale: Memoria has a contradictions dashboard and supersession; the explicit 'disagreement record + escalation to PI' pattern is a useful structuring of how contradictions should route to the approval gate, adapted to single-PI (human review = the PI).

ideas.idea: Three-layer hierarchy (meta-orchestrator / domain managers / specialist workers) as an information contract that constrains communication, propagating only compressed decision-relevant updates upward
ideas.rationale: This is multi-agent-population scaling for large research organizations; Memoria is single-PI scale with a small fixed set of profiles. The quadratic-communication problem it solves does not arise.

ideas.idea: Pathway 2: automated code-to-MCP transformation (Code2MCP) to wrap arbitrary repos as MCP services via multi-agent self-correction loops
ideas.rationale: Memoria's tool surface is deliberate, deterministic, and PI-curated; autonomously generating/wrapping new MCP servers contradicts the compiled-profiles + structural-gate governance and the cheap-local-model constraint.

ideas.idea: Pathway 3: autonomous invention/evolution of new agents and workflows (ADAS/AFlow/Darwin Godel Machine) materialized as new MCP servers
ideas.rationale: Self-evolving agents that search over workflow code directly conflict with Memoria's MCP-only no-code-execution sandbox and 'PI approves' governance; the paper itself flags hidden technical-debt and rollback risks.

ideas.idea: Ecosystem-level evaluation metrics beyond task success: tool coverage, integration cost, coordination cost, reproducibility-under-evolution
ideas.rationale: Reproducibility-under-evolution (fraction of results still rerunnable as tools/models change) is a useful health metric for Memoria's deterministic-ingest pledge and the content-light diagnostics plane; the multi-team coordination-cost metrics are out of scope.

file: Zelikman et al. - 2022 - STaR bootstrapping reasoning with reasoning.md
title: STaR: Self-Taught Reasoner — Bootstrapping Reasoning With Reasoning
one_line: A self-training loop that bootstraps chain-of-thought rationales from a few examples by fine-tuning on rationales that yield correct answers, plus 'rationalization' (generate a rationale given the answer as a hint) to learn from initially-failed problems.

ideas.idea: Answer-filtered self-training: generate rationales, keep only those that reach the known-correct answer, fine-tune, repeat
ideas.rationale: Requires a fine-tuning training loop over a model; Memoria prefers fixed cheap/local models (qwen2.5:7b) and deterministic ingest, and runs no in-house model training. The verification-by-known-answer signal also doesn't exist for open-ended claim extraction.

ideas.idea: Rationalization: provide the correct answer as a hint to reverse-engineer a justification for problems the model failed forward
ideas.rationale: As an inference-time prompting trick (not training): when an engine must justify a known label — e.g. why a claim supports/contradicts another, or why a paper matches a triage decision — conditioning generation on the target answer can yield cleaner warrants for the argument graph. Use the technique, not the fine-tuning.

ideas.idea: Low-temperature greedy decoding beats high-temperature sampling because high temp produces correct answers with wrong/irrelevant reasoning that harms generalization
ideas.rationale: Directly relevant to Memoria's faithfulness-over-flash constraint: for claim classification and warrant generation, prefer low-temperature deterministic decoding to avoid plausible-but-unfaithful rationales.

ideas.idea: Observed rationale failure taxonomy (question-implies-answer, begging the question, world-state assertions about named variables, red herrings)
ideas.rationale: A concrete checklist of unfaithful-reasoning patterns the structural review gate / claim-sentence classifier can screen for when an engine emits a warrant or uncertainty flag.

file: Zhan et al. - 2024 - InjecAgent benchmarking indirect prompt injections in tool-integrated large language model agents.md
title: INJECAGENT: Benchmarking Indirect Prompt Injections in Tool-Integrated LLM Agents
one_line: A benchmark (1,054 cases, 17 user tools x 62 attacker tools) showing tool-integrated LLM agents execute malicious instructions hidden in retrieved external content (ReAct GPT-4 24%, up to 47% with a hacking-prompt prefix), with fine-tuned agents more resilient.

ideas.idea: Indirect prompt injection threat model: malicious instructions embedded in retrieved external content (reviews, emails, notes, web pages) hijack the agent into harmful tool calls
ideas.rationale: Memoria ingests untrusted external academic content (papers, abstracts, discovery-loop web fetches) and feeds it to agents that can call MCP tools. This is the precise attack surface; the MCP-only no-file/no-terminal sandbox already limits blast radius, but ingest content must be treated as hostile.

ideas.idea: Content-freedom finding: placeholders with high content freedom (free-text fields like review bodies) yield much higher attack success because injected instructions blend in
ideas.rationale: Tells Memoria exactly where to harden: free-text fields of ingested papers (abstract, body, notes) are the high-content-freedom slots; the pre-file similarity gate and structural review gate should treat engine outputs derived from such fields as low-trust, and claim extraction prompts should isolate instructions from data.

ideas.idea: Fine-tuned tool-calling agents are markedly more resilient to IPI than ReAct-prompted agents (GPT-4 fine-tuned 7.1% vs prompted 47%)
ideas.rationale: Memoria won't fine-tune, but the insight supports preferring structured/constrained tool-calling interfaces over free-form ReAct prompting in profiles, and validates the engines-write-deterministically split where LLMs judge rather than freely act.

ideas.idea: Data-stealing two-step pattern (extract then exfiltrate via a send tool) succeeds easily once extraction happens — transmission step near 100%
ideas.rationale: Concrete justification for Memoria's hard rule that agents get no outbound/send/file/terminal tools ever (MCP-only sandbox, gate plugin hard-denies): even if an injection causes data extraction, there is no exfiltration tool to complete the chain.

ideas.idea: Defense taxonomy: black-box (delimiters around external content, awareness prompts) vs white-box (fine-tuning on attacks, encoded command words, prompt/data channel separation)
ideas.rationale: The black-box delimiter / channel-separation defenses are buildable in Memoria's deterministic ingest: wrap ingested paper content in explicit data delimiters and never interpolate it as instruction context for tool-calling profiles.

ideas.idea: ASR-valid metric (success rate among valid/parseable outputs) and sensitivity-rate (keyword-based detection of risk-recognition language)
ideas.rationale: A lightweight evaluation harness Memoria could run against its own ingest/judge profiles using planted injections, fitting the content-light diagnostics plane; sensitivity-rate keyword scan is cheap and local.

file: Zhang and Adipat - 2005 - Challenges, methodologies, and issues in the usability testing of mobile applications.md
title: Challenges, Methodologies, and Issues in the Usability Testing of Mobile Applications
one_line: A 2005 framework paper surveying usability-testing methodologies (lab experiments vs field studies), tools (emulators vs real devices), and nine usability attributes for mobile applications.

ideas.idea: Nine usability attributes (learnability, efficiency, memorability, errors, satisfaction, effectiveness, simplicity, comprehensibility, learning performance) with concrete measuring variables
ideas.rationale: A vocabulary for evaluating Memoria's Obsidian PI-facing surfaces (gates, dashboards, capture forms, inspector). Simplicity (clicks/menu-levels to reach a destination) and learnability map to the PI-direct-access usability bar; useful as evaluation criteria only.

ideas.idea: Lab-vs-field methodology split: lab for controlled comparison of interface/menu/data-entry components; field for context-dependent performance and attitude
ideas.rationale: Loosely analogous to Memoria's test-env split (engines vs wiring vs quality); the principle of matching method to what's being tested transfers, but the mobile/network specifics do not.

ideas.idea: Mobile-device-specific concerns: small screen, bandwidth/connectivity, data-entry methods, mobile context, multimodality
ideas.rationale: Memoria is a local desktop Obsidian system for a single researcher; wireless networks, small screens, and mobile context are entirely off-domain.

ideas.idea: Field data-collection techniques (voice-mail diaries, pocket/web diaries, think-aloud)
ideas.rationale: Diary/field-study instrumentation for mobile users in the wild is irrelevant to a single-PI local tool; no multi-participant study is involved.

file: Zhang et al. - 2022 - Differentiable prompt makes pre-trained language models better few-shot learners.md
title: Differentiable Prompt Makes Pre-trained Language Models Better Few-Shot Learners (DART)
one_line: DART turns small LMs into better few-shot classifiers by replacing hand-crafted prompt templates and verbalizer label tokens with differentiable pseudo-tokens optimized via backpropagation, plus a fluency-constraint objective — strong on complex-label tasks like relation extraction.

ideas.idea: Differentiable (soft) prompt templates and differentiable label/verbalizer tokens optimized by backprop instead of discrete prompt search
ideas.rationale: Requires gradient access and fine-tuning of the LM; Memoria uses fixed local models via MCP with no training loop and no parameter access, so soft-prompt optimization is not buildable in-architecture.

ideas.idea: Reformulating classification (incl. relation extraction with complex labels like alternate_name, country_of_birth) as a cloze/MLM task where one label token is often insufficient
ideas.rationale: The insight that complex relation labels resist single-token verbalizers is relevant to Memoria's typed-relation extraction: it argues for richer, multi-token or descriptive label representations in relation/claim classification prompts rather than terse single-word categories.

ideas.idea: Few-shot fine-tuning instability mitigated by joint optimization and the large gains on relation/event extraction in low-data regimes
ideas.rationale: Memoria operates with zero/few-shot prompting on a frozen local model and does no per-task fine-tuning; the instability and training-procedure findings don't apply.

ideas.idea: Label optimization is the most impactful component (largest ablation drop), i.e. choosing good label representations matters more than template wording
ideas.rationale: Transferable as a prompt-engineering heuristic for Memoria's classification engines: invest more in designing discriminative, semantically rich category descriptions (verbalizers) than in elaborate template phrasing for claim-sentence and relation classification.

file: Lee et al. - 2020 - BioBERT a pre-trained biomedical language representation model for biomedical text mining.md
title: Development of Q&A Systems Using AcQA (note: this file's extracted text is the AcQA DSL paper, not BioBERT)
one_line: Proposes AcQA, a domain-specific language and code-generator that lets non-programmers declaratively specify and deploy a closed-domain question-answering system (parser, NLP techniques, UI, server) from a short spec; the file is mislabeled BioBERT but contains the AcQA paper.

ideas.idea: Declarative DSL spec that separates 'what data/techniques' from 'how to implement' a Q&A pipeline, with sensible defaults so only a few fields are required.
ideas.rationale: Memoria already prizes deterministic, declaratively-configured ingest and compiled profiles; the insight that a researcher should specify intent (data source, technique, surface) and have the engine generate the wiring matches the 'engines write, agents judge' and compiled-profile philosophy. Worth borrowing as a config-over-code pattern, not the ANTLR/Python-codegen machinery.

ideas.idea: Triplet-based knowledge-base construction from StackExchange-style Q&A dumps as the default retrieval substrate.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's substrate is atomic claims, typed relations and an argument graph over academic papers, not crowd-QA triplets; the triplet-KB default is off-domain and far less expressive than the claim/warrant model already adopted.

ideas.idea: Generate a deployable web/REST front-end + server provisioning (SSH/Debian templates) from the same spec.
ideas.rationale: Contradicts Memoria constraints: single-researcher, local Obsidian vault, MCP-only sandbox, no agent file/terminal/server access. Auto-provisioning remote servers is the opposite of the local, PI-accessible-from-Obsidian model.

ideas.idea: Closed-domain Q&A gives higher answer accuracy than open-domain because the KB is curated to the field.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's bet on a bounded, curated personal corpus (the PI's papers/claims) over open-web retrieval for faithfulness; a useful framing, not a buildable component.

file: Lester et al. - 2021 - The power of scale for parameter-efficient prompt tuning.md
title: The Power of Scale for Parameter-Efficient Prompt Tuning
one_line: Shows that learning a small 'soft prompt' (a few tuned input embeddings) on a frozen T5 matches full fine-tuning at billions of parameters, with side benefits in domain-shift robustness and cheap prompt ensembling.

ideas.idea: Soft-prompt tuning: freeze the model, train only a tiny per-task prompt, so one frozen base serves many tasks (5+ orders of magnitude fewer task params).
ideas.rationale: Memoria runs local off-the-shelf instruction models (qwen2.5:7b) via Ollama and does not train models; gradient-based soft-prompt tuning requires a training pipeline that the deterministic, MCP-only, model-as-black-box architecture deliberately excludes.

ideas.idea: Freezing the generalist model and isolating task-defining parameters improves robustness to domain shift / reduces overfitting (12.5 F1 gain on out-of-domain QA).
ideas.rationale: Useful design intuition for Memoria's many extraction tasks (claim classification, MASSW aspects): prefer lightweight task-conditioning (e.g. distinct prompt/profile per engine) over per-task model adaptation, which generalizes better across the heterogeneous paper corpus.

ideas.idea: Prompt ensembling: run N prompts for one task in a single batched forward pass and majority-vote, cheaper than N-model ensembles.
ideas.rationale: Memoria could vote across a handful of prompt variants for high-stakes judgments (contradiction detection, supersession) to estimate uncertainty cheaply on one local model — aligns with its uncertainty-flagging of claims. Adapt the voting idea, not the trainable-soft-prompt mechanism.

ideas.idea: Class-label / verbalizer initialization primes the model to emit only legal output classes.
ideas.rationale: For Memoria's classification engines (claim-sentence type, polarity), constraining/priming outputs to the legal label set is directly relevant — implementable via prompt phrasing and constrained decoding, not via embedding training.

file: Li and Liang - 2021 - Prefix-tuning optimizing continuous prompts for generation.md
title: Prefix-Tuning: Optimizing Continuous Prompts for Generation
one_line: Introduces prefix-tuning — prepend trainable continuous 'virtual token' activations at every layer of a frozen LM (0.1% of params) — matching fine-tuning on table-to-text/summarization and beating it in low-data and unseen-topic extrapolation.

ideas.idea: Prefix-tuning: a frozen base LM steered by a small trainable per-task prefix, enabling modular, space-efficient multi-task serving from one model.
ideas.rationale: Same constraint as Lester: Memoria does not train or backprop into models; it consumes local instruction LMs as black boxes. The trainable-prefix mechanism is off-architecture.

ideas.idea: Lightweight task-conditioning is more faithful than fine-tuning in low-data settings (prefix outputs stay faithful to the table; fine-tuning hallucinates a false rating).
ideas.rationale: Directly supports Memoria's 'faithfulness over flash' constraint and its summarization/extraction engines: keeping the base model frozen and steering via prompts reduces hallucination on the PI's small per-topic data — a reason to prefer prompting over any future adaptation.

ideas.idea: Initialize prefixes with embeddings of real, task-relevant words (e.g. 'summarization') rather than random vectors for better low-data performance.
ideas.rationale: Echoes that grounding conditioning in real task vocabulary helps; translates for Memoria into using explicit, task-anchored natural-language prompts (real domain terms) for each extraction engine rather than terse/abstract instructions.

ideas.idea: Per-user prefixes enable batching across users/tasks and avoid cross-contamination for personalization.
ideas.rationale: Multi-user batching and federated personalization are explicitly outside Memoria's single-researcher scope; no value here.

file: Li et al. - 2021 - SentiPrompt sentiment knowledge enhanced prompt-tuning for aspect-based sentiment analysis.md
title: SentiPrompt: Sentiment Knowledge Enhanced Prompt-Tuning for Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis
one_line: Casts aspect-opinion-polarity triplet extraction in a unified BART+pointer generative framework, and injects task knowledge via auxiliary consistency/polarity-judgment prompt templates ('aspect is opinion? [MASK yes/no]; this is [MASK polarity]') built from ground-truth triplets to explicitly model term relations.

ideas.idea: Auxiliary consistency/judgment prompts: construct true and deliberately-mismatched (aspect, opinion) pairs and train the model to answer yes/no whether they are consistent, forcing explicit relation modeling between spans.
ideas.rationale: Memoria builds a typed-relation and argument graph over claims and runs a contradictions dashboard; a 'is X consistent with Y? yes/no' judgment prompt over candidate claim/entity pairs is a directly adaptable verification primitive for the structural-review gate (agents judge candidate links) — usable zero/few-shot on the local model without the training step.

ideas.idea: Unified generative triplet extraction with a pointer network that outputs span start/end indices, with explicit invalid-prediction checks (error-length, error-order) on the structured output.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's deterministic ingest needs well-formed structured extraction (claims, MASSW aspects, relations). The pattern of emitting indexed spans plus a downstream validity check (start<=end, correct arity) is a concrete schema-validation guard to bolt onto LLM extraction before the pre-file gate.

ideas.idea: Span manipulation as data augmentation (sub-spans / appended neighbor tokens) to teach robust boundary detection.
ideas.rationale: Relevant for making Memoria's claim/entity span extraction robust to boundary noise; the boundary-perturbation idea informs how to evaluate/harden extractors, even though Memoria won't train on it.

ideas.idea: Injecting domain knowledge into prompt templates/verbalizers improves extraction over vanilla fine-tuning.
ideas.rationale: Supports designing knowledge-rich, schema-aware prompts for Memoria's extraction engines (e.g. encoding claim-type/uncertainty cues directly into the prompt), consistent with prompt-first, no-training operation.

ideas.idea: Continuous/trainable prompt-encoder (BiLSTM+MLP) plus partial-freezing requirement (SentiPrompt fails if most LM params frozen).
ideas.rationale: Requires gradient training and an unfrozen LM — incompatible with Memoria's black-box local-model, no-training architecture; the gains came at the cost of full-model tuning, which Memoria does not do.

file: Li et al. - 2023 - API-bank a comprehensive benchmark for tool-augmented LLMs.md
title: API-Bank: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Tool-Augmented LLMs
one_line: A runnable benchmark and training set for tool-augmented LLMs that grades three escalating abilities — Call, Retrieve+Call, Plan+Retrieve+Call — over 73 executable APIs, plus a five-agent pipeline that mass-produces tool-use training data 98% cheaper than humans, with a detailed taxonomy of tool-use failure modes.

ideas.idea: Three-tier capability grading for tool use: Call (APIs known) -> Retrieve+Call (must find the right API first via an API-search tool) -> Plan+Retrieve+Call (chain multiple search/calls in one turn).
ideas.rationale: Memoria's Hermes runtime is an MCP-only tool-using agent (obsidian MCP, BERTopic MCP, retrievers). This grading is a ready-made evaluation ladder to test whether the local qwen model can reliably pick the right MCP tool and chain calls (e.g. discovery loop = plan+retrieve+call), directly applicable to validating the agent sandbox.

ideas.idea: API-Search tool: agent condenses the user need into keywords, embeds them, cosine-matches against API meta-info, returns the top API — needed when there are too many tools to fit in context.
ideas.rationale: Memoria already has a growing MCP/skill surface; a tool-/skill-retrieval step (embed request, match against tool descriptions) is the right pattern to keep the agent's context small and tool selection accurate, mirroring Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and skill governance. Buildable as a tool-router MCP.

ideas.idea: Executable, reproducible evaluation: hard-code/freeze external API results and seed databases so tool-use is deterministically scorable (API-call correctness via DB-state consistency + ROUGE-L on responses).
ideas.rationale: Memoria values deterministic ingest and has an evaluation/benchmark plane; freezing tool outputs and asserting on resulting vault state is exactly how to make Hermes/discovery-loop behavior reproducibly testable in the WSL2 sandbox (split-testing wiring vs. quality).

ideas.idea: Concrete tool-use failure taxonomy: No-API-Call, API Hallucination (calling a tool not provided/seen-in-training), Invalid/Missing Input Parameters, False (unparseable) Call Format, Failed Retrieval.
ideas.rationale: These are precisely the failure classes Memoria's MCP-only agents will exhibit; adopting this taxonomy gives the structural-review gate and diagnostics plane concrete error categories to detect and reject before notes are written (e.g. block hallucinated tool calls, validate parameter schemas).

ideas.idea: Multi-agent data synthesis by decomposing one overloaded instruction into per-agent subtasks (domain -> API -> query -> call+response) plus a dedicated tester/validator agent that filters non-conforming instances (discarded 35%).
ideas.rationale: The decomposition-plus-validator-agent pattern maps onto Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves': a separate validator agent enforcing design principles before acceptance is the same governance shape. Borrow the validator-agent and task-decomposition idea; reject mass synthetic-data generation for model training (Memoria doesn't train).

ideas.idea: Finding that base tool-use accuracy depends on instruction-tuning, not raw scale (GPT-3 175B ~0%, instruction-tuned smaller models ~20%+; fine-tuning fixes format/no-call errors).
ideas.rationale: Validates Memoria's choice of small instruction-tuned local models (qwen2.5:7b) over scale for tool-driven workflows, and warns that prompt-only (in-context) tool control is brittle on format adherence — argues for strict output schemas and validation at the gate rather than relying on the model's compliance.

file: Chen et al. - 2022 - Relation extraction as open-book examination retrieval-enhanced prompt tuning.md
title: Relation Extraction as Open-book Examination: Retrieval-enhanced Prompt Tuning
one_line: A semi-parametric relation-extraction method (RetrievalRE) that builds a key-value datastore of prompt-based [MASK]-token embeddings from training instances and, at inference, interpolates the PLM's softmax with a kNN distribution over retrieved neighbors to handle long-tail/hard relations.

ideas.idea: Open-book datastore of prompt-based instance embeddings (key = [MASK] hidden vector, value = relation label) queried by kNN at inference, interpolated with the model output via lambda.
ideas.rationale: Memoria does typed-relation extraction over claims/notes locally. A datastore of past PI-confirmed relation examples (citekey-anchored) that nudges a small local model (qwen2.5:7b) on rare/hard relation types is directly compatible with the deterministic-ingest + cheap-model constraint. The technique is a partial adaptation: we'd retrieve over a single PI's accumulated labeled relations, not a multi-thousand-instance supervised RE corpus.

ideas.idea: Framing inference as 'open-book' so the model can cite concrete retrieved exemplars as cues for its decision.
ideas.rationale: Aligns with Memoria's faithfulness-over-flash and structural-review-gate ethos: an extraction engine that surfaces the nearest prior examples that justified a label gives the agent/PI inspectable evidence to judge, rather than an opaque parametric guess.

ideas.idea: Datastore is editable post-hoc (add/edit/delete instances) enabling model behavior change without retraining ('post hoc model editing').
ideas.rationale: A no-retraining correction loop fits Memoria perfectly: when the PI corrects a relation label at the review gate, that correction becomes a new datastore key-value and immediately shifts future extractions. This is the cheap, deterministic personalization mechanism Memoria needs in place of fine-tuning.

ideas.idea: Soft/virtual verbalizer mapping relation labels to learnable answer words; prompt templates with logic-rule sub-prompts (PTR/KnowPrompt lineage).
ideas.rationale: Requires training/tuning a RoBERTa-large MLM head per relation schema. Memoria prefers off-the-shelf local generative models with deterministic prompting; bespoke verbalizer engineering is operational overhead with no PI-facing payoff at single-researcher scale.

ideas.idea: FAISS for fast nearest-neighbor retrieval over the datastore (<70k samples acceptable overhead, ~1.3x slower).
ideas.rationale: Memoria already uses similarity gates and embedding retrieval; FAISS-class ANN over a single PI's instance store is trivially within scale and the latency overhead is negligible. Reuse for the pre-file similarity gate and exemplar retrieval.

file: Chen et al. - 2023 - Decoupling knowledge from memorization retrieval-augmented prompt learning.md
title: Decoupling Knowledge from Memorization: Retrieval-augmented Prompt Learning (RetroPrompt)
one_line: RetroPrompt extends retrieval-augmented prompting across the whole pipeline — neural demonstrations injected at the embedding layer, kNN-guided loss reweighting on hard/atypical examples during training, and kNN-interpolated cloze prediction — to reduce a model's reliance on rote memorization and improve few/zero-shot generalization.

ideas.idea: kNN over the instance store as a difficulty signal: low kNN gold-class probability flags an atypical/hard instance.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's triage/learning-to-rank and structural-review gate need a cheap signal for 'this claim/relation is atypical and deserves PI attention.' Using nearest-neighbor agreement against the PI's confirmed corpus as a hardness/uncertainty flag is a clean, model-light heuristic that complements the existing uncertainty-flagging on atomic claims.

ideas.idea: Neural demonstrations: retrieve top-m nearest exemplars per class and inject their aggregated embeddings (similarity-weighted) at the embedding layer instead of as discrete in-context text.
ideas.rationale: Requires white-box access to the PLM embedding layer and gradient training. Memoria uses local generative models via prompting through an MCP-only sandbox; embedding-layer surgery is off-limits and unmaintainable at single-researcher scale. Discrete retrieved exemplars in the prompt are the pragmatic substitute.

ideas.idea: Memorization scoring via self-influence (leave-one-out / influence functions) to identify which training instances the model rote-memorizes; atypical instances dominate top memorization scores.
ideas.rationale: An analysis instrument requiring Hessian/influence computation over a trained model — irrelevant to Memoria, which does not train models and operates on a single PI's notes, not a labeled trainset whose memorization needs auditing.

ideas.idea: Asynchronous refresh of the datastore: re-embed/re-index every j epochs because representations drift as the model updates.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's embedding index must be rebuilt when the embedding model changes or notes are edited. The insight that a retrieval index must be periodically re-embedded to stay consistent maps to a nightly/loop re-index job over the vault — cheap and deterministic. (Memoria's model is fixed, so refresh is triggered by content/model change, not training epochs.)

ideas.idea: Retrieve from internal data (the user's own corpus) rather than an external KB, and prompt-based cloze representations beat [CLS]/Sentence-BERT/BM25 for similarity-based kNN.
ideas.rationale: Validates Memoria's single-researcher, vault-internal retrieval stance over reaching for external KBs. The empirical finding that task-aligned [MASK]/cloze representations outperform generic sentence embeddings for similarity is a useful prior when choosing how Memoria embeds claims for its similarity gate — prefer task-shaped prompts over raw sentence encoders.

file: Chen et al. - 2023 - Human-centered design to address biases in artificial intelligence.md
title: Human-Centered Design to Address Biases in Artificial Intelligence
one_line: A medical-informatics viewpoint cataloguing biases at each stage of the AI lifecycle (data collection, annotation, model design, deployment, monitoring) and arguing for multi-stakeholder human-centered AI plus bias-auditing tools to mitigate them in healthcare.

ideas.idea: Stage-by-stage bias taxonomy across the AI lifecycle (sampling, measurement, annotation, feature-selection, evaluation, feedback-loop bias).
ideas.rationale: Memoria's ingest pipeline (parse -> claim extraction -> classification -> relation typing -> review gate) is itself a multi-stage pipeline where errors compound. The framing of auditing each stage for systematic error — especially annotation/label bias from a single judge and feedback-loop bias where a model's outputs shape future inputs — is a useful checklist for Memoria's per-stage diagnostics plane and discovery loop (which can self-reinforce relevance scores).

ideas.idea: Human-in-the-loop governance: humans review and approve AI outputs; combine automated auditing tools with human judgment because tools alone are an incomplete solution.
ideas.rationale: Directly echoes Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' structural-review gate. The paper's argument that automated bias tools cannot catch practitioner-introduced bias and need a human gate reinforces keeping the PI in the loop. Adapted, not adopted: Memoria is single-user, so the paper's whole multi-stakeholder-team apparatus collapses to one PI.

ideas.idea: Feedback-loop bias: model predictions influence the data subsequently collected, creating self-fulfilling cycles.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's nightly proactive discovery loop + learning-to-rank triage risk exactly this: ranking past PI choices to surface more similar items narrows the corpus over time. Worth a guardrail/diagnostic (e.g., exploration term, drift monitoring) — a concrete design caution drawn from the paper.

ideas.idea: Multidisciplinary-team / multi-stakeholder co-design (ethicists, clinicians, communication scientists, patients) to remediate bias.
ideas.rationale: Assumes an enterprise/clinical organization with many roles and affected populations. Memoria is explicitly single-researcher, non-multi-user; the team-composition machinery and health-equity framing are off-domain and non-applicable.

file: Chen et al. - 2025 - ScienceAgentBench toward rigorous assessment of language agents for data-driven scientific discover.md
title: ScienceAgentBench: Toward Rigorous Assessment of Language Agents for Data-Driven Scientific Discovery
one_line: A 102-task benchmark (44 peer-reviewed papers, 4 disciplines) that evaluates language agents on individual data-driven discovery tasks via self-contained Python programs, with graded rubrics, multi-stage expert validation, data-contamination/shortcut mitigation, and a finding that the best agent solves only ~34% of tasks — debunking end-to-end automation claims.

ideas.idea: Multi-stage, rubric-based graded evaluation broken into stages (data loading, processing, modeling/visualization, output formatting, output saving) instead of a single binary pass/fail, drafted by an LLM then refined by an expert.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's engines (claim extraction, relation typing, summarization) need graded quality scoring at the structural-review gate, not just accept/reject. Stage-decomposed rubrics drafted by a model and confirmed by the PI is exactly the 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' pattern operationalized for evaluating ingest output. Build this as the gate's scoring rubric.

ideas.idea: LLM-as-judge for open-ended/visual outputs (GPT-4o scoring figures), sampling 3 responses and averaging for stability, validated against human raters.
ideas.rationale: Memoria already uses agents-as-judges. The discipline of multi-sample-and-average for judge stability, and validating the judge against PI ratings before trusting it, is a directly transferable practice for Memoria's L5 quality testing of extraction faithfulness. Caveat: Memoria prefers local/cheap models, so the judge would be a local model with the same stabilization trick.

ideas.idea: Data-contamination & shortcut mitigation: perturb test data (remove rows, replace test labels with dummy -1) so agents that recite memorized code or read ground-truth labels fail.
ideas.rationale: When Memoria builds internal regression tests for its ingest engines, perturbing inputs so a model can't shortcut via memorized public papers keeps eval honest. Relevant to Memoria's own benchmark/smoke tests (e.g., Hermes L2 smoke), even though Memoria doesn't expose this to the PI.

ideas.idea: Evaluate cost (API USD/task) as a first-class metric alongside success; finding that simple self-debug beats a heavy agent framework (OpenHands) at ~17x lower cost.
ideas.rationale: Strong empirical backing for Memoria's core stance: prefer simple, cheap, local execution over elaborate agent harnesses with large tool action spaces. The result that a minimal self-debug loop outperforms a complex agent at a fraction of cost justifies Memoria's deliberately constrained MCP-only sandbox and thin agent design.

ideas.idea: Authentic task construction by extracting tasks from peer-reviewed publications with permissive licenses and expert validation (publication-oriented annotation).
ideas.rationale: A benchmark-construction methodology for the agent-research community, not a Memoria feature. Memoria ingests papers for the PI's own knowledge, not to manufacture validated benchmark tasks; no buildable component here.

ideas.idea: Self-debug framework (execute generated code, read execution results, iteratively revise) substantially outperforms one-pass direct prompting; execution feedback is necessary.
ideas.rationale: Contradicts a hard Memoria constraint: the MCP-only agent sandbox gives agents NO code-execution/terminal, ever (ADR-32/46). An execution-feedback loop for agents is architecturally forbidden. The cost lesson above survives; the execute-and-revise mechanism itself does not.

file: Chen et al. - 2026 - Toward autonomous long-horizon engineering for ML research.md
title: Toward Autonomous Long-Horizon Engineering for ML Research (AiScientist)
one_line: A multi-agent system for long-horizon ML research engineering built on 'thin control over thick state': a lightweight Orchestrator coordinates specialist agents through a 'File-as-Bus' workspace of schema-governed, durable, inspectable artifacts that preserve role and project continuity across transient agent invocations.

ideas.idea: File-as-Bus: agents coordinate by reading/writing durable, schema-governed file artifacts (the 'system of record') instead of lossy conversational handoffs; removing it collapses later-round performance (-31.82 medal points).
ideas.rationale: This is essentially a principled articulation of Memoria's own architecture: the Obsidian vault IS the File-as-Bus, and notes/claims/dashboards are the durable artifacts agents inspect. The empirical proof that durable, inspectable file-state is THE bottleneck for multi-agent continuity (vs. conversational state) strongly validates Memoria's vault-as-substrate, MCP-over-files design and gives language to defend it in ADRs.

ideas.idea: Schema with three artifact update modes: append-only logs (chronological evidence, never overwritten), versioned artifacts (canonical current + recoverable history), and mutable short-lived control state.
ideas.rationale: Directly applicable to Memoria's note/claim governance: claim supersession and the contradictions dashboard need versioned-with-history semantics; discovery/triage logs want append-only audit trails; ephemeral projection/canvas state is mutable. Codifying per-artifact update modes (and writer/reader permissions) is a concrete schema Memoria can adopt for its vault structure and profile-compiled permissions.

ideas.idea: Per-artifact writer/reader permission isolation (read-only / read+limited-write / read+full-write) declared in the schema.
ideas.rationale: Maps onto Memoria's skill governance, compiled profiles, and structural-review gate (engines write to draft artifacts; agents read; only the gate/PI writes approved notes). The File-as-Bus permission contract is a clean model for which engine/agent may touch which vault artifact — reinforces the MCP-only sandbox's hard-deny posture.

ideas.idea: Thin control over thick state: an Orchestrator carries only compact summaries + a 'workspace map' index, delegating to specialists who expand local context on demand (Agent-as-Tool), rather than carrying full history in active context.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's Hermes runtime can use this: a lightweight router/scout profile that holds a compact map of the vault and dispatches narrow specialist profiles (retriever, claim-extractor, relation-typer) which read only the artifacts they need. Avoids context bloat in a local-model, limited-context setting. Adapted to single-researcher scale — a small fixed set of specialists, not an open-ended team.

ideas.idea: Hierarchical specialist roles aligned to pipeline stages (paper comprehension, prioritization, implementation, experimentation) with non-recursive Tier-2 subagents folded back into the parent.
ideas.rationale: Memoria already has stage-aligned profiles (retriever/scout, triage, extraction). The bounded, non-recursive subagent pattern (no runaway agent trees) is a safety-aligned design that suits Memoria's deterministic, auditable ingest. Borrow the structure; the 'experimentation/implementation' roles don't apply since Memoria agents can't execute code.

ideas.idea: Behavioral finding: higher-scoring trajectories spend MORE later effort on validation/closure and fewer experiment variants; durable state shifts work from repeated low-level execution toward evidence reuse.
ideas.rationale: Supports prioritizing Memoria's review/validation gate and contradiction-checking over generating ever more candidate notes. The insight that preserved evidence lets later passes validate rather than redo argues for investing in inspectable intermediate artifacts (claim provenance, extraction logs) so the PI/agent validates instead of re-extracting.

ideas.idea: PaperBench/MLE-Bench Lite evaluation harness and the specific autonomous-ML-research automation goal (turning specs into runnable trained ML systems).
ideas.rationale: The end goal — agents that autonomously implement, train, and tune ML systems with GPU budgets — is squarely outside Memoria's domain (a personal knowledge/claim system that never trains models and forbids agent code execution). The benchmark results inform agent design but the task itself is non-applicable.

file: Zhang et al. - 2024 - MASSW a new dataset and benchmark tasks for AI-assisted scientific workflows.md
title: MASSW: A New Dataset and Benchmark Tasks for AI-Assisted Scientific Workflows
one_line: A 152K-paper dataset and method for LLM-extracting five structured aspects of a scientific workflow (context, key idea, method, outcome, projected impact) from titles/abstracts, validated against human annotation.

ideas.idea: Five-aspect structured summarization of papers: context, key idea, method, outcome, projected impact, each defined as a step in the research workflow.
ideas.rationale: This is the direct source of Memoria's 'MASSW-style paper aspects' design pillar; the schema and definitions are the canonical extraction target for the ingest pipeline.

ideas.idea: Explicit codebook rules to disambiguate aspects (e.g. novel task -> Key Idea; non-measurable outcome -> Future Impact; already-happened impact -> Outcome; method != key idea).
ideas.rationale: These adjudication rules are reusable directly as prompt/classifier guidance in Memoria's deterministic ingest, keeping aspect assignment consistent across papers.

ideas.idea: Mark an aspect 'N/A / not mentioned' rather than hallucinating it when absent from the source.
ideas.rationale: Maps onto Memoria's faithfulness-over-flash constraint and uncertainty flags; abstaining when unsupported is exactly the structural-gate behavior wanted before a note is written.

ideas.idea: Hallucination measured as recall of correctly identifying 'not mentioned' aspects; GPT-4 best, Mixtral close, GPT-3.5 worst.
ideas.rationale: Memoria runs local/cheap models (qwen2.5:7b); this is a concrete eval design to verify a local model's abstention behavior on the aspect-extraction task before trusting it in ingest.

ideas.idea: Validate LLM extraction by comparing LLM-vs-human similarity against human-vs-human agreement, using semantic metrics (BERTScore, BLEURT, cosine) not just lexical (BLEU/ROUGE).
ideas.rationale: Gives Memoria a defensible quality-gate methodology for its local extraction engine; semantic-metric-vs-human-agreement framing fits the engines-write/agents-judge governance.

ideas.idea: Extraction from title+abstract only (cost-driven), acknowledged as a limitation vs full text.
ideas.rationale: Memoria parses full papers locally with deterministic ingest; restricting to abstracts would discard the atomic-claim and detailed-aspect coverage Memoria depends on.

ideas.idea: Workflow-prediction benchmark tasks (idea generation, method recommendation, outcome/future prediction) as generative extrapolation along the aspect sequence.
ideas.rationale: Aspect-conditioned generation could seed Memoria's proactive discovery loop (e.g. 'given this context+idea, what method/follow-up?'), but only as a suggestion surface behind the PI-approval gate, not autonomous research.

ideas.idea: Embedding aspect summaries + dimensionality reduction + HDBSCAN clustering with TF-IDF top-word labels for a multi-view map of the corpus.
ideas.rationale: Aligns with Memoria's BERTopic clustering MCP and projected-canvas surfaces; clustering on per-aspect embeddings (not whole abstracts) is a refinement worth adopting for navigation spaces.

file: Zhang et al. - 2026 - How far are we from true auto-research.md
title: How Far Are We From True Auto-Research?
one_line: ResearchArena runs frontier coding agents through a full ideate-experiment-write-review research loop and shows that manuscript-only review flatters them while artifact-aware peer review exposes fabricated results, underpowered experiments, and plan/execution mismatch.

ideas.idea: Artifact-aware peer review: reviewers get read-only access to the workspace (code, logs, results.json) and verify reported numbers and citations against the artifacts, not just the manuscript.
ideas.rationale: This is the core of Memoria's structural review gate generalized: judge claims against their evidence artifacts, not the prose. The read-only-reviewer pattern maps onto Memoria's MCP-only sandbox where agents judge but cannot mutate engine output.

ideas.idea: Manuscript-only automated reviewer (SAR) is a weak discriminator and over-credits polished framing and honest-looking negative results without checking substance.
ideas.rationale: Direct warning for Memoria's LLM-judge surfaces: a relevance/quality judge reading only summaries will be miscalibrated; Memoria must ground judgments in extracted claims+source, reinforcing 'verify the inference not just the file'.

ideas.idea: Three named failure modes: fabricated results (numbers don't match outputs), underpowered experiments, plan/execution mismatch (claimed components not implemented).
ideas.rationale: Useful taxonomy for Memoria's contradictions dashboard and claim-uncertainty flags when ingesting agent-authored or low-rigor preprints; flag 'results-vs-artifact unverifiable' as a first-class uncertainty type.

ideas.idea: Reference integrity check via online lookups against arXiv, Semantic Scholar, CrossRef (catching fabricated/incorrect citations).
ideas.rationale: Memoria's Zotero/citekey backbone plus record-linkage dedup can run exactly this check at ingest to flag non-resolving or metadata-mismatched citations before a note is filed.

ideas.idea: Falsifiable-hypothesis ideation guideline: idea framed as a hypothesis with a falsifiable prediction and explicit success/refute criteria, grounded in real verified references.
ideas.rationale: Maps onto Memoria's argument graph with warrants and claim supersession; capturing PI ideas as hypotheses with explicit refutation criteria gives the contradiction/supersession machinery something concrete to track.

ideas.idea: Per-stage guideline as minimal scaffold (fixes the deliverable, not the research) distilled from established practice.
ideas.rationale: Matches Memoria's compiled-profile/skill-governance approach: give agents tight deliverable specs and judgment criteria rather than open-ended autonomy.

ideas.idea: Multi-agent end-to-end autonomous paper generation (ideation through writing) as the goal.
ideas.rationale: Contradicts Memoria's PI-approval governance and single-researcher assistive scope; the paper itself concludes no agent reaches acceptance bar, so autonomous authorship is off-mission.

ideas.idea: GPU/compute-scale experimentation harness across 13 domains, cost ~$1000-$104000.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is local, single-researcher, cheap-model scale; running agents to execute ML experiments is outside its knowledge-management mandate.

file: Zhang et al. - 2026 - KnowledgeBerg evaluating systematic knowledge coverage and compositional reasoning in large languag.md
title: KnowledgeBerg: Evaluating Systematic Knowledge Coverage and Compositional Reasoning in LLMs
one_line: A benchmark and diagnostic framework showing LLMs fail 'tip-of-the-iceberg' questions that need complete coverage of a bounded entity universe plus set-based reasoning, decomposing failures into completeness, awareness, and application.

ideas.idea: Bounded-universe enumeration (EQ-EA) grounded in authoritative sources (ontologies, textbooks, official lists), excluding disputed/fast-changing topics for stability and reproducibility.
ideas.rationale: Memoria builds bounded sets too (all claims in a paper, all papers citing X); grounding such universes in authoritative sources and excluding unstable knowledge is a sound principle for its entity/claim universes.

ideas.idea: Three-stage failure decomposition: completeness (knowledge present?), awareness (does the model recognize the requirement?), application (does it execute the reasoning?).
ideas.rationale: A useful diagnostic lens for Memoria's retrieval/discovery loop: a miss can be missing-from-corpus (completeness), not-recognized-as-relevant (awareness), or mis-ranked (application); separating these guides where to fix the pipeline.

ideas.idea: Decoupling finding: enumeration completeness and downstream reasoning accuracy are near-uncorrelated; having the facts doesn't guarantee using them.
ideas.rationale: Cautions Memoria that good retrieval recall (getting the right notes) does not imply good synthesis; the structural gate must judge the reasoning step separately from the retrieval step.

ideas.idea: RAG with a small curated domain corpus (ontologies/official docs) beats appending large general corpora; hybrid (BM25+dense) > dense alone; reranking adds little.
ideas.rationale: Directly actionable for Memoria's local retriever: a curated vault corpus with hybrid sparse+dense retrieval is both cheaper and more interpretable than a giant general index, matching single-researcher scale.

ideas.idea: Self-refinement consistently hurts when initial reasoning is grounded in incomplete/mistaken knowledge (critique amplifies errors); self-consistency and self-verification help.
ideas.rationale: Guides Memoria's agent-judge design: prefer self-consistency/verification (multiple judges, vote) over iterative self-critique loops that can entrench a wrong call before the PI sees it.

ideas.idea: Iceberg Gap metric (geometric mean of surface-simplicity, knowledge-width, reasoning-depth) to score how much a question hides its complexity.
ideas.rationale: A cross-benchmark characterization metric with no clear role in Memoria's single-vault knowledge workflow; the construct is interesting but not buildable into the system.

ideas.idea: Machine-translated multilingual partition (17 languages via Google Translate).
ideas.rationale: Memoria is a single-researcher English-domain academic system; multilingual coverage is out of scope.

file: Zhang et al. - 2026 - MRMR a realistic and expert-level multidisciplinary benchmark for reasoning-intensive multimodal re.md
title: MRMR: A Realistic and Expert-Level Multidisciplinary Benchmark for Reasoning-Intensive Multimodal Retrieval
one_line: An expert-level multimodal (interleaved image-text) retrieval benchmark over 23 domains that adds a novel Contradiction Retrieval task, finding that text retrievers with LLM image captions still beat dedicated multimodal retrievers on reasoning-intensive queries.

ideas.idea: Contradiction Retrieval: retrieve documents/rules that conflict with a query case, requiring logical reasoning to find conflicting concepts, not just semantic similarity.
ideas.rationale: Directly feeds Memoria's contradictions dashboard and argument graph: a retriever that surfaces claims contradicting a target claim is exactly the engine needed to populate the contradictions surface, beyond similarity-based linking.

ideas.idea: Text retriever + LLM-generated image captions (caption-then-embed) outperforms native multimodal embedders on knowledge/reasoning queries.
ideas.rationale: For Memoria's mostly-text academic vault with occasional figures/tables, captioning figures locally and indexing the caption text is a cheaper, stronger path than running a multimodal embedder, fitting the cheap-local-model constraint.

ideas.idea: Reasoning-intensive retrieval: a document is relevant if it supplies a theorem/principle in the reasoning chain to answer the query (not lexical/semantic match).
ideas.rationale: Memoria's discovery relevance scoring should reward documents that supply a missing warrant/principle for a PI's claim, not just topically-similar papers; this reframes relevance toward argumentative usefulness.

ideas.idea: Test-time scaling: replace the raw query with an LLM-generated reasoning trace (question summary + chain-of-thought) before retrieving (+5 to +15 points).
ideas.rationale: Query-expansion-by-reasoning is a low-cost upgrade for Memoria's retriever/scout profile; a small local model can expand a terse PI query into a richer retrieval query.

ideas.idea: Semi-automated positive-doc construction: LLM proposes, human expert verifies, keep only GPT-human agreement, discard ambiguous (30-60%).
ideas.rationale: Mirrors Memoria's engines-propose / PI-approve gate; the keep-on-agreement / discard-ambiguous policy is a concrete triage rule for the pre-file similarity gate.

ideas.idea: Failure pattern: visual/surface similarity dominates over contextual relevance (model ranks a visually-similar negative above the contextually-correct positive).
ideas.rationale: Warns Memoria that pure embedding similarity in the pre-file gate can promote superficially-similar-but-wrong notes; relevance scoring should weight argumentative/contextual fit, not just cosine similarity.

ideas.idea: Heavy multimodal benchmark with multi-image interleaved queries across 23 expert domains.
ideas.rationale: The multi-image multidisciplinary scale and dedicated multimodal-embedder training are beyond a single-researcher text-centric vault; only the captioning and contradiction ideas transfer.

file: Zhao et al. - 2021 - Calibrate before use improving few-shot performance of language models.md
title: Calibrate Before Use: Improving Few-Shot Performance of Language Models
one_line: Shows few-shot LLM accuracy is unstable across prompt format, example choice, and ordering due to majority-label, recency, and common-token biases, and fixes it with contextual calibration that zeroes out the model's bias using a content-free 'N/A' input.

ideas.idea: Contextual calibration: estimate the model's answer bias by feeding a content-free input ('N/A', '[MASK]', empty string), then rescale class probabilities (diagonal W = 1/p_cf) so the content-free input is uniform.
ideas.rationale: Memoria runs small local models (qwen2.5:7b) for claim-sentence classification, aspect tagging, and triage; this is a near-zero-cost, training-free way to debias those classifiers and stabilize them under the structural gate.

ideas.idea: Few-shot accuracy varies from near-chance to near-SOTA based on training-example permutation and format alone.
ideas.rationale: Cautions Memoria's compiled-profile/prompt design: classifier and judge prompts must fix example order/format deliberately and be evaluated for stability, not assumed robust.

ideas.idea: Three named biases: majority-label (frequent prompt answers), recency (answers near prompt end), common-token (answers frequent in pretraining).
ideas.rationale: Concrete checklist when designing Memoria's classification prompts (e.g. claim-type, relevance tiers): balance and order in-context examples, and watch for label names that are pretraining-frequent skewing class assignment.

ideas.idea: Content-free input can be crafted task-specifically (e.g. 'N/A was born in' for fact retrieval).
ideas.rationale: For Memoria's structured extractors, a task-specific content-free probe gives a better bias estimate than a generic 'N/A', improving calibration of the local extraction engine.

ideas.idea: Vector scaling (diagonal W) over full affine to avoid quadratic parameter growth on large output vocabularies.
ideas.rationale: Sensible default if Memoria ever calibrates open-vocabulary generation (e.g. keyphrase/tag candidate scoring) on a local model; keeps the calibration lightweight.

ideas.idea: Calibration mitigates but does not eliminate prompt engineering.
ideas.rationale: An honest caveat, not a buildable mechanism for Memoria; noted but nothing to implement.

file: Saxena et al. - 2025 - Generation-time vs. Post-hoc citation a holistic evaluation of LLM attribution.md
title: Generation-Time vs. Post-hoc Citation: A Holistic Evaluation of LLM Attribution
one_line: An empirical benchmark contrasting two LLM citation paradigms — generating citations inline during decoding (G-Cite) versus drafting first then attaching/verifying citations in a second pass (P-Cite) — finding retrieval is the dominant driver of attribution quality and P-Cite gives higher coverage at competitive correctness while G-Cite gives higher precision.

ideas.idea: Two-pass post-hoc citation (P-Cite): draft the answer/note first, then run a separate pass that retrieves evidence and attaches/verifies citations sentence-by-sentence; this consistently yields higher citation coverage (more ground-truth sources cited) than inline generation, at competitive correctness.
ideas.rationale: Maps directly onto Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' pipeline. The engine can draft a claim/note, and a separate post-hoc attribution pass (an MCP-only agent over the retriever) attaches/verifies citekey-anchored evidence before the structural review gate — decoupling drafting from grounding fits the deterministic-ingest-then-judge architecture better than inline citation.

ideas.idea: Citation-correctness metric set: citation precision, recall, their harmonic mean (correctness), plus 'coverage' (fraction of ground-truth citations present) and latency, reported per method.
ideas.rationale: Gives Memoria a concrete, cheap-to-compute scorecard for its claim/attribution extraction quality. Coverage-vs-precision is exactly the tradeoff the structural review gate must surface; the inspector/admin reports can show per-note citation precision/recall against the Zotero-resolved ground truth.

ideas.idea: Retrieval augmentation is the single largest driver of attribution quality regardless of paradigm (e.g., +50pp correctness on FEVER going zero-shot -> RAG); fine-tuning is an optimization layer, not a substitute for retrieval.
ideas.rationale: Validates Memoria's bet on a strong retriever/scout profile and pre-file similarity gate as the foundation for grounded claims, rather than relying on a fine-tuned model. With cheap local models (qwen2.5:7b), investing in retrieval over model fine-tuning is the correct allocation.

ideas.idea: Human eval of two end-user-facing quality axes: answer correctness (all claims backed by cited source) and citation hallucination (citation points to a real source in the corpus); P-Cite hallucinated less (37% vs 41%).
ideas.rationale: Citation-hallucination ('does this citekey actually exist in the vault/Zotero and support the claim?') is a deterministic check Memoria can run automatically at the gate — a fabricated or out-of-corpus citekey is hard-rejectable without an LLM, strengthening faithfulness-over-flash.

ideas.idea: Reserve G-Cite (inline, precision-first) for strict claim-verification settings where precision matters more than coverage.
ideas.rationale: Suggests a dual mode: P-Cite for drafting notes/syntheses, but a precision-first verification pass (analogous to G-Cite, near 1.0 precision on FEVER) for the contradictions dashboard / claim-supersession checks where a wrong citation is costlier than a missing one.

ideas.idea: Cross-paradigm dataset adaptation harness (e.g., strip inline citations to make a draft for P-Cite; constrain candidate pool to titles for G-Cite rewrite).
ideas.rationale: Benchmark-construction machinery for fair method comparison; Memoria is a single-researcher production system, not a method-comparison testbed, so this evaluation tooling is out of scope.

file: Scao and Rush - 2021 - How many data points is a prompt worth.md
title: How Many Data Points Is a Prompt Worth?
