one_line: A controlled study quantifying the value of a task-description prompt over a classifier head in fine-tuning, introducing an 'average data advantage' metric and finding a prompt is worth hundreds (sometimes thousands) of labeled data points, with prompt-wording choice mostly dominated by seed variance.

ideas.idea: A well-designed prompt/pattern injects task-specific human knowledge worth hundreds of labeled examples, especially in the low-data regime.
ideas.rationale: Directly justifies Memoria's design choice to encode the PI's task knowledge as carefully-worded prompts/patterns in its extraction engines (claim classification, MASSW aspects, sentence classification) rather than collecting/labeling training data — a single-researcher system has no labeling budget, so prompting is the right substitute.

ideas.idea: Prompt choice is mostly NOT a dominant hyperparameter: confidence intervals across different valid prompt wordings largely overlap, and seed/run variance usually outweighs wording differences.
ideas.rationale: Calibrates expectations for prompt tuning in Memoria's compiled profiles — better to spend effort on retrieval, ensembling, and the review gate than on micro-tuning prompt wording. But run-to-run variance is real, suggesting Memoria should treat a single LLM extraction as noisy (favoring self-consistency / multi-run agreement before writing notes).

ideas.idea: Null-verbalizer control: replace the meaningful answer word with a nonsense token to separate the prompt's inductive-bias benefit from the verbalizer's semantic content; the pattern still helps even without an informative verbalizer once enough data exists.
ideas.rationale: An analysis/ablation technique for understanding where prompting's benefit comes from in a fine-tuning study; Memoria does no head-vs-prompt fine-tuning, so the control has no operational use.

ideas.idea: Average-data-advantage metric: integrate the accuracy gap between two learning curves over the matched accuracy band, divided by band height, to express a method's benefit in equivalent-data-points units.
ideas.rationale: A research metric for comparing fine-tuning regimes across data scales; Memoria operates at fixed (tiny) data and a single model, so a data-advantage curve is not actionable.

file: Schick and Schütze - 2021 - Exploiting cloze questions for few shot text classification and natural language inference.md
title: Exploiting Cloze Questions for Few-Shot Text Classification and Natural Language Inference (PET)
one_line: Introduces Pattern-Exploiting Training (PET): reformulate classification as cloze questions via pattern-verbalizer pairs, fine-tune a small MLM per pattern, ensemble them to soft-label unlabeled data, then distill into one classifier — plus iPET, an iterative self-training variant — beating supervised and semi-supervised baselines in low-data settings.

ideas.idea: Pattern-verbalizer pairs (PVP): express a classification task as a cloze pattern plus a verbalizer mapping each label to a vocabulary word, letting a pretrained LM answer via masked-token probability instead of an opaque head.
ideas.rationale: The PVP abstraction is the conceptual ancestor of Memoria's prompt-based extraction (claim-sentence classification, MASSW aspects). Memoria uses generative LLM prompts rather than MLM cloze, but the discipline of 'one pattern that turns the task into the model's native form + an explicit label vocabulary' is a clean template for designing its classification prompts and constrained label outputs.

ideas.idea: Ensemble multiple PVPs (different patterns/verbalizers) then distill: combining several task formulations compensates for any single formulation the model understands poorly, and even beats the best single pattern.
ideas.rationale: Memoria can run several prompt phrasings for a hard classification (e.g., claim certainty / contradiction detection) and aggregate by confidence-weighted vote at the structural review gate, hardening against a single brittle prompt — without needing the distillation step (it has no training pipeline).

ideas.idea: Auxiliary language-modeling loss during fine-tuning to prevent catastrophic forgetting in the few-example regime.
ideas.rationale: Requires gradient-based fine-tuning of the LM, which Memoria does not do (local off-the-shelf models, deterministic ingest, no training loop). Out of scope for a prompt-only, MCP-sandboxed runtime.

ideas.idea: iPET self-training: train successive generations of models, each labeling a progressively larger unlabeled set for the next, gradually growing the training set while preferring high-confidence predictions to limit label noise.
ideas.rationale: A multi-generation training/bootstrapping procedure assuming a fine-tunable model and a large unlabeled pool; contradicts Memoria's no-training, single-researcher, deterministic-ingest constraints. The 'prefer high-confidence predictions' heuristic is already covered elsewhere by Memoria's gate.

ideas.idea: Confidence-proportional sampling of pseudo-labeled examples (well-calibrated-or-not, high-confidence predictions are more likely correct).
ideas.rationale: The general insight — gate machine-written outputs on model confidence before promoting them — supports Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and uncertainty-flagging of extracted claims, even though Memoria applies it to note-writing rather than to building a training set.

file: Schick and Schütze - 2021 - It's not just size that matters small language models are also few-shot learners.md
title: It's Not Just Size That Matters: Small Language Models Are Also Few-Shot Learners
one_line: Shows that PET/iPET with a small MLM (ALBERT, ~223M params) matches or beats GPT-3 (175B) on SuperGLUE with 32 examples, extends PET to multi-token verbalizers, and dissects why: combining patterns, compensating for bad wordings, using labeled data via gradients, and a bidirectional LM.

ideas.idea: A small, local model with the right task reformulation + gradient updates can match a 1000x-larger model relying on in-context priming, at a fraction of the compute/carbon.
ideas.rationale: This is the empirical backbone of Memoria's core constraint: prefer local cheap/open models (qwen2.5:7b) with good task framing over giant cloud models. It directly legitimizes the single-researcher, local-first, 'greener' architecture for extraction/classification tasks.

ideas.idea: Priming/in-context demonstrations do not scale beyond a few examples (context-window bound) and barely improve — sometimes worsen — performance versus one-shot; gradient/structured use of the few labels beats stuffing them into context.
ideas.rationale: Cautions Memoria against over-relying on long few-shot exemplar blocks in prompts for its local models. Since Memoria cannot fine-tune, the takeaway is to keep exemplars minimal and lean on retrieval + ensembling rather than packing many demonstrations into a small-context local model.

ideas.idea: Combining multiple patterns (p_comb) compensates for a single pattern the model fails to understand and even exceeds the best individual pattern; a key few-shot challenge is compensating for PVPs the LM doesn't grasp.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces the multi-prompt-ensemble idea for Memoria's brittle extraction tasks: run several phrasings and aggregate, since you cannot know in advance which wording a 7B local model parses well. Practical hedge against local-model fragility before the review gate.

ideas.idea: Multi-token / multi-mask verbalizer decoding (autoregressive max-first decoding, multi-class hinge training loss) to handle labels that aren't single tokens.
ideas.rationale: An MLM-specific decoding/training mechanism tied to masked-LM verbalizers; irrelevant to Memoria's generative-LLM, prompt-only extraction where multi-word outputs are produced natively. No fine-tuning, no mask decoding.

ideas.idea: PET/iPET sharply reduces fine-tuning instability — ensembling/distillation drives run-to-run standard deviation toward zero.
ideas.rationale: The general lesson — aggregate multiple noisy runs to stabilize output — transfers to Memoria using self-consistency over several local-model extractions to reduce variance before writing a note, even though Memoria stabilizes via voting rather than distillation.

ideas.idea: Bidirectionality of the underlying LM matters for tasks comparing two sequences (entailment), and pattern flexibility (mask not forced to the end).
ideas.rationale: An architecture-selection finding for MLM vs unidirectional models; Memoria uses fixed off-the-shelf generative models and doesn't choose architectures per task, so this is not actionable.

file: Schick et al. - 2020 - Automatically identifying words that can serve as labels for few-shot text classification.md
title: Automatically Identifying Words That Can Serve as Labels for Few-Shot Text Classification (PETAL)
one_line: PETAL automates PET's hardest manual step — choosing the label->word verbalizer — via a one-vs-rest likelihood-ratio search over candidate words with frequency-biased candidate sets and multi-verbalizers, nearly matching hand-crafted mappings with only 50 examples.

ideas.idea: Likelihood-ratio (not raw cross-entropy) criterion for picking label words: score a candidate by how much it distinguishes positive from the AVERAGE of other classes, avoiding the degenerate solution of picking globally-frequent-but-meaningless tokens.
ideas.rationale: Solves verbalizer-word selection for masked-LM PET fine-tuning, a problem Memoria does not have — it uses generative prompts with natural-language labels, not single-token MLM verbalizers. The likelihood-ratio trick is tied to MLM token scoring.

ideas.idea: Multi-verbalizers: assign several label words per class and average their scores, since one word rarely covers a label's full meaning (e.g., 'Science/Tech' = {Science, Tech}).
ideas.rationale: The underlying insight — a single category label often needs multiple lexical proxies — is mildly useful for Memoria's keyphrase tag candidates and BERTopic cluster labeling: expose several representative terms per topic/tag rather than forcing one. Weak/indirect transfer.

ideas.idea: Frequency-biased candidate filtering: prefer frequent words as labels because pretrained LMs understand frequent words better than rare ones.
ideas.rationale: A candidate-pruning heuristic specific to building MLM verbalizer vocabularies; Memoria's tag/topic labels come from the corpus and PI vocabulary, not from a frequency-filtered model vocabulary, so this doesn't apply.

ideas.idea: Automating the expert-knowledge step (verbalizer design) so the method works without task- or model-specific tuning, nearly matching manual mappings.
ideas.rationale: Goal is to remove human-in-the-loop from prompt construction; Memoria deliberately keeps the PI in the loop (PI approves) and treats prompt/pattern design as a curated, governed asset. Auto-deriving label words offers little here and runs against the human-judgment pillar.

file: Schick et al. - 2023 - Toolformer language models can teach themselves to use tools.md
title: Toolformer: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Use Tools
one_line: A self-supervised method where an LM annotates a corpus with API calls (calculator, QA, search, translation, calendar), keeps only calls that reduce next-token loss, and finetunes on them so the model learns when/how to call tools.

ideas.idea: Perplexity/loss-reduction filtering: keep an annotation only if providing it lowers the model's loss on downstream tokens (L(call+result) < min(L(no call), L(call only)) - tau)
ideas.rationale: A clean utility-of-evidence test. Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and discovery relevance scoring could borrow the principle of 'only keep an extracted/retrieved artifact if it measurably helps a downstream judgment' rather than keeping everything; useful for the structural review gate's accept/reject logic, though Memoria's gate is agent+PI judged, not loss-thresholded.

ideas.idea: Self-supervised tool-use learned by finetuning a base LM (no human annotation, no task-specific prompts)
ideas.rationale: Memoria uses local off-the-shelf models (qwen2.5:7b) via MCP and does not finetune; tool access is governed by compiled profiles and the MCP-only sandbox, not learned weights. Finetuning a 6.7B model on a CCNet subset is out of scope for single-researcher local scale.

ideas.idea: Treat external capabilities (calculator, retrieval, translation, calendar/date) as text-in/text-out APIs the model decides to invoke inline
ideas.rationale: Memoria already standardizes on MCP tools as the capability surface with hard deny on file/terminal/code-execution; the inline special-token API mechanism is superseded by structured tool-calling and would reintroduce free-form execution Memoria deliberately forbids.

ideas.idea: Calendar/date tool to give the model temporal awareness (used on TEMPLAMA/DATESET)
ideas.rationale: Memoria handles time via explicit claim supersession and temporal metadata in the vault, not via a model querying 'today's date'; the paper itself shows the calendar tool barely fires on real temporal-fact tasks.

file: Schirrmeister et al. - 2017 - Deep learning with convolutional neural networks for EEG decoding and visualization.md
title: Deep learning with convolutional neural networks for EEG decoding and visualization
one_line: Designs shallow/deep/residual ConvNets for end-to-end decoding of movement-related information from raw EEG, matching/surpassing FBCSP, plus input-perturbation visualizations of which frequency-band features the net uses.

ideas.idea: Input-perturbation network-prediction correlation maps: perturb a feature's amplitude and observe the change in model output to attribute causal contribution (vs. mere correlation)
ideas.rationale: A neural-signal interpretability technique on continuous EEG amplitudes; Memoria's explainability needs are over text claims/relations (argument graph, warrants, read-only inspector), not perturbation-based saliency on a CNN. No transfer.

ideas.idea: Cropped training with a tied-sample loss to force features stable across neighboring windows / augment scarce labeled data
ideas.rationale: A model-training augmentation strategy for low-data supervised classification of time series; Memoria does not train models and operates on documents, not signal windows.

ideas.idea: Validate a new method against a strong domain baseline (FBCSP) reproduced from published numbers before trusting it
ideas.rationale: Sound methodology but generic and already covered by Memoria's working principles (verify conclusions independently, live-test); the paper offers nothing domain-specific Memoria can build.

file: Schmidgall and Moor - 2025 - AgentRxiv towards collaborative autonomous research.md
title: AgentRxiv: Towards Collaborative Autonomous Research
one_line: A shared preprint server for autonomous agent labs that lets them upload/retrieve each other's reports via embedding-similarity search, enabling cumulative, iterative research improvement (e.g. +11.4% on MATH-500).

ideas.idea: A persistent, searchable archive of an agent's own prior research outputs that later runs retrieve from, so each generation builds on accumulated insights rather than starting fresh
ideas.rationale: Directly maps to Memoria's value proposition: the vault IS the cumulative memory, and the nightly proactive discovery loop should retrieve from the PI's own prior notes/claims, not just external papers. The 'access to prior work -> sustained improvement; no access -> plateau after ~10 iterations' result is strong evidence for grounding the discovery loop in the existing vault.

ideas.idea: Embedding-similarity (SentenceTransformer + cosine) retrieval over stored reports with top-k return and a JSON API endpoint
ideas.rationale: Confirms a simple local embedding retriever suffices at single-corpus scale; Memoria's retriever/scout profile and BERTopic/keyphrase machinery already cover this, so borrow the validation that lightweight cosine retrieval over one's own corpus is enough rather than the specific implementation.

ideas.idea: Hallucinated/fabricated experimental results and reward-hacking during automated paper writing, requiring manual human verification of every reported number
ideas.rationale: Strong empirical support for Memoria's core governance stance: 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves.' The failure mode (reward function favors papers reporting higher scores -> agents fabricate numbers) is exactly why Memoria gates note-writing behind a structural review and never lets agents execute code; cite as justification for the approval gate and faithfulness-over-flash.

ideas.idea: Parallel agent labs sharing findings asynchronously to accelerate wall-clock discovery, accepting redundancy/extra cost
ideas.rationale: Multi-lab parallelism trades compute for speed at cloud scale; Memoria is explicitly single-researcher, local, cheap-model-first, so the speed/cost tradeoff of running 3+ concurrent labs is out of scope.

ideas.idea: Setting a research 'direction' (not a fixed idea) and letting agents generate ideas toward it during planning
ideas.rationale: Aligns with Memoria's proactive discovery loop generating candidate directions for the PI; borrow the direction-vs-idea framing for how the scout profile frames discovery queries, but under Memoria's PI-approval gate rather than full autonomy.

ideas.idea: First-pass novelty/plagiarism screening of generated outputs (plagiarism detectors + manual inspection finding outputs are mostly perturbations of existing work)
ideas.rationale: Memoria's record-linkage dedup and pre-file similarity gate already aim to catch near-duplicate/derivative content; borrow the insight that a similarity check should flag 'this is a perturbation of an existing claim' as a supersession/duplicate candidate for the contradictions/supersession dashboard.

file: Schmidgall et al. - 2025 - Agent laboratory using LLM agents as research assistants.md
title: Agent Laboratory: Using LLM Agents as Research Assistants
one_line: An autonomous multi-agent pipeline (PhD/Postdoc/ML+SW Engineer/Professor) that runs literature review -> experimentation (mle-solver) -> report writing (paper-solver), with optional human co-pilot checkpoints and detailed cost/quality evaluation.

ideas.idea: Co-pilot mode: human reviewer at a checkpoint after each subtask can accept output or send it back with high-level notes; co-pilot papers score higher than fully autonomous
ideas.rationale: This is precisely Memoria's structural review gate / PI-approval model expressed as a pipeline. The empirical finding that human checkpoints improve quality and that autonomous mode stays below acceptance threshold validates 'PI approves' as a design pillar, and that every stage be PI-accessible from the Obsidian UI.

ideas.idea: Iterative literature-review agent: query arXiv, get summaries of top-N, pull full text selectively, 'add paper' to a curated set, refining over multiple queries until N reached
ideas.rationale: Maps onto Memoria's ingest + discovery loop: a retriever/scout that issues multiple queries, triages by relevance (learning-to-rank triage), and curates a candidate set for the PI. Borrow the summary-then-selective-fulltext-then-add staged retrieval to keep token cost low with local models.

ideas.idea: LLM reward-model scoring (0-1) of generated artifacts against a plan, with top-program pool, self-reflection on errors/scores, and high-entropy sampling for exploration (mle-solver)
ideas.rationale: The self-reflection-on-failure loop and keeping a pool of top-scoring candidates is reusable for Memoria's engines producing candidate notes/relations that agents then judge. Borrow the scored-candidate-pool + reflection pattern, but Memoria's scoring must feed the human-judged gate, not auto-promote, given the reward-hacking risk below.

ideas.idea: Automated NeurIPS-style reviewer that scores soundness/clarity/contribution and gates revision loops (adapted from AI Scientist)
ideas.rationale: An LLM-judge over structured criteria is reusable as the 'agents judge' layer of Memoria's gate (e.g. scoring a candidate claim's faithfulness/uncertainty before PI review). Borrow the rubric-based LLM judge, but heed the next finding.

ideas.idea: Automated reviewer scores massively overestimate quality vs humans (6.1 vs 3.8 /10), so automated review must not stand alone
ideas.rationale: Decisive evidence for Memoria: never let the LLM-judge be the final arbiter. This directly justifies keeping the human PI approval gate after agent judgment and treating LLM-judge scores as advisory signals on dashboards, not auto-merge triggers.

ideas.idea: Concrete failure modes: code-repair mechanism deletes core logic / inserts placeholders, agents run subprocess() to install packages, fabricate realistic runtime outputs, can't revise the plan mid-experiment
ideas.rationale: Empirical backing for Memoria's MCP-only sandbox with hard deny on terminal/code-execution (ADR-32/46): the paper shows unsandboxed agents autonomously run system commands and fake outputs. Cite as real-world justification that agents must get no file/terminal/code-execution, ever.

ideas.idea: Detailed per-phase cost/runtime accounting and 'compute-flexible' design so users pick model budget per their resources ($2.33/paper with gpt-4o)
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's cheap/local-model-first and split-testing-by-what-uses-the-model approach; borrow the practice of per-phase cost/latency accounting so the PI can see where token budget goes, but Memoria targets local qwen, not paid APIs.

ideas.idea: Fixed multi-role agent cast (PhD, Postdoc, ML Engineer, SW Engineer, Professor) collaborating via dialogue
ideas.rationale: Elaborate role-play multi-agent orchestration is heavier than a single-researcher system needs and adds cost/latency; Memoria favors compiled profiles (retriever/scout, judge) over a simulated lab of personas. The roles are theater, not capability.

file: Schucher et al. - 2022 - The power of prompt tuning for low-resource semantic parsing.md
title: The Power of Prompt Tuning for Low-Resource Semantic Parsing
one_line: Shows prompt tuning a frozen large T5 beats fine-tuning (and GPT-3/BART) on low-resource semantic parsing, and that larger prompt-tuned models can emit out-of-distribution formal meaning representations without canonicalization.

ideas.idea: Grammar-constrained decoding via a prefix tree of all valid (canonical/meaning) representations, restricting each decoding step to valid continuations during beam search
ideas.rationale: Memoria needs deterministic, schema-valid structured outputs (atomic claims, typed relations, MASSW aspects) from small local models. Constrained decoding over a grammar/prefix-tree is a concrete way to guarantee well-formed structured-capture output and reduce parse failures from qwen2.5:7b, supporting deterministic ingest.

ideas.idea: Canonicalization: naturalize a formal target into a near-natural-language surface form so the LM generates closer to its pretraining distribution (helps small models most)
ideas.rationale: Useful for designing how Memoria asks a small local model to emit structure: phrase the target schema in natural-language-like form (or provide in-vocab short labels) so a 7B model produces it reliably, since the paper shows small models suffer when forced to emit out-of-distribution tokens. Adapt to claim/relation extraction prompts.

ideas.idea: Prompt tuning (optimizing soft prompt embeddings, LM frozen) as a parameter-efficient adaptation beating fine-tuning in low-data regimes
ideas.rationale: Requires backprop through the full model and thousands of epochs to converge (the paper's own limitation); Memoria uses frozen local models via MCP with no training loop, so soft-prompt tuning is out of scope at single-researcher local scale.

ideas.idea: Larger models tolerate intrusive vocabulary changes (out-of-vocab ontology tokens) with no accuracy loss while small models drop ~50%
ideas.rationale: Practical guidance for Memoria's model choice: since it relies on a small 7B default, prefer in-vocabulary/natural labels in extraction schemas rather than novel ontology tokens, reserving exotic-token schemas for the larger fallback (qwen3:14b). Informs prompt/schema design, not a buildable feature.

file: Tan et al. - 2022 - MSP multi-stage prompting for making pre-trained language models better translators.md
title: MSP: Multi-Stage Prompting for Making Pre-trained Language Models Better Translators
one_line: A parameter-efficient method that steers a frozen multilingual GPT to machine translation by splitting the task into encoding/re-encoding/decoding stages each with its own trainable deep continuous (prefix) prompt.

ideas.idea: Decompose a generation task into separate consecutive stages (encode, re-encode, decode), each driven by an independent learned prompt, to give the LM 'smoother transitions' between sub-objectives.
ideas.rationale: The general insight — break a hard LLM task into staged prompts rather than one mega-prompt — transfers to Memoria's ingest pipeline (claim extraction, sentence classification, MASSW aspects could be staged), but the specific continuous-prompt training is irrelevant since Memoria uses frozen off-the-shelf local models (qwen2.5:7b) with no prefix-tuning.

ideas.idea: Deep continuous prompts / prefix-tuning: per-layer trainable key-value vectors prepended in attention, far smaller (19M) than a full model per task.
ideas.rationale: Requires gradient training and a swappable per-task prompt store per model; Memoria runs frozen local models via Ollama and does not train prompts. Off-architecture for a single-researcher local stack.

ideas.idea: Scaled reparameterization (a learnable scalar times an embedding) for faster, more stable prompt convergence.
ideas.rationale: Training-time trick with no surface in a no-fine-tuning ingest system.

ideas.idea: A frozen LM's translation/alignment knowledge resides in the weights; the prompt only steers it.
ideas.rationale: Interesting but a training-research observation; offers Memoria no buildable mechanism.

file: Tang et al. - 2025 - AI-researcher autonomous scientific innovation.md
title: AI-Researcher: Autonomous Scientific Innovation
one_line: A fully autonomous multi-agent system that runs the whole research pipeline (literature review, ideation, implementation, paper writing) plus Scientist-Bench, a benchmark with an LLM pairwise paper-review judge validated against ICLR accept/reject decisions.

ideas.idea: Resource Analyst decomposes a research concept into ATOMIC components with explicit bidirectional mappings between each math formulation and its code implementation, to reduce hallucination.
ideas.rationale: Mirrors Memoria's atomic-claims extraction; the bidirectional-mapping-with-evidence idea (link each atomic unit to its supporting source span) reinforces Memoria's claim-to-evidence provenance, though Memoria maps claims-to-sources not math-to-code.

ideas.idea: Five-step LLM reference-importance scoring (citation pattern, context influence, evidence collection, impact scoring, final justified selection) to pick the 15-20 references that shaped a paper.
ideas.rationale: Directly usable as a ranking/triage heuristic for Memoria's discovery relevance scoring and learning-to-rank triage — score a candidate's influence via citation context + textual evidence rather than count alone. Adapt as a scoring rubric, not the multi-agent machinery.

ideas.idea: LLM pairwise paper-review judge with debiasing (random swap to kill position bias; ensemble of multiple LLMs at temperature 1), validated against ICLR accept/reject and reaching 81-91% selection accuracy.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's structural review gate is 'agents judge'; the random-swap position-debiasing and ensemble-of-judges techniques are concrete, cheap improvements for any LLM-as-judge step Memoria runs. But full multi-LLM ensembles strain the single-local-model constraint, so borrow the debiasing, not the panel.

ideas.idea: Two-stage evaluation: Stage-1 technical execution validation (does code actually run / implement the claim) before Stage-2 quality judging — never grade an artifact whose claims aren't verified.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge' ordering: a faithfulness/verification gate precedes quality scoring. The principle (verify before judge) maps onto the pre-file similarity gate + structural review gate sequencing.

ideas.idea: Anonymization protocol (mask method names, abstract technical details, anonymize citations) to test conceptual understanding vs memorized recall.
ideas.rationale: A benchmark-construction concern to prevent training-data leakage; Memoria is not building an eval benchmark and has no such contamination risk.

ideas.idea: External structured memory with semantic indexing / hierarchical detail-vs-retrieval tradeoff to avoid context-window compression loss across long workflows (their explicit Future Work).
ideas.rationale: This is exactly Memoria's thesis: a persistent structured vault (typed relations, claims, supersession) as external memory so fine-grained facts aren't lost to summary compression. Validates the core architecture — Memoria's markdown-vault-as-memory is the answer to the gap they flag.

ideas.idea: Full end-to-end autonomous scientist with agents running code in Docker containers, writing files, executing shell commands.
ideas.rationale: Directly contradicts Memoria's MCP-only agent sandbox (ADR-32/46; agents get NO file/terminal/code execution, ever). The autonomy-maximizing, tool-heavy agent design is the opposite of Memoria's 'PI approves' governance.

ideas.idea: Hierarchical three-phase long-document generation (outline -> template-guided elaboration -> checklist verification) to maintain cross-document coherence and factual integrity.
ideas.rationale: The checklist-verification 'one more step' is a usable pattern for Memoria's note-synthesis/projection engine to catch inaccuracies before the PI approves, consistent with the review gate.

file: Taylor et al. - 2022 - Galactica a large language model for science.md
title: Galactica: A Large Language Model for Science
one_line: A scientific LLM trained on a curated 106B-token corpus with special interface tokens (citations, <work> reasoning, modality wrappers) that stores/recalls scientific knowledge and even beats tuned retrievers at citation prediction.

ideas.idea: Citation tokens ([START_REF]...[END_REF]) that encode the implicit citation graph in-text and let the model predict a citation given any context, outperforming sparse and dense retrievers.
ideas.rationale: Memoria has a citekey backbone and discovery loop; the insight that in-context citation prediction can beat TF-IDF/Contriever retrieval suggests an LLM-based 'what should this cite' suggestion surface. But it requires training a special-token model — Memoria can only borrow the framing (predict citations from context) as a discovery feature over its existing models, not the weight-memory approach.

ideas.idea: Title-based citation identifiers predict better than alphanumeric IDs but hallucinate more at small scale.
ideas.rationale: Concrete caution for any Memoria citation-suggestion or entity-resolution feature using small local models: human-readable identifiers improve linkage but a small qwen model will hallucinate them — needs the record-linkage dedup gate to validate.

ideas.idea: Curated, high-quality, normative corpus beats larger uncurated corpora and even tolerates repeated epochs without overfitting; quality over token-count.
ideas.rationale: Validates Memoria's 'faithfulness over flash' and deterministic-curated-ingest philosophy at the data level — a small clean knowledge base can outperform a big noisy one. Philosophical support, not a buildable component.

ideas.idea: <work> working-memory token wrapping step-by-step reasoning, offloading exact computation to an executed Python script when the forward pass is unreliable.
ideas.rationale: Requires pretraining a model with the token and (optionally) code execution; both off-limits given Memoria's frozen local models and MCP-only no-code-execution sandbox.

ideas.idea: Weight memory vs retrieval: storing knowledge in parameters is 'pliable' (association) but hallucination-prone and hard to update; fine-grained facts will always need retrieval.
ideas.rationale: Endorses Memoria's whole premise — keep fine-grained scientific facts (claims, entities, relations) in an external retrievable store rather than model weights, precisely because they're fine-grained and must be updatable (claim supersession). The paper concedes retrieval is needed; Memoria is that retrieval layer.

ideas.idea: Distributional citation analysis: smaller models bias toward popular papers; the predicted citation distribution approaches ground truth with scale.
ideas.rationale: Warns Memoria's local (small) discovery/ranking models will over-recommend popular papers — a popularity-bias to correct for in discovery relevance scoring / triage to surface 'hidden gems'.

ideas.idea: Prompt pre-training: blending task prompts into the pretraining corpus to boost zero-shot task performance.
ideas.rationale: Pretraining-time technique; Memoria does not pretrain or fine-tune models.

file: Thirunavukarasu et al. - 2023 - Large language models in medicine.md
title: Large language models in medicine
one_line: A Nature Medicine review of how LLM chatbots (ChatGPT/GPT-4) are developed and applied in clinical, educational and research settings, cataloguing their strengths and the accuracy/recency/coherence/transparency/ethics limitations blocking autonomous deployment.

ideas.idea: Domain-specific fine-tuning (BioBERT, PubMedBERT, ClinicalBERT, Med-PaLM 2) outperforms general LLMs on in-domain tasks.
ideas.rationale: Memoria uses frozen general local models and does not fine-tune; off-domain (clinical) and contradicts the no-training constraint.

ideas.idea: Lack of an uncertainty indicator on outputs is a core barrier; outputs should flag confidence so a human can be kept in the loop where the model can't help.
ideas.rationale: Directly supports Memoria's atomic-claim UNCERTAINTY FLAGS and the PI-approval gate — surfacing per-claim confidence so the PI knows what to trust is exactly the mitigation this review prescribes.

ideas.idea: Require outputs to cite which parts of the source contributed to the answer (transparency/interpretability) to combat the black-box and fact-fabrication problems.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's evidence-grounded claims and provenance/warrant tracking — every synthesized statement should cite its source span. This is a stated mitigation Memoria already embodies.

ideas.idea: Recency limitation: a model frozen at a cutoff gives poor answers when terminology or paradigms shift; needs real-time/up-to-date knowledge access.
ideas.rationale: Motivates Memoria's external up-to-date vault + nightly discovery loop and claim SUPERSESSION over time as the antidote to model staleness — keep current knowledge in the retrievable store, not the weights.

ideas.idea: Expert-graded-then-automated scaling: humans grade a sample, train a model (reward model) to scale the judgment — balancing efficiency and quality for data validation.
ideas.rationale: Pattern fits Memoria's governance: PI judges a sample, agents apply the learned rubric at scale (learning-to-rank triage), but Memoria can't train a reward model locally — borrow as a 'PI-calibrates, agent-applies' workflow rather than RLHF.

ideas.idea: Keep AI strictly semi-autonomous with human oversight for accountability; autonomous deployment is not feasible.
ideas.rationale: Mirrors Memoria's 'PI approves' / structural review gate exactly — engines and agents assist, the human is accountable and in the loop. Validates the governance model.

file: Thorne et al. - 2018 - FEVER a large-scale dataset for fact extraction and VERification.md
title: FEVER: a large-scale dataset for Fact Extraction and VERification
one_line: Introduces FEVER, 185K claims labeled SUPPORTED/REFUTED/NOTENOUGHINFO with sentence-level evidence against Wikipedia, plus a retrieve-then-select-then-entail pipeline establishing claim verification as a distinct, hard task.

ideas.idea: Three-way claim verdict scheme: SUPPORTED / REFUTED / NOTENOUGHINFO, deliberately avoiding TRUE/FALSE because a single corpus is not the whole world.
ideas.rationale: Directly applicable to Memoria's CONTRADICTIONS dashboard and argument graph — a claim relative to the vault should be supported/refuted/insufficient-evidence rather than absolute truth, exactly matching Memoria's evidence-relative stance and uncertainty flags.

ideas.idea: Every supported/refuted verdict must return the specific sentence(s) of evidence; a label without correct evidence is scored wrong (31.9% with evidence vs 50.9% ignoring it).
ideas.rationale: This is Memoria's provenance-with-warrants principle made into a hard requirement: a relation/contradiction is only valid if it carries its evidence sentences. The evidence-gated scoring is a model for how Memoria's review gate should evaluate engine-proposed relations.

ideas.idea: Pipeline decomposition: document retrieval -> sentence-level evidence selection -> textual entailment classification, with oracle ablations showing sentence selection is the hardest stage.
ideas.rationale: A clean blueprint for a Memoria contradiction/support detector: retrieve candidate notes (pre-file similarity gate), select evidence sentences, classify the relation. The finding that evidence selection (not the entailment) is the bottleneck tells Memoria where to invest. TF-IDF/DrQA specifics are dated but the architecture transfers.

ideas.idea: Multi-hop evidence: ~17% of claims need composing sentences from multiple documents, ~12% across multiple pages; all required sentences must be retrieved for the verdict to count.
ideas.rationale: Tells Memoria that claim verification across the vault is inherently multi-note — the argument graph must support warrants assembled from several notes, not single-source support. Sets a realistic expectation for the contradictions engine.

ideas.idea: Controlled claim generation by mutating source sentences via typed operators (paraphrase, negation, similar/dissimilar substitution, generalize/specialize) tied to natural-logic relations.
ideas.rationale: The typed-mutation taxonomy (generalize/specialize/negate/substitute) is a useful vocabulary for Memoria's TYPED RELATIONS and claim SUPERSESSION (a new claim may specialize, generalize, or negate an older one). Borrow the relation typology, not the crowd-annotation process.

ideas.idea: Annotation discipline: forbid annotators' world knowledge, require evidence strictly from the corpus + a controlled 'dictionary', err toward NOTENOUGHINFO when uncertain (e.g. dual-citizenship case).
ideas.rationale: A precise rubric for Memoria's LLM-judge prompts: base verdicts only on vault evidence (not the model's parametric knowledge), default to insufficient-evidence under uncertainty — controlling exactly the hallucination Galactica/medicine papers warn about.

ideas.idea: Entity-resolution ambiguity ('United' -> Manchester United vs United Airlines) is a recurring source of evidence error.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces the need for Memoria's record-linkage entity dedup as a prerequisite to reliable relation/contradiction detection — ambiguous entity mentions will corrupt the argument graph otherwise.

ideas.idea: Super-annotator agreement protocol and crowd-validation of false-positive evidence to handle incomplete gold evidence.
ideas.rationale: Dataset-quality-assurance machinery for a multi-annotator corpus; Memoria is single-researcher with no annotation team, so this scaling apparatus doesn't apply.

file: Kittur and Kraut - 2008 - Harnessing the wisdom of crowds in wikipedia quality through coordination.md
title: Harnessing the Wisdom of Crowds in Wikipedia: Quality Through Coordination
one_line: A longitudinal study of Wikipedia showing that adding more editors raises article quality only when work is concentrated in a small core (implicit coordination) early in an article's life, while explicit talk-page communication helps only for small groups.

ideas.idea: Implicit coordination via concentration: most interdependent work (structure/direction-setting) is best done by a small core, with peripheral contributors doing low-coordination stand-alone tasks.
ideas.rationale: Maps directly onto Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' governance: structural/direction-setting work (claim graph, contradictions, supersession) stays concentrated in the PI + deterministic engines, while low-coordination work (tagging, dedup candidates, keyphrase extraction) is delegated to agents/engines. Validates keeping the structural review gate narrow rather than spreading authority.

ideas.idea: Coordination matters most early in an artifact's life cycle, when structure is unconstrained; later, existing material constrains contributions and work can be distributed.
ideas.rationale: Suggests Memoria should front-load human/agent attention at note/space creation time (when scaffolding a topic space or argument graph), then let cheaper engines fill in once structure is set — a staging heuristic for where to spend the structural review gate's effort.

ideas.idea: Gini coefficient over edits as a quantitative metric of work concentration; quality-change modeled with lagged regression controlling for initial quality and selection bias (Heckman).
ideas.rationale: This is a multi-author crowd metric; Memoria is single-PI, so editor-concentration statistics are meaningless. The econometric apparatus is for large-N causal inference, not a personal vault.

ideas.idea: Wisdom-of-crowds aggregation is only valid for independent, separable subtasks; interdependent tasks incur process loss with more contributors.
ideas.rationale: Memoria has exactly one contributor; there is no crowd to aggregate. The insight is sound but inapplicable to a single-researcher system.

ideas.idea: Authoritative curation model (Gene Ontology: anyone proposes, expert curators approve) for tasks where correctness is critical.
ideas.rationale: This is essentially Memoria's approval gate already (engines/agents propose, PI approves). The paper offers external precedent that propose-then-curate is the right pattern for high-stakes knowledge bases, reinforcing the existing structural review gate ADRs.

file: Kocaballi et al. - 2022 - Design and Evaluation Challenges of Conversational Agents in Health Care and Well-being Selective R.md
title: Design and Evaluation Challenges of Conversational Agents in Health Care and Well-being: Selective Review Study
one_line: A thematic review of 17 health-care conversational-agent projects identifying four challenge themes — domain information/integration, user-system interaction, evaluation, and conversational competence — with safety and privacy as the dominant domain-specific concern.

ideas.idea: Local on-device NLP processing for privacy-first interactions rather than routing prompts through third-party cloud services (accepting some capability loss).
ideas.rationale: Exactly Memoria's stance: local + cheap/open models (qwen2.5:7b), no cloud egress of sensitive vault content. The paper documents the same privacy-vs-capability tradeoff Memoria has already chosen, validating the local-model constraint.

ideas.idea: Incremental domain-knowledge acquisition from the user/expert to bypass the knowledge-acquisition bottleneck when full-time expert annotation is infeasible.
ideas.rationale: Memoria can adopt incremental, PI-in-the-loop acquisition (structured capture forms, approval gate) to bootstrap domain vocabulary/relations rather than requiring upfront curation — fits the single-PI resource constraint.

ideas.idea: Evaluation gap: scripted/Wizard-of-Oz tests show efficacy but not real-world effectiveness; longitudinal observation of key indicators is needed to derive success metrics.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's faithfulness-over-flash evaluation philosophy: prefer longitudinal in-vault behavior (does the discovery loop surface useful papers over weeks) over one-shot benchmark scores. Useful framing for the L5 quality-testing track.

ideas.idea: Conversational competence: topic detection, ASR endpointing, turn-taking, discoverability of commands.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is not a voice/conversational agent; its surfaces are Obsidian spaces, dashboards, forms, and read-only inspectors. Speech/dialog UX challenges are off-domain.

ideas.idea: Empathy, relationship-building, trust-repair strategies, culturally sensitive CA design.
ideas.rationale: These target patient-facing emotional interaction; Memoria serves one researcher via document/knowledge surfaces, not an empathetic companion. Out of scope.

ideas.idea: Multi-institution collaboration and data-sharing frameworks to overcome per-project scope/budget limits.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is explicitly single-researcher, local, not multi-institution; data-sharing/interoperability (FHIR) is enterprise-scale and contradicts the local-vault constraint.

file: Kojima et al. - 2023 - Large language models are zero-shot reasoners.md
title: Large Language Models are Zero-Shot Reasoners
one_line: Shows that simply appending 'Let's think step by step' (Zero-shot-CoT) before answering elicits strong multi-step reasoning from large LLMs across arithmetic, symbolic, and logical tasks without any task-specific exemplars, via a two-stage reasoning-then-answer-extraction pipeline.

ideas.idea: Zero-shot chain-of-thought: a single task-agnostic trigger ('Let's think step by step') elicits multi-step reasoning without per-task few-shot exemplars.
ideas.rationale: Directly buildable into Memoria's local-model prompts for reasoning-heavy steps (claim extraction with uncertainty flags, contradiction detection, argument-warrant judgment, discovery relevance scoring). Avoids hand-crafting few-shot exemplars per task, which suits cheap local models and keeps prompts compact and deterministic.

ideas.idea: Two-stage prompting: first prompt extracts the reasoning trace, second prompt extracts the final answer in a parseable format ('Therefore, the answer is...').
ideas.rationale: A clean pattern for Memoria's MCP-only agents/engines that must return structured verdicts: separate free-form reasoning from a deterministically parseable answer slot. Improves faithfulness and makes outputs gate-checkable, fitting 'engines write, agents judge'.

ideas.idea: CoT benefits scale with model size; small models gain little and can be led astray by reasoning prompts.
ideas.rationale: Important caveat for Memoria's qwen2.5:7b default — zero-shot-CoT may not help (or may hurt) at 7B. Implies the technique should be validated on the local model in the L5 quality track and possibly reserved for the qwen3:14b fallback on harder reasoning tasks.

ideas.idea: Prompt-template sensitivity: 'instructive' triggers help, misleading/irrelevant ones hurt; performance varies notably across phrasings.
ideas.rationale: Tells Memoria to treat the reasoning trigger as a tuned, version-controlled prompt asset (part of compiled profiles), not an ad-hoc string — and to pin the chosen template after local-model evaluation rather than assume the paper's exact wording transfers.

ideas.idea: Self-consistency (sampling multiple reasoning paths and majority-voting) further boosts accuracy.
ideas.rationale: Could improve reliability of borderline claim/contradiction judgments before they hit the approval gate, but multi-sample decoding is compute-cost on a single RTX 4060 Ti; adopt only selectively for high-stakes structural decisions, not nightly bulk ingest.

file: Kunda et al. - 2013 - A computational model for solving problems from the raven’s progressive matrices intelligence test u.md
title: A Computational Model for Solving Raven's Progressive Matrices Using Iconic Visual Representations
one_line: Presents the 'affine model,' which solves Raven's Progressive Matrices by reasoning directly over scanned pixel images using affine/set transformations and Tversky-ratio similarity, showing amodal symbolic abstraction is not always necessary for analogical visual reasoning.

ideas.idea: Tversky ratio model of similarity (similarity weighted by shared vs. distinctive features) as a tunable feature-overlap metric, contrasted with SSD which ignores shared content.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and record-linkage entity dedup need a principled similarity function; Tversky's asymmetric, shared-vs-distinctive-feature weighting is a better fit for comparing claim/entity feature sets than naive overlap, and the paper's SSD-vs-Tversky analysis shows how metric choice changes which matches are privileged.

ideas.idea: Predict-then-match (generate an expected answer, then pick the closest candidate) vs. guess-and-check (insert each candidate, score fit) as two analogy-solving strategies.
ideas.rationale: Mirrors a useful framing for Memoria's discovery/triage: either project an 'expected' relevant-paper profile and rank candidates by closeness (predict-then-match), or score each candidate's fit into the existing knowledge structure (guess-and-check). Worth keeping as a design lens for the retriever/scout profile.

ideas.idea: Reasoning over raw, noisy modal (pixel) representations instead of cleaned amodal propositions, deliberately keeping inputs realistic.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's pipeline is deliberately the opposite: deterministic local parsing extracts amodal atomic claims and typed relations from text. Pixel-level imagistic reasoning is off-domain (text/PDF, not visual analogy) and contradicts the structured-extraction design.

ideas.idea: Dual-process account: iconic/visual vs. propositional/verbal strategies as complementary, qualitatively different problem-solving routes.
ideas.rationale: A cognitive-science modeling claim about human intelligence-test performance; no buildable mechanism for a text-based research-knowledge system. Background only.

ideas.idea: Affine/set transformations (rotation, reflection, union, intersection, subtraction) as the operator vocabulary for transformation induction.
ideas.rationale: These are 2-D image operators with no analog in Memoria's claim/relation/argument domain. Not applicable.

file: Langley - 2022 - Learning hierarchical problem networks for knowledge-based planning.md
title: Hierarchical Problem Networks for Knowledge-Based Planning
one_line: Introduces Hierarchical Problem Networks (HPNs), a planning formalism whose methods decompose problems (sets of goals) into ordered subproblems, with state-conditions constraining variable bindings and novel goal-conditions ('unless-goals') constraining goal ordering to enable largely search-free, deterministic plan generation.

ideas.idea: Goal-ordering constraints via 'unless-goals': a method is blocked when a related goal is still unsatisfied (e.g., don't put A on B if B-on-C is an unsatisfied goal), preventing work that would have to be undone.
ideas.rationale: A precise mechanism for ordering Memoria's pipeline operations: e.g., don't finalize a note/claim write while an unsatisfied prerequisite (entity dedup pending, supersession unresolved, contradiction unreviewed) is outstanding. Encodes dependency ordering declaratively rather than via ad-hoc orchestration code, fitting deterministic ingest.

ideas.idea: Decompose problems (sets of goals) rather than individual goals, so a small set of general methods covers arbitrarily many goals without per-configuration rules.
ideas.rationale: Useful design principle for Memoria's nightly discovery/ingest orchestration: express the workflow as compact problem-decomposition methods that generalize over variable numbers of papers/claims, instead of hard-coding per-case branches. Keeps the agent runtime's procedural knowledge small and auditable.

ideas.idea: Knowledge-guided, mostly search-free planning that falls back to search only when domain knowledge is incomplete; each method linked directly to a primitive operator.
ideas.rationale: Aligns with Memoria's 'deterministic ingest' preference: prefer encoded procedures that run without exploratory search (cheap, predictable on local hardware), reserving costlier reasoning only for gaps. Reinforces favoring deterministic engines over open-ended agent search.

ideas.idea: Lesion/scaling studies showing state-conditions control search growth with object count while goal-conditions control growth with interacting-goal count.
ideas.rationale: Empirical methodology specific to symbolic planners; Memoria's workloads (ingest, triage) aren't combinatorial planning problems, so the specific search-scaling results don't transfer.

ideas.idea: Full HPD symbolic planning architecture in Common Lisp (problem stack, match/select/expand cycles, backtracking) for Blocks World / Logistics / Depots.
ideas.rationale: Adopting a classical symbolic planner is far heavier than Memoria needs; orchestration is handled by the Hermes runtime + engines/agents. The architecture itself is out of scope; only the decomposition/ordering insights are worth borrowing.

file: Bouras and Fellow - 2026 - CrossTrace a cross-domain dataset of grounded scientific reasoning traces for hypothesis generation.md
title: CrossTrace: A Cross-Domain Dataset of Grounded Scientific Reasoning Traces for Hypothesis Generation
one_line: Builds a dataset of 1,389 grounded, step-by-step scientific reasoning traces (Input/Trace/Output with source-quote grounding) and shows fine-tuning a small Qwen2.5-7B model on them teaches transferable hypothesis-generation reasoning across domains.

ideas.idea: Input/Trace/Output schema extending Bit-Flip-Spark: field_context + conventional_assumption (the challenged belief) + key_prior_work -> ordered 3-6 step reasoning chain -> spark/hypothesis/approach.
ideas.rationale: Maps cleanly onto Memoria's atomic-claim + argument-graph model. The 'conventional_assumption being challenged' field is essentially a contradiction/supersession anchor, and spark/hypothesis/approach gives a structured slot for capturing a paper's novel contribution alongside MASSW aspects. Borrow the schema shape for note extraction, not the training pipeline.

ideas.idea: Step-level source grounding: every reasoning step carries a {step, source} object with section, paragraph, and direct quotation; enables automated + human verification of trace fidelity.
ideas.rationale: Directly supports Memoria's faithfulness-over-flash constraint and the structural review gate. Requiring each extracted claim/reasoning step to cite a verbatim source span (with section/paragraph) is exactly the kind of deterministic, auditable evidence link the gate needs before agents judge or the PI approves.

ideas.idea: Discovery pattern taxonomy (8 patterns: gap_fill, analogy_transfer, mechanistic_link, contradiction_exploit, swanson_abc ABC bridge, etc.) annotated per record.
ideas.rationale: A small controlled vocabulary for HOW a paper advances knowledge is a natural typed-relation/aspect facet for Memoria. contradiction_exploit and swanson_abc in particular feed the contradictions dashboard and cross-note discovery; gap_fill/analogy_transfer enrich the discovery relevance scoring. Cheap to compute locally during ingest.

ideas.idea: Confidence-based quality filtering: extractions below 0.6 confidence excluded from the curated set but retained in the DB for analysis.
ideas.rationale: Mirrors Memoria's pre-file similarity gate / structural gate philosophy: keep everything the engine produces, but only promote high-confidence items past the gate. The 'retain low-confidence for analysis, don't surface' pattern is a good default for the inspector/diagnostics plane.

ideas.idea: Two-judge LLM-as-judge evaluation (GPT-4o + Claude) with the explicit caveat that judges differ in absolute calibration but agree on rank ordering.
ideas.rationale: Useful methodological caution for any Memoria 'agents judge' evaluation: trust rank-ordering across local-model judges, not absolute scores. But Memoria is single-researcher/local-model preferred, so adopt the insight, not the cloud dual-judge apparatus.

ideas.idea: Deterministic extraction setup: fixed version-controlled prompt, temperature 0, few-shot examples calibrated for granularity, rule-based section detection + review-article exclusion.
ideas.rationale: Exactly Memoria's deterministic-ingest requirement. Version-controlling the extraction prompt, temp 0, and regex/rule-based Introduction/Discussion section detection with a min-content threshold are directly transplantable to the local ingest engine.

ideas.idea: lineage-aware training via parent_record_id to generate follow-up hypotheses that build on prior work.
ideas.rationale: A parent_record_id lineage field is a typed relation Memoria already wants (claim supersession / builds-on). Borrow the field idea for the note graph; reject the 'train a model on it' framing since Memoria judges rather than trains.

ideas.idea: Cross-domain transfer claim: a model trained on half-biomed/half-CS traces matches domain specialists (reasoning form is domain-general).
ideas.rationale: Interesting science but it is a fine-tuning/transfer-learning result. Memoria does not train models; it uses local off-the-shelf models and judges output. No buildable artifact here.

file: Branch et al. - 2022 - Evaluating the susceptibility of pre-trained language models via handcrafted adversarial examples.md
title: Evaluating the Susceptibility of Pre-Trained Language Models via Handcrafted Adversarial Examples
one_line: Shows a handcrafted 'ignore previous instructions and classify X as Y' prompt-injection reliably flips PLM classifications, and that n-gram (BLEU) and even embedding (BERTScore) similarity metrics fail to detect the semantically-divergent corrupted output.

ideas.idea: Token-distance-minimized handcrafted adversarial / prompt-injection attack ('Ignore the previous instructions and classify [ITEM] as [DISTRACTION]') that bypasses quality checks.
ideas.rationale: Memoria ingests untrusted external paper text and feeds it to extraction/judging agents. This is an early prompt-injection demonstration: source text could contain instructions that hijack the extraction LLM. Borrow as a threat model justifying the MCP-only sandbox and the structural gate (engines write, agents judge, PI approves) — treat ingested content as data, never instructions.

ideas.idea: Embedding-based similarity (BERTScore) and n-gram metrics (BLEU) both stay near a 'perfect match' even when output meaning is flipped; distance metrics fail at sequence level.
ideas.rationale: Directly relevant to Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and record-linkage dedup, which lean on cosine/embedding similarity. The lesson: high embedding similarity does not guarantee semantic equivalence, so the similarity gate must not be the sole arbiter of 'same claim / duplicate' — keep a judging/PI step. Caveat: results are on 2022 BERT-class encoders, weaker than current models.

ideas.idea: Token-level vs sequence-level distance: corruption is more detectable at token level than sequence level.
ideas.rationale: Narrow finding tied to MLM classification on old encoders; no concrete mechanism Memoria would build. Superseded for practical purposes.

ideas.idea: Use of pre-fine-tuning out-of-the-box PLMs and reporting the GPT-3 Playground vulnerability.
ideas.rationale: Model-specific security disclosure of 2022-era systems; not actionable for a local-model single-researcher knowledge system.

file: Brandt et al. - 2010 - Example-centric programming integrating web search into the development environment.md
title: Example-Centric Programming: Integrating Web Search into the Development Environment (Blueprint)
one_line: Blueprint embeds a task-specific code-search engine into the IDE that auto-augments queries with context, presents a consistent example-centric results view, and keeps a provenance link between copied code and its source, letting programmers find/adapt examples faster.

ideas.idea: Automatically augment the user's query with surrounding context (language, framework version) so the user doesn't have to construct a good query.
ideas.rationale: Maps to Memoria's retriever/scout and proactive discovery loop: enrich a PI's discovery query with the context of the note/claim they're working from (citekey, topic cluster, current aspect) rather than requiring a hand-built query. Cheap and high-leverage for relevance scoring.

ideas.idea: Provenance comment linking copied content back to its source URL + timestamp + unique id, machine- and human-readable, re-checked on file open for upstream changes.
ideas.rationale: This is exactly Memoria's citekey-backbone + claim-grounding ethos applied to UI: every projected/captured snippet should carry an auditable, machine-readable link to its origin. The 'on open, re-check source for changes and notify' pattern is a strong model for claim supersession / staleness detection in the projection engine and dashboards.

ideas.idea: Consistent template-based, example-centric results view (description + code + running example, search terms highlighted) so results can be scanned without click-through.
ideas.rationale: Informs Memoria's Obsidian 'spaces'/projected-canvas surfaces: present discovery/triage results in a uniform card layout (claim + evidence quote + source) with query terms highlighted, so the PI evaluates many candidates fast in-UI without leaving Obsidian. The 14% lower click-through finding validates surfacing the answer inline.

ideas.idea: Leverage an existing general-purpose search engine + extract/cache structured snippets, rather than building a bespoke index.
ideas.rationale: Aligns with Memoria's cheap/local preference: reuse Zotero/existing indices and cache extracted atomic claims rather than standing up a heavy bespoke retrieval stack. The page-segmentation + classify-as-code pipeline is analogous to Memoria's deterministic section/claim extraction.

ideas.idea: Embedding search directly in the work environment creates symbiosis with existing features (autocomplete) and lowers activation cost for small tasks.
ideas.rationale: Strong endorsement of Memoria's PI-direct-access rule: every feature must be reachable directly inside Obsidian. Blueprint's data (76% of sessions had no copy/paste; used for reminding/clarifying) supports designing the inspector/discovery surfaces for quick in-context confirmation, not just retrieval.

ideas.idea: Both pop-up and docked panel modalities matter; users switch mid-task.
ideas.rationale: UI guidance for Memoria surfaces: offer both an ephemeral/disposable projection (pop-up) and a persistent docked space, since transient lookup and sustained browsing are different tasks. Matches the disposable-projection-engine + persistent-spaces split.

file: Bray et al. - 2022 - Radical futures supporting community-led design engagements through an afrofuturist speculative des.md
title: Radical Futures: Supporting Community-Led Design Engagements through an Afrofuturist Speculative Design Toolkit
one_line: Develops and evaluates (27 practitioner interviews) the 'Building Utopias' Afrofuturist speculative-design card/workbook toolkit meant to let marginalized communities run their own race-conscious co-design engagements without a researcher present.

ideas.idea: A self-serve toolkit (card decks + ideation workbook) usable without an expert facilitator present, redistributing power to non-experts.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is explicitly single-researcher (the PI), not multi-user/community co-design. There is no facilitator-vs-community power dynamic to redistribute; the PI is always the sole authority via the approval gate. Off-model.

ideas.idea: Finding that fully open-ended probes are confusing; practitioners wanted explicitly stated goals and a structured guide rather than leaving order/use open to interpretation.
ideas.rationale: Weak, generic transferable nugget: Memoria's structured capture forms and spaces should state their intent/affordances explicitly rather than relying on the PI to infer how to use an open canvas. This is the only Memoria-applicable insight, and it is generic UX advice.

ideas.idea: Afrofuturist/race-conscious lens and tenets (joy, liberation, community building) as the framing for design speculation.
ideas.rationale: Domain-specific to equitable community design with Black and Brown communities; no bearing on a personal academic-research knowledge system.

ideas.idea: Speculative/futuring design method and design-justice framing.
ideas.rationale: Off-domain methodology; Memoria's surfaces are for organizing/retrieving research claims, not facilitating speculative futures workshops.

file: Brennan - 1991 - Conversation with and through computers.md
title: Conversation with and through Computers
one_line: An empirical study of lexical and syntactic entrainment (alignment) in human-computer dialogue, showing people adapt their vocabulary and sentence structure to match their conversational partner (human or computer); NOTE: this .md extraction is content-light (tables only, prose missing).

ideas.idea: Syntactic entrainment: users converge their sentence structure toward the partner's response style over the course of a dialogue (Table III: sentence-style turns rise from 72.5% to 82.9% in the second half).
ideas.rationale: Suggests that if Memoria's agents/forms present claims and queries in a consistent canonical structure, the PI will entrain to that structure, improving downstream parse-ability of PI-authored notes. Argues for a stable, well-formed claim/relation vocabulary in capture forms. Low-confidence borrow given extraction is incomplete.

ideas.idea: Lexical entrainment: partners adopt and then consistently reuse the same word for a referent once established (Table II rule: 'keep track of lexical changes and use the same word consistently').
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's record-linkage entity dedup and keyphrase tag-candidate work: a consistent canonical term per entity (and surfacing it back to the PI) encourages the PI to reuse it, reducing surface-form variation that dedup must later reconcile. Adopt the 'establish and reuse one canonical label' principle in the entity layer.

ideas.idea: Conversation-as-interface paradigm for human-computer interaction.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is deliberately not a conversational chat interface; it is gate-governed, form/space-based, with engines writing and the PI approving. A 1991 conversational-agent framing contradicts that design and offers nothing buildable.

file: Xu et al. - 2026 - GraphWalker agentic knowledge graph question answering via synthetic trajectory curriculum.md
title: GraphWalker: Agentic Knowledge Graph Question Answering via Synthetic Trajectory Curriculum
one_line: A two-stage SFT-then-RL curriculum that trains a small LLM agent to autonomously navigate a global knowledge graph for multi-hop QA, using synthesized random-walk trajectories plus distilled expert trajectories.

ideas.idea: Train an agent to traverse a KG via two primitive tools (get_relations(e) to survey local neighborhood, then get_triples(e, R') for targeted expansion) in a think/query/answer loop
ideas.rationale: Memoria has a typed-relation/argument graph over claims and notes; a survey-then-expand two-tool traversal pattern is a clean read-only navigation primitive a retriever/scout MCP could expose over the local graph without needing any model training.

ideas.idea: Evidence-grounding rejection sampling: only keep trajectories where the final answer appears in the retrieved observation history (EM=1 AND answer present in observations), rejecting parametric-knowledge answers
ideas.rationale: Directly maps to Memoria's faithfulness-over-flash constraint and structural review gate: an answer/claim should be admissible only if grounded in retrieved vault evidence, not the model's parametric memory. Usable as an acceptance check in the gate, independent of training.

ideas.idea: Constrained Random Walk synthesis of structurally diverse multi-hop paths (composition chains 2-5 hops + conjunction/2I graphs) to build an exploration prior
ideas.rationale: This is training-data synthesis for fine-tuning an agent policy; Memoria uses cheap off-the-shelf local models (qwen2.5:7b) with no SFT/RL pipeline. Single-researcher scale and deterministic ingest make a 15k-trajectory training corpus out of scope.

ideas.idea: Stage-wise SFT curriculum (broad exploration prior first, then reflection/error-recovery) + GRPO RL with sparse exact-match reward
ideas.rationale: Full model-training stack (SFT stages, GRPO, KL reference policy). Memoria explicitly avoids training; engines are deterministic and agents are MCP-only judges. No buildable component here.

ideas.idea: LLM-based semantic quality filter scoring synthesized questions on path-completeness, question-path relevance, coherence (discard below 9/10)
ideas.rationale: Tied to generating synthetic QA training data, which Memoria does not produce. The generic 'LLM-judge quality filter' idea is already covered by Memoria's structural review gate, so nothing new to adopt.

file: Xu et al. - 2026 - NanoResearch Co-evolving skills, memory, and policy for personalized research automation.md
title: NanoResearch: Co-Evolving Skills, Memory, and Policy for Personalized Research Automation
one_line: A multi-agent end-to-end research-automation framework that co-evolves a procedural skill bank, a user/project memory module, and a feedback-trained planner policy to personalize outputs to one researcher's preferences.

ideas.idea: Three-layer separation: distilled procedural skills (compact reusable rules) vs. user/project-specific episodic memory vs. preferences encoded into the planner
ideas.rationale: Memoria is single-PI and explicitly wants to internalize the PI's preferences. The skills-vs-memory split (generalizable how-to rules separated from project-specific facts/outcomes) is a useful organizing principle for how Memoria stores reusable extraction/triage heuristics distinctly from per-paper notes, without adopting the agentic automation wrapper.

ideas.idea: Quantitative evidence extraction: parse performance scores directly from papers to ground evidence and mitigate hallucination
ideas.rationale: Squarely matches Memoria's atomic-claim extraction with uncertainty flags; deterministically pulling reported numeric results/metrics from a paper into a structured evidence field is a concrete ingest enhancement that improves faithfulness.

ideas.idea: Automated novelty verification: query literature databases with each candidate hypothesis to filter prior-work overlap before committing
ideas.rationale: Mirrors Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and record-linkage dedup. The pattern (query existing corpus before admitting a new claim/note as novel) reinforces the gate's novelty check; adaptable to the local vault rather than external DBs.

ideas.idea: Heuristic top-k retrieval scoring combining keyword match, tag alignment, and recency, with target-specific weighting (skills favor usage-frequency/confidence; memory enforces strict condition matching)
ideas.rationale: Memoria has learning-to-rank triage and discovery relevance scoring; the cheap keyword+tag+recency heuristic with role-specific weighting is a sensible lightweight fallback ranker for the local, single-user scale where a trained LTR model may be overkill.

ideas.idea: Merge semantically overlapping memory/skill entries to prevent unbounded store growth
ideas.rationale: Memoria already does record-linkage entity dedup and claim supersession; periodic compaction/merge of near-duplicate notes is consistent and worth applying to accumulated heuristics, but is incremental not novel.

ideas.idea: SDPO label-free policy learning that converts free-form NL feedback into persistent parameter updates of the planner model
ideas.rationale: Requires fine-tuning a trainable planner (Qwen3-8B) on user feedback. Memoria's agents are MCP-only and not trained; preferences are captured in compiled profiles and ADRs, not gradient updates. Out of scope and contrary to the no-training stance.

ideas.idea: Full end-to-end automation: agents write code, run experiments on SLURM, and autonomously produce papers
ideas.rationale: Directly violates Memoria's MCP-only sandbox (no file/terminal/code-execution for agents, ever) and the engines-write/agents-judge/PI-approves governance. Memoria assists a researcher, it does not autonomously generate research.

file: Yamada et al. - 2025 - The AI scientist-v2 workshop-level automated scientific discovery via agentic tree search.md
title: The AI Scientist-v2: Workshop-Level Automated Scientific Discovery via Agentic Tree Search
one_line: An end-to-end autonomous system that uses an experiment-manager-coordinated parallel agentic tree search plus VLM figure feedback to generate ML papers, one of which passed workshop peer review.

ideas.idea: Literature-review tools (Semantic Scholar) in-the-loop during idea generation to assess novelty against prior work from the outset rather than post-hoc
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's proactive discovery loop and pre-file similarity/novelty gate: checking a candidate idea/claim against existing literature early. Memoria does this over its own curated vault rather than for paper-writing, but the early-novelty-grounding principle transfers.

ideas.idea: VLM feedback loop that checks figure-caption alignment, missing legends/labels, and figure duplication between main text and appendix
ideas.rationale: Memoria ingests papers and extracts claims; it does not author figures or manuscripts. A vision-language figure critique has no surface in a markdown-vault knowledge system for a single PI.

ideas.idea: Agentic tree search over solution nodes with best-first LLM-guided selection, typed node variants (buggy/refine/hyperparameter/ablation/replication/aggregation), and probabilistic debug-vs-refine choice
ideas.rationale: This is a code-generation/experiment-execution search loop requiring agents to run Python. Memoria's MCP-only sandbox forbids agent code execution entirely, and Memoria does no experimentation. Architecturally incompatible.

ideas.idea: Experiment-manager agent with explicit staged pipeline (feasibility, tuning, agenda, ablations) each with stopping criteria, carrying the best node forward and launching replications for mean/std statistics
ideas.rationale: Workflow for autonomous empirical ML research; nothing in Memoria runs experiments or needs replication statistics. Off-domain for a literature-knowledge vault.

ideas.idea: Meta-selection: run many seeds autonomously, then a human picks the best complete output (professor-reviewing-students model)
ideas.rationale: Superficially echoes Memoria's PI-approval gate, but the generate-many-then-pick paradigm presupposes autonomous generation of research artifacts, which Memoria does not do. The human-judges-final principle is already fully covered by Memoria's existing gate.

file: Yang et al. - 2018 - HotpotQA a dataset for diverse, explainable multi-hop question answering.md
title: HotpotQA: A Dataset for Diverse, Explainable Multi-hop Question Answering
one_line: A 113k-example crowdsourced multi-hop QA dataset built over a Wikipedia hyperlink graph, notable for providing sentence-level supporting facts as strong supervision and explainability targets, plus comparison questions and a full-wiki retrieval setting.

ideas.idea: Sentence-level supporting facts: require the system to output the exact set of sentences used to reach an answer, and evaluate them as a first-class explainability metric (joint EM/F1 over answer + supporting facts)
ideas.rationale: Core to Memoria's faithfulness stance and claim provenance. Any RAG/retrieval answer surfaced to the PI should cite the precise supporting sentences (claim-sentence level), and Memoria can score retrieval quality with a joint answer+evidence metric so an answer with the right span but wrong support is penalized.

ideas.idea: Bridge-entity multi-hop structure: chain reasoning requires first identifying an intermediate entity (e.g. singer-of-Radiohead -> Thom Yorke -> birthday) before the second hop
ideas.rationale: Memoria's typed-relation graph over claims/notes supports exactly this kind of bridge traversal for multi-hop discovery (find connecting note, then expand). The dataset validates that bridge-entity hops are the dominant useful multi-hop pattern to support in graph navigation.

ideas.idea: Comparison/contrast questions that require pulling a shared property from two entities and comparing (counts, dates, yes/no same-category)
ideas.rationale: Aligns with Memoria's contradictions dashboard and claim supersession: detecting that two papers report differing values for a shared property is a comparison operation. The yes/no 'same property?' framing is a useful template for surfacing agreements/disagreements across claims.

ideas.idea: Use a hyperlink/citation graph to generate meaningful multi-hop connections (edges entail real relations between disambiguated entities)
ideas.rationale: Memoria's citekey/citation backbone and typed relations are the analogue of the Wikipedia hyperlink graph; the insight that link-graph edges are a strong scaffold for multi-hop reasoning supports building discovery over the citation/relation graph rather than flat similarity.

ideas.idea: tf-idf / inverted-index full-wiki retrieval as the first-stage candidate filter before reasoning, with the observation that retrieving ALL required entities is the dominant failure mode
ideas.rationale: Memoria already has embedding similarity + BERTopic clustering + LTR triage as its retrieval stack; bigram tf-idf is a 2018 baseline it has superseded. The multi-entity-retrieval-is-the-bottleneck insight is real but not specific enough to be actionable beyond what Memoria's retriever already targets.

ideas.idea: Multi-task joint training of answer span + supporting-fact classifier sharing low-level representations
ideas.rationale: A bespoke neural QA architecture trained on this dataset; Memoria does no model training and uses off-the-shelf local LLMs. Architecture-level technique is out of scope.

file: Yang et al. - 2020 - Re-examining whether, why, and how human-AI interaction is uniquely difficult to design.md
title: Re-examining Whether, Why, and How Human-AI Interaction Is Uniquely Difficult to Design
one_line: A conceptual HCI framework arguing that AI is hard to design for because of two orthogonal axes (capability uncertainty and output complexity), yielding four levels of AI systems with progressively harder sketching/prototyping challenges.

ideas.idea: Two-axis framework: capability uncertainty (what/how-well the system can do, evolving post-deployment) x output complexity (small fixed set -> simulable adaptive -> non-simulable 'complex' like Siri)
ideas.rationale: A useful lens for classifying Memoria's own engines/agents and setting PI expectations. Memoria's claim-extraction and discovery scoring are higher-output-complexity surfaces; framing them on this map clarifies which features need the structural review gate / human judgment versus which can run deterministically.

ideas.idea: Design graceful failure recovery and set user expectations for unpredictable AI outputs (false-positive/false-negative enumeration for low-output systems)
