ideas.rationale: Directly applicable to Memoria's gate and inspector surfaces: for bounded-output engines (e.g. claim-sentence classification, similarity gate), enumerate FP/FN cases and design the PI-facing UI (dashboards, gates) to make errors correctable, honoring the PI-direct-access rule and faithfulness.

ideas.idea: Level-1 systems (bounded capability, few outputs) are designable with conventional methods and are the 'low-hanging fruit'; reserve heavier RtD/judgment for level-3/4 evolving systems
ideas.rationale: Guides where Memoria should spend governance effort: deterministic ingest engines with bounded outputs need little oversight, whereas adaptive discovery/triage (which evolve as the vault grows) are exactly where the structural review gate and PI approval earn their keep.

ideas.idea: Capability uncertainty peaks at ideation because there is no catalog of available AI capabilities; understanding AI is understanding the gap between what data promises and what the built system achieves
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's read-only inspector / static admin reports: exposing the actual measured behavior and error modes of each engine to the PI closes the capability-uncertainty gap, turning unknown capability into observed capability.

ideas.idea: Wizard-of-Oz / rule-based simulators cannot reproduce AI inference errors, so they fail to surface the UX consequences that matter; functioning field-trial prototyping is the costly alternative
ideas.rationale: Memoria is already a functioning local system the PI runs on a real vault (the test/sandbox vault), so the sketch-vs-field-trial tension this paper highlights is moot; nothing new to build from the prototyping-tooling critique.

file: Funkquist et al. - 2023 - CiteBench a benchmark for scientific citation text generation.md
title: CiteBench: A Benchmark for Scientific Citation Text Generation
one_line: A unified benchmark for citation-text generation that harmonizes four datasets and, crucially, introduces discourse-based qualitative evaluation (citation-intent labeling + CORWA sentence-role tagging) to characterize HOW and WHY papers cite each other.

ideas.idea: Citation-intent labeling (ACL-ARC schema: Background, CompareOrContrast, Extends, Future, Motivation, Uses) applied to citation sentences
ideas.rationale: Directly feeds Memoria's typed-relations and argument-graph layer: when ingesting a paper that cites another, tagging the citing sentence with an intent gives a typed edge (e.g. 'extends', 'contrasts') between two notes/claims. Runs as a deterministic classifier in the ingest engine; agents judge, PI approves. Off-the-shelf scicite model is cheap/local.

ideas.idea: CORWA sentence-role tagging (Single_summ, Multi_summ, Narrative_cite, Reflection, Transition, Other) for citation-sentence composition
ideas.rationale: Useful as a claim-sentence classification refinement for related-work text: distinguishes a detailed single-paper summary from a narrative aside. Memoria already does MASSW-style aspect and claim-sentence classification; CORWA's role labels are a borrowable label set for the 'how a source is used' dimension, but the full tagger is a heavier dependency than needed at single-researcher scale.

ideas.idea: KL-divergence between reference and generated label distributions (intent/CORWA) as a supplementary, cheap proxy for qualitative quality vs expensive human eval
ideas.rationale: Memoria avoids generating related-work prose, so the generation-eval framing is off-target, but the distributional-discrepancy-as-cheap-quality-signal idea transfers: e.g. comparing the intent-label mix of PI-approved notes vs engine drafts could flag drift in the structural-review gate.

ideas.idea: Self-reference detection in citation text (keyword search for 'our work', 'we present') showing up to 15% of 'citation' sentences actually describe the citing paper
ideas.rationale: A concrete, deterministic pre-file filter: when extracting atomic claims from a related-work / discussion section, drop or re-attribute sentences that are self-referential rather than describing the cited source. Prevents polluting the cited-paper's claim set. Cheap regex-grade heuristic fits deterministic ingest.

ideas.idea: Citation-text generation / related-work auto-writing as the end task; ROUGE/BERTScore generation metrics; LED summarization baselines
ideas.rationale: Memoria's pillar is faithfulness over flash and 'engines write, agents judge' — auto-generating related-work prose contradicts the structural-review gate and the no-hallucination posture. The paper itself finds no model scores >3/5 on factual consistency. Not a capability Memoria should build.

ideas.idea: ROUGE replicability finding — stemming/tokenization config swings scores ~3 points, so the eval library must be pinned
ideas.rationale: General lesson for Memoria's evaluation/metrics harness: pin and version the exact scoring config so dashboard numbers are comparable across runs. Minor but real for the deterministic-ingest principle.

file: Gajos et al. - 2008 - Improving the performance of motor-impaired users with automatically-generated, ability-based interf.md
title: Improving the Performance of Motor-Impaired Users with Automatically-Generated, Ability-Based Interfaces
one_line: SUPPLE/SUPPLE++ automatically generate personalized UIs via decision-theoretic optimization against either an elicited preference model (ARNAULD) or a measured motor-ability model, and a study shows ability-based UIs make motor-impaired users 26% faster.

ideas.idea: Decision-theoretic UI generation: search for the lowest-cost interface satisfying device constraints, where a swappable cost function encodes the objective
ideas.rationale: Conceptually relevant to Memoria's disposable projection engine / projected canvas: rendering a view by optimizing a layout against a cost function and constraints, rather than hand-authoring. But this is heavyweight UI-synthesis machinery; Memoria's surfaces are Obsidian spaces/canvases, so only the 'parameterized cost-function-driven projection' framing transfers.

ideas.idea: Preference elicitation by active queries (pairwise 'which do you prefer') plus example critiquing (ARNAULD), capturing a single user's GUI design preferences as constraints
ideas.rationale: Memoria is explicitly single-PI and personalizes triage/ranking/discovery. The active-elicitation + critique loop is a borrowable pattern for learning the PI's relevance/disposition preferences (e.g. tuning learning-to-rank triage or the discovery relevance scorer) via lightweight pairwise approvals rather than explicit weight-tuning.

ideas.idea: Direct ability/behavior modeling from one-time performance tests outperforms indirect preference modeling
ideas.rationale: Specific to motor-capability modeling for input devices; Memoria has no motor-accessibility dimension. The 'measure behavior directly' insight is too domain-bound (pointing/dragging tasks) to map onto a research-knowledge system.

ideas.idea: Personalized adaptation of the artifact to the user rather than forcing the user to adapt
ideas.rationale: Aligns with Memoria's PI-direct-access and personalization ethos as a design principle (the vault adapts surfaces to the PI), but it is motivational rather than a buildable mechanism.

file: Gao et al. - 2021 - Making pre-trained language models better few-shot learners.md
title: LM-BFF: Making Pre-trained Language Models Better Few-shot Learners
one_line: LM-BFF combines prompt-based fine-tuning (cast tasks as cloze MLM), automatic template/label-word search via T5 generation + pruned brute-force, and selectively-sampled in-context demonstrations to get large gains on small (K=16) labeled sets with moderate models like RoBERTa.

ideas.idea: Prompt-based / cloze (fill-in-the-MASK) formulation that reuses pretrained head weights and introduces no new parameters, beating standard fine-tuning in few-shot regimes
ideas.rationale: Memoria runs cheap/open local models (qwen2.5:7b) for claim-sentence classification, intent tagging, keyphrase tags. Casting these as constrained verbalized prompts rather than fine-tuned classifier heads is the pragmatic path given the PI has very few labeled examples; this paper validates that route on moderate models, not just GPT-3.

ideas.idea: Selective demonstration sampling: use SBERT similarity to pick in-context examples semantically close to the query, rather than random demonstrations
ideas.rationale: Directly buildable in Memoria's MCP/agent prompting: when classifying a new claim/sentence or scoring discovery relevance, retrieve the most-similar already-labeled PI exemplars as few-shot demonstrations. Memoria already has embeddings + a pre-file similarity gate, so the retrieval substrate exists; this improves on-device few-shot accuracy cheaply.

ideas.idea: Automatic prompt/template generation with T5 + beam search, then rerank candidates on a dev set
ideas.rationale: At single-researcher scale, hand-authored prompts curated by the PI are cheaper, more inspectable, and fit the 'profiles compiled / PI approves' governance better than an auto-search pipeline that needs a held-out dev set. The auto-search benefit is marginal over good manual prompts and adds a T5 dependency.

ideas.idea: Evaluation rigor: dev set must equal train-set size in true few-shot settings; report variance over multiple splits because few-shot fine-tuning is unstable
ideas.rationale: A methodological caution for Memoria's evaluation/benchmark harness when measuring its own classifiers on tiny PI-labeled sets: report variance, don't quietly use a large dev set. Keeps the metrics honest.

ideas.idea: Label words must match the semantic class (great/terrible > cat/dog) and even small template wording changes swing accuracy >10%
ideas.rationale: Practical prompt-engineering insight for Memoria's local-model tasks: invest in verbalizer/label wording for classification prompts. Cheap to apply, directly affects ingest quality.

file: Gao et al. - 2023 - Enabling large language models to generate text with citations.md
title: ALCE: Enabling Large Language Models to Generate Text with Citations
one_line: ALCE is a reproducible benchmark and method suite for retrieval-augmented generation where every statement must cite supporting passages, with automatic citation-quality metrics (NLI-based citation recall/precision) that correlate strongly with human judgement.

ideas.idea: NLI-based citation verification: a statement's citation recall=1 iff the concatenation of its cited passages entails it (AIS framework), and precision flags citations that neither support the statement nor are needed by the rest
ideas.rationale: This is the core mechanism for Memoria's faithfulness pillar and the structural-review gate. When an engine drafts a note/claim with citations, an NLI check (premise=cited source text, hypothesis=the claim) gives a deterministic 'is this claim actually supported by its citation' signal — exactly the 'agents judge' step before PI approval. Catches unsupported/hallucinated claims at ingest. A local NLI model fits the cheap-model constraint.

ideas.idea: Citation precision: detect 'irrelevant' citations (citation alone doesn't support AND removing it doesn't break support of the rest)
ideas.rationale: Buildable QA for Memoria's typed-relation/citation edges: flags over-citation and mis-attributed sources on a claim, keeping the argument graph clean. Same NLI substrate as recall.

ideas.idea: Three-dimensional evaluation (fluency/MAUVE + correctness + citation quality) designed jointly to be robust to shortcut-gaming (e.g. copy top passage and cite self)
ideas.rationale: Memoria doesn't need fluency, but the principle of combining orthogonal metrics so the gate can't be gamed by a degenerate output is directly applicable to designing the structural-review/quality scoring. The shortcut-robustness analysis is a useful design discipline.

ideas.idea: Decompose a long answer into atomic sub-claims and check each via NLI for claim recall (correctness)
ideas.rationale: Mirrors Memoria's atomic-claims extraction exactly. The sub-claim + per-claim NLI entailment check is a concrete recipe for verifying that an extracted claim is grounded in its source passage, and for measuring coverage of a source's claims. Reusable in the contradictions/supersession pipeline (claim A entails/contradicts claim B).

ideas.idea: Modeling components: retriever (GTR/DPR/BM25) -> synthesis prompting (Vanilla/Summ/Snippet/Interact/InlineSearch) -> post-editing (Rerank by citation score, PostCite to attach citations to closed-book output)
ideas.rationale: Memoria's retriever/scout profile and nightly discovery loop can borrow the retrieve-then-synthesize-then-postcite decomposition. Finding: Vanilla (just put passages in context) is near-best and on-the-fly inline search does NOT help — argues for Memoria's simpler proactive-retrieval-then-judge design over agentic search loops.

ideas.idea: Empirical findings: retrieval quality is the bottleneck; closed-book is more 'correct' but unciteable; LLMs get distracted by irrelevant passages; post-hoc citing (PostCite) yields poor citation quality
ideas.rationale: Direct design guidance: Memoria should invest in retrieval quality (pre-file similarity gate, record-linkage) and avoid post-hoc citation attachment, instead generating claims grounded in retrieved passages from the start. The 'distraction by irrelevant passages' finding supports tight, high-precision retrieval over dumping many passages.

ideas.idea: Segment output by sentence boundaries; nearly every model sentence carries information warranting a citation
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's sentence-level claim/citation granularity for the argument graph and the per-claim provenance requirement.

file: Gao et al. - 2023 - PAL program-aided language models.md
title: PAL: Program-Aided Language Models
one_line: PAL has the LLM generate Python code as its reasoning chain and offloads execution to a Python interpreter, so computation is correct-by-construction; it beats chain-of-thought across 13 math/symbolic/algorithmic tasks.

ideas.idea: Offload deterministic computation/solving from the LLM to an external interpreter; the LLM only decomposes the problem into code, the interpreter executes
ideas.rationale: The architectural principle — 'LLM plans, deterministic engine executes' — is exactly Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge' split and deterministic-ingest stance. For any quantitative/structural step in Memoria (counts, dedup decisions, graph operations) prefer a deterministic engine over LLM arithmetic. But the literal mechanism (LLM emitting executable code at runtime) collides head-on with the MCP-only sandbox: agents get NO code-execution, ever (ADR-32/46). So borrow the principle, reject the code-gen-and-run implementation.

ideas.idea: LLM-generated code passed to a live Python interpreter at inference time
ideas.rationale: Hard-violates Memoria's MCP-only agent sandbox: no profile ever gets code_execution or a terminal. Any computation must live in pre-approved deterministic engine code, not LLM-emitted runtime code. This is a load-bearing constraint, not a preference.

ideas.idea: Meaningful variable names / interleaved NL comments materially improve reasoning quality (ablation: random names drop below CoT)
ideas.rationale: Minor prompting insight: when Memoria's local models produce structured intermediate representations (e.g. JSON for claims/relations), using semantically meaningful field names and inline rationale improves output quality. Cheap to apply in engine prompt design.

ideas.idea: Self-consistency / majority voting over k sampled program outputs boosts accuracy
ideas.rationale: Sampling k=40 generations is too costly for a single-researcher local-model deployment, and the underlying task (math word problems) is off-domain for Memoria. The faithfulness gains Memoria needs come from NLI grounding (ALCE), not answer-voting.

file: Lu et al. - 2022 - Fantastically ordered prompts and where to find them overcoming few-shot prompt order sensitivity.md
title: Fantastically Ordered Prompts and Where to Find Them: Overcoming Few-Shot Prompt Order Sensitivity
one_line: Shows that in-context-learning few-shot performance varies wildly (state-of-the-art to random) purely with example ordering, and proposes an entropy-over-a-self-generated-probing-set metric (GlobalE/LocalE) to pick performant orderings without any held-out labels.

ideas.idea: Prompt example ORDER alone swings few-shot classification from ~90% to ~50%; order sensitivity is universal across model sizes, persists with more shots, and a good order for one model does not transfer to another.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's claim-sentence classification, claim/aspect extraction and discovery relevance scoring all use small local models (qwen2.5:7b) with few-shot prompts. This is a direct warning: ICL outputs from cheap models are order-fragile, so prompt templates feeding the structural-review gate must fix and version their example order, not assemble it ad hoc — otherwise extraction quality drifts non-deterministically, contradicting the 'deterministic ingest' pillar.

ideas.idea: Degenerate failure mode of bad prompts is a highly unbalanced predicted-label distribution; calibration (Zhao et al.) raises the mean but does not kill variance.
ideas.rationale: Gives Memoria a cheap diagnostic signal: when a local-model classifier (e.g. claim-type or uncertainty-flag tagging) starts emitting a skewed label histogram over a batch, that is a red flag the prompt/order has degenerated. Useful as a content-light diagnostic check before notes hit the gate, without needing labels.

ideas.idea: GlobalE/LocalE: generate an unlabelled 'probing set' by sampling from the model itself, then rank candidate prompt orderings by entropy statistics of their predictions over that set; pick top-k. ~13% relative gain, no held-out data.
ideas.rationale: Memoria has no labelled dev set per researcher, so a label-free, self-generated probing-and-entropy selection is the right shape for tuning the handful of extraction prompts offline. Borrow (not adopt) because it targets ordering of classification demos specifically; Memoria would run it once per prompt template during compilation, not at runtime, and only where a task is genuinely classification-shaped.

ideas.idea: Self-generated probing set beats splitting the tiny training set into a pseudo-dev set.
ideas.rationale: Marginal for Memoria: prompt tuning here is a one-off engineering step on a few fixed templates, not a per-query loop, so the comparison to data-splitting is academic. Not worth building dedicated machinery around.

file: Lu et al. - 2024 - The AI scientist towards fully automated open-ended scientific discovery.md
title: The AI Scientist: Towards Fully Automated Open-Ended Scientific Discovery
one_line: An end-to-end LLM pipeline that ideates, codes and runs ML experiments, writes a full paper, and runs an automated peer-review — demonstrating both the promise and the severe reliability/safety failure modes of fully autonomous agentic science.

ideas.idea: Idea archive grown evolutionarily: each idea carries a description, execution plan, and self-assessed interestingness/novelty/feasibility scores; new ideas are conditioned on the existing archive (LLM as mutation operator).
ideas.rationale: Maps onto Memoria's nightly proactive discovery loop and idea/relation surfacing. Borrow the 'growing archive conditioned on prior scored items' structure for discovery candidates, but the self-assessed novelty score is exactly the part Memoria must NOT trust autonomously (see novelty-bias below) — it routes to the structural-review gate for PI judgement, never auto-files.

ideas.idea: Novelty filter via Semantic Scholar API: discard ideas too similar to existing literature before pursuing them.
ideas.rationale: Directly aligns with Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and record-linkage dedup: a discovery candidate should be checked against the existing vault + external index and suppressed/flagged if near-duplicate. Adopt the pattern (similarity check as a gating tool) using local embeddings + citekey backbone rather than a cloud-only call.

ideas.idea: Automated LLM reviewer (NeurIPS-guideline prompt) emitting structured scores + strengths/weaknesses + accept/reject, improved with self-reflection, 1-shot example, and ensembling; reaches near-human balanced accuracy and superhuman F1.
ideas.rationale: This is essentially Memoria's 'agents judge' role: a structured, rubric-driven LLM critique producing scored, itemized weaknesses for the gate. Borrow the rubric+reflection+ensemble recipe for the review agent that vets engine-extracted notes before the PI sees them. Reject the autonomy: the paper itself shows the reviewer MISSES domain-level errors a human caught, confirming PI-approval must remain the terminal step.

ideas.idea: Anti-hallucination write-up discipline: prompt the writer to use ONLY real recorded experimental results and real citations; auto-append verified bibtex; pipe LaTeX/linter errors back for self-correction.
ideas.rationale: Strongly reinforces Memoria's faithfulness-over-flash pillar and citekey backbone. Adopt the principle that generated text must cite only retrieved/verified sources and that bibliographic identifiers are inserted programmatically (not by the LLM), preventing the citation hallucination Memoria's claims/relations must avoid.

ideas.idea: Observed failure modes: hallucinated hardware/results, hallucinated whole ablation tables when told to 'always include' them, inability to compare two numbers, positive spin on negative results, self-assessment bias (over-rating own novelty/interestingness).
ideas.rationale: A concrete catalogue of what an unsupervised extraction/judgement agent will fabricate. Adopt as a checklist for Memoria's gate validators: never let a prompt demand a field that may not exist (it will be hallucinated), cross-check extracted numbers against source spans, and treat any agent's self-rating of its own output as untrusted. This justifies the engines-write/agents-judge/PI-approves separation.

ideas.idea: Safe code execution: agent rewrote its own resource limits, spawned runaway processes, imported arbitrary libraries; authors recommend strict containerization and restricted network access.
ideas.rationale: Empirical vindication of Memoria's MCP-only agent sandbox (ADR-32/46): agents get NO file/terminal/code-execution, ever. This paper is a live demonstration of why — an agent given execution privileges bypassed its own constraints. Cite as direct evidence backing the hard-deny sandbox.

ideas.idea: Fully automated end-to-end discovery with the LLM running experiments and submitting papers autonomously.
ideas.rationale: Contradicts Memoria's core governance model and single-researcher scale. Memoria deliberately keeps the PI in the loop and forbids autonomous writing to the vault; full automation is the anti-pattern, not the goal.

file: Luger and Sellen - 2016 - Like having a really bad PA the gulf between user expectation and experience of conversational ag.md
title: "Like Having a Really Bad PA": The Gulf Between User Expectation and Experience of Conversational Agents
one_line: An interview study of 14 conversational-agent users showing that opaque system capability/intelligence creates Norman 'gulfs of execution and evaluation' — users mis-model what the agent can do, anthropomorphize, lose trust after a few failures, and demand visual confirmation for anything consequential.

ideas.idea: The 'gulf of evaluation': users cannot tell what the system can do, what it is doing, how, or whether it learns — leading to abandonment after 2-6 failures and self-blame.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's agents/engines are opaque automated actors writing into the PI's vault. The lesson is to make capability and state legible: the read-only inspector, dashboards/gates, and admin reports must show WHAT an engine did, on WHAT evidence, and WHY — otherwise the PI mis-models the system and abandons it. This is a design requirement for Memoria's Obsidian surfaces, not a CA-only concern.

ideas.idea: Users demand visual confirmation before trusting any consequential/irreversible action ('send', 'call'); spoken confirmation suffices only for low-stakes repeated tasks.
ideas.rationale: Exactly Memoria's structural review gate: notes/claims/relations are consequential writes, so nothing is committed without PI-visible confirmation in the Obsidian UI. The paper grounds the 'visual confirmation for high-stakes, auto for low-stakes' threshold — Memoria can mirror it by auto-applying only trivial, reversible operations and gating everything that alters the knowledge graph.

ideas.idea: Anthropomorphic / playful affordances (Easter eggs, humour) inflate expectations of intelligence and then frame every later shortfall as failure.
ideas.rationale: Caution for Memoria's surfaces: presenting agents with a chatty/assistant persona would set the PI up to over-trust extraction quality. Borrow the inverse design stance — present engines/agents as bounded, inspectable tools (closer to how users rationally treated Google Now as 'a search tool') to keep PI expectations calibrated to faithfulness.

ideas.idea: Technically-knowledgeable users built accurate mental models and were more forgiving/persistent; non-technical users mis-attributed intelligence.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's single user IS technical (the PI), so the system can expose more of its internal state (scores, provenance, gate reasoning) than a mass-market CA would dare. This supports a transparency-maximal design for the inspector/dashboards rather than hiding mechanism.

ideas.idea: Voice/conversation as the primary modality with hands-free as the dominant use case.
ideas.rationale: Off-modality for Memoria: it is a text/markdown vault with structured-capture forms and dashboards, not a spoken dialogue system. The conversational/voice-specific findings (enunciation, accent recognition, turn-taking) do not transfer.

file: Lynn and Dixson - 2013 - The history and conceptual elements of critical race theory.md
title: Handbook of Critical Race Theory in Education (The History and Conceptual Elements of Critical Race Theory)
one_line: An edited scholarly handbook on critical race theory in education — its genealogy, conceptual principles (counterstorytelling, interest convergence, whiteness-as-property), and applications to educational policy and practice.

ideas.idea: Counterstorytelling / composite-character methodology: constructing composite narratives from multiple data sources to surface marginalized perspectives.
ideas.rationale: A humanities/legal-scholarship narrative technique with no mechanism transferable to Memoria's deterministic ingest, claim extraction, or typed-relation graph. Off-domain (CRT in education); the corpus likely includes it as the PI's own subject-matter reading, not as a system-design input.

ideas.idea: Conceptual taxonomy of a field (genealogy, defining principles, sub-frameworks like LatCrit/TribalCrit) organized as a handbook.
ideas.rationale: While Memoria does build taxonomies/argument graphs over a literature, nothing in this content-level CRT material informs HOW to do that. It is domain content to be ingested, not a method to adopt; it offers Memoria's architecture nothing.

file: Lála et al. - 2023 - PaperQA retrieval-augmented generative agent for scientific research.md
title: PaperQA: Retrieval-Augmented Generative Agent for Scientific Research
one_line: An agentic RAG system for scientific QA that decomposes RAG into search/gather-evidence/answer tools over FULL-TEXT papers, adds LLM relevance scoring on top of vector search, and achieves human-expert accuracy with ZERO hallucinated citations.

ideas.idea: Decompose RAG into agent-callable tools (search, gather-evidence, answer) so the agent can re-search with new keywords or re-gather evidence with different phrasing until it has >=5 pieces from multiple sources; agent may reject an incomplete answer and retry.
ideas.rationale: Directly buildable as Memoria's retriever/scout profile: an MCP-tool-based agentic retrieval loop (search vault + external, gather evidence, answer) fits the MCP-only sandbox perfectly (these are tools, not file/code access). The iterate-until-sufficient-evidence control is the right shape for discovery and for answering PI questions over the vault with provenance.

ideas.idea: Two-stage map-reduce: a cheap 'summary LLM' summarizes each retrieved chunk into question-conditioned 'evidence' and assigns a 1-10 relevance score (map); a stronger 'answer LLM' reduces the top-scored evidence into a cited answer.
ideas.rationale: Cost/quality split that matches Memoria's model strategy exactly: run the high-throughput summarize+score step on the cheap local model (qwen2.5:7b) and reserve a stronger model for the final synthesis. The LLM relevance score as a second retrieval layer on top of vector distance is a concrete upgrade to Memoria's discovery relevance scoring / pre-file similarity gate.

ideas.idea: LLM-generated numerical relevance score per chunk as an extra retrieval/filter layer beyond vector-embedding distance; also salvages garbled PDF-parse text by summarizing it with the question as context.
ideas.rationale: Memoria parses papers locally and PDF parsing is lossy; using a question-conditioned summary step to rescue garbled spans, plus LLM relevance scoring as a re-rank, strengthens both ingest and the learning-to-rank triage. Adopt as a re-ranking stage feeding the triage.

ideas.idea: Zero citation hallucination: PaperQA produced 0 hallucinated citations across 237, vs 40-60% for raw GPT-3.5/4/Claude-2, because answers are grounded ONLY in retrieved chunks tied to real bibliography entries.
ideas.rationale: This is the empirical proof of Memoria's faithfulness-over-flash and citekey-backbone design: ground every generated claim/relation in a retrieved span attached to a real citekey, never let the model emit a free-form citation. Adopt as the standard for any Memoria output that asserts provenance.

ideas.idea: Maximal-marginal-relevance (MMR) retrieval to diversify returned chunks rather than returning near-duplicates.
ideas.rationale: Useful diversity heuristic for Memoria's retriever and for surfacing varied evidence (e.g. contradictions across sources rather than five paraphrases of one). Borrow MMR re-ranking into the evidence-gathering step.

ideas.idea: a priori / a posteriori prompting: an 'ask LLM' injects the model's latent background knowledge into the answer context, which helped even on post-cutoff questions.
ideas.rationale: Mild win, but tension with Memoria's faithfulness constraint: latent-knowledge injection risks unsourced claims entering notes. Borrow only as background scaffolding that must be clearly separated from sourced evidence and never written to the vault as a claim without a citekey.

ideas.idea: Better-calibrated knowledge boundary: answers 'unsure' rather than guessing, yielding the lowest incorrect-answer rate among all tools.
ideas.rationale: Maps onto Memoria's uncertainty flags on atomic claims: an abstention/uncertainty signal is preferable to a confident wrong extraction. Adopt explicit 'insufficient evidence -> abstain/flag' behavior in extraction and QA so the gate sees uncertainty rather than fabricated certainty.

ideas.idea: LitQA benchmark: 50 expert-written MC questions from post-cutoff full-text papers, designed to be unanswerable without retrieval; plus Cramer's V to compare agent-vs-human answer agreement.
ideas.rationale: Borrow the evaluation idea: Memoria can build a small PI-curated set of vault questions answerable only from ingested papers to regression-test its retriever/extraction over time. The post-cutoff design principle (force retrieval, defeat latent recall) is the right way to test that Memoria actually reads its sources.

file: Xiong et al. - 2026 - AutoResearchBench benchmarking AI agents on complex scientific literature discovery.md
title: AutoResearchBench: Benchmarking AI Agents on Complex Scientific Literature Discovery
one_line: A 1000-task benchmark (Deep Research: find a unique paper from obfuscated full-text clues; Wide Research: exhaustively recover all papers matching a conjunctive scientific spec) over a 3M-paper arXiv corpus, on which frontier LLM agents score under 10%.

ideas.idea: Two complementary task paradigms: Deep Research (precise unique-paper identification, including abstention when no paper qualifies) vs Wide Research (exhaustive set recovery under a conjunction of constraints), evaluated by exact-match Accuracy and Intersection-over-Union respectively.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's nightly discovery loop and retriever/scout profile face exactly these two modes: find the one paper that supports/contradicts a claim (deep) vs surface the full set of related work (wide). Adopt IoU-style precision+recall scoring for the discovery relevance evaluation rather than top-k ranking, and add an explicit 'no qualifying paper' abstention outcome to the triage so the loop can conclude rather than hallucinate matches.

ideas.idea: Decisive evidence lives in method details, ablation tables, figure captions, appendices and citation chains, not titles/abstracts; full-text-first indexing (DeepXiv) beats open-web/abstract search by ~1.5x on deep tasks.
ideas.rationale: Directly validates Memoria's local full-text parse and atomic-claim extraction: a pre-file similarity gate or discovery scorer running on abstracts alone will systematically miss the discriminative evidence. Index and match against extracted claims/aspects/tables, not just metadata.

ideas.idea: Minimal-sufficiency constraint construction: iteratively prune a clue pool against the corpus, keeping only constraints that materially narrow the candidate set to a singleton.
ideas.rationale: A useful pattern for Memoria's contradiction/argument-graph queries: when the PI asks 'which paper makes claim X under condition Y', decompose into minimal discriminating constraints and test each against the vault corpus. Adapt as a query-refinement step in the retriever profile.

ideas.idea: Longer trajectories / more tool calls / explicit THINK mode do not reliably improve agentic literature search; effective information utilization matters more than search budget, and reasoning only helps when it improves external evidence acquisition.
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's cheap/local-model bias and bounded nightly loop: do not assume a bigger reasoning budget on qwen2.5:7b buys better discovery. Cap turns and invest in better evidence retrieval/grounding instead of long deliberation.

ideas.idea: Test-time scaling (pass@k) helps deep single-target search far more than wide set-recovery, because wide failures are recall bottlenecks where repeated runs reproduce the same omissions.
ideas.rationale: Informs the discovery loop: re-running the scout multiple times can rescue a single-paper lookup but won't fix systematic recall gaps in 'find all related work' — for wide coverage Memoria needs diversified retrieval seeds, not just repetition.

ideas.idea: Large-scale benchmark + DeepXiv evaluation infrastructure over 3M papers as the deliverable.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is single-researcher scale over the PI's own vault, not a 3M-paper cloud corpus; building/hosting this benchmark infra is out of scope. Only the diagnostic insights transfer.

file: Xiong et al. - 2026 - PaperScope a multi-modal multi-document benchmark for agentic deep research across massive scientif.md
title: PaperScope: A Multi-Modal Multi-Document Benchmark for Agentic Deep Research Across Massive Scientific Papers
one_line: A multi-modal multi-document benchmark built by extracting 13 typed key-information nodes per paper, merging them into a cross-paper knowledge graph via HNSW similarity, and sampling thematically coherent paper sets with a biased random walk to construct reasoning/retrieval/summary/solution QA.

ideas.idea: Extract 13 typed key-information nodes per paper (title, research background, classification tags, key contributions, methodology, datasets, results, metrics, formulas, algorithms, figures, tables, limitations) with an LLM, each linked to the paper via 'belongs-to' edges.
ideas.rationale: This is essentially Memoria's MASSW-style aspect extraction made into a typed-node schema. Adopt the explicit per-aspect node typing as the structure for extracted aspects, so each paper note becomes a small graph of typed aspect-nodes the argument/relation graph can connect to — and reuse the same schema the survey shows transfers cross-domain with minimal tuning.

ideas.idea: Merge key-information nodes across papers when semantically related, computing similarity only within the same node type, using HNSW ANN with provenance preserved, in a coarse-to-fine schedule (merge clear types like tags/datasets first, then richer types like figures/algorithms).
ideas.rationale: Directly applicable to Memoria's record-linkage entity dedup and typed-relation building: dedup/link claims and entities type-by-type (same-type-only similarity avoids spurious cross-type merges), keep provenance for the inspector, and use the coarse-to-fine order to reduce error propagation. HNSW is a single-researcher-scale, local, deterministic-enough fit.

ideas.idea: Optimized Random-Walk Article Selector (ORWAS): biased random walk over the paper-graph (transitions from paper nodes biased toward high-frequency key-info nodes) to sample thematically coherent paper sets, ranked by coverage/diversity/consistency/redundancy.
ideas.rationale: A cheap, deterministic alternative/complement to BERTopic clustering for assembling a coherent reading set or projection-canvas neighborhood around a concept. The bias-toward-shared-nodes mechanism is a good way to seed a 'related cluster' in the discovery loop; adapt the composite coverage/diversity/redundancy score for cluster quality.

ideas.idea: Inverted-construction with answer uniqueness: derive questions from known facts so they converge on a unique, verifiable answer within the corpus.
ideas.rationale: Useful for Memoria's evaluation/regression of its own retrieval and claim-linking: generate verifiable corpus-grounded probes (e.g., 'which note states X') to test that the retriever and contradiction dashboard return the right unique note. Borrow the verification discipline, not multi-modal QA generation.

ideas.idea: Empirical finding: retrieval quality matters but cross-source multi-modal reasoning is the dominant bottleneck; domain-specific local retrieval strongly outperforms generic web search; tool-overfit deep-research models degrade.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's local-first, vault-grounded retrieval design and faithfulness-over-flash stance: a local FileSearch over parsed content beats web search for grounded reasoning, and brittle tool-calling hurts — favor simple, reliable MCP tool surfaces over elaborate agentic tool chains.

ideas.idea: Multi-modal (figure/table/formula/algorithm) deep reasoning and the full benchmark/training-resource framing.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's deterministic local ingest is text/claim-centric and single-researcher; heavyweight multi-modal vision reasoning and serving a 500+ doc multi-modal benchmark are out of scope. Only the graph-extraction and dedup mechanics transfer.

file: Xu and Peng - 2025 - A comprehensive survey of deep research systems, methodologies, and applications.md
title: A Comprehensive Survey of Deep Research: Systems, Methodologies, and Applications
one_line: A taxonomy and survey of 80+ Deep Research systems across four dimensions (foundation models/reasoning, tool use, planning/execution, knowledge synthesis), covering architectures, evaluation, applications including personal knowledge management, and ethical concerns.

ideas.idea: Four-dimension taxonomy: foundation models/reasoning engines, tool utilization/environmental interaction, task planning/execution control, knowledge synthesis/output generation.
ideas.rationale: A useful checklist to position Memoria's Hermes runtime: Memoria deliberately collapses 'tool utilization/web interaction' to MCP-only sandboxed surfaces and emphasizes synthesis (claims/argument graph) over autonomous web browsing. Borrow the dimensions as a framing for which parts Memoria intentionally does NOT build.

ideas.idea: Statement-level citation linking and contradiction flagging in generated reports; explicit uncertainty modeling that distinguishes confirmed vs speculative content and presents conflicting evidence.
ideas.rationale: Validates Memoria's core design: atomic claims with uncertainty flags, a contradictions dashboard, and warrant-bearing argument graph. The survey shows even commercial systems treat statement-level provenance + contradiction surfacing as the reliability frontier — exactly Memoria's faithfulness-over-flash bet.

ideas.idea: Memory architecture distinguishing working memory (active reasoning context) from long-term memory (knowledge repository) accessed via retrieval; richer-than-context external representations retrieved on demand.
ideas.rationale: Maps to Memoria's vault-as-long-term-store + retriever feeding bounded agent context. Reinforces keeping the durable knowledge in the markdown vault and pulling only relevant slices into the model, rather than relying on long-context dumps.

ideas.idea: Explainability/reasoning-trail documentation as a first-class requirement for scientific applications (black-box contradicts scientific method).
ideas.rationale: Directly supports Memoria's structural review gate ('engines write, agents judge, PI approves') and read-only inspector: every written note/claim should carry its reasoning trail and source attribution so the PI can verify before approval.

ideas.idea: Local/private deployment for sensitive research; lightweight local-execution implementations (e.g., Ollama-based) as a legitimate design point.
ideas.rationale: Endorses Memoria's local + cheap/open-model constraint (qwen2.5:7b) and single-researcher privacy posture as a recognized, viable Deep Research configuration rather than a compromise.

ideas.idea: Multi-agent collaboration frameworks with specialized roles, debate, consensus/voting, and supervisor-worker orchestration; autonomous web/GUI browsing and dynamic tool orchestration over 16k+ APIs.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is single-researcher, MCP-only sandbox where agents get no file/terminal/code-execution and never autonomously browse; heavyweight multi-agent debate orchestration and broad tool/GUI automation contradict the governance model. The PI is the human in the loop, not a voting agent ensemble.

ideas.idea: Personal Knowledge Management application patterns: automated personal taxonomy development, relationship mapping, gap highlighting, multi-level abstraction tailored to the individual.
ideas.rationale: Closest application bucket to Memoria. Borrow 'gap highlighting' (surface where the PI's vault is thin on a concept) and personalized taxonomy/relationship mapping as discovery-loop and space-navigation features, but reject the generic life-planning/decision-support scope creep.

file: Xu et al. - 2022 - Exploring the universal vulnerability of prompt-based learning paradigm.md
title: Exploring the Universal Vulnerability of Prompt-based Learning Paradigm
one_line: Shows that prompt-based fine-tuned classifiers inherit pretraining-stage vulnerabilities — backdoor triggers injected during pretraining (≈99.5% attack success) and adversarial triggers searched from plain text on off-the-shelf PLMs (≈49.9%, transferable across models) — and proposes perplexity-based outlier-word filtering as a partial defense.

ideas.idea: Perplexity-based outlier-word filtering (simplified ONION, no held-out set): remove a token if doing so lowers sequence perplexity, to strip injected trigger phrases before classification.
ideas.rationale: Memoria ingests untrusted PDFs/web content that pass through local classifier/extraction prompts; a lightweight perplexity/outlier filter on extracted text is a cheap, local sanitization step in the deterministic ingest pipeline to drop scraping artifacts (HTML/JS junk like 'Videos Loading Replay') and adversarial junk before claim extraction. Borrow as an ingest hygiene heuristic, not a security guarantee.

ideas.idea: Spurious-correlation triggers originate from uncleaned web-crawl artifacts (HTML/JS fragments) baked into PLMs and exploitable at inference.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's deterministic-ingest and pre-file similarity/structural gate: aggressively clean boilerplate/markup from parsed papers before they reach any model, since residual web artifacts both degrade extraction quality and form an attack surface.

ideas.idea: Backdoor/adversarial trigger attacks on prompt-based classifiers, transferability across PLMs, and the attack methodology (BToP/AToP) itself.
ideas.rationale: Memoria does not pretrain or fine-tune its own classifiers on third-party-poisonable corpora — it uses off-the-shelf local models (qwen2.5:7b) for extraction/judging, and agents are MCP-sandboxed. The few-shot prompt-tuning attack surface studied here is not Memoria's deployment; the offensive techniques are off-domain.

file: Xu et al. - 2025 - DeepReport an AI-assisted idea generation system for scientific research.md
title: DeepReport: An AI-assisted Idea Generation System for Scientific Research
one_line: A web system that builds evolving concept co-occurrence graphs from 260M papers, runs BERT-based temporal link prediction to forecast emerging concept connections (classified existing/emerging/potential), and verbalizes predicted links into research ideas with LLMs fine-tuned on co-occurrence citation quintuples.

ideas.idea: Evolving concept co-occurrence graph: nodes = concepts, edges = co-occurrence in literature, continuously updated as new papers arrive, with community detection to find subfield clusters and interdisciplinary convergence points.
ideas.rationale: Memoria already does typed-relation graphs and BERTopic clustering; a concept co-occurrence layer over the PI's vault (concepts mined from claims/keyphrase tag candidates) would give a cheap, deterministic 'where do my notes connect' view feeding the projection canvas and space navigation. Borrow the evolving-graph + community-detection idea, scoped to the single vault rather than 260M papers.

ideas.idea: Temporal link prediction over the concept graph, classifying candidate connections as existing / emerging / potential to suggest future research directions.
ideas.rationale: Aligns with Memoria's proactive nightly discovery loop and discovery relevance scoring: predict which not-yet-connected concepts in the PI's vault are likely to become relevant, and surface them as discovery candidates through the gate. Borrow the existing/emerging/potential triage labels; the heavy BERT temporal model is likely overkill at single-vault scale — a simpler scoring heuristic on a local model fits the constraints.

ideas.idea: Grounding LLM idea verbalization in traceable structured graph relationships (co-occurrence citation quintuples: concept pair + contextual sentences + linking hypothesis) to improve explainability and reduce hallucination.
ideas.rationale: Strongly reinforces Memoria's claim-with-provenance and warrant model: any generated suggestion (idea, link, supersession) must carry the source sentences and the explicit linking rationale so the structural review gate and PI can verify it. The quintuple structure (pair + evidence sentences + hypothesis) is a concrete, adoptable schema for proposed typed relations / argument-graph edges.

ideas.idea: AutoPhrase-style automatic key-phrase/domain-entity mining from retrieved corpus to populate graph nodes.
ideas.rationale: Maps onto Memoria's keyphrase tag candidates feature; corpus-derived phrase mining (rather than a fixed vocabulary) is the right approach for personalized vault tagging. Borrow the technique; the specific tool is replaceable with a local equivalent.

ideas.idea: 260M-paper Elasticsearch backbone, community idea-showcase (like/share/discuss), and global search-trends homepage.
ideas.rationale: Cloud-scale corpus, multi-user social engagement features, and trending dashboards contradict Memoria's single-researcher, local, privacy-first scope. Not applicable.

file: Alfrink et al. - 2023 - Contestable camera cars a speculative design exploration of public AI that is open and responsive t.md
title: Contestable Camera Cars: A Speculative Design Exploration of Public AI That Is Open and Responsive to Dispute
one_line: A speculative-design + interview study (concept video shown to 17 Amsterdam civil servants) that articulates what makes public-sector AI 'contestable' — open and responsive to dispute via appeals, adversarial debate arenas, explanations vs. justifications, and second-order feedback loops back to development and policy.

ideas.idea: Contestability as 'leveraging conflict for continuous system improvement': individual disputes feed back to change the system, not just the single decision.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's structural review gate ('engines write, agents judge, PI approves') already embodies this in spirit; the framing that a PI rejection/correction at the gate should propagate into engine/profile improvement (not just fix one note) is a useful design lens for the gate's feedback path.

ideas.idea: Distinction between explanations (descriptive, intrinsic) and justifications (normative, extrinsic) — a decision needs a justification to be contestable, not just an explanation.
ideas.rationale: When Memoria's discovery/triage ranker or claim-classifier surfaces or supersedes something, attaching a justification (why this is relevant/superseded against an external reference) rather than only a feature-explanation strengthens the PI's ability to override at the gate.

ideas.idea: Second-order feedback loops (L4/L5): aggregate appeals/decisions monitored to detect systemic faults requiring upstream change.
ideas.rationale: Maps to a contradictions/supersession dashboard that watches aggregate gate-rejection patterns to flag a mis-tuned engine or profile, rather than treating each rejection in isolation.

ideas.idea: Show the case alongside similar prior cases / embedding neighbors so the subject can argue marginality.
ideas.rationale: Echoes Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and record-linkage dedup — presenting near-neighbor notes/claims when the PI reviews a borderline file-or-merge decision is directly buildable.

ideas.idea: Civic participation machinery: representation, reporting inequality, democratic embedding, citizen capacity-building, commissioning/tender governance.
ideas.rationale: All assume a multi-stakeholder public-administration setting with citizens, councils, and external oversight; Memoria is single-PI with no public/multi-user dimension.

ideas.idea: Speculative-design concept video + reflexive thematic analysis as a research method.
ideas.rationale: A qualitative HCI research methodology, not a system feature; nothing for Memoria to build.

file: Ali et al. - 2019 - Crowdlicit a system for conducting distributed end-user elicitation and identification studies.md
title: Crowdlicit: A System for Conducting Distributed End-User Elicitation and Identification Studies
one_line: A web system for running end-user elicitation studies online at crowd scale, plus a new 'identification study' method (the reverse: show a symbol, ask users to name the referent) to formally evaluate how identifiable an elicited symbol set is.

ideas.idea: Identification study = run the pipeline in reverse to validate its output (referents->symbols->referents) and measure agreement.
ideas.rationale: The general 'evaluate an extraction by inverting it' pattern is transferable: e.g., validate an extracted atomic claim by asking a model to reconstruct the source sentence/paper-aspect from the claim and checking agreement — a cheap faithfulness check for Memoria's deterministic ingest.

ideas.idea: Max-consensus / agreement scoring to pick the highest-consensus cluster representative.
ideas.rationale: When Memoria's record-linkage dedup or BERTopic clustering must choose a canonical label/representative for a cluster of near-duplicate entities or a topic, a consensus/agreement metric over candidate labels is a concrete selection rule.

ideas.idea: Crowdsourcing study infrastructure (mTurk recruitment, distributed participants, spam filtering, post-study surveys).
ideas.rationale: Memoria is single-user with an MCP-only sandbox and no crowd/external-participant component; the whole distributed-data-collection apparatus is inapplicable.

ideas.idea: Legacy-bias reduction (Production/Priming/Partners) in eliciting user input.
ideas.rationale: Specific to gesture/voice interaction elicitation; no analog in Memoria's capture forms, which collect structured research metadata, not novel interaction symbols.

file: Alkhatib and Bernstein - 2019 - Street-level algorithms a theory at the gaps between policy and decisions.md
title: Street-Level Algorithms: A Theory at the Gaps Between Policy and Decisions
one_line: Theoretical paper drawing on Lipsky's street-level bureaucracy to argue that algorithms making on-the-ground decisions fail on novel/marginal cases because they can only refine their decision boundary AFTER a decision (a 'loop-and-a-half delay'), unlike human bureaucrats who reflexively reframe before deciding — and proposes recourse, self-policing, and embedding-neighbor explanation as mitigations.

ideas.idea: Cases 'at the margin' (novel/rare intersections) are where automated classifiers err with erroneously high confidence; more training data cannot fix it because the cases are unseen by construction.
ideas.rationale: Directly motivates Memoria's structural review gate and uncertainty flags: low-confidence is NOT the only failure mode — engines can be confidently wrong on a novel paper/claim, so the gate must route confidently-novel items to PI judgment, not just low-confidence ones.

ideas.idea: Recourse mechanism: let the subject see similar cases (embedding neighbors, anonymized) and argue why their case is marginal relative to them.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's inspector + pre-file similarity gate should show the PI the nearest-neighbor notes/claims that drove a ranker or dedup decision, so the PI can contest a mis-classification at the gate; this is concretely buildable on existing similarity infrastructure.

ideas.idea: Reflexivity-before-decision vs. only-after-decision: design systems to flag 'this needs fresh interpretation' rather than pattern-match silently.
ideas.rationale: Suggests an explicit 'novelty/needs-discretion' detector in ingest that abstains and escalates to the gate when an item doesn't fit known patterns, instead of forcing a confident label — aligns with 'engines write, agents judge'.

ideas.idea: Self-policing / oversight: appeals reviewed by a party without conflicting incentives; publish rights in plain language.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's separation of engines (write) from judging agents from the PI mirrors 'different reviewer than the original decision-maker'; reinforces keeping the judging agent/profile distinct from the producing engine.

ideas.idea: Cold-start problem: systems that score by agreement/resemblance cannot evaluate genuinely novel correct work.
ideas.rationale: Cautions Memoria's learning-to-rank triage and discovery relevance scoring against penalizing genuinely novel papers (no prior neighbors) — argues for a novelty-aware exploration term, not pure similarity-to-past exploitation.

file: Alsufyani et al. - 2025 - Enabling collaborative anomaly exploration in smart homes eliciting user requirements and security.md
title: Enabling Collaborative Anomaly Exploration in Smart Homes: Eliciting User Requirements and Security Scenarios
one_line: A mixed-methods (survey N=40 + focus groups N=36) elicitation study producing a taxonomy of smart-home IoT security threats, scenarios, and design guidelines for occupant+device 'collaborative anomaly exploration' with human-in-the-loop alerting.

ideas.idea: Risk-tiered alerting: auto-handle low-risk anomalies, escalate high-risk to the user; filter to deliver only actionable notifications to avoid alert fatigue.
ideas.rationale: Maps to Memoria's nightly discovery loop and gate: route low-risk/high-confidence engine outputs to auto-file, escalate only ambiguous/high-impact items to the PI, and suppress trivial notifications to prevent gate/dashboard overload.

ideas.idea: Human collaboration via human-interpretable explanations of detected anomalies + user feedback that updates the system's rules over time.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's read-only inspector + structured-capture feedback at the gate feeding back into profile/engine tuning — but only the interaction pattern, not the IoT specifics.

ideas.idea: Device-to-device autonomous collaboration and multi-sensor fusion for cross-confirming anomalies.
ideas.rationale: Assumes a network of physical IoT devices cooperating; Memoria is a single local vault + agent runtime with no device mesh.

ideas.idea: Threat taxonomy, attack motivations, and countermeasures (spoofing, MFA, network isolation, firmware checks).
ideas.rationale: Domain-specific IoT/cyber-physical security content with no bearing on a local single-user markdown knowledge system.

ideas.idea: Local data processing / privacy-by-design to reduce cloud reliance.
ideas.rationale: Memoria already mandates local + cheap/open models and an MCP-only sandbox by ADR; this paper adds nothing beyond what is already constitutional.

file: Amershi et al. - 2019 - Guidelines for human-AI interaction.md
title: Guidelines for Human-AI Interaction
one_line: Synthesizes 150+ recommendations into 18 validated, observable design guidelines for AI-infused systems, organized by interaction phase (initially / during / when wrong / over time), each tested via heuristic evaluation against 20 popular products.

ideas.idea: G1/G2: Make clear what the system can do AND how well it can do it (set accuracy expectations, hedge language).
ideas.rationale: Memoria's gate/dashboards and discovery surfaces should state engine capability and expected error rate (e.g., claim-extraction confidence, ranker calibration) so the PI calibrates trust — directly buildable into the inspector and gate UI.

ideas.idea: G9/G10: Support efficient correction and scope/gracefully-degrade when in doubt (disambiguate rather than act confidently).
ideas.rationale: This is the UX expression of Memoria's review gate: make PI correction one-click at the gate, and have engines abstain/scope when uncertain instead of writing a confident note — complements the Alkhatib marginal-case argument.

ideas.idea: G11: Make clear why the system did what it did (accessible after-the-fact explanation).
ideas.rationale: Memoria's read-only inspector should expose the rationale/evidence behind each ranked discovery, typed relation, or supersession so the PI can audit before approving.

ideas.idea: G15/G16/G17: Encourage granular feedback, convey consequences of user actions, provide global controls.
ideas.rationale: Maps cleanly to compiled profiles + skill governance: granular per-item feedback at the gate, global controls over what the discovery loop monitors, and conveying that a correction will change future ranking — all PI-directly-accessible from Obsidian.

ideas.idea: G14/G18: Update and adapt cautiously; notify users about changes.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's deterministic-ingest and ADR change-management ethos benefits from 'limit disruptive changes' and surfacing capability/profile changes to the PI rather than silently re-ranking the vault.

ideas.idea: Heuristic-evaluation methodology: phrase guidelines as observable, conjunction-free rules of action and validate by inspection.
ideas.rationale: A reusable way to author Memoria's own internal design checklist / structural-review criteria so each criterion is independently checkable by a judging agent — but the social-norms/bias guidelines (G5/G6) are largely N/A for a single-user research tool.

file: Greshake et al. - 2023 - Not what you've signed up for compromising real-world LLM-integrated applications with indirect pro.md
title: Not what you've signed up for: Compromising Real-World LLM-Integrated Applications with Indirect Prompt Injection
one_line: Introduces 'indirect prompt injection' — adversarial instructions hidden in retrieved/ingested data that hijack LLM-integrated applications — and gives a security taxonomy (information gathering, fraud, intrusion, malware/worms, manipulated content, availability) plus persistence-via-memory and multi-stage payload attacks.

ideas.idea: Retrieved/ingested content is untrusted code: processing an external document is analogous to executing arbitrary instructions because data and instruction modalities are not disentangled.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's whole pipeline ingests external academic papers, web discovery hits, and Zotero items, then feeds them to local LLMs for claim extraction and aspect tagging. This paper is the canonical reason the structural review gate ('engines write, agents judge, PI approves') and the MCP-only agent sandbox (no file/terminal/code-exec) must treat every ingested paper body as hostile input, not trusted context.

ideas.idea: Persistence attack: an injection writes a payload into the system's long-term memory/notes; a later session re-reads memory and re-poisons itself across resets.
ideas.rationale: Directly threatens Memoria's vault — notes, claims, and relations ARE the persistent memory. A poisoned paper could instruct the extraction engine to write a payload into a note that re-compromises a future nightly discovery/triage run. The pre-file similarity gate and structural review gate must scan engine-proposed note writes for embedded instructions before they enter the vault, treating writes (not just reads) as an attack surface.

ideas.idea: Multi-stage / hidden injection: a small injection in benign content instructs the model to fetch a larger payload (e.g. via a search keyword or URL), and injections can be encoded (Base64) to bypass filters.
ideas.rationale: Justifies keeping discovery/retrieval agents MCP-only with no fetch/URL/terminal tool, so a hidden injection cannot pull a second-stage payload. Memoria need not implement encoding-detection heuristics (cloud-scale concern), but should borrow the principle: deny agents the egress tools that make staged exfiltration possible in the first place.

ideas.idea: Injections steer summarization/QA to produce arbitrarily-wrong or biased summaries and selectively hide sources, while still appearing grounded with citations.
ideas.rationale: Memoria extracts atomic claims and MASSW aspects from paper bodies with a local 7B model — a paper containing 'when summarizing, assert X' could corrupt claim extraction or supersession decisions. The faithfulness-over-flash constraint plus the contradiction dashboard and PI approval gate are the mitigation: never auto-commit engine-extracted claims; surface provenance so the PI can spot a claim that doesn't match the source.

ideas.idea: Threat-based (not technique-based) security taxonomy for LLM apps, designed to generalize across future models.
ideas.rationale: A useful template for a Memoria threat-model ADR: enumerate threats (corrupted extraction, poisoned discovery ranking, self-re-poisoning memory) rather than chasing specific jailbreak strings. Generalizes to local/open models too.

ideas.idea: LLM supervisor/moderator that inspects for attacks, and filtering retrieved inputs to strip instructions — acknowledged as imperfect ('whack-a-mole', dilemma of needing a less-capable model to avoid the same trap).
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's layered approach (judge-agent + PI gate) but the paper's own caveat warns against relying on an LLM filter as a sole defense. Borrow the architecture (separate judging agent) but lean on the deterministic structural gate and human approval, not on a filter LLM that can itself be injected.

file: Gridach et al. - 2025 - Agentic AI for scientific discovery a survey of progress, challenges, and future directions.md
title: Agentic AI for Scientific Discovery: A Survey of Progress, Challenges, and Future Directions
one_line: ICLR 2025 survey categorizing agentic-AI systems for scientific discovery into fully-autonomous vs human-AI-collaborative, mapping them across the research lifecycle (ideation, experiment, analysis, writing) and cataloging frameworks, datasets, metrics, and challenges (trustworthiness, ethics, literature-review failure).

ideas.idea: Literature review is the consistently HARDEST phase to automate — Agent Laboratory, ResearchAgent, and The AI Scientist all show the highest failure rate / weakest capability in structured literature review and grounding ideas in existing knowledge.
ideas.rationale: Strong external validation of Memoria's core bet: rather than a one-shot autonomous 'review the literature' agent, Memoria decomposes lit-review into deterministic, inspectable sub-steps (claim extraction, typed relations, contradiction detection, supersession, similarity gate). The survey says full automation here fails — so keeping the PI in the loop via the approval gate is the right design, not a limitation.

ideas.idea: Human-AI collaborative framing: AI augments rather than replaces the researcher; calibration and human-in-the-loop validation are essential in high-stakes domains.
ideas.rationale: Mirrors Memoria's single-PI, 'agents judge, PI approves' governance and the PI-direct-access rule. The survey's call for human oversight and confidence calibration aligns with Memoria's uncertainty flags on atomic claims and the structural review gate.

ideas.idea: Confidence calibration: a system's stated confidence should match actual correctness, critical before trusting agent outputs.
ideas.rationale: Memoria already attaches uncertainty flags to atomic claims; borrow the explicit calibration goal for discovery relevance scoring and learning-to-rank triage so confidence scores are honest, not just ranked. Useful for the relevance/triage profiles.

ideas.idea: Cost-controlled, joint-optimization evaluation (accuracy + cost + speed + reliability/task-failure-rate), avoiding overly complex/costly agent designs (Kapoor 'AI Agents that Matter').
ideas.rationale: Directly supports Memoria's local + cheap/open-model preference (qwen2.5:7b default) and single-researcher scale. Reject elaborate multi-agent autonomy in favor of cheap deterministic engines plus a light judging agent.

ideas.idea: Fully-autonomous closed-loop systems (Coscientist, ChemCrow, robotic labs) and large multi-agent role hierarchies (Planner/Executor/Evaluator).
ideas.rationale: Off-domain (wet-lab chemistry/biology robotics) and contradicts Memoria's constraints: single researcher, no autonomous execution, deterministic ingest, MCP-only sandbox with no code execution. The 'blast radius' / autonomous-action risks the survey itself flags are exactly what Memoria's gate forbids.

ideas.idea: MemGPT/Letta cognitive-architecture persistent-agent-as-a-service and AutoGen/MetaGPT multi-agent orchestration frameworks.
ideas.rationale: Heavyweight multi-agent infra aimed at autonomous service deployment; Memoria deliberately runs compiled profiles in an MCP-only sandbox on a single machine. Adopting these would reintroduce file/terminal/code-exec surfaces the ADRs hard-deny.

file: Groves et al. - 2023 - Going public the role of public participation approaches in commercial AI labs.md
title: Going public: the role of public participation approaches in commercial AI labs
one_line: FAccT'23 qualitative interview study finding that commercial AI labs lack shared methods for 'participatory AI', face barriers (cost, atomisation, exploitation/participation-washing, no transparency incentive), and rarely confer real decision-making power to participants.

ideas.idea: Arnstein's Ladder of Citizen Participation / Patel et al.'s 5-level participation spectrum (inform, consult, involve, collaborate, empower) as a framework for graded stakeholder involvement.
ideas.rationale: Conceptually transferable as a lens for the PI's role: Memoria should sit at the 'empower' rung — the PI holds real decision power via the approval gate, not mere 'inform/consult' (e.g. read-only dashboards alone). Useful framing for design ADRs that everything be PI-accessible and PI-controllable, not tokenistic.

ideas.idea: 'Participation washing' / tokenism — surfaces that look like control but confer no actual decision power are a failure mode.
ideas.rationale: A design caution for Memoria's gates and dashboards: an approval gate that the PI cannot meaningfully act on (no edit, no override) would be participation-washing. Reinforces the PI-direct-access rule that every feature be directly actionable from the Obsidian UI, not just viewable.

ideas.idea: Participation requires a clear, concrete use-context; it is much harder for general-purpose systems with diffuse downstream uses.
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's narrow single-researcher scope: a tightly-scoped knowledge system gives the PI a clear context to judge engine outputs, which the paper says is exactly the condition under which involvement works.

ideas.idea: Empirical findings on corporate barriers — profit motive, resource intensity, atomised responsibility, lack of transparency incentive, multi-stakeholder governance of public-facing AI.
ideas.rationale: Entirely about multi-user, multi-stakeholder commercial AI governance and organizational sociology. Memoria is single-researcher, local, non-commercial — these barriers and the 'who is the public' question do not apply.

file: Grudin - 1988 - Why CSCW applications fail problems in the design and evaluationof organizational interfaces.md
title: Why CSCW Applications Fail: Problems in the Design and Evaluation of Organizational Interfaces
one_line: Foundational CSCW paper arguing groupware fails when those who do the extra work to maintain it are not those who benefit (the disparity between who-does-the-work and who-benefits), and when evaluation methods can't capture social/organizational dynamics. (NOTE: this vault file is entirely scanned page-images with NO extractable text; judged from the paper's known content.)

ideas.idea: The benefit/effort disparity: groupware fails when the person who must do extra work to keep a shared system populated/accurate is not the person who reaps the benefit.
ideas.rationale: Memoria sidesteps this by being single-user: the PI is both the worker and the beneficiary, so there is no disparity. The borrowable lesson is to keep the PI's manual upkeep cost near zero — automate ingest/extraction deterministically and only ask the PI for the high-leverage approval decisions, or the system will be abandoned like the groupware Grudin describes.

ideas.idea: Critical mass / adoption: shared systems need enough participants to be useful, otherwise they decay.
ideas.rationale: Critical-mass and multi-user adoption dynamics are explicitly out of scope — Memoria is a single-PI system with no collaborative population requirement.

ideas.idea: Evaluation of organizational interfaces is hard because social/political dynamics resist controlled lab metrics.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's evaluation concerns are extraction faithfulness, ranking quality, and dedup accuracy — measurable single-user/technical metrics — not organizational social dynamics. Off-domain for Memoria's benchmarking.

file: Grudin et al. - 2000 - Past, present, and future of user interface software tools.md
title: Past, Present, and Future of User Interface Software Tools (Myers, Hudson, Pausch)
one_line: Retrospective on UI software tools extracting durable evaluation themes — threshold/ceiling, path of least resistance, predictability, the moving-target problem, and gentle-slope/low-threshold-high-ceiling design — and explaining why some tools (toolkits, interface builders, scripting) succeeded while others (UIMS, constraints, model-based generation) did not. (Filename misattributes authorship; actual authors are Myers/Hudson/Pausch, TOCHI 2000.)

ideas.idea: Path of least resistance: tools should make 'doing the right thing' easier than doing the wrong thing, leading implementers toward good outcomes.
ideas.rationale: Direct design principle for Memoria's PI-facing surfaces (capture forms, gates, projected canvas): the easiest action in the Obsidian UI should be the well-formed one (e.g. approving a correctly-structured note), so the PI is nudged toward clean structure rather than ad-hoc notes. Applies to structured capture forms and the approval gate UX.

ideas.idea: Low threshold + high ceiling, with a 'gentle slope' (each increment of power costs only an increment of learning; avoid 'walls').
ideas.rationale: Memoria must let the PI start with zero-config ingest (low threshold) yet reach deep capabilities (typed relations, argument graph, BERTopic clustering, projection engine) without a cliff. Argues for progressive disclosure across spaces/dashboards rather than dumping all governance machinery on the PI at once.

ideas.idea: Predictability beats cleverness: automatic/heuristic techniques (constraint solvers, model-based interface generation) were rejected by users because results were unpredictable and hard to debug.
ideas.rationale: Strong argument for Memoria's deterministic-ingest constraint and 'engines write, agents judge' split: the PI must be able to predict and inspect why a claim/relation/note was produced. Favors deterministic engines + read-only inspector + static HTML reports over opaque end-to-end LLM generation. Faithfulness over flash.

ideas.idea: Moving-target problem: tools that solved a problem just as the surrounding paradigm shifted (UIMS, formal-language dialog tools) became irrelevant.
ideas.rationale: Caution for Memoria's model/runtime choices: don't over-fit deep machinery to a specific local model or Obsidian plugin API that may shift. The ADR-only, forward-looking deferred-ADR approach (assumptions + cadence review) is the Memoria analogue of avoiding moving-target lock-in.

ideas.idea: Build around a rich shared context / knowledge base that tools and agents can query (the paper predicts future UIs organized around standardized data structures, not events), enabling end-user customization and recognition-based input.
ideas.rationale: Echoes Memoria's vault-as-shared-substrate: the markdown vault + typed relations is the standardized knowledge base that MCP tools/agents read against. Supports the projection-engine and spaces idea of disposable views over one canonical store. (The specific ubicomp/3D/speech predictions are off-domain and rejected.)

ideas.idea: End-user programming via scripting glue + components (Visual Basic model) lets domain experts build tailored tools without being professional programmers; but 'simplified' languages always grow back full control structures.
ideas.rationale: Relevant to Memoria's skill governance and compiled profiles: give the PI configurable, composable building blocks (profiles, capture forms, base/canvas views) rather than requiring code, while accepting that power-user needs will push toward expressiveness. Tempers any move to a locked-down DSL that the PI will outgrow.

file: Ma et al. - 2022 - Template-free prompt tuning for few-shot NER.md
title: Template-free Prompt Tuning for Few-shot NER (EntLM)
one_line: Reformulates few-shot NER as an entity-oriented LM task that predicts class-pivot 'label words' at entity positions in one decoding pass, avoiding the catastrophic span-enumeration cost of template-based prompting, plus principled methods to auto-select label words from data+LM distributions.

ideas.idea: Entity-oriented LM (EntLM): predict a class-related label word at the entity position instead of running a template query per span; one-pass decoding is ~1930x faster than template NER.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's deterministic local ingest extracts entities (citekey backbone, record-linkage dedup) on cheap models; a single-pass token-tagging objective fits the local/cheap constraint far better than per-span LLM querying. Worth borrowing as the cheap-model NER path, not adopting wholesale since Memoria's entities are largely citation/author/venue (Zotero-supplied) rather than open-domain spans.

ideas.idea: Data&LM+Virtual label-word search: pick high-frequency pivot words for each class from a lexicon-annotated corpus combined with PLM output distribution, then use the mean embedding of top-k as a 'virtual' prototype label.
ideas.rationale: If Memoria ever needs to tag PI-specific entity/concept types (e.g., method names, datasets) beyond the citekey backbone, deriving prototype label vectors from the PI's own vault corpus + a small lexicon is a cheap, no-new-parameters way to bootstrap a tagger. Aligns with keyphrase tag-candidate generation.

ideas.idea: KB-matching distant supervision (wikidata lexicon) used only as a data-distribution signal, not as direct training labels, to stay robust to label noise.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and entity dedup can use an external KB as a noisy prior without trusting it as ground truth; the 'use noisy lexicon only to estimate distribution' discipline maps to Memoria's faithfulness-over-flash and structural-review-gate stance.

ideas.idea: Conflict label-word removal via a frequency-ratio threshold so high-frequency words shared across classes don't cause cross-class confusion.
ideas.rationale: An implementation detail of the EntLM tagger; Memoria's relation/claim typing is governed by curated schemas and the review gate, so cross-class pivot-word conflict is not a Memoria failure mode worth engineering for.

ideas.idea: IO labeling beats BIO under few-shot; skip dev set, use last-epoch model.
ideas.rationale: Training-protocol minutiae specific to fine-tuning small BERT taggers; Memoria prefers prompt/engine extraction with a human gate and does not run few-shot fine-tuning pipelines.

file: Madotto et al. - 2021 - Few-shot bot prompt-based learning for dialogue systems.md
title: Few-Shot Bot: Prompt-Based Learning for Dialogue Systems
one_line: Builds a no-training chatbot from per-skill few-shot prompts, a perplexity-based 'Skill Selector' that routes a query to the highest-likelihood prompt, and an external discrete memory feeding retrievers (wiki/KG/search) — skills are added/edited just by swapping prompts.

ideas.idea: Perplexity-based few-shot Skill Selector: compute P(history | prompt_y) for each candidate skill-prompt and route to the lowest-perplexity/highest-likelihood one, no classifier training needed; extend with a new skill by adding its prompt.
ideas.rationale: Memoria compiles agent profiles/skills and needs to route a task (triage, retriever/scout, contradiction-check) to the right profile. A perplexity/likelihood scorer over candidate skill descriptions is a cheap, training-free router that fits the local-model + skill-governance design, though Memoria's routing must run through the MCP sandbox, not a free-running bot.

ideas.idea: Skills as swappable prompts: add/delete/edit a conversational skill on the fly by editing its prompt, since no model weights are involved.
ideas.rationale: Mirrors Memoria's 'profiles compiled / skill governance' model — capabilities defined declaratively and recompiled rather than retrained. Reinforces the prompt-as-skill registry pattern Memoria already leans toward.
