ideas.idea: External discrete memory dict that stores retriever outputs and user preferences, then injects them into the prompt by skill template each turn.
ideas.rationale: Analogous to Memoria writing retrieved evidence/structured fields into notes that later condition agents; the 'memory is an external store keyed by skill, not model state' discipline matches Memoria's vault-as-memory and MCP-only (no agent-side state) constraints.

ideas.idea: Conversational parsing: generate a DSL query (Wikipedia title, search query, KG path, dialogue state) from history, then execute it against a retriever/KG/search engine.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's discovery/retriever profile can use the same 'LM emits a structured query, deterministic backend executes it' split, keeping the LM out of direct tool execution (sandbox-safe). The KG-path-by-lowest-perplexity decoding is a concrete pattern for traversing Memoria's typed-relation/argument graph.

ideas.idea: End-to-end no-fine-tuning chatbot as a deployable conversational product; GPT-J-6B locally or GPT-Jurassic-178B via API.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is not a chatbot and forbids free-running agents with direct tool/internet access; the chit-chat task suite (PersonaChat, Empathetic Dialogues, Image Chat) is off-domain for a single-researcher knowledge vault.

ideas.idea: Image-sharing/reaction via caption-to-keyword-to-web-search, and large-model-scaling claims for dialogue quality.
ideas.rationale: Multimodal social dialogue and 178B-scale API dependence contradict Memoria's text-centric, local, cheap-model constraints.

file: Maharana et al. - 2024 - Evaluating very long-term conversational memory of LLM agents.md
title: Evaluating Very Long-Term Conversational Memory of LLM Agents (LOCOMO)
one_line: Introduces LOCOMO (very long multi-session dialogues grounded on personas + temporal event graphs) and finds that storing history as a database of atomic 'observations' for RAG beats long-context and raw-log retrieval, while long-context models hallucinate and fail temporal/causal reasoning.

ideas.idea: Convert conversation/history into a database of atomic 'observations' (assertions about each entity) and retrieve over those observations rather than raw logs or summaries; this gave the best QA accuracy.
ideas.rationale: This is exactly Memoria's atomic-claim store: ingest decomposes papers into atomic claims with uncertainty flags, and retrieval/triage runs over claims, not raw PDF text. LOCOMO is direct empirical support that observation/claim-granularity retrieval outperforms log- or summary-level retrieval — validates a core Memoria pillar.

ideas.idea: Signal-to-noise matters: top-5 observations beat top-25/50; adding more retrieved context degrades the reader.
ideas.rationale: Directly informs Memoria's discovery relevance scoring and learning-to-rank triage — keep the retrieved evidence set small and high-precision before handing to an agent. Argues for a tight pre-file similarity gate and aggressive reranking rather than dumping context.

ideas.idea: FactScore-style evaluation: decompose reference and hypothesis into atomic facts and score precision/recall of atomic-fact overlap.
ideas.rationale: Memoria needs a faithfulness metric for engine-written notes and claim extraction; atomic-fact precision/recall is the natural acceptance test at the structural-review gate ('engines write, agents judge') and aligns with the atomic-claim representation already in the pipeline.

ideas.idea: Long-context LLMs underperform on long inputs (lost-in-the-middle), hallucinate on adversarial/unanswerable questions, and misattribute statements to the wrong speaker.
ideas.rationale: Strong evidence for Memoria's RAG-over-claims + cheap-model design instead of stuffing whole corpora into a long context; the speaker-misattribution finding maps to claim-to-source attribution errors Memoria must guard against (provenance/citekey binding, contradictions dashboard).

ideas.idea: Temporal event graph of causally interlinked events per agent, with reflect-and-respond conditioning on events between sessions.
ideas.rationale: The event-graph-with-causal-edges-over-time structure maps onto Memoria's claim supersession and temporal reasoning; borrow the representation (typed temporal/causal edges) for tracking how a research finding evolves, while rejecting the generative-agent simulation framing.

ideas.idea: Five QA reasoning categories incl. an adversarial 'unanswerable — should abstain' class for evaluating memory.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's read-only inspector / evaluation harness can adopt a typed question set (single-hop, multi-hop, temporal, adversarial-abstain) to test whether vault retrieval correctly declines to answer when evidence is absent — abstention discipline supports faithfulness-over-flash.

ideas.idea: Machine-generated-then-human-edited dataset pipeline using generative agents to simulate humans.
ideas.rationale: Synthetic multi-user conversation generation and parasocial-agent concerns are off-domain; Memoria ingests real papers for one PI and does not simulate conversational personas.

ideas.idea: Multimodal image-sharing/image-reaction dialogue and MM-Relevance metric.
ideas.rationale: Out of scope for a text/markdown research vault; the paper itself notes captions replace images with little loss.

file: Majumder et al. - 2024 - DiscoveryBench towards data-driven discovery with large language models.md
title: DiscoveryBench: Towards Data-Driven Discovery with Large Language Models
one_line: Formalizes a data-driven hypothesis as (context, variables, relationship), organizes hypotheses into a hierarchical 'semantic tree', and proposes a faceted Hypothesis Match Score that decomposes gold vs predicted hypotheses into sub-hypotheses and grades context/variable/relationship alignment separately — best LLM agent scores only 25%.

ideas.idea: Structured hypothesis formalism h := psi(context, variables, relationship): every hypothesis is 'under context c, variables v have relationship r'.
ideas.rationale: This is a precise schema for Memoria's atomic claims and argument graph. Capturing each extracted claim as (context, variables, relationship) gives a typed, comparable representation that powers the contradictions dashboard and supersession (two claims contradict when same context+variables but conflicting relationship). Directly buildable into the claim extraction schema.

ideas.idea: Hypothesis Match Score (HMS): decompose both hypotheses into sub-hypotheses; match by context (ctxF1), then score variable overlap (varF1) and relationship accuracy (relacc, with partial credit when predicted relation is broader than gold) per matched pair.
ideas.rationale: A concrete, faceted way for Memoria's 'agents judge' layer to compare an engine-extracted claim against a reference/source sentence and to compare two claims for the contradictions/dedup pipeline. The partial-credit relacc heuristic (broader-encompasses = 50) is a usable rule for claim-supersession granularity.

ideas.idea: Hypothesis semantic tree / semantic forest: internal nodes are intermediate (possibly latent) target variables, leaves are observed independent variables; a derived variable can itself be the target of a prior hypothesis.
ideas.rationale: Maps onto Memoria's argument graph with warrants — a claim's independent variable can be the conclusion of a supporting sub-claim. Borrow the tree/forest structure for chaining warrants, though Memoria works over textual claims from papers rather than dataset columns.

ideas.idea: Context-first finding: accurate context identification is a necessary precondition for getting variables and relationships right.
ideas.rationale: Tells Memoria's claim-extraction engine to nail the scope/boundary condition of a claim first (e.g., population, regime) before extracting the relationship — improves faithfulness of atomic-claim parsing and reduces false contradictions from context mismatch.

ideas.idea: Mask one facet (context, variable, or relationship) of a known hypothesis to auto-generate a discovery goal/question.
ideas.rationale: A cheap recipe for Memoria's proactive discovery loop and for self-test question generation in the inspector: take a vault claim, mask its relationship, and ask whether new papers fill the gap. Reusable for triage-relevance probes.

ideas.idea: Full autonomous data-analysis agents (CodeGen/ReAct/DataVoyager) that execute Python over datasets to verify hypotheses.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's MCP-only sandbox forbids agents from code execution/file access, ever (ADR-32/46). The agentic statistical-analysis loop violates the hard architectural gate; Memoria reasons over papers' reported claims, not by running its own data analysis.

ideas.idea: LLM-synthesized datasets + reflexion-with-oracle-feedback for benchmark scaling.
ideas.rationale: Synthetic dataset generation and oracle-score self-improvement loops are benchmark-construction concerns irrelevant to a single PI's real-paper vault.

file: Malaviya et al. - 2024 - ExpertQA expert-curated questions and attributed answers.md
title: ExpertQA: Expert-Curated Questions and Attributed Answers
one_line: Expert-in-the-loop study across 32 fields that evaluates LLM answers claim-by-claim for informativeness, factuality, cite-worthiness, attribution completeness, and source reliability — finding retrieve-and-read gives the most complete attributions, retrieval source drives quality, and automatic AIS/FactScore metrics have high precision but low recall vs experts.

ideas.idea: Per-claim attribution rubric: split an answer into sentence-level claims and judge each for Informativeness, Factuality, Cite-worthiness, Attribution completeness (complete/partial/missing), and Source Reliability.
ideas.rationale: A ready-made review schema for Memoria's structural-review gate: every engine-written claim/note can be gated on exactly these axes before it is filed. 'Cite-worthiness' + 'attribution completeness' operationalize Memoria's citekey-backbone provenance requirement, and the rubric drives the contradictions/uncertainty-flag fields.

ideas.idea: Retrieve-and-read produces more complete attributions than vanilla prompting (which hallucinates plausible-but-wrong URLs) or post-hoc retrieval; the retrieval CORPUS quality dominates factuality and source reliability.
ideas.rationale: Justifies Memoria grounding all agent claims in the local vault/Zotero corpus (a curated, reliable source) rather than letting agents emit free citations — and argues for the pre-file similarity gate and a vetted retrieval source over open-web. Vanilla-prompt URL hallucination is precisely why Memoria binds claims to citekeys, not model-generated references.

ideas.idea: Automatic metrics (AutoAIS/NLI, FactScore) are high-precision but low-recall vs expert judgement; fine-tuning on in-domain labels closes much of the gap; NLI misses implicit world knowledge and multi-source attributions.
ideas.rationale: Tells Memoria to treat an NLI/FactScore auto-check as a precision filter that flags clearly-unsupported claims, but NOT as a substitute for the human gate (it will miss true-but-multi-source support). Calibrates how much the 'agents judge' layer can be trusted before PI approval; the multi-source-attribution gap is relevant to Memoria's argument-graph warrants that synthesize several sources.

ideas.idea: Sentence = claim granularity is cheaper than fully atomic claims, but loses atomicity so factuality must be judged exhaustively over the whole sentence.
ideas.rationale: A direct tradeoff note for Memoria's atomic-claim extractor: full atomicity costs more but yields cleaner per-fact factuality/attribution. Supports investing in finer claim decomposition where the contradictions dashboard needs precision, accepting sentence-level elsewhere.

ideas.idea: Question typology by information need (directed/single-answer, open-ended, summarization, advice, scenario-based, resource-list, opinion) including scenario-based as the most common.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's structured capture forms and triage can use this typology to classify PI queries and tailor retrieval/answer strategy (e.g., resource-list vs single-answer), and to test the inspector against realistic expert question shapes.

ideas.idea: Building a long-form QA training set by having experts revise answers, then fine-tuning Llama2/Vicuna/FlanT5 on it.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is single-researcher and does not crowdsource 484-annotator datasets or fine-tune answer-generation models; the dataset-construction and model-training apparatus is out of scope, though the rubric it produced is adopted above.

file: Dourish - 2004 - What we talk about when we talk about context.md
title: What we talk about when we talk about context
one_line: A conceptual HCI essay arguing that context is not a stable, pre-definable set of features to be represented and captured, but an emergent, occasioned property that arises from and is sustained by interaction (an 'interactional' rather than 'representational' problem).

ideas.idea: Context as interactional, not representational: contextual relevance is emergent and occasioned, defined dynamically in the doing rather than delineable a priori.
ideas.rationale: Tempers Memoria's claim/relation extraction: which claims are 'contextually relevant' to a note or question shifts with the PI's current activity. Argues against freezing one global relevance model; favors the disposable projection engine and projected canvas (relevance computed per-occasion) over a single fixed context schema. Adopting wholesale would contradict deterministic ingest, so borrow the caution, not the anti-representational stance.

ideas.idea: Systems that display their own context / state for interpretation ('gulf of interpretation', revealing why a system did X rather than hiding it).
ideas.rationale: Directly supports Memoria's read-only inspector, structural review gate, and static HTML admin reports: 'engines write, agents judge' is more trustworthy when the system surfaces WHY a claim was extracted/linked/superseded (provenance, the warrant) rather than presenting opaque outputs. Faithfulness over flash.

ideas.idea: Structure emerges from interaction (spatial hypertext: clusters/piles the user arranges, system reads salient structure) rather than being specified in advance.
ideas.rationale: Aligns with projected canvas and spaces as navigation: let the PI arrange notes/claims and have Memoria detect emergent structure (BERTopic clusters, similarity) instead of forcing a fixed taxonomy. Borrow the principle; Memoria still keeps typed relations as the durable backbone.

ideas.idea: Reflective/tailorable architectures exposing 'deep' system structure for user-initiated customization.
ideas.rationale: Memoria already compiles profiles and exposes skill/profile governance; the insight that customization should reach structure (not just surface UI) supports making profiles/relation-types PI-editable. But run-time reflective code mutation conflicts with the MCP-only sandbox (agents get no code execution), so borrow only the user-facing tailorability.

ideas.idea: Ubicomp sensor-fusion / ambient context-aware computing (location, identity, environment of mobile devices).
ideas.rationale: Off-domain: Memoria is a single-researcher local markdown+agent system, not a sensor-driven ambient environment. No adoptable mechanism.

file: Dow et al. - 2010 - Parallel prototyping leads to better design results, more divergence, and increased self-efficacy.md
title: Parallel Prototyping Leads to Better Design Results, More Divergence, and Increased Self-Efficacy
one_line: A controlled HCI experiment showing that creating multiple design alternatives in parallel (then getting feedback) yields better, more divergent results and higher self-efficacy than serial refinement, because parallel comparison aids learning and avoids fixation on a local optimum.

ideas.idea: Parallel generation of multiple alternatives before feedback beats serial refine-one, avoiding hill-climbing to a local optimum (simulated-annealing analogy: explore broadly before optimizing).
ideas.rationale: Applies to Memoria's discovery/triage and projection engines: when an engine drafts notes/relations or the agent proposes candidate hypotheses, generating several divergent candidates for the PI to compare at the structural review gate is better than iterating one. Borrow as a generation strategy, not a literal UX.

ideas.idea: Comparison/juxtaposition of multiple items side-by-side helps extract underlying principles and key relations.
ideas.rationale: Supports a contradictions dashboard and side-by-side claim-supersession views: presenting competing/related claims together (not one at a time) helps the PI judge warrants. Reinforces a design Memoria already leans toward; borrow the juxtaposition rationale.

ideas.idea: Targeted critique from a fixed corpus of high-level, non-judgmental statements outperformed random or no critique (follow-up study).
ideas.rationale: Maps to the agent-judge layer: structured, principle-based review rubrics (a curated checklist of structural-quality statements) likely produce better gate decisions than free-form or absent critique. Borrow the 'targeted principled critique > random' finding for designing review prompts.

ideas.idea: Self-efficacy / investing in process-not-a-single-idea, separating ego from object; serial fixation makes critique feel like personal rebuke.
ideas.rationale: A human-psychology finding about novice designers' confidence; Memoria has one PI and automated engines. No buildable mechanism — the gate already separates 'engines write' from 'PI approves'.

ideas.idea: Crowdsourced (Mechanical Turk) pairwise similarity rating to measure divergence/diversity of a set.
ideas.rationale: Multi-rater crowdsourcing contradicts single-researcher, local, cheap-model constraints; pairwise similarity for dedup is already covered locally by Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and record-linkage.

file: Du et al. - 2022 - Understanding iterative revision from human-written text.md
title: Understanding Iterative Revision from Human-Written Text (ITERATER)
one_line: Introduces ITERATER, a multi-domain (ArXiv/Wikipedia/Wikinews) corpus of iterative document revisions with an edit-intention taxonomy (Fluency/Clarity/Coherence/Style vs Meaning-changed), plus a RoBERTa edit-intention classifier and evidence that edit intentions improve revision models.

ideas.idea: Meaning-changed vs non-meaning-changed edit distinction, with revision-depth tracking across successive document versions (a claim's content can be updated in later revisions).
ideas.rationale: Directly feeds Memoria's claim supersession and temporal knowledge-updating: when a paper (esp. ArXiv abstract) is re-ingested across versions, classifying whether an edit CHANGES MEANING (vs cosmetic) determines whether a stored atomic claim must be superseded/re-extracted vs left intact. This is a concrete deterministic-ingest signal.

ideas.idea: RoBERTa-based edit-intention classifier trained on ~4k human labels then used to auto-annotate 196k edits (bootstrapping a small gold set to scale via a model).
ideas.rationale: Pattern matches Memoria's claim-sentence classification and keyphrase candidate generation: train a small local classifier on a modest hand-labeled set, then scale. Borrow the bootstrap workflow; a 7B local model or distilled classifier substitutes for RoBERTa to honor cheap/local constraints.

ideas.idea: Edit-intention taxonomy as explicit labels appended to model input to improve generation quality.
ideas.rationale: Conditioning an engine on an explicit intent label improving output is reusable for Memoria's projection/summarization engines (label the aspect being produced). Borrow the conditioning trick, not the writing-assistant goal.

ideas.idea: Finding that standard automatic metrics (SLOR, EntityGrid, FKGL, BLEURT) correlate poorly with human judgments of revision quality.
ideas.rationale: A caution Memoria must internalize for its evaluation/benchmarks: don't trust off-the-shelf automatic text metrics as gate criteria; the structural review gate's human (PI) approval and targeted rubric matter precisely because automatic quality scores are unreliable.

ideas.idea: Models (FELIX) can't decide WHEN to stop iterating; PEGASUS learned to stop near human (~1.6 iterations).
ideas.rationale: Relevant to any iterative agent loop (nightly discovery, revision of notes): needs an explicit stopping criterion (edit-distance/quality threshold) rather than fixed max iterations. Borrow the stop-criterion design for engine loops.

ideas.idea: Treating writing-assistant text revision (grammar/clarity rewriting) as the end goal.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is faithfulness-first knowledge management, not a prose-polishing assistant; rewriting the PI's text is out of scope and risks altering claims.

file: Dwivedi-Yu et al. - 2022 - EditEval an instruction-based benchmark for text improvements.md
title: EditEval: An Instruction-Based Benchmark for Text Improvements
one_line: An instruction-based benchmark unifying editing tasks (fluency, clarity, coherence, paraphrase, simplification, neutralization, and reference-grounded UPDATING) with multiple prompts and metrics, showing instruction-tuned LLMs lag supervised SOTA—especially on updating and neutralization—and that metrics and prompts are inconsistent.

ideas.idea: The 'updating' task: deriving/inserting new information into existing text grounded in retrieved reference documents (WAFER-INSERT, FRUIT), with citations attached.
ideas.rationale: This is essentially Memoria's claim-updating-with-evidence over the Zotero/citekey backbone: when new papers arrive, update or supersede stored claims grounded in retrieved reference chunks, carrying the citation. A concrete, faithfulness-grounded task formulation Memoria can build into temporal knowledge updating.

ideas.idea: Neutralization: removing subjective/biased ('POV') language grounded in editor comments (WNC).
ideas.rationale: Relevant to Memoria's uncertainty flags and atomic-claim extraction: detecting non-neutral / hedged / opinionated phrasing helps flag a claim's epistemic status. Borrow the bias/POV-detection signal as a claim-quality flag, not as a text-rewriter.

ideas.idea: Finding that editing metrics (BLEU/iBLEU, ROUGE/UpdateROUGE, SARI, GLEU, EM) often don't correlate and can even conflict; ROUGE rewards copying the input.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's evaluation design: a single automatic metric is untrustworthy for judging engine output; pick task-appropriate metrics and rely on the human gate. 'ROUGE rewards copying' is a direct warning for any update/dedup scoring Memoria builds.

ideas.idea: Prompt sensitivity: small wording changes (e.g. 'Rewrite this' vs 'Rewrite with different wording') swing performance and robustness; max-performing prompt != most-robust prompt.
ideas.rationale: Critical for Memoria's compiled profiles and skill governance: instruction wording for local engines must be tested for robustness, not just peak score, and frozen via profile compilation. Concrete guidance for deterministic prompt management.

ideas.idea: UpdateROUGE: score only the updated sentences, not the whole document, to avoid rewarding copy-through.
ideas.rationale: A neat metric trick: when evaluating a claim-update engine, measure only the changed region. Borrow for Memoria's internal eval of supersession/update engines.

ideas.idea: Instruction-based zero-shot evaluation of giant cloud models (InstructGPT/GPT-3 175B via API).
ideas.rationale: Cloud 175B API models contradict Memoria's local/cheap/open-model (qwen2.5:7b) and sandbox constraints; PEER-style smaller (3B/11B) fine-tuned models are the relevant comparison, not the API baselines.

file: Edelson et al. - 1999 - Addressing the challenges of inquiry-based learning through technology and curriculum design.md
title: Addressing the Challenges of Inquiry-Based Learning through Technology and Curriculum Design
one_line: A learning-sciences design history of four generations of scientific-visualization software/curriculum, distilling five challenges of inquiry-based learning (motivation, accessibility, background knowledge, managing extended activities, practical constraints) and design strategies (supportive interfaces/scaffolds, staging/bridging activities, embedded information sources, record-keeping tools).

ideas.idea: Supportive user-interface that embeds an expert's tacit knowledge as scaffolding (default settings, contextual labels/units, always-visible operations) so a novice tool is usable without expert priors.
ideas.rationale: Maps to Memoria's PI-direct-access pillar and structured capture forms/inspector: surface provenance, units, default views, and labels so the PI can interpret extracted claims/relations without recalling the extraction internals. Borrow scaffolding-as-embedded-expertise for the Obsidian surfaces.

ideas.idea: Embedded information sources: a resource library linked just-in-time to the investigation tool (Ask Systems / Goal-Based Scenario), providing background knowledge at point of need.
ideas.rationale: Aligns with Memoria's retrieval/RAG and discovery loop: surface relevant prior notes/claims/evidence inline at the moment the PI is reviewing or writing, rather than requiring a separate search. Borrow the just-in-time embedded-resource pattern.

ideas.idea: Record-keeping / structured-hypermedia notebook scaffolding inquiry into questions, conjecture, evidence, plans (Collaboratory Notebook task-model structure).
ideas.rationale: Echoes Memoria's argument graph (claims, warrants, evidence) and typed relations: structuring captured work into question/conjecture/evidence components is exactly the argument-graph schema. Borrow the inquiry task-model as a vocabulary for structured capture; Memoria's version is single-PI, not collaborative.

ideas.idea: Tool-use can degrade: students used a rich visualization tool merely as a point-data lookup, missing its intended analytic use; needed curricular models of use.
ideas.rationale: A caution for Memoria: powerful engines (BERTopic clustering, projection canvas) may be under-used or misused without guided exemplars; ship example workflows/spaces so the PI uses them as intended. Borrow the 'provide models of use' lesson.

ideas.idea: Iterative design history grounded in real classroom observation across four generations, fitting practical constraints (schedule, hardware, performance).
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's ADR-driven iterative method and single-researcher practical constraints (local hardware, cheap models, fixed UI); the 'practical constraints can doom a design' lesson validates the test-env hardware/model discipline. Borrow as method, not a buildable feature.

ideas.idea: K-12 science-classroom pedagogy, motivation/self-efficacy of students, multi-student collaboration over a network.
ideas.rationale: Off-domain: Memoria serves one expert PI, not motivating novice students; classroom-specific and multi-user collaboration concerns don't transfer.

file: Card and Moran - 1986 - User technology—from pointing to pondering.md
title: User Technology — From Pointing to Pondering
one_line: A retrospective from Xerox PARC on the psychology of human-computer interaction (GOMS/keystroke-level model, device studies, and mental-model vs. no-model user performance) charting HCI's shift from low-level input control toward supporting higher-level reasoning ('pondering').

ideas.idea: Engineering-model approach: build predictive cognitive models (GOMS, Keystroke-Level Model) so an interface can be evaluated analytically before deployment rather than only by trial.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's structural review gate is exactly an analytic-evaluation-before-commit philosophy ('engines write, agents judge, PI approves'); the spirit of cheap predictive checks pre-write maps onto the pre-file similarity gate and dedup gate, but the specific GOMS timing math is irrelevant to a markdown knowledge system.

ideas.idea: Mental-model vs. no-model finding: users given a conceptual model of the system vastly outperform on 'invention' (novel) tasks (67% vs 25%) while routine-task performance is unchanged.
ideas.rationale: Supports surfacing an explicit conceptual model of Memoria's claim/relation/argument-graph structure to the PI (e.g. the read-only inspector and dashboards) rather than just task buttons; the payoff is on novel research moves, not routine filing. Insight only, not a buildable mechanism.

ideas.idea: Empirical input-device comparison (mouse fastest/lowest-error vs joystick/step-keys/text-keys) via Fitts'-law positioning-time decomposition.
ideas.rationale: Pointing-device ergonomics are outside Memoria's scope; the PI interacts through the Obsidian UI on standard hardware. No design lever here.

ideas.idea: 'From pointing to pondering' thesis: the frontier of HCI is supporting the user's higher cognitive work (problem-solving, sense-making), not just motor input.
ideas.rationale: This is essentially Memoria's reason for existing — augmenting research cognition (contradictions, supersession, discovery) rather than mechanizing input. Useful framing/motivation, not a technique.

file: Card et al. - 1991 - A morphological analysis of the design space of input devices.md
title: A Morphological Analysis of the Design Space of Input Devices
one_line: Introduces a generative design-space framework: input devices are described as compositions of primitive transducers over a small set of physical properties (position/force × linear/rotary × absolute/relative), letting designers systematically enumerate, compare, and evaluate the whole space rather than point designs.

ideas.idea: Morphological/design-space method: define orthogonal primitive dimensions and a small composition algebra so the entire space of designs can be generated and unoccupied/poor regions identified.
ideas.rationale: Memoria already implicitly enumerates a design space via 110 ADRs and 'profiles compiled'; a morphological lens (e.g. systematically enumerating relation types, claim-aspect taxonomy slots, or surface/space configurations along orthogonal axes) is a useful discipline for completeness checks. The specific physical-device primitives do not transfer.

ideas.idea: Footprint/expressiveness/bandwidth as quantitative figures of merit for comparing alternatives within the space (e.g. mouse vs headmouse vs fingers, bits/s, precision).
ideas.rationale: The pattern of attaching comparable quantitative figures-of-merit to alternatives maps onto Memoria's learning-to-rank triage and discovery relevance scoring (compare candidates on a common scale before committing). Pattern only; the device metrics themselves are off-domain.

ideas.idea: Devices as points in a structured space rather than a flat catalog, enabling 'is there a gap here?' reasoning.
ideas.rationale: Directly applies to hardware transducers, not to a single-researcher markdown knowledge base; no input-device design happens in Memoria.

file: Carey - 2014 - On learning new primitives in the language of thought reply to rey.md
title: On Learning New Primitives in the Language of Thought: Reply to Rey
one_line: A developmental-psychology / philosophy-of-mind reply defending that genuinely new conceptual primitives can be learned (defeating Fodor's nativism) via domain-specific learning mechanisms and 'Quinean bootstrapping' — aligning a meaningless placeholder symbol structure with existing implicit representations until it acquires new content.

ideas.idea: Quinean bootstrapping: a placeholder symbol structure, initially meaningful only through relations among its external symbols, gains content by being structurally aligned with already-manifest representations plus an inductive leap.
ideas.rationale: A theory of human ontogenetic concept acquisition; Memoria's claim/relation extraction is deterministic NLP over existing scientific text, not a model of how a mind grows new primitives. No buildable mechanism for a knowledge vault.

ideas.idea: Possession vs. manifestation distinction and the critique of treating learnable concepts as a pre-existing hypothesis space.
ideas.rationale: Pure cognitive-science debate (against Fodor/Rey); orthogonal to retrieval, ranking, or claim modeling. Off-domain.

ideas.idea: Placeholder-then-fill via structural alignment of two independently ordered structures (count list ↔ cardinal magnitudes; Alex/macaque studies).
ideas.rationale: Even as a loose analogy to schema alignment / record linkage, it is too abstract and psychology-specific to inform Memoria's entity-dedup or graph alignment, which already have concrete record-linkage techniques. Nothing actionable.

file: Carrillo et al. - 2026 - Personal data flows and privacy policy traceability in third-party LLM apps in the GPT ecosystem.md
title: Personal Data Flows and Privacy Policy Traceability in Third-party LLM Apps in the GPT Ecosystem
one_line: A large-scale measurement study of 5,286 OpenAI GPTs showing ~35% of third-party API parameters transmit personal/sensitive data while only ~15% of privacy policies fully disclose it, built on a human-AI pipeline (fine-tuned RoBERTa PI classifier + LLM-assisted policy-to-taxonomy mapping + active payload audit).

ideas.idea: Fine-tuned small model (RoBERTa-large, 355M, ~3.5GB) beats GPT-4o zero-shot on parameter PI classification (Macro F1 0.87 vs 0.61), at a fraction of compute.
ideas.rationale: Direct empirical backing for Memoria's 'local + cheap/open models preferred' and 'engines write' stance: for structured classification tasks (claim-sentence classification, keyphrase tag candidates, MASSW aspect tagging), a fine-tuned small/local classifier is both cheaper and more accurate than prompting a large LLM. Reinforces using qwen2.5:7b-class or even smaller fine-tuned heads for deterministic ingest.

ideas.idea: Use the LLM only as a data-enrichment/annotation aid (TextGrad-optimized GPT-4o to surface rare-class examples; human-in-the-loop annotation), then train the small model on the curated set.
ideas.rationale: Maps to Memoria's 'agents judge, PI approves' division: an LLM can propose/annotate candidates feeding a deterministic engine, with the PI as the human-in-the-loop, but the production classifier stays small/local. A practical recipe for bootstrapping Memoria's own classifiers cheaply.

ideas.idea: Cluster-guided sampling (HDBSCAN over embeddings of unlabeled items, pick representatives per cluster) to build a class-balanced training/annotation set for rare categories.
ideas.rationale: Useful for Memoria's BERTopic clustering MCP and for assembling balanced training data for any in-house classifier or for surfacing diverse-but-representative items in the discovery loop; cheap and local.

ideas.idea: Traceability/compliance grading (complete/partial/broken) by comparing declared behavior (policy) against actual behavior (data sent), surfacing discrepancies.
ideas.rationale: Structurally identical to Memoria's contradictions dashboard and structural review gate: compare a claim/declaration against evidence and flag mismatches with a three-level disposition. The 'stated vs. actual' diff pattern transfers to claim-vs-source faithfulness checks.

ideas.idea: Concern that LLM apps silently forward whole user conversations to third-party APIs, and that instructing the model to redact PII does NOT work (active audit confirmed unredacted payloads).
ideas.rationale: Strong validation of Memoria's MCP-ONLY agent sandbox and hard data-egress denial: do NOT trust prompt-level instructions ('please redact') as a privacy control; enforce egress at the platform/gate layer. Reinforces ADR-32/46 deny posture and that agents get no uncontrolled outbound channels.

ideas.idea: Pre-execution data preview / editable outbound-payload preview and schema linting as platform-level privacy mitigations instead of static policy docs.
ideas.rationale: Mirrors Memoria's structural review gate and read-only inspector: show the PI exactly what an engine/agent is about to write or send before it commits. An 'editable preview of the outbound payload' is a good UI pattern for any Memoria action that touches external services.

ideas.idea: Adopting MCP as the standardized model-API interface but calling for a 'privacy-first' MCP with contextual disclosure of what data each tool call transmits.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is already MCP-centric (Librarian over obsidian MCP, MCP-only sandbox); the paper's privacy-first-MCP framing argues for per-tool data-flow transparency, aligning with profile compilation and skill governance. Confirms the architectural bet while flagging a transparency requirement.

ideas.idea: Multi-Action co-occurrence enabling cross-contextual profiling (data collated across otherwise disconnected services).
ideas.rationale: A multi-app-ecosystem / cross-creator linkage risk; Memoria is single-researcher, local, with no third-party creator marketplace, so cross-contextual profiling is not a threat model here.

file: Carroll and Rosson - 1992 - Getting around the task-artifact cycle how to make claims and design by scenario.md
title: Getting Around the Task-Artifact Cycle: How to Make Claims and Design by Scenario
one_line: Proposes claims-analysis and scenario-based design: an artifact embodies implicit psychological 'claims' (a feature, with its hypothesized upsides and downsides for the user), surfaced via usage scenarios analyzed across a goal/execution/perception/interpretation/evaluation framework, so design rationale becomes explicit and revisable.

ideas.idea: A 'claim' = a feature/artifact element paired with its hypothesized psychological consequences, including explicit upsides AND downsides (tradeoffs), as the unit of design rationale.
ideas.rationale: Resonates with Memoria's atomic-claim + argument-graph-with-warrants model: representing a claim together with its supporting/undermining consequences is exactly a warrant structure. The upside/downside pairing is a good template for how Memoria records a claim's qualifications and counter-evidence rather than a bare assertion.

ideas.idea: Question framework for analyzing an artifact along the stages of action (goal formation, planning, execution, perception, interpretation, evaluation) — a structured probe list to elicit claims.
ideas.rationale: A structured aspect-probe schema parallels Memoria's MASSW-style paper aspects: a fixed question grid applied to each item to extract structured facets. Could inform a checklist the judging agent runs over each note before the gate. The specific HCI stage labels don't transfer, but the grid pattern does.

ideas.idea: Scenario-based design: use concrete usage narratives as the medium for surfacing and reasoning about claims, keeping rationale grounded in real use.
ideas.rationale: Maps to Memoria's structured capture forms and projected canvas / projection engine: concrete scenarios as a lightweight, PI-facing way to elicit and validate design/usage rationale directly in the Obsidian UI. Method-level borrow.

ideas.idea: Task-artifact cycle: artifacts created for tasks change the tasks, which generate requirements for new artifacts — make this implicit loop explicit and analyzable.
ideas.rationale: Justifies Memoria treating its own ADR/design history as a living, revisable rationale graph (decisions change how the PI works, which generates new decisions) — but recorded only in ADR/releasing per the current-state-only rule. Framing insight, not a new component.

ideas.idea: Claims as explicit, accumulable, reusable design knowledge that can be inherited/refined across artifacts.
ideas.rationale: The notion of a shared, cross-project reusable claims library is aimed at a design-community knowledge base; Memoria is a single-PI vault and already captures decision rationale in ADRs, so a separate reusable HCI-claims repository adds nothing.

file: Parrish et al. - 2022 - BBQ a hand-built bias benchmark for question answering.md
title: BBQ: A Hand-Built Bias Benchmark for Question Answering
one_line: A hand-built QA benchmark of templated ambiguous/disambiguated contexts that measures whether models default to social stereotypes when context is under-informative, and whether biases override a correct answer when it is present.

ideas.idea: Ambiguous vs disambiguated context pairs: when context is under-informative the correct answer is an explicit UNKNOWN/'cannot be determined' option, testing whether a model fabricates an answer instead of abstaining.
ideas.rationale: Directly relevant to Memoria's claim-extraction and contradiction engines: a claim that the source does not actually support should be flagged 'unknown/unsupported' rather than asserted. The abstain-when-underinformed evaluation pattern can shape how the structural review gate judges over-confident extractions.

ideas.idea: Separate accuracy from a directional 'bias score' computed only over non-UNKNOWN outputs, since identical accuracy can mask very different error distributions.
ideas.rationale: The insight that aggregate accuracy hides systematic directional error is useful for Memoria's learning-to-rank triage and discovery-relevance scoring evals: track not just hit-rate but directional skew (e.g., over-surfacing one author/topic) so engine quality reviews catch systematic drift.

ideas.idea: Hand-written, source-attested templates with documented provenance for each tested phenomenon, validated by 4/5 crowdworker agreement.
ideas.rationale: The discipline of attaching an attested source/provenance to every test item mirrors Memoria's faithfulness-over-flash stance; a small curated, provenance-tagged eval set (vs auto-mined) is the right model for evaluating Memoria's local extraction engines.

ideas.idea: Nine US-centric protected-class social-bias categories with intersectional subsets; measuring representational harm in QA outputs.
ideas.rationale: Off-domain for a single-researcher academic-knowledge system; Memoria ingests scientific papers, not user-facing QA over people, so the specific bias taxonomy and harm framing are not applicable.

ideas.idea: Caveat that a low benchmark score does not prove a model is unbiased (small sample per template, US-English only).
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's principle that engine eval scores are diagnostic, not certifications; the structural review gate should treat passing an automated eval as necessary-not-sufficient before notes are written.

file: Perez and Ribeiro - 2022 - Ignore previous prompt attack techniques for language models.md
title: Ignore Previous Prompt: Attack Techniques for Language Models
one_line: Introduces PROMPTINJECT and demonstrates goal-hijacking and prompt-leaking attacks where adversarial text inside user input derails an LLM application's instructions, with an analysis of factors that make attacks more or less effective.

ideas.idea: Prompt injection via string-substituted user input: adversarial 'Ignore previous instructions...' text in the data slot can override the application's instruction and hijack the model's goal or leak the system prompt.
ideas.rationale: Memoria ingests untrusted external paper text and feeds it to local LLMs for claim extraction/aspect tagging; that text is an injection vector. The structural review gate and MCP-only sandbox (agents get no file/terminal/code-exec) are exactly the right mitigations, but ingest prompts must additionally treat paper body as untrusted data and never as instructions.

ideas.idea: Separate trusted 'instruction' parameter from untrusted 'data' parameter so the model never follows instructions embedded in data.
ideas.rationale: This is a concrete design rule for every Memoria engine prompt: wrap ingested paper text in a clearly delimited data block and instruct the model to treat it as inert content. Cheap, deterministic, aligns with faithfulness goals.

ideas.idea: Defenses that work: stop sequences, capping max output tokens, putting fixed instruction text AFTER the user/data input, and post-processing/moderating outputs.
ideas.rationale: Directly buildable hardening for Memoria's local extraction calls (output caps, schema-validated/post-processed outputs, instruction-after-data ordering). Low cost, improves determinism of ingest.

ideas.idea: Inverse-scaling observation: more capable instruction-following models are MORE susceptible to injection because they follow injected instructions too.
ideas.rationale: Relevant to Memoria's model-selection stance (qwen2.5:7b default): a stronger fallback model is not automatically safer for processing untrusted text; injection-robustness should be part of engine eval, not just extraction quality.

ideas.idea: Delimiter/escape-character manipulation increases attack success; effect of exact delimiter type is unclear.
ideas.rationale: This is offensive red-team detail (how to craft attacks) with little net-new defensive value beyond the general 'treat data as untrusted' rule already adopted; not worth building tooling around at single-researcher scale.

ideas.idea: GPT-3-era cloud-API success-rate measurements (text-davinci-002 etc.).
ideas.rationale: Model-specific, cloud-API, superseded; Memoria runs local open models, so the specific numbers don't transfer.

file: Perez et al. - 2021 - True few-shot learning with language models.md
title: True Few-Shot Learning with Language Models
one_line: Shows that when no held-out set is available, selecting prompts/hyperparameters via cross-validation or MDL barely beats random and far underperforms validation-based selection, so prior 'few-shot' results overstated LM ability because they secretly tuned on many examples.

ideas.idea: Distinguish multi-distribution vs tuned vs TRUE few-shot learning: any example used to pick prompts/hyperparameters/objectives must be counted as part of the data budget.
ideas.rationale: Memoria tunes prompts and thresholds for many engines (pre-file similarity gate, discovery relevance, keyphrase candidates) on the PI's small corpus. This paper's accounting rule means: be honest that every threshold/prompt tuned on the PI's notes is 'spent' supervision, and don't report engine quality on the same notes used to tune it.

ideas.idea: Prompt choice has high variance and small wording changes (a comma, 'print' vs 'say') swing accuracy; choosing the best prompt without held-out data is unreliable.
ideas.rationale: Core constraint for Memoria's deterministic-ingest goal: extraction prompts should be frozen, version-pinned, and validated on a small held-out provenance-tagged set, rather than informally hand-tuned per engine; expect variance and don't chase marginal prompt tweaks.

ideas.idea: Cross-validation / MDL (online coding) as model-selection criteria usable in deep-learning/low-data regimes.
ideas.rationale: For Memoria's learning-to-rank triage and relevance scorer, leave-one-out CV is a sane, cheap way to estimate generalization on the inevitably tiny single-researcher label set, with the caveat from this paper that it has high variance and may pick worse-than-average configs.

ideas.idea: Larger models make prompt selection HARDER (heavier tails, worse worst-case) because they need more validation examples.
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's preference for a small default model plus frozen prompts over chasing a bigger fallback model whose behavior is harder to tune reliably on scarce PI data.

ideas.idea: Reporting protocol: disclose all prompts/hyperparameters tried, count validation examples in the data budget, evaluate on test only once.
ideas.rationale: Good hygiene for Memoria's engine-evaluation harness and ADR record-keeping; keeps faithfulness claims about engines honest.

file: Press et al. - 2024 - CiteME can language models accurately cite scientific claims.md
title: CiteME: Can Language Models Accurately Cite Scientific Claims?
one_line: A manually curated benchmark of unambiguous single-citation excerpts from recent ML papers, plus CiteAgent (a search-and-read LM agent over Semantic Scholar), showing LMs are weak at attributing a claim to its exact source paper (humans 69.7% vs best agent 35.3%).

ideas.idea: Citation attribution as a task: given a claim/excerpt, identify the exact source paper that the claim is evidence-attributed to.
ideas.rationale: This is almost a named feature for Memoria: every extracted atomic claim and warrant in the argument graph needs its source paper, and Memoria should be able to verify/repair a claim->citation link rather than trust the author's anonymized citation. A 'claim attribution / citation-verification' engine fits squarely into the claims+argument-graph pillar.

ideas.idea: Excerpt quality taxonomy for attribution: attributable vs unattributable, unambiguous vs ambiguous, non-trivial vs trivial; only 'reasonable' excerpts (single clear referent) are usable.
ideas.rationale: Directly reusable as a pre-file gate / classifier for Memoria: before storing a claim<->citation edge, classify whether the supporting sentence is actually attributable and unambiguous; ambiguous/unattributable claims should be flagged for the structural review gate rather than asserted as typed relations.

ideas.idea: CiteAgent = LLM + search(Semantic Scholar)+read(full PDF)+select, iterating up to N actions with rationale-before-action, filtering out papers published after the source.
ideas.rationale: Mirrors Memoria's nightly discovery loop and retriever/scout profile, but must be adapted to the MCP-only sandbox: the agent gets search/read as MCP tools (no raw file/terminal/code-exec), and the temporal filter (exclude papers newer than the citing context) is a concrete, adoptable rule for discovery and supersession reasoning.

ideas.idea: Error taxonomy: misunderstands excerpt (focuses on wrong entity / adjacent citation), stops prematurely at a roughly-matching paper, or finds the citing paper instead of the cited target.
ideas.rationale: These failure modes are a ready-made checklist for Memoria's structural review gate when judging engine-proposed citation links: penalize 'adjacent citation' confusion and 'selected the citing paper not the target' so agents judge before notes are written.

ideas.idea: Full-paper reading beats title+abstract-only retrieval (SPECTER2 scored 0%); attribution needs body text, and only the strongest agent benefited from the read command.
ideas.rationale: Validates Memoria's local full-paper parsing/ingest over abstract-only metadata for relation/claim work, and tempers expectations: a weak local model may not exploit full-text reading, so the citation-verify engine should degrade gracefully (surface candidates for PI approval rather than auto-asserting).

ideas.idea: Small (100-200 item) human-curated benchmarks with reported human accuracy are sufficient and cheaper to evaluate than million-item sets.
ideas.rationale: Perfect fit for single-researcher scale: Memoria's engine evals (extraction, attribution, dedup) should be small, hand-curated, provenance-tagged sets with a human-accuracy reference line, not large auto-mined corpora.

ideas.idea: Decontamination by filtering out instances a strong model already answers from memory, to test genuine retrieval rather than memorization.
ideas.rationale: Useful eval-design tactic for Memoria: when evaluating the discovery/attribution engines, separate 'the local model already knew this' from 'the retrieval pipeline found it' so engine quality isn't inflated by model memorization.

ideas.idea: o1 memorized ~38.7% of the dataset, inflating its score and muddying claims of true improvement.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces the decontamination point and Memoria's faithfulness stance: attribution must be grounded in actual retrieved evidence, and eval must guard against memorization-driven false confidence.

file: Wang et al. - 2022 - LiST lite prompted self-training makes parameter-efficient few-shot learners.md
title: LiST: Lite Prompted Self-Training Makes Parameter-Efficient Few-Shot Learners
one_line: A few-shot fine-tuning method combining prompt-based fine-tuning, self-training on unlabeled data with meta-reweighting of noisy pseudo-labels, and lightweight adapters (only ~14M tunable params) to match full fine-tuning on NLU classification with 10-30 labels.

ideas.idea: Prompted self-training: teacher assigns pseudo-prompt-labels on unlabeled in-domain data to train a student, iterated M times.
ideas.rationale: Memoria does not train classifiers; ingest classification (claim-sentence type, aspects) uses local LLMs (qwen2.5:7b) via prompting, not a custom RoBERTa training loop. The training-loop machinery contradicts Memoria's deterministic-ingest + cheap-prompted-model posture.

ideas.idea: Meta-reweighting of noisy pseudo-labels via gradient-based weight perturbation, filtering out samples with negative weight.
ideas.rationale: This is an inner-loop training technique with no surface in a no-train, LLM-prompting pipeline; not buildable in the MCP-only/engines-write architecture.

ideas.idea: Parameter-efficient adapters so multiple task-specific heads share one frozen encoder backbone, cutting storage 20x.
ideas.rationale: Memoria runs off-the-shelf local models; it does not host or fine-tune per-task encoder adapters. Single-researcher scale makes the storage-sharing argument irrelevant.

ideas.idea: True-few-shot evaluation discipline: no held-out dev set, report over multiple seeds/splits with std-dev, incremental sample injection (D10 subset of D20 subset of D30).
ideas.rationale: The evaluation hygiene (multiple seeds/splits, report variance, nested few-shot subsets) is a transferable discipline for Memoria's own benchmark harness when measuring ingest classifier/extractor quality at the L5 quality tier, where prompt-based classifiers are evaluated on tiny hand-labeled sets.

ideas.idea: Soft pseudo-labels outperform hard ones and stabilize training; KD warmup reduces variance.
ideas.rationale: Specific to the gradient-training regime Memoria does not use.

file: Wang et al. - 2022 - STRUDEL structured dialogue summarization for dialogue comprehension.md
title: STRUDEL: Structured Dialogue Summarization for Dialogue Comprehension
one_line: Proposes decomposing dialogue summarization into a fixed 7-slot structured schema (relationship, theme, two intentions, problem, solution, conclusion) and shows that learning these structured embeddings plus a typed semantic graph + GNN improves downstream comprehension.

ideas.idea: Structured, multi-aspect schema replacing free-form summary: a fixed set of named slots, each either a concise abstractive value or explicit 'N/A' when not inferable.
ideas.rationale: Directly mirrors Memoria's MASSW-style paper-aspect extraction. The 'N/A when not inferable' convention is a concrete, cheap mechanism to encode uncertainty/absence in aspect extraction and avoid hallucinated fills, fitting faithfulness-over-flash and the uncertainty-flag goal.

ideas.idea: Per-slot prompt questions ('Summarize: what is the relationship...?') to query a model for each structured entry independently.
ideas.rationale: A clean, decomposed prompting pattern: one targeted prompt per aspect rather than one mega-prompt. Improves faithfulness and lets each aspect be independently validated by the structural review gate; directly buildable in the prompted ingest engine with qwen2.5:7b.

ideas.idea: Typed semantic graph over the structured entries with named relation edges (has_relationship, faces_problem, finds_solution, reaches_conclusion...).
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's typed-relations + argument-graph design: extracted aspects/claims become typed nodes with named edges. Borrow the idea of fixed typed edges from entities to aspects; reject the GNN/embedding-matching machinery (training-based, not in a no-train ingest).

ideas.idea: Train encoder to emit a vector embedding per slot matched against frozen-encoder embedding of human annotation via cosine, then feed into a GNN (QA-GNN).
ideas.rationale: Requires supervised training and a GNN reasoning module; contradicts Memoria's no-train, LLM-prompting, single-researcher pipeline. The structural value is the schema, not the neural plumbing.

ideas.idea: Multi-task post-training that jointly optimizes structured-summary generation and downstream task accuracy.
ideas.rationale: Training-time co-optimization is off-domain for Memoria, which has no downstream task-accuracy objective to co-train against.

file: Wang et al. - 2022 - Super-NaturalInstructions generalization via declarative instructions on 1600+ NLP tasks.md
title: Super-NaturalInstructions: Generalization via Declarative Instructions on 1600+ NLP Tasks
one_line: A 1,616-task instruction benchmark with a uniform instruction schema (definition + positive/negative examples + explanations) and Tk-Instruct, showing instruction tuning + task diversity generalizes to unseen tasks, with empirical findings on what instruction elements actually help.

ideas.idea: Uniform instruction schema per task: a complete natural-language DEFINITION plus a few POSITIVE and NEGATIVE EXAMPLES (each with a short explanation).
ideas.rationale: Directly transferable to how Memoria authors its compiled agent/skill prompts and ingest-engine task specs: a crisp definition plus a couple of positive AND negative few-shot exemplars per extraction/classification step (e.g., what counts as an atomic claim vs not). Fits the 'profiles compiled' and skill-governance design.

ideas.idea: Empirical finding: task definition + 2 positive examples is the sweet spot; negative examples help a little; explanations HURT when the model isn't large enough; >2 examples add nothing.
ideas.rationale: Concrete prompt-engineering guidance for Memoria's small local models (qwen2.5:7b): keep ingest prompts to definition + 2 positive exemplars, drop chain-of-thought explanations and extra shots that waste context and may degrade a 7B model. Actionable for prompt compilation.

ideas.idea: Models trained on encodings with BOTH definition and examples are robust across encoding variants; definition-only or example-only models are brittle.
ideas.rationale: Argues for always including both a definition and exemplars in ingest prompts so behavior is stable across phrasings; a robustness insight worth honoring even without instruction-tuning a model.

ideas.idea: Cross-task generalization scaling: generalization grows log-linearly with number of distinct training tasks but saturates at ~64 instances/task; task diversity substitutes for model size.
ideas.rationale: This is about training a general instruction-following model at scale; Memoria uses pre-instruction-tuned off-the-shelf models and does not train Tk-Instruct-style models, so the scaling law is background only.

ideas.idea: ROUGE-L as an aggregate automatic metric correlating with human judgment (Pearson 0.998) across diverse open-ended tasks, with caveat it fails on copy-heavy tasks (rewriting/error-correction).
ideas.rationale: Useful caution for Memoria's eval harness: a cheap overlap metric can proxy quality for extraction/summarization but is misleading where output overlaps input; informs metric choice in the L5 quality tier but is not a core build.

ideas.idea: Community-contributed task collection with multi-round peer review + automated schema checks before merge.
ideas.rationale: A multi-contributor crowdsourcing governance model; Memoria is single-researcher, so the peer-review pipeline is off-scale.

file: Wang et al. - 2023 - DiffusionDB a large-scale prompt gallery dataset for text-to-image generative models.md
title: DiffusionDB: A Large-scale Prompt Gallery Dataset for Text-to-Image Generative Models
one_line: Releases a 6.5TB dataset of 14M Stable Diffusion images with 1.8M real user prompts and hyperparameters, plus analysis of prompt syntax/semantics, model failure modes, and NSFW/misuse, aimed at text-to-image prompt-engineering research.

ideas.idea: Phrase-hierarchy extraction: split prompts on commas, run NER + noun-phrase parsing (spaCy), group phrases by shared NP root into a hierarchy; visualize via circle-packing sized by frequency.
ideas.rationale: The NER + noun-phrase-root grouping is a cheap, deterministic way to derive a frequency-ranked phrase/keyphrase hierarchy from a corpus, adaptable to Memoria's keyphrase tag-candidate generation over the PI's note/claim corpus. Borrow the parsing recipe; the image-prompt domain is irrelevant.

ideas.idea: Embedding-space map of a corpus: encode items (CLIP here), UMAP to 2D, KDE contour, label grid tiles with top TF-IDF keywords for an interactive topic overview.
ideas.rationale: This 'embed -> UMAP -> tile -> TF-IDF label' pipeline is a lightweight alternative/companion to Memoria's BERTopic clustering MCP for surveying the vault; the tile-labeling-by-TF-IDF trick is a concrete cluster-naming heuristic. Borrow the labeling idea, not the visualization stack (Memoria surfaces are Obsidian spaces/canvas, not a web KDE plot).

ideas.idea: Alignment/error detection via cosine distance between paired text and image embeddings, flagging pairs >4 sigma from the mean distribution as likely failures.
ideas.rationale: The general pattern of using a statistical outlier threshold on embedding-similarity to flag misalignment maps onto Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and contradiction/anomaly detection (e.g., flag a note whose claim text and its cited source embedding diverge); borrow the outlier-on-similarity mechanism.

ideas.idea: Per-item NSFW/toxicity scoring with a stored numeric score and a user-chosen threshold for filtering.
ideas.rationale: Off-domain content moderation for a public image dataset; a single-researcher academic vault has no NSFW-filtering need.

ideas.idea: Datasheet-for-Datasets style documentation (provenance, collection, consent, distribution, maintenance).
ideas.rationale: Useful for public dataset release governance, but Memoria's provenance is the Zotero/citekey backbone and ADR records; a full datasheet ceremony is multi-stakeholder overhead not needed at single-researcher scale.

ideas.idea: Generation-through-search: search an existing large index instead of re-running the expensive generator.
ideas.rationale: Specific to amortizing image-generation cost; no analog in Memoria's retrieval, which already searches the vault by design.

file: Wang et al. - 2023 - Self-consistency improves chain of thought reasoning in language models.md
title: Self-Consistency Improves Chain of Thought Reasoning in Language Models
one_line: A decoding strategy that samples multiple diverse chain-of-thought reasoning paths and marginalizes over them by majority vote on the final answer, substantially improving reasoning accuracy with no training, plus yielding a consistency-based uncertainty/confidence estimate.

ideas.idea: Sample-and-marginalize: draw several diverse reasoning paths (temperature/top-k sampling) and take the majority-vote final answer instead of greedy decoding.
ideas.rationale: Directly buildable in Memoria's prompted ingest engines for fixed-answer sub-tasks: claim-sentence classification, aspect-slot values, uncertainty/contradiction labels. With a cheap local model (qwen2.5:7b) sampling 5-10 paths cheaply raises faithfulness exactly where a single greedy pass is brittle, and needs no training or extra model. Fits engines-write.

ideas.idea: Consistency (% of samples agreeing with the aggregated answer) is correlated with accuracy and serves as a calibration / 'knows-when-it-doesn't-know' uncertainty estimate.
ideas.rationale: This gives Memoria a model-derived uncertainty flag for free: low agreement across sampled extractions -> mark the claim/aspect low-confidence and route it to the structural review gate for PI scrutiny. Aligns precisely with the uncertainty-flag and 'agents judge, PI approves' pillars.

ideas.idea: Majority vote roughly equals normalized-probability-weighted vote because the LM is poorly calibrated and rates generations as similarly likely.
ideas.rationale: Tells Memoria to use simple unweighted vote (no need to extract token logprobs from a local model, which the MCP-only sandbox may not expose) rather than probability weighting; a concrete implementation simplification.

ideas.idea: Self-consistency extends to open-ended generation if a consistency metric between generations can be defined (e.g., whether two answers agree or contradict).
ideas.rationale: For free-text extractions (atomic claim wording, summaries) Memoria can cluster sampled outputs by agreement/contradiction and keep the consensus, reusing the same contradiction/entailment check it already builds for the contradictions dashboard. Borrow the generalized consistency idea; needs an entailment comparator.

ideas.idea: Self-consistency is robust to sampling params and to imperfect/noisy prompts, recovering accuracy lost to prompt mistakes.
ideas.rationale: Reassuring robustness property: makes the technique safe to apply to Memoria's compiled ingest prompts without heavy prompt tuning per task. Borrow as a design justification.

ideas.idea: Main cost limitation: N-fold inference cost; ~5-10 paths captures most of the gain.
ideas.rationale: Sets a sensible default for Memoria's local-compute budget: cap at 5-10 samples to balance the RTX 4060 Ti throughput against faithfulness gains; only escalate sample count for low-consistency items flagged for review.

file: Liu et al. - 2026 - The last human-written paper agent-native research artifacts.md
title: The Last Human-Written Paper: Agent-Native Research Artifacts
one_line: Proposes the Agent-Native Research Artifact (ARA): a four-layer (logic/src/trace/evidence) file-system protocol that replaces the narrative paper with an agent-executable knowledge package, plus a Live Research Manager, a PDF->ARA Compiler, and a tiered machine-verifiable review 'Seal'.

ideas.idea: Four-layer ontology: /logic (falsifiable claims with proof pointers + typed related-work dependencies), /src (executable spec), /trace (branching exploration DAG with dead-end nodes), /evidence (raw outputs that ground every claim).
ideas.rationale: This is almost exactly Memoria's atomic-claims + typed-relations + argument-graph + supersession design, expressed as a vault-native file ontology. Memoria already has claims, MASSW aspects, and contradiction tracking; the explicit claim->experiment->evidence proof chain and the dead_end node type are directly buildable as note types/relations in the Obsidian vault.

ideas.idea: Cross-layer 'forensic bindings' linking claims to the code and evidence that support them, made machine-traversable.
ideas.rationale: Maps onto Memoria's typed relations between notes/claims; binding a claim to its supporting evidence note (and warrant) is the argument-graph backbone. Worth adopting as an explicit relation type with resolution checking.

ideas.idea: Dead-end / pivot nodes preserving hypothesis, failure mode, and lesson ('git log for research'), so negative knowledge is queryable rather than discarded.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is single-PI and tracks the researcher's own evolving thinking; capturing abandoned directions and the reason for revision (while retaining original rationale) fits claim-supersession and the contradictions dashboard. A high-value, cheap addition to the note schema.

ideas.idea: Live Research Manager implemented as an agent 'skill' (NL spec, no SDK) that runs a 3-stage retrospective pipeline (Context Harvester -> Event Router -> Maturity Tracker) at session boundaries, distilling conversations into typed events with provenance tags (user / ai-suggested / ai-executed / user-revised).
ideas.rationale: Memoria's skill-governance + compiled-profiles model and 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' gate align with the skill-as-spec idea and provenance tagging. Borrow the provenance taxonomy and the maturity/staging-before-crystallization rhythm; but the always-on background capture must route through Memoria's structural review gate, not auto-write, and agents stay MCP-only (no file/shell).

ideas.idea: Progressive crystallization: raw observations are staged in /staging and only promoted to formal claims on 'closure signals' (topic abandonment, explicit affirmation, empirical resolution), avoiding premature structure.
ideas.rationale: Directly matches Memoria's pre-file similarity gate + structural review gate philosophy: don't commit notes until they're settled. A staging area with promotion criteria is a concrete mechanism for the gate.

ideas.idea: ARA Compiler: top-down PDF+repo->ARA translation (Semantic Deconstruction -> Cognitive Mapping -> Physical Grounding -> Exploration Graph), with code-paper reconciliation to surface tacit heuristics, an in-loop schema-validation fix loop, and provenance tags distinguishing stated vs inferred (collective_inference) knowledge.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's deterministic local ingest already parses papers and extracts claims/aspects; borrow the generate->validate->fix loop against a schema and the stated-vs-inferred provenance tag for extracted claims. The full executable-code layer is off-scope (Memoria ingests papers, it is not reproducing experiments), so adopt only the cognitive/evidence extraction.

ideas.idea: ARA Seal: three escalating machine-verifiable levels (structural integrity -> argumentative rigor via a Rigor Auditor agent on a 1-5 rubric -> execution reproducibility), gating before human review; LLM generates findings, verdict computed deterministically.
ideas.rationale: Level 1 (schema/cross-reference integrity) and Level 2 (argumentative-rigor auditor: evidence relevance, falsifiability quality, methodological rigor) map cleanly onto Memoria's structural review gate and could power a claim-quality dashboard. Level 3 execution reproducibility is out of scope. Crucial borrowed lesson: have the LLM emit findings with severity + verbatim evidence spans and compute the grade deterministically, to dodge grade-inflation/finding-score-decoupling.

ideas.idea: Mutation-benchmark to validate the auditor: inject known defects (fabricated claim, missing falsification, orphan experiment, over-claim, rebutted-branch leak) and measure detection; move deterministically-checkable defects (orphans) to a structural level.
ideas.rationale: A cheap, self-oracling way to test Memoria's own structural-review/contradiction engines: seed the vault with planted defects and measure catch rate. The finding that orphan-reference detection belongs in a deterministic check, not the LLM, is a direct design rule for Memoria's gate.

ideas.idea: Withholding ground-truth (evidence layer) from a verification agent to prevent fabrication-by-copying; layered access control over the artifact.
ideas.rationale: Useful pattern for Memoria's MCP-only judging agents: when an agent audits a claim's support, withhold the asserted conclusion so it must reason from evidence. Adapt as a context-scoping rule for the review profile.

ideas.idea: Progressive disclosure via a ~500-token manifest (PAPER.md frontmatter + layer index) so agents load only task-relevant layers, keeping context windows clean.
ideas.rationale: Directly applicable to Memoria's retriever/scout profiles and MCP resource design: a per-note manifest/frontmatter index lets cheap local models triage relevance before loading full content, saving tokens on qwen2.5:7b.

ideas.idea: Artifact lineage: each artifact declares parent(s) and expresses its contribution as a structured diff; consumption becomes maintenance (agents repair staleness, propagate corrections).
ideas.rationale: Lineage-as-diff and staleness repair resonate with Memoria's claim supersession and temporal updating; borrow the 'declare parent + diff' relation for tracking how the PI's notes derive from and supersede prior ones. Self-maintaining upstream propagation is over-engineered for single-PI scale.

ideas.idea: (Human+AI)^2 network / shared scientific commons where agents submit/retrieve/fork certified artifacts across teams.
ideas.rationale: Multi-team, multi-user publishing commons contradicts Memoria's single-researcher, local, non-enterprise scope. The forking/merging social layer is irrelevant; only the per-artifact structure matters.

ideas.idea: On-demand rendering: the canonical structured artifact is rendered into whatever surface the reader needs (paper, slides, demo, dialogue) shaped by reader intent/budget.
ideas.rationale: Mirrors Memoria's disposable projection engine + projected canvas / spaces: the vault is canonical, surfaces are disposable renderings. Confirms the projection-engine direction; nothing new to build but validates the architecture.

file: Liévin et al. - 2023 - Can large language models reason about medical questions.md
title: Can Large Language Models Reason about Medical Questions?
one_line: An empirical study showing GPT-3.5 (InstructGPT/Codex) and Llama-2-70B can pass medical board-exam QA benchmarks via zero/few-shot Chain-of-Thought, self-consistency ensembling, and BM25 retrieval augmentation, with expert annotation of CoT reasoning/knowledge/comprehension errors.

ideas.idea: Self-consistency: sample k CoT completions at temperature, marginalize the answer distribution by majority vote across prompts/samples to improve accuracy and yield calibrated predictive distributions.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's claim-extraction and faithfulness-over-flash stance can use self-consistency as a confidence/uncertainty signal: sample a local model k times on a claim-classification or extraction decision and treat agreement as the uncertainty flag. Cheap and local-model friendly; but k=100 is too costly at single-PI scale, so borrow at small k.

ideas.idea: Expert annotation protocol decomposing answers into three skills (reasoning step, knowledge recall, reading comprehension) with paired success/failure patterns (A-F), allowing one output to carry both a correct and incorrect tag.
ideas.rationale: Useful template for Memoria's LLM-judge evaluation of its own claim-extraction/classification: score each extracted claim along orthogonal failure axes rather than a single pass/fail, and allow co-occurring strengths/defects. Adapt the multi-axis annotation rubric, not the medical content.

ideas.idea: Retrieval augmentation (BM25 over Wikipedia) gives small, inconsistent gains over a strong direct prompt; grounding helps some tasks and not others.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's retrieval is far more sophisticated (learning-to-rank triage, discovery scoring, similarity gate); a 2022-era BM25-Wikipedia result offers no buildable technique and the marginal-gain finding is dated. Off-domain (clinical QA).

ideas.idea: Domain-specific CoT cues ('let's think step by step like a medical expert') tested across 29 variants, with only marginal benefit over the generic cue.
ideas.rationale: Prompt-cue tuning for MCQA accuracy is not relevant to Memoria's ingest/governance pipeline, and the paper itself shows it barely helps. No adoptable mechanism.

ideas.idea: Answering bias: both direct and CoT prompts produce skewed label-frequency distributions vs ground truth, persisting even under permuted labels.
ideas.rationale: A reminder for Memoria's LLM-judge gates: watch for positional/label bias in any classification step (e.g. claim-sentence classification, triage ranking) and check predicted-label distributions against priors. A diagnostic insight, not a component.

file: Logan IV et al. - 2022 - Cutting down on prompts and parameters simple few-shot learning with language models.md
title: Cutting Down on Prompts and Parameters: Simple Few-Shot Learning with Language Models
one_line: Shows that for masked-LM few-shot text classification, 'null prompts' (just inputs + [MASK], no engineered template) plus prompt-based finetuning rival hand-tuned prompts, and bias-only finetuning (BitFit, ~0.1% of params) matches full finetuning, giving simple-and-memory-efficient task adaptation.

ideas.idea: Null prompts: drop the engineered pattern/template entirely (concatenate inputs + [MASK]); under finetuning, prompt engineering becomes nearly irrelevant, so few-shot success is not mainly from prompt inductive bias.
ideas.rationale: Relevant to Memoria's claim-sentence classification / keyphrase tagging if any local finetuning is ever done: it argues against spending effort on elaborate prompt templates for small classifiers. But Memoria's pipeline is generative-LLM + MCP, not masked-LM finetuning, so this is a background insight, not a build item.

ideas.idea: BitFit / parameter-efficient finetuning: updating only bias terms (~10^5 params) matches or beats full finetuning while letting one base model be reused with tiny per-task deltas swapped at inference.
ideas.rationale: If Memoria ever trains small task-specific classifiers locally (triage, similarity gate, dedup), parameter-efficient finetuning keeps storage/compute tiny on the single RTX 4060 Ti — aligned with the local/cheap constraint. Borrow as an option; current stack uses prompted qwen2.5:7b so it isn't immediately needed.

ideas.idea: Principled true-few-shot evaluation: cross-validation for model/hyperparameter selection without ever touching the test metric, and a '# Wins' significance-based comparison (Welch's t-test per dataset).
ideas.rationale: Good evaluation hygiene for Memoria's own benchmark/metrics work: select thresholds via held-out folds, never peek at the final eval set, and use significance tests rather than raw means when comparing engine variants. Transferable methodology.

ideas.idea: Lightweight-tuning comparison (calibration < LM-head < BitFit, diminishing returns toward full finetuning) showing updating hundreds of millions of params on 16 examples is suboptimal.
ideas.rationale: Specific to masked-LM finetuning regimes Memoria does not operate; the detailed adapter/calibration trade-offs have no place in an MCP-only, prompted-LLM, deterministic-ingest system.

file: Logan et al. - 2021 - Cutting down on prompts and parameters simple few-shot learning with language models.md
title: Cutting Down on Prompts and Parameters: Simple Few-Shot Learning with Language Models (arXiv preprint)
one_line: Pre-publication arXiv version of the same paper as 'Logan IV et al. - 2022' (ACL Findings): null prompts + prompt-based finetuning rival engineered prompts, and BitFit bias-only finetuning (~0.1% of params) matches full finetuning for memory-efficient few-shot classification.

ideas.idea: Duplicate of the ACL Findings 2022 version (same authors, abstract, results); only affiliation/wording differs.
ideas.rationale: This is the same work as the 2022 file; for a single-PI vault it is a record-linkage duplicate. Memoria's entity-dedup / pre-file similarity gate should merge these two citekeys rather than store both as distinct sources.

ideas.idea: Null prompts make engineered prompt templates largely unnecessary for finetuned masked-LM classifiers.
ideas.rationale: Same borrow as the 2022 version: a background argument against over-investing in prompt templates for small classifiers; not a build item given Memoria's generative-LLM stack.

ideas.idea: BitFit bias-only parameter-efficient finetuning for tiny, swappable per-task deltas.
ideas.rationale: Same as the 2022 version: a viable local/cheap option if Memoria ever finetunes small classifiers on the test-env GPU; aligned with the local-first constraint.

file: Long - 2026 - AI-supervisor autonomous AI research supervision via a persistent research world model.md
title: AI-Supervisor: Autonomous AI Research Supervision via a Persistent Research World Model
