one_line: A multi-agent research-automation framework built around a persistent, uncertainty-annotated knowledge graph ('Research World Model') of methods/modules/benchmarks/gaps/limitations, with empirical gap probing, multi-agent consensus before committing nodes, and cross-domain '5-WHY' mechanism search.

ideas.idea: Persistent Research World Model as a typed, uncertainty-annotated KG (nodes: paper/method/module/benchmark/gap/limitation; edges: proposes/uses/evaluated_on/has_limitation/causes/solves) that is the orchestration backbone and persists across projects.
ideas.rationale: Memoria already has a typed-relation graph and an argument graph over claims; the typed node/edge schema (esp. causes/solves and has_limitation edges) is a concrete vocabulary to borrow for Memoria's relation taxonomy. The 'world model is the persistent artifact, the LLM is just the engine' framing matches 'engines write, vault is canonical'. Multi-agent orchestration backbone is heavier than single-PI scale needs, so borrow the schema, not the orchestration.

ideas.idea: Uncertainty annotation U in {0,1} on every node AND edge: everything starts unverified (U=1) and is promoted to verified (U=0) only after corroboration / empirical testing; edges carry actual metric vectors.
ideas.rationale: This is precisely Memoria's atomic-claims-with-uncertainty-flags requirement made structural. Adopt verified/unverified status on claims and relations, promoted only on corroboration, as a first-class field driving the contradictions dashboard and triage. The paper's own limitation (binary is too coarse) argues Memoria should use a small calibrated scale instead.

ideas.idea: Gap synthesis from shared limitations: a limitation appearing across >=3 papers is promoted to a field-level 'gap' node — a structural mechanism for surfacing gaps invisible in any single paper.
ideas.rationale: Directly buildable in Memoria's discovery loop: cluster limitation/weakness claims across the PI's ingested papers and surface recurring ones as candidate open problems on a dashboard. Aligns with BERTopic clustering + keyphrase candidates already planned. Single-PI scale, fully local.

ideas.idea: Module deduplication via semantic-similarity equivalence classes, choosing a canonical representative and flagging modules shared across >=3 papers as 'building blocks'.
ideas.rationale: Exactly Memoria's record-linkage entity-dedup with a frequency threshold; the 'shared building block' flag is a useful derived signal for the entity layer. Concrete and in-scope.

ideas.idea: Multi-agent consensus: parallel agents independently produce findings, then a second round with full shared visibility lets them corroborate; only corroborated findings commit to the KG (and naive union of agents is shown to be worse than a single agent).
ideas.rationale: Memoria is single-PI and MCP-only, so a standing multi-agent committee is over-scope; but the corroboration-before-commit gate and the finding that 'naive merge adds noise' reinforce Memoria's structural review gate. Borrow as: require independent corroboration (or a second judging pass) before a claim flips to verified, rather than merging raw outputs.

ideas.idea: Cross-domain mechanism search: 5-WHY root-cause analysis maps a domain-specific gap to an abstract mechanism, then searches OTHER fields using their vocabulary (constraint: not the original field) for solutions; naive cross-domain borrowing without mechanism analysis is shown to be the worst approach.
ideas.rationale: Relevant to Memoria's discovery/scout profile: when surfacing related work for a PI gap, abstract the mechanism and query analogous problems in other fields. Borrow the 'abstract the mechanism first, then translate vocabulary' query-expansion idea; the autonomous method-development loop is out of scope (Memoria does not run experiments).

ideas.idea: Cross-project knowledge transfer: shared nodes (e.g. a method reused across projects) create structural bridges enabling gap transfer; structured KG beats context-window text memory, which 'remembers summaries but cannot reason about shared modules'.
ideas.rationale: Strong evidence for Memoria's core thesis that a persistent typed vault graph beats stuffing prior context into a prompt — justifies the structural memory investment over chat-history memory. Directly supports the existing architecture.

ideas.idea: LLM generates findings; verdicts/quality-gates computed deterministically from a checklist of binary criteria with routing back to the failing stage.
ideas.rationale: Same governance principle as Memoria's gate and echoed by the Liu ARA paper: keep the LLM in the find-evidence role and compute pass/fail deterministically. Borrow the deterministic checklist + route-to-failing-stage pattern for the review gate.

ideas.idea: Model-agnostic, runs on Qwen-72B / local LLaMA-DeepSeek at ~$8-16 or ~$0 local, no GPU required for the orchestration; elastic scaling with token budget.
ideas.rationale: Validates Memoria's local/cheap-model constraint (the paper's main results use Qwen, and it notes <=9B models may fail section-specific extraction — a useful caution for Memoria's qwen2.5:7b default on extraction-heavy steps; consider the qwen3:14b fallback for those). Borrow the caution, not a component.

ideas.idea: Full end-to-end autonomous research supervisor: reproduces baselines on GPUs, develops methods, writes papers, submits to review, and a vision of a community-validated shared world model replacing peer review.
ideas.rationale: Memoria assists a human PI's reading/knowledge work; it deliberately does NOT run experiments, write papers autonomously, or operate a multi-researcher reputation commons. Agents are MCP-only with no code execution, and the PI judges. The autonomous-scientist and shared-commons framing is squarely out of scope.

file: Bach et al. - 2022 - PromptSource an integrated development environment and repository for natural language prompts.md
title: PromptSource: An Integrated Development Environment and Repository for Natural Language Prompts
one_line: PromptSource is a web IDE plus repository (P3, 2000+ prompts/170 datasets) for authoring, versioning, and reviewing natural-language prompt templates as data-linked Jinja2 functions, with community quality standards enforced via code review.

ideas.idea: Treat prompts as versioned, data-linked artifacts written in a constrained templating language (Jinja2 with placeholders + a ||| input/target separator) rather than as arbitrary code buried in app logic.
ideas.rationale: Memoria runs many local LLM extraction tasks (atomic claims, claim-sentence classification, MASSW aspects, keyphrase candidates) on cheap models where prompt wording materially changes output. A constrained, file-based, reviewable prompt template store fits the deterministic-ingest and 'profiles compiled' design and makes prompt changes auditable in git.

ideas.idea: Templating language as the deliberate middle ground between pure code (expressive but unanalyzable) and rigid rule-based generation (analyzable but inflexible), keeping logic minimal so prompts can be inspected and reused outside the tool.
ideas.rationale: Aligns with Memoria's MCP-only sandbox where agents get no code execution: prompts that are declarative templates (not Python) are safer and more inspectable than embedding executable logic, and portable across the engine/agent split.

ideas.idea: Per-prompt metadata: name, source/rationale reference, whether it expresses the original intended task, valid output set, whether the template states valid outputs, and applicable metrics.
ideas.rationale: Memoria can attach analogous metadata to each extraction prompt (which engine/profile uses it, expected output schema, validation metric) to support the structural review gate and reproducibility of nightly runs.

ideas.idea: Explicitly listing valid completions in a separate field and scoring only those completions for closed-set tasks (classification/binary QA).
ideas.rationale: Directly applicable to Memoria's claim-sentence classification and uncertainty-flag tasks on small local models (qwen2.5:7b): constraining to a valid label set and scoring those improves reliability of deterministic ingest classifiers.

ideas.idea: Live side-by-side preview of a prompt applied to many real examples to iterate on wording before committing.
ideas.rationale: A small read-only inspector / dashboard in Obsidian that shows a prompt's output over a sample of the PI's own papers would let the PI tune extraction prompts directly from the UI, matching the PI-direct-access rule.

ideas.idea: Community-driven contribution process with multi-reviewer code review and automatic tests as a gate before merge into the global pool.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is single-researcher; multi-contributor crowd review and inter-annotator agreement do not apply. The 'review gate before commit' idea is already covered by Memoria's structural review gate, so the crowd-sourcing apparatus is unnecessary.

ideas.idea: Shared public pool (P3) for massively multitask prompted training of models like T0.
ideas.rationale: Memoria does not train models; it uses fixed local models. A public multitask training corpus is off-scope.

file: Baek et al. - 2025 - ResearchAgent iterative research idea generation over scientific literature with large language mod.md
title: ResearchAgent: Iterative Research Idea Generation over Scientific Literature with Large Language Models
one_line: ResearchAgent generates research ideas (problem, method, experiment) from a core paper by augmenting an LLM with citation-graph neighbors and an entity co-occurrence knowledge store, then iteratively refines via multiple ReviewingAgents whose criteria are induced from real human judgments.

ideas.idea: Entity-centric knowledge store: a sparse entity x entity co-occurrence matrix built across many papers, used to retrieve relevant entities OUTSIDE the current context to inject cross-domain concepts (e.g., bridging Drosophila panel + CRISPR).
ideas.rationale: Memoria already extracts entities and does record-linkage dedup and keyphrase candidates. A global co-occurrence store over the PI's corpus is a cheap, deterministic, local structure that powers cross-pollination/discovery scoring and the nightly proactive discovery loop without needing a large model.

ideas.idea: Citation-graph-based literature survey: seed with a core paper, expand to cited/citing neighbors, then narrow by abstract similarity to keep the LLM context focused and coherent.
ideas.rationale: Maps directly onto Memoria's Zotero/citekey backbone, retriever/scout profile, and pre-file similarity gate; building the context for any per-paper reasoning from citation neighbors filtered by similarity is exactly the discovery/triage substrate.

ideas.idea: Decompose generation into ordered sub-steps p = f(L), m = f(p,L), d = f(p,m,L) (problem -> method -> experiment), each its own prompt/template.
ideas.rationale: The decompose-and-chain pattern fits Memoria's engine pipeline (each stage a separate compiled profile/prompt), keeping local-model tasks small. Memoria's focus is analysis/claims not net-new idea generation, so adopt the structure, not the goal wholesale.

ideas.idea: ReviewingAgents: multiple LLM critics, each scoring a facet on 5 criteria, feeding iterative refinement; gains saturate after ~3 iterations.
ideas.rationale: Echoes Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge' governance: agent critics can score draft notes/claims against rubric criteria before the PI gate. The 3-iteration saturation is a useful budget cap for a local, cost-sensitive runtime; full multi-agent idea generation is heavier than Memoria needs.

ideas.idea: Human-preference-aligned evaluation criteria induced by prompting an LLM with ~10 human-scored example pairs, so the LLM-judge's score distribution matches human judgments.
ideas.rationale: A lightweight way to calibrate Memoria's LLM-as-judge components (discovery relevance scoring, learning-to-rank triage) to the single PI's preferences from a handful of the PI's own labels - fits single-researcher scale and the personalization goal.

ideas.idea: Reference-free LLM-as-judge (GPT-4) for scoring generated ideas, validated against human via Spearman/Cohen's kappa agreement.
ideas.rationale: Memoria can use a local LLM judge for ranking/relevance, but the paper shows quality collapses on weaker models (Mixtral, GPT-3.5 marginal). Borrow the agreement-measurement methodology to validate that qwen-class judges are good enough before trusting them; don't assume small-model judging is reliable.

ideas.idea: Selecting only high-citation core papers (>20 cites) as quality proxy, with higher-impact cores yielding higher-quality ideas.
ideas.rationale: Memoria curates the PI's own chosen library; gating on citation count would discard exactly the niche/recent papers a single researcher cares about and contradicts the personal-knowledge focus.

ideas.idea: Open-ended autonomous research-idea generation as the system's primary output.
ideas.rationale: Memoria prioritizes faithfulness over flash and explicitly judges/extracts rather than fabricates; the paper itself flags hallucination/plagiarism risk. Autonomous idea generation is out of Memoria's claim-grounded scope, though the supporting machinery (above) is valuable.

file: Bai et al. - 2022 - Constitutional AI harmlessness from AI feedback.md
title: Constitutional AI: Harmlessness from AI Feedback
one_line: Constitutional AI trains a harmless-but-non-evasive assistant using a short natural-language list of principles (a 'constitution') to drive model self-critique/revision (SL) and AI-generated preference labels (RLAIF), with chain-of-thought making the judgments legible.

ideas.idea: Encode governing behavior as a small, explicit, human-readable list of natural-language principles (a constitution) rather than thousands of opaque labels.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's skill governance, profile compilation, and structural review gate can be expressed as an explicit, versioned natural-language principle set the agents must apply - transparent, PI-editable from Obsidian, and far cheaper to maintain than collecting preference data.

ideas.idea: Self-critique -> revision loop: model critiques its own draft against a principle, then rewrites; repeatable in sequence, with most gain on the first revision.
ideas.rationale: Directly usable as a pre-gate quality step: an engine drafts a note/claim, an agent critiques it against Memoria's principles and revises, before the PI approval gate ('engines write, agents judge'). The first-revision-dominates finding gives a cheap one-pass budget for local models.

ideas.idea: Chain-of-thought before a binary/multiple-choice judgment to improve and make the decision legible.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's agent judges (contradiction detection, claim supersession, triage) benefit from recorded CoT rationale for the read-only inspector and audit trail; but local models are weaker at CoT, so apply selectively where the judgment is load-bearing.

ideas.idea: Ensembling over multiple rephrased principles for each label to get more robust, diverse judgments.
ideas.rationale: A practical robustness trick for Memoria's small-model judges: sampling a few principle phrasings can stabilize relevance/contradiction scoring without a bigger model. Cheap and local-friendly.

ideas.idea: Prefer non-evasive responses that explain objections over canned refusals; penalize evasiveness in the reward signal.
ideas.rationale: Useful framing for Memoria agents at the review gate: when an engine output is rejected, the agent should record WHY (a structured objection the PI can act on) rather than silently dropping the note - improves the gate's usefulness.

ideas.idea: Clamping/soft-labeling preference probabilities (e.g., 40-60% clamp) to avoid Goodharting and over-confident RL signals.
ideas.rationale: This is RL-training calibration detail; Memoria does no model training and uses fixed local models, so the clamping mechanics are off-scope.

ideas.idea: RLAIF: distill AI preference labels into a preference model and fine-tune the policy with RL to scale supervision.
ideas.rationale: Requires large-scale training infrastructure and 52B-class models; contradicts Memoria's local, cheap, no-training constraints. The constitution and self-critique ideas survive without the RL pipeline.

file: Bansal et al. - 2022 - Certified neural network watermarks with randomized smoothing.md
title: Certified Neural Network Watermarks with Randomized Smoothing
one_line: A provably unremovable neural-network watermark: by applying randomized smoothing (median of trigger-set accuracy under Gaussian parameter noise) the trigger-set accuracy is guaranteed preserved unless an adversary moves model weights beyond a certified L2 radius, and it is also empirically more removal-resistant.

ideas.idea: Randomized-smoothing certificate over parameter space: take the median of an accuracy function under Gaussian noise to bound worst-case change within an L2 ball.
ideas.rationale: Memoria neither trains nor distributes models; model-IP watermarking against theft is entirely outside a single-researcher knowledge-vault's scope.

ideas.idea: Backdoor/trigger-set watermarking verifiable via black-box API predictions on out-of-distribution images.
ideas.rationale: No applicability to a markdown/claims knowledge system; no model-ownership threat model exists for Memoria's local fixed models.

ideas.idea: Sufficient vs necessary vs perfect threat-model framing to bound what radius is meaningful to certify.
ideas.rationale: This adversarial-robustness threat-modeling vocabulary has no transferable use to Memoria's governance or retrieval; Memoria's sandbox is about agent capability denial, not L2 perturbation defense.

ideas.idea: Median (percentile) smoothing as a robust aggregator that bounds the effect of noisy perturbations.
ideas.rationale: Even the generic 'use a median for robustness' idea offers nothing concrete to Memoria's ranking/dedup that simpler ensembling wouldn't; the certified-radius machinery is overkill and irrelevant. No buildable Memoria component.

file: Bardzell - 2010 - Feminist HCI taking stock and outlining an agenda for design.md
title: Feminist HCI: Taking Stock and Outlining an Agenda for Design
one_line: A foundational HCI agenda paper proposing six 'feminist interaction design qualities' (pluralism, participation, advocacy, ecology, embodiment, self-disclosure) as generative lenses for designing and evaluating interactive systems.

ideas.idea: Self-disclosure quality: a system should render visible HOW it constitutes/profiles the user and give the user controls to correct that model (the Amazon 'Fix this recommendation' / 'Don't use for recommendations' example).
ideas.rationale: Directly applicable to Memoria's discovery relevance scoring and learning-to-rank triage: the PI should see WHY an item was surfaced or ranked and be able to override the signal from the Obsidian UI - a transparent, correctable recommender matches the PI-direct-access and inspector design.

ideas.idea: Pluralism: resist a single totalizing/'universal' model; design for the margins and for difference rather than one idealized user.
ideas.rationale: Reframed for a single PI: Memoria should not force one universal ranking/relevance notion but adapt to the PI's idiosyncratic, evolving interests (personalization). The anti-universalism caution applies to default scoring heuristics; the multi-population framing itself does not (single user).

ideas.idea: Participation: knowers are not substitutable; involve the user in iterative shaping/evaluation of the artifact rather than treating them as a distant test subject.
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's structured capture forms, approval gate, and PI-in-the-loop philosophy: the PI co-shapes what gets written. Useful as a design principle, though the CSCW/multi-stakeholder apparatus is unnecessary at single-researcher scale.

ideas.idea: Ecology quality: evaluate an artifact by its effects within a whole system of artifacts and on all stakeholders, not in isolation.
ideas.rationale: Mirrors Memoria's typed-relations / argument-graph stance: a note/claim's value depends on its place in the graph (contradictions, supersession), not in isolation - a justification for the structural-review-gate's holistic view rather than per-note approval only.

ideas.idea: Critique-based vs generative contributions distinction; feminism as a generative design resource not just after-the-fact critique.
ideas.rationale: A meta-framing reminder that Memoria's review gate should be generative (surface concrete fixes/relations) not merely a pass/fail critic - reinforces the 'explain objections' stance from the CAI paper.

ideas.idea: Advocacy, embodiment, and gender-identity qualities; feminist standpoint epistemology and the 'marginal user'.
ideas.rationale: These socio-political and embodiment design concerns target multi-user public-facing systems and gendered use contexts; they have no actionable mapping to a single-researcher local claims/knowledge vault.

file: Wu et al. - 2022 - PromptChainer chaining large language model prompts through visual programming.md
title: PromptChainer: Chaining Large Language Model Prompts through Visual Programming
one_line: A CHI extended-abstract interface for visually authoring multi-step LLM prompt chains, with node typing, function-signature handles, and multi-granularity (unit/end-to-end/breakpoint) debugging of cascading errors.

ideas.idea: Treat each LLM step as a node with an explicit 'function signature' (named input/output handles synced to prompt placeholders) so downstream steps have stable, typed contracts.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's deterministic ingest pipeline (parse -> claim extraction -> classification -> aspects) is itself a chain of LLM/engine steps; enforcing a declared input/output schema per stage (claim object shape, aspect fields) prevents an upstream prompt edit from silently breaking a downstream engine. This is schema discipline, not a UI to adopt.

ideas.idea: Multi-granularity debugging: unit-test a node in isolation, run end-to-end with per-node output logging, and breakpoint/edit an intermediate output to test downstream stages independently.
ideas.rationale: Useful for the structural review gate / inspector: a PI inspecting why a note was rejected should see per-stage outputs and be able to pin a corrected intermediate (e.g. fix an extracted claim) to re-run only downstream judging. The cascading-error framing maps directly to ingest pipeline observability.

ideas.idea: Helper/processing nodes plus an example gallery that nudges users toward prompt patterns whose outputs are parsable by predefined processors.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's engines are deterministic and code-owned, not PI-authored prompt chains; an end-user visual prompt-authoring surface contradicts the 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' governance model and adds an authoring burden the single PI doesn't need.

ideas.idea: Visual node-edge chain-authoring canvas for non-experts to compose pipelines.
ideas.rationale: Off-model for Memoria: pipelines are fixed and versioned in code under ADR governance, not visually wired per-session by the PI. The projected canvas in Memoria is for navigating knowledge, not building LLM pipelines.

file: Wu et al. - 2023 - AutoGen enabling next-gen LLM applications via multi-agent conversation.md
title: AutoGen: Enabling Next-Gen LLM Applications via Multi-Agent Conversation
one_line: An open-source framework casting LLM applications as conversations among customizable 'conversable' agents (LLM/human/tool-backed) with auto-reply control flow, dynamic group chat, and human-in-the-loop.

ideas.idea: Grounding agent / verifier agent pattern: a dedicated agent that validates another agent's output against rules (e.g. chess legal-move board agent, ALFWorld commonsense grounding) and forces a re-propose on failure, giving 8-35% gains and avoiding error loops.
ideas.rationale: This is exactly Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge' gate: a judging agent that checks an extraction/relation against deterministic rules (citekey integrity, claim-sentence schema, contradiction constraints) and bounces it before the PI approve step. The board-agent-vs-prompt ablation justifies a separate deterministic validator over a prompted self-check.

ideas.idea: Configurable human-in-the-loop via a user_proxy with human_input_mode (NEVER/ALWAYS/conditional) governing when human approval is solicited.
ideas.rationale: Maps to Memoria's structural review gate where the PI is solicited at defined checkpoints. Borrow the explicit, per-stage 'when to ask the human' configuration; reject AutoGen's framework itself since Memoria's PI access must be from the Obsidian UI, not a chat loop.

ideas.idea: Safeguard agent that vets code before a Commander executes it (OptiGuide multi-agent coding).
ideas.rationale: Memoria's agents are MCP-only and get NO code execution ever (ADR-32/46, MEMORIA.md). The safeguard pattern presumes agents writing/executing code, which is hard-denied in the sandbox; the safety need is met by removing execution entirely, not by adding a guard agent.

ideas.idea: Interactive retrieval: when retrieved context lacks the answer, the assistant emits 'UPDATE CONTEXT' to trigger more retrieval instead of terminating or saying 'I don't know'.
ideas.rationale: Relevant to Memoria's nightly discovery/retriever loop and pre-file similarity gate: a retrieval agent that explicitly signals insufficiency and re-queries (rather than hallucinating or abstaining) improves recall. Adapt as a control signal in the scout/retriever profile.

ideas.idea: General multi-agent conversation framework with auto-reply control flow and dynamic group-chat speaker selection.
ideas.rationale: Single-researcher scale with a fixed, governed pipeline doesn't need open-ended dynamic multi-agent chat; Memoria deliberately constrains agents to compiled profiles and an MCP sandbox. Free-form agent conversations work against deterministic ingest and auditability.

file: Wu et al. - 2025 - LongMemEval benchmarking chat assistants on long-term interactive memory.md
title: LongMemEval: Benchmarking Chat Assistants on Long-Term Interactive Memory
one_line: A 500-question benchmark over scalable chat histories testing five memory abilities (info extraction, multi-session reasoning, temporal reasoning, knowledge updates, abstention), plus a unified indexing/retrieval/reading framework with value-granularity, key-expansion, time-aware query, and Chain-of-Note optimizations.

ideas.idea: Unified three-stage memory model (indexing -> retrieval -> reading) with four control points: value granularity, key, query, reading strategy.
ideas.rationale: A clean, directly buildable architecture for Memoria's note/claim memory: it factors exactly the decisions Memoria already makes (what unit to store, how to index it, how to query, how to present to a reading LLM). Use it as the design skeleton for the retriever/discovery layer over the vault.

ideas.idea: Fact-augmented key expansion: index the original value's key concatenated with extracted facts/keyphrases (document expansion), giving +9.4% recall@k and +5.4% QA, rather than replacing the value with a lossy summary.
ideas.rationale: Directly informs Memoria's keyphrase tag candidates and pre-file similarity gate: keep the full claim/note as the retrievable unit but expand its index with extracted keyphrases/facts for multi-pathway retrieval. The 'don't replace with summary' finding validates faithfulness-over-compression, a core Memoria constraint.

ideas.idea: Value granularity: storing per-round (vs whole-session) is optimal; further compressing to facts loses info EXCEPT it helps multi-session reasoning.
ideas.rationale: Atomic-claim extraction is Memoria's version of fine granularity; this confirms atomic units help cross-document synthesis (argument graph, contradictions) while warning that over-compression hurts detailed recall — so keep verbatim claim text alongside the atomic form.

ideas.idea: Time-aware indexing + LLM query expansion: tag values with event dates and have an LLM extract a time range to restrict retrieval, improving temporal recall 6.8-11.3% — but only with a strong LLM (Llama-8B hallucinates ranges).
ideas.rationale: Memoria tracks claim supersession and temporal updates; timestamp-scoped retrieval supports 'which claim was current as of date X'. Caveat is load-bearing: Memoria's default qwen2.5:7b is small, so the time-range extractor may be unreliable — gate it or escalate to the qwen3:14b fallback per the test-env model note.

ideas.idea: Knowledge-update and abstention as first-class evaluated abilities; commercial systems overwrite old facts or fail to abstain on false-premise questions.
ideas.rationale: Maps onto Memoria's claim supersession and contradictions dashboard: the system must represent an updated fact without silently destroying the prior (versioned supersession), and must abstain / flag false premises rather than confabulate. This is a concrete evaluation target for Memoria's update logic.

ideas.idea: Chain-of-Note reading + structured JSON formatting of retrieved items, worth up to 10 absolute QA points even under perfect retrieval.
ideas.rationale: For the reading/synthesis step over retrieved claims (e.g. answering a PI query or drafting a note), have the agent first extract per-item notes then reason, and present retrieved claims as structured JSON. Cheap to implement and model-agnostic, fitting local-model constraints.

ideas.idea: LLM-as-judge evaluation with >97% human agreement for flexible-answer QA.
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's evaluation/benchmarks plane for measuring retrieval/answer quality without brittle exact-match; but the judge here is GPT-4o, so calibrate any local-judge variant before trusting it.

file: Wu et al. - 2026 - Intern-atlas a methodological evolution graph as research infrastructure for AI scientists.md
title: Intern-Atlas: A Methodological Evolution Graph as Research Infrastructure for AI Scientists
one_line: A method-centric evolution graph built from ~1M AI papers with seven typed causal edges (extends/improves/replaces/adapts/uses_component/compares/background), each grounded in verbatim bottleneck-mechanism-tradeoff evidence, plus operators for lineage reconstruction, graph-grounded idea evaluation, and gap-driven idea generation.

ideas.idea: Shift the atomic unit from documents to method entities with alias resolution, and type every citation edge with a causal relation grounded in a verbatim quote naming the bottleneck it resolves.
ideas.rationale: This is Memoria's typed-relations + argument-graph + claim extraction agenda made concrete for the research-method domain. Adopt: entities (methods/concepts) as nodes, typed edges (extends/improves/replaces...), and every causal edge carrying a verbatim warrant — which mirrors Memoria's warrants and faithfulness-over-flash requirement (every relation cites source text).

ideas.idea: Verbatim validator: a deterministic post-checker that discards any edge whose quoted spans fail substring match against the source, whose endpoints violate publication-year ordering, or that contradicts an opposite-direction edge.
ideas.rationale: Exactly the kind of deterministic check Memoria's structural review gate should run before a relation/claim is written: substring-grounding enforces non-hallucinated warrants, year-ordering and opposite-edge checks catch contradictions. Cheap, model-free, and aligns with 'engines/checks judge, PI approves'.

ideas.idea: Bottleneck taxonomy (14 axes, e.g. computational cost, long-range dependency) tagging each causal edge, enabling reasoning about which problem categories still lack a strong solution.
ideas.rationale: A useful structured aspect to attach to claims/relations alongside MASSW aspects — lets Memoria surface 'open problems' and feed the discovery loop's relevance scoring. Borrow the idea of a bounded, source-grounded category set rather than this exact AI-specific taxonomy.

ideas.idea: Graph-grounded idea evaluation: deterministic per-dimension scoring (novelty/feasibility/significance/validity/clarity) from graph topology, with an anti-stacking penalty and a one-sided LLM veto that can only LOWER the score (never raise it) to avoid LLM positive bias.
ideas.rationale: Strong fit for Memoria's discovery triage / learning-to-rank: compute relevance/novelty from graph structure (connectivity, gaps) deterministically, and constrain any LLM judge to a one-sided veto. The one-sided-veto design is a directly portable governance pattern for the review gate to prevent agents inflating scores.

ideas.idea: Strategy-driven idea generation with an evidence certificate (edge, verbatim bottleneck quote, justification) exact-matched before a proposal is returned; deterministic fallback if it fails.
ideas.rationale: Memoria can borrow the 'every generated suggestion must carry a verifiable source certificate' rule for the proactive discovery loop — surfaced suggestions to the PI must point to real grounded evidence, with a deterministic guard rejecting fabricated motivations. Full auto-ideation is lower priority than grounded discovery, hence borrow.

ideas.idea: SGT-MCTS lineage reconstruction over the strong-causal subgraph with an evidence+temporal-coherence prior (edge confidence x year-gap function peaked at 1-3 years), recovering parallel branches by masking covered edges.
ideas.rationale: Useful for tracing claim/idea lineage and supersession chains in Memoria's argument graph, but MCTS is heavier than a single-PI vault needs; borrow the temporal-coherence-weighted, evidence-weighted path scoring rather than the full MCTS machinery.

ideas.idea: Positioning a typed knowledge graph as the persistent shared data layer that agents query, vs each agent re-retrieving unstructured text per task (citing PDB/ImageNet as precedents).
ideas.rationale: This is the core thesis behind Memoria itself: a persistent, queryable, structured vault that agents read via MCP rather than re-deriving knowledge each session. Strong external validation for Memoria's architecture; reinforces investing in the typed-relation/claim graph as the substrate the scout/retriever/judge profiles all share.

file: Xie et al. - 2022 - UnifiedSKG unifying and multi-tasking structured knowledge grounding with text-to-text language mod.md
title: UnifiedSKG: Unifying and Multi-Tasking Structured Knowledge Grounding with Text-to-Text Language Models
one_line: Unifies 21 structured-knowledge-grounding tasks (semantic parsing, table/KB QA, data-to-text, fact verification, dialog) into one text-to-text format, benchmarks fine-tuned T5 to near-SOTA, and studies multi-task prefix-tuning plus structured-knowledge linearization/ordering effects.

ideas.idea: Linearize heterogeneous structured knowledge (tables, KG triples, databases, ontologies) into text so a single text-to-text model can ground over it.
ideas.rationale: When Memoria feeds structured vault content (claim tables, relation triples, frontmatter) to a local LLM for synthesis or answering, a consistent linearization scheme matters. Borrow the technique; the multi-task PLM-training thrust itself is out of scope (Memoria doesn't train models).

ideas.idea: Input-ordering and order-sensitivity findings: place the user request before the structured knowledge (rs > sr); models are highly sensitive to ontology ordering but robust to table/DB column order.
ideas.rationale: Concrete, low-cost prompt-construction guidance for Memoria's local reading step over linearized claims/relations — put the query first, and don't assume order-invariance for ontology-like structured inputs. Directly applicable to keeping cheap local models reliable.

ideas.idea: Converting structured knowledge to natural-language templates helps in low-resource settings (notably QA/WikiSQL) but can slightly hurt semantic parsing.
ideas.rationale: Memoria operates with small local models on limited per-task data; verbalizing structured records into NL before a local LLM reads them may improve recall for QA-style tasks. Borrow as a per-task heuristic, with the caveat it's not a universal win.

ideas.idea: Multi-task prefix-tuning (pretrain a shared prefix, then per-task init) beats naive multi-task fine-tuning; manual source/target task selection for transfer is unreliable.
ideas.rationale: Requires model training/prefix-tuning infrastructure that Memoria explicitly avoids — it relies on off-the-shelf local models (qwen2.5:7b) and prompting, not task-specific fine-tuning at single-researcher scale.

ideas.idea: Faithfulness error taxonomy for generation (missing info, contradiction, hallucination, ungrammatical) and the finding that automatic metrics (BLEU) diverge from human judgments.
ideas.rationale: Maps onto Memoria's uncertainty flags and faithfulness-over-flash goal: when an agent drafts a note/summary from claims, classify outputs by these error types (esp. hallucination vs contradiction) in the review gate, and don't trust surface metrics. Borrow the taxonomy as a faithfulness check vocabulary.

ideas.idea: Large PLMs (T0/GPT-3/Codex) struggle at zero/few-shot SKG, especially induction/classification tasks (near-random on TabFact).
ideas.rationale: A dated benchmarking result about 2022-era models; offers no buildable mechanism for Memoria and the specific model limitations don't transfer to current local models.

file: Yang et al. - 2024 - Large language models for automated open-domain scientific hypotheses discovery.md
title: Large Language Models for Automated Open-domain Scientific Hypotheses Discovery (MOOSE / TOMATO)
one_line: Introduces the TOMATO task (generate novel+valid social-science hypotheses from raw web corpus) and the MOOSE multi-module LLM framework with three feedback mechanisms (present/past/future) plus a background-inspiration-hypothesis pipeline and multi-aspect over-generate-then-filter checkers.

ideas.idea: Decompose hypothesis generation into a serial pipeline of single-responsibility modules (background finder -> inspiration title finder -> inspiration finder -> hypothesis suggestor -> hypothesis proposer) where each module consumes prior modules' outputs plus corpus.
ideas.rationale: Mirrors Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge' decomposition; the named-module-with-typed-inputs pattern is a good template for a discovery/synthesis engine that proposes connections between PI notes, but Memoria's task is connecting existing claims, not full social-science hypothesis authorship.

ideas.idea: Multi-aspect checker modules (reality/validness checker, novelty checker, clarity checker) that score generation along orthogonal philosophical axes for hypothetical induction.
ideas.rationale: Directly maps to Memoria's structural review gate: a candidate note/claim/relation can be judged on faithfulness (reality), novelty vs existing vault (novelty checker against the corpus/surveys), and clarity. The novelty checker that queries a related-survey corpus via BM25 is a concrete analog of Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and contradiction/novelty scoring.

ideas.idea: Present-feedback: LLM provides feedback on a module output and the module regenerates with (output, feedback) as input - iterative refine rather than just filter.
ideas.rationale: Useful inside an MCP-only agent loop where an agent judges an engine's draft and asks for regeneration; but Memoria favors deterministic ingest and PI approval over many unbounded refine iterations, so cap iterations (paper shows helpfulness peaks ~3 iterations, then plateaus/declines).

ideas.idea: Past-feedback: when a module's output (e.g. chosen inspiration) cannot be judged directly, defer evaluation until a downstream module produces something gradable, then propagate feedback backward.
ideas.rationale: Credit-assignment insight applies to Memoria's discovery pipeline: a retrieval/scout choice can only be judged after the relation/claim it enabled is scored. Worth borrowing as an offline learning-to-rank signal, but the synchronous backward-refine loop is heavyweight for single-researcher nightly batch.

ideas.idea: GPT-4 evaluation shows high (>0.75 soft) consistency with PhD-expert evaluation on validness/novelty/helpfulness, suggesting LLM-as-judge can stand in for expert scoring.
ideas.rationale: Supports using a cheap local model as an LLM-judge for Memoria's gate scoring, but the validation was with GPT-4; faithfulness-first Memoria should calibrate qwen2.5:7b against PI spot-checks before trusting it, and the paper itself warns GPT-4 may grade validness by familiarity (penalizing novel-but-correct), a bias the contradiction/supersession dashboard must guard against.

ideas.idea: Reject BLEU/ROUGE/METEOR and relevance/significance metrics for open-ended generation; use validness+novelty+helpfulness on a Likert scale with hard/soft consistency.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's evaluation of extracted claims and proposed relations is open-ended; n-gram overlap is inappropriate. The hard/soft consistency metric (|diff|=0/1/2... -> 1.0/0.75/0.50) is a clean, cheap inter-rater agreement formula Memoria can reuse for gate-judge vs PI agreement tracking.

ideas.idea: Validness-novelty trade-off: random/BM25 background+inspiration gives higher novelty but lower validness than LLM-picked; over-stressing novelty yields vague/incorrect output.
ideas.rationale: Direct design constraint for Memoria's discovery relevance scoring and triage: tuning the retriever toward surprise must be balanced against faithfulness, exactly Memoria's 'faithfulness over flash' pillar. The empirical curve justifies an explicit validness floor before a discovered connection surfaces to the PI.

file: Yao et al. - 2022 - CPT colorful prompt tuning for pre-trained vision-language models.md
title: CPT: Colorful Prompt Tuning for Pre-trained Vision-Language Models
one_line: Reformulates visual grounding/relation detection as a fill-in-the-blank (masked-LM) problem using color co-referential markers in image and text, enabling zero/few-shot grounding without new task heads, plus a cross-modal prompt search to pick model-sensitive colors.

ideas.idea: Cast a discriminative task as a fill-in-the-blank cloze over the pre-trained MLM head to close the pretrain/finetune gap and gain few-/zero-shot ability without task-specific parameters.
ideas.rationale: The general 'reformulate-as-cloze for data efficiency' insight is mildly relevant to Memoria's local claim-sentence classification and relation typing with small open models, where labeled data is scarce; but it is the generic prompt-tuning idea, not CPT's cross-modal specifics.

ideas.idea: Open-world label production: by filling masks from the vocabulary, semantic labels (e.g. relation types) come from an open vocabulary rather than a fixed label set.
ideas.rationale: Marginally interesting for Memoria's typed-relation extraction where the relation inventory may grow; a cloze formulation could propose relation labels not yet in the schema. Low priority given Memoria prefers a governed, finite typed-relation set under the structural gate.

ideas.idea: Color-based visual sub-prompts (segmentation masks / blocks) and cross-modal prompt search for grounding referring expressions in images.
ideas.rationale: Vision-language grounding is off-domain for Memoria, which ingests text papers; there is no image-region grounding task. The color-marker mechanism has no analog in a markdown research vault.

file: Yao et al. - 2024 - $τ$-bench a benchmark for tool-agent-user interaction in real-world domains.md
title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains
one_line: A benchmark where a language agent must interact with a (LM-simulated) user and domain API tools while following a policy document, evaluated by deterministic end-state database comparison plus a new pass^k consistency metric; SOTA agents fail <50% and are highly inconsistent.

ideas.idea: State-based (not trajectory-based) evaluation: judge an agent solely by whether the final database state matches the unique annotated ground-truth state, allowing diverse valid paths.
ideas.rationale: Strong fit for Memoria's deterministic ingest + structural gate: an engine/agent run can be judged by the resulting vault state (notes/claims/relations written) versus an expected state, independent of the conversational path. Enables objective, cheap regression tests for the 'engines write, agents judge' pipeline without LLM-judging every trajectory.

ideas.idea: pass^k metric: probability that ALL k i.i.d. trials succeed (consistency/reliability), contrasted with pass@k (at least one succeeds / discovery).
ideas.rationale: Memoria deploys local stochastic models in a governance-critical gate; reliability matters more than best-of-k. pass^k is exactly the right metric to certify that an extraction/gate engine behaves consistently across reruns before the PI trusts it unattended in the nightly loop.

ideas.idea: Ablation: removing the policy document barely hurts on commonsense-y rules but drops sharply on complex ad-hoc rules - agents only truly consult policy when rules exceed common sense, and weak models can't process complex policy at all.
ideas.rationale: Directly informs Memoria's profile/skill governance: compiled profiles and the structural-review rules must be tested with a policy-ablation to prove the agent actually follows them, and complex governance rules may exceed a 7B model's capacity - validating Memoria's choice to keep agents MCP-only with hard-deny gates rather than relying on the model to honor prose policy.

ideas.idea: LM-simulated user with a textual scenario prompt that guarantees a unique outcome, used to generate stochastic-but-faithful conversation variation for testing.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is single-user with mostly batch (non-conversational) flows, so a simulated end-user is not core; but a simulated-PI fixture that issues capture/approval requests could stress-test the approval gate and structured capture forms deterministically.

ideas.idea: Reflexion/self-reflection and slow planning are unrealistic for real-time single-shot user service; native function-calling beats text-ReAct, and a bolted-on 'think' function didn't help untrained FC models.
ideas.rationale: Tempers Memoria's agent design: prefer native tool-calling over verbose ReAct scaffolds for the MCP agents, and don't assume a generic reasoning wrapper improves a small local model not trained for it; reserve heavy planning for the offline nightly loop, not interactive PI-facing actions.

ideas.idea: Cost is dominated (95.9%) by the long system prompt (policy + tool definitions), not completions.
ideas.rationale: Relevant to Memoria's compiled-profile and MCP tool-surface design: keep per-agent policy/tool context lean (compile profiles, scope tools per task) to control local-inference latency/cost, even though Memoria runs cheap local models.

file: Ye and Durrett - 2022 - The unreliability of explanations in few-shot prompting for textual reasoning.md
title: The Unreliability of Explanations in Few-shot Prompting for Textual Reasoning
one_line: Shows LLM-generated explanations for QA/NLI are often consistent-with-the-answer but NOT factually grounded in the input, that nonfactual explanations reliably signal wrong answers, and that a tiny calibrator over a lexical-overlap factuality score lets the model abstain and improves accuracy.

ideas.idea: Distinguish two explanation axes: factuality (grounded in the input) vs consistency (entails the prediction); LLMs produce consistent-but-nonfactual explanations even on trivial synthetic tasks.
ideas.rationale: Core to Memoria's claim-extraction faithfulness: an extracted atomic claim with a plausible-sounding rationale can still be ungrounded in the source paper. Memoria's structural gate should score grounding/factuality separately from internal coherence, and flag the consistent-but-unfaithful failure mode explicitly (uncertainty flags).

ideas.idea: Nonfactual explanation strongly predicts an incorrect prediction; explanation factuality can be used as a post-hoc verification/abstention signal (reject answer-explanation pairs whose explanation isn't grounded, iterate top-k until a grounded one is found).
ideas.rationale: Direct buildable mechanism for Memoria: gate an engine's extracted claim by checking whether its supporting span actually appears in the source; abstain (route to PI / mark uncertain) when grounding fails. The top-k 'reject until grounded' loop lifted SYNTH accuracy 52->75% and is cheap, deterministic, and fits MCP-only agents that cannot run code but can compare strings.

ideas.idea: Approximate factuality cheaply via lexical overlap between explanation sentences and source paragraphs (max-overlap normalized by explanation length, min over sentences) - no fine-tuned NLI model needed.
ideas.rationale: Exactly Memoria's regime: local, cheap, deterministic, black-box-friendly. A lexical/span-overlap faithfulness score is a low-cost pre-file gate feature for claim extraction and citation grounding, far simpler than running an entailment model, and degrades gracefully on the local hardware.

ideas.idea: Lightweight learned calibrator: softmax(W[p; v] + b) over the model probability p plus a factuality factor v, trainable with ~32 extra labeled examples (no explanation annotations needed) to reweight/abstain.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's learning-to-rank triage and discovery-relevance scoring can fold a faithfulness factor into a tiny calibrator trained on a handful of PI approve/reject decisions - feasible at single-researcher scale. Borrow rather than adopt because Memoria may prefer a transparent threshold/rule over a learned layer inside the governance gate.

ideas.idea: Calibrating with explanation-based features outperforms probability-only calibration and nearest-neighbor prompt selection in the data-scarce regime, and lets you use extra examples that won't fit in the prompt.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces that Memoria should mine the PI's scarce approval labels via a calibrator rather than stuffing context, aligning with compiled-profile/cheap-model constraints; specifics are NLI-task-bound so borrow the principle.

ideas.idea: Caveat: fluent, grammatical explanations can deceive users into trusting wrong answers (form vs meaning).
ideas.rationale: Justifies a Memoria UI/governance rule: never surface an engine-generated rationale to the PI as authoritative without its faithfulness score / grounding status, so the inspector and gate dashboards must show provenance, not just prose - faithfulness over flash.

file: Ye et al. - 2022 - Ontology-enhanced prompt-tuning for few-shot learning.md
title: Ontology-enhanced Prompt-tuning for Few-shot Learning (OntoPrompt)
one_line: Injects external ontology knowledge into prompt-tuning by converting ontology schema (rdfs:comment descriptions + meta-relation paths) into auxiliary text prompts, using a span-based visible attention matrix to limit knowledge noise and collective training to fuse representations, improving few-shot RE/EE/KGC.

ideas.idea: Ontology transformation: convert structured KG/ontology (concepts, edges, textual descriptions via rdfs:comment) into natural-language auxiliary prompt text appended to the input, addressing 'knowledge missing'.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's typed-relation schema and entity records are a lightweight ontology; rendering relevant schema descriptions as context for a local extraction model could improve few-shot claim/relation extraction. Borrow the 'verbalize the schema as a prompt' idea, but Memoria runs decoder/instruct models, not the BERT-MLM cloze setup here.

ideas.idea: Span-sensitive knowledge injection via a visibility (attention-mask) matrix so only the relevant entity span attends to its injected ontology description, preventing irrelevant knowledge from corrupting the rest of the sentence ('knowledge noise').
ideas.rationale: Requires modifying the attention mask inside the encoder - infeasible with Memoria's black-box local API models (Ollama/qwen) and contradicts the deterministic, model-agnostic, MCP-only constraints. The selective-knowledge insight is valid but must be realized via retrieval filtering, not internal attention surgery.

ideas.idea: Knowledge noise principle: indiscriminate knowledge injection degrades performance; context- and task-specific selection of which facts to inject is critical.
ideas.rationale: Direct design rule for Memoria's retriever/scout and pre-file similarity gate: feed an extraction/discovery engine only narrowly relevant vault context, not the whole neighborhood, or quality drops. This is the architecture-independent takeaway worth building in.

ideas.idea: Meta-relation text: include the ontology path between an entity pair (e.g. Person -> ... -> Organization) as extra prompt context for relation extraction.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's argument graph already has typed paths between claims/notes; surfacing the connecting path as context when an agent judges a candidate relation is a cheap, schema-native enrichment, applicable without the prompt-tuning machinery.

ideas.idea: Collective training: initialize learnable virtual tokens with real-word embeddings, optimize them with the LM frozen, then unfreeze all parameters to fuse heterogeneous (knowledge vs text) representations.
ideas.rationale: Soft-prompt / continuous-token training and full-model fine-tuning are out of scope for Memoria, which uses frozen local off-the-shelf models with deterministic prompting and no training infrastructure at single-researcher scale.

file: Liu et al. - 2021 - Pre-train, prompt, and predict a systematic survey of prompting methods in natural language process.md
title: Pre-train, Prompt, and Predict: A Systematic Survey of Prompting Methods in NLP
one_line: A foundational survey that formalizes prompt-based learning (template + answer + tuning-strategy typology) and catalogs prompt-engineering, answer-engineering, multi-prompt, and tuning techniques across NLP tasks.

ideas.idea: Cloze/prefix template framework: reformulate any task (classification, NER, NLI, summarization) as a slot-filling prompt with a defined answer space Z mapped back to labels Y.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's local extraction engines (claim-sentence classification, MASSW aspects, contradiction labeling) are exactly classification/text-span tasks; the cloze-template + constrained answer-space pattern is the cleanest way to drive qwen2.5:7b deterministically and is what the ingest prompts should standardize on.

ideas.idea: Answer engineering with a small constrained verbalizer set (e.g. Z={good,bad}->{+,-}) and answer mapping.
ideas.rationale: Constraining the local model's output to a fixed label vocabulary (then mapping to canonical labels) makes ingest deterministic and parseable, which directly serves Memoria's deterministic-ingest constraint and the structural review gate.

ideas.idea: Prompt ensembling: run multiple template variants and combine (vote/average) to reduce prompt-sensitivity.
ideas.rationale: Useful as an optional reliability boost for high-stakes labels (contradiction, supersession) where a single prompt is brittle; but it multiplies local inference cost, so apply only selectively, not as the default.

ideas.idea: Prompt decomposition for span/sequence-labeling tasks (NER): enumerate spans, ask 'X is a [Z] entity' per span.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's entity dedup / record-linkage and entity-relation extraction can use per-span typing prompts; worth borrowing the decomposition pattern, though enumerating all spans is costly at single-researcher scale, so gate it behind a candidate generator.

ideas.idea: Prompt composition for relation extraction (sub-prompts for entity type + relation, combined via rules).
ideas.rationale: Directly relevant to Memoria's typed-relation/argument-graph extraction; the compose-from-subtasks idea improves precision on relation typing, adaptable to the local model.

ideas.idea: Tuning strategy taxonomy (tuning-free prompting / fixed-LM prompt tuning / fixed-prompt LM tuning / prompt+LM tuning).
ideas.rationale: Provides vocabulary for deciding Memoria stays in the tuning-free / in-context regime (no fine-tuning local models, faithfulness over flash); the taxonomy clarifies why Memoria should NOT do LM tuning, which is itself a useful design constraint to record.

ideas.idea: Continuous/soft prompt tuning (prefix-tuning, P-tuning, WARP) requiring gradient training and storing learned prompt vectors.
ideas.rationale: Requires training infrastructure and per-task gradient updates; contradicts Memoria's deterministic, local, no-training, MCP-only-agent posture and single-researcher scale where data is too scarce to tune.

file: Liu et al. - 2021 - What makes good In-context examples for GPT-$3$.md
title: What Makes Good In-Context Examples for GPT-3? (KATE)
one_line: Shows in-context/few-shot performance is highly sensitive to demonstration choice and proposes KATE: retrieve the k nearest-neighbor training examples (via sentence embeddings) to a test input to build the prompt.

ideas.idea: kNN retrieval of semantically-similar examples (sentence-embedding nearest neighbors) to construct the in-context prompt instead of random sampling.
ideas.rationale: Memoria already has embedding-based similarity (pre-file similarity gate, BERTopic clustering); the same nearest-neighbor retrieval should select few-shot exemplars for the local extraction/classification prompts, improving qwen2.5:7b accuracy cheaply and deterministically.

ideas.idea: Prompt is sensitive to example choice (SST-2 accuracy swung 86.9-95.8 over random sets); retrieval removes the variance entirely (fixed retrieved set => no variance).
ideas.rationale: Determinism is a Memoria pillar; deterministically retrieving the same exemplars per input eliminates run-to-run label variance in ingest, which is exactly what the structural review gate needs to trust engine output.

ideas.idea: Task-fine-tuned sentence encoders (NLI/STS-B) retrieve better exemplars for matching-heavy tasks, but a mismatched encoder hurts.
ideas.rationale: Argues for choosing/aligning the embedding model to the task (claim-similarity vs topic vs entity); Memoria can pick the closest off-the-shelf local encoder per retrieval purpose, but fine-tuning one is out of scope, so only borrow the encoder-selection insight.

ideas.idea: Example ordering effect is small and data-dependent (default vs reverse distance order both near-equal).
ideas.rationale: Negligible and dataset-specific gain; not worth engineering an ordering policy at single-researcher scale.

ideas.idea: Retrieval module is complementary to the LLM (kNN-alone collapses to random guessing; retrieval+LLM wins).
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's architecture of retrieval feeding generation rather than retrieval-as-classifier; a useful framing for the retriever/scout profile and discovery loop, though not a new mechanism to build.

file: Liu et al. - 2022 - Generated knowledge prompting for commonsense reasoning.md
title: Generated Knowledge Prompting for Commonsense Reasoning
one_line: Generate natural-language knowledge statements from an LM via a few human-written demonstrations, then feed each as a prompt to an inference model and pick the highest-confidence answer.

ideas.idea: Two-stage generate-then-integrate: elicit explicit NL knowledge statements, then condition the answer on them, selecting the statement that most strongly supports a choice (max-confidence aggregation).
ideas.rationale: Maps to Memoria turning an implicit judgment (e.g. does claim A contradict B, what aspect is this sentence) into an explicit intermediate rationale before deciding; the confidence-based selection over generated rationales is adaptable to local-model claim adjudication, improving faithfulness/auditability.

ideas.idea: Few-shot demonstrations (5 human-written Q->knowledge pairs) generalize without task-specific templates or fine-tuning, and transfer to new tasks cheaply.
ideas.rationale: Directly fits Memoria's no-fine-tune, single-PI posture: a handful of PI-authored demonstrations can seed each extraction prompt, far cheaper than building knowledge bases or templates per task.

ideas.idea: Quantity-of-knowledge effect: sample M~20 statements, performance rises then saturates/declines as noisy statements accumulate.
ideas.rationale: A tuning heuristic for any sample-and-select step in Memoria (e.g. multiple candidate extractions), with a clear warning that more samples eventually add noise; useful but a minor knob.

ideas.idea: Human evaluation along grammaticality/relevance/factuality/helpfulness, finding factuality strongly predicts helpfulness (helpful+rectifying knowledge is 95% factual).
ideas.rationale: These axes are a ready-made rubric for the structural review gate to score engine-generated intermediate statements/claims; factuality-as-gating-signal aligns with Memoria's uncertainty flags and faithfulness emphasis.

ideas.idea: Generated knowledge can replace/rival a retrieval knowledge base when no in-domain KB exists, and amplifies a model's own latent knowledge.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's entire premise is grounding in the PI's actual papers/claims, not hallucinating model-internal 'knowledge'; using an LM as a substitute knowledge source contradicts faithfulness-over-flash and the citekey-grounded design.

file: Liu et al. - 2022 - P-tuning v2 prompt tuning can be comparable to fine-tuning universally across scales and tasks.md
title: P-Tuning v2: Prompt Tuning Comparable to Fine-tuning Across Scales and Tasks
one_line: Deep (per-layer) continuous prompt tuning that matches full fine-tuning on classification AND hard sequence-labeling tasks with only 0.1-3% trainable parameters, across 300M-10B models.

ideas.idea: Deep prompt tuning: inject trainable continuous prompts at every transformer layer (not just input) to reach fine-tuning parity with tiny parameter budget.
ideas.rationale: Requires gradient training and per-task prompt-parameter storage against a frozen backbone; Memoria runs frozen off-the-shelf local models with no training pipeline (deterministic ingest, MCP-only agents), so this is out of scope.

ideas.idea: Replace verbalizer+LM-head with a randomly-initialized [CLS] linear classification head in the full-data supervised setting (simpler, no answer-engineering, handles sequence labeling).
ideas.rationale: Assumes a trainable head and full-data supervision; Memoria has neither labeled corpora nor a training loop at single-researcher scale, and relies on verbalizer-style constrained answers from a frozen model instead.

ideas.idea: Casting NER / extractive QA / SRL as per-token sequence-labeling problems solvable by one unified frozen-backbone approach.
ideas.rationale: The unification is achieved via trained classifiers, not prompting a frozen model; the prompt-survey already gives Memoria the usable (training-free) decomposition patterns for these tasks.

ideas.idea: Empirical lesson: parameter-efficient adaptation only becomes fine-tuning-competitive when carefully optimized; naive prompt tuning is brittle below 10B params.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces the design decision that Memoria should NOT chase prompt-tuning/PEFT shortcuts with small local models and should stay with in-context + retrieval; useful as a documented rationale, not a buildable feature.

file: Liu et al. - 2022 - Pre-train, prompt, and predict a systematic survey of prompting methods in natural language process.md
title: Pre-train, Prompt, and Predict: A Systematic Survey of Prompting Methods in NLP (duplicate of 2021 version)
one_line: Duplicate (arXiv/journal) of the same prompting survey: formalizes prompt-based learning and catalogs prompt-engineering, answer-engineering, multi-prompt, and tuning strategies.

ideas.idea: Same cloze/prefix template + constrained answer-space + answer-mapping framework for reformulating Memoria's classification/extraction tasks.
ideas.rationale: Identical content to the other file; the template+verbalizer pattern is the standard for driving Memoria's frozen local model deterministically through ingest classification.

ideas.idea: Same prompt ensembling / composition / decomposition multi-prompt techniques.
ideas.rationale: Decomposition (per-span NER) and composition (relation extraction) are adaptable to Memoria's entity/relation extraction; ensembling is an optional reliability boost for high-stakes labels.

ideas.idea: Duplicate paper in the corpus (same survey as the 2021 entry).
ideas.rationale: Flag for Memoria's record-linkage/dedup pipeline: this is a near-exact duplicate of another vault entry and should be merged/superseded, which is itself a live test case for the pre-file similarity gate and dedup logic.

file: Wang et al. - 2023 - Self-instruct aligning language models with self-generated instructions.md
title: SELF-INSTRUCT: Aligning Language Models with Self-Generated Instructions
one_line: A bootstrapping pipeline where a vanilla LM generates its own instruction/input/output task data, filters low-quality/duplicate generations heuristically, and is finetuned on the result to gain instruction-following ability.

ideas.idea: ROUGE-L similarity gate (<0.7) before adding a newly generated instruction to the pool, to enforce diversity / avoid near-duplicates
ideas.rationale: Memoria already has a pre-file similarity gate and record-linkage entity dedup; a cheap lexical-overlap threshold is a sound deterministic pre-filter to reject near-duplicate claim/keyphrase candidates before the expensive structural review, complementing embedding-based dedup.

ideas.idea: Heuristic invalid-generation filters (too long/short, output repeats input, exact-duplicate instances, banned keywords) before content is accepted
ideas.rationale: Matches Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge' gate: cheap deterministic sanity heuristics belong in the engine stage so the structural-review agent and PI only see plausible claim/relation candidates.

ideas.idea: Output-first generation for classification tasks (generate label space first, then condition inputs) to avoid label-distribution bias
ideas.rationale: Useful insight for Memoria's claim-sentence classification prompts: enumerating the label set first and conditioning on it reduces the model's prior-label bias, improving balanced extraction with cheap local models (qwen2.5:7b).

ideas.idea: Self-Instruct as a whole: synthesize a 52K instruction dataset from a vanilla LM and finetune the base model on it
ideas.rationale: Memoria does not train or finetune models; it relies on local off-the-shelf models for deterministic ingest. Building an instruction-tuning corpus is out of scope and contradicts the no-training posture.

ideas.idea: Distillation: regenerate outputs with a stronger model (InstructGPT-003) to raise data quality ~10%
ideas.rationale: Cloud-model distillation contradicts Memoria's local/cheap-model and single-researcher constraints; no model improvement loop exists in the design.

file: Wang et al. - 2024 - ReelFramer human-AI Co-creation for news-to-video translation.md
title: ReelFramer: Human-AI Co-Creation for News-to-Video Translation
one_line: A human-AI co-creation system that turns print news into TikTok reel scripts/storyboards via an intermediate, editable 'premise' (characters/plot/setting/key info) that the human approves before full generation.

ideas.idea: Intermediate editable 'premise' step between input and full generation: AI proposes foundational structured details, human accepts/edits/regenerates, and only then is the full artifact generated
ideas.rationale: This is exactly Memoria's structural-review-gate philosophy at the micro level: surface a small structured intermediate (the equivalent of extracted claims/aspects/relations) for PI accept/edit/regenerate before committing the expensive downstream note. Validates the gate-before-write pattern with empirical evidence (with-premise scripts more coherent and complete).

ideas.idea: BERT/MiniLM semantic-matching to highlight which key information points are (or are not) covered in the generated artifact, with a 0.5 similarity threshold, so omissions are visible at a glance
ideas.rationale: Directly reusable for Memoria's faithfulness checks: highlight in a projected note which source claims a generated summary covers vs. drops, flagging omission/over-compression. Cheap local embedding matching fits the no-cloud constraint and supports 'faithfulness over flash'.

ideas.idea: Accept / Regenerate / Edit at every step with saved history of generations
ideas.rationale: Maps to Memoria's PI-approval surfaces and disposable projection engine; per-step accept/regenerate/edit with a viewable history is a good interaction contract for structured capture forms and the approval gate.

ideas.idea: Three narrative framings spanning an infotainment spectrum for news-to-video translation
ideas.rationale: Domain-specific to journalism/short-video creativity; Memoria is a research-knowledge system, not a content-creation tool. The framing taxonomy has no analog in claim/argument extraction.

ideas.idea: Finding: humans must evaluate and edit AI output at every step; AI ideates/generates, human judges
ideas.rationale: Reinforces a core Memoria pillar (engines write, agents judge, PI approves); the study gives empirical backing that ungated generation drifts off-source, justifying the mandatory review gate before notes are written.

file: Wang et al. - 2024 - SciMON scientific inspiration machines optimized for novelty.md
title: SCIMON: Scientific Inspiration Machines Optimized for Novelty
one_line: A framework that takes a problem/background context plus a seed term, retrieves 'inspirations' from three literature sources (semantic-neighbor ideas, KG neighbors, citation neighbors), generates a natural-language research idea, and iteratively boosts its novelty by retrieve-compare-update against prior work.

ideas.idea: IE pipeline that classifies abstract sentences into Background/Method/Objective, extracts Task/Method/Metric/Material entities and used-for relations, normalizes via coreference (SciCo) and abbreviation expansion (ScispaCy), keeping only high-confidence outputs
ideas.rationale: This is almost exactly Memoria's claim-sentence classification + MASSW-style aspect extraction + typed-relation graph, validated end-to-end on 67K papers. The confidence-thresholded, multi-stage deterministic IE with measured per-stage precision (91-100% except RE at 65%) is a concrete blueprint for Memoria's deterministic ingest and for tagging extraction uncertainty.

ideas.idea: Three retrieval sources for grounding/discovery: semantic-neighbor problem-solution pairs, knowledge-graph one-hop neighbors of a seed term, and citation-graph neighbors
ideas.rationale: Memoria's nightly discovery loop and scout/retriever profile can use exactly this triad: semantic similarity over the vault, typed-relation KG neighbors, and Zotero citation links. Combining KG neighbors with semantic neighbors was shown to add technical depth, motivating a multi-source discovery scorer.

ideas.idea: Iterative novelty boosting: use the generated idea as a query, retrieve k nearest prior ideas, and if any exceed a similarity threshold (0.6), instruct the model to make the idea significantly different, looping until under threshold
ideas.rationale: A clean mechanism for a Memoria 'is this actually new?' check: when a candidate claim/idea is too close to existing vault claims, flag it as non-novel or supersession rather than filing a duplicate. Fits the contradiction/supersession tracking and pre-file similarity gate; adapt the loop as a one-shot novelty score rather than self-improvement training.

ideas.idea: In-context contrastive objective (InfoNCE over decoder states) penalizing copying salient terms from the input context, to discourage paraphrase-as-idea
ideas.rationale: Requires custom finetuning of T5 with a bespoke loss; Memoria does no model training and uses off-the-shelf local models. The insight (penalize copying) is useful but the mechanism is out of scope.

ideas.idea: Temporal train/dev/test split by publication year to avoid contamination, plus a hand-vetted gold subset filtering trivial background-to-target leakage
ideas.rationale: Memoria tracks claim supersession over time; temporal partitioning of the corpus and contamination-aware evaluation is a sound principle for any internal benchmarking of its discovery/triage rankers and for honest temporal reasoning.

ideas.idea: Honest negative finding: LLM-generated ideas remain incremental, low-depth, mostly superficial recombinations (ground truth rated significantly more novel in 85% of comparisons)
ideas.rationale: Calibrates Memoria's ambitions: an automated 'idea generator' would be low-value, so the design should keep humans (PI) as the idea source and use the machinery for grounding, retrieval, and novelty-flagging rather than autonomous hypothesis authoring. Supports faithfulness-over-flash.

file: Wang et al. - 2024 - Unleashing the emergent cognitive synergy in large language models a task-solving agent through mul.md
title: Unleashing the Emergent Cognitive Synergy in LLMs: Solo Performance Prompting (SPP)
one_line: Solo Performance Prompting turns a single LLM into a 'cognitive synergist' by having it dynamically identify task-specific personas, brainstorm, and iteratively self-collaborate with feedback, reducing factual hallucination on knowledge-intensive tasks.

ideas.idea: Single-LLM multi-persona self-collaboration to reduce factual hallucination without external retrieval or multiple model instances
ideas.rationale: Memoria's MCP-only sandbox favors cheap single-model calls; a single-LLM persona/critique pass (e.g., 'extractor' then 'skeptic' persona) is a low-cost way to add a self-check before the structural-review gate, without spinning up multiple agents or violating the no-extra-infrastructure preference.

ideas.idea: Dynamic fine-grained personas beat fixed/generic personas; persona NAME alone (no detailed profile) suffices to elicit domain knowledge (SPP-Profile gave no gain)
ideas.rationale: Cheap prompt-engineering insight for Memoria's compiled profiles/skills: a concise role label (e.g., 'Claim-faithfulness reviewer') is enough; elaborate persona profiles add tokens without benefit, keeping local-model prompts lean.

ideas.idea: Emergence finding: cognitive synergy only appears in GPT-4-level models; GPT-3.5/Llama2-13b fail (early-termination, no gain)
ideas.rationale: Critical caution for Memoria: SPP-style self-collaboration will NOT work on qwen2.5:7b / qwen3:14b local models per this paper's own evidence, so adopting the full SPP loop on Memoria's validated local stack is unjustified. Worth recording as a constraint, but the technique itself is rejected for the local-model setting.

ideas.idea: Iterative self-refine can hurt (changes already-good answers); SPP's structured critique avoided this on Codenames
ideas.rationale: Tempers any self-refinement Memoria might add to extraction/summarization: unconditional refine loops can degrade good outputs, so a refine step must be gated on a detected problem, consistent with faithfulness-over-flash.

ideas.idea: New evaluation tasks (Trivia Creative Writing with string-match factuality metric) to isolate hallucination from subjective quality
ideas.rationale: Benchmark construction for general LLM reasoning is off-domain for Memoria's single-researcher knowledge management; no direct reuse.

file: Wang et al. - 2025 - OpenHands an open platform for AI software developers as generalist agents.md
title: OpenHands: An Open Platform for AI Software Developers as Generalist Agents
one_line: An open platform for code-acting generalist agents built on an event-stream state, a docker-sandboxed runtime (bash/IPython/browser), an extensible AgentSkills tool library, and multi-agent delegation, with an integrated 15-benchmark evaluation framework.

ideas.idea: Event-stream state: agent state is a chronological log of all actions and observations (including user messages/feedback), from which each step's prompt is reconstructed
ideas.rationale: A clean, auditable architecture for Memoria's Hermes runtime: representing an agent run as an append-only action/observation event history aids the read-only inspector and reproducibility of gate decisions, and matches 'agents judge' being fully traceable for PI review.

ideas.idea: Multi-agent delegation via an explicit AgentDelegateAction (generalist delegates a subtask to a specialist, with delegation metadata tracked in state)
ideas.rationale: Memoria has distinct profiles (retriever/scout, structural-review judge, etc.); a typed delegation primitive with tracked provenance is a reasonable pattern for routing, e.g., a triage agent handing a candidate to a dedup/entity-linkage specialist, while keeping each step auditable.

ideas.idea: AgentSkills inclusion philosophy: only wrap a capability as a tool when the LLM can't trivially write the code itself or when it needs an external model; don't re-teach things the model already knows
ideas.rationale: Directly governs Memoria's skill governance and MCP tool surface: keep the tool set minimal and high-value (external-model calls like BERTopic/embeddings, deterministic editors), avoiding bloated profiles. A concrete principle for compiling lean profiles.

ideas.idea: Code-as-action (CodeAct): give agents a small set of general primitives (run Python, run bash, browse) instead of many bespoke JSON tools; agents compose/create their own tools
ideas.rationale: Fundamentally incompatible with Memoria's hard constraint: the MCP-only agent sandbox gives agents NO file/terminal/code-execution, EVER (D40/D41, ADR-32/46). Arbitrary code execution in a docker sandbox is exactly what Memoria forbids; only the curated-MCP-tool model is allowed.

ideas.idea: Docker-sandboxed runtime with an in-sandbox action-execution API for safe arbitrary code execution and arbitrary-image support
ideas.rationale: Memoria deliberately rejects sandboxed code execution as the agent capability model; safety is achieved by denying execution entirely (gate plugin hard-denies), not by sandboxing it. Out of scope and contradicts the architecture.

ideas.idea: Integrated evaluation framework: a standard harness running agents over many established benchmarks with cost-per-instance tracking
ideas.rationale: The pattern of a built-in, repeatable eval harness with per-run cost accounting is worth adapting for Memoria's own internal evaluation of extraction/triage quality (its L5 quality testing), keeping the single-researcher scale and cost-awareness in view.

file: Zhao et al. - 2026 - PaperMind benchmarking agentic reasoning and critique over scientific papers in multimodal LLMs.md
title: PaperMind: Benchmarking Agentic Reasoning and Critique over Scientific Papers in Multimodal LLMs
one_line: A benchmark evaluating integrated, agent-oriented scientific paper reasoning across four task families (multimodal grounding, experimental interpretation, cross-source evidence reasoning, critical assessment), with an eight-way error taxonomy for tool-augmented QA failures.

ideas.idea: Critical Assessment task: rebut reviewer-style critiques over substantive scientific claims (limitations, unclear assumptions, methodological choices, missing evidence), built from filtered OpenReview threads that led to score improvements.
ideas.rationale: Directly mirrors Memoria's argument graph / contradictions dashboard.
