An agent that judges a claim's weaknesses (missing evidence, unstated assumptions) is exactly the 'agents judge' role; the taxonomy of substantive concerns is a useful template for warrant/objection typing on the claim graph.

ideas.idea: Eight-way error taxonomy for tool-augmented reasoning: Question Misunderstanding, Reasoning-Plan-Failure, Incorrect-Tool-Use, Insufficient-Interaction-Steps, Inaccurate-Query-Keywords, Shallow-Evidence-Integration, Generic-Academic-Assumption, Failure-to-Detect-Question-Error.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's MCP-only agents do nightly discovery and retrieval; this taxonomy is a ready-made diagnostic vocabulary for logging/auditing agent failures in the structural review gate and discovery loop, especially 'Shallow-Evidence-Integration' and 'Generic-Academic-Assumption' which map to low-faithfulness note generation.

ideas.idea: Tool-budget ablation: gains saturate at 6 tool calls; 6->8 marginal, 10 degrades because excess context dilutes evidence.
ideas.rationale: Concrete, cheap-model-relevant tuning knob for Memoria's local agent runtime (Hermes): cap discovery/retrieval tool calls around 6 to avoid context dilution and wasted compute on the single-researcher local stack.

ideas.idea: Background-context injection: adding the paper's Introduction as context improves figure/experiment grounding (~13-14% F1).
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria feeding paper-aspect context (intro/abstract) into claim-extraction and aspect-classification prompts rather than isolated sentences; faithfulness improves when local context is supplied.

ideas.idea: Failure-to-Detect-Question-Error: models answer a flawed-premise question instead of flagging that the paper doesn't address it / the premise is wrong.
ideas.rationale: Maps to Memoria's uncertainty flags on atomic claims and the 'engines write, agents judge' gate: an agent should be able to return 'not addressed / premise false' rather than confabulate, which is a guardable behavior in the review gate.

ideas.idea: LLM-as-a-Judge on a 5-point scale plus F1, with reported variance and acknowledged instability across question types.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's quality-tier (L5) tests need an eval harness; the dual-metric + variance-reporting approach is reusable, but the paper itself flags LLM-judge instability, so adopt cautiously as a triage signal not a gate.

ideas.idea: Full-benchmark multi-domain agentic scaffolding via smolagents + ReAct with web/arxiv retrievers.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's sandbox is MCP-only with NO code execution/file/terminal access ever (ADR-32/46); the smolagents code-executing ReAct setup contradicts that constraint. Use the findings, not the framework.

file: Zheng and Huang - 2022 - Exploring prompt-based few-shot learning for grounded dialog generation.md
title: Exploring Prompt-based Few-shot Learning for Grounded Dialog Generation
one_line: Shows that distinguishing input constructs (grounding source vs. context vs. speaker) via continuous or discrete prompts boosts few-shot grounded dialog generation, with discrete (natural-language) prompts generally beating continuous and large general LMs beating conversational models.

ideas.idea: Discrete natural-language prompts (task instructions + textual role/component descriptions) generally outperform learned continuous prompts and are robust to minor perturbation, so they need not be laboriously optimized.
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's preference for hand-written, stable discrete prompts in compiled profiles over fragile soft-prompt tuning; robustness-to-perturbation justifies not over-engineering prompt wording for local qwen models.

ideas.idea: Explicitly delimiting heterogeneous input components (grounding source vs. conversation context vs. speaker) with indicative tokens/headers improves the model's ability to use grounding.
ideas.rationale: Maps to how Memoria assembles ingest/extraction prompts from multiple structured parts (paper text, retrieved related claims, aspects); clearly tagging each construct should improve faithfulness of claim extraction on a small local model.

ideas.idea: Semantic initialization of soft-prompt tokens (averaging embeddings of the explanatory word) beats random; effectiveness requires a large enough, general-objective backbone.
ideas.rationale: Continuous/soft-prompt tuning requires training model parameters, which Memoria does not do (uses off-the-shelf local Ollama models, no fine-tuning pipeline); the insight is superseded by instruction-tuned models that take discrete prompts directly.

ideas.idea: Prompted general language models beat dialog-specialized conversational models at using external grounding.
ideas.rationale: Dated 2022 GPT2/T5-vs-DialoGPT finding; irrelevant to Memoria, which already uses general instruction-tuned LLMs and is not a dialog system.

file: Zheng et al. - 2026 - Feasibility of the “LvL UP” trial a pilot sequential multiple assignment randomised trial of an ada.md
title: Feasibility of the “LvL UP” trial: a pilot SMART of an adaptive, holistic mHealth lifestyle coaching intervention
one_line: A pilot SMART (sequential multiple assignment randomised trial) testing the feasibility of an adaptive mHealth lifestyle-coaching app that escalates non-responders from digital coaching to human motivational-interviewing sessions.

ideas.idea: Adaptive escalation: start everyone on low-intensity automated support, re-assess at a decision point, and escalate only non-responders (defined by simple engagement proxies) to higher-intensity intervention.
ideas.rationale: A useful tiering pattern for Memoria's agent/engine pipeline: run cheap deterministic engines first, and escalate only items that fail a response/quality threshold (e.g., low-confidence extractions, pre-file similarity-gate borderline cases) to a more expensive LLM/agent pass. The 'imperfect proxy vs. outcome-based' lesson argues for outcome-based escalation triggers.

ideas.idea: Pre-specified progression criteria (quantitative go/no-go thresholds set in advance with an advisory board) decide whether to advance to a full trial.
ideas.rationale: Mirrors Memoria's deferred-ADR 'assumes:' + cadence-review model and the structural review gate: define explicit numeric thresholds up front for promoting a pipeline change, rather than judging post hoc.

ideas.idea: Full clinical SMART machinery: randomisation, GEE with weight-and-replicate, CONSORT pilot reporting, mHealth engagement-decay curves, recruitment-channel conversion.
ideas.rationale: Off-domain. Memoria is a single-researcher knowledge system, not a multi-participant RCT; there is no population, randomisation, or behavioural-outcome measurement to apply this to.

file: Zhicheng Liu et al. - 2008 - Distributed cognition as a theoretical framework for information visualization.md
title: Distributed Cognition as a Theoretical Framework for Information Visualization
one_line: Argues that the distributed-cognition (DCog) framework, treating cognition as an emergent property of coordination between humans and external representations, should ground InfoVis theory, design, and evaluation.

ideas.idea: Externalizing information into observable representations offloads cognition (Zhang & Norman 'problem isomorphs': externalized constraints yield faster, lower-error problem solving).
ideas.rationale: Core justification for Memoria's whole premise: turning a researcher's implicit knowledge into externalized, manipulable Obsidian notes/claims/argument-graphs amplifies the human+system cognitive unit. Supports prioritizing rich external representations (dashboards, projected canvas, contradictions view) as cognition-bearing, not decorative.

ideas.idea: Epistemic actions vs. pragmatic actions: users take actions whose value is to change their own internal state (reduce cognitive load), not to advance the task goal.
ideas.rationale: Direct design guidance for Memoria's PI-facing surfaces: support cheap, reversible 'epistemic' interactions (re-layout, filter, projection toggles, disposable projection engine) that let the PI think, not just commit final edits. Justifies the disposable/projected-canvas approach explicitly.

ideas.idea: Warning: a rigid digital interface can eliminate epistemic actions and inhibit users' creative coordination strategies; systems should support appropriation/customization and interoperate with analog media.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's PI-direct-access and Obsidian-native stance: keep notes as plain markdown the PI can freely appropriate, avoid locking knowledge into rigid app-only structures that foreclose the PI's own workflows.

ideas.idea: Evaluation should study the human+system cognitive unit (cognitive ethnography, in-situ) rather than reducing to lab control variables; usefulness before usability.
ideas.rationale: Tempers how Memoria should validate surfaces: judge whether the PI+vault unit produces insight in real research practice, not just isolated feature benchmarks. Single-researcher scale makes lightweight in-situ observation feasible; full cognitive-ethnography methodology is heavier than needed.

ideas.idea: Cognition as fundamentally social/collaborative, expanding the unit of analysis to multiple people and shared visualizations (Many Eyes, social visualization).
ideas.rationale: Memoria is explicitly single-researcher (NOT multi-user/collaborative); the social-cognition expansion of the framework does not apply to the design constraints.

file: Zhong et al. - 2021 - Adapting language models for zero-shot learning by meta-tuning on dataset and prompt collections.md
title: Adapting Language Models for Zero-shot Learning by Meta-tuning on Dataset and Prompt Collections
one_line: Introduces meta-tuning: fine-tune an LM on a collection of 43 classification datasets unified into a Yes/No QA format with 441 hand-written label descriptions, directly optimizing the zero-shot-classification objective so the model answers prompts better on unseen tasks.

ideas.idea: Unify heterogeneous classification tasks into a single Yes/No question-answering format with the input as context and the label semantics expressed as a natural-language question.
ideas.rationale: Directly applicable to Memoria's many local-model classifiers (claim-sentence classification, MASSW aspect tagging, uncertainty flagging, pre-file similarity gate): casting each as a Yes/No or multi-question QA prompt gives one consistent, swappable interface for the qwen2.5:7b stack.

ideas.idea: Annotate 1-3 paraphrased label descriptions per label and ensemble them (average probabilities) to improve robustness; some phrasings work far better than others for the same label.
ideas.rationale: Cheap, training-free win for Memoria's local-model classifications: maintain a few paraphrased prompt variants per classifier label and ensemble, improving faithfulness without fine-tuning; the 'atheism vs. against religious belief' example shows wording sensitivity Memoria must guard against.

ideas.idea: Define 'unseen' harshly via tag-based dataset grouping (same property tags => same task) so generalization isn't inflated by near-duplicate train/test tasks.
ideas.rationale: The tag-grouping notion of similarity is a clean heuristic for Memoria's record-linkage/dedup and pre-file similarity gate: group notes/claims by property tags to decide 'same enough' for dedup, and to avoid leakage when evaluating its own classifiers.

ideas.idea: Pre-training does the heavy lifting; meta-tuning only teaches the model to use latent ability, and early stopping is crucial (over-training memorizes tasks and hurts unseen generalization).
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's no-fine-tune posture: rely on already-capable instruction-tuned local models and good prompting rather than bespoke tuning; if any light adaptation is ever done, the early-stopping/memorization warning is the key caution.

ideas.idea: Robust evaluation via AUC-ROC distributional statistics (E[delta], P[delta>t]) and scatter-plot comparisons, avoiding single-scalar/threshold-sensitive metrics like F1/accuracy.
ideas.rationale: Threshold-free, distribution-aware comparison is a sound way for Memoria to compare classifier/prompt versions in its quality tier without committing to a brittle decision cutoff, which matters because local-model calibration drifts.

ideas.idea: Meta-tuning on user-supplied prompts as a centralized API business model, with attendant privacy/poisoning/fairness risks at scale.
ideas.rationale: Assumes a multi-user cloud inference API collecting prompts; contradicts Memoria's local, single-researcher, no-data-egress design. The risk discussion is irrelevant to a local vault.

file: Yong et al. - 2023 - BLOOM+1 adding language support to BLOOM for zero-shot prompting.md
title: BLOOM+1: Adding Language Support to BLOOM for Zero-Shot Prompting
one_line: An empirical study of language-adaptation strategies (continued pretraining vs. adapters vs. (IA)^3) for extending the 176B BLOOM multilingual LLM to eight unseen languages under resource-constrained zero-shot prompting.

ideas.idea: Adapter-based parameter-efficient finetuning (MAD-X, (IA)^3) beats full continued pretraining for adapting large models, at far fewer trainable parameters and lower compute.
ideas.rationale: Memoria runs cheap local off-the-shelf models (qwen2.5:7b) and does no model training/adaptation; it does not own a multilingual-adaptation pipeline. The constraint is engine-side prompting, not finetuning.

ideas.idea: Perplexity does not correlate with downstream task quality — a held-out LM-loss metric can mislead about real performance.
ideas.rationale: A useful cautionary insight for Memoria's L5 quality evaluation: don't proxy claim-extraction/triage quality with token-level likelihood; measure the actual downstream task (claim faithfulness, triage accuracy) directly. Weak, general principle only.

ideas.idea: Including a new language in the multitask instruction-tuning mixture is the most effective way to teach it, but needs diverse tasks.
ideas.rationale: Memoria does no instruction tuning; English-only single-researcher corpus. Off-scope.

file: Yoo et al. - 2021 - GPT3Mix leveraging large-scale language models for text augmentation.md
title: GPT3Mix: Leveraging Large-scale Language Models for Text Augmentation
one_line: Uses a large LM to generate synthetic mixed text samples with soft pseudo-labels to augment training data and distill the LM's knowledge into a small fine-tuned classifier in data-scarce settings.

ideas.idea: Prompt-based data augmentation: generate synthetic labeled examples from a few real anchors to train a small downstream classifier, avoiding online LLM inference at serving time.
ideas.rationale: If Memoria ever needs a local lightweight classifier (e.g. claim-sentence type, discovery-relevance) with too few PI-labeled examples, an LLM can bootstrap training data offline so the runtime classifier is cheap/deterministic — fits the 'engines write, cheap models serve' posture. But labeled data is scarce by design (single PI), so this is a contingency, not a core need.

ideas.idea: Soft-label knowledge distillation: use the LM's normalized label-token probabilities as soft targets rather than hard labels, transferring class-distribution knowledge.
ideas.rationale: Adaptable to learning-to-rank triage / discovery-relevance scoring: a strong model's calibrated soft scores can train a cheaper local scorer. Technique-level borrow only.

ideas.idea: Decoupling-via-date benchmark (RT20): collect eval data dated after the model's training cutoff to rule out memorization.
ideas.rationale: Directly relevant to Memoria's evaluation discipline — when measuring discovery/claim-extraction quality on a local model, post-cutoff papers give a clean test of genuine extraction vs. memorized content. A concrete, transferable eval-design trick.

ideas.idea: Iterated augmentation amplifies bias; recommend human-in-the-loop filtering of synthetic samples.
ideas.rationale: Memoria already enforces a structural-review gate and PI approval; this generic warning adds nothing beyond the existing governance model.

file: Yu and Wang - 2026 - Knows agent-native structured research representations.md
title: Knows: Agent-Native Structured Research Representations
one_line: Proposes a thin schema-validated YAML 'sidecar' (KnowsRecord) that binds structured claims, evidence, provenance, and typed relations to a PDF so LLM agents consume papers cheaply and traceably without re-parsing prose.

ideas.idea: A per-paper structured sidecar carrying typed statements (claim/assumption/limitation/method/question/definition), evidence items, and a typed relation graph (supported_by, challenged_by, depends_on, supersedes, cites) that agents read INSTEAD of the PDF.
ideas.rationale: This is essentially Memoria's note model written as a portable artifact: atomic claims + claim-sentence classification + MASSW aspects + typed relations + argument graph. Memoria already builds this internally; Knows validates the design and offers a concrete field schema and an agent-consumption protocol to align our extraction output to. Strong confirmation that the structured layer (not the PDF) should be the agent's primary substrate.

ideas.idea: Two-dimensional confidence on each statement: claim_strength (how strongly the author asserts) separated from extraction_fidelity (how faithfully the metadata represents the source).
ideas.rationale: Maps almost exactly onto Memoria's uncertainty flags on atomic claims, but splits author-assertion-strength from extraction-faithfulness — a distinction Memoria should adopt explicitly because 'engines write, agents judge' needs to track our own extraction confidence separately from the paper's hedging.

ideas.idea: Deterministic schema linter (knows-lint): structural checks (schema validation, cross-ref integrity, ID uniqueness, prefix conventions, predicate constraints) that fail fast, with a clean separation that structural validity is verifiable but semantic correctness is not.
ideas.rationale: Directly fits Memoria's deterministic-ingest + structural review gate: a lint pass over extracted claim/relation records before notes are written, catching dangling relations and ID collisions deterministically while leaving semantic judgment to the agent/PI. This is the gate, formalized.

ideas.idea: Singly-linked version chain with replaces pointer + record_status: superseded, plus a freshness block (as_of, update_policy, stale_after) for cache invalidation.
ideas.rationale: Memoria already has claim supersession over time; the explicit replaces chain + freshness/stale_after fields give a concrete mechanism for the supersession dashboard and for the nightly discovery loop to know when a claim record is stale and needs re-checking.

ideas.idea: Cross-record references via record_id#local_id grammar, enabling review-as-sidecar where a review's weakness statements link to specific claims in the reviewed paper.
ideas.rationale: This is exactly how Memoria's contradictions dashboard and argument graph should link across notes — a typed edge from a critique/contradiction to a specific atomic claim ID in another note, making warrants and challenges machine-traversable. Adopt the stable-claim-ID + cross-note-reference discipline.

ideas.idea: Statements-only minimal sidecar retains ~88% of comprehension accuracy at 93% fewer tokens; relations aid traceability not comprehension; dropping evidence costs ~2pp.
ideas.rationale: Actionable for Memoria's retrieval/projection engine: when feeding a local cheap model context, a statements-only projection of a note is a high-value/low-token tier; reserve full evidence+relations for traceability tasks. A concrete token-budget tiering for the projection engine.

ideas.idea: Author-asserted, not certified: structural verification by linter, semantic verification (does the number actually appear in the PDF) deferred to LLM cross-checking against source_anchors; structured malice (schema-valid fabricated numbers) is more dangerous than ambiguous prose.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' exactly: deterministic lint is necessary but insufficient, so the agent must cross-check extracted numeric claims against source anchors before the gate. The source_anchor (page/section/figure pointer) per claim is a field Memoria should require for faithfulness.

ideas.idea: Sidecar generation quality varies sharply by model: only strong models produce content-complete records; cheap models pass lint but miss substance on complex papers.
ideas.rationale: Tempers Memoria's local-cheap-model default: extraction of the structured record may need the strongest available model (or PI review) for complex papers, while lint/validation and simple papers can run locally. Informs the test-split-by-what-uses-the-model policy.

ideas.idea: Hybrid 'Knows+Fallback' consumption: read structured record first, fall back to full text only when confidence is below threshold, localized by source anchors.
ideas.rationale: A clean retrieval protocol for Memoria's retriever/scout profile: answer from the structured note layer first, escalate to the parsed full text (anchored to the cited section) only on low confidence — saving tokens and keeping answers grounded.

ideas.idea: Community sidecar hub / agent-native publishing vision where structure is primary and PDF is a rendered projection.
ideas.rationale: The multi-publisher hub, signed attestation, and inversion-of-publishing trajectory assume an ecosystem/multi-party scale; Memoria is single-researcher and consumes existing PDFs. The vision is out of scope; only the per-paper schema/protocol transfers.

file: Yu et al. - 2026 - ArtifactLinker linking scientific artifacts for automatic state-of-the-art discovery.md
title: ArtifactLinker: Linking Scientific Artifacts for Automatic State-of-the-Art Discovery
one_line: Models HuggingFace as a heterogeneous artifact graph (models/datasets/papers/code with evaluation edges) and does rank-then-verify SOTA discovery: a GNN/LLM ranks unobserved model-dataset links, then an agent verifies the top candidates by running code.

ideas.idea: Rank-then-verify pipeline: cheap graph/LLM ranker prunes a combinatorial candidate space, then expensive verification runs only on top-ranked candidates.
ideas.rationale: Maps directly onto Memoria's nightly discovery loop + learning-to-rank triage + pre-file similarity gate: a cheap relevance ranker over many candidate papers/links, then expensive agent judgment/PI review only on the top few. Confirms and sharpens the existing two-stage discovery architecture.

ideas.idea: Selection-bias correction via factorization E[Y] = P(viable) x E[Y|viable], trained as a link-predictor head times an attribute head over a shared encoder.
ideas.rationale: Relevant to discovery-relevance scoring: don't train the relevance/score predictor only on accepted/observed items (the PI's past filed notes) — that over-predicts high scores for items that would actually be rejected. Memoria's triage should model 'probability the PI would accept' separately from 'value if accepted'. A genuine modeling caution for learning-to-rank.

ideas.idea: Cross-instance self-evolving memory: an LLM reviewer distills root causes/workarounds from each run's logs into structured, categorized memories (model/dataset/task-specific) injected into the next run's system prompt.
ideas.rationale: An accumulating-procedural-memory pattern usable for Memoria's agent runtime / skill governance: distill recurring ingest or verification failures into reusable, categorized hints. Must respect the MCP-only sandbox (no code execution), so adopt only the memory-distillation idea, not the code-running executor.

ideas.idea: Planner/executor multi-agent separation: planner drafts an error-aware blueprint from memory, executor implements/debugs via a CodeAct (code-running) workflow.
ideas.rationale: The executor's value here is autonomous code execution against artifacts; Memoria's agents get NO code-execution/terminal/file access ever (ADR-32/46, MCP-only sandbox). Determinism and faithfulness are done by engines, not a code-running agent. Contradicts the core constraint.

ideas.idea: Heterogeneous artifact graph with typed nodes (paper/model/dataset/code) and quantified evaluation edges (e.g. F1 as edge attribute); structural enrichment with paper/code nodes lifts ranking quality.
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's typed-relation graph: adding auxiliary typed nodes/edges (a claim's supporting dataset, the benchmark it was evaluated on, with the metric value as an edge attribute) enriches retrieval and contradiction detection beyond plain note-to-note links. Borrow the typed-edge-with-quantitative-attribute idea; the GNN training apparatus is overkill at single-PI scale.

ideas.idea: GNNs beat LLM+serialized-neighborhood at high node degree because serializing large neighborhoods overflows the context window; LLMs win at cold-start (low degree) via textual priors.
ideas.rationale: A practical hybrid heuristic for Memoria's similarity/relevance scoring: use lightweight graph/embedding signals when a note is densely connected, fall back to LLM judgment for sparse/new notes where graph evidence is absent. Informs the pre-file similarity gate and entity-dedup record-linkage thresholds.

ideas.idea: Redact ground-truth metrics from node text before summarizing to prevent leakage into the ranking model.
ideas.rationale: A training-data-leakage hygiene step specific to their supervised GNN benchmark; Memoria isn't training such a predictor on its own corpus, so not applicable.

file: Yuan et al. - 2022 - Wordcraft story writing with large language models.md
title: Wordcraft: Story Writing With Large Language Models
one_line: A human-AI co-writing text editor where pre-built and user-defined LLM controls support story writing, with a 25-writer study showing scaffolded controls beat raw continuation and raw chat without harming ownership.

ideas.idea: Provide pre-built, tested prompt-backed controls rather than relying on users to write good prompts, because raw chat (even with full model access) underperformed scaffolded controls.
ideas.rationale: Directly supports Memoria's PI-direct-access + structured-capture-forms posture: surface curated, compiled-prompt actions in the Obsidian UI (capture forms, gate actions) instead of expecting the PI to craft prompts. The chat-vs-scaffold result is evidence that compiled profiles/skills beat freeform agent chat for the PI.

ideas.idea: Combine pre-built controls WITH an on-the-fly custom-request control; the custom control had the highest engagement.
ideas.rationale: Argues Memoria should pair its fixed surfaces (dashboards, gates, inspector) with an escape-hatch free-form query against a note/claim — but constrained to MCP read-only tools. Borrow the dual scaffold+custom pattern, bounded by the sandbox.

ideas.idea: Related-example prompting / meta-prompting: seed a zero-shot request with loosely related exemplars; have the model suggest what to ask next.
ideas.rationale: Meta-prompting (the system proposing useful next questions/relations) could power Memoria's discovery/inspector to suggest relevant follow-up queries or candidate relations to the PI. A lightweight prompting technique, not a core mechanism.

ideas.idea: Output need not be 'accurate' to be useful; co-writing tools need holistic/flexible evaluation, not single accuracy metrics; preserve user ownership.
ideas.rationale: This is the right philosophy for open-ended creative writing, but Memoria prizes faithfulness over flash and deterministic, gated outputs over inspiring-but-wrong suggestions. The 'imperfect-but-inspiring' value model contradicts Memoria's faithfulness constraint for a research-knowledge system.

file: Cheng et al. - 2023 - Binding language models in symbolic languages.md
title: Binding Language Models in Symbolic Languages (BINDER)
one_line: A training-free neural-symbolic framework that parses a natural-language question into an executable program (SQL/Python) with embedded LM API calls f(Q;D), so deterministic interpreter logic handles what it can and an LM is invoked only for the sub-parts the grammar cannot express.

ideas.idea: Hybrid execution: a deterministic symbolic interpreter does the bulk of the work and the LM is invoked only at explicit, bounded API-call points (f(question; data)) where determinism cannot reach.
ideas.rationale: Mirrors Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge' / deterministic-ingest pillar: extraction and structural ops should be deterministic, with the LM confined to narrow, auditable sub-questions (e.g. 'is this claim uncertain?', 'is country X in North America?') rather than end-to-end generation. Adapting the f(Q;D) call-site discipline keeps faithfulness over flash.

ideas.idea: Interpretable, debuggable intermediate program over end-to-end output — the explicit program lets a human localize and fix the error.
ideas.rationale: Aligns with Memoria's read-only inspector / structural review gate: an explicit intermediate representation (e.g. the typed extraction or relation plan) the PI can audit before notes are written is exactly the governance posture Memoria wants. Borrow the principle, not table-QA specifics.

ideas.idea: Scalability via passing only 3 table rows + schema instead of the whole knowledge source, keeping inputs small while accuracy holds.
ideas.rationale: Useful for Memoria's local/cheap-model constraint (qwen2.5:7b, 16GB GPU): feed schema + a tiny sample to the parser, let deterministic code touch the full data. Relevant to keeping retrieval/extraction prompts within small-model context windows.

ideas.idea: Majority-voting (MBR-EXEC) over n sampled programs to pick the final answer.
ideas.rationale: Sampling 20 programs per item via a frontier code model (Codex) is expensive and non-deterministic — contradicts Memoria's deterministic-ingest and cheap-local-model constraints. Single-researcher scale doesn't justify the cost.

ideas.idea: Whole approach depends on GPT-3 Codex (code-davinci-002), a large proprietary cloud model, for both parsing and execution.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is local + open/cheap-model-first and MCP-sandboxed; a cloud Codex dependency for the core loop is off-constraint. The architectural insight is transferable but the concrete model choice is not.

file: Cheng et al. - 2023 - The now and future of ChatGPT and span style=font-va.md
title: The now and future of ChatGPT and GPT in psychiatry
one_line: A timely review surveying current and speculative uses of ChatGPT/GPT in psychiatry (documentation, writing assistance, future empathy/emotion-aware systems) plus its limitations and ethical guardrails.

ideas.idea: GPT fabricates facts and references when asked to summarize prior studies, so human verification of generated content is mandatory.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's faithfulness-over-flash and PI-approval gate: any LLM-summarized claim or citation must pass a structural review / supersession check before being written to the vault. Generic but directly motivates the contradiction/citation-verification machinery.

ideas.idea: Human-in-the-loop supervision as the safest operating mode — AI assists, the professional takes full responsibility for output.
ideas.rationale: Exactly Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' governance stance, restated for a high-stakes domain. Confirms the design posture rather than adding mechanism.

ideas.idea: Prompt sensitivity: outputs vary widely with prompt wording, requiring deliberate prompt iteration per task.
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's compiled-profiles / skill-governance approach — fixed, versioned prompts per engine rather than ad-hoc prompting. Minor reinforcement.

ideas.idea: Future fully-automated psychotherapy systems integrating empathy, emotion recognition, wearable/physiological signals, and facial-expression analysis.
ideas.rationale: Multimodal clinical/affective system for patient care; off-domain for a single-researcher academic knowledge vault. No buildable component for Memoria.

ideas.idea: Background tutorial on transformer/GPT architecture and NLP history.
ideas.rationale: Pure background; no design mechanism for Memoria.

file: Cheng et al. - 2024 - Emotion-LLaMA multimodal emotion recognition and reasoning with instruction tuning.md
title: Emotion-LLaMA: Multimodal Emotion Recognition and Reasoning with Instruction Tuning
one_line: A multimodal LLM (audio HuBERT + multi-view visual encoders + LLaMA, LoRA-tuned) plus the auto-annotated MERR dataset, achieving SOTA on multimodal emotion recognition/reasoning.

ideas.idea: Coarse-to-fine annotation pipeline: auto-label a large pool with a cheap LLM (coarse), then expert-refine a small subset (fine), each stage measurably improving downstream quality.
ideas.rationale: Transferable to Memoria's ingest: cheap-model coarse extraction/classification of all incoming papers, then a small PI-reviewed fine tier at the structural gate. Matches single-researcher economics and the gate model — borrow the staged-annotation idea, not the emotion task.

ideas.idea: Multi-tool deterministic feature pipeline (OpenFace AUs -> peak-frame selection -> per-modality describers -> LLaMA aggregation) producing a structured multimodal description.
ideas.rationale: The pattern of deterministic specialized extractors feeding a final LLM aggregation step mirrors Memoria's deterministic-ingest-then-judge architecture (parse locally, LLM assembles). Worth borrowing the orchestration shape for claim/aspect extraction.

ideas.idea: LoRA parameter-efficient tuning (0.495% of params trainable) to specialize a 7B model for a niche task.
ideas.rationale: If Memoria ever needs a specialized local extractor beyond prompting qwen2.5:7b, LoRA fits the 16GB-GPU constraint. Keep as a fallback technique, not a current need (Memoria prefers prompting over training).

ideas.idea: Multimodal emotion recognition from audio/video/facial micro-expressions as the core capability.
ideas.rationale: Memoria ingests text papers (Zotero/citekey backbone); audio/visual affect recognition is off-domain. No claim/argument-graph relevance.

ideas.idea: MERR dataset and challenge-benchmark leaderboard results (DFEW, MER2023/2024).
ideas.rationale: Domain-specific benchmark artifacts with no transfer to a personal research vault.

file: Choi et al. - 2026 - CiteGuard faithful citation attribution for LLMs via retrieval-augmented validation.md
title: CiteGuard: Faithful Citation Attribution for LLMs via Retrieval-Augmented Validation
one_line: A retrieval-augmented agent that, given a claim excerpt, finds the paper(s) a human would cite — reframing citation evaluation as attribution alignment and beating LLM-as-a-Judge by grounding every judgment in real search actions.

ideas.idea: Reframe citation evaluation as 'citation attribution alignment' — does the suggested citation match what a human author would cite for this exact claim — rather than asking an LLM to judge correctness in a vacuum.
ideas.rationale: Directly buildable for Memoria's claim/argument graph: when an extracted atomic claim asserts something attributable, run an attribution check that grounds it in retrieved sources before writing the supports/cites relation. Strengthens the warrant/contradiction machinery with verifiable provenance.

ideas.idea: Expanded retrieval action set — ask_for_more_context (pull the surrounding paragraph of the claim) and search_text_snippet (search inside paper bodies, not just title/abstract) — as the main driver of accuracy over PDF-read-dependent baselines.
ideas.rationale: Concrete tools for Memoria's MCP-only retriever/scout profile: snippet-level body search plus claim-context expansion fit the sandbox (no file/PDF-read needed) and improve the pre-file similarity gate and discovery relevance scoring. Body-text snippet search over title/abstract-only is a directly adoptable upgrade.

ideas.idea: LLM-as-a-Judge is unreliable for citation/claim verification — recall as low as 16-17%, rejecting correct citations over minor terminology variation, and biased/prompt-sensitive.
ideas.rationale: Critical design constraint for Memoria: do NOT use a bare LLM judge at the structural review gate for attribution/contradiction calls; require retrieval grounding first. This validates Memoria's 'agents judge' only with evidence-in-hand posture and warns against ungrounded LLM-judge shortcuts.

ideas.idea: Suggest multiple valid alternative citations (lateral + backward reasoning), not just the single oracle, via iterative runs with an exclusion set excluding already-selected papers.
ideas.rationale: Maps onto Memoria's typed-relations and discovery loop: surfacing alternative supporting references for a claim enriches the argument graph and feeds proactive discovery. The exclusion-set iterative retrieval is a simple, deterministic mechanism to adopt for 'find more support for this claim'.

ideas.idea: Model-agnostic and cheap: works with small/open models (Qwen3, DeepSeek-R1) at ~$0.005/sample, approaching human accuracy (68.1% vs 69.2%); portable across retrieval backends (Semantic Scholar, arXiv).
ideas.rationale: Fits Memoria's local/cheap-open-model and single-researcher constraints exactly — the agent's value is in retrieval actions, not a frontier model. Backend portability suits a local Zotero/Semantic-Scholar-backed vault.

ideas.idea: Retrieval-vs-long-context tradeoff: snippet retrieval uses ~half the tokens of reading full papers at small accuracy cost, with a token-budget switch.
ideas.rationale: Useful tuning knob for Memoria's small-context local models: default to snippet retrieval, allow full-read only when the token budget permits. Borrow the budget-aware switch.

file: Chordia et al. - 2023 - Deceptive design patterns in safety technologies a case study of the citizen app.md
title: Deceptive Design Patterns in Safety Technologies: A Case Study of the Citizen App
one_line: A qualitative CHI case study showing how the Citizen app uses deceptive design patterns (forced action, misdirection, obstruction, publish, obscure, social investment) to manufacture anxiety and drive monetization, proposing 'emotional load' and 'social injustice' as new harm categories.

ideas.idea: Forced action / artificial dependency pattern: requiring tangential actions (enable all alerts to view the feed; share entire contact list to add one friend) to complete a task.
ideas.rationale: An anti-pattern catalog for Memoria's Obsidian surfaces: the dashboards/gates/forms must never coerce the PI (e.g. force-enabling a firehose to see anything). Reinforces the PI-direct-access and faithfulness pillars by naming what to avoid.

ideas.idea: Manufacturing anxiety by indiscriminately raising the salience of low-relevance, non-proximate, or stale items in an undifferentiated notification stream ('boy who cried wolf', 1-in-20 useful).
ideas.rationale: Direct caution for Memoria's nightly proactive discovery loop and triage: surfacing too many low-relevance discoveries erodes trust. Motivates strong relevance ranking, severity/recency filtering, and a quiet, PI-controlled cadence rather than alert spam — exactly what learning-to-rank triage and the relevance gate should prevent.

ideas.idea: Obscure pattern (hiding what data is collected/how) erodes user trust and willingness to engage.
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's read-only inspector + transparency posture: the PI should always be able to see exactly what an engine extracted, stored, and why. Transparency-as-trust is a design value to keep, framed here as the inverse harm.

ideas.idea: Misdirection / social-investment / obstruction monetization patterns (floating upgrade button, hidden Skip, social-metric nudges).
ideas.rationale: Monetization/engagement-maximization tactics for a commercial multi-user app; Memoria is a single-researcher non-commercial tool with no upsell or engagement KPI. Off-constraint.

ideas.idea: Proposed expansion of the harm taxonomy to include 'emotional load' and 'social injustice'; deceptive-infrastructure framing.
ideas.rationale: Sociocultural harm theory for consumer safety tech; valuable scholarship but no buildable mechanism for an academic knowledge vault.

file: Hämäläinen et al. - 2023 - Evaluating large language models in generating synthetic HCI research data a case study.md
title: Evaluating Large Language Models in Generating Synthetic HCI Research Data: a Case Study
one_line: Empirically evaluates GPT-3 as a generator of synthetic open-ended HCI questionnaire responses and, in doing so, introduces an automated LLM-based qualitative-coding + embedding + clustering pipeline for comparing text corpora.

ideas.idea: Automated two-level inductive coding: prompt the LLM to emit compact semicolon-separated codes per answer, then embed-cluster the codes into themes (analogous to Braun & Clarke thematic-analysis stages 2-3).
ideas.rationale: Directly maps onto Memoria's atomic-claim/aspect extraction and keyphrase tag candidates: distilling each note/answer into compact codes before embedding removes the noise of multi-reason text. A useful pre-clustering normalization step for the BERTopic clustering MCP.

ideas.idea: Coding-then-clustering reduces embedding noise versus embedding raw text, because raw multi-reason text confuses the embedding; the same coding biases are applied to all corpora, enabling fair comparison.
ideas.rationale: Memoria already extracts atomic claims; this is the justification for clustering over distilled atoms rather than full note text, and the 'same bias applied uniformly enables comparison' insight supports deterministic, comparable contradiction/topic dashboards.

ideas.idea: Pipeline of LLM-coding -> embedding -> UMAP -> HDBSCAN -> frequency counting, with cluster names taken from codes nearest the cluster-mean embedding (explicitly the BERTopic recipe plus a coding step).
ideas.rationale: Concrete, cheap, local-runnable recipe for Memoria's BERTopic clustering MCP and topic-frequency surfaces; cluster-centroid naming gives human-readable space/cluster labels for the projection engine.

ideas.idea: Heuristic output-quality filters for generated text: cut at first 'Researcher:'/newline, discard <10-word or >10-char-repetition outputs and regenerate.
ideas.rationale: Memoria engines do deterministic ingest; cheap structural validity filters (length, repetition, dodging) before content reaches the structural review gate cut obvious garbage without an LLM judge call.

ideas.idea: Frechet distance + precision/recall over code embeddings as corpus-distribution quality/diversity metrics; observation that instruction-tuned models have high precision but low recall (less diversity).
ideas.rationale: Memoria could measure whether discovery/clustering output preserves the diversity of the corpus; the precision/recall framing flags when a cheap local model collapses to a few dominant themes ('Journey bias'), relevant to triage/discovery-relevance scoring quality checks.

ideas.idea: Errors must be caught by domain knowledge: 'imaginary gameplay' factual errors are coherent and undetectable from surface text, unlike dodging/incompleteness which a classifier can catch.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' gate: cheap structural checks handle surface anomalies, but factual/claim-level errors require evidence-grounded judging (claim supersession / contradiction graph) or PI review — not a single LLM pass.

ideas.idea: Use LLMs to generate synthetic user-study/questionnaire participant data for piloting experiments.
ideas.rationale: Off-domain for Memoria: a single-researcher knowledge system ingests real papers/claims, not synthetic survey respondents. Faithfulness-over-flash and 'validate with real data' caveats make synthetic-participant generation out of scope.

file: Höök and Löwgren - 2012 - Strong concepts intermediate-level knowledge in interaction design research.md
title: Strong Concepts: Intermediate-Level Knowledge in Interaction Design Research
one_line: Proposes 'strong concepts' as a named intermediate level of generative knowledge between concrete instances and general theories, and gives criteria (contestable/defensible/substantive) and a horizontal/vertical grounding process for constructing and validating them.

ideas.idea: An explicit three-tier abstraction ladder for knowledge: instances <-> intermediate-level concepts <-> theories, with concepts abstracted from instances by 'scope of applicability'.
ideas.rationale: A natural typed-relation/abstraction structure for Memoria's note graph: atomic claims and paper instances at the bottom, an 'intermediate concept' note type that generalizes across several papers, theories above. Gives a principled rung between per-claim notes and broad topic spaces.

ideas.idea: Vertical grounding (link a concept down to the instances that embody it and up to the theories it concretizes) and horizontal grounding (relate it to sibling concepts, like a Related Work section).
ideas.rationale: Maps cleanly to Memoria's typed-relation + argument graph: down-edges (evidenced-by / instantiated-by), up-edges (concretizes-theory), sideways-edges (related-to / contrasts-with). A buildable schema for relating concept-notes to claims and to each other.

ideas.idea: Academic quality criteria for a knowledge contribution: contestable (novel, not already believed), defensible (grounded empirically/analytically/theoretically with criticizable reasoning), substantive (relevant/generative).
ideas.rationale: A concrete rubric for the structural review gate when an engine proposes a new synthesized concept-note: is it contestable (not redundant with existing notes — pre-file similarity gate), defensible (cites supporting claims), substantive (links to enough instances). Turns 'agents judge' into checkable tests.

ideas.idea: Concepts are provisional and contestable — they may last only briefly and validation includes examining rejected alternative abstractions and why they were inferior.
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's claim-supersession and contradiction handling: synthesized concept-notes carry uncertainty/provisional flags and record superseded alternatives rather than asserting permanence — faithfulness over flash.

ideas.idea: Knowledge construction as ongoing discursive conversation requiring shared language ('languaging' of design); generativity (potential to create new instances) as a value of a contribution.
ideas.rationale: This is about a multi-scholar community building shared vocabulary; Memoria is explicitly single-researcher (the PI), so the communal-discourse framing doesn't transfer. The generativity value is interesting but not a buildable mechanism here.

file: Imani et al. - 2023 - MathPrompter mathematical reasoning using large language models.md
title: MathPrompter: Mathematical Reasoning Using Large Language Models
one_line: Improves LLM arithmetic accuracy by generating multiple analytical solutions (algebraic expression + Python function), compute-verifying them on random variable assignments, and accepting answers only when they reach consensus.

ideas.idea: Multi-verification / cross-checking: solve the same problem via two independent representations (algebraic and Pythonic) and require their outputs to agree before accepting.
ideas.rationale: The cross-representation consensus pattern transfers to Memoria's extraction reliability: e.g., extract a claim/relation via two prompts or two routes and accept only on agreement, raising confidence before the structural gate. A cheap self-consistency signal for the judging layer.

ideas.idea: Compute verification via Python eval() of generated expressions on multiple random inputs to validate intermediate steps, not just the final answer.
ideas.rationale: Requires executing model-generated code; Memoria's agents are MCP-only sandboxed with no code execution ever (D40/D41, ADR-32/46). The execution-based verifier is architecturally forbidden, and arithmetic is not a Memoria task.

ideas.idea: Statistical significance: repeat generation N~5 times and report the most frequent (consensus) value to absorb one-step errors.
ideas.rationale: Majority-vote self-consistency over repeated cheap-model runs is a usable reliability boost for noisy local-model extractions (qwen2.5:7b), where a single pass is error-prone; bounded N keeps cost local-friendly.

ideas.idea: Surface the model's intermediate 'thought process' (the generated expression/function) to give the user a hint and build trust in predictions.
ideas.rationale: Aligns with Memoria's read-only inspector and provenance ethos: show the derivation/evidence behind an engine output so the PI can audit it, rather than an opaque verdict.

file: Izacard et al. - 2022 - Atlas few-shot learning with retrieval augmented language models.md
title: Atlas: Few-shot Learning with Retrieval Augmented Language Models
one_line: A retrieval-augmented LM (Contriever dual-encoder + Fusion-in-Decoder T5) jointly pre-trained so an 11B model beats a 540B parametric model on knowledge tasks, with an editable, swappable, temporally-updatable document index.

ideas.idea: Outsource knowledge to an external non-parametric index so a small model handles reasoning while the index holds facts; far fewer parameters needed for knowledge-intensive tasks.
ideas.rationale: Exactly Memoria's posture: the Obsidian vault is the non-parametric knowledge store and a cheap local model (qwen2.5:7b) does the reasoning over retrieved notes. Validates the architecture of preferring small local models + strong retrieval over giant parametric models.

ideas.idea: Index updateability/temporal swap: change or version the index at test time (e.g., 2017 vs 2020 Wikipedia) and the model faithfully reflects the new knowledge with no retraining; index can be edited to add/remove documents.
ideas.rationale: Core to Memoria's claim-supersession and continual ingestion: new papers/claims update behavior immediately by re-indexing, no model retraining. The TempLAMA faithfulness result (model conditions strongly on supplied index) supports 'faithfulness over flash' and keeping current-state knowledge live.

ideas.idea: Interpretability via retrieved-passage inspection plus leakage auditing (n-gram overlap check; re-run with leaked passages filtered to estimate true performance).
ideas.rationale: Memoria's read-only inspector should show which notes/claims grounded an answer; the leakage/overlap audit is a usable pre-file similarity-gate technique to detect near-duplicate or verbatim-overlapping evidence and avoid double-counting.

ideas.idea: Dense dual-encoder retriever (Contriever) trained unsupervised via contrastive loss, with relevance = dot product of independently-encoded query/doc embeddings.
ideas.rationale: A concrete blueprint for Memoria's retriever/scout profile and discovery relevance scoring; unsupervised training means no per-note annotation needed, fitting single-researcher scale where labeled relevance data is scarce.

ideas.idea: Product-Quantization index compression: Wikipedia index 49GB->4GB, combined 587GB->50GB with negligible accuracy loss, fitting on a single GPU.
ideas.rationale: Directly relevant to running retrieval on the test env's single 16GB RTX 4060 Ti — PQ compression makes a sizable personal vault index fit local hardware, supporting Memoria's local/cheap constraint.

ideas.idea: Query-side fine-tuning: freeze the document encoder so the index never needs recomputation, avoiding expensive index refresh; in few-shot it even slightly beats full fine-tuning (less overfitting).
ideas.rationale: For Memoria's small-data, single-researcher regime, adapting only the query encoder avoids re-embedding the whole vault on every update — a cheap, stable personalization path for the retriever profile.

ideas.idea: Train the retriever from LM signal (Perplexity Distillation / ADist / EMDR2 / LOOP) so no document relevance annotations are required — the LM tells the retriever which docs helped.
ideas.rationale: Removes the need for hand-labeled relevance judgments Memoria can't realistically collect at single-user scale; PDist is noted as the most stable/efficient choice — a sensible default if Memoria ever trains its own retriever. Full joint pre-training is likely too heavy locally, hence borrow not adopt.

ideas.idea: De-biased inference for multiple-choice via cyclic permutation of answer-letter assignments to remove letter-position bias.
ideas.rationale: Specific to MMLU-style multiple-choice answering, which Memoria does not do; the 4x inference cost and MC framing don't fit Memoria's claim/relation extraction surfaces.

file: Jacobs et al. - 2021 - Designing AI for trust and collaboration in time-constrained medical decisions a sociotechnical len.md
title: Designing AI for Trust and Collaboration in Time-Constrained Medical Decisions: A Sociotechnical Lens
one_line: Co-design study with primary-care physicians showing that clinical AI decision-support fails for sociotechnical reasons, and arguing for on-demand contrastive explanations (diff vs. standards of care) and one-time rather than per-decision trust.

ideas.idea: On-demand explanations as contrastive differentials: instead of explaining every prediction, surface why the model output diverges from existing guidelines/standards only when it diverges.
ideas.rationale: Strong fit for Memoria's contradictions dashboard and gate: don't justify every engine-written note, but when a new claim contradicts or supersedes an established one, present the diff vs. the prior standard. Contrastive 'what changed and why' is exactly the supersession/contradiction surface.

ideas.idea: Don't force a trust decision at every interaction — make validation/provenance findable on demand, not pushed into every view; users establish trust once.
ideas.rationale: Maps to Memoria's read-only inspector + structural gate UX: per-note approval shouldn't demand the PI re-evaluate trust each time; provenance/validation should be one click away (findable) but not clutter the default surface. Reduces gate fatigue.

ideas.idea: Feature-importance explanations were judged useless by busy experts because they show correlated features, not actionable causes/next steps.
ideas.rationale: Caution for Memoria's explanation surfaces: prefer evidence/provenance and actionable next links (which claims, which papers) over raw saliency/feature attributions. Shapes what the inspector should show the PI.

ideas.idea: Surprising/divergent model output drives demand for deeper explanation; users abandon recommendations that conflict with their mental model without a causal account.
ideas.rationale: Informs Memoria's triage/discovery ranking and gate: when a discovered paper or extracted claim contradicts the PI's established notes, escalate with extra grounding rather than presenting it bare, or it will be dismissed.

ideas.idea: Let users voice skepticism / flag surprising outputs so the underlying model and guidelines can adapt over time.
ideas.rationale: A lightweight PI-feedback loop: flagging a surprising engine output feeds learning-to-rank triage and discovery-relevance scoring, adapting Memoria to the PI without multi-user complexity.

ideas.idea: Design clinical DSTs as multi-user systems supporting patient-provider collaboration (shared decision-making), with patient-facing tools.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is explicitly single-researcher (the PI); the multi-user patient-provider collaboration premise contradicts its scale and PI-direct-access model. Not applicable.

ideas.idea: Connect predictions to existing workflow processes / next steps rather than showing an isolated score; require up-front determination of when the tool should not be shown at all.
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and proactive discovery loop: an engine output should arrive wired to a next action (link, merge, supersede) and be suppressed when low-relevance, rather than dumped as a bare suggestion.

file: Ruan et al. - 2024 - Identifying the risks of LM agents with an LM-emulated sandbox.md
title: Identifying the Risks of LM Agents with an LM-Emulated Sandbox
one_line: ToolEmu uses an LM to emulate tool execution (a virtual sandbox) plus an LM-based safety/helpfulness evaluator to scalably red-team and quantify the risks of tool-using LM agents, finding even the safest agent fails ~24% of the time.

ideas.idea: LM-as-sandbox: an LM emulates tool execution from tool specs alone (no real implementation), with enforced requirements of input validation, accuracy, and state consistency.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's MCP-only sandbox already hard-denies file/terminal/code execution to agents; an LM emulator is a way to dry-run an MCP tool call before it touches the vault. Useful as a pre-flight check for write-path MCP tools (e.g. simulate what a 'write note' action would do) so the structural review gate can judge consequences before commit. Not core, but a partial technique worth keeping.

ideas.idea: Adversarial emulator that seeds long-tail high-risk states from a test case's declared 'potential risks & risky actions' to surface rare failures.
ideas.rationale: Memoria could pre-author a small red-team suite of underspecified PI instructions (ambiguous citekeys, conflicting claims) to stress-test ingest/triage agents, adapting the 'seed the worst case' idea to a single-researcher governance harness rather than enterprise tool safety.

ideas.idea: LM-based safety evaluator scoring trajectories on likelihood x severity, plus a complementary helpfulness evaluator to expose the safety/helpfulness tradeoff; agreement with humans matched inter-annotator agreement (Cohen kappa ~0.48).
ideas.rationale: Mirrors Memoria's 'agents judge' role. A likelihood-severity rubric and a separate did-it-actually-help dimension is a clean pattern for the structural review gate's judge step. The kappa-vs-human baseline is a useful validity bar for any LLM-judge Memoria deploys, but the threat model (financial/physical harm) is far heavier than Memoria's faithfulness-of-notes risk.

ideas.idea: Threat model of benign-but-underspecified instructions causing harm when the agent acts without seeking clarification; prompting agents to 'seek user confirmation before risky actions' improved both safety and helpfulness.
ideas.rationale: Directly supports Memoria's 'PI approves' pillar: when an ingest/triage instruction is ambiguous, the agent should surface it to the PI rather than guess (e.g. fabricate a citekey or merge the wrong entity). The finding that confirmation-seeking does not hurt helpfulness justifies the approval gate's cost.

ideas.idea: Whole framework targets cloud API agents (GPT-4/Claude) acting on banking, IoT, robots, traffic control at scale.
ideas.rationale: The high-stakes real-world actuation domain and the cloud-LM, enterprise-tool scale are off-domain for a single-researcher local markdown vault whose worst-case is a bad note. The full benchmark/emulator infrastructure is overkill for Memoria's deterministic, MCP-gated ingest.

file: Rule et al. - 2018 - Exploration and explanation in computational notebooks.md
title: Exploration and Explanation in Computational Notebooks
one_line: Three studies (1.23M GitHub notebooks, 221 academic notebooks, 15 analyst interviews) document a tension between messy exploratory work and clean shareable explanation, finding analysts rarely record reasoning/results and treat notebooks as personal artifacts.

ideas.idea: Notebooks record steps (88%) far more than reasoning (34%) or results (38%); explanatory text clusters at the start and decays toward the end.
ideas.rationale: Strong empirical motivation for Memoria's claim/aspect extraction: researchers under-document the WHY. Memoria's atomic-claim extraction, MASSW aspects, and argument-graph warrants exist precisely to capture reasoning that humans skip. This validates prioritizing 'reasoning/result' aspects, not just 'method' aspects, in the extraction schema.

ideas.idea: Analysts split work at points 'where a human has to evaluate it' (P7), keeping messy and presentation versions separate.
ideas.rationale: Maps exactly onto Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' gate and its disposable projection engine: keep the raw exploratory ingest separate from the clean projected/approved surface. Reinforces the structural review gate as the human-evaluation boundary.

ideas.idea: Provenance tracking is desired ('I wanted Jupyter to track what I did') but does not happen automatically, especially when state is overwritten/re-run.
ideas.rationale: Justifies Memoria's deterministic ingest + claim supersession over time: the system should automatically retain prior versions/dead-ends (superseded claims, contradiction history) so the PI never loses 'what was tried', solving the pain P6/P14 describe manually.

ideas.idea: Annotation should piggyback on existing user behavior or give immediate benefit (e.g. floating table-of-contents from markdown headers prompted more annotation); leverage structure to auto-generate documentation.
ideas.rationale: Design principle for Memoria's Obsidian surfaces: capture forms, keyphrase tag candidates, and spaces should reduce annotation effort or yield immediate navigation payoff, not add burden. Adapt the 'leverage existing structure' insight to keyphrase/aspect candidates the PI merely approves.

ideas.idea: Notebooks rarely stand alone; a narrative flows across multiple notebooks + README + emails + slides.
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's typed relations and argument graph spanning notes/claims rather than single self-contained notes. The cross-artifact narrative is what Memoria's relation graph and projected canvas are meant to make navigable.

ideas.idea: Specific findings about Jupyter cell ordering, GitHub rendering, package import frequencies, technical-debt framing of cleanup.
ideas.rationale: Notebook-platform-specific and collaboration-norm specifics (social expectations across labs, journal incentives) are not actionable for a single-researcher markdown vault and outside Memoria's domain.

file: Sainz et al. - 2021 - Label verbalization and entailment for effective zero- and few-shot relation extraction.md
title: Label Verbalization and Entailment for Effective Zero- and Few-Shot Relation Extraction
one_line: Recasts relation extraction as textual entailment by hand-writing short verbalization templates per relation (<15 min each) plus entity-type constraints, hitting 63% F1 zero-shot and 69% with 16 examples/relation on TACRED using an off-the-shelf NLI model.

ideas.idea: Reformulate relation extraction as NLI entailment: turn each relation label into a natural-language hypothesis template (e.g. '{subj} was born on {obj}') and score with an off-the-shelf entailment model, picking the max-entailment relation.
ideas.rationale: Directly buildable for Memoria's typed-relation extraction between notes/claims. A small NLI model is cheap, local, and deterministic-ish, fitting the qwen-class/local constraint, and avoids training a supervised RE classifier the single PI cannot label data for. The entailment score doubles as a confidence/uncertainty signal for the structural review gate.

ideas.idea: Entity-type constraints (delta_r function) disambiguate templates that verbalize multiple relations and zero out type-incoherent candidates at inference.
ideas.rationale: Memoria already has entity/record-linkage and typed notes; gating candidate relations by the linked entity types is a clean precision lever that costs nothing and reduces spurious relations before the PI approves them.

ideas.idea: Threshold-based no-relation detection (return NO-RELATION if no hypothesis beats T; T=0.5 default, tuned with ~2 examples/relation) outperformed a no-relation template; the no-relation/null case is where low-data systems mainly lag.
ideas.rationale: Critical for Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and contradiction/relation engines: most note pairs have NO relation, so a tunable abstain threshold prevents over-linking. The insight that 'detecting nothing is the hard part' should drive conservative defaults so the gate surfaces only high-confidence relations.

ideas.idea: Use the entailment model to generate high-quality silver labels on unlabeled data, then train a cheaper supervised model that drops into existing pipelines.
ideas.rationale: A path for Memoria to bootstrap a small fast local relation classifier from NLI pseudo-labels once the PI has approved enough relations, replacing per-call LLM inference in the nightly loop. Partial adopt: only worth it if NLI inference cost becomes a bottleneck.

ideas.idea: Hand-crafting verbalizations capped at 15 minutes/relation gives near-supervised performance; larger NLI backbones (DeBERTa) buy up to 12 F1 points zero-shot.
ideas.rationale: Confirms a low-effort path: the PI authors a small set of relation verbalizations for Memoria's relation taxonomy once. Choice of NLI backbone matters, so Memoria should pick the strongest local NLI model that fits the 16GB test-env GPU rather than the smallest.

file: Sanh et al. - 2022 - Multitask prompted training enables zero-shot task generalization.md
title: Multitask Prompted Training Enables Zero-Shot Task Generalization (T0)
one_line: Fine-tuning T5 on a large multitask mixture of datasets reformatted into diverse natural-language prompts (P3, ~2073 prompts) yields strong zero-shot generalization to held-out tasks, beating GPT-3 16x its size on 9/11 datasets.

ideas.idea: Multiple diverse prompt templates per task/dataset; training on more prompts per dataset improves median performance AND reduces variance (robustness to wording).
ideas.rationale: Actionable for Memoria's local-model prompting: for each extraction task (claim classification, aspect extraction, relation verbalization) author several paraphrased prompts and aggregate/ensemble to cut sensitivity to wording, improving determinism of ingest on a weak local model. Pure inference-time technique, no training needed.

ideas.idea: Cast every task into a unified text-to-text prompted format with explicit input/target templates and metadata (P3 templating language).
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's compiled-profile/skill-governance approach: a single templated prompt format with metadata (expected output schema, eval metric) standardizes how engines call the local model and makes outputs validatable before the gate. Borrow the template+metadata discipline, not the model.

ideas.idea: Train an 11B encoder-decoder via massively multitask prompted fine-tuning to induce zero-shot task generalization.
ideas.rationale: Memoria does not train foundation models and runs cheap local models (qwen2.5:7b); building/serving T0 is far outside single-researcher scale and the deterministic local constraint. Modern instruction-tuned local models already deliver this generalization off the shelf, superseding the need to replicate T0.

ideas.idea: True zero-shot evaluation hygiene: no prompt selection on held-out splits, report median + IQR across prompts rather than a cherry-picked best prompt.
ideas.rationale: Good evaluation discipline for Memoria's own benchmarks/metrics: when measuring an extraction engine's quality, report distribution across prompt variants, not a tuned best case, to avoid overstating capability. Relevant to the evaluation/benchmark pillar.

file: Santu and Feng - 2023 - TELeR a general taxonomy of LLM prompts for benchmarking complex tasks.md
title: TELeR: A General Taxonomy of LLM Prompts for Benchmarking Complex Tasks
one_line: Proposes the TELeR taxonomy (Turn, Expression, Level-of-Details 0-6, Role) so prompts for ill-defined complex tasks are categorized on a common standard, enabling apple-to-apple benchmarking; illustrated on meta-review generation and narrative braiding.

ideas.idea: Level-of-Details ladder (0-6): from bare data, to a one-line directive, to explicit goals, to enumerated distinct sub-tasks, to evaluation criteria/format constraints, to IR-fetched context, to explanation/justification-seeking.
ideas.rationale: A directly buildable spec for how Memoria's engines should construct prompts for complex extraction (claim atomization, contradiction analysis, meta-summaries). Memoria should default to high-level prompts (L4-L6: clear goals + sub-task decomposition + format limits + justification) for tasks the gate must audit, and standardize this so prompt changes are tracked.

ideas.idea: Explanation/justification-seeking as an explicit prompt dimension (Level 6): require the model to justify why it included/discarded information.
ideas.rationale: Core to Memoria's 'agents judge, PI approves' governance: every engine output that feeds the structural review gate should carry a justification so the PI can audit it, and so contradictions/claim-supersession decisions are explainable rather than opaque.

ideas.idea: Few-shot-or-criteria dimension: when no examples are given, the prompt should describe what a good vs bad response looks like (word limits, output format, allowed sources).
ideas.rationale: Memoria's single-PI setting rarely has labeled examples, so encoding explicit quality criteria/format constraints into engine prompts is the practical substitute and makes outputs schema-validatable before commit, fitting deterministic ingest.

ideas.idea: Distinct sub-task decomposition spelled out as numbered items so the LLM handles each individually (e.g. meta-review: contributions, common strengths, weaknesses, suggestions, missing refs).
ideas.rationale: Matches Memoria's MASSW-style aspect extraction and atomic-claim decomposition: forcing the model to emit per-aspect answers yields the structured fields Memoria stores, rather than a blob. The meta-review use case maps almost 1:1 onto Memoria's multi-source synthesis surfaces.

ideas.idea: Report the TELeR category of prompts used so independent benchmarking studies are comparable; just reporting accuracy without prompt details is meaningless.
ideas.rationale: Memoria should record the TELeR level of each engine's prompt as metadata in its evaluation/benchmark records and ADRs, so quality regressions can be attributed to prompt-detail changes versus model changes. Useful provenance discipline at single-researcher scale.

ideas.idea: System-role defined-vs-undefined and single-vs-multi-turn dimensions.
ideas.rationale: Minor knobs for Memoria's compiled profiles (retriever/scout role prompts). Worth recording as part of profile metadata, but lower leverage than the Level-of-Details ladder; multi-turn is mostly irrelevant to Memoria's batch ingest engines.

file: Barrett et al. - 2019 - Emotional expressions reconsidered challenges to inferring emotion from human facial movements.md
title: Emotional Expressions Reconsidered: Challenges to Inferring Emotion From Human Facial Movements
one_line: A large psychology review arguing that emotion cannot be reliably/specifically inferred from facial configurations, and laying out the reverse-inference criteria (reliability, specificity, generalizability, validity) that any such inference must meet.

ideas.idea: Reverse-inference rigor: an inferred latent state from a surface signal requires four explicit criteria - reliability p(signal|state), specificity p(state|signal), generalizability across populations/contexts, and validity against an independent ground truth - before the inference is warranted.
ideas.rationale: Directly transferable as a discipline for Memoria's claim extraction and discovery relevance scoring: when an engine infers a latent label (e.g. 'this sentence is a CLAIM', 'paper X supersedes Y', 'note is relevant'), the structural review gate should treat it as a reverse inference and flag low specificity rather than asserting it as fact. Reinforces Memoria's uncertainty-flags philosophy.

ideas.idea: Base-rate sensitivity: an apparently consistent signal-to-label mapping can be an artifact of how frequently the signal/label occurs, producing 'indiscriminate' labeling that looks reliable.
ideas.rationale: A concrete failure mode to guard against in learning-to-rank triage and the pre-file similarity gate - a label or tag that fires on almost everything carries no information; bake base-rate checks into scoring/eval rather than trusting raw hit rates.

ideas.idea: Prefer neutral descriptive terms over interpretive labels when criteria are unmet (call it 'a smile', not 'an expression of happiness').
ideas.rationale: Maps to Memoria's faithfulness-over-flash constraint: when claim classification or aspect extraction is uncertain, surface the descriptive quote rather than an unsupported interpretive category - the contradictions/argument-graph layers should distinguish observed text from inferred stance.

ideas.idea: Automated affect/emotion recognition from facial action units as a perception pipeline.
ideas.rationale: Entirely off-domain - Memoria ingests text-based academic papers, not faces/video; no facial-coding component exists or is wanted.

file: Basar et al. - 2024 - To what extent are large language models capable of generating substantial reflections for motivatio.md
title: To What Extent Are Large Language Models Capable of Generating Substantial Reflections for Motivational Interviewing Counseling Chatbots? A Human Evaluation
one_line: A human-evaluation study comparing GPT-4, BLOOM and FLAN-T5 against human-authored motivational-interviewing reflections on four criteria, finding GPT-4 matches/exceeds humans but that manual review remains essential.

ideas.idea: Multi-criterion human evaluation of generated text (appropriateness, specificity, naturalness, engagement) each with a definition plus positive/negative anchor examples on a 7-point scale.
ideas.rationale: A reusable rubric design for Memoria's L5 quality testing of LLM-generated artifacts (claim paraphrases, summaries, relation justifications) - decompose 'is this good?' into named criteria with anchored examples rather than a single holistic judgment.

ideas.idea: RankME magnitude-estimation plus TrueSkill to rank multiple generations against a fixed human reference, avoiding all-pairwise comparisons.
ideas.rationale: A cheap, scalable ranking method usable in Memoria's discovery relevance scoring / learning-to-rank triage evaluation where many candidate outputs must be ordered against a reference without full pairwise judging.

ideas.idea: Even a state-of-the-art LLM produces occasional inappropriate/confrontational or instruction-violating outputs (asked not to ask questions, it asked questions), so a comprehensive manual review must gate deployment.
ideas.rationale: Direct empirical support for Memoria's core governance pillar - 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' and the structural review gate before notes are written; LLM-generated note content must pass review, never auto-commit.

ideas.idea: Non-expert crowd evaluators can rate reflections as reliably as domain experts (citing Wu et al. 2023).
ideas.rationale: Assumes a crowd of evaluators; Memoria is single-researcher (one PI is the sole judge), so crowd-equivalence findings don't apply to its evaluation surface.

ideas.idea: Hybrid architecture: predetermined/templated dialogue scaffold with LLM-generated free-form reflections slotted in for personalization.
ideas.rationale: Conversational health-counseling chatbot architecture is off-domain; Memoria is not a dialogue agent and has no MI/counseling use case.

file: Baudisch and Mueller - 2017 - Personal fabrication.md
title: Personal Fabrication
one_line: A monograph surveying 3D-printing/digital-fabrication and arguing it will reach consumers only once hardware, embodied domain knowledge, interactive feedback, and machine knowledge mature (the six-challenge agenda).

ideas.idea: AD/DA pattern: digitize a problem, solve/store/share it in software, convert back - the intermediate digital representation, not the round-trip itself, unlocks the high-value workflows (storing, sharing, filtering).
ideas.rationale: A generic historical framing about digital-fabrication hardware; Memoria already operates entirely in the digital/text domain, so there is no analog conversion to design and nothing buildable here.

ideas.idea: Software must embody the expert domain knowledge that non-experts lack, plus interactive WYSIWYG feedback and undo, to let a non-expert achieve expert results.
ideas.rationale: True but too generic to act on - it restates the motivation behind Memoria's whole agent stack without offering a specific mechanism; Memoria's PI is already a domain expert, inverting the paper's premise.

ideas.idea: 3D printing hardware, slicing, materials, infill/metamaterials, IP/DRM for physical objects, sustainability of fabrication.
ideas.rationale: Physical-fabrication subject matter entirely outside Memoria's scope (local text knowledge system).

file: Beaudouin-Lafon - 2000 - Instrumental interaction an interaction model for designing post-WIMP user interfaces.md
title: Instrumental Interaction: An Interaction Model for Designing Post-WIMP User Interfaces
one_line: Introduces 'instrumental interaction' - users act on domain objects through interaction instruments (mediators) - and three evaluative properties (degree of indirection, integration, compatibility) for designing and comparing post-WIMP interfaces. (Note: this markdown extraction is degenerate - only OCR'd comparison tables survived, so judged on the paper's established content.)

ideas.idea: Instrument/domain-object separation: an interface is decomposed into domain objects (the data) and interaction instruments (mediators the user manipulates to act on those objects).
ideas.rationale: A clean conceptual model for Memoria's Obsidian surfaces: notes/claims are domain objects; dashboards, the approval gate, the projection engine + projected canvas, structured capture forms and the read-only inspector are instruments mediating over them. Reinforces the PI-direct-access pillar - every instrument must be reachable from the Obsidian UI to act on the underlying knowledge objects.

ideas.idea: Three design properties: degree of indirection (spatial/temporal distance between instrument action and object effect), degree of integration (input DOF vs DOF the instrument controls), degree of compatibility (similarity between user action and object response).
ideas.rationale: A concrete evaluation lattice for comparing Memoria's projection/dashboard/gate surfaces - e.g. a low-indirection, high-compatibility approval gate (approve directly on the projected note) is preferable to a high-indirection CLI step, which aligns with the rule that CLI access does not count as PI access.

ideas.idea: Reification, polymorphism and reuse of instruments across object types as design principles.
ideas.rationale: Supports building generic Memoria instruments (one inspector/gate that works over claims, relations, contradictions) rather than bespoke UI per object type - matches the disposable projection engine's goal of one reusable projection mechanism.

file: Beaudouin-Lafon and Mackay - 2002 - Prototyping tools and techniques.md
title: Prototyping Tools and Techniques
one_line: A handbook chapter characterizing prototypes along representation/precision/interactivity/evolution and distinguishing rapid, iterative and evolutionary prototypes used to explore, expand and contract a design space. (Note: this markdown extraction is degenerate - only the table of contents survived, so judged on the paper's established content.)
