ideas.idea: Prototype taxonomy by evolution: rapid/throwaway prototypes (deliberately disposable, built only to explore) versus iterative versus evolutionary prototypes that become the product.
ideas.rationale: Conceptual backing for Memoria's disposable projection engine and projected canvas - explicitly disposable artifacts that exist to let the PI explore/inspect without becoming durable vault state, distinct from the persisted notes (the 'evolutionary' product).

ideas.idea: Exploring the design space by alternately expanding (idea generation) and contracting (selecting alternatives) it.
ideas.rationale: Loosely maps to Memoria's nightly proactive discovery (expand - surface candidates) followed by triage/relevance scoring and the review gate (contract - select what gets filed); a useful framing for the discovery loop's two phases.

ideas.idea: Offline (paper, video, Wizard-of-Oz) vs online software prototyping techniques and prototyping toolkits/architectures.
ideas.rationale: UI-construction process advice for design teams; Memoria is a built single-user system, not a design-studio prototyping workflow, so the specific techniques are not buildable features.

file: Tian et al. - 2023 - Opportunities and challenges for ChatGPT and large language models in biomedicine and health.md
title: Opportunities and Challenges for ChatGPT and Large Language Models in Biomedicine and Health
one_line: A 2023 survey cataloguing LLM applications (retrieval, QA, summarization, NER/RE, education) and risks (hallucination, bias, privacy, evaluation) in biomedicine.

ideas.idea: Retrieval-augmentation (retrieve relevant docs first, then prompt LLM to answer grounded in them) as the primary mitigation for hallucination in knowledge QA.
ideas.rationale: Confirms Memoria's faithfulness-over-flash stance: claim/answer generation should be grounded in retrieved source passages, never free-form recall. Reinforces the retriever/scout profile + citation-linking already planned; nothing new to build.

ideas.idea: Augmenting LLMs with database/tool APIs (e.g. GeneGPT querying NCBI) when an entity is only resolvable via an external authority, not via the model's parametric knowledge.
ideas.rationale: Maps to Memoria's MCP-only tool sandbox: agents resolve citekeys/entities via Zotero/record-linkage tools rather than guessing. Validates the tools-not-memory pattern; it's an architecture Memoria already commits to.

ideas.idea: 'Retrieve, Summarize, and Verify' framing plus the warning that in-context summaries are NOT guaranteed error-free and must be verified.
ideas.rationale: Directly supports the structural review gate: engines produce a grounded summary/claim, but a verify step (agent judges, PI approves) is mandatory before a note is written. Concrete justification for the gate as a faithfulness control.

ideas.idea: Fine-tuned smaller encoder LMs (BioBERT-style) still beat zero/few-shot LLMs on NER and relation extraction; LLMs rephrase entities making outputs hard to verify.
ideas.rationale: Argues Memoria's deterministic-ingest entity/relation extraction should prefer constrained extraction (offsets, canonical spans) over free-text LLM generation, so spans stay verifiable for record-linkage dedup. Insight, not a component.

ideas.idea: Lack of evaluation consensus; need for PRISMA-style reporting axes and LLM-based but scalable evaluation metrics for free-text outputs (correlation with human judgement is weak for ROUGE/BLEU).
ideas.rationale: Useful caution for Memoria's evaluation/benchmark work: lexical-overlap metrics are unreliable for judging extracted claims/summaries; prefer criterion-based LLM-judge checks. Methodological note.

ideas.idea: Domain-specific pretraining/fine-tuning of biomedical LLMs (BioGPT, Med-PaLM, PMC-LLaMA), RLHF, soft-prompt tuning.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is single-researcher, local, cheap-model-preferred (qwen2.5:7b); training/fine-tuning billion-param domain models is out of scope and contradicts the constraints.

ideas.idea: Clinical chatbots, patient communication, medical education tutors, privacy/legal frameworks for patient data.
ideas.rationale: Off-domain (clinical deployment, multi-user, PHI) and irrelevant to a personal research-knowledge vault.

file: Turing - 1950 - I.—computing machinery and intelligence.md
title: Computing Machinery and Intelligence
one_line: Turing's foundational 1950 philosophy paper proposing the imitation game and rebutting objections to machine thinking, including an early sketch of a learning machine.

ideas.idea: Proposition store with typed status: facts can be tagged as 'well-established facts', 'conjectures', 'mathematically proved theorems', 'statements given by an authority', or expressions lacking belief-value; imperatives fire actions only once classified 'well-established'.
ideas.rationale: Anticipates Memoria's claim-status model: atomic claims with uncertainty flags, supersession, and an argument graph. The status-typed proposition store is conceptually the same as gating actions on claim confidence; a historical anchor, not a buildable spec.

ideas.idea: Distinguishing 'errors of functioning' (mechanism faults) from 'errors of conclusion' (a true-looking but false output).
ideas.rationale: A clean framing for Memoria's diagnostics plane vs. contradiction handling: pipeline failures (deterministic-ingest bugs) are a different class from an extracted claim being wrong. Useful conceptual separation only.

ideas.idea: The 'super-critical mind' analogy: one idea triggering a cascade of secondary/tertiary ideas, versus sub-critical minds that drop ideas.
ideas.rationale: Loosely motivates the nightly proactive discovery loop / relation-graph expansion (one note seeding related discoveries). Inspirational framing, not a mechanism.

ideas.idea: The imitation game / Turing test as a criterion for machine intelligence.
ideas.rationale: Behavioral indistinguishability is irrelevant to Memoria, which values verifiable faithfulness and provenance over human-like conversation; the test is anti-aligned with the system's goals.

ideas.idea: Child-machine plus education process, evolutionary search over machine structures, reward/punishment training.
ideas.rationale: A speculative learning-from-scratch program; Memoria uses fixed local models and learning-to-rank on PI feedback, not raising/evolving a machine. Out of scope.

file: Uddin et al. - 2026 - From recall to forgetting benchmarking long-term memory for personalized agents.md
title: From Recall to Forgetting: Benchmarking Long-Term Memory for Personalized Agents (Memora)
one_line: Introduces Memora, a long-horizon memory benchmark stressing consolidation and mutation, and FAMA, a metric that explicitly penalizes reuse of obsolete or invalidated memory.

ideas.idea: Forgetting-Aware Memory Accuracy (FAMA): score = max(0, memory-presence-accuracy − λ·(1−forgetting-absence-accuracy)), separating 'valid info correctly included' from 'invalidated info correctly excluded', with λ set per-item by the presence/forget criterion counts.
ideas.rationale: Directly buildable into Memoria's claim-supersession + contradictions dashboard: a note/answer must both include current claims AND exclude superseded ones. FAMA gives a concrete dual-criterion scoring scheme for evaluating whether the vault surfaces current vs. obsolete claims.

ideas.idea: Explicit memory trace: record full memory state before/after every update (add/update/delete) so ground truth tracks how each entity was introduced, revised, or invalidated over time.
ideas.rationale: This is essentially Memoria's claim-supersession ledger. Maintaining an explicit before/after trace per claim is the mechanism that lets the contradictions dashboard and supersession-over-time work; validates and sharpens that design.

ideas.idea: Three memory-grounded task types -- Remembering (recall), Reasoning (consolidate/aggregate across many entries), Recommending (act on current, not stale, preferences) -- as distinct capabilities that don't co-occur.
ideas.rationale: Useful taxonomy for evaluating Memoria retrieval: recall-a-claim vs. synthesize-across-claims vs. act-on-current-state are separable, and the paper shows reasoning is the weakest -- a caution that consolidation/aggregation over the vault needs dedicated handling, not just retrieval.

ideas.idea: Atomic, criterion-level binary evaluation with a heterogeneous 3-model LLM-judge majority vote (different families/providers), deterministic decoding, with retry/parse safeguards and 88% human agreement.
ideas.rationale: A robust evaluation protocol Memoria can adapt for judging extracted claims / gate decisions -- decompose into atomic yes/no criteria and vote. But multi-provider cloud judges conflict with local/cheap constraints; borrow the criterion-decomposition + majority-vote design, run it on local models.

ideas.idea: Finding: explicit-memory agents accumulate obsolete memories and their staleness gets WORSE as history grows (forgetting-penalty reduction rises weekly→quarterly); standard presence-only metrics overstate quality.
ideas.rationale: Strong argument for making forgetting/supersession a first-class engine in Memoria, not an afterthought: a growing vault will increasingly surface superseded claims unless invalidation is actively enforced. Motivates a pre-file/supersession gate that checks new claims against invalidated ones.

ideas.idea: Append-only vs. mutable operational constraints per memory type (e.g. expense logs are add-only; preferences/documents allow update+delete), preventing illegal transitions like updating a non-existent entry.
ideas.rationale: Maps to typed-relation/claim governance: some note types are immutable provenance records, others supersede. Encoding per-type allowed operations is a sound rule for Memoria's structural review gate.

ideas.idea: Simulation-driven synthetic conversation generation with a multi-agent intent/flow/prompt pipeline and an auto-eval grounding loop.
ideas.rationale: Memora's content domain is personal-assistant chat (preferences, expenses, fitness), and the synthetic-conversation generator is benchmark machinery, not applicable to ingesting real academic papers/claims in Memoria.

file: Utama et al. - 2021 - Avoiding inference heuristics in few-shot prompt-based finetuning.md
title: Avoiding Inference Heuristics in Few-shot Prompt-based Finetuning
one_line: Shows few-shot prompt-based finetuning makes models adopt lexical-overlap heuristics (worse than zero-shot), and that EWC/L2 weight-preserving regularization mitigates this destructive forgetting.

ideas.idea: Lexical-overlap heuristic: classifiers infer entailment/duplicate just because two sentences share words (e.g. 'cat chased mouse' ⊨ 'mouse chased cat'), failing on counterexamples.
ideas.rationale: Directly relevant to Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and record-linkage entity dedup: high surface/lexical overlap must NOT be treated as 'same claim' or 'duplicate'. Argues for adversarial/counter-example checks (reversed-role pairs) in the dedup and contradiction logic rather than trusting overlap.

ideas.idea: Few-shot finetuning is destructive: a model robust in zero-shot degrades on out-of-distribution challenge sets as it overfits a small, heuristic-supporting training set.
ideas.rationale: Cautions Memoria against fine-tuning the local model on small PI-feedback sets for classification (claim-sentence type, relevance); zero-shot/in-context prompting may be more robust. Favors learning-to-rank / lightweight adaptation over weight-finetuning.

ideas.idea: EWC / L2 weight-preservation regularization (RecAdam) keeping finetuned weights near pretrained values to avoid catastrophic forgetting.
ideas.rationale: Memoria does not train or finetune model weights (local cheap models used as-is via prompting/MCP). The training-time regularizer is inapplicable to the system's no-finetuning posture.

ideas.idea: Challenge/counterexample datasets (HANS, PAWS, Scramble) as diagnostics that reveal failures hidden by held-out accuracy.
ideas.rationale: Method insight for Memoria's evaluation plane: build small adversarial test sets (e.g. near-duplicate-but-different claims, reversed relations) to validate the dedup/contradiction engines, since standard accuracy conceals these failure modes.

file: Vattam et al. - Understanding complex natural systems by articulating structure-behavior- function models.md
title: Understanding Complex Natural Systems by Articulating Structure-Behavior-Function Models
one_line: Proposes Structure-Behavior-Function (SBF) as a conceptual representation/ontology for articulating how complex systems work, used to scaffold learners' causal mental models.

ideas.idea: Structure-Behavior-Function (SBF) schema: represent a system as Structure (components/connections), Function (intended role/output), and Behavior (causal mechanism linking structure to function), with behaviors as state-transition chains carrying causal annotations.
ideas.rationale: A concrete typed-relation/aspect template Memoria could adapt for representing a paper's method or mechanism: map MASSW-style aspects (problem/mechanism/result) onto function/behavior/structure, giving the argument graph causal-link types rather than flat 'relates-to' edges.

ideas.idea: Behaviors decomposed into stateful transitions annotated with causal explanations (by-behavior, using-function, as-per-pointers), making the 'why/how' explicit and traceable.
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's warrant-bearing argument graph: an edge between claims should carry an explicit causal/justification annotation (a warrant), not just a relation label. SBF's transition-with-causal-annotation is a model for typed warrants.

ideas.idea: Articulating a model forces explicit externalization of an otherwise tacit mental model, surfacing gaps and misconceptions for review.
ideas.rationale: Aligns with Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' gate and projected canvas: externalizing the structure/behavior of an argument into an inspectable artifact is exactly what the projection engine + structural review enable. Conceptual support.

ideas.idea: SBF as a learner-facing pedagogical/cognitive-support tool with conceptual-change goals (education context).
ideas.rationale: Memoria serves a single expert researcher, not learners undergoing conceptual change; the pedagogical framing and classroom evaluation are off-domain.

ideas.idea: Domain-specific (aquarium/biological ecosystem) SBF ontology and authored model libraries.
ideas.rationale: The specific biological-systems ontology is not Memoria's domain (academic-paper claims/relations); only the abstract S/B/F decomposition transfers, not the concrete library.

file: Chen et al. - 2021 - Revisiting self-training for few-shot learning of language model.md
title: Revisiting Self-Training for Few-Shot Learning of Language Model (SFLM)
one_line: SFLM is a prompt-based few-shot text-classification learner that adds a self-training loss (pseudo-label the weakly-augmented view, predict it from the strongly-augmented view) plus an MLM auxiliary loss, beating PET/LM-BFF with only a handful of in-domain unlabeled examples.

ideas.idea: Consistency-regularized self-training (FixMatch transferred to text): pseudo-label a weak augmentation, train the strong augmentation to match it, gated by a confidence threshold tau.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's claim/aspect classifiers run on a single researcher's small labeled set; the confidence-threshold-gated pseudo-labeling is a cheap way to exploit the PI's large pool of unlabeled vault claims to sharpen a local qwen2.5-based classifier without new annotation. Borrow the threshold-gated pseudo-labeling discipline, not the full training loop.

ideas.idea: Dropout as weak augmentation, 15% token masking as strong augmentation; masking beats crop/delete/swap because it preserves semantics.
ideas.rationale: This is fine-tuning-time augmentation tuning for RoBERTa-base. Memoria prefers deterministic ingest with off-the-shelf local models and does not fine-tune its own encoders; the augmentation-recipe detail is not actionable.

ideas.idea: Gains concentrate near gold-label neighborhoods in embedding space (SimCSE binning analysis); self-training helps most on examples similar to labeled data.
ideas.rationale: Useful design caution: when Memoria auto-labels claims via pseudo-labeling, trust is highest for items near already-approved exemplars. This argues for a similarity-to-approved gate before accepting a machine label, which dovetails with the existing pre-file similarity gate.

ideas.idea: Self-training requires a reasonably strong backbone (DistilRoBERTa degrades sharply); weak teachers inject noise from extra unlabeled data.
ideas.rationale: Tempers any plan to lean on the smallest local model for self-improving classifiers; aligns with the test-env decision to keep qwen2.5:7b as the floor rather than going smaller.

file: Chen et al. - 2022 - AdaPrompt adaptive model training for prompt-based NLP.md
title: AdaPrompt: Adaptive Model Training for Prompt-based NLP
one_line: AdaPrompt bridges the LM-pretraining vs. task gap by using prompt-aware queries (input + prompt + PLM-predicted label words) to retrieve task-relevant text via ElasticSearch for continual pretraining, and augments verbalizers automatically using an NLI entailment model.

ideas.idea: Prompt-aware retrieval: build the retrieval query from the input PLUS the filled prompt PLUS the model's top predicted label words, so retrieved text reflects both task and prompt characteristics.
ideas.rationale: Maps onto Memoria's proactive discovery / scout retrieval: enriching a query with the note's prompt-context and the model's own candidate labels (e.g., candidate aspect/claim-type words) could improve discovery relevance scoring. Borrow the query-enrichment idea; reject the continual-pretraining endpoint.

ideas.idea: NLI-driven automatic verbalizer augmentation: accept a candidate label word c only if 'This X is c' entails 'This X is <seed>' under an off-the-shelf MNLI model.
ideas.rationale: Memoria can reuse an entailment model to vet whether two candidate tag/label phrases are mutually entailing before merging them, supporting keyphrase tag-candidate dedup and verbalizer-free label expansion with a local model. Concrete and self-contained.

ideas.idea: Continual pretraining (DAPT/TAPT) of the PLM on retrieved data to adapt to domain+prompt.
ideas.rationale: Memoria does not own/train its PLM and favors deterministic local inference; continual pretraining a RoBERTa-large is off-constraint (cost, GPU, non-determinism) for a single-researcher vault.

ideas.idea: Retrieval size is a noise/relevance tradeoff: accuracy rises then falls as top-k grows (lower-ranked retrieved data adds noise).
ideas.rationale: Direct support for capping fan-out in the discovery loop and the retriever profile: more retrieved candidates is not strictly better; tune k as a relevance/noise knob.

file: Chen et al. - 2022 - Good visual guidance makes a better extractor hierarchical visual prefix for multimodal entity and.md
title: Good Visual Guidance Makes A Better Extractor: Hierarchical Visual Prefix for Multimodal Entity and Relation Extraction (HVPNeT)
one_line: HVPNeT injects object-level image features as a pluggable 'visual prefix' into each BERT self-attention layer, with a dynamic gate aggregating multi-scale ResNet pyramid features, to make social-media NER/RE robust to irrelevant images.

ideas.idea: Treat auxiliary signal as a pluggable prefix prepended to key/value at each attention layer, so irrelevant auxiliary input is softly down-weighted rather than corrupting the text path (error insensitivity).
ideas.rationale: The mechanism is architectural fusion inside a fine-tuned BERT; Memoria is text-only over academic papers, does not fine-tune encoders, and ingests no images. The robustness-to-irrelevant-side-info framing is interesting but not buildable in Memoria's MCP-only, deterministic-ingest setup.

ideas.idea: Dynamic per-layer gate that routes low-level visual features to low BERT layers and high-level to high layers (multi-scale matching).
ideas.rationale: Pure vision-language modeling detail; no analogue in a text-only single-researcher knowledge system.

ideas.idea: Robustness analysis by partitioning examples into relevant / weak-relevant / irrelevant image-text pairs to show the method only helps when the side signal is relevant.
ideas.rationale: Evaluation methodology tied to multimodal relevance; Memoria's relevance gating is already handled by the pre-file similarity gate and discovery scoring on text. Nothing transferable beyond generic 'gate irrelevant signals,' which Memoria already does.

file: Chen et al. - 2022 - KnowPrompt knowledge-aware prompt-tuning with synergistic optimization for relation extraction.md
title: KnowPrompt: Knowledge-aware Prompt-tuning with Synergistic Optimization for Relation Extraction
one_line: KnowPrompt does relation extraction by injecting learnable 'virtual type words' (around entities, seeded from the prior distribution of entity types implied by each relation) and 'virtual answer words' (one per relation, seeded by decomposing the relation label), then co-optimizes them with a triple-based structural constraint loss.

ideas.idea: Type-prior from relation labels: each relation implies a prior distribution over subject/object entity types (per:country_of_birth => subj person, obj country), estimated by frequency, and the relation reciprocally constrains entity types.
ideas.rationale: Directly useful for Memoria's typed-relation extraction and record-linkage entity dedup: a relation-type-to-argument-type prior lets the structural review gate validate or reject candidate relations (a relation whose argument types violate the prior is a likely extraction error / contradiction). Buildable as a deterministic typed-relation schema with type constraints, no training needed.

ideas.idea: Decompose a structured relation label into its constituent words to seed a semantic representation of the relation (city_of_headquarters => {city, of, headquarters}).
ideas.rationale: Cheap, deterministic way to give Memoria's typed-relation vocabulary semantic grounding (e.g., for relation-similarity, merging near-duplicate relation types, or prompting a local model with relation gloss). No fine-tuning required to reuse the decomposition heuristic.

ideas.idea: Implicit structural-constraint loss treating (subject-type, relation, object-type) as a KG triple with negative sampling (corrupt to an impossible type) to enforce consistency among prompt embeddings.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's argument graph / contradictions dashboard can use the triple-consistency idea as a validation rule rather than a loss: flag triples whose subject/object types are 'impossible' for the asserted relation. The training mechanism is rejected; the consistency-check semantics are borrowed.

ideas.idea: Virtual answer words as learnable, single-token, model-agnostic verbalizers that behave like prototype representations for relation labels (shown via t-SNE to sit near 'founder/chair/ceo' for founded_by).
ideas.rationale: Requires expanding the MLM head with learnable embeddings and gradient tuning; contradicts Memoria's no-encoder-training, local-inference constraint. The prototype intuition is already covered by embedding-based relation clustering Memoria can do without tuning.

ideas.idea: Big low-resource gains (up to +22 F1 at 8-shot) from knowledge-seeded initialization over random init.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces a Memoria design principle: seed extractors/classifiers with structured priors (relation-type tables, schema) rather than cold starts, since the PI provides very few labels. Methodological takeaway, not a component.

file: Chen et al. - 2022 - LightNER a lightweight tuning paradigm for low-resource NER via pluggable prompting.md
title: LightNER: A Lightweight Tuning Paradigm for Low-resource NER via Pluggable Prompting
one_line: LightNER recasts NER as seq2seq generation with a unified learnable verbalizer (handles new/unseen entity classes without new classifier heads) plus a pluggable, parameter-efficient guidance module inserted into frozen BERT/BART attention, enabling cross-domain/class transfer from a few examples.

ideas.idea: Unified learnable verbalizer that maps each entity category to the set of words composing its (possibly long/complex) label name (return_date.month_name => {return, date, month, name}), weighted-averaged, so new categories need no new classifier head ('class transfer').
ideas.rationale: Memoria's claim/aspect/entity-type taxonomy evolves as the PI adds new note/relation types; a label-name-decomposition verbalizer lets extractors absorb new types without retraining a head. The decomposition-and-average scheme is deterministic and reusable on a local generative model — fits 'engines write' extraction over a growing schema.

ideas.idea: Pluggable, swappable guidance module (small key/value matrices in attention) trained per domain while the LM stays frozen; modules can be loaded/averaged at inference for cross-domain and even cross-task (NER<->POS) zero-shot transfer.
ideas.rationale: Conceptually mirrors Memoria's compiled-profile / skill-governance idea: small per-task swappable artifacts over a frozen base. Memoria can borrow the 'tiny swappable adapters loaded at inference' framing for its retriever/scout vs. judge profiles, though it would realize this via prompts/profiles rather than trained prefix matrices (no encoder training in the sandbox).

ideas.idea: Averaging two source-domain guidance modules yields a usable mixed module for a third domain (parameter interpolation for zero-shot).
ideas.rationale: Weight-space interpolation of trained adapters is off-constraint for Memoria (no trained adapters, no GPU training loop in a single-researcher MCP sandbox). The result is a nice curiosity but not buildable here.

ideas.idea: Generative pointer-network framing that emits (start_idx, end_idx, type) tuples for entities, decoupling span detection from classification.
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria choosing a generation-based extraction format (a local model emitting span+type tuples) over rigid classifier heads, which is more natural with qwen2.5 and adapts to schema growth. Borrow the output-format design, not the trained pointer net.

ideas.idea: Guidance applied to higher LM layers suffices (high-low vs low-high ablation), since high layers carry task semantics.
ideas.rationale: Layer-placement tuning is meaningful only when you control/train the encoder internals, which Memoria does not.

file: Qi et al. - 2023 - Large language models are zero shot hypothesis proposers.md
title: Large Language Models are Zero Shot Hypothesis Proposers
one_line: Constructs a date-split biomedical background-knowledge/hypothesis dataset, shows LLMs can generate validated novel hypotheses zero-shot, evaluates them on four metrics (novelty/relevance/significance/verifiability) via ChatGPT and humans, and adds a role-based multi-agent framework.

ideas.idea: Four-axis hypothesis quality rubric: novelty, relevance, significance, verifiability, scored 0-3 with a step-by-step justification per score.
ideas.rationale: Directly reusable as the scoring rubric for Memoria's structural review gate on any agent-proposed claim/hypothesis/idea-gap note; the per-score justification fits the 'agents judge, PI approves' model and gives the PI an auditable rationale. Cheap local LLM can apply it.

ideas.idea: Temporal publication-date dataset split to guarantee strict test-set invisibility (train before a cutoff, evaluate on papers after).
ideas.rationale: Memoria is single-researcher, not training models, but the principle maps onto claim supersession and discovery evaluation: use Zotero publication dates as a hard temporal boundary so a 'novelty' or 'already-known' judgment is anchored to what existed at the time, avoiding leakage when validating the discovery loop.

ideas.idea: ChatGPT-as-annotator correlates strongly (Pearson/Spearman > 0.7) with human scores on hypothesis quality.
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's LLM-judge gate, but the correlation was measured with a frontier API model; with qwen2.5:7b the agreement is unverified, so treat the rubric as decision-support surfaced to the PI, not an autonomous accept/reject.

ideas.idea: Role-specialized multi-agent loop (Analyst extracts keywords -> Engineer searches PubMed -> Scientist formulates -> Critic evaluates and loops back).
ideas.rationale: The Analyst/Scientist/Critic division mirrors Memoria's engines-write/agents-judge separation and the discovery+critique loop; adopt the role decomposition pattern but keep all roles MCP-only sandboxed with no tool execution beyond approved retrievers.

ideas.idea: 'Increasing uncertainty facilitates candidate generation' — few-shot examples and tool use REDUCED novelty by collapsing the model's exploration space.
ideas.rationale: Useful calibration insight for Memoria's idea-generation/discovery scoring: over-grounding with retrieved exemplars suppresses novel connections, so the discovery relevance scorer should not over-weight similarity to existing notes when the goal is gap-finding.

ideas.idea: Domain fine-tuning (PMC-LLaMA/MedAlpaca) and tool-use (ReAct/function calling) gave minimal hypothesis-quality gains over zero-shot.
ideas.rationale: Negative result about model training/tooling that Memoria isn't doing; no buildable mechanism, only reinforces 'don't over-engineer the generator'.

file: Qiao et al. - 2023 - Reasoning with language model prompting a survey.md
title: Reasoning with Language Model Prompting: A Survey
one_line: A taxonomy survey of LLM reasoning-via-prompting methods, splitting work into strategy-enhanced reasoning (prompt engineering, process optimization, external engines) and knowledge-enhanced reasoning (implicit vs explicit/retrieved knowledge).

ideas.idea: Self-consistency / ensemble-optimization: sample multiple reasoning chains and take a majority/step-aware-voted answer to improve reliability.
ideas.rationale: Applicable to Memoria's extraction engines: for high-stakes atomic-claim or contradiction judgments, sample several local-model reasoning passes and flag disagreement as an uncertainty signal for the review gate, rather than trusting a single decode.

ideas.idea: Explicit-knowledge / retrieval-augmented CoT (IRCoT, RR): interleave retrieval with reasoning steps to ground each step and reduce hallucination.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces grounding every extracted claim/relation in the source passage; the per-step retrieval pattern matches Memoria's faithfulness-over-flash constraint, though Memoria's retrieval is over its own vault not open web.

ideas.idea: Decompose-then-reason (least-to-most, self-ask, multi-stage prompting) for complex tasks.
ideas.rationale: Worth applying to deterministic ingest: break aspect/claim extraction into staged sub-prompts (classify sentence type -> extract claim -> flag uncertainty) which is more robust on a small 7B model than one mega-prompt.

ideas.idea: CoT prompting is an emergent ability that only helps at >~100B scale; below that it can hurt; small LMs benefit instead from fine-tuning on rationales / distillation.
ideas.rationale: Cautionary but the prescribed remedies (distillation, scaling) don't fit Memoria's fixed local 7B/14B setup; the actionable takeaway (don't assume CoT helps small models) is a constraint, not a feature to build.

ideas.idea: Code-interpreter / PAL / PoT: offload computation to executable programs for robustness.
ideas.rationale: Contradicts Memoria's MCP-only sandbox where agents get no code execution ever; not buildable under the security model.

file: Qiao et al. - 2026 - SciAtlas a large-scale knowledge graph for automated scientific research.md
title: SciAtlas: A Large-Scale Knowledge Graph for Automated Scientific Research
one_line: A 43M-paper / 157M-entity / 3B-triple heterogeneous scholarly knowledge graph (built from OpenAlex, Neo4j) with a neuro-symbolic tri-path retrieval algorithm (keyword + vector + title matching seeds, random-walk-with-restart graph propagation, citation-aware reranking) and downstream apps for review, idea grounding, trend prediction.

ideas.idea: Reusable-keyword extraction: instruct an LLM to extract 3-8 keywords that are reusable across papers, explicitly avoiding paper-specific system names, marketing/narrative phrasing, and bespoke architecture names; attach an importance score per keyword.
ideas.rationale: Exactly the discipline Memoria's keyphrase tag-candidate engine needs: 'good vs bad keyword' examples and the anti-marketing instruction prevent tag sprawl from over-specific note titles; importance score feeds tag-candidate ranking at the review gate. Runs on the local qwen extractor.

ideas.idea: Tri-path collaborative recall: lexical exact-match + vector similarity + title fuzzy-match (LCS + Jaccard token overlap, threshold 0.88) as parallel seed-node finders, then merge with max-score dedup.
ideas.rationale: A concrete, deterministic hybrid retrieval recipe for Memoria's retriever/scout and pre-file similarity gate; combining exact + embedding + title-fuzzy raises recall while the thresholds keep it tunable. Scales fine at single-researcher vault size.

ideas.idea: Random-walk-with-restart over a typed note/claim graph with per-edge-type weights (cites, related, authored, coauthor, co-occur) and restart probability, surfacing graph-discovered nodes not in the initial seed set.
ideas.rationale: Memoria already has typed relations and an argument graph; RWR is a deterministic, cheap (no LLM) way to do topological discovery over the PI's vault, finding indirectly-related notes the proactive nightly loop would miss with pure vector search. Edge-type weights map onto Memoria's typed-relation vocabulary.

ideas.idea: Graph-support gating: a node with zero initial semantic relevance must show exceptionally strong topological support to outrank primary candidates; final score = weighted blend of pre-graph relevance (0.35), graph support (0.45), citation importance (0.20) + title bonus.
ideas.rationale: The gating idea prevents graph diffusion from over-promoting distant junk — directly useful for Memoria's discovery relevance scoring and learning-to-rank triage. The specific weights/citation-importance term need adapting (a personal vault has no meaningful global citation counts).

ideas.idea: Path-based explanations + per-component score breakdown returned with every retrieval result as a transparent 'cognitive map'.
ideas.rationale: Fits Memoria's read-only inspector and faithfulness mandate: surfacing WHY a note was retrieved (which path/edges) lets the PI audit the triage and trust the proactive loop. Buildable as part of the projection/inspector surfaces.

ideas.idea: Idea grounding/evaluation pipeline: use an idea/paper as query, retrieve relevant papers, segment into paragraphs, extract refined claims across motivation/method/experiment, then LLM-compare to find similar vs differentiating points and assess novelty/feasibility/soundness.
ideas.rationale: Maps almost one-to-one onto Memoria's contradictions dashboard + claim supersession + similarity gate: the motivation/method/experiment claim decomposition and similar/different-point output is a ready template for grounding a new note's claims against existing vault claims at the review gate.

ideas.idea: Incremental KG update via OpenAlex daily API / bi-monthly changefiles, with GROBID as a lightweight PDF metadata/reference fallback for missing papers.
ideas.rationale: GROBID is a concrete, local, deterministic tool that fits Memoria's deterministic-ingest pillar for parsing references/metadata from PDFs the Zotero/OpenAlex path misses; the daily-changefile cadence informs the nightly discovery loop's update strategy.

ideas.idea: Massive 43M-paper Neo4j graph as a shared cognitive substrate for AI agents across 26 disciplines.
ideas.rationale: The scale and global-corpus framing is cloud/enterprise; Memoria is single-researcher over a personal vault. The retrieval algorithm transfers, but building/hosting the full multi-disciplinary KG does not.

ideas.idea: Plan to expose KG via CLI and distilled agentic 'skills' for one-click agent loading.
ideas.rationale: CLI access doesn't count as PI-accessible under Memoria's direct-Obsidian-UI rule; the skill-governance model is already covered by ADRs and this adds nothing buildable.

file: Qin and Eisner - 2021 - Learning how to ask querying LMs with mixtures of soft prompts.md
title: Learning How to Ask: Querying LMs with Mixtures of Soft Prompts
one_line: Learns continuous 'soft prompts' (and a tuned mixture/ensemble of them) by gradient descent to extract relational facts from frozen masked LMs, dramatically beating manual and hard-prompt baselines on T-REx/Google-RE/ConceptNet.

ideas.idea: Frame relational knowledge extraction as cloze fill-in-the-blank prompting per relation type (x born in ___) to elicit facts from an LM.
ideas.rationale: The relation-as-cloze framing is a lightweight pattern for Memoria's typed-relation / entity-attribute extraction over note text, but Memoria extracts from the PI's own documents (grounded) rather than querying the LM as a knowledge base, which the paper itself warns is unreliable.

ideas.idea: Soft prompts: tune continuous prompt vectors (and deep per-layer perturbations) instead of discrete words, requiring backprop into the model.
ideas.rationale: Requires gradient access and per-relation training against a tunable model; incompatible with Memoria's frozen local Ollama models and deterministic, training-free ingest. Not buildable in the MCP-only, no-training setup.

ideas.idea: Mixture-of-prompts ensembling: learn multiple lexical-variant prompts and weighted-combine their predictions for better coverage of how a relation is phrased.
ideas.rationale: The insight that one relation surfaces via many phrasings is useful: Memoria's extraction prompts can include several hand-written paraphrase templates and union their hits to raise recall — without any learned weights, just prompt diversity.

ideas.idea: Treating LMs as approximate knowledge bases whose factual recall is 'underestimated' and elicitable by better prompting.
ideas.rationale: Contradicts Memoria's faithfulness/grounding pillar — Memoria must never treat the LM's parametric memory as a fact source; claims must trace to the ingested document.

file: Qin et al. - 2022 - Exploring universal intrinsic task subspace via prompt tuning.md
title: Exploring Universal Intrinsic Task Subspace via Prompt Tuning
one_line: Empirically shows PLM adaptations to ~100 diverse NLP tasks can be reparameterized into a single ~250-dimensional 'intrinsic task subspace' (via an auto-encoder over soft prompts), recovering most full-prompt-tuning performance by tuning only a few hundred free parameters.

ideas.idea: Intrinsic-vectors as low-dimensional task representations that cluster by task type (classification vs generation) and whose proximity reflects task similarity (PCA-visualizable).
ideas.rationale: The conceptual framing — that diverse tasks live near each other in a low-dim space — loosely supports embedding-based task/note clustering, but Memoria already gets this from BERTopic clustering and embeddings; this paper adds an interpretation, not a new mechanism.

ideas.idea: Intrinsic prompt tuning (auto-encoder over soft prompts; tune only ~250 parameters in a learned subspace) as a parameter-efficient adaptation/analysis method.
ideas.rationale: Requires training auto-encoders and soft-prompt tuning against the model — no training happens in Memoria's frozen-local-model, deterministic-ingest design. Off-architecture.

ideas.idea: Cross-task generalization improves with more/diverse training tasks; subspaces biased toward seen task types generalize poorly to unseen types.
ideas.rationale: An ML-training observation about multi-task subspaces; no actionable component for a single-researcher knowledge system that does not train task adapters.

ideas.idea: Prompt tuning is high-variance/unstable across runs; constraining to a low-dim subspace (or using it as PT initialization) sharply lowers std.
ideas.rationale: Stability problem is specific to soft-prompt training, which Memoria does not do; the determinism Memoria needs comes from temperature/seed control on inference, not subspace constraints.

file: Bryant et al. - 2005 - Becoming wikipedian transformation of participation in a collaborative online encyclopedia.md
title: Becoming Wikipedian: Transformation of Participation in a Collaborative Online Encyclopedia
one_line: A qualitative interview study using activity theory and legitimate peripheral participation to describe how Wikipedia contributors evolve from casual readers/editors into community caretakers, surfacing the role of watchlists, talk pages, and surveillance tools.

ideas.idea: Watchlists as personal surveillance/caretaker tooling: members 'watch' a bounded set of articles and review only diffs since last check, rather than sifting the whole corpus.
ideas.rationale: The PI is overwhelmed by a large vault; a per-note 'watch list' surfacing recent changes/diffs (new contradictions, superseding claims, relations added to watched claims/notes) is a directly adaptable single-user dashboard pattern for Memoria's gates/spaces, even though the social-vandalism motivation does not apply.

ideas.idea: Tool-awareness scales with the user's zone of proximal development: the same interface exposes more relevant features as the user's goals deepen (novice sees search/edit; expert uses history, talk, watchlist).
ideas.rationale: Supports progressive disclosure in Memoria's Obsidian surfaces — keep the PI's everyday capture simple and reveal argument-graph/contradiction/supersession machinery as engagement deepens, rather than exposing all governance machinery up front.

ideas.idea: Diversity/rigor of edits (number of editors, number of edits) as a proxy quality signal for an article.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is single-researcher; there is no crowd of editors to derive diversity from, so this multi-author quality heuristic does not transfer. Quality must come from claim faithfulness and the structural review gate instead.

ideas.idea: Separating conflict from content: disputes are negotiated on talk/discussion pages distinct from the article, with consensus and neutral-point-of-view as governing principles.
ideas.rationale: Memoria has no multi-party disputes to mediate; contradiction handling is a structured dashboard over claims, not social consensus-building. The mechanism is built for community governance Memoria explicitly is not.

ideas.idea: Featured-article peer review and identity/userpage reputation mechanics as incentives.
ideas.rationale: Social reputation, public recognition, and crowd incentives have no analog for a single PI; off-model for Memoria's faithfulness-over-flash, single-user constraints.

file: Buchmann and Gurevych - 2026 - Citation failure definition, analysis and efficient mitigation.md
title: Citation Failure: Definition, Analysis and Efficient Mitigation
one_line: Defines and disentangles 'citation failure' (valid response, incomplete evidence) from 'response failure', introduces the CITECONTROL benchmark that controls reasoning-type and overtness of the response-evidence relation, and shows that combining generative, attention-based, and retrieval-based citation (CITENTION) efficiently improves evidence attribution.

ideas.idea: Disentangle citation failure from response failure: evaluate citation quality only on instances where the answer is actually correct (filtered recall@k).
ideas.rationale: Directly applicable to Memoria's claim-extraction/provenance QA: when scoring whether an extracted atomic claim is correctly grounded to its source sentence/paper, separate 'the claim is wrong' from 'the claim is right but the cited span is incomplete'. This sharpens the structural review gate's faithfulness checks instead of conflating two error types.

ideas.idea: Combine generative + attention-based + retrieval-based citation scores via a cheap learned linear aggregation (ensemble), since methods are complementary across relation types.
ideas.rationale: Memoria runs local cheap models (qwen2.5:7b) and already has retrievers/BM25-style components; a lightweight score-combination layer over generation-based grounding plus a BM25/dense retriever for evidence linking fits the local/cheap/deterministic constraints and improves provenance recall without finetuning. Attention extraction is harder under Ollama/Flash-Attention, so the retrieval+generation portion is the realistic adopt.

ideas.idea: Reasoning-type (single/multi-hop/intersection) and overtness (implicit vs explicit) as the axes that govern when grounding fails; models under-generate citations and miss implicit, upstream-in-the-chain evidence.
ideas.rationale: Useful diagnostic vocabulary for Memoria's relation/argument graph: multi-hop warrants and implicitly-supporting claims are exactly the evidence the system will most often fail to link. The taxonomy guides where to flag low-confidence provenance for PI review.

ideas.idea: LLM-based attribution evaluators drop to ~50% accuracy on complex cases, so prefer benchmarks with verifiable answers and known ground-truth evidence over LLM judges.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' caution: do not trust an LLM-judge alone to certify claim-to-source grounding; use deterministic checks (exact-span/ROUGE overlap, retrieval recall) where possible and route uncertain cases to the gate.

ideas.idea: Retrieval-based citation gets a strong signal from the question, while generative citation is biased toward verbatim/explicit evidence; queries combining question + answer help find implicit evidence.
ideas.rationale: For Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and discovery retriever, querying with both the claim text and its surrounding context (not just the claim) should improve linkage to implicitly-related prior notes/claims.

ideas.idea: Masking chain-of-thought reasoning tokens improves attention-based attribution.
ideas.rationale: Attention-internals extraction requires an extra forward pass and model-internal access that Memoria's Ollama-served local models do not cleanly expose; the gain is architecture-sensitive (works for Llama, weaker for Qwen, Memoria's default family). Not worth the engineering for a single-user system.

file: Buchner et al. - 2022 - The impact of augmented reality on cognitive load and performance a systematic review.md
title: The Impact of Augmented Reality on Cognitive Load and Performance: A Systematic Review
one_line: A PRISMA systematic review of 58 studies on whether augmented reality raises or lowers cognitive load in learning/training, concluding AR is generally less demanding and higher-performing than traditional media but that media-comparison designs are weak and value-added designs are needed.

ideas.idea: Reduce extraneous cognitive load by integrating related information in spatial/temporal proximity (split-attention effect, contiguity, signalling principle).
ideas.rationale: Translatable to Memoria's Obsidian surfaces: co-locate a claim with its source span, warrants, and contradicting claims (projected canvas / inspector) rather than forcing the PI to shift between separate notes/dashboards. The principle of cueing attention to the most relevant element supports the design of gates and dashboards even though AR hardware is irrelevant.

ideas.idea: Value-added experimental designs (vary one feature of the same tool) are methodologically superior to media-comparison ('is tool X better than Y') for learning which design choices help.
ideas.rationale: Methodological hygiene for Memoria's own evaluation: when judging whether a new engine feature (e.g., a keyphrase tag candidate generator or BERTopic clustering view) actually helps the PI, A/B a single feature against the same pipeline rather than comparing wholesale tool swaps.

ideas.idea: Systematic-review/PRISMA workflow itself: rigorous inclusion/exclusion criteria, dual-rater screening with agreement reconciliation, structured coding of each study.
ideas.rationale: Mirrors Memoria's ingest+structural-review-gate ethos: deterministic inclusion criteria plus 'two engines extract, agents reconcile disagreement, PI approves' is exactly a PRISMA-style dual-screening pattern applied to claim extraction; reinforces (does not newly invent) the design.

ideas.idea: AR hardware specifics (see-through glasses, spatial AR, trigger images, HMDs) and their cognitive-load tradeoffs.
ideas.rationale: Off-domain: Memoria is a local markdown/agent knowledge system with no AR/wearable surface; the hardware findings have no buildable analog.

ideas.idea: Collaborative cognitive load theory: distributing working-memory load across multiple co-located learners.
ideas.rationale: Single-researcher system; there is no collaborating group to distribute cognitive load across, so the collective-working-memory mechanism does not apply.

file: Bursztyn et al. - 2022 - Learning to perform complex tasks through compositional fine-tuning of language models.md
title: Learning to Perform Complex Tasks through Compositional Fine-Tuning of Language Models
one_line: Proposes compositional fine-tuning (CFT): decompose a target task into a curriculum of dependency-ordered component tasks (facts -> comparisons -> decisions) and fine-tune a small LM on them, matching 175B chain-of-thought with a model 7.4% of the size and working in domains absent from pretraining.

ideas.idea: Decompose a complex task into explicit, dependency-ordered component sub-tasks (Decompose/Demonstrate/Fine-Tune) so a SMALL local model can match a giant model's chain-of-thought.
ideas.rationale: Strongly aligned with Memoria's local/cheap-model constraint (qwen2.5:7b): instead of one mega-prompt asking a 7B model to extract atomic claims + uncertainty flags + MASSW aspects + classification at once, run an explicit pipeline of small component steps (fact-statement -> claim-sentence classification -> aspect mapping). This is the engine-decomposition pattern Memoria already favors and CFT gives evidence it raises quality at fixed model size.

ideas.idea: Component-task curriculum beats end-to-end even with equal training data (the gain is from compositional structure, not more data); tiny auxiliary tasks (12 negative-preference-interpretation examples) yield outsized accuracy gains.
ideas.rationale: If Memoria ever fine-tunes/LoRA-adapts a local model for claim or relation extraction, structuring a small curriculum of component tasks (and adding tiny targeted corrective sub-tasks for known failure modes like negation/hedging) is a cheap, data-efficient lever suited to single-PI scale.

ideas.idea: Prefer fine-tuned small models over prompt-only giant models when the domain is proprietary/absent from pretraining and per-call cost matters (CFT ~50x cheaper than DaVinci CoT).
ideas.rationale: Endorses Memoria's local-first stance: the PI's vault is private/niche and out-of-distribution for big hosted models, exactly the regime where the paper shows local decomposed fine-tuning wins on cost and coverage.

ideas.idea: CFT-built models are themselves composable: an upstream agent splits a hard case (|P|>1, |I|>2) into base cases and recombines outputs.
ideas.rationale: Matches Memoria's MCP-only agent sandbox model where agents orchestrate deterministic engines: an agent can fan a multi-claim paragraph into single-claim extraction calls and recombine, keeping each engine call simple and verifiable for the gate.

ideas.idea: Manual task decomposition (the Decompose step is hand-authored, not automated).
ideas.rationale: Acceptable as a research limitation but not a feature to adopt verbatim; Memoria's pipelines are authored deterministically anyway, and the recommendation/decision-template task domain itself (travel/dining recsys) is off-domain for a research-knowledge system.

file: Bødker - 2006 - When second wave HCI meets third wave challenges.md
title: When Second Wave HCI Meets Third Wave Challenges
one_line: A NordiCHI keynote arguing second-wave HCI theory (activity theory, distributed cognition, situated action) still has unfinished contributions for third-wave challenges, focusing on multiplicity of mediators, context across settings, boundaries, experience/reflexivity, and participation.

ideas.idea: Webs/configurations of mediators: tools are never monolithic; users assemble, chain, juxtapose, and substitute multiple artifacts, and good design must let a mediator be isolated, analyzed, and re-inserted into the configuration rather than replacing the whole.
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's modular, disposable-projection design: the Obsidian vault plus Hermes plus dashboards/gates/spaces is a 'web of mediators', and Bodker's argument validates keeping projections disposable and engines swappable (insert/remove a component) rather than building one monolithic app. Conceptual reinforcement, not a buildable feature.

ideas.idea: Document-first (STAR) vs application-first interaction: in Xerox STAR the document came first and functionality attached to it, preserving spatial/historical structure users impose (e.g., tax officers' functional desk regions).
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's note/claim-centric (document-first) model in Obsidian: the markdown note and its claims are primary and engines/relations attach to them, rather than tools owning the data. Endorses the existing architecture; the spatial-memory insight also motivates the projected canvas as a place where the PI imposes their own structure.

ideas.idea: Reflexivity and the breakdown-as-learning principle: ubiquity should not make computation vanish entirely; breakdowns that make a hidden mediator reappear are the foundation for the user's understanding and control.
ideas.rationale: Argues against fully-invisible automation in Memoria — the structural review gate and read-only inspector deliberately surface what engines did so the PI retains understanding and control. This paper gives theoretical grounding for 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' over silent autonomy.

ideas.idea: Tailorability through cooperation among users (not isolated end-user programming), and participatory design bringing the user's 'whole life' into design.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is explicitly single-user with no cooperating community to tailor across; the cooperative/participatory-design and consumer co-determination agenda has no multi-actor substrate here.

ideas.idea: Boundary objects / contextual units as self-contained artifacts that travel across contexts of use.
ideas.rationale: The paper itself judges boundary objects over-exploited and underdeveloped; for a single PI's local vault there is no cross-organization context-crossing to design for, so the construct adds no actionable Memoria mechanism.

file: Li et al. - 2023 - CAMEL communicative agents for mind exploration of large language model society.md
title: CAMEL: Communicative Agents for "Mind" Exploration of Large Language Model Society
one_line: A role-playing multi-agent framework where a task-specifier turns a vague idea into a concrete task and two LLM agents (AI user / AI assistant) cooperate autonomously via 'inception prompting' to solve tasks and generate instruction-following datasets.

ideas.idea: Task-specifier agent that turns a vague human 'idea' into a concrete, bounded task before any work begins.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's structural review gate and projects benefit from a step that converts a fuzzy PI prompt (e.g. 'explore claim contradictions in X') into a precisely scoped task spec before engines run; cheap, deterministic-ish, and reduces wasted local-model cycles.

ideas.idea: Inception prompting with hard role constraints (never flip roles, never instruct me, decline impossible instructions, fixed output format, explicit end-of-task token).
ideas.rationale: The concrete prompt-engineering tricks (forbid role-flip, force a structured Solution: format, require an explicit DONE token, mandate honest decline) directly map to making Memoria's MCP-only judge/scout profiles behave deterministically and terminate; useful for compiled profiles even though Memoria does not use two chatty peer agents.

ideas.idea: Two cooperating peer agents (user+assistant) autonomously chatting in a loop to drive task completion.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's governance is 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' with a single PI; open-ended autonomous agent-to-agent dialogue is the opposite of the deterministic, gated, single-researcher pipeline and risks the infinite-loop/flake-reply failure modes the paper itself documents.

ideas.idea: Explicit catalogue of multi-agent failure modes (role flipping, instruction repetition, flake replies, infinite loops) plus termination conditions (no-instruct rounds, role-reversal detection, message/token caps).
ideas.rationale: These failure modes and the cap-based termination guards are a useful checklist for any loop Memoria does run (e.g. nightly discovery loop, refinement iterations) to guarantee bounded, cheap runs.

ideas.idea: Using role-play conversations to synthesize instruction datasets and fine-tune a small model (LLaMA-7B -> CAMEL-7B) for capability emergence.
ideas.rationale: Memoria prefers off-the-shelf local models (qwen2.5:7b) and deterministic ingest; bespoke fine-tuning on self-generated agent chatter is out of scope and off-domain for a single-researcher vault.

file: Li et al. - 2023 - Making large language models better reasoners with step-aware verifier.md
title: Making Large Language Models Better Reasoners with Step-Aware Verifier (DIVERSE)
one_line: DIVERSE boosts LLM reasoning by (1) diversifying prompts not just samples, (2) training a verifier to weight votes across reasoning paths, and (3) scoring each reasoning STEP individually to locate where a chain goes wrong.

ideas.idea: Voting verifier: a trained scorer (DeBERTa head) weights each candidate reasoning path so the final answer is a verifier-weighted vote rather than plain majority vote.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's learning-to-rank triage and discovery relevance scoring already use a small supervised scorer over candidates; weighting agreement by a learned quality estimate (instead of raw counts) is directly applicable to ranking discovered papers or candidate claim extractions, and the backbone is a small local classifier, not a gigantic LLM.

ideas.idea: Step-aware verification: assign a correctness label to each intermediate step and score steps individually, pinpointing where a chain first goes wrong.
ideas.rationale: Maps onto Memoria's atomic-claim extraction and argument-graph warrants: scoring per-claim/per-step rather than per-document lets the structural review gate flag exactly which extracted claim or warrant is unreliable, improving faithfulness and giving the PI interpretable failure localisation.

ideas.idea: Step-label derivation by matching intermediate results against positive paths, with an off-the-shelf NLI model (roberta-large-mnli) checking semantic equivalence of steps.
ideas.rationale: Using a small local NLI model to decide whether two statements are semantically equivalent is reusable in Memoria for record-linkage entity dedup, claim supersession (is this claim the same as / contradicting an existing one), and contradiction detection without a large model.

ideas.idea: Diverse prompts (vary the exemplar set, M1 prompts x M2 samples) yield more distinct reasoning paths than sampling from one fixed prompt.
ideas.rationale: For Memoria's extraction/judging engines, varying the prompt template (not just temperature) to get diverse candidates before the verifier picks is a cheap way to raise recall on hard claims; bounded fan-out fits single-researcher cost limits.

ideas.idea: Verifier needs a labeled training set of reasoning paths with ground-truth answers; performance only drops ~2% when training data is cut 75%.
ideas.rationale: Reassures Memoria that a small (250-1000 example) PI-curated set is enough to train the triage/relevance scorers, matching single-researcher data scale; the data-efficiency finding is the actionable insight.

ideas.idea: Whole method targets math/commonsense/inductive reasoning benchmarks via gigantic OpenAI models (davinci, code-davinci-002).
ideas.rationale: The benchmark task and reliance on gigantic cloud models are off-domain and against Memoria's local/cheap-model constraint; only the verifier/step-scoring mechanisms transfer, not the arithmetic-reasoning use case.

file: Li et al. - 2024 - Chain of ideas revolutionizing research via novel idea development with LLM agents.md
title: Chain of Ideas: Revolutionizing Research via Novel Idea Development with LLM Agents
one_line: CoI organizes the literature of a topic into a chain of progressively-developing ideas (backward to origins, forward to follow-ups) so an LLM grasps the research trend and generates grounded novel ideas, plus 'Idea Arena' a pairwise-ELO evaluation protocol.

ideas.idea: Chain-of-Ideas: build a directed chain of papers/ideas by extending an anchor paper backward (references it built on / baselines / same-topic) and forward (citing works ranked by embedding similarity), then summarize the trend between adjacent ideas.
ideas.rationale: Directly buildable on Memoria's typed-relations + argument graph + claim-supersession: a citekey-anchored chain that traces a line of development (supersedes/builds-on/baseline edges) is exactly the structure a PI wants for a topic, and it can be rendered in a disposable projection engine / projected canvas as a navigable space.

ideas.idea: Trend analysis: explicitly summarize the delta between each pair of adjacent ideas (what Paper i+1 changed vs Paper i) and predict future directions from the progression.
ideas.rationale: A per-edge 'what changed' annotation is a concrete enrichment for Memoria's typed relations and contradictions/supersession dashboard; it turns a citation edge into an interpretable warrant the PI can read, and the future-trend prediction feeds the proactive discovery loop.

ideas.idea: Backward extension terminates at a 'milestone' paper (>1000 citations) as a strong chain root, and stops when no reference is topic-relevant.
ideas.rationale: A cheap, deterministic stopping heuristic (citation-count milestone + topic-relevance gate) for bounding how far Memoria's discovery/scout walks a citation network, keeping nightly runs cheap for a single researcher.

ideas.idea: Multiple CoI branches (K perspectives via K generated queries) to avoid over-reliance on a single chain.
ideas.rationale: Generating a few perspective-queries per topic and building parallel chains is a useful retrieval-diversification idea for Memoria's retriever/scout profile; the paper shows width gives diminishing returns, so a small K keeps cost low.

ideas.idea: Idea Arena: pairwise round-robin tournament with ELO scoring and order-reversed double evaluation, shown to align with humans far better than Likert scoring.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's evaluation/benchmark work (e.g. ranking discovery relevance scorers or judge profiles) should prefer pairwise+ELO over absolute Likert; the position-bias mitigation (evaluate both orders) is a concrete, adoptable evaluation discipline.

ideas.idea: Novelty-checker agent that retrieves similar papers and refines the idea if embedding similarity is too high.
ideas.rationale: Mirrors Memoria's pre-file similarity gate: a similarity-based check that blocks near-duplicate output and triggers a refine loop is reusable for the structural review gate before a note is written.

ideas.idea: Entity extraction (datasets, baseline models, key terms) from each paper to ground generated ideas and improve clarity.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's entity/relation extraction and record-linkage: surfacing key entities per paper improves grounding of any generated synthesis and supports dedup.

ideas.idea: Fully autonomous LLM idea generation as the end goal (generate novel research ideas without human participation).
ideas.rationale: Memoria's pillar is PI-in-the-loop ('agents judge, PI approves'); auto-generating ideas to ship is misaligned. The chain/trend STRUCTURE is what's valuable, not the autonomous-ideation goal.

file: Li et al. - 2025 - MLR-copilot autonomous machine learning research based on large language models agents.md
title: MLR-Copilot: Autonomous Machine Learning Research based on Large Language Model Agents
one_line: A three-stage autonomous ML-research pipeline (IdeaAgent generates idea+plan, ExperimentAgent retrieves prototype code/models/data and writes executable code, then runs/debugs with optional human feedback), with an RL-tuned idea generator scored on novelty/feasibility/effectiveness.

ideas.idea: Structured idea representation = {paper info P, retrieved recent works R, generated methodology h, experiment plan e}, generated by first extracting research tasks/gaps/keywords then retrieving and synthesizing.
ideas.rationale: The task/gap/keyword extraction step before retrieval is a clean, reusable pattern for Memoria's MASSW-style paper-aspect extraction and keyphrase tag candidates; structuring a paper into task+gap+keywords feeds both the relations graph and the retriever query expansion.

ideas.idea: Reward-model scoring of generated ideas along three explicit, rubric-defined dimensions: Novelty, Feasibility, Effectiveness (with 1-10 anchored scoring guidelines in the appendix).
ideas.rationale: The anchored multi-dimensional rubrics (and the idea of separate scorers per dimension) are directly usable as judge criteria in Memoria's structural review gate / discovery triage, even without the RL training; rubric anchoring improves local-model judge consistency.

ideas.idea: Human-in-the-loop iterative debugging: ExperimentAgent runs, captures execution/debug feedback, and lets a human inject corrections to refine the implementation.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's MCP-only sandbox hard-denies agents any code-execution/terminal/file access (ADR-32/46, D40/D41); an agent that writes and runs experiment code is fundamentally incompatible with Memoria's security model.

ideas.idea: RL fine-tuning (SFT + multi-reward RL) of an idea-generation LLM on 4271 OpenReview conference papers with collected rating/novelty/feasibility/effectiveness scores.
ideas.rationale: Bespoke RL training of a custom idea model is out of scope and cost for a single-researcher vault committed to off-the-shelf local models; not buildable within Memoria's constraints.

ideas.idea: Model/data retrieval from HuggingFace via natural-language prompts to assemble an experiment.
ideas.rationale: Off-domain for Memoria (knowledge curation, not ML experiment execution); the auto-experimentation goal is not part of Memoria's mission.

ideas.idea: Stated limitation: strictly sequential stages with no backward transition (failed experiments should revisit hypotheses) is a weakness.
ideas.rationale: A design caution for Memoria's pipeline: where feasible, allow downstream gate rejections to feed back and revise upstream extractions/relations rather than treating ingest as one-way; informs supersession/revision flow.

file: Li et al. - 2025 - SciLitLLM how to adapt LLMs for scientific literature understanding.md
title: SciLitLLM: How to Adapt LLMs for Scientific Literature Understanding
one_line: A pipeline to adapt a general LLM (Qwen2.5) to scientific literature via continual pre-training + SFT, centered on practical data-engineering: LLM-based PDF parse-error correction, a lightweight quality-classifier filter, and keyword+task-guided synthetic instruction generation with multi-aspect LLM filtering.

ideas.idea: LLM-based format & grammar correction of PDF-parsed text (prompt Llama3-8B to fix formatting/syntax errors PyPDF2 introduces).
ideas.rationale: Memoria parses papers locally and deterministically; a post-parse cleanup pass (a small local model fixing OCR/parse garble before claim extraction) directly raises ingest quality. The exact failure mode (garbled references, broken formulas) is what Memoria's ingest faces, and a cheap local model (qwen2.5:7b) can do it.

ideas.idea: Lightweight text quality classifier: have a larger LLM score a 50k subset for 'educational value' (0-5), then transfer-train a small classifier (fineweb-edu checkpoint) to score the whole corpus and drop the lowest 25%.
ideas.rationale: This is a buildable, cheap content-quality gate: train a small classifier from a modest PI/LLM-labeled set to score incoming paper text and filter low-value segments (references, garbled spans) before they reach the structural review gate. Fits Memoria's pre-file gating and content-light diagnostics plane, and runs locally.

ideas.idea: Segment-level filtering to strip low-information sections (references, garbled text) from the corpus.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's deterministic ingest can apply the same idea: drop reference lists and parse-artifact segments before claim-sentence classification, reducing noise in atomic-claim extraction.

ideas.idea: Keyword+task-guided synthetic instruction generation: build a domain keyword probability table from high-impact papers, a task-description list from existing scientific datasets, then sample 20 keywords + a task to prompt GPT-4o for diverse (context, question, answer) triples.
ideas.rationale: The keyword-probability-table-from-corpus idea is reusable for Memoria's keyphrase tag candidates and for generating diverse internal eval/test instructions for a domain the PI works in; the (context,question,answer) structure mirrors Memoria's literature-grounded QA needs. Adapt with a local model rather than GPT-4o.

ideas.idea: Multi-aspect LLM-based instruction quality filter: score each synthetic instruction on clarity, complexity, correctness, usefulness, adaptability; drop average < 4.
ideas.rationale: A concrete multi-criterion LLM-judge rubric reusable for Memoria's structural review gate when deciding whether a generated note/claim is high enough quality to write; anchored aspect scoring improves local-judge reliability.

ideas.idea: Levenshtein-distance heuristic deduplication of generated instructions before LLM filtering.
ideas.rationale: Cheap edit-distance dedup as a first cull before any model call mirrors Memoria's record-linkage/pre-file similarity gate ordering: do cheap string dedup first, expensive semantic checks second.

ideas.idea: Hybrid CPT+SFT recipe to specialize a base model, including mixing in general-domain data (Redpajama/Infinity-Instruct) to preserve general ability.
ideas.rationale: Continual pre-training on 23.7B tokens over 32 A100s for days is far outside single-researcher/local-hardware constraints (RTX 4060 Ti 16GB); Memoria uses off-the-shelf models. The DATA-ENGINEERING modules transfer, the train-your-own-LLM strategy does not.

ideas.idea: Finding that a smaller well-curated, high-quality corpus beats brute size (7B beats 70B on SciRIFF).
ideas.rationale: Validates Memoria's 'faithfulness over flash' + curation-first philosophy at single-researcher scale: invest in quality filtering and curation rather than volume; supports the pre-file quality gate as high-leverage.

file: Han et al. - 2021 - PTR prompt tuning with rules for text classification.md
title: PTR: Prompt Tuning with Rules for Text Classification
one_line: PTR composes class-specific prompts from reusable sub-prompts joined by first-order logic rules (conditional functions) to handle many-class classification (notably relation extraction), encoding human prior knowledge while leveraging a frozen-ish PLM's masked-LM head.

ideas.idea: Decompose a many-class label space into reusable sub-prompts (unary entity-type functions + binary relation functions) composed by logic rules, instead of designing one template per class.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's claim-sentence classification, MASSW aspect tagging, and typed-relation extraction are exactly many-class problems where per-class prompt design doesn't scale. A sub-prompt-plus-rule composition gives interpretable, maintainable prompts for a local qwen2.5:7b and encodes the PI's domain priors, fitting the 'engines extract deterministically' pillar.

ideas.idea: Use conditional functions as first-order-logic predicates (e.g. is-person(x), is-parent-of(x,y)) whose conjunction defines a relation label.
ideas.rationale: Maps cleanly onto Memoria's argument graph / typed relations: a relation between two notes can be validated as a conjunction of typed-entity checks plus a semantic-connection check, which the structural-review gate can audit predicate-by-predicate rather than as an opaque label.

ideas.idea: Reversing relation direction in prompts yields large gains, showing PLM relation representations are position-aware and not symmetric.
ideas.rationale: Useful caution for Memoria's relation extraction: directionality of a typed relation (cites vs cited-by, supersedes vs superseded) must be made explicit in the prompt/template, not assumed symmetric. Adopt as a prompt-design rule, not a feature.

ideas.idea: Train/fine-tune the PLM encoder plus a few learnable prompt tokens (full RoBERTa-large fine-tune) per task.
ideas.rationale: Memoria prefers frozen local open models with deterministic prompting; per-task gradient fine-tuning of a large encoder is operationally heavy for a single-researcher local setup and not how Hermes engines are meant to run.

file: Han et al. - 2021 - Pre-trained models past, present and future.md
title: Pre-Trained Models: Past, Present and Future
one_line: A broad survey tracing PTMs from transfer/self-supervised learning through Transformer/GPT/BERT to architectures, multimodal/knowledge-enhanced pre-training, efficiency, interpretation, and open problems (incl. 'modeledge' and cognitive memory).

ideas.idea: Sustainable external long-term memory for Transformers via retrieval (REALM/RAG): tensorize a corpus and retrieve relevant passages as context for generation.
ideas.rationale: This is the conceptual backbone of Memoria's retrieval/RAG layer: the vault is the external memory, and Hermes retrieves relevant notes/claims as context. Validates Memoria's choice to keep knowledge in an inspectable external store rather than in model weights.

ideas.idea: Knowledge-enhanced PTMs: inject KG entities/relations or serialize structural knowledge into text so the model learns knowledge-text alignment.
ideas.rationale: Memoria has a typed-relation/argument graph; serializing relevant graph neighborhoods into the prompt (rather than retraining) is a cheap, local way to ground extraction/judgement engines in the PI's existing structure.

ideas.idea: Working-memory architectures that select/forget (CogLTX MemRecall) to handle long documents beyond a fixed window.
ideas.rationale: Paper ingest must handle long PDFs on a 16GB GPU; a select-then-process strategy (retrieve the salient sentences, then run the extraction engine) is more viable than feeding whole papers, and supports per-sentence claim classification.

ideas.idea: Uncertainty / over-confidence problem: PTMs don't know what they don't know; Bayesian/OOD detection to flag unreliable outputs.
ideas.rationale: Directly supports Memoria's atomic-claim uncertainty flags and the structural-review gate: engine outputs should carry confidence and OOD signals so agents/PI can triage low-confidence extractions rather than auto-filing them.

ideas.idea: Prompt tuning / continuous prompts / parameter-efficient adaptation as 'beyond fine-tuning'.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces using prompting over fine-tuning for local models; the specifics (continuous prompts needing billion-param models) are a caution that discrete, human-readable prompts are the right fit for Memoria's auditability and small local models.

ideas.idea: Large-scale distributed training, model/pipeline parallelism, MoE, trillion-param scaling, quantization-for-clusters.
ideas.rationale: Cloud-scale training/serving infrastructure is irrelevant to a single-researcher local deployment; Memoria consumes pretrained open models, never trains them.

ideas.idea: Multimodal/multilingual pre-training (V&L, DALLE, CLIP, mBART).
ideas.rationale: Off-domain for a text/markdown academic-paper system; no current Memoria surface needs image generation or cross-lingual alignment.

ideas.idea: 'Modeledge' / universal continuous knowledge base storing knowledge as model vectors.
ideas.rationale: Contradicts Memoria's core principle: knowledge must be symbolic, inspectable markdown/claims the PI can read and approve, not opaque real-valued vectors in model weights.

file: Hao et al. - 2023 - Optimizing prompts for text-to-image generation.md
title: Optimizing Prompts for Text-to-Image Generation (Promptist)
one_line: Promptist learns a language model (SFT then PPO/RLHF) that rewrites a user's text-to-image prompt into a model-preferred prompt, with a reward combining a CLIP relevance score (preserve intent) and an aesthetic score.

ideas.idea: Automatically rewrite a user's raw input into a downstream-model-preferred prompt via a learned LM, treating the target generator as a black box.
ideas.rationale: Memoria could use a lightweight local 'query rewriter' to translate the PI's natural-language discovery/retrieval queries into the retriever/scout's preferred form, improving the nightly discovery loop without touching the underlying model.

ideas.idea: Two-part reward = relevance (preserve original intent, gated) + quality, with a KL penalty to the SFT model to prevent over-optimization.
ideas.rationale: The 'relevance gate before optimizing for a secondary objective' pattern maps onto Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and discovery relevance scoring: only boost on a quality/aesthetic-style signal once a relevance threshold is cleared, and penalize drift from a trusted baseline.

ideas.idea: Heuristic-tag baseline (appending a fixed set of common modifiers) is brittle and varies wildly across domains; learned per-input adaptation generalizes better.
ideas.rationale: Cautionary insight for Memoria's keyphrase/tag candidate generation: a static stopword/modifier list won't generalize across subfields; per-note adaptation is preferable. Useful as a design caution, not a build.

ideas.idea: RLHF/PPO pipeline (SFT then PPO with a learned reward) to train the rewriter.
ideas.rationale: Full PPO training (32 V100s, 2.5 days) is far outside a single-researcher local budget and contradicts Memoria's preference for frozen cheap models with deterministic prompting; the goal of rewriting can be met by a prompt template, not RL.

ideas.idea: Aesthetic/CLIP image scoring and text-to-image generation itself.
ideas.rationale: Off-domain: Memoria is a text/claim knowledge system with no image-generation surface.

file: Hassabis et al. - 2017 - Neuroscience-inspired artificial intelligence.md
title: Neuroscience-Inspired Artificial Intelligence
one_line: A review arguing brain science should guide AI, surveying neuroscience-inspired mechanisms (attention, episodic memory/experience replay, working memory with external stores, continual learning, imagination/planning) at Marr's computational/algorithmic levels.

ideas.idea: Complementary learning systems: a fast instance-based store (hippocampus / episodic memory) for one-shot encoding plus a slow parametric store (neocortex) that consolidates via replay.
