ideas.rationale: This is a clean architectural metaphor for Memoria: the vault is the fast, content-addressable episodic store (rapidly capture a claim/note in one shot), and slow consolidation = the structural-review gate plus claim supersession that fold new items into stable structure over time. Names exactly why 'capture fast, judge/consolidate deliberately' is sound.

ideas.idea: Episodic control: act by similarity to stored past experiences (k-NN over an episodic buffer) rather than waiting for slow parametric learning, advantageous when experience is limited.
ideas.rationale: Justifies Memoria's record-linkage entity dedup and pre-file similarity gate as content-addressable retrieval over an episodic store; single-researcher scale (limited 'experience') is precisely where instance-based similarity beats heavyweight learning.

ideas.idea: Working memory with separated controller and external memory matrix (Neural Turing Machine / DNC): an executive that attends/reads/writes to an explicit external store.
ideas.rationale: Mirrors Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge' separation: Hermes agents are the controller that reads/writes the external markdown memory but hold no durable state themselves, consistent with the MCP-only sandbox where agents get no persistent file state.

ideas.idea: Continual learning via elastic weight consolidation (anchor parameters important to prior tasks) to avoid catastrophic forgetting.
ideas.rationale: Conceptually supports claim supersession/contradiction handling: when new knowledge arrives, important prior structure should be protected/anchored rather than overwritten. The mechanism is for neural weights, but the principle informs how Memoria stages updates against existing notes.

ideas.idea: Prioritized experience replay: replay high-reward/high-salience events more often.
ideas.rationale: Maps to Memoria's learning-to-rank triage and nightly discovery loop prioritizing salient/relevant items for the PI's attention rather than uniform processing.

ideas.idea: Virtual brain analytics / interpretability tools (activity maximization, lesioning, dimensionality reduction) to open AI black boxes.
ideas.rationale: Aligns with Memoria's read-only inspector and static HTML admin reports: treat engines as inspectable systems whose behavior is visualized and probed, supporting the faithfulness-over-flash and PI-approval pillars.

ideas.idea: Deep RL agents (DQN/Atari/Go), backprop biological-plausibility, grid-cell coding, generative imagination for planning.
ideas.rationale: These are agent-control and neuro-mechanism details with no buildable surface in a markdown knowledge-management system; interesting background only.

file: He et al. - 2022 - Towards a unified view of parameter-efficient transfer learning.md
title: Towards a Unified View of Parameter-Efficient Transfer Learning
one_line: Unifies adapters, prefix tuning, and LoRA as modifications to hidden states along four design dimensions (functional form, modified representation, insertion form, composition function), then mixes the best choices into a MAM Adapter matching full fine-tuning while tuning ~0.5-6.7% of parameters.

ideas.idea: A unifying-framework methodology: decompose a family of competing methods into shared design dimensions, then mix-and-match best-performing choices to derive new variants.
ideas.rationale: A transferable research-design pattern, not a feature: when Memoria evaluates competing extraction/ranking/clustering techniques, casting them into a common set of design axes (input, transform, composition) lets the PI reason about and ablate them systematically. Meta-level value only.

ideas.idea: Parameter-efficient tuning (LoRA/adapters/prefix) lets one frozen base model serve many tasks via small added modules, avoiding a full fine-tuned copy per task and catastrophic forgetting.
ideas.rationale: If Memoria ever needs to specialize a local model to the PI's subfield, a single small LoRA adapter is far more compatible with the constrained local GPU (16GB) and 'one model, many engines' setup than full fine-tuning. Conditional/future, hence borrow not adopt.

ideas.idea: Empirical finding: FFN-targeted modifications use added capacity more effectively at larger budgets; attention/multi-head targeting wins at very small (~0.1%) budgets.
ideas.rationale: An internal implementation detail of where to inject adapters in a Transformer; Memoria consumes models as black boxes and does not modify their internals, so this guides nothing buildable here.

ideas.idea: Caution: methods claiming parity with full fine-tuning on easy benchmarks (GLUE/E2E) can fail on harder/higher-resource tasks; report on diverse benchmarks.
ideas.rationale: Good evaluation hygiene for Memoria: validate engines on diverse, hard, in-domain tasks (the L5 quality tier) rather than declaring success on a single easy benchmark, matching the verify-conclusions-independently principle.

file: Faste et al. - 2013 - Brainstorm, chainstorm, cheatstorm, tweetstorm new ideation strategies for distributed HCI design.md
title: Brainstorm, Chainstorm, Cheatstorm, Tweetstorm: New Ideation Strategies for Distributed HCI Design
one_line: A design-driven HCI study proposing 'cheatstorming' (ideation by re-judging a random pool of previously-generated ideas instead of generating new ones) and 'chainstorming' (passing idea subsets along communication chains), arguing the value of ideation lies in unconventional juxtaposition, not novel generation.

ideas.idea: Cheatstorming: ideation as selecting/recombining a random sample of *previously generated* ideas against a new prompt, rather than generating fresh ones.
ideas.rationale: Maps onto Memoria's discovery loop and projected canvas: seeding the PI with a small random sample of stored atomic claims/notes juxtaposed against a current research question could provoke unexpected connections, a low-effort variant of the discovery relevance scorer that deliberately injects 'strangeness' rather than nearest-neighbor relevance.

ideas.idea: Constrain the candidate set tightly (small N, time limits) to force quick visceral judgments; larger pools felt tedious and produced 'less inspired' results.
ideas.rationale: Argues for capping the size of similarity/discovery candidate lists Memoria surfaces in a gate or canvas; a tight, time-boxed shortlist is better for PI judgment than an exhaustive ranked list.

ideas.idea: Remove already-seen/'used' ideas from successive rounds; track viewed items so content stays unfamiliar.
ideas.rationale: Directly applicable to Memoria's proactive nightly discovery: maintain a 'seen/dismissed' set so the discovery loop and triage queue never re-surface items the PI already judged, keeping the surfaced set novel.

ideas.idea: Four-stage ideation model (prompting, sharing, selecting, committing) and avoid color-coding inputs because it injects a value-judgment bias.
ideas.rationale: The staged group-ideation model and provenance-bias caveat are about multi-person facilitated workshops; Memoria is single-researcher with deterministic provenance tracking, so the social-process framing does not transfer.

ideas.idea: Tweetstormer / distributed crowd ideation over Twitter as a social platform.
ideas.rationale: Multi-user, social-network, cloud-crowd mechanism; contradicts Memoria's single-PI local-vault scale.

file: Fekete et al. - 2008 - The value of information visualization.md
title: The Value of Information Visualization
one_line: A position paper arguing that InfoVis's value is hardest to measure precisely where it matters most (insight, exploration, 'answering questions you didn't know you had'), grounded in preattentive/Gestalt perception, exploratory data analysis, and an economic cost/value model of visualization.

ideas.idea: InfoVis amplifies cognition as external memory and a fast perceptual filter, accelerating the Popperian 'falsify among competing theories' loop, valuable precisely when the user does not yet know what question to ask.
ideas.rationale: Justifies Memoria's projected canvas / spaces as exploratory surfaces over the claim/argument graph: the goal is browsing-driven insight and better questions, not query-answering, so dashboards should optimize for overview-and-pattern-spotting rather than precise lookup (which the inspector/search already covers).

ideas.idea: Conditions where browsing beats querying (good underlying structure so neighbors are similar; user can't verbalize the need; recognition easier than description).
ideas.rationale: A concrete checklist for when Memoria should surface a similarity/projection view vs. a structured query form, e.g. the pre-file similarity gate and contradictions dashboard benefit from spatial proximity-encodes-similarity layout.

ideas.idea: Gestalt + preattentive encoding principles (proximity=similarity, closure, color preattentive only up to ~7 categories) to choose visual encodings.
ideas.rationale: Direct guidance for the disposable projection engine and canvas layout in Obsidian: place related claims/notes spatially close, cap categorical color coding, so visual queries (clusters, outliers, contradictions) are preattentive.

ideas.idea: Anscombe's Quartet / 'visualize before you model' — summary statistics hide structure that a plot reveals.
ideas.rationale: Supports giving the PI a visual view over BERTopic clusters and claim distributions rather than only aggregate counts in admin reports, since a single number can mislead.

ideas.idea: Economic model of value: profit = nm·W(deltaK) - costs; great viz is used often, by many, for high-value insight, at low per-session cost, with tight integration into the user's existing tool.
ideas.rationale: Strong design principle for Memoria: every surface must minimize per-session cost (Ce) by being integrated directly in the Obsidian UI (the PI-direct-access rule), not a separate tool, and target frequently-repeated high-value tasks; features whose insight-value doesn't beat their cognitive cost should be cut.

ideas.idea: Insight is temporally elusive and multi-causal, so success of a viz is hard to attribute/measure (success-story scarcity).
ideas.rationale: A caution for Memoria's evaluation/metrics plane: don't try to quantify 'insight generated' by the discovery loop with a single hard metric; use qualitative/longitudinal signals and PI feedback instead.

ideas.idea: Visual-analytics: combine data mining with visualization when datasets exceed direct visualization scale.
ideas.rationale: Endorses Memoria's mix of automated engines (BERTopic clustering, LTR triage) feeding human-judged visual surfaces — engines reduce scale, the PI judges visually.

file: Feng and Liu - 2026 - A visionary look at vibe researching.md
title: A Visionary Look at Vibe Researching
one_line: A vision paper defining 'vibe researching' (human as creative director / quality gatekeeper, LLM agents executing literature, code, analysis, and drafting under natural-language direction), cataloguing its enabling techniques, seven technical limitations, and societal impacts with mapped future directions.

ideas.idea: Human-in-control orchestration ('human is the meta-agent') with delegation-plus-oversight, iterative refinement, and human accountability — explicitly rejecting vibe-coding's 'accept all'.
ideas.rationale: This IS Memoria's governance pillar stated in different words: 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' and the structural review gate. The paper independently validates the architecture; reinforces that the approval gate must never become rubber-stamping.

ideas.idea: Three-layer agent memory (working / episodic / semantic) with semantic memory as the project's persistent institutional store, separate from raw conversation logs.
ideas.rationale: Maps cleanly onto Memoria's vault-as-semantic-memory: atomic claims, typed relations, MASSW aspects, and decisions (ADRs) are the durable semantic layer that survives across sessions, distinct from transient agent context; argues for indexing 'design decisions/established conventions' as first-class memory.

ideas.idea: Quality gates / checkpoints at fixed workflow points (after retrieval: are papers real and summaries accurate; after analysis: numbers sane and consistent), the researcher's defense against compounding errors.
ideas.rationale: Concretely specifies what the Memoria structural review gate should check before notes are written: citekey/paper existence, claim-to-source faithfulness, internal consistency (feeds the contradictions dashboard).

ideas.idea: Atomic-claim-level fact verification (FActScore-style decomposition + check each assertion against a source) and citation-existence pipelines to catch fabrication before the human sees it.
ideas.rationale: Memoria already extracts atomic claims with uncertainty flags; this paper says the verification stage should operate at exactly that granularity — wire a per-claim source-grounding check into the ingest/gate, turning hallucination into detectable retrieval error.

ideas.idea: Self-reflection functions: output verification, consistency checking against prior project results (flag a metric that improved in one table but worsened in another), and explicit uncertainty signaling ('this is outside my training distribution').
ideas.rationale: Consistency-checking against prior stored results is exactly Memoria's contradictions/claim-supersession machinery; uncertainty signaling matches the uncertainty flags on claims — adopt agent uncertainty escalation so the gate routes low-confidence extractions to PI attention.

ideas.idea: Verification asymmetry: reviewing delegated work costs nearly as much expertise as producing it; under deadline pressure people skim, which is the main path errors enter.
ideas.rationale: A core risk for Memoria's whole model. Mandates verification *tooling* (diff views, side-by-side source, citation checks in the read-only inspector) that lowers PI review cost, not just a 'please review' gate; faithfulness-over-flash is the right stance.

ideas.idea: Persistent cross-session project memory via hierarchical retrieval (RAPTOR recursive summarization) and structured knowledge stores that auto-load relevant context.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's spaces/projection and retriever profile can borrow hierarchical summary-tree retrieval over the vault so the agent reconstructs project state without the PI re-establishing context each session; cheaper than holding everything in-context.

ideas.idea: Diversity-aware retrieval to counter convergent thinking / English-Western-mainstream bias of LLM literature review (agents over-favor highly-cited mainstream work).
ideas.rationale: Memoria's discovery relevance scorer and triage LTR should include a deliberate diversity/novelty term (cf. cheatstorming 'strangeness') so the nightly loop surfaces heterodox/long-tail work, not just nearest-neighbors to what the PI already cites.

ideas.idea: Treat the interaction trace as a first-class reproducibility artifact: log prompts/responses, model versions (e.g. claude-opus-4-...), artifact versions in git, config snapshots, for after-the-fact provenance audit.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's deterministic-ingest + provenance ethos; the gate's audit trail should record model id and engine versions so any written claim is traceable to its generating run.

ideas.idea: Standardized agent-native infrastructure via the Model Context Protocol (MCP) as a uniform interface to tools/data.
ideas.rationale: Vindicates Memoria's MCP-only agent sandbox (ADR-32/46): agents reach tools/data only through MCP, which is both the security boundary and the 'agent-native API' the paper calls for.

ideas.idea: Privacy tension: cloud frontier models are best but leak unpublished work; self-hosted open models preserve privacy but lag, needing significant GPU; hybrid local-for-sensitive / cloud-for-hard.
ideas.rationale: Directly supports Memoria's local + cheap/open-model preference (qwen2.5:7b on a 16GB GPU); unpublished PI research stays local. Confirms the constraint and the hybrid-escalation option for hard reasoning.

ideas.idea: Five-phase workflow (ideation, exploration, experimentation, synthesis, refinement) with human-controlled entry/exit of each phase.
ideas.rationale: A usable scaffold for Memoria's profiles/skills: scout/retriever profiles handle exploration; engines handle synthesis; the PI controls phase boundaries via gates. Borrow the phase structure, not the autonomous-experiment parts (out of single-PI scope).

ideas.idea: Full end-to-end autonomous 'auto research' (AI Scientist-style: generate hypotheses, run experiments, simulate peer review, no human).
ideas.rationale: Explicitly the pole Memoria rejects; conflicts with the approval gate and human accountability. The paper itself argues reliability favors the human-in-control side for science.

file: Forbus - 1984 - Qualitative process theory.md
title: Qualitative Process Theory
one_line: Forbus's classic AI theory formalizing commonsense reasoning about physical processes via qualitative quantities, individual views, processes with preconditions/quantity-conditions, and signed influences (I+, I-) plus qualitative proportionalities, to derive behavior without numeric models. (Note: the vault extraction is near-empty — only an OCR'd fluid/heat-flow process box survived.)

ideas.idea: Represent causal structure as signed direct influences (I+/I-) and qualitative proportionalities (alpha-Q+) between quantities, instead of numeric equations.
ideas.rationale: A vocabulary for Memoria's typed relations and argument graph: 'increases/decreases', 'supports/undermines', and proportional 'depends-on' edges between claims could carry a sign/direction, enabling lightweight qualitative inference over the claim graph without numeric models.

ideas.idea: Processes/views activated only when preconditions and quantity-conditions hold (active only in a context).
ideas.rationale: Maps to conditional/contextual validity of claims — a claim or relation that holds only under stated conditions; useful for claim supersession (a finding is superseded when its preconditions no longer hold) and for the contradictions dashboard to scope conflicts to shared contexts.

ideas.idea: Full qualitative-simulation / envisionment machinery to predict all possible physical behaviors of a device.
ideas.rationale: A physics/device-reasoning engine far outside Memoria's domain (academic literature claims); the simulation apparatus has no buildable role in a single-researcher knowledge vault.

file: Friedman et al. - 2013 - Value sensitive design and information systems.md
title: Value Sensitive Design and Information Systems
one_line: Presents Value Sensitive Design: a tripartite (conceptual / empirical / technical) iterative methodology for embedding human values into system design, with stakeholder analysis, value-conflict mapping, and three case studies (cookie informed consent, plasma-display 'window', UrbanSim).

ideas.idea: Designing for credibility/legitimation via behavioral transparency, open process, live documentation that embeds the source code computing each indicator, sensitivity analyses, and historical validation.
ideas.rationale: Directly informs Memoria's read-only inspector and static HTML admin reports: each derived value (discovery score, similarity, claim extraction) should be inspectable down to the engine/code that produced it ('live documentation'), so the PI can judge and trust outputs — the credibility basis for the approval gate.

ideas.idea: Indicators: distilled variables that surface an attribute of interest, grouped into value categories, that stakeholders select to evaluate scenarios.
ideas.rationale: A model for Memoria dashboards/spaces: expose configurable 'indicators' (contradiction count, unsupported-claim count, supersession events) the PI can pick to monitor the vault's health, rather than one fixed report.

ideas.idea: Minimal distraction as a derived design criterion: too many consent/attention prompts numb the user so important ones get rote-accepted.
ideas.rationale: Critical for Memoria's gate design: if the structural review gate fires too often on trivial items, the PI will rubber-stamp and miss consequential ones — batch/triage gate prompts by importance (ties to the LTR triage and the vibe-researching 'verification asymmetry' risk).

ideas.idea: Conceptual investigation: rigorously define each value/construct (with formal criteria from literature) before measuring it.
ideas.rationale: Method for Memoria's own ontology work: precisely define 'claim', 'contradiction', 'supersession', 'warrant' with explicit criteria before building engines that detect them, so extraction and the gate apply consistent definitions.

ideas.idea: Direct vs. indirect stakeholder analysis and value-conflict mapping (treat conflicts as design constraints, not either/or) across many stakeholders.
ideas.rationale: The multi-stakeholder apparatus assumes many affected parties (UrbanSim citizens, 'the watched'); Memoria has a single PI stakeholder, so stakeholder enumeration and power-equalization techniques don't transfer.

ideas.idea: Agile/modular architecture decoupling indicator computation from models so new indicators/values can be added as emergent concerns arise.
ideas.rationale: Endorses Memoria's compiled-profiles / modular-engine and disposable-projection approach: keep scoring/indicator logic separate and swappable from core ingest so new dashboards or relation types can be added without rewriting engines.

ideas.idea: Interactional stance: technology's features make some values easier and others harder, but use depends on the human's goals; values are neither inscribed nor purely social.
ideas.rationale: A sober framing for Memoria's governance: the gate and MCP sandbox make faithfulness/safety *easier* but cannot guarantee good research on their own; the PI's judgment remains decisive, so don't over-trust automation.

file: Schwappach - 2025 - Machines like us scientists AI tools for mining the scientific literature in basic biomedical scie.md
title: Machines like us scientists? AI tools for mining the scientific literature in basic biomedical science
one_line: A molecular cell biologist's hands-on commentary stress-testing ChatGPT/SciSpace/Scopus AI on three real literature-mining tasks, exposing where RAG-grounded tools help and where they fail (hallucinated citations, abstract over-reliance, inability to separate review from primary evidence).

ideas.idea: RAG with direct links to original PDFs (SciSpace/Scopus AI) sharply reduces fabricated references versus bare ChatGPT, which invented a plausible-sounding citation and admitted to 'merging findings'.
ideas.rationale: Directly validates Memoria's citekey/Zotero backbone and faithfulness-over-flash stance: every extracted claim must link to the source span, and the system should never surface a claim without a resolvable provenance link. Strengthens the case for the pre-file similarity gate and provenance-required claim extraction.

ideas.idea: None of the tools could cleanly distinguish information retrieved from review articles versus original research articles — a barrier to digging into primary/negative/control data.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's MASSW-style aspect extraction and claim classification should tag source-type (review vs primary vs preprint) as a first-class field, and the contradictions/argument graph should weight primary evidence over review restatements. This is a concrete, buildable metadata dimension.

ideas.idea: Abstract over-reliance: Scopus AI works only on abstracts/metadata, over-weighting the abstract and missing evidence buried in methods/figures/negative results; literature 'bias' where many abstracts repeat an under-evidenced claim creates a citation echo that tools amplify.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's atomic-claim + uncertainty-flag design: a claim repeated across N papers without new primary evidence should be flagged as low-independent-support, not high-confidence. Borrow the insight as a scoring signal (independent-corroboration count vs raw citation/repetition count) in claim supersession and discovery relevance.

ideas.idea: Query-refinement behavior: tools rephrase the user's question, expand it into a boolean keyword search, or ask clarifying questions before answering (e.g., Scopus AI auto-generating an OR/AND keyword expansion).
ideas.rationale: Useful pattern for Memoria's retriever/scout profile and nightly discovery loop — auto-expand a PI question into a structured query and surface the expansion for inspection. But keep it inspectable/approved, not silent, consistent with engines-write-agents-judge-PI-approves.

ideas.idea: Tools fail to critically challenge the literature or generate genuinely novel alternative hypotheses; suggestions remain generic and 'require critical human thought'.
ideas.rationale: Confirms a boundary rather than a feature to build: Memoria should not over-promise autonomous hypothesis generation. The PI-in-the-loop judgment model already encodes this; no new mechanism to adopt.

ideas.idea: Aspiration that future tools mine structured data/figures and databases rather than natural-language abstracts ('decrease articles written in natural language').
ideas.rationale: Out of scope for a single-researcher Obsidian system; Memoria ingests the literature as-published (PDF/text), it cannot restructure the scholarly record. Off-domain wish-list item.

file: Shahi and Hummel - 2026 - Automating categorization of scientific texts with In-context learning and prompt-chaining in large.md
title: Automating Categorization of Scientific Texts with In-Context Learning and Prompt-Chaining in Large Language Models
one_line: Evaluates off-the-shelf local LLMs (via Ollama) on classifying papers into the 3-level ORKG taxonomy, showing hierarchical prompt-chaining (predict domain, then subject within domain, then topic within subject) beats flat in-context learning and even older BERT for top two levels.

ideas.idea: Hierarchical prompt-chaining: decompose a nested-taxonomy classification into a top-down sequence of constrained-choice prompts, where each step only offers the children of the previously chosen node.
ideas.rationale: Directly buildable for Memoria's keyphrase tag candidates and any taxonomy/space assignment. Constraining the LLM to a small valid option set per level fits the deterministic-ingest + cheap-local-model constraint and the structural review gate (each step's output is inspectable). Lifts accuracy without fine-tuning — exactly Memoria's regime.

ideas.idea: Temperature sweep finding an optimum at 0.8 for classification (not 0), with performance declining above it.
ideas.rationale: Counterintuitive and worth a calibration pass in Memoria's local-model engines, but it is model/task-specific (their best model was Llama-3.1-70B, not qwen2.5:7b). Borrow the methodology (sweep + pick), not the literal 0.8 value.

ideas.idea: Tolerant match metrics — exact match, normalized Levenshtein string distance (>0.7), and BERT embedding cosine (>0.7) — to credit near-miss label predictions ('Computer Science' vs 'Computer Science & Engineering').
ideas.rationale: Memoria's record-linkage entity dedup and tag-candidate evaluation need exactly this kind of fuzzy-match scoring to avoid penalizing semantically-correct-but-lexically-different outputs. Cheap, deterministic, local-embeddings-friendly.

ideas.idea: Models fail on multi-label / multivalent taxonomy nodes (collapse a 6-term compound label to a single term), and deep (3rd-level/topic) classification plateaus around 50%.
ideas.rationale: A realism check for Memoria: deep auto-tagging should be treated as candidate suggestions routed through the approval gate, not authoritative writes. Borrow as a confidence-threshold/route-to-PI rule for fine-grained tags.

ideas.idea: Few-shot beats one-shot beats zero-shot for in-context learning on this task.
ideas.rationale: Standard but confirms that Memoria's compiled profiles should include curated few-shot exemplars for classification/extraction prompts. Modest, generic insight.

ideas.idea: Whole pipeline runs locally on Ollama with open models (Gemma2, Llama3.1, Mistral-Nemo, Phi-3.5) — no cloud, no fine-tuning.
ideas.rationale: Strong external validation of Memoria's local+open+no-finetune architecture; their setup mirrors Hermes/qmd. Confirms the approach is viable, and the model lineup informs fallback choices (though their 70B exceeds the 16GB test-env budget).

file: Shaikh et al. - 2023 - On second thought, let's not think step by step! Bias and toxicity in zero-shot reasoning.md
title: On Second Thought, Let's Not Think Step by Step! Bias and Toxicity in Zero-Shot Reasoning
one_line: Shows zero-shot chain-of-thought ('let's think step by step') reliably increases bias and toxicity on socially sensitive tasks — worsening with scale, mitigated only by alignment plus explicit instruction — cautioning that CoT is not a universally safe default.

ideas.idea: Zero-shot CoT is not universally beneficial: on tasks requiring nuance/uncertainty it degrades output (here, into bias/toxicity), and CoT can fabricate an irrelevant disambiguating line of reasoning ('stereotyped hallucination') that flips an ambiguous correct answer to a wrong one.
ideas.rationale: Direct design rule for Memoria's engines: do not blanket-apply CoT in claim extraction, contradiction judging, or relevance scoring where the right answer is often 'uncertain/unknown'. CoT-induced over-confident disambiguation is exactly what would corrupt atomic-claim uncertainty flags. Bake 'prefer explicit uncertainty over forced reasoning' into prompt design and structural review.

ideas.idea: Reasoning strategy as a tunable design pattern: changing the reasoning verbalizer changes behavior, so the prompt's reasoning mode should be chosen per task and audited.
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's compiled-profile/skill-governance model — reasoning mode is a per-engine configuration that should be version-controlled and inspectable, not an ad hoc default. 'Audit the reasoning steps' aligns with the engines-write/agents-judge gate.

ideas.idea: Adding an explicit mitigation instruction (choose 'unknown' when info is insufficient; treat groups equally) sharply reduces CoT harm — but only on a well-aligned model.
ideas.rationale: Borrow the 'when info is insufficient, choose unknown' instruction pattern verbatim for Memoria's uncertainty-flagging and claim classification prompts; it operationalizes faithfulness. Caveat: efficacy depends on model alignment, and Memoria runs smaller local models, so verify per-model.

ideas.idea: LLM explanations/CoTs are often post-hoc and unfaithful — they don't reliably reflect the actual basis for the answer.
ideas.rationale: Cautions Memoria not to treat an engine's self-explanation as a trustworthy warrant in the argument graph. Borrow as a verification principle: warrants must trace to source spans, not to model self-narration.

ideas.idea: Specific bias benchmarks (CrowS-Pairs, StereoSet, BBQ, HarmfulQ) and scaling/alignment trend analysis.
ideas.rationale: Social-bias/toxicity safety auditing of a public chatbot is off-domain for a single-researcher scientific-literature tool; Memoria isn't a user-facing dialogue product handling sensitive social queries. The benchmarks themselves aren't applicable.

file: Shanahan et al. - 2023 - Role-play with large language models.md
title: Role-Play with Large Language Models
one_line: A conceptual paper arguing LLM dialogue agents are best understood as role-play / a superposition of simulacra rather than as entities with beliefs or intentions, using this framing to demystify apparent deception and self-awareness.

ideas.idea: Behavioral test to distinguish 'making things up' from 'confidently wrong in good faith': regenerate the same prompt multiple times — high semantic variation across regenerations indicates fabrication, low variation indicates a stable (possibly wrong) encoded belief.
ideas.rationale: A cheap, model-agnostic self-consistency signal Memoria can use to flag low-confidence/hallucinated claim extractions during ingest: regenerate, measure semantic variance, route high-variance outputs to the structural review gate as uncertain. Fits local-model, deterministic-check constraints.

ideas.idea: Simulator-vs-simulacra distinction: there is no authentic 'voice' of the base model; an agent's stated goals/identity are role-played, so its self-reports should not be taken literally.
ideas.rationale: Useful framing for Memoria's MCP-only sandbox rationale — agents have no genuine agenda, which is partly why hard MCP-level denies (not trust in agent intent) enforce the sandbox. Reinforces existing architecture; not a new build.

ideas.idea: Avoid anthropomorphizing agents; describe behavior in role-play terms to predict/control without ascribing beliefs.
ideas.rationale: Philosophical/communication guidance, not a buildable mechanism. Memoria's agents are MCP tool-callers, not personas; no feature follows.

ideas.idea: Role-played self-preservation/deception becomes materially dangerous once an agent has real-world tools (email, web, bank access).
ideas.rationale: Memoria already forecloses this risk by construction: MCP-only sandbox, no file/terminal/code execution, ever (ADR-32/46). The paper argues for a guardrail Memoria already has; nothing new to adopt.

file: Shanmugam et al. - 2024 - Generative AI in medicine.md
title: Generative AI in Medicine
one_line: A comprehensive review of generative-AI use cases across clinicians, patients, trial organizers, researchers, and trainees, paired with cross-cutting challenges (privacy, transparency, hallucination, equity, evaluation, accountability) and research directions.

ideas.idea: RAG over a verified knowledge base reduces hallucination and makes information sources transparent, letting users assess reliability; pair with natural-language queries like 'relevant family history for chest pain' that synthesize across fragmented records.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's retrieval core: ground every generated synthesis in retrieved, citable vault content and expose sources for inspection. The 'proactively surface likely-relevant info based on current stage' idea maps onto Memoria's nightly proactive discovery loop and pre-file similarity gate.

ideas.idea: Automated systematic literature review: LLM screens thousands of abstracts, identifies relevant papers (reported ~91% vs human), and justifies each include/exclude decision.
ideas.rationale: Maps directly onto Memoria's discovery-relevance scoring, learning-to-rank triage, and discovery loop. The 'justify inclusion/exclusion' requirement is exactly the warrant/inspectable-decision pattern Memoria's review gate wants — adopt the justification-per-decision practice.

ideas.idea: Fine-grained real-world evaluation: judging models on final answer alone overestimates efficacy; must also measure guideline adherence, sensitivity to input order, and specify both a prompt set and explicit quality criteria.
ideas.rationale: Concrete evaluation discipline for Memoria's L5 quality testing — score extraction/relevance engines on faithfulness and order-robustness, not just top-1 correctness. Defines what 'good enough to write to the vault' should mean at the gate.

ideas.idea: LLM self-explanations sound plausible but aren't necessarily faithful; interpretability methods give divergent, sometimes misleading answers — prefer rigorous evaluation over trusting explanations.
ideas.rationale: Consistent with the Shaikh paper: Memoria's argument graph should not treat model-generated rationales as authoritative warrants; rely on source-grounded verification. Borrow as a governance principle.

ideas.idea: Models over-index on demographic correlations / amplify skew (e.g., generating Black patients for sarcoidosis 97% of the time), exaggerating true population distributions.
ideas.rationale: Not a clinical concern for Memoria, but the general failure mode — LLMs exaggerating a correlation present in training data — is a warning for claim-relevance scoring and supersession: a frequently-stated association may be model-amplified, not evidence-weighted. Borrow as a calibration caveat.

ideas.idea: Synthetic data generation, ambient clinical scribing, diagnosis differentials, patient-facing chatbots, consent-form simplification, federated learning, surgical guidance, accountability/liability frameworks.
ideas.rationale: Multi-user clinical-deployment, regulated-PHI, and patient-interaction concerns are categorically out of scope for a single-researcher local literature tool. These assume hospital-scale infrastructure and human-subject contexts Memoria does not touch.

file: Min et al. - 2022 - Rethinking the role of demonstrations what makes In-context learning work.md
title: Rethinking the Role of Demonstrations: What Makes In-Context Learning Work?
one_line: An empirical study showing that in-context learning gains come from the label space, input distribution, and format of demonstrations rather than ground-truth input-label mappings.

ideas.idea: Ground-truth labels in demonstrations matter little; random labels barely hurt — what matters is the label space, input distribution, and the input-label pair FORMAT.
ideas.rationale: For Memoria's local engines that do few-shot extraction (atomic claims, claim-sentence classification, MASSW aspects) on cheap qwen2.5:7b, this means prompt demonstrations should prioritize covering the full label space and realistic in-distribution examples rather than agonizing over per-example gold labels — cheaper to author robust prompts.

ideas.idea: Specifying the label space explicitly (even via random-but-in-space labels) drives direct-model performance; out-of-distribution demo inputs hurt.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's classification prompts should enumerate the allowed label set and use demo inputs drawn from actual paper sentences (in-distribution), improving extraction reliability without needing more labeled data.

ideas.idea: Meta-training with an ICL objective (MetaICL) magnifies reliance on format over mapping.
ideas.rationale: Memoria uses off-the-shelf local models and does not meta-train; building an ICL-objective training pipeline contradicts the deterministic-ingest, no-custom-training constraint.

ideas.idea: You can approach k-shot performance with zero labeled data by pairing unlabeled inputs with random labels.
ideas.rationale: Cute zero-shot trick but Memoria's extraction tasks are generation/structured-output, not the closed-set classification this applies to; the paper itself flags it doesn't extend to generation.

file: Minsky - 1974 - A framework for representing knowledge.md
title: A Framework for Representing Knowledge
one_line: Minsky's foundational frames theory: stereotyped data-structures with terminals, default assignments, and frame-systems linked by a similarity network for representing and revising knowledge.

ideas.idea: Frames as structured templates with named terminals/slots that carry marker conditions (type, must-be-assigned, relations to other slots).
ideas.rationale: Memoria's MASSW paper aspects and structured-capture forms are effectively frames-with-slots; framing extraction schemas as typed terminals with constraints is a clean design language for the structural review gate to validate against.

ideas.idea: Default assignments: terminals come pre-filled with weakly-bound defaults, displaced when better-fitting evidence arrives.
ideas.rationale: Maps to Memoria's claim supersession and uncertainty flags — a slot/field can hold a provisional (default) value that a later, better-attested extraction overrides, with the displacement recorded rather than silently overwritten.

ideas.idea: Frame-systems share terminals across views; an error/mismatch generates an 'error comment' that selects a replacement frame via a similarity network.
ideas.rationale: The match-fail-then-substitute pattern is a useful model for Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and record-linkage dedup: when a new note doesn't fit the proposed frame/entity, emit a structured mismatch signal that routes to an alternative candidate rather than forcing a bad merge.

ideas.idea: Story/discourse super-frames with slots (setting, protagonists, main event, moral) accumulate information incrementally across sentences.
ideas.rationale: Narrative-comprehension super-frames are off-domain for a scientific-claim system; Memoria's argument graph with warrants is the relevant structure, not story scripts.

ideas.idea: Logic is poorly suited to approximate/common-sense reasoning; prefer stereotype-matching with tolerances ('strain') over strict quantifiers.
ideas.rationale: Memoria deliberately wants faithfulness and auditable typed relations/warrants; embracing tolerant pseudo-logical default-substitution as a reasoning engine conflicts with the 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' governance and contradiction-tracking goals.

file: Mishra et al. - 2022 - Cross-task generalization via natural language crowdsourcing instructions.md
title: Cross-Task Generalization via Natural Language Crowdsourcing Instructions
one_line: Introduces NATURAL INSTRUCTIONS, a dataset and unified schema for task instructions (definition, things-to-avoid, positive/negative examples, reason, suggestion) enabling models to generalize to unseen tasks.

ideas.idea: A unified instruction SCHEMA: title, prompt, definition, things-to-avoid, emphasis/caution, positive examples, negative examples, reason, suggestion.
ideas.rationale: Memoria compiles profiles/skills for its sandboxed agents; structuring every extraction/judging task definition with exactly these fields (esp. definition + things-to-avoid + reason'd examples) is a directly buildable template for the profile-compilation and skill-governance layer.

ideas.idea: Decompose complex crowdsourcing tasks into minimal standalone subtasks each with its own instruction.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's deterministic ingest already splits work into engines; codifying extraction as minimal single-purpose subtasks (claim extraction, uncertainty flagging, aspect tagging) with per-subtask instructions improves reliability on small local models and eases the review gate.

ideas.idea: Negative examples surprisingly HURT model performance (models learn worse with them).
ideas.rationale: Concrete, actionable: when authoring Memoria's local-model extraction prompts, favor positive examples + explicit definitions and avoid loading prompts with negative examples — saves tokens on cheap models and improves output quality.

ideas.idea: Definition is most helpful for classification tasks; positive examples most helpful for generation tasks (benefit depends on task category).
ideas.rationale: Lets Memoria tune prompt composition per engine type — claim-sentence classification leans on crisp definitions, claim/keyphrase generation leans on exemplars.

ideas.idea: Cross-task generalization improves with the number of seen training tasks (meta-training many instructions).
ideas.rationale: Requires fine-tuning on a large multi-task instruction corpus; contradicts Memoria's no-custom-training, off-the-shelf-local-model constraint.

file: Mishra et al. - 2022 - Reframing instructional prompts to GPTk’s language.md
title: Reframing Instructional Prompts to GPTk's Language
one_line: Five manual prompt-reframing techniques (low-level patterns, itemizing, decomposition, restraining/constraints, specialization) that boost LM instruction-following without fine-tuning.

ideas.idea: Itemizing reframing: turn dense paragraph requirements into bulleted lists and convert negations into positive assertions ('don't make unanswerable' -> 'make answerable').
ideas.rationale: Cheap, model-agnostic prompt-engineering directly applicable to Memoria's local extraction/judging prompts on qwen2.5:7b; bulleted positive constraints fix the 'first-instruction bias' and 'negation challenge' that small models suffer most.

ideas.idea: Decomposition reframing: break implicit multi-step tasks into sequential sub-prompts.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's engine-pipeline design — multi-step extraction (e.g. claim -> uncertainty -> relation) should be separate prompted steps, which the paper shows materially raises quality on weaker models.

ideas.idea: Restraining reframing: append an explicit output constraint to fight conventional-task bias (e.g. force answer-TYPE output instead of answering the question).
ideas.rationale: Memoria needs strict structured outputs (claim objects, MASSW aspects, JSON for forms); appending explicit output-format/type constraints is a concrete lever to keep cheap models on-format and reduce review-gate rejections.

ideas.idea: Specialization reframing: drop generic/motivational text and state the concrete low-level task directly.
ideas.rationale: Token-efficient and improves local-model accuracy — Memoria's compiled profiles should strip boilerplate and speak directly to the engine's job.

ideas.idea: Reframed prompts transfer across model sizes/architectures (unlike per-model fine-tuning) and can rival a 200x-larger supervised model.
ideas.rationale: Validates investing in prompt quality over model swaps — Memoria's default/fallback model split (qwen2.5:7b vs qwen3:14b) benefits because well-reframed prompts hold up across both.

ideas.idea: Length reduction is not the primary driver of gains — structure/concreteness matters more than brevity.
ideas.rationale: Guards against a naive 'shorten everything' optimization in Memoria prompt authoring; restructure for concreteness, not just token count.

file: Mitchell et al. - 2022 - Enhancing self-consistency and performance of pre-trained language models through natural language i.md
title: Enhancing Self-Consistency and Performance of Pre-Trained Language Models through Natural Language Inference (ConCoRD)
one_line: ConCoRD boosts consistency/accuracy of frozen QA models by using a pre-trained NLI model to estimate pairwise entailment/contradiction between candidate answers and resolving them with a MaxSAT factor graph — no fine-tuning.

ideas.idea: Use a pre-trained NLI model to DYNAMICALLY estimate entailment/contradiction/equivalence relations between statements, rather than maintaining a hand-written constraint set.
ideas.rationale: This is the engine Memoria's contradictions dashboard and typed-relation extraction need: run an off-the-shelf NLI model over pairs of atomic claims to auto-detect contradiction/entailment, replacing manual constraint curation at single-researcher scale.

ideas.idea: Convert (question,answer) pairs into declarative statements before NLI, via a small fine-tuned converter, so inputs match the NLI model's training distribution.
ideas.rationale: Memoria already extracts atomic claims as declarative sentences — they are NLI-ready, so the converter step is mostly unneeded, but the insight (normalize to declaratives before relation detection) is worth preserving for claim-pair comparison.

ideas.idea: Factor-graph + weighted MaxSAT inference combining base-model confidence with NLI relation confidence to pick a globally self-consistent answer set.
ideas.rationale: Memoria could use the same machinery to resolve a self-consistent set of accepted claims given pairwise contradiction edges and per-claim confidence — but as an analysis/triage signal surfaced to the gate, not an autonomous overwrite, given 'PI approves' governance. Solve times <20ms fit single-researcher local scale.

ideas.idea: Context-statement injection: constrain a context node true and let NLI relate new evidence to existing beliefs, enabling test-time 'model editing' / belief update.
ideas.rationale: Directly maps to Memoria's claim supersession over time — when a new paper's claim contradicts/updates an existing one, NLI-detected relations against the new evidence can flag supersession candidates for the review gate.

ideas.idea: Detecting and filtering NLI relations can outperform rigid a-priori gold constraints, and entailment+contradiction together beat either alone.
ideas.rationale: Supports building Memoria's contradiction detection on estimated relations with a confidence threshold (lambda) rather than a brittle fixed rule base; use both relation types for the argument graph.

ideas.idea: Best NLI model varies by domain and needs hyperparameter (beta, lambda) tuning per problem.
ideas.rationale: Flags an operational cost: Memoria would need a small validation set of claim pairs to pick/tune an NLI checkpoint for the scientific domain — acceptable one-time setup, but a caveat for the deterministic-ingest goal.

file: Ding et al. - 2021 - OpenPrompt an open-source framework for prompt-learning.md
title: OpenPrompt: An Open-source Framework for Prompt-learning
one_line: A modular PyTorch toolkit (Template + Verbalizer + PromptModel) that unifies prompt-learning over masked/LM/seq2seq PLMs, with a dict-style template language and pluggable verbalizers.

ideas.idea: Verbalizer abstraction: map a class label to a SET of label words and aggregate their token logits into a class score (with per-word importance weights / KnowledgeableVerbalizer)
ideas.rationale: Memoria's claim-sentence classification and MASSW aspect tagging run on a local qwen2.5:7b. A verbalizer-style label-word mapping is a cheap, deterministic way to do few-shot/zero-shot classification on small local models without training a head — useful for the pre-file similarity gate and tag-candidate scoring where labeled data is scarce.

ideas.idea: Template language with hard/soft tokens, shareable embeddings, post-processing, and shortenable/non-truncatable spans
ideas.rationale: Soft-prompt embedding training and BERT-era template engineering assume a trainable PLM pipeline; Memoria uses frozen local instruction models via MCP and plain natural-language prompting. The mechanism is heavier than Memoria's deterministic-ingest constraint warrants.

ideas.idea: Calibration of prompt predictions (calibrate-before-use) to correct label-word bias
ideas.rationale: If Memoria ever does logit-based local classification (claim type, contradiction polarity), calibrating against a content-free baseline reduces majority-label bias cheaply — a small reliability win for the engines feeding the structural review gate.

ideas.idea: Framework combinability / one-click trainer with FewshotSampler
ideas.rationale: This is research-tooling for comparing prompt methods at scale; Memoria is a single-researcher product, not a prompt-method benchmark harness.

file: Ding et al. - 2021 - Prompt-learning for fine-grained entity typing.md
title: Prompt-Learning for Fine-Grained Entity Typing
one_line: Reformulates fine-grained entity typing as a cloze task ('In this sentence, [Ent] is a [MASK]') with entity-oriented verbalizers, plus a self-supervised distribution-matching strategy for zero-shot typing.

ideas.idea: Cloze-style entity typing: copy the marked mention into a declarative template and read the [MASK] distribution to assign a type — strongly outperforms a fine-tuned classifier in 1-2 shot regimes
ideas.rationale: Memoria does record-linkage entity dedup and typed relations over a single PI's vault — inherently a low-data regime. The cloze framing lets a local model type entities/claims with zero or few labels, matching Memoria's 'cheap/local model, no training' constraint better than a supervised head.

ideas.idea: Expand the label-word set per type with related nouns (e.g. CITY -> {city, metropolis, town, municipality}) to enrich the verbalizer
ideas.rationale: Cheap precision boost for Memoria's keyphrase tag candidates and entity-type assignment: synonym-expanded label words give a richer match surface without any training, and the related-words list can be precomputed deterministically at ingest.

ideas.idea: Self-supervised zero-shot typing: minimize Jensen-Shannon divergence between predicted label-word distributions of same-entity sentence pairs (positive) vs different-entity pairs (negative), restricting prediction to the label vocabulary
ideas.rationale: Requires a ~1M-pair entity-linked Wikipedia training run and gradient updates to the encoder — violates Memoria's deterministic-ingest, frozen-local-model, single-researcher-scale constraints. The insight (same entity -> similar type) is already implicit in record-linkage dedup.

ideas.idea: Templates matter: small wording changes ('in this sentence') and number of soft tokens shift accuracy substantially
ideas.rationale: Reinforces that Memoria's extraction prompts (claims, aspects, relations) need versioned, evaluated prompt templates — supports the compiled-profile / prompt-governance discipline rather than ad-hoc prompts.

file: Dohan et al. - 2022 - Language model cascades.md
title: Language Model Cascades
one_line: Position paper framing chain-of-thought, verifiers, STaR, selection-inference, and tool-use as a single probabilistic-program abstraction ('cascades') over string-valued random variables with sample/observe semantics.

ideas.idea: Verifier model: a separate LLM judges whether a thought/answer is valid, used to rank K sampled candidates and reject invalid ones (ancestral sampling + verifier filtering)
ideas.rationale: This is exactly Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' posture expressed as inference: the extraction engine proposes claims/relations, a verifier agent scores/rejects them before the structural review gate. Sample-then-verify is a directly buildable pattern for raising extraction faithfulness.

ideas.idea: Natural-language critique as verifier (Scheurer/Saunders): the verifier emits a critique string rather than a 0/1 label, which is more sample-efficient
ideas.rationale: Memoria's contradiction dashboard and claim-supersession judgments benefit from a critique-string verifier whose output is itself human-readable and attachable to the note — fits the read-only inspector and gives the PI an auditable reason, not just a score.

ideas.idea: Selection-Inference: split reasoning into a selection module (pick a subset of facts) then an inference module (derive a new fact), iterated for multi-step reasoning
ideas.rationale: Maps onto Memoria's argument graph with warrants: select supporting claims, then infer the warrant/entailment. A select-then-infer decomposition makes the reasoning trace inspectable and each step attributable, which the structural review gate needs.

ideas.idea: Cascades-as-probabilistic-programs with control flow, dynamic halting (should_stop random variable), and tool-use written directly into the program
ideas.rationale: Useful conceptual framing for Hermes's nightly discovery/triage loops as composable, observable string-valued programs, but full PPL/posterior-inference machinery is overkill for a single-researcher deterministic pipeline; adopt the composition pattern, not the inference engine.

ideas.idea: STaR-style bootstrapping: fine-tune on self-generated rationales filtered by correct answers (stochastic EM)
ideas.rationale: Requires fine-tuning loops and a correctness oracle Memoria does not have at single-researcher scale; contradicts the frozen-local-model and deterministic-ingest constraints.

file: Domingos - 2012 - A few useful things to know about machine learning.md
title: A Few Useful Things to Know About Machine Learning
one_line: Classic folk-wisdom essay distilling 12 lessons of applied ML: learning = representation + evaluation + optimization, generalization vs overfitting, curse of dimensionality, feature engineering, ensembles, and 'more data beats a cleverer algorithm'.

ideas.idea: It's generalization that counts: never tune/test on training data; hold out a final test set and use cross-validation, beware multiple-testing inflation (false discovery rate)
ideas.rationale: Directly applicable to Memoria's learning-to-rank triage and discovery relevance scoring: any threshold/ranker tuned on the PI's accept/reject feedback must be evaluated on held-out feedback, and the multiple-testing caution guards against over-fitting the relevance model to a small single-user signal.

ideas.idea: Feature engineering is the key; the most useful learners let you state domain knowledge explicitly and produce human-understandable output (rule sets)
ideas.rationale: Argues for Memoria's preference for interpretable, inspectable scoring (keyphrase/aspect features, transparent triage rules) over opaque models — consistent with faithfulness-over-flash and the read-only inspector surfacing why a paper was triaged.

ideas.idea: Bias-variance / 'strong false assumptions can beat weak true ones' and 'simplicity does not imply accuracy'; prefer simple learners first
ideas.rationale: Supports starting Memoria's triage/dedup with simple deterministic baselines (naive scoring, k-NN similarity gate) before any heavier model, given the tiny single-researcher data regime where variance dominates.

ideas.idea: Ensembles (bagging/boosting/stacking) reliably beat single models
ideas.rationale: Ensembling many trained learners adds operational complexity disproportionate to a single-user vault; Memoria's gains come from better features/prompts and a verifier, not from learner ensembles.

ideas.idea: Correlation is not causation, but is a guide to further investigation
ideas.rationale: Reinforces how Memoria should label discovered links/relations: a co-occurrence or similarity edge is a candidate (typed relation suggestion) for PI review, not an asserted causal/warrant claim — fits the contradictions/argument-graph governance.

file: Dong et al. - 2026 - EpiBench benchmarking multi-turn research workflows for multimodal agents.md
title: EpiBench: Benchmarking Multi-turn Research Workflows for Multimodal Agents
one_line: An episodic multi-turn benchmark that scores research agents on proactive citation-guided paper search, cross-paper figure/table evidence fusion, and memory-based evidence reuse — with process-level metrics (evidence correctness, minimality gap, typed failure attribution) showing the best model hits only 29% on the hard split.

ideas.idea: Process-level evaluation with annotated required-evidence units (paper-id + figure/table-id): score Evidence Correctness = fraction of required evidence actually accessed on correctly-answered turns, catching 'right answer for wrong reason'
ideas.rationale: Memoria's extraction engines and discovery loop need exactly this: every extracted claim/relation should be scored not just on output correctness but on whether it cites the required source span. Provenance-based scoring is buildable as a gate metric ('is this claim grounded in the cited evidence?') feeding the structural review gate.

ideas.idea: Typed failure attribution to the EARLIEST point of deviation: Retrieve / Perception / Reasoning / Reading-Memory / Other
ideas.rationale: A clean diagnostic taxonomy for Memoria's pipeline failures (discovery-retrieve vs parse-perception vs claim-reasoning vs supersession/memory-reuse), and it dovetails with the existing content-light diagnostics plane. Attributing to the first failure prevents masking root causes in the admin HTML reports.

ideas.idea: Minimality gap: compare agent tool-calls against a minimal-viable checklist to measure redundant tool usage
ideas.rationale: Useful efficiency metric for Hermes's nightly loops — flag over-browsing/redundant MCP calls against a known-minimal path. Adapt as a cost guardrail, since cheap-local-model budget matters at single-researcher scale.

ideas.idea: Evidence memory as an episode-level chronological store, with a memory-only final turn (tools disabled) to test whether previously acquired evidence is correctly reused
ideas.rationale: This is Memoria's core thesis — accumulated, reusable knowledge — and the ablation finding (allowing re-browse on the final turn sharply recovers accuracy => memory reuse is the bottleneck) is direct evidence that a well-structured evidence store with indexed provenance is the high-value component to build, not just retrieval.

ideas.idea: Proactive citation-guided search from indirect hints (author/venue/semantic description) rather than a known target identifier
ideas.rationale: Exactly Memoria's nightly proactive discovery loop and retriever/scout profile: resolve papers from citation cues, not given IDs. The benchmark validates this as a distinct, hard capability worth its own engine and scoring.

ideas.idea: GPT-5.2-as-judge with a reliability study (Cohen's kappa vs Gemini and human judges) showing near-perfect agreement
ideas.rationale: LLM-as-judge for binary correctness is usable for Memoria's offline extraction-quality evals, but the perfect-kappa claim warrants caution; borrow the practice of validating the judge against a human-scored subset before trusting it, consistent with 'verify the inference, not just the file'.

ideas.idea: Benchmark-construction pipeline: GPT-5.2 drafts episodes then 5 PhD annotators heavily rewrite (94% of hard episodes substantially revised) to avoid model-phrasing bias
ideas.rationale: If Memoria builds its own internal eval set for extraction/triage quality, this 'machine-draft, human-curate, measure revision intensity' protocol is a sound, single-PI-scalable template; the heavy-revision finding is a caution against trusting raw LLM-generated gold.

ideas.idea: Multimodal cross-figure/table integration as a first-class research capability
ideas.rationale: Memoria's local deterministic ingest currently centers on text/claim extraction via Zotero/citekey; full multimodal figure-value reading is out of scope for the current architecture and local-model constraints, though it flags a future direction.

file: Shneiderman and Maes - 1997 - Direct manipulation vs. interface agents.md
title: Direct Manipulation vs. Interface Agents (Shneiderman-Maes debate)
one_line: A transcribed 1997 IUI/CHI debate between Shneiderman (direct manipulation: visible, predictable, controllable, user-responsible interfaces) and Maes (personalized proactive software agents that delegate tasks), converging on the view that agents and direct manipulation are complementary, with the central challenge being user understanding and control.

ideas.idea: Direct manipulation principles: rapid, incremental, reversible actions; selection by pointing; immediate (~100ms) feedback; overview-first, zoom, filter, details-on-demand (the visual information-seeking mantra).
ideas.rationale: This is the exact design grammar Memoria's Obsidian surfaces should follow: dashboards, contradictions board, projected canvas, inspector should give an overview, let the PI filter/zoom, and reveal claim/relation details on demand with immediate feedback. Faithfulness-over-flash maps directly onto predictable, reversible UI.

ideas.idea: The agent should run 'below the table' (collaborative filtering / adaptation) while the user-facing layer stays predictable and controllable; adaptivity is fine only where it does not interfere with the user's prediction of behavior.
ideas.rationale: This is precisely Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' split: discovery/ranking/clustering engines adapt under the hood, but the vault surface the PI sees stays deterministic and reviewable. Validates keeping LTR triage and relevance scoring as background processes feeding a stable gate UI.

ideas.idea: Agents must expose the user model ('make the user model available to the user'), provide explanations, and keep methods of operation understandable; understanding + control are the twin requirements for trust.
ideas.rationale: Directly supports Memoria's read-only inspector and the structural review gate: the PI must be able to see WHY a note/relation/discovery was proposed (the agent's 'user model' = the relevance/triage features and warrants). Argues for surfacing scoring rationale, not just verdicts.

ideas.idea: Preserve user responsibility; anthropomorphic/autonomous-agent framing undermines accountability and misleads designers into neglecting the interface.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces the PI-as-final-authority governance model and the MCP-only sandbox: agents are tools that propose, the PI owns decisions. Justifies never auto-writing notes and never personifying Hermes.

ideas.idea: Proactive 'extra eyes/ears' agents (Letizia look-ahead, Remembrance Agent reminding of related prior items) that surface relevant material the user forgot to ask about.
ideas.rationale: This is conceptually Memoria's nightly proactive discovery loop and pre-file similarity gate (surfacing related claims/notes). Borrow the 'extra eyes' framing but reject the always-on autonomous-watcher implementation; Memoria batches it into a reviewable nightly loop, not live keystroke monitoring.

ideas.idea: Insist on scientific evaluation of interfaces (real users, real tasks, measured performance/error/satisfaction) over 'friendlier/more intuitive' rhetoric.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is single-user, so formal QUIS-style studies don't apply, but the discipline of measuring whether a surface actually reduces the PI's errors/time (vs. assuming it helps) should inform how new spaces/dashboards are justified rather than added on intuition.

ideas.idea: Anthropomorphic/chatty/personified agent characters as the interaction model; natural-language-command and speech interfaces as the future.
ideas.rationale: Off-target for Memoria: the PI works in Obsidian markdown with structured capture forms and direct manipulation, not a personified assistant or voice. Speech's cognitive-load argument is irrelevant to a local vault workflow.

ideas.idea: Collaborative filtering / cross-user agent matchmaking (Yenta, Firefly, Kasbah marketplace agents).
ideas.rationale: Inherently multi-user; Memoria is explicitly single-researcher scale. No cross-user recommendation or agent-to-agent negotiation applies.

file: Si et al. - 2023 - Prompting GPT-3 to be reliable.md
title: Prompting GPT-3 to be Reliable
one_line: A systematic study of four reliability facets of LLM prompting (generalizability, social bias, calibration, factuality/knowledge-updating) showing simple prompting strategies, including retrieval augmentation, in-context knowledge updating, calibrated confidence from LM probability/self-consistency, and human question decomposition, make a prompted LLM more reliable than smaller supervised models.

ideas.idea: In-context knowledge updating: when a prompt supplies a passage conflicting with the model's memorized fact, the model adopts the updated answer ~85% of the time (vs ~33% for supervised T5), and larger models update better.
ideas.rationale: Core evidence for Memoria's claim supersession / knowledge-updating design: a retrieved newer claim placed in the prompt can override the engine's stale belief. Justifies feeding superseding/contradicting claims into the extraction/judgment prompt so updated knowledge wins, and flagging the conflict rather than silently trusting parametric memory.

ideas.idea: Retrieval-augmented prompting (Contriever top-k passages prepended) consistently boosts factual QA, and better retrieval recall yields better answers.
ideas.rationale: Directly validates Memoria's retriever/scout profile and RAG-style grounding: extraction and any answering over the vault should be grounded in retrieved claims/notes, and investment in retriever recall pays off in faithfulness. Local qwen2.5:7b benefits from this even more than GPT-3 (smaller models gain more).

ideas.idea: Calibration via LM probability or self-consistency frequency, used for selective prediction: rank predictions by confidence, auto-trust the top, route the rest to human verification.
ideas.rationale: This is the mechanism for Memoria's uncertainty flags and the structural review gate: tag each extracted claim with a confidence score (token-logprob or self-consistency among samples), auto-accept high-confidence, queue low-confidence for PI review. Turns the gate from binary into a triaged worklist.

ideas.idea: Increasing the number of demos improves accuracy but NOT calibration; few-shot prompting is more robustly calibrated than supervised models and stable across OOD/domain shift.
ideas.rationale: Useful design caution for Memoria's ingest: don't assume more in-context examples make confidence scores more trustworthy. Keep calibration measurement (Brier/ECE) separate from accuracy tuning when validating the local extraction model.

ideas.idea: Human-written question decomposition injected into the prompt corrects the model's reasoning chain on multi-hop questions far better than self-generated CoT.
ideas.rationale: For Memoria's argument graph / warrant construction, a PI- or template-supplied decomposition (sub-claims, intermediate steps) can steer the engine more reliably than free-form chain-of-thought. Fits the structured-capture-forms philosophy.

ideas.idea: Bias mitigation via demographic-balanced demonstration sampling, random demo shuffling, and natural-language fairness instructions.
ideas.rationale: Memoria has no demographic/fairness task, but the underlying mechanism, the demonstration set's distribution biases the output and a natural-language instruction can steer it, transfers to in-context-example selection for claim classification (avoid skewing the extractor toward one claim-type by unbalanced demos).

ideas.idea: Entity-swap counterfactual evaluation methodology for measuring memorization-vs-updating.
ideas.rationale: A concrete eval recipe Memoria could adapt to test whether its local model honors superseding claims over parametric memory before relying on the knowledge-updating pipeline; relevant to the L5 quality testing track.

file: Somasekharan et al. - 2026 - SCICONVBENCH benchmarking LLMs on multi-turn clarification for task formulation in computational sc.md
title: SciConvBench: Benchmarking LLMs on Multi-Turn Clarification for Task Formulation in Computational Science
one_line: A benchmark for multi-turn clarification in scientific task formulation across four computational-science domains, separating final-correctness from conversation-grounded resolution to expose that frontier LLMs frequently make silent, unaudited assumptions ('silent resolution') instead of explicitly surfacing missing or contradictory requirements.

ideas.idea: Distinguish Final Resolution Rate (got the right answer) from Conversation-Grounded Resolution Rate (resolved it via explicit clarification), with Silent Resolution Rate (correct but never surfaced) as a distinct failure mode that is a reproducibility/auditability risk.
ideas.rationale: This is a sharp governance metric for Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge' pipeline: an extraction/judgment engine that silently fills in a missing field (e.g., guesses a relation type or an uncertainty flag) is a silent repair the PI cannot audit. Memoria should require engines to flag assumptions/low-confidence rather than silently resolve, and the gate should distinguish 'resolved + flagged' from 'resolved silently'.

ideas.idea: Detecting and surfacing internal inconsistencies (CONFLICT) vs. eliciting missing information (MISSING) are separable capabilities; models are better at the former but still under-flag.
ideas.rationale: Maps onto Memoria's contradictions dashboard and uncertainty flags: contradiction-detection (CONFLICT) and gap-detection (MISSING extraction field) are different engine jobs and should be measured/tuned separately. Validates having an explicit contradictions surface plus a 'missing/underspecified' flag class on claims.

ideas.idea: Rubric-based LLM-judge evaluation supplied with per-case planted-issue annotations and required evidence for grounding, validated against human raters and shown robust across judge model, prompt paraphrase, and user-simulator choice.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's L5 quality track can borrow this: judge engine outputs with an explicit rubric (per-claim resolution criteria + required evidence) rather than string match, and run robustness ablations (swap the local judge model, paraphrase the judge prompt) to ensure verdicts aren't artifacts of one model. Fits 'agents judge' but with checked judges.

ideas.idea: A structured task ontology (objective, geometry, physics, properties, BCs, ICs, numerics, outputs, tool settings) against which missing/conflicting components are tagged, giving component-level breakdowns of where formulation breaks.
ideas.rationale: The ontology-component idea transfers to Memoria's paper-aspect / claim schema: tag which structured field (e.g., method, dataset, finding) is missing or contradicted, enabling component-level quality readouts of the extractor rather than a single pass/fail.

ideas.idea: Upstream clarification dialogue: an assistant should ask the user one question per turn to refine an ill-posed request before acting.
ideas.rationale: Partially relevant: Memoria favors structured capture forms over conversational turn-taking, but the principle, surface missing requirements to the PI before the engine commits to a note, supports a 'needs PI input' state in the gate. Borrow the elicit-before-commit principle, not the chat UI.

ideas.idea: Domain-specific clarification (fluid/solid mechanics, materials, PDEs) requiring deep physics grounding; computational-science task formulation.
ideas.rationale: Off-domain for Memoria, a general personal-research knowledge system, not a CFD/FEA assistant. The specific domains and simulator-tool framing don't apply.

file: Strobelt et al. - 2022 - Interactive and visual prompt engineering for ad-hoc task adaptation with large language models.md
title: Interactive and Visual Prompt Engineering for Ad-hoc Task Adaptation with Large Language Models (PromptIDE)
one_line: PromptIDE, a visual IDE that lets a domain expert craft, combinatorially vary, evaluate on small-then-large data, and export prompt templates plus answer-choice verbalizers for zero-shot ad-hoc NLP tasks, grounding prompt choices in quantitative task metrics with progressive visualization.

ideas.idea: Answer-choice / verbalizer engineering: the wording mapping model outputs back to labels matters as much as the template; inspecting the top-5 generated tokens per class reveals that a better label word (e.g., 'Science' vs 'Technology') boosts accuracy.
ideas.rationale: Directly applicable to Memoria's claim-sentence classification and keyphrase/tag candidate engines: the label verbalizers fed to qwen2.5:7b (e.g., claim-type names, relation-type names, uncertainty labels) should be empirically chosen by inspecting which tokens the local model actually emits, not assumed. A cheap, high-leverage tuning step for deterministic ingest.

ideas.idea: Small-data-first then large-data workflow: iterate prompts interactively on a handful of examples (early stopping allowed), then validate promising candidates on a larger held-out set run to completion.
ideas.rationale: A practical methodology for developing Memoria's extraction/classification prompts on the local model: prototype on a few vault papers, then validate on a larger curated set before wiring into the deterministic pipeline. Fits the L5 quality track and 'faithfulness over flash'.

ideas.idea: Confusion matrix + per-example confidence bar (predicted vs ground-truth probability, 'was it a coin flip?') to diagnose where a prompt confuses classes.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's admin static-HTML reports could include a confusion view for the claim-classifier and a per-claim confidence bar, giving the PI/maintainer a read-only diagnostic of where extraction is shaky, complementing the uncertainty flags. Reuses the calibration idea from the Si paper visually.

ideas.idea: Export the validated prompt as a portable JSON artifact ('prompt cart') for deployment, decoupling exploration from production runs.
ideas.rationale: Aligns with Memoria's 'profiles compiled' approach: prompt templates and verbalizers should be versioned artifacts (compiled into the engine config), separating the tuning environment from the deterministic runtime. Borrow the artifact-export discipline.

ideas.idea: Choosing a templating language (Jinja) over a general programming language for prompts, explicitly to avoid server-side code-execution attack surface.
ideas.rationale: Security rationale resonates with Memoria's MCP-only, no-code-execution sandbox: prompt definitions should be declarative templates, never executable code, removing an injection/attack surface. Reinforces ADR-32/46 constraints.

ideas.idea: A full interactive GUI tool (notebook-style sections, shopping cart, progressive client/server visualization) as the primary prompt-engineering surface.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is single-researcher on Obsidian; building a separate web prompt-IDE is out of scope and violates the 'PI-accessible directly from Obsidian' constraint. The techniques (verbalizer inspection, small-then-large eval) are adopted, but not the standalone heavyweight tool.

ideas.idea: Community shared prompt repository (PromptSource read-only cart) for inspiration.
ideas.rationale: Multi-user/community sharing has no place in a single-PI local system; Memoria's prompts are private compiled artifacts.

file: Su et al. - 2022 - Selective annotation makes language models better few-shot learners.md
title: Selective Annotation Makes Language Models Better Few-Shot Learners (vote-k)
one_line: A two-step in-context-learning framework, graph-based vote-k selective annotation (pick diverse + representative examples to label once) followed by similarity-based prompt retrieval per test instance, that matches supervised finetuning with 10-100x less annotation and is robust to small budgets and domain shift.

ideas.idea: vote-k selective annotation: build a kNN similarity graph over Sentence-BERT embeddings, greedily pick high-(discounted)-degree nodes to maximize representativeness while penalizing closeness to already-picked nodes for diversity, then diversify further by confidence-bucketing.
ideas.rationale: Memoria can use vote-k to choose which vault items the PI labels to seed its extraction/classification demonstration pool, achieving good few-shot performance from a tiny hand-labeled set, exactly the single-researcher, cheap-annotation regime. The graph diversity+representativeness criterion is directly buildable on top of existing Sentence-BERT embeddings.

ideas.idea: Fast vote-k: drop the per-instance confidence-score computation (no LLM forward passes), keep just the graph degree-discount scoring, for a 10x+ speedup with near-equal quality.
ideas.rationale: This is the version Memoria should actually build: it needs no model inference, only embeddings already computed for the similarity gate/retriever, so it runs cheaply on local hardware (RTX 4060 Ti) and fits deterministic ingest. Preferred over full vote-k for the test env.

ideas.idea: Two-step decomposition: a fixed annotated pool selected before test time + per-instance similarity-based prompt retrieval at inference; total annotation cost is the explicit budget to minimize.
ideas.rationale: This is essentially Memoria's retriever/scout architecture stated as an annotation-efficiency principle: maintain a curated labeled pool, retrieve the most-similar exemplars per new paper at extraction time. Validates the pre-file similarity gate and retriever profile as the right structure, and frames PI labeling effort as a budget to economize.

ideas.idea: Similarity-based retrieval is essential; random prompt retrieval fails to benefit from a well-selected pool (the two steps must be combined), and ICL with similarity retrieval is robust to outliers/small pools where finetuning + active learning is not.
