ideas.rationale: Strong justification for grounding every extraction in nearest-neighbor exemplars rather than a fixed prompt, and for preferring in-context retrieval over finetuning the local model, robustness to tiny, noisy pools matches Memoria's reality. Confirms the embedding retriever is load-bearing, not optional.

ideas.idea: Selective annotation's gains grow under domain shift (different users/topics), exactly when deployment data differs from the labeled pool.
ideas.rationale: Reassuring for Memoria: as the PI's research interests drift, a diverse vote-k pool + similarity retrieval degrades gracefully on new topics, important since a personal vault continuously shifts domains. Argues for periodically refreshing the labeled pool with vote-k over new ingest.

ideas.idea: Deterministic selection (vote-k is deterministic given the unlabeled set) reduces variance and improves stability of in-context learning.
ideas.rationale: Determinism aligns with Memoria's 'deterministic ingest' pillar: a deterministic exemplar-selection step keeps extraction reproducible across runs, unlike random demo sampling. Borrow determinism as an explicit requirement for the selection engine.

file: Waldman - 2020 - Cognitive biases, dark patterns, and the ‘privacy paradox’.md
title: Cognitive biases, dark patterns, and the 'privacy paradox'
one_line: An opinion essay arguing the 'privacy paradox' is not user apathy but the predictable result of platforms exploiting cognitive biases (anchoring, framing, hyperbolic discounting, overchoice, metacognition) and dark patterns, proposing an information-fiduciary legal remedy.

ideas.idea: Dark patterns = interface design that coerces/steers/deceives users into choices they wouldn't make if fully informed (Mathur et al. definition).
ideas.rationale: A useful negative design constraint for Memoria's Obsidian surfaces (gates, capture forms, dashboards): the structural review gate and approval flows must NOT use steering/obfuscation against the PI. Memoria's 'faithfulness over flash' and PI-direct-access pillars are the inverse of dark patterns; this paper supplies vocabulary for an anti-pattern checklist, nothing buildable.

ideas.idea: Overchoice / choice overload paralyzes users when too many yes/no decisions are required.
ideas.rationale: Directly relevant to gate/triage UX: the approval gate and discovery triage should batch and rank decisions rather than dump N independent approvals on the single PI, otherwise the PI 'nihilistically' rubber-stamps. Supports the existing learning-to-rank triage and pre-file similarity gate as load-reducers.

ideas.idea: Metacognitive 'difficulty = impossibility' cue makes users cede control to defaults.
ideas.rationale: Argues Memoria's defaults (compiled profiles, structural review gate) must be safe-by-default because the PI will default-accept when a task feels hard; reinforces 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' with conservative auto-behavior, but offers no mechanism.

ideas.idea: Information-fiduciary legal framework (duties of care/confidentiality/loyalty) for data collectors.
ideas.rationale: Legal/policy proposal for multi-user commercial platforms; Memoria is a single-researcher local-vault system with no third-party data collection, so the fiduciary regime is off-domain.

ideas.idea: Notice-and-consent / privacy-policy reading cost is impractical at scale.
ideas.rationale: Concerns consumer web platforms; no Memoria surface relies on notice-and-consent against its own single user.

file: Wallat et al. - 2024 - Correctness is not faithfulness in RAG attributions.md
title: Correctness is not Faithfulness in RAG Attributions
one_line: Disentangles citation 'correctness' (cited doc supports the statement) from citation 'faithfulness' (the model actually used the doc causally), shows RAG models post-rationalize up to 57% of citations, and proposes counterfactual perturbation tests to detect it.

ideas.idea: Citation faithfulness requires a causal dependency between cited document and generated statement, not just NLI-style support (correctness).
ideas.rationale: Core to Memoria's claim-extraction + attribution backbone: when engines attach a citekey/source to an extracted atomic claim, Memoria must not treat NLI/support-match as proof the claim came from that source. The structural review gate should flag 'correct-but-possibly-post-rationalized' attributions for agent judgment before a note is written.

ideas.idea: Post-rationalization: model answers from parametric memory then token-matches a document to cite — correct yet unfaithful, fostering misplaced trust.
ideas.rationale: A concrete failure mode the contradictions/argument-graph layer must guard against. Memoria prefers local/cheap models (qwen2.5:7b) which are MORE prone to this; the warrant in the argument graph should distinguish 'source actually grounds claim' from 'source merely co-mentions the claim token'.

ideas.idea: Counterfactual perturbation test for faithfulness: (1) alter the cited doc's relevant info -> answer/attribution should change; (2) add irrelevant docs -> attribution should not change. Concretely: append a short extracted statement (2-4 tokens) into a random/uncited document and check if the model then cites it.
ideas.rationale: A deterministic, cheap, offline test Memoria's ingest QA can run on its own attributions: take an extracted claim, inject its span into a decoy source, re-run extraction, and if the engine re-cites the decoy, mark the attribution low-confidence. Fits the MCP-only sandbox (no model self-introspection needed, just re-running the engine) and the uncertainty-flag design.

ideas.idea: Desiderata for good citations: correctness, appropriateness (relevant/meaningful), comprehensiveness (covers key points), faithfulness; plus the problem that 'inappropriate' citations re-stating the question skew averaged metrics.
ideas.rationale: A ready-made rubric for Memoria's attribution scoring and the read-only inspector: score each claim-source link on these four axes; down-weight inappropriate citations (e.g., a source cited only because it repeats a title from the query) so dashboard quality metrics aren't inflated.

ideas.idea: Underspecified statements vs underlying claims: the cited span ('tallest') is shorter than the standalone claim ('Emperor penguin: tallest: in the world'); evaluation needs the reconstructed claim.
ideas.rationale: Validates Memoria's atomic-claim extraction (vs surface-span tagging): attributions should bind to the reconstructed standalone claim, not the literal sentence fragment, which is exactly what claim-sentence classification + atomic-claim normalization aim to produce.

ideas.idea: Detecting unfaithfulness rigorously needs model-internal probing / white-box methods (attention, causal interventions).
ideas.rationale: Requires white-box access and probing infrastructure incompatible with Memoria's MCP-only agent sandbox and local cheap-model runtime; the black-box perturbation test above is the adoptable substitute.

file: Wang and Chang - 2022 - Toxicity detection with generative prompt-based inference.md
title: Toxicity Detection with Generative Prompt-based Inference
one_line: Proposes zero-shot generative prompt-based classification — scoring whether an LLM is more likely to generate text x under a positive vs negative prompt p(x|y^p) vs p(x|y^n) — and shows it beats discriminative yes/no prompting for toxicity detection.

ideas.idea: Generative classification: classify by comparing p(x|positive_prompt) vs p(x|negative_prompt) instead of asking a discriminative yes/no question; gives 'improved contrastiveness' and higher zero-shot accuracy.
ideas.rationale: A reusable zero-shot classification trick for Memoria's claim-sentence classification and MASSW aspect tagging where labeled data is scarce. Caveat: requires token-logprob access, which local Ollama models (qwen2.5:7b) expose, so it is technically feasible in the deterministic-ingest engine layer, but it is a marginal improvement over instruction-tuned discriminative prompting on modern models — borrow as a fallback, not a default.

ideas.idea: Zero-shot prompting avoids injecting spurious feature-label correlations that supervised fine-tuning learns from biased labels.
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's preference for prompt-based engines over training task-specific classifiers on small/biased single-PI data; reduces overfitting to one researcher's idiosyncratic labels in keyphrase/aspect tagging.

ideas.idea: Demonstration ensembling: average generative scores conditioned on a few unlabeled in-domain examples to match the target distribution.
ideas.rationale: Cheap domain adaptation for the PI's own corpus without labels — seed the classifier with a few of the PI's own notes/abstracts. Minor, but compatible with local-model + no-training constraints.

ideas.idea: Prompt wording extreme sensitivity; LIME token-attribution to debug which tokens drive predictions; toxicity-lexicon keyword matching is unreliable for implicit content.
ideas.rationale: Toxicity/hate-speech detection is off-domain for a personal research-knowledge system; the LIME interpretability and lexicon findings are tied to that task and add nothing to Memoria's claim/relation pipeline.

file: Wang and Luan - 2026 - PARNESS a paper harness for end-to-end automated scientific research with dynamic workflows, full-t.md
title: PARNESS: A Paper Harness for End-to-End Automated Scientific Research with Dynamic Workflows, Full-Text Indexing, and Cross-Run Knowledge Accumulation
one_line: An open-source thin DAG-kernel framework for autonomous research where pipelines are user-editable YAML (a four-field agent contract), full PDFs are parsed into typed indexed objects, and a typed knowledge graph over papers/ideas/experiments/code persists cross-run with scenario-typed retrieval.

ideas.idea: Scenario-typed retrieval: a knowledge graph with four edge types (structural/internal/semantic/walk) composed into retrieval presets — similar / opposite(contradictory) / cross-domain / counter-intuitive — each feeding a different reasoning role.
ideas.rationale: Directly extends Memoria's typed-relations + contradictions dashboard + discovery loop. Memoria already tracks contradictions and typed relations; PARNESS shows how to turn those edges into retrieval modes — e.g., a 'contradiction' retrieval feeding the contradictions dashboard, a 'cross-domain' walk feeding the nightly proactive discovery loop and scout profile. Buildable on the existing BERTopic/record-linkage substrate.

ideas.idea: Cumulative-corpus over instant-corpus: parse full text once into a permanent typed, queryable index so abstract-only inputs are later resolved against indexed full-text neighbours ('reading capability grows monotonically'); graceful abstract-only fallback.
ideas.rationale: Matches Memoria's deterministic local ingest and Zotero/citekey backbone: parse each paper once into typed objects (sections, tables, claims) with (paper_id, page, bbox) provenance and keep them; when only an abstract is available, resolve it against already-indexed neighbours rather than re-reading. The provenance-anchored evidence model strengthens claim attribution and faithfulness (cf. the Wallat paper).

ideas.idea: Thin DAG kernel + four-field agent contract (_route/_routes/_score/_metadata): the framework knows nothing about domain semantics; all routing/scoring/termination decisions live in modules returning reserved fields.
ideas.rationale: A clean engine/judge separation that mirrors Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge' governance and compiled-profile model. Memoria's Hermes runtime could express ingest/discovery/review stages as declarative wiring with scoring gates, but full programmable YAML pipelines exceed single-PI needs and could undercut the deterministic-ingest + structural-review-gate guarantees — borrow the contract idea (typed module + score-gate), not the open 50-pipeline DSL.

ideas.idea: Paper<->code as a first-class typed graph: derivation edges paper->repo, similarity edges repo<->repo, so a paper without code reaches the nearest sibling repository; code is the ground-truth spec the paper omits.
ideas.rationale: Valuable for a research PI who reproduces methods: Memoria could add a typed 'has_implementation' relation linking a paper note to its repo and surface nearest-sibling repos. But cloning/analyzing repos under a sandbox conflicts with the MCP-only, no-code-execution agent sandbox (ADR-32/46); only a metadata-level link (repo URL extraction + similarity) is adoptable, not the clone-and-analyze agent.

ideas.idea: Cognitive-role multi-agent ideation: six orthogonal roles (Reader/Analyst/Connector/Contrarian/Synthesizer/Critic) each wired to a scenario-typed retrieval slice, fanned out and aggregated.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's retriever/scout and discovery-relevance profiles are single-role; a Contrarian (find contradictions) and Connector (cross-domain) role over the existing typed graph would enrich discovery. But the paper itself admits the benefit is unmeasured ('flag as future work') and token-expensive; for a single-PI local-model budget, borrow 1-2 targeted roles, not a six-agent fan-out.

ideas.idea: Cross-run knowledge persistence in normalized SQLite stores + Neo4j with UNIQUE-constraint dedup at the storage layer (e.g. LOWER(TRIM(seed)) per type) so re-running cannot create duplicate rows.
ideas.rationale: Storage-layer dedup constraints reinforce Memoria's record-linkage entity dedup and pre-file similarity gate as a last-line guard. The architectural pattern (durable typed stores outside the LLM, retrieve a slice per step) is sound, but Memoria's substrate is the Obsidian markdown vault, not Neo4j/SQLite — borrow the dedup-by-normalized-key discipline and the 'retrieve a finite slice, never dump the whole graph' rule.

ideas.idea: Verification as a typed output: an experiment runner emits a result AND a separate verifier artifact, and the pipeline routes on the verifier ('separating ran from passed'); validator with schema/contract/type/topology passes on every pipeline.
ideas.rationale: The 'verifier output is a first-class typed artefact you can route on' mirrors Memoria's structural review gate (separating 'engine produced a note' from 'note passed review'). The layered-validator-in-CI discipline is good practice for Memoria's profile compilation. Borrow the principle; the experiment-running machinery is out of scope for a knowledge vault.

ideas.idea: Self-describing single-file modules (INPUT_SPEC/OUTPUT_SPEC emitted as JSON) so any coding agent can extend the framework with no custom plugin.
ideas.rationale: This is a framework-extensibility ergonomic for external coding agents (Claude Code/Cursor); Memoria's agents are MCP-only sandboxed with no file/code-execution, and skill governance is intentionally gated — an open 'any agent rewrites any module' surface contradicts Memoria's governance pillar.

ideas.idea: End-to-end paper writing (auto-generated draft, citation insertion, LaTeX compile) producing full manuscripts autonomously.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's mandate is knowledge management for one researcher's reading/claims/discovery, not autonomous paper generation; the auto-writing stage (and its self-reported 18% MAE-degraded fabricated result) is off-mission and at odds with 'faithfulness over flash'.

file: Wang et al. - 2022 - Learning to prompt for continual learning.md
title: Learning to Prompt for Continual Learning (L2P)
one_line: Replaces rehearsal buffers in continual learning with a learnable key-value 'prompt pool': an instance-wise query selects task-relevant prompts to instruct a frozen pre-trained model, decoupling shared (task-invariant) from task-specific knowledge without needing task identity at test time.

ideas.idea: Key-value prompt pool with instance-wise top-N query: a frozen feature extractor produces a query, cosine-matched against learnable keys to retrieve a small set of composable prompts.
ideas.rationale: This is a model-weight/training mechanism (learned prompt parameters trained by backprop) for image-classification continual learning. Memoria does not fine-tune or train prompt parameters — it uses frozen local models via MCP with deterministic ingest; there is no training loop to host this.

ideas.idea: Decouple task-invariant (shared) from task-specific knowledge to reduce interference, with a diversified-selection penalty (frequency table) encouraging varied prompt use.
ideas.rationale: The conceptual insight — separate shared/general knowledge from specific/local knowledge to avoid interference — loosely informs how Memoria's relation graph and spaces could segregate cross-cutting concepts from project-specific notes. Purely conceptual; nothing buildable transfers since Memoria has no shared-parameter interference problem.

ideas.idea: Avoid storing raw past data ('rehearsal buffer') for privacy/scale, using a compact learned memory instead.
ideas.rationale: Memoria deliberately DOES retain all source material (the vault is the corpus, single-PI, local, no privacy-from-self constraint); the motivation that drives L2P (can't keep past data) is inverted for Memoria, so the compact-memory tradeoff is irrelevant.

ideas.idea: Use a frozen pre-trained model as a deterministic, parameter-free query encoder for retrieval.
ideas.rationale: Mildly reinforces Memoria's existing embedding-based similarity/retrieval (pre-file similarity gate, BERTopic): using a frozen encoder's [class]/pooled vector as a stable query key is already standard practice Memoria follows; no new mechanism, just confirmation.

file: Bisht et al. - 2026 - Agentic AI scientists are not built for autonomous scientific discovery.md
title: Agentic AI Scientists Are Not Built For Autonomous Scientific Discovery
one_line: A position paper arguing LLM agentic systems cannot do autonomous science (problem selection, missing tacit/failure knowledge, diversity compression, invalid benchmarks) and that they belong in a co-scientist role with human judgment in the loop.

ideas.idea: The 'co-scientist' framing: AI extends human judgment rather than replacing it; highest-quality outputs come from human-AI collaboration, quality declines under full automation (Bianchi et al.).
ideas.rationale: This is precisely Memoria's posture: engines write, agents judge, PI approves. The paper is direct external validation of the structural review gate and the single-PI-in-the-loop design over any 'autonomous research agent' ambition.

ideas.idea: Diversity compression / 'hypothesis hivemind': querying multiple frontier LLMs yields semantically near-identical hypotheses (cosine sim stays high even when diversity is desired); effective epistemic sample size ~1.
ideas.rationale: Cautions against Memoria leaning on LLM-generated hypotheses/relations as if they were independent signal. Argues discovery/relevance scoring and contradiction-finding should be grounded in the corpus and PI's own judgments, not in model consensus; dedupe near-identical agent suggestions before surfacing.

ideas.idea: Failure/tacit knowledge is filtered out of the published corpus; models trained on conclusions cannot learn when to abandon a hypothesis, and anomalies get discarded as noise.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's claim supersession + contradictions dashboard: the system must explicitly record abandoned/superseded claims and negative findings the PI encounters, since they are exactly what the literature omits. Memoria can be a personal store of this otherwise-discarded knowledge.

ideas.idea: Persistent world model: an explicit, mutable epistemic state (e.g. Bayesian network over hypotheses/observations, nodes with posteriors updated by results, new nodes on anomalies) that survives across sessions since LLM context is read-only and non-transferable.
ideas.rationale: Maps onto Memoria's argument graph + claim/relation store as the durable epistemic state outside the model's context window. The Bayesian-posterior-per-node idea is heavier than single-PI scale warrants, but the principle (durable structured belief state, anomalies spawn new nodes/contradictions) is directly applicable.

ideas.idea: Preregistration repository: register machine-readable hypotheses before testing to curb p-hacking/postdiction at machine scale.
ideas.rationale: Aimed at community-scale AI-science integrity and shared schemas; Memoria is single-researcher and does not run automated wet/dry experiments. No multi-party integrity problem to solve.

ideas.idea: McNamara fallacy: AI pushes problem selection toward what is measurable/benchmark-amenable, narrowing the questions asked.
ideas.rationale: A design caution for Memoria's learning-to-rank triage and discovery relevance scoring: optimizing only easily-scored relevance can narrow the PI's intake. Argues for surfacing novelty/under-represented topics (BERTopic clusters) deliberately, not just high-score items.

ideas.idea: Benchmarks lack construct/ecological validity; single-turn accuracy, leakage, no physical feedback loop.
ideas.rationale: About evaluating autonomous-science systems against physical reality; off-domain for a markdown knowledge vault that ingests papers. No buildable mechanism for Memoria.

file: Blackwell et al. - 2001 - Cognitive dimensions of notations design tools for cognitive technology.md
title: Cognitive Dimensions of Notations: Design Tools for Cognitive Technology
one_line: A vocabulary/framework of ~13+ 'cognitive dimensions' (viscosity, visibility, hidden dependencies, premature commitment, etc.) for evaluating how usable a notation or information artifact is for a given user activity.

ideas.idea: Dimension 'viscosity' (resistance to change; repetition vs knock-on) as a usability lens.
ideas.rationale: A concrete evaluation criterion for Memoria's note/claim editing surfaces: when the PI corrects an extracted claim or relation, how many actions does it take, and what knock-on consistency edits cascade? Directly usable as an acceptance check for the structural review gate UX.

ideas.idea: 'Hidden dependencies' — important links between entities not visible (spreadsheet cells, style defs).
ideas.rationale: Memoria's value is typed relations/argument graph; this dimension names the failure mode where changing one claim silently affects dependents. Argues for making relation/supersession dependencies overtly visible in both directions (inspector, contradictions dashboard).

ideas.idea: 'Premature commitment' and 'provisionality' — being forced to order/fix things too early vs. supporting sketchy, not-yet-defined marks.
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's disposable projection engine / projected canvas and structured capture forms: the PI should be able to record provisional, uncertain claims (uncertainty flags) before committing them past the review gate. Design principle, not a feature.

ideas.idea: 'Secondary notation' — extra meaning via colour/layout/comments beyond formal syntax.
ideas.rationale: Justifies letting the PI annotate in Obsidian markdown freely (callouts, tags, colour) outside Memoria's formal claim/relation schema; the system should preserve, not overwrite, this PI secondary notation.

ideas.idea: Dimensions are evaluative only relative to an 'activity profile' (search, incrementation, transcription, modification, exploratory design); no dimension is inherently good/bad.
ideas.rationale: Memoria has distinct PI activities (triage/search vs. building the argument graph vs. exploratory discovery). Profiling surfaces by activity, rather than one-size-fits-all, is a sound design heuristic for the spaces/dashboards.

ideas.idea: Formalisation efforts (CiDa state machines, Empirical Modelling ISMs), workshop governance of who may add dimensions, name-bikeshedding.
ideas.rationale: Meta-level academic process about maintaining the CDs framework itself; no engineering content for Memoria.

file: Blumenthal and Clark - 2001 - Rethinking the design of the internet the end-to-end arguments vs. the brave new world.md
title: Rethinking the Design of the Internet: The End-to-End Arguments vs. the Brave New World
one_line: A policy/architecture essay on how commercialization, untrusted endpoints, ISPs, and third parties pressure the Internet's end-to-end design principle to move function into the network core.

ideas.idea: End-to-end argument: keep function out of the shared core; put application-specific function at the endpoints to preserve generality, simplicity, and the ability to add unanticipated applications.
ideas.rationale: Read as a software-architecture principle, it echoes Memoria's deterministic-ingest-vs-judging-agents split: keep the ingest/engine core simple and general, push application-specific judgment to the edge (agents/PI). A loose design analogy only; the paper itself is off-domain (network policy, multi-party trust).

ideas.idea: Labels on content as a middle point between total visibility and total opacity (PICS, ADV: prefixes, metadata tags) so an intermediary can act without seeing full content.
ideas.rationale: Weakly analogous to Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and structural review labels: attach machine-readable labels/flags (uncertainty, claim-type, supersession status) so downstream surfaces can filter/route notes without re-reading full text. Concept only, not the network mechanism.

ideas.idea: Trusted-third-party / certificate-authority / revocation machinery, traffic analysis, NAT, firewalls, anonymity, wiretap, taxation, multi-party trust models.
ideas.rationale: All assume a multi-party, networked, mutually-distrustful environment. Memoria is single-PI, local, MCP-only sandbox; none of this maps to a personal markdown vault.

ideas.idea: Technical mechanisms behave predictably a priori; legal/social mechanisms act after the fact — pick the layer to place enforcement.
ideas.rationale: Governance-layer policy reasoning irrelevant to a single-user system; Memoria's governance is the review gate, not multi-stakeholder law/markets.

file: Boehner et al. - 2007 - How HCI interprets the probes.md
title: How HCI Interprets the Probes
one_line: A meta-analysis of ~90 HCI papers showing 'cultural probes' get adopted as a recipe that strips their interpretive/subversive methodology, and arguing for distinguishing method from methodology and respecting open (vs closed) interpretation.

ideas.idea: Open vs closed interpretation: closing interpretation to a single 'correct' answer (via inter-rater reliability, follow-up interviews, statistics) destroys the surprise/inspiration the method was designed for.
ideas.rationale: A caution for Memoria's claim extraction and aspect classification: forcing every PI-facing artifact into one canonical interpretation can erase genuinely ambiguous/contested claims. Argues for preserving uncertainty flags and multiple readings rather than always collapsing to a single label.

ideas.idea: Method-without-methodology / 'discount probes': copying surface techniques while dropping the grounding rationale yields hollow results.
ideas.rationale: Relevant to how Memoria borrows techniques from these very papers: adopt the mechanism only with its rationale (e.g. don't bolt on BERTopic clustering or LTR triage without the activity/justification). A reviewing-discipline reminder more than a feature.

ideas.idea: Uncertainty/ambiguity as an asset; idiosyncratic, single-subject data is valid and need not be generalized.
ideas.rationale: Validates Memoria's single-PI, faithfulness-over-flash stance: a personal knowledge system need not produce population-level generalizations; fragmentary, subjective, PI-specific notes are legitimate and should be retained.

ideas.idea: Cultural probes as a concrete data-collection/design-engagement method (packets, postcards, provocative tasks).
ideas.rationale: A design-research field method for engaging human participants; Memoria ingests papers and serves one researcher. No probe-style data collection in scope.

file: Bostock et al. - 2011 - D³ data-driven documents.md
title: D³: Data-Driven Documents
one_line: D3 is a web visualization kernel that binds data directly to the native DOM via selections, data joins (enter/update/exit), and immediate-evaluation transforms, prioritizing representational transparency, debuggability, and standards reuse over a custom scenegraph.

ideas.idea: Representational transparency: operate on the native, inspectable representation (the DOM) rather than a custom intermediate scenegraph, so standard developer tools work and output is directly debuggable.
ideas.rationale: Strong principle for Memoria's surfaces: render dashboards/canvas/inspector over native, inspectable artifacts (Obsidian markdown, .canvas JSON, static HTML) rather than an opaque app abstraction. Keeps everything PI-accessible and debuggable from the Obsidian UI, matching the disposable-projection and read-only-inspector design.

ideas.idea: Data join with enter/update/exit subselections binding data to elements by key, enabling minimal, targeted updates and object constancy across changes.
ideas.rationale: Directly applicable to the disposable projection engine: when re-projecting notes/claims onto a canvas or dashboard, diff against existing elements (enter/update/exit by stable key e.g. citekey/claim-id) instead of regenerating, preserving layout and only touching changed nodes.

ideas.idea: Immediate evaluation over deferred/implicit evaluation — operators apply instantly, moving control flow into user code, avoiding hidden state revealed only on crash.
ideas.rationale: Argues Memoria's projection/rendering steps should be deterministic and eagerly evaluated (no hidden lazy recompute), which aligns with the deterministic-ingest principle and makes projections reproducible and debuggable.

ideas.idea: Compatibility / standards network-effect: reuse widely-known standards (CSS, SVG, Selectors API) instead of a bespoke lexicon to maximize accessibility and offload work to specialized tools.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces building on Obsidian-native formats (markdown, Bases, JSON Canvas) and standard HTML for admin reports rather than a custom UI toolkit; lowers PI learning cost and lets existing Obsidian tooling augment Memoria.

ideas.idea: D3 helper modules (geo projections, force layout, treemap, chord, scales, color brewer) as optional layers atop the kernel.
ideas.rationale: Layout algorithms (force-directed, treemap, hierarchical) are reusable for projecting the argument graph / topic clusters onto a canvas. Adopt the specific layouts as needed; the broader web-visualization framing is mostly off Memoria's local-markdown path.

ideas.idea: Performance benchmarking of SVG vs Flash vs Protovis; browser rendering specifics.
ideas.rationale: Implementation-detail benchmarks of 2011 web tech; irrelevant to a local single-user vault's scale and stack.

file: Abbasian et al. - 2024 - Conversational health agents a personalized LLM-powered agent framework.md
title: Conversational Health Agents: A Personalized LLM-Powered Agent Framework (openCHA)
one_line: openCHA is an open-source LLM agent framework with an orchestrator that plans/executes tasks against external data sources, knowledge bases, and analysis models to produce personalized, grounded healthcare answers.

ideas.idea: Orchestrator with separate Task Planner (plans/reasons) and Response Generator (composes final answer), each swappable to a different LLM/prompting technique
ideas.rationale: Memoria already separates 'engines write, agents judge' roles; the planner/responder split maps onto using a cheap local model for routing/planning and a quality model only for synthesis, which fits the qwen2.5:7b-default constraint.

ideas.idea: Tasks declared with name/chat_name/description/dependencies metadata so the planner LLM knows when and how to invoke each tool
ideas.rationale: A typed, self-describing tool registry is a good pattern for Memoria's MCP-only sandbox where agents must discover capabilities; the 'description = when to use, priority hints' convention is directly reusable for skill governance / profile compilation.

ideas.idea: Data Pipe: intermediate large/multimodal results stored under a unique key, with only the key passed into the planner prompt to dodge token limits
ideas.rationale: Useful for Memoria's deterministic ingest where parsed PDFs/claims exceed context windows; pass handles/citekeys not blobs into the agent. Adapt as a vault-resident artifact store, not in-memory.

ideas.idea: Explainability via a persisted 'previous actions' log the user can query ('name the tasks used')
ideas.rationale: Aligns with Memoria's read-only inspector and structural review gate provenance; recording which engine/tool produced a note supports the audit trail before notes are written.

ideas.idea: Tree-of-Thought / ReAct / Plan-and-Solve as interchangeable planning strategies, with self-consistency as future robustness
ideas.rationale: Worth borrowing as optional reasoning modes for the triage/discovery agent, but heavyweight ToT (3 strategies + pros/cons) is costly on a local 7B model; use sparingly, prefer single-pass for nightly loops.

ideas.idea: Offload computation and sensitive data to external 'AI and Analysis Models' rather than trusting the LLM to compute
ideas.rationale: This is exactly Memoria's deterministic-ingest principle: claim extraction, dedup, BERTopic clustering, similarity gates are engines, not LLM free-text; the paper validates 'reserve the LLM for reasoning/response, never for calculations'.

ideas.idea: Full healthcare/CHA domain stack: EHR/biosignal/wearable ingestion, stress estimation, multilingual translators, empathy/companionship
ideas.rationale: Off-domain for a single-researcher academic-paper knowledge system; patient data, empathy metrics, and clinical multimodality have no place in Memoria.

ideas.idea: Service-based multi-user scalability and third-party API hosting concerns
ideas.rationale: Memoria is explicitly single-PI, local, cheap/open models; cloud service scaling and many-simultaneous-users contradict its constraints.

file: Abbasian et al. - 2024 - Foundation metrics for evaluating effectiveness of healthcare conversations powered by generative AI.md
title: Foundation Metrics for Evaluating Effectiveness of Healthcare Conversations Powered by Generative AI
one_line: A taxonomy of user-centered LLM evaluation metrics (accuracy, trustworthiness, empathy, performance) for healthcare chatbots, arguing intrinsic surface-form metrics like BLEU/ROUGE fail to capture semantics, groundedness, and up-to-dateness.

ideas.idea: BLEU/ROUGE surface-form similarity is inadequate; two semantically equivalent sentences score 0.39/0.13, so n-gram overlap misjudges correctness
ideas.rationale: Directly informs how Memoria should evaluate its own claim-extraction and summarization engines: prefer semantic/grounded judgments (LLM-judge or BERTScore-style) over n-gram match when comparing extracted claims to source sentences.

ideas.idea: Groundedness metric: verify generated statements against factual evidence from reliable references, checking true-causal relations among generated words
ideas.rationale: Maps onto Memoria's atomic-claim + warrant/argument-graph design and the structural review gate; faithfulness-over-flash means every written claim must be traceable to a source passage. A groundedness check is a natural gate criterion.

ideas.idea: Up-to-dateness metric, achieved by integrating retrieval-based models so the latest evidence informs answers
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's claim-supersession / contradictions dashboard and nightly discovery loop; the insight that currency must be measured (not assumed) suggests a 'staleness' flag on claims tied to newer superseding papers.

ideas.idea: Interpretability metric: trace responses back to specific rules, algorithms, or data sources
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's provenance/inspector surfaces; an answer should be decomposable to its source claims/notes, which the typed-relation graph already enables.

ideas.idea: Confounding variables (user type, domain type, task type) that change which metrics matter, plus a per-answer vs per-session scoring distinction
ideas.rationale: Memoria has effectively one user (the PI) so user-type drops out, but task-type conditioning (claim extraction vs triage-ranking vs summarization each need different metrics) is a useful framing for engine evaluation. Per-answer scoring is the right granularity for claim-level checks.

ideas.idea: Empathy/emotional-support/health-literacy metrics and patient-facing trust-building
ideas.rationale: Memoria has no end-user-patient surface; empathy/companionship metrics are irrelevant to a research-knowledge tool.

ideas.idea: FLOP/memory/parameter-count performance metrics and a public comparative leaderboard of competing chatbot models
ideas.rationale: Single-local-deployment system; cross-model leaderboards and FLOP accounting are enterprise/research-competition concerns, not Memoria's. Latency matters but doesn't need this metric machinery.

ideas.idea: Bias/fairness/demographic-representation metrics
ideas.rationale: These target multi-population public-facing models; not applicable to a single PI's private paper vault.

file: Abdallah et al. - 2026 - TEMPO a realistic multi-domain benchmark for temporal reasoning-intensive retrieval.md
title: TEMPO: A Realistic Multi-Domain Benchmark for Temporal Reasoning-Intensive Retrieval
one_line: TEMPO is the first benchmark fusing temporal reasoning with reasoning-intensive retrieval, introducing step-wise retrieval planning, multi-level temporal annotations, and cross-period coverage/precision metrics, and showing current retrievers retrieve temporally incomplete evidence.

ideas.idea: Step-wise retrieval planning: decompose a complex query into sequential steps, each mapped to gold documents targeting specific time periods/aspects (multi-hop)
ideas.rationale: Memoria's discovery/triage and contradiction-detection need exactly this: a query like 'how has claim X been superseded' decomposes into baseline-period and current-period retrieval steps. Query+Step beat Step-Only, so keep the original query as context.

ideas.idea: Temporal Coverage@k and Temporal Precision@k metrics measuring whether top-k results span required baseline AND comparison periods
ideas.rationale: Directly usable to evaluate Memoria's retriever/scout profile and claim-supersession surfacing: a good supersession answer must cover both the old and new claim's time windows, not just the most recent. A coverage metric operationalizes that.

ideas.idea: Passage-level temporal annotations: ISO time scope [ts,te], dominant tense, temporal signals/events, confidence
ideas.rationale: Fits Memoria's deterministic claim/aspect extraction: stamping each atomic claim with an ISO temporal scope enables the contradictions dashboard and supersession ordering to reason about which claim is newer/in-force.

ideas.idea: 10-class temporal reasoning taxonomy (trend/cross-period comparison, event localization, causation analysis, etc.) used to analyze retrieval difficulty by class
ideas.rationale: A lighter subset (trend/cross-period, supersession, causation) can tag PI queries so the triage loop routes hard cross-period questions differently; full 10-class scheme is more than a single vault needs.

ideas.idea: Key finding: temporally incomplete retrieved docs actively HURT generation — no-retrieval (77.3) beat all RAG configs; retrieving wrong temporal evidence is worse than nothing
ideas.rationale: Strong justification for Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and structural review gate: never inject low-confidence/temporally-mismatched evidence into a note. Better to abstain than mislead — directly supports 'faithfulness over flash'.

ideas.idea: Normalized variant (explicit temporal intent tags added to query) dramatically helps reasoning retrievers (+8 NDCG for ReasonIR)
ideas.rationale: Suggests Memoria should annotate queries with structured temporal-intent tags before retrieval rather than hoping the embedder infers them; cheap, deterministic preprocessing that boosts local retrievers.

ideas.idea: LLM-as-judge for temporal relevance and dataset quality scoring (Qwen-72B judge, Cohen's kappa human verification)
ideas.rationale: Memoria can use a local LLM-judge for relevance scoring in the structural review gate, but Qwen-72B is too large for the 16GB test box; adapt to qwen2.5:7b/qwen3:14b and keep human (PI) as the kappa anchor.

ideas.idea: Multi-LLM query reformulation + web search to mine hard negatives (topically similar, temporally mismatched)
ideas.rationale: Hard-negative mining is useful for training/calibrating Memoria's discovery relevance scorer and pre-file similarity gate, but the GPT-4o/Gemini cloud pipeline must be swapped for local models given the local-first constraint.

ideas.idea: Stack Exchange as the corpus / building a large multi-domain public benchmark
ideas.rationale: Memoria's corpus is the PI's Zotero/citekey academic papers, not community Q&A; benchmark-construction at 1.6M-doc scale is not a Memoria deliverable.

file: Abowd - 2012 - What next, ubicomp celebrating an intellectual disappearing act.md
title: What next, Ubicomp? Celebrating an Intellectual Disappearing Act
one_line: A reflective essay arguing ubicomp has succeeded so thoroughly it should 'disappear' as a niche, and that the unfinished challenge is a 'HyperCard for ubicomp' — development tools whose environment matches the run-time experience so designers, not just engineers, can build.

ideas.idea: The design-time/run-time gap: tools should let creators author in an environment that exactly matches the end-user experience ('programming environments using tools that match the run-time')
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's PI-direct-access pillar and the disposable projection engine + projected canvas: the PI should shape/preview knowledge structures in Obsidian's actual surface, not a divorced config/CLI. CLI doesn't count as PI access.

ideas.idea: Lower the barrier so non-experts (designers/domain experts), not only engineers, can build applications — the 'HyperCard' moment
ideas.rationale: Reinforces the requirement that every Memoria feature be operable directly from the Obsidian UI by the single PI (structured capture forms, dashboards/gates) rather than requiring developer tooling.

ideas.idea: 'Living laboratories': build and dogfood in authentic continuous real-world use
ideas.rationale: Matches Memoria's sandbox-vs-production split (memoria-test for dogfooding); validates iterating on a real running vault rather than synthetic demos.

ideas.idea: 'Your noise is my signal': repurpose already-deployed infrastructure for services it wasn't designed for
ideas.rationale: Mirrors Memoria building on Obsidian + native obsidian MCP + Zotero rather than bespoke infrastructure — leverage existing local infra for new knowledge services.

ideas.idea: Crowdsourcing / human computation and a shared family digital assistant; many-computers-per-person and fourth-generation conjoined human-computer vision
ideas.rationale: Multi-person crowdsourcing and shared/family devices contradict Memoria's single-PI, local, privacy-bounded model; the speculative hardware vision is off-scope.

ideas.idea: Dissolving a research community's identity / sociology of the ubicomp field
ideas.rationale: Meta-commentary on a research community; no buildable mechanism for a personal knowledge system.

file: Abowd and Mynatt - 2000 - Charting past, present, and future research in ubiquitous computing.md
title: Charting Past, Present, and Future Research in Ubiquitous Computing
one_line: A landmark survey of ubicomp's three interaction themes (natural interfaces, context-awareness, automated capture and access) that proposes 'everyday computing' — scaling interaction across time with interruption/resumption, time representation, and associative storage.

ideas.idea: Automated capture-and-access: capture live experience cheaply, then derive structure and provide flexible later access/retrieval (Bush's memex / Remembrance Agent lineage)
ideas.rationale: This is Memoria's core loop in HCI ancestry: deterministic ingest captures papers, engines derive atomic claims/aspects, and spaces/dashboards provide access. The capture-then-derive-then-access separation validates Memoria's pipeline staging.

ideas.idea: Derive additional information from raw streams (segmentation, topic-change detection) rather than storing only for direct playback
ideas.rationale: Justifies Memoria's claim extraction, keyphrase tagging, and BERTopic clustering: raw PDF is the stream; the value is in derived structure, not verbatim storage.

ideas.idea: Associative (not strictly hierarchical) information models — reacquire info from multiple viewpoints/contexts, since rigid folder hierarchies decay into a 'morass'
ideas.rationale: Strong endorsement of Memoria's typed-relations + argument graph over folder trees; the observation that email-folder hierarchies fail predicts why a relation/claim graph plus projected views beats static foldering.

ideas.idea: Time as a first-class discriminator and interface element (how recently a paper/claim was seen informs interpretation); time-machine/Lifestreams/Timewarp interfaces
ideas.rationale: Complements TEMPO: Memoria's claim-supersession and a 'recently touched / staleness' signal in spaces benefit from treating time as a navigable axis, not just metadata.

ideas.idea: Interruption-and-resumption: model activity as resumable steps, remind the user of uncompleted actions, support background monitoring at varying intrusiveness
ideas.rationale: Maps to Memoria's gates/dashboards: the PI dips in and out, so surfaces should show pending review items and resumable triage state. The 'levels of intrusiveness' idea fits push vs ambient notification of discovery results.

ideas.idea: Evaluation guidance: build a compelling user-need story, do feasibility studies before heavy engineering, evaluate in authentic long-term use; task-centric usability methods are inappropriate for continuous everyday computing
ideas.rationale: Useful methodology for Memoria's own ADR/dogfooding cadence — validate a feature against the PI's real workflow before building robust infra, and don't over-rely on task-completion metrics for an open-ended research tool.

ideas.idea: Visibility/control of sensing for privacy: indicate when capture/sensing is occurring; keep bits local
ideas.rationale: Aligns with Memoria's MCP-only sandbox and local-first stance; 'keep the bits local' is literally Memoria's deployment posture, and visible-sensing maps to the read-only inspector / provenance surfaces.

ideas.idea: Recognition-error handling, context fusion from multiple sensors, augmented reality, tangible/natural (speech/pen/gesture) interfaces
ideas.rationale: Sensor fusion, AR, and physical-world natural interfaces are off-domain for a markdown-vault paper system; Memoria's 'input' is structured text/PDFs, not noisy real-world signals.

file: White et al. - 2023 - CliniDigest a case study in large language model based large-scale summarization of clinical trial.md
title: CliniDigest: A Case Study in Large Language Model Based Large-Scale Summarization of Clinical Trial Descriptions
one_line: A prompt-engineered GPT-3.5 pipeline that recursively batch-summarizes up to ~85 clinical trial descriptions into a single ~200-word reference-bearing summary while limiting hallucination.

ideas.idea: Cascading/hierarchical batch summarization to circumvent context-window limits (split into batches of 15, summarize each, then summarize the intermediary summaries with a second combine prompt that weights paragraphs by word count)
ideas.rationale: Memoria parses papers locally with small/cheap models (qwen2.5:7b) that have limited effective context; a recursive map-reduce summarize-then-combine pattern with proportional length budgets is directly reusable for digesting many notes/claims into a single space or dashboard summary without exceeding the local model's window.

ideas.idea: Force in-text references/citations into every summary so the model must ground each statement in an inputted source, reducing hallucination
ideas.rationale: Memoria's pillar is faithfulness over flash with a structural review gate; requiring every synthesized sentence to cite a source claim/citekey is exactly the kind of grounding constraint the engines should enforce before agents judge and the PI approves.

ideas.idea: Set temperature=0 plus explainability prompting to minimize hallucination in closed-domain summarization
ideas.rationale: Deterministic ingest is a Memoria constraint; temperature 0 for all extraction/summarization engine calls aligns with the determinism goal and is trivially adoptable.

ideas.idea: Keyword steering ('thesis'/'argument' beat 'summary'/'essay') to force cross-document synthesis instead of per-document enumeration
ideas.rationale: Memoria builds an argument graph with warrants; prompting the synthesis layer to produce an 'argument' rather than a list is a concrete trick to get cross-claim synthesis rather than a flat digest of notes.

ideas.idea: Track source-coverage metric (mean % of input sources actually cited) and length distribution as a quality signal, noting diminishing returns past ~50 inputs
ideas.rationale: A coverage/utilization metric is a cheap, automatable faithfulness check for Memoria's diagnostics plane; the diminishing-returns inflection also tells Memoria to cap how many claims feed one synthesized summary.

ideas.idea: SMOG readability scoring and ROUGE-L F1 against human-written summaries as evaluation
ideas.rationale: Readability for a lay clinical-coordinator audience is off-domain for a single expert PI who reads at research level; ROUGE-L against gold human summaries needs a labeled corpus Memoria does not maintain. Not aligned with Memoria's faithfulness-centric, reference-grounded evaluation.

file: Williams et al. - 2021 - What’s the right customer experience for your brand.md
title: What’s the Right Customer Experience for Your Brand?
one_line: An HBR marketing article arguing brands face a frictionless-vs-memorable customer-experience tradeoff and should pick a strategy by market-share/brand-DNA quadrant.

ideas.idea: Frictionless-vs-memorable tradeoff and a 2x2 strategy matrix (mass-market, convenience, boutique, gravity) keyed on market share
ideas.rationale: Pure consumer-brand marketing strategy with no information, knowledge, retrieval, or NLP content; multi-user commercial framing is the opposite of Memoria's single-researcher scale. Nothing buildable.

ideas.idea: Diminishing-returns / zero-sum frontier when optimizing two goals jointly past a point
ideas.rationale: Generic and not load-bearing; Memoria already handles tradeoffs (e.g., precision vs recall in triage) via concrete ranking models, so this abstract observation adds nothing. Appears to be a course-reading PDF that landed in the corpus by accident.

file: Wong et al. - 2024 - Voice assistants for mental health services designing dialogues with homebound older adults.md
title: Voice Assistants for Mental Health Services: Designing Dialogues with Homebound Older Adults
one_line: A participatory-design study where older adults and caregivers co-wrote VIPA dialogues for mental-health interventions, finding nuanced, context-dependent preferences about anthropomorphism, empathy, autonomy, and consent.

ideas.idea: Context-dependent anthropomorphism: users reject human-like affect ('I'm excited') in task/assessment turns but want empathy/humor in emotional-support turns
ideas.rationale: Maps to Memoria's agent-voice design: the system's agents/dashboards should stay plain and factual for ingest/judge/extraction surfaces (no fake enthusiasm), reserving warmth only where it serves the PI. Reinforces faithfulness-over-flash in UI copy, though only loosely.

ideas.idea: Per-instance consent for data sharing rather than one-time blanket consent; never auto-broadcast or auto-add without asking
ideas.rationale: Echoes Memoria's governance stance: engines propose, agents judge, PI approves at the point of action (the structural review gate before notes are written). Confirms a per-write approval model over blanket automation, though the privacy framing is multi-stakeholder and not Memoria's single-PI case.

ideas.idea: Scaffolding / rapport-building progression from formal-to-friendly over a longitudinal relationship
ideas.rationale: Relational-continuity and companionship design for vulnerable users is off-domain; Memoria serves one expert PI who wants efficient tools, not a relationship with the system.

ideas.idea: Voice/VIPA as the interaction modality, emotion recognition from prosody, caregiver-burden reduction
ideas.rationale: Memoria is an Obsidian-vault + local-agent text system with MCP-only sandboxed agents; voice UI, audio emotion sensing, and caregiver workflows are entirely outside its surface set and constraints.

ideas.idea: Co-design with probing materials (example dialogue variants) to elicit preferences from users who struggle to ideate abstractly
ideas.rationale: A single-PI system does not run participatory-design sessions; the PI directly configures features from the Obsidian UI. Methodology not transferable.

file: Workman et al. - 2024 - ChatGPT-4 extraction of heart failure symptoms and signs from electronic health records.md
title: ChatGPT-4 Extraction of Heart Failure Symptoms and Signs from Electronic Health Records
one_line: A clinical-NLP study showing LLM zero-shot learning with a simple yes/no prompt beats trained ML and rule-based baselines for symptom/sign extraction, and that a basic prompt plus temperature 0 outperforms a more elaborate prefix prompt.

ideas.idea: Zero-shot LLM extraction beats supervised ML + rule-based baselines without any task-specific annotation (95% F1 vs 65.5%)
ideas.rationale: Directly validates Memoria's deterministic local-LLM ingest for atomic-claim and aspect extraction without building/maintaining annotated training sets — a single PI cannot annotate corpora, so annotation-free zero-shot extraction is the right architecture.

ideas.idea: Constrain output to a strict binary 'yes/no' to fulfill the classification task AND suppress tangential/hallucinatory narrative
ideas.rationale: Memoria does claim-sentence classification and uncertainty flagging; constraining the engine to a closed label set (yes/no/uncertain) is a concrete, deterministic-friendly way to cut hallucination and keep outputs parseable by the structural gate.

ideas.idea: Surprising finding that a generic/basic prompt beat the elaborate engineered prefix prompt for a simple extraction task
ideas.rationale: Cautions Memoria against over-engineering extraction prompts; favors simple prompts on cheap local models, which suits the qwen2.5:7b default and reduces brittleness in the deterministic ingest path.

ideas.idea: Keep an explicit 'uncertain' annotation class and exclude/flag ambiguous instances rather than forcing a positive/negative call
ideas.rationale: Memoria already extracts claims WITH uncertainty flags; this paper's three-way (yes/no/uncertain) annotation and consensus-on-disagreement workflow is a direct template for the uncertainty-flag field.

ideas.idea: Synthetic-snippet generation (semantically equivalent paraphrases preserving keyword + clinical context) to evaluate when real data can't leave a secure boundary
ideas.rationale: Useful for Memoria's local testing: generate synthetic paper snippets to validate extraction engines without exposing private vault content to any cloud model, aligning with the local/sandboxed constraint.

ideas.idea: Lower temperature improves extraction performance across prompt styles
ideas.rationale: Reinforces deterministic ingest — run extraction at temperature 0; trivially adoptable and consistent with Memoria's determinism pillar.

ideas.idea: Use a commercial cloud LLM (ChatGPT-4) as the extractor, with HIPAA-driven synthetic-data workaround
ideas.rationale: Memoria mandates local + cheap/open models and an MCP-only sandbox; relying on a proprietary cloud API contradicts its core constraints. Adopt the zero-shot technique, reject the cloud dependency.

file: Workshop et al. - 2023 - BLOOM a 176B-parameter open-access multilingual language model.md
title: BLOOM: A 176B-Parameter Open-Access Multilingual Language Model
one_line: The BigScience collaboration's release of BLOOM, a 176B open-access multilingual decoder-only LLM, documenting its corpus (ROOTS), architecture, governance, and ethical charter.

ideas.idea: Open-access, openly-licensed local-runnable LLMs as an alternative to closed proprietary models
ideas.rationale: Directly affirms Memoria's local + open-model preference; the open-weights philosophy is the lineage that makes today's qwen2.5:7b / qwen3:14b local runtime viable. The principle is adopted; 176B BLOOM itself is far too large for the 16GB test-env GPU.

ideas.idea: Multitask prompted finetuning improves zero/few-shot task performance
ideas.rationale: Background justification for why Memoria can rely on instruction-tuned small local models for extraction/classification without per-task training; informs model selection but isn't itself buildable.

ideas.idea: Documented, charter-driven governance process (ethical charter, working groups, reproducibility) for building the artifact
ideas.rationale: Loosely parallels Memoria's ADR-driven decision model and structural review gate — documenting design decisions and governance up front. The multi-institution, 1200-participant scale is irrelevant to a single PI, so only the document-your-decisions principle transfers.

ideas.idea: 176B-parameter scale trained on a supercomputer; massive multilingual ROOTS corpus across 59 languages
ideas.rationale: Cloud/supercomputer scale and broad multilingual coverage are irrelevant to a single-researcher, single-language(English-academic), 16GB-GPU local deployment. Contradicts Memoria's scale and hardware constraints.

file: Zhong et al. - 2021 - Factual probing is [MASK] learning vs. Learning to recall.md
title: Factual Probing Is [MASK]: Learning vs. Learning to Recall
one_line: Introduces OptiPrompt (continuous prompt optimization) for factual probing of BERT, then shows via random-model/random-embedding controls that data-driven prompts can 'recover facts' even from randomly-initialized networks by exploiting class priors and subject-token correlations, so probing accuracy cannot be read as a clean lower bound on stored knowledge.

ideas.idea: Random-control diagnostic: fit the same optimizer to a randomly-initialized model / random embeddings to measure how much 'performance' is learnable from the training distribution alone (class prior + lexical correlations).
ideas.rationale: Memoria's learning-to-rank triage and discovery relevance scorer are trained on the PI's accept/reject history; a Naive-Bayes/majority-class control baseline would expose when ranking gains come from distributional shortcuts (e.g. always-accept a venue) rather than genuine relevance — a cheap guard against a triage model that looks good but only learned priors.

ideas.idea: Easy/hard test partition: split eval examples into those predictable by a trivial control vs the rest, and report accuracy separately to see where real signal lives.
ideas.rationale: When evaluating Memoria's claim-classification or relevance models against the PI, partitioning by 'guessable from metadata alone' vs 'needs the content' gives a more honest quality read than aggregate accuracy on the single-researcher scale.

ideas.idea: OptiPrompt continuous prompt optimization via gradient descent in embedding space with white-box access.
ideas.rationale: Requires gradient/white-box access to the model; Memoria runs local Ollama models behind inference, prefers discrete/manual prompting and deterministic ingest, and has no use for soft-prompt tuning of a probing task.

ideas.idea: Ethical caution: 'facts' elicited from an LM are not real facts and probing devolves into stereotype-learning on imbalanced data.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's faithfulness-over-flash stance — never treat an LLM's confident completion as an extracted claim; atomic-claim extraction must be grounded in the source text with provenance, not the model's parametric guess.

file: Zhou et al. - 2022 - Learning to prompt for vision-language models.md
title: Learning to Prompt for Vision-Language Models (CoOp)
one_line: CoOp adapts CLIP-like vision-language models to downstream image classification by replacing hand-crafted text prompts with learnable continuous context vectors (unified or class-specific), beating hand-engineered prompts by ~15% at 16 shots while keeping the backbone frozen.

ideas.idea: Replace brittle hand-crafted prompts with learned continuous context vectors, trained few-shot with the backbone frozen.
ideas.rationale: Vision-language image classification with gradient-based soft-prompt tuning is off-domain for Memoria (text papers, local LLMs, no white-box tuning) and contradicts the deterministic/manual-prompt constraint.

ideas.idea: Observation that a tiny wording change in a prompt causes large performance swings, motivating automation of prompt design.
ideas.rationale: Generic but relevant motivation: Memoria's compiled profiles and skill prompts should be versioned and regression-tested because small edits to an extraction/classification prompt can shift outputs materially; argues for treating prompts as governed artifacts.

ideas.idea: Continuous prompts are uninterpretable (nearest-vocabulary words are noise), unlike discrete prompts.
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's preference for inspectable, human-readable prompts/skills over opaque learned vectors — interpretability is a governance requirement for the 'agents judge, PI approves' model.

file: Zhou et al. - 2023 - Large language models are human-level prompt engineers.md
title: Large Language Models Are Human-Level Prompt Engineers (APE)
one_line: APE frames instruction-writing as black-box program synthesis: an LLM proposes candidate instructions from input-output demos, scores them by execution accuracy or log-prob on a held-out set, optionally resamples semantically-similar variants (Monte Carlo), and keeps the best — matching or beating human-written prompts on 24/24 instruction-induction tasks.

ideas.idea: Automatic prompt engineering: propose candidate instructions with an LLM, then select by an execution-accuracy score on a small labeled set.
ideas.rationale: Memoria has many internal prompts (atomic-claim extraction, claim-sentence classification, MASSW aspect extraction, relevance scoring). Given a handful of PI-graded gold examples per task, an offline APE-style propose-and-score loop could auto-tune these prompts for the local qwen2.5:7b model — entirely offline, no white-box access, fits the engines-write/agents-judge split.

ideas.idea: Execution accuracy aligns with downstream performance better than log-probability as a selection metric.
ideas.rationale: When auto-selecting or A/B-ing extraction prompts, score by actual task success (does the extracted claim/aspect match the gold?) rather than the model's token likelihood; directly applicable to Memoria's eval harness for prompt/skill quality.

ideas.idea: Adaptive multi-stage filtering: evaluate all candidates on a tiny subset, give surviving candidates more eval budget, run only finalists on the full set.
ideas.rationale: A cheap-compute pattern for Memoria's single-researcher scale and local models — apply the same staged-budget idea to the pre-file similarity gate and discovery scoring so expensive LLM passes only run on candidates that survive a cheap first cut.

ideas.idea: Iterative Monte Carlo resampling of high-scoring instructions gives only marginal gains over a single proposal round.
ideas.rationale: Useful negative result: keep Memoria's prompt-tuning loop simple (one propose-and-score pass), avoid building an expensive iterative search the payoff doesn't justify.

ideas.idea: Using APE to discover better zero-shot chain-of-thought / answer-prefix prompts (e.g. 'Let's work this out step by step').
ideas.rationale: Memoria's tasks are extraction/classification/ranking, not multi-step math reasoning; the CoT-prefix application has little payoff and the deterministic-ingest constraint disfavors free-form reasoning chains in the pipeline.

file: Zhou et al. - 2023 - Least-to-most prompting enables complex reasoning in large language models.md
title: Least-to-Most Prompting Enables Complex Reasoning in Large Language Models
one_line: Least-to-most prompting decomposes a hard problem into a sequence of dependent subproblems and solves them in order (each answer feeding the next), enabling easy-to-hard generalization — e.g. 99.7% on SCAN length-split vs 16% for chain-of-thought, with only a handful of exemplars.

ideas.idea: Two-stage decompose-then-sequentially-solve, where each subproblem's answer becomes context for the next.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's harder LLM tasks (extracting an argument graph with warrants, or building a typed-relation set from a paper) could be staged: first decompose 'list the claims', then per-claim 'find supporting/attacking claims', feeding prior answers forward — more reliable than one monolithic prompt on a 7B local model.

ideas.idea: Decomposition prompts do not transfer across (or even within) domains — each task class needs its own decomposition exemplars.
ideas.rationale: Tells Memoria that any decomposition-based skill must be task-specific and authored/curated per pipeline stage, not a single generic chain; reinforces per-skill governance and scoping rather than one universal reasoning prompt.

ideas.idea: Using few-shot prompting + Python intermediate representations to solve compositional benchmarks without any training/finetuning.
ideas.rationale: Symbolic-manipulation and math-word-problem benchmarks are off-domain; Memoria's MCP-only sandbox forbids agent code execution, so a model emitting Python to be run is incompatible with the architecture.

ideas.idea: Closing note that one-directional prompting should evolve toward bidirectional human-in-the-loop interaction with immediate feedback.
ideas.rationale: Echoes Memoria's PI-approval gate philosophy — the PI's accept/reject on dashboards is exactly the corrective feedback loop the authors gesture at; supports keeping the human in the loop rather than fully autonomous extraction.

file: Zhou et al. - 2026 - Externalization in LLM agents a unified review of memory, skills, protocols and harness engineering.md
title: Externalization in LLM Agents: A Unified Review of Memory, Skills, Protocols and Harness Engineering
one_line: A systems-level survey arguing that reliable LLM-agent capability comes from 'externalization' — relocating cognitive burdens out of model weights into persistent, inspectable, governable infrastructure (memory = state, skills = procedure, protocols = interaction) unified by a 'harness' that supplies sandboxing, approval gates, observability, provenance and rollback.

ideas.idea: Harness as the unifying runtime: agent capability lives in external infrastructure (memory + skills + protocols) coordinated by a governed runtime, not in the model alone.
ideas.rationale: This is essentially Memoria's thesis stated generally — Hermes is the harness, the vault is externalized memory, skills are governed, MCP is the protocol layer. The paper gives Memoria a named, citable conceptual frame ('engines write, agents judge, PI approves' = harness-mediated externalization) and validates the architecture against the literature.

ideas.idea: Human-oversight / mandatory approval gates and review gates as a first-class harness dimension (Section 6.2.3).
ideas.rationale: Directly validates Memoria's STRUCTURAL REVIEW GATE before notes are written; the survey treats approval gates as core harness infrastructure, confirming this is a recognized design axis rather than a quirk.

ideas.idea: Sandboxing and execution isolation as a harness dimension, plus the 'Lethal Trifecta' (sensitive data + unconstrained external comms + unverified execution) and protocol-level validation independent of skill correctness.
ideas.rationale: Provides the threat-model justification for Memoria's MCP-ONLY agent sandbox (agents get no file/terminal/code-execution ever): protocol-boundary checks catch a forbidden operation even when the skill itself is 'correct'. Strong external support for ADR-32/46/28/27 and the gate plugin hard-denies.

ideas.idea: Governance as co-designed infrastructure: provenance tracking, deterministic rollback, regression testing, and version control for memory/skill changes — not bolted on after.
ideas.rationale: Maps onto Memoria's compiled profiles, ADR-only decision model, and structural review gate; argues Memoria should ensure every engine-written note carries provenance and that skill/profile changes are regression-tested before deployment.

ideas.idea: Memory taxonomy: working context vs episodic experience vs semantic knowledge vs personalized memory, each with different retention/retrieval/privacy rules.
ideas.rationale: A useful lens for organizing the vault: papers/claims = semantic; discovery-run traces and triage decisions = episodic; PI preferences/learned ranking = personalized. Memoria can keep PI-preference memory separate from general knowledge, as the paper urges, but at single-researcher scale need not build the full tiered OS-style swap machinery.

ideas.idea: Memory-to-skill experience distillation: promote repeated episodic trajectories into reusable skill artifacts (and write skill execution traces back to memory for self-correction).
ideas.rationale: Memoria could distill recurring PI accept/reject patterns or repeated manual fixes into candidate skills/heuristics surfaced for PI approval — but must route through the review gate (the paper warns over-aggressive distillation entrenches noisy behavior; conservative + PI-gated promotion fits Memoria).

ideas.idea: Progressive disclosure / lazy-loading of skills and minimal-sufficiency to manage the scarce context budget (modules compete for tokens; over-retrieval and tool sprawl degrade reasoning).
ideas.rationale: Directly relevant to Memoria's profile compilation and skill governance — load skill detail only when a task pattern triggers it, and budget context across retrieval vs skill vs schema, keeping local 7B-14B models from drowning in injected material.

ideas.idea: Input/output failure taxonomy: retrieval errors (wrong context), skill errors (flawed procedure), protocol errors (malformed action) are separately debuggable layers.
ideas.rationale: Gives Memoria a clean way to attribute pipeline failures — a bad note could come from the similarity-gate surfacing the wrong source, a flawed extraction skill, or a malformed MCP call; separating these aids the read-only inspector and diagnostics plane.

ideas.idea: Evaluation agenda: measure externalized infrastructure via transferability (swap the base model), maintainability, recovery robustness, context efficiency, governance quality — not just task pass-rate.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's test split already separates engines (no model) from wiring (cheap/local) from quality (prod); the transferability metric (hold harness constant, swap qwen2.5:7b for the qwen3:14b fallback) is exactly the kind of eval Memoria should run to know how much quality is harness vs model.

ideas.idea: Self-evolving harness (RL / program-synthesis / evolutionary search over orchestration logic that the agent itself revises).
ideas.rationale: Contradicts Memoria's governance model — agents must not self-modify orchestration; the paper itself warns adaptive harnesses 'drift without adequate governance.' Memoria keeps harness/profile changes human-authored ADR-gated, so autonomous self-evolution is out of scope.

ideas.idea: Shared/multi-agent infrastructure: transactive shared memory, shared skill marketplaces, cross-org protocol standardization at ecosystem scale.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is explicitly single-researcher, not multi-user/enterprise; shared-infrastructure governance, swarm broadcast, and skill marketplaces are out of scope and bring attack surface (skill injection, protocol spoofing) Memoria avoids by staying local and single-tenant.

ideas.idea: Embodied/VLA cerebrum-cerebellum decomposition extension.
ideas.rationale: Robotics/embodied action is entirely off-domain for a text-knowledge system; no applicability.

file: Hinckley et al. - 2010 - Pen + touch = new tools.md
title: Pen + Touch = New Tools
one_line: A UIST design study of simultaneous pen+touch interaction on a tabletop scrapbook app, advocating the division of labor 'the pen writes, touch manipulates, and pen+touch yields new tools.'

ideas.idea: Differentiated roles for input modes ('pen writes, touch manipulates') rather than treating modes interchangeably, grounded in observed natural behaviors.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is a markdown/Obsidian system with no pen+touch tabletop surface; the modality-division insight has no buildable surface in the vault-plus-agent architecture.

ideas.idea: Phrasing/chunking: integrating selection + mode-switch + multi-step command into a single 'cognitive chunk' (hold-an-object disambiguates intent).
ideas.rationale: Weak analogy only: the PI's structured capture forms and the approval gate could phrase select-classify-approve into one reviewer action rather than separate clicks; but this is a generic UX principle, not a technique Memoria needs from this paper.

ideas.idea: Self-revealing novice-to-expert gestures (bezel menu, marking menus) and graceful degradation across contexts.
ideas.rationale: Gestural UI affordances on a touch display are off-domain for Obsidian spaces/dashboards; Memoria surfaces are point-and-click markdown views.

file: Hinds and Gupta - 2023 - Customer experience is everyone’s responsibility.md
title: Customer Experience Is Everyone's Responsibility
one_line: An HBR opinion piece arguing that great customer experience comes from cross-functional collaboration, broad feedback sharing, dogfooding, and humans-in-the-loop during tech rollouts.

ideas.idea: Cross-functional collaboration and breaking silos drive standout customer experience.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is explicitly single-researcher (one PI), not multi-team/enterprise; there are no functional silos to break, making the entire thesis inapplicable.

ideas.idea: Broad sharing of negative feedback/reviews increases accountability (BuildDirect example).
ideas.rationale: No multi-user audience exists in Memoria's single-PI scope; the contradictions dashboard already surfaces 'negative' signal to the one stakeholder.

ideas.idea: Don't over-invest in shiny AI tech without involving end users (humans-in-the-loop during rollout).
ideas.rationale: Conceptually echoes Memoria's 'PI approves' gate and faithfulness-over-flash constraint, but the paper is org-management advice with no mechanism to adopt; the principle is already an ADR-level decision derived from sources actually about agents.

file: Holtzman et al. - 2022 - Surface form competition why the highest probability answer isn't always right.md
title: Surface Form Competition: Why the Highest Probability Answer Isn't Always Right
one_line: Shows that scoring multiple-choice answers by raw LM probability is biased because synonymous surface forms compete for probability mass, and proposes Domain-Conditional PMI (PMI_DC) to reweight options by their a priori in-domain likelihood.

ideas.idea: Domain-Conditional PMI scoring: divide P(answer|question,domain) by P(answer|domain-premise) to factor out surface-form frequency bias when an LM ranks a fixed set of options.
ideas.rationale: Memoria runs many constrained classification calls on cheap local models (claim-sentence classification, MASSW aspect tagging, uncertainty flags). PMI_DC is a cheap, prompt-only, deterministic post-hoc reweighting (difference of two log-probs) that improves zero-shot label accuracy without finetuning — directly buildable where logprobs are available from the local model.

ideas.idea: Length-normalized log-likelihood (AVG) as a baseline that partially compensates for unconditional probability via BPE token-length.
ideas.rationale: A simple, cheaper fallback when domain-premise estimates are unavailable; useful for Memoria's relevance/triage scoring where calibrated option ranking matters but full PMI is overkill.

ideas.idea: Calibration to remove common-token / majority / recency bias before trusting LM option scores.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces that Memoria's classification engines must calibrate before thresholding; relevant to the discovery relevance scorer and pre-file similarity gate where a miscalibrated 'yes/no' would skew the nightly loop.

ideas.idea: Insight that 'I don't know' / generic answers get high probability under model uncertainty, relegating correct answers to the poorly-calibrated tail.
ideas.rationale: Argues for surfacing an explicit uncertainty/abstain path rather than forcing a label — aligns with Memoria's uncertainty-flag design on extracted claims.

file: Hong et al. - 2024 - MetaGPT meta programming for a multi-agent collaborative framework.md
title: MetaGPT: Meta Programming for a Multi-Agent Collaborative Framework
one_line: A multi-agent LLM framework that encodes human Standardized Operating Procedures (SOPs) into role-specialized agents communicating via structured documents through a shared publish-subscribe message pool, with executable feedback for self-correction.

ideas.idea: Encode SOPs (Standardized Operating Procedures) into prompt sequences so each agent role produces standardized structured intermediate artifacts that downstream roles can verify.
ideas.rationale: Maps directly onto Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' pipeline: the ingest -> claim-extraction -> structural-review-gate flow IS an SOP. Compiled profiles with explicit goal/constraints per role is exactly MetaGPT's role-profile mechanism, validating Memoria's structured-handoff-over-chatter design.

ideas.idea: Structured communication interface (documents/schemas) instead of free-form natural-language dialogue between agents, to prevent telephone-game information loss and idle chatter.
ideas.rationale: Memoria already favors deterministic ingest and structured capture; this confirms agents should hand off typed/structured records (claims, relations, review verdicts) rather than prose. Reinforces the MCP-only sandbox where agents emit structured tool calls.

ideas.idea: Shared message pool with role-based subscription (agents pull only role-relevant info based on their profile) to avoid information overload and quadratic one-to-one messaging.
ideas.rationale: Useful conceptually for Hermes runtime: agents/profiles subscribe to the vault subset relevant to their role (retriever/scout, reviewer) rather than scanning everything. But Memoria is single-researcher with few agents, so a heavyweight pub-sub bus is over-engineered; the subscription-by-profile filtering idea is the transferable part.

ideas.idea: Executable feedback loop: generate, run unit tests, read errors from memory, debug, retry up to N times (self-correction grounded in real execution).
ideas.rationale: Memoria's agents get NO code-execution ever (ADR-32/46, MCP-only sandbox, hard-denied since PR #364). A run-debug-retry loop directly contradicts a core architectural constraint; verification must come from deterministic engines/validators and the PI gate, not agent-run code.
