ideas.idea: Self-referential improvement: agents review prior-project feedback and rewrite their own constraint prompts, stored in long-term memory.
ideas.rationale: Contradicts Memoria's profile-compilation and skill-governance model — profiles are compiled and PI-governed, not self-mutating; auto-rewriting agent constraints would bypass the review gate and is an enterprise/AGI-flavored ambition outside single-PI scale.

ideas.idea: Multi-agent economies / credit assignment via market pricing (AgentStore, EOM).
ideas.rationale: Pure multi-tenant marketplace concept; irrelevant to a single local researcher with cheap/open models.

file: Horvitz - 1999 - Principles of mixed-initiative user interfaces.md
title: Principles of Mixed-Initiative User Interfaces
one_line: The seminal CHI'99 paper (Lookout email/calendar agent) that frames automated assistance as an expected-utility decision: act, ask, or do nothing based on inferred probability of the user's goal weighed against the costs/benefits of being wrong.

ideas.idea: Expected-utility decision for autonomous action: take an action only when its expected value exceeds inaction, computing a probability threshold from the utilities of the four outcomes (act/not-act x goal-desired/not-desired).
ideas.rationale: This is the formal backbone for Memoria's structural review gate and nightly proactive discovery loop: the system should auto-file/auto-suggest only above a cost-benefit threshold, otherwise defer to the PI. Directly buildable as the decision rule deciding when an engine result is auto-applied vs. routed to the gate vs. dropped.

ideas.idea: Three-tier response to uncertainty: (1) do nothing, (2) engage the user in a dialog about intent, (3) act — selected by inferred-probability bands.
ideas.rationale: Maps onto Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves': below threshold drop, mid-band surface to the approval gate/structured form for PI confirmation, high-confidence auto-write. Gives a principled basis for gate routing thresholds.

ideas.idea: Probabilistic goal inference from observable context (SVM/Bayes text classifier over message content) producing a likelihood used to gate the second, expensive phase of analysis.
ideas.rationale: A cheap classifier as a cost-saving pre-gate before invoking expensive analysis is exactly Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and discovery relevance scoring guarding the costlier claim-extraction/LLM stages — keeps local-model spend down.

ideas.idea: Graceful failure / minimizing the cost of being wrong: time-out proportional to inferred probability, easy direct invocation when the agent guesses wrong, unobtrusive 'courteous butler' withdrawal.
ideas.rationale: Informs how Memoria surfaces low-confidence proactive suggestions: make them dismissible and reversible, and always provide a manual PI-invoked path (PI direct-access rule) so a bad automated guess costs the PI almost nothing.

ideas.idea: Continual learning by observing the user (classifier trained by watching the PI work) and remembering recent interactions to adjust behavior.
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's learning-to-rank triage learning from PI accept/reject decisions at the gate; but must respect the local-only, PI-governed, deterministic-where-possible constraints rather than opaque always-on learning.

file: Shanmugam et al. - 2025 - Generative artificial intelligence in medicine.md
title: Generative Artificial Intelligence in Medicine
one_line: A broad review cataloguing generative-AI use cases across clinicians, patients, trial organizers, researchers, and trainees, plus the challenges (privacy, transparency, hallucination, equity, evaluation, accountability) that must be addressed.

ideas.idea: RAG over a private corpus with source-grounded generation to reduce hallucination and surface citable provenance for each answer.
ideas.rationale: Memoria already centers retrieval/discovery and faithfulness; the paper's framing that RAG both reduces hallucination AND makes sources transparent reinforces requiring every generated claim/answer to carry retrievable provenance — useful as a design principle, not new mechanism.

ideas.idea: LLM as a literature-review assistant that justifies inclusion/exclusion of papers (Guo et al. 91% accuracy with rationale).
ideas.rationale: Maps onto Memoria's discovery triage and pre-file similarity gate: the actionable insight is to require the agent to emit an inclusion/exclusion rationale, fitting 'agents judge, PI approves' governance.

ideas.idea: LLM extraction of structured fields from unstructured notes (e.g., demographics) to enable downstream comparison.
ideas.rationale: Parallels Memoria's atomic-claim and aspect extraction from papers; confirms the engines-extract-structured-data pattern, though the medical demographic specifics are off-domain.

ideas.idea: Fine-grained real-world evaluation: specify both a prompt set and explicit quality criteria; beware guideline non-adherence and order-sensitivity of LLM outputs.
ideas.rationale: Directly relevant to Memoria's evaluation/benchmark plane and L5 quality testing — adopt the discipline of separating test inputs from quality rubrics and probing for prompt-order sensitivity.

ideas.idea: Interpretability-by-design and local/global explanations for model outputs.
ideas.rationale: Aimed at opaque billion-parameter medical diagnostic models in high-stakes clinical care; Memoria's faithfulness comes from deterministic ingest + provenance + human gate, not post-hoc explainability research.

ideas.idea: Synthetic data generation, federated learning, multi-institutional privacy-preserving sharing, informed consent, accountability/liability frameworks.
ideas.rationale: All presuppose multi-user/clinical/regulatory and cross-institution scale; Memoria is a single-researcher local vault with no patient data or multi-party data sharing.

ideas.idea: Ambient documentation, diagnostic differentials, patient-facing chatbots, trainee feedback.
ideas.rationale: Clinical workflow use cases with no analog in a personal research-knowledge system.

file: Shapiro - 2019 - Integrative visualization exploring data collected in collaborative learning contexts.md
title: Integrative Visualization: Exploring Data Collected in Collaborative Learning Contexts
one_line: Proposes 'integrative visualization' — an iterative, theory-laden, human-centered process of transcribing rich qualitative data amplified by visualization to build grounded theory and bespoke computational tools (illustrated by museum interaction-geography).

ideas.idea: Iterative pipeline: data-context -> transcription amplified by visualization -> grounded theory & computational tools, where each visualization pass surfaces new codes, categories, units of analysis, and questions.
ideas.rationale: Mirrors Memoria's intended loop of projecting notes/claims into disposable views (projection engine, projected canvas) to surface new relations/questions; reinforces visualization as an analytic instrument, not just output.

ideas.idea: Transcription/coding is theory-laden and selective — representational choices encode commitments and must be made explicit.
ideas.rationale: Useful caution for Memoria's claim extraction and typed-relation schema: the extraction taxonomy itself embeds theoretical commitments, so the schema (claim types, aspects) should be treated as a deliberate, revisable design artifact.

ideas.idea: Small-multiples and layered space-time views to compare entities across a shared frame; click-to-activate linking from visualization back to source.
ideas.rationale: The click-from-view-to-source linking pattern maps to Memoria's read-only inspector and projected canvas needing back-links from any visual node to the underlying note/claim; small-multiples could inform contradiction/argument dashboards.

ideas.idea: Domain-specific space-time-cube transcription of physical movement and conversation (interaction geography, IGS tool).
ideas.rationale: Method is tied to embodied/spatial collaborative-learning data (movement across museum galleries); off-domain for a text/claim-centric research vault.

ideas.idea: Need for stronger CSCL–infoVis community connections for parsing/mining skills.
ideas.rationale: A field-building call to action, not a buildable technique for Memoria.

file: Shi et al. - 2025 - Retrieval models aren't tool-savvy benchmarking tool retrieval for large language models.md
title: Retrieval Models Aren't Tool-Savvy: Benchmarking Tool Retrieval for Large Language Models
one_line: Introduces TOOLRET, a 7.6k-task / 43k-tool benchmark showing that even strong IR models retrieve tools poorly (low term overlap, task-shift), that poor retrieval drops agent pass rate, and that instruction-augmented queries plus a 200k instructional training set fix much of the gap.

ideas.idea: Tool retrieval is its own task: low query-tool lexical overlap (ROUGE-L ~0.06) and a domain shift from document retrieval cause off-the-shelf retrievers to fail.
ideas.rationale: Memoria has a retriever/scout profile and an MCP-only agent toolset; the insight that selecting the right tool/skill via generic semantic search is unreliable argues for curated tool descriptions or a small specialized index rather than trusting a general embedder.

ideas.idea: Target-aware instruction augmentation: pair each query with an instruction describing relevance criteria (query intent + target functionality); instruction-tuned retrievers improve most.
ideas.rationale: Cheap, deterministic to apply locally — Memoria can attach an instruction string describing what a good match looks like to discovery/retrieval queries to raise precision without retraining; fits cheap/local-model constraint.

ideas.idea: Completeness@K metric: score 1 only if ALL target tools are in top-K (vs recall/precision), because tasks need a combination of tools.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's learning-to-rank triage and discovery relevance scoring could adopt a completeness-style metric when a query needs a SET of notes/claims (e.g., all papers supporting a contradiction), not just one good hit.

ideas.idea: Retrieval quality bottlenecks downstream agent success (pass rate drops ~10-11 points when oracle toolset replaced with retrieved one).
ideas.rationale: Directly justifies Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and a measurable retrieval-quality SLO: degrade-and-measure end-to-end, since the whole engines/agents/gate pipeline inherits retrieval errors.

ideas.idea: Cross-encoder re-ranking gave limited or negative gains on tool retrieval.
ideas.rationale: A cautionary data point: Memoria should not assume a re-ranker stage automatically helps its discovery pipeline; validate before adding cost.

ideas.idea: Building a 200k-instance instructional training set and fine-tuning bespoke IR models.
ideas.rationale: Large-scale retriever fine-tuning is beyond single-researcher/local-cheap-model constraints; Memoria prefers off-the-shelf local embedders plus prompt/instruction tricks.

ideas.idea: K-means clustering over embeddings to sample/deduplicate a heterogeneous corpus while preserving coverage.
ideas.rationale: Reusable for Memoria's BERTopic clustering MCP and record-linkage dedup: cluster-then-sample is a clean way to deduplicate near-identical ingested items while keeping diversity.

file: Shin et al. - 2020 - AutoPrompt eliciting knowledge from language models with automatically generated prompts.md
title: AutoPrompt: Eliciting Knowledge from Language Models with Automatically Generated Prompts
one_line: A gradient-guided search that automatically constructs cloze-style prompts (trigger tokens + learned label tokens) to probe masked LMs for sentiment, NLI, factual knowledge, and relation extraction without fine-tuning.

ideas.idea: MLMs can act as zero-fine-tune relation extractors via cloze prompts, outperforming a supervised LSTM RE model when real context is supplied.
ideas.rationale: Relevant to Memoria's entity/relation extraction: prompting a small local model to fill (subject, relation, [MASK]) is a lightweight extraction path, though modern instruction-tuned LLMs supersede the gradient-search machinery.

ideas.idea: Perturbed-sentence diagnostic: replace the true object with a random one to test whether the model is EXTRACTING from text vs reciting memorized facts (MLM accuracy collapsed, confirming reliance on prior knowledge).
ideas.rationale: A directly buildable faithfulness check for Memoria's claim/relation extraction: counterfactually perturb the source sentence and confirm the extractor's output tracks the text, not the model's priors — guards against hallucinated 'extractions' before the structural gate.

ideas.idea: Gradient-based discrete trigger-token search to auto-generate prompts.
ideas.rationale: Requires white-box gradients and per-task labeled data, produces uninterpretable non-grammatical prompts, and is superseded by instruction-following LLMs; incompatible with Memoria's MCP-only, off-the-shelf-model setup.

ideas.idea: Prompting as a lower bound on model knowledge / alternative to fine-tuning for serving many tasks from one frozen model.
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's compiled-profile approach: keep one local base model and vary prompts/profiles per task rather than maintaining many fine-tuned checkpoints.

ideas.idea: Automated label-token selection via a logistic probe over [MASK] embeddings.
ideas.rationale: Tied to the MLM-classifier probing paradigm; Memoria does classification (claim-sentence types) with instruction-tuned generation, not marginalized label-token sets.

file: Shin et al. - 2021 - Constrained language models yield few-shot semantic parsers.md
title: Constrained Language Models Yield Few-Shot Semantic Parsers
one_line: Bootstraps semantic parsers by having an LM paraphrase user utterances into a controlled English-like canonical form, with grammar-constrained decoding (a nextTokens function over a trie/CFG) guaranteeing only well-formed outputs, achieving strong few-shot accuracy.

ideas.idea: Grammar-constrained decoding via a nextTokens function (trie or incremental Earley parser) that filters the beam to only tokens leading to a well-formed, type-safe output.
ideas.rationale: Directly buildable for Memoria's deterministic ingest: constrain a local model's structured-extraction output (claim records, typed relations, MASSW aspects, frontmatter) to a schema/grammar so it is always valid by construction — eliminates a class of malformed-output failures before the structural gate.

ideas.idea: Target an English-like canonical sublanguage rather than a raw machine representation, then mechanically map canonical->formal (and it improves accuracy with cheap/small models).
ideas.rationale: Memoria can have local models emit near-natural-language claim/relation statements (faithful, human-checkable at the gate) and deterministically convert them to the formal record, raising fidelity with qwen2.5:7b-class models and aiding PI review.

ideas.idea: Dynamic prompt construction by selecting the most relevant few-shot examples per input (scored by p(u|u_i)).
ideas.rationale: A retrieval-augmented-prompting pattern Memoria can use for extraction: pull the k most similar already-approved exemplars from the vault into the extraction prompt, reusing the existing similarity/retrieval infra.

ideas.idea: accuracy@K reporting motivated by human-in-the-loop correction: present top-K candidate parses for the annotator to pick the correct one.
ideas.rationale: Fits Memoria's 'PI approves' gate exactly — surface top-K candidate extractions/relations in the review gate so the PI selects rather than free-edits, leveraging that accuracy@5 was near-ceiling even for a rapid prototype.

ideas.idea: Writing a synchronous context-free grammar (SCFG) by hand per domain to define canonical<->meaning mappings.
ideas.rationale: Hand-authoring a full SCFG is heavyweight, but the idea of a single declarative schema that both validates output and defines the canonical<->formal mapping is worth adapting for Memoria's claim/relation schemas.

ideas.idea: Using GPT-3 / large proprietary LMs as the paraphrase generator.
ideas.rationale: Memoria prefers local cheap/open models; the paper itself shows fine-tuned BART (much smaller) often matches or beats GPT-3 here, which validates staying local.

file: Ratner et al. - 2023 - Parallel context windows for large language models.md
title: Parallel Context Windows for Large Language Models
one_line: A training-free inference trick (PCW) that splits long context into parallel windows with reused positional embeddings and restricted attention, letting an off-the-shelf LLM attend to B times more in-context examples or retrieved documents, with largest gains on many-class classification.

ideas.idea: Parallel context windows: carve long context into chunks that each attend only to themselves while the task tokens attend across all windows, reusing positional embeddings — a model-surgery technique needing access to the attention mask and position embeddings.
ideas.rationale: Memoria runs inference through cheap/local off-the-shelf models (qwen2.5:7b) via Ollama and MCP, where there is no hook to rewrite attention masks or positional embeddings; modern models already have 32K-128K windows that exceed single-researcher needs, so the core mechanism is inapplicable and superseded.

ideas.idea: PCW shines specifically on classification tasks with many output classes (6-150+ labels), with gains correlating to log(#classes); parallelizing the label/example space stabilizes variance and reduces order-sensitivity.
ideas.rationale: Memoria does multi-class work (claim-sentence classification, MASSW aspect tagging, keyphrase tag candidates) where the label space is large. The insight worth keeping is the failure mode, not PCW: when few-shot exemplars overflow the window, partition exemplars into independent batches and aggregate rather than cramming them into one prompt — a prompting-level pattern usable through MCP without model surgery.

ideas.idea: For retrieval-augmented QA, reading several retrieved documents in independent parallel windows raises answer-span recall over concatenation; works for 'comparison' (independent evidence) but hurts 'bridge' multi-hop questions needing sequential conditioning.
ideas.rationale: Relevant to Memoria's RAG retrieval and contradictions/comparison work: when comparing claims across multiple notes, processing each source independently then aggregating avoids cross-contamination — but the bridge-question limitation warns that argument-graph reasoning requiring chained warrants must NOT be parallelized. Useful as a design caveat, not a buildable component.

file: Ren et al. - 2025 - Towards scientific intelligence a survey of LLM-based scientific agents.md
title: Towards Scientific Intelligence: A Survey of LLM-based Scientific Agents
one_line: A mechanism-centric survey of 120+ LLM-based scientific agents organized around four architectural components — Planner, Memory, Action Space, and Verifier — with fine-grained sub-taxonomies and ethics/reproducibility framed as design constraints.

ideas.idea: Four-component agent architecture (Planner / Memory / Action Space / Verifier) as a reusable blueprint, with the Verifier as a first-class gate that examines intermediate results before they are stored in Memory and only returns output once validity/reproducibility standards are met.
ideas.rationale: This is a near-exact articulation of Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' structural review gate: the Verifier-before-Memory loop maps onto the structural review gate before notes are written. The blueprint validates separating the writing engine from the judging verifier as a named architectural pattern.

ideas.idea: Verifier taxonomy: V1 self-correction/reflective, V2 multi-agent critique/role-based, V3 human-in-the-loop/expert oversight, V4 tool-based/computational validation.
ideas.rationale: Directly informs Memoria's gate design. V3 (human-in-the-loop = PI approval) plus V4 (deterministic tool-based validation, e.g. citekey/record-linkage checks) plus V2 (agent judges) is exactly Memoria's layered governance. Adopt the explicit V1-V4 vocabulary to structure which checks are deterministic engine checks vs agent-judgment vs PI sign-off.

ideas.idea: Three-type Memory taxonomy: M1 historical context (within-session + cross-session persistent archives, solution/experience replay, consolidation of 'what merits long-term retention'), M2 external knowledge base (RAG over literature + structured KGs/ontologies, entity-centric stores from literature co-occurrence), M3 intrinsic parametric knowledge.
ideas.rationale: Memoria IS an M1+M2 system: the Obsidian vault is durable cross-session memory (M1), and the typed-relation/argument graph plus claim store is an M2 entity-centric knowledge base built from paper co-occurrence. The survey's framing of 'consolidation challenge: what merits long-term retention' is precisely the structural-review-gate decision, and 'entity-centric knowledge store from literature co-occurrences to cross-pollinate ideas' is a concrete pattern for Memoria's discovery loop.

ideas.idea: Context-augmented (P2) planning: ground each planning/generation step in retrieved prior records and extracted entities, with novelty checks comparing generated output to retrieved prior papers (SciMON, CoI, ResearchAgent) and triggering replanning until sufficient novelty differentiation.
ideas.rationale: Maps onto Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and discovery relevance scoring: comparing a candidate note/claim against retrieved priors to assess novelty before filing is the SciMON loop. Borrow the 'novelty differentiation as a gate condition' idea for the pre-file similarity gate, scoped to single-researcher (no multi-agent debate needed).

ideas.idea: Deliberative/reflective (P3) and self-correction (V1) loops: generate, self-critique against explicit criteria, regenerate until convergence or iteration cap — error-driven replanning where failures trigger revision.
ideas.rationale: Useful for Memoria's claim-extraction and aspect-tagging engines: a bounded self-critique pass (does this extracted claim have a clear subject/uncertainty flag?) before handing to the gate. Borrow the bounded-iteration pattern, but keep it cheap (local model, few rounds) given single-researcher scale and the no-code-execution sandbox.

ideas.idea: Search-based planners (P4, Tree-of-Thought/MCTS over plan space) and multi-agent role-interactive planning (P5, supervisor + specialized critic/executor agents debating to consensus).
ideas.rationale: MCTS plan search and large multi-agent debate ensembles assume heavy compute and autonomous lab/tool execution; Memoria is single-researcher, deterministic-ingest, MCP-only (agents get no code execution) and favors faithfulness over elaborate orchestration. The coordination overhead and cost contradict Memoria's constraints — a single judge agent plus PI is sufficient.

ideas.idea: Programmatic plan synthesis (P6, plans as executable code/DSL/DAG) and tool-use action space (T1: invoking simulators, lab robots, domain models like AlphaFold; T3: code generation and execution).
ideas.rationale: Memoria's hard architectural rule (ADR-32/46, MCP-only sandbox) forbids agents from file/terminal/code execution, ever. Code-executing and instrument-controlling agents are exactly what Memoria's sandbox hard-denies, so these action types are out of scope by design.

ideas.idea: Ethics & reproducibility as intrinsic design constraints embedded in the verifier/architecture rather than peripheral concerns; emphasis on source attribution to reduce hallucination and enable verification.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's faithfulness-over-flash and citekey-backbone provenance are the same principle: every claim must carry source attribution (Zotero citekey) and uncertainty flags. Reinforces building provenance/attribution into the gate as a hard validation, not an afterthought.

file: Reynolds and McDonell - 2021 - Prompt programming for large language models beyond the few-shot paradigm.md
title: Prompt Programming for Large Language Models: Beyond the Few-Shot Paradigm
one_line: Argues few-shot examples mainly perform 'task location' rather than learning, and proposes prompt-programming techniques — direct/zero-shot task specification, serialized reasoning, constraining behavior, metaprompts, and LLM-as-evaluator — as more effective ways to elicit capabilities.

ideas.idea: Task location vs meta-learning: well-crafted 0-shot prompts can match or beat few-shot, because examples mostly signal WHICH task to perform; few-shot examples can even hurt via 'semantic contamination' when the model treats them as a continuing narrative rather than independent instances.
ideas.rationale: Directly applicable to Memoria's local-model engines (claim classification, aspect tagging) where prompt budget is tight on qwen2.5:7b. Favor a precise direct task specification over stuffing few-shot examples; and when examples are used, frame them explicitly as independent instances to avoid contamination — concrete, cheap, deterministic-ingest-friendly.

ideas.idea: Constraining behavior: design prompts that are inconsistent with undesired continuations (syntactic constraints, clear stop conditions) so 'any reasonable continuation can only be the desired behavior'.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's engines must produce structured, parseable output (atomic claims, classification labels) deterministically. The 'constrain so only the intended output is plausible' principle plus fill-in-the-blank templates improves extraction reliability and reduces derailment — directly buildable in the extraction prompts feeding the structural gate.

ideas.idea: Serialized reasoning / scratch space: force decomposition into steps before a verdict (precursor to chain-of-thought) so the model isn't forced to answer in a single feed-forward pass; discourage premature verdicts.
ideas.rationale: Useful for harder judgment steps (contradiction detection, claim supersession reasoning) where a one-token verdict is unreliable. Borrow step-before-verdict prompting for the agent-judge role, but keep it bounded given cost; less needed for simple deterministic classification.

ideas.idea: Metaprompts: seed the model to generate its own task-specific prompt (e.g. 'expert generator' that names the most-qualified persona, or fill-in-the-blank templates) to avoid hand-writing a prompt per task.
ideas.rationale: Memoria compiles profiles/skills; a metaprompt that derives an extraction prompt per paper-type could reduce hand-tuning. Borrow cautiously — the paper itself warns metaprompts add derailment risk needing fill-in-the-blank control, and Memoria values deterministic, auditable prompts over open-ended self-generation.

ideas.idea: LLM-as-evaluator and 'isolate catastrophic failures': use a model to verify open-ended outputs (easier to verify than produce), and report metrics separating task-not-attempted failures from genuine task failures.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's agent-judge in the gate is an LLM-as-evaluator; the verify-is-easier-than-generate framing supports that split. 'Isolate catastrophic failures' maps to a useful diagnostic for extraction-engine quality (did the engine attempt extraction vs produce wrong claims) feeding the content-light diagnostics plane.

ideas.idea: Anthropomorphic / memetic-proxy and cultural-anchor prompting (invoke a named persona to encode a complex intention).
ideas.rationale: Persona/memetic-proxy prompting trades determinism for evocative open-endedness and is brittle across small local models; it conflicts with Memoria's faithfulness-over-flash and reproducible-ingest goals. Prefer explicit task specification over invoking 'ask Gandhi'-style proxies.

file: Ricchezze et al. - 2025 - Ethical reflections on integrating artificial intelligence in care practices.md
title: Ethical Reflections on Integrating Artificial Intelligence in Care Practices
one_line: A bioethics commentary on integrating AI/robotics into disability care, arguing for human-centered, participatory, fairness-aware, explainable systems that complement rather than replace clinicians under a biopsychosocial model.

ideas.idea: AI should complement, not replace, human judgment; automation bias and over-reliance erode human competencies, so systems should be aids requiring human oversight.
ideas.rationale: Echoes Memoria's PI-in-the-loop gate philosophy (engines/agents assist, PI decides) but in a clinical-care context. The transferable kernel — keep the human as the final decision-maker to prevent skill/competency erosion — already underpins Memoria's governance; nothing new to build, only mild reinforcement.

ideas.idea: Explainability/transparency and source attribution to overcome the 'black box', building trust and accountability in algorithmic decisions.
ideas.rationale: Generic principle that Memoria already realizes via provenance (citekeys), read-only inspector, and static HTML admin reports. Worth noting as alignment, but the paper offers no mechanism beyond what Memoria has.

ideas.idea: Fairness-aware learning, inclusive datasets, algorithmic-bias mitigation, equitable access, participatory/co-design with affected communities, regulatory governance.
ideas.rationale: These target multi-stakeholder clinical-care equity, demographic dataset representation, and policy/regulatory frameworks — none apply to a single-researcher personal knowledge vault over academic papers. Off-domain for Memoria's constraints.

ideas.idea: Biopsychosocial model and brain-computer-interface / socially-assistive-robot care scenarios.
ideas.rationale: Entirely clinical disability-care domain with no bearing on paper ingest, claim extraction, or research-knowledge management.

file: Rogers - 2006 - Moving on from weiser’s vision of calm computing engaging UbiComp experiences.md
title: Moving on from Weiser's Vision of Calm Computing: Engaging UbiComp Experiences
one_line: A foundational HCI critique arguing UbiComp should abandon 'calm/proactive computing' (machines acting for us) in favor of 'engaging' systems that make people themselves more proactive — bounded, user-controlled tool ensembles that augment human intellect rather than predict and decide on the user's behalf.

ideas.idea: Shift from 'proactive computing' (system takes initiative and acts for you) to 'proactive people' (technology engages and extends what the person does, leaving them in control); over-automation of decision-making erodes our ability to learn, remember, and think.
ideas.rationale: This is a direct philosophical anchor for Memoria's PI-direct-access and 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' stance: the system surfaces, structures, and proposes, but the PI stays in control and does the thinking. Rogers' argument that fully-proactive systems are both intractable AND undesirable justifies Memoria's deliberate refusal to auto-file notes without a review gate.

ideas.idea: 'Weak UbiComp' over 'strong UbiComp': pursue bounded, attainable, domain-specific tools for specific activities rather than an omniscient context-aware system that models user intent — because modeling human context/intent accurately is intractable and brittle, and errors are unforgiving.
ideas.rationale: Validates Memoria's single-researcher, bounded-scope design over an enterprise/multi-user 'smart' system. The 'don't try to model the user's intent, give them controllable tools' principle supports Memoria's structured-capture forms, disposable projection engine, and inspector — surfaces the PI drives, not predictive automation guessing what they want.

ideas.idea: Tool ensembles assemblable and customizable by non-experts ('mash' combinations of lenses/surfaces/tools), controlling their own setup/evolution without 'an army of computer scientists', enabling new information flow and information management (storing, recording, re-using).
ideas.rationale: Maps onto Memoria's requirement that every feature be PI-accessible directly from the Obsidian UI (spaces, dashboards, bases, projected canvas) without CLI. The 'user assembles and controls bounded tools' vision is precisely Memoria's Obsidian-as-control-surface design and disposable projection engine.

ideas.idea: Augment human intellect for science: deliver multiple streams of data into powerful 'windows/lenses' so researchers can compute, integrate, inquire, and make inferences from masses of archived recordings, with largely-automated management but human interpretation 'at relevant times'.
ideas.rationale: Essentially Memoria's mission statement for a researcher: automate the management of the paper/claim corpus (deterministic ingest, dedup, clustering) but provide lenses (spaces, contradictions dashboard, argument graph, BERTopic clustering) for the PI to interpret and discover. 'Automated management + human interpretation at relevant times' is the projection-engine + proactive-discovery-loop split.

ideas.idea: Persuasive/self-monitoring practices: gentle, time-sensitive 'nagging' that surfaces information the user wasn't aware of at salient moments, leaving the data owned and controlled by the user (circumventing privacy concerns).
ideas.rationale: Loosely informs Memoria's nightly proactive-discovery loop and triage: surface relevant items at salient moments without acting autonomously. Borrow the 'surface, don't act; user owns the data' framing for discovery notifications; the behavior-change/health specifics are off-domain.

ideas.idea: Calm-computing critique of pervasive sensing/monitoring as 'panopticon' with privacy harms; data should be owned/controlled by the user locally.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's local-vault, single-user, hands-off-production privacy posture (everything local, PI owns the data). Not a new mechanism but supports the local-first constraint against any cloud/telemetry temptation.

file: Bell and Dourish - 2007 - Yesterday’s tomorrows notes on ubiquitous computing’s dominant vision.md
title: Yesterday's Tomorrows: Notes on Ubiquitous Computing's Dominant Vision
one_line: A critical-theory essay arguing that ubicomp's 'proximate future / seamless infrastructure' vision blinds researchers to the messy, heterogeneous, culturally-situated present, and advocating a 'ubicomp of the present' centered on infrastructural messiness and seamful design.

ideas.idea: Infrastructure is inherently messy, relational, never seamless; design should treat heterogeneity/messiness as a first-class resource ('seamful design') rather than something to be cleaned up later.
ideas.rationale: Memoria already embraces this implicitly — ingest is deterministic but the corpus is heterogeneous (mixed PDFs, citekeys, contradictions). The insight argues for surfacing messiness (e.g. the contradictions dashboard, uncertainty flags on claims, supersession) rather than hiding it behind a clean 'answer'. Use it as a design principle for inspector/dashboards, not a buildable feature.

ideas.idea: The 'proximate future' framing lets builders defer hard present problems as 'someone else's cleanup'; instead solve for the actual present.
ideas.rationale: A useful governance attitude for Memoria's single-PI scope: build for the PI's real current workflow (Obsidian + local models) rather than a speculative future agent ecosystem. Reinforces the 'faithfulness over flash' and 'cheap/local model' constraints. Not a mechanism, just a stance.

ideas.idea: Time-machine / future-envisionment research and cross-cultural ethnography of technology adoption.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is a single-researcher personal tool, not a societal/cultural-deployment study; the ethnographic and multi-cultural-adoption framing has no buildable analogue.

file: Ben-David et al. - 2022 - PADA example-based prompt learning for on-the-fly adaptation to unseen domains.md
title: PADA: Example-based Prompt Learning for On-the-fly Adaptation to Unseen Domains
one_line: A T5-based method that, per test example, autoregressively generates a human-readable prompt of Domain-Related Features (DRFs) projecting the example into a semantic space spanned by known source domains, then conditions the task prediction on that prompt — enabling adaptation to domains unseen at training time.

ideas.idea: Domain-Related Feature (DRF) extraction via Mutual Information between tokens and a one-vs-rest domain label, with a frequency-ratio (rho) filter that lets a feature be domain-characteristic yet shared across related domains.
ideas.rationale: This is a cheap, deterministic, model-free way to compute discriminative 'signature' terms per cluster/topic — directly applicable to Memoria's keyphrase tag candidates and BERTopic cluster labeling. MI + class-based TF-IDF runs locally without an LLM, fitting the deterministic-ingest and cheap-model constraints. The rho-tuned 'characteristic but shared' notion is a nice refinement over plain TF-IDF for naming overlapping topic clusters.

ideas.idea: Generate a per-example human-readable prompt (domain name + signature tokens) and condition downstream classification on it; the prompt is interpretable rather than a soft/continuous vector.
ideas.rationale: Aligns with Memoria's faithfulness/inspectability ethos: a generated, human-readable 'why this was classified here' signature is auditable by the PI, unlike opaque prompt-tuning. Could inform how the claim-classifier / aspect-extractor explains its routing. Borrow the interpretable-signature idea, not the T5 training pipeline.

ideas.idea: Single shared model across all 'domains' instead of Mixture-of-Experts (one model per domain), because domain partitioning is arbitrary and MoE scales parameters linearly.
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's single-local-model preference (qwen2.5:7b): avoid per-topic specialized models; rely on one model plus lightweight signature prompts. A useful argument against building many small expert models on a 16GB GPU.

ideas.idea: Train T5 with a two-stage multi-task objective (alpha mixture) to generate prompts and predict labels jointly; tune on labeled multi-source data.
ideas.rationale: Memoria does not train or fine-tune models (MCP-only sandbox, no labeled multi-domain corpus, deterministic ingest). The supervised multi-source training regime is off-constraint; only the DRF/signature extraction transfers.

file: Bender et al. - 2021 - On the dangers of stochastic parrots can language models be too big .md
title: On the Dangers of Stochastic Parrots: Can Language Models Be Too Big?
one_line: A position paper arguing that ever-larger LMs carry environmental/financial cost, encode hegemonic bias from uncurated web data, produce fluent-but-meaningless 'stochastic parrot' text, and should be replaced by careful data curation, documentation, efficiency-as-metric, and value-sensitive pre-development planning.

ideas.idea: Prefer carefully curated, well-documented datasets over scale; budget for documentation up front and only collect what can be documented ('documentation debt').
ideas.rationale: Directly supports Memoria's deterministic-ingest + provenance ethos: every ingested paper has a citekey backbone, structured capture, and the structural review gate. Treat the vault as a curated, documented corpus rather than an undifferentiated dump — the 'document at creation, not post-hoc' principle maps onto Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and capture forms.

ideas.idea: Efficiency (energy/compute) should be a first-class evaluation metric; smaller models are often sufficient and the largest LMs are too slow for real-time needs.
ideas.rationale: This is essentially Memoria's existing constraint (local + cheap/open models, qwen2.5:7b default on a 16GB GPU). The paper provides the principled justification for not reaching for ever-larger/cloud models — reinforces the engines-no-model, wiring-cheap-model, quality-prod-model split already in the test policy.

ideas.idea: Pre-mortem / value-sensitive design exercises before building: enumerate stakeholders, hypothetical failures, and worst-case (not just average-case) behavior up front.
ideas.rationale: Maps onto Memoria's ADR-only decision model and structural review gate ('agents judge, PI approves'). A pre-mortem framing could inform how deferred ADRs document assumptions and failure modes. Borrow the worst-case-evaluation mindset for the gate and for LLM-judge prompts.

ideas.idea: LMs are 'stochastic parrots' with no communicative intent or grounding; fluent output should not be mistaken for understanding, and accountability for generated text matters.
ideas.rationale: Core justification for Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' governance and faithfulness-over-flash stance: never let a generated note enter the vault unreviewed as if it carried meaning/authority. Argues for atomic-claim extraction with uncertainty flags and human-in-the-loop approval rather than free generation — already a Memoria pillar, now grounded.

ideas.idea: PII / memorized-training-data extraction risk from large LMs; watermarking synthetic text.
ideas.rationale: Memoria runs local open models over the PI's own corpus (single user, no public deployment, no synthetic text dissemination), so the extraction-attack and watermarking concerns don't apply at this scale.

ideas.idea: Environmental-racism / marginalized-community harm and societal-bias auditing of deployed LMs.
ideas.rationale: These are societal-scale, multi-stakeholder deployment harms; Memoria is a single-researcher local tool with no external users or social-distribution surface. Off-scope for design, though it informs the general 'be modest about model claims' attitude already captured above.

file: Beneteau et al. - 2020 - Assumptions checked how families learn about and use the echo dot.md
title: Assumptions Checked: How Families Learn About and Use the Echo Dot
one_line: A four-week deployment study of 10 non-early-adopter families finding that breadth of voice-assistant use narrows over time due to poor feature discoverability, and proposing 'just-in-time', voice-based, 'near-peer' learning where the device teaches its own functionality in context.

ideas.idea: Just-in-time, in-context learning: when a user's request fails or repeats, the system proactively offers relevant functionality ('Would you like to hear things I can do related to [keyword]?') instead of relying on tutorials/onboarding that users forget.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is PI-accessible directly from Obsidian; feature discoverability of its many surfaces (spaces, gates, dashboards, capture forms, inspector) is a real single-user risk. Contextual, in-place hints (e.g. a dashboard surfacing 'you have 3 unresolved contradictions' or a gate suggesting the next action) beat a forgotten manual. Borrow the just-in-time prompt pattern for Memoria's Obsidian surfaces.

ideas.idea: Device-initiated suggestions framed as marketing/upsell erode trust and get ignored; suggestions must be relevant and non-manipulative to be a 'trusted learning influencer'.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces faithfulness-over-flash: Memoria's proactive discovery loop and triage suggestions must read as genuinely useful to the PI, not noisy/promotional, or the PI will tune them out (exactly the breadth-narrowing failure). A relevance-quality bar for proactive surfaces is the takeaway.

ideas.idea: Breadth of use decays over time without reinforcement; users settle into a narrow rut of a few features.
ideas.rationale: A risk Memoria should design against: the PI may collapse to using only retrieval and ignore the argument graph / supersession / discovery. Periodic in-context resurfacing of underused-but-relevant capabilities (the nightly discovery loop is a natural vehicle) counters this.

ideas.idea: 'Near-peer' / circles-of-social-influence framework from diffusion-of-innovation theory; family-group multi-user dynamics; manufacturer emails are the least trusted source.
ideas.rationale: The social-distance/near-peer and multi-member-household framing is multi-user and consumer-product specific; Memoria is single-PI with no social learning network or manufacturer-vs-peer dynamic. Only the discoverability mechanics (above) transfer.

file: Benyon - 1997 - Domain models for user interface design.md
title: Domain Models for User Interface Design
one_line: A conceptual HCI paper comparing three domain-modelling paradigms — task-based, object-oriented, and data-centred (entity-relationship) — for designing human-computer systems, arguing each is 'made from' its primitives and that data modelling best captures the abstract signals exchanged between agents and devices.

ideas.idea: Distinguish agents (intentional, autonomous, goal-forming) from devices (sophisticated but non-volitional) as the two participant classes in an interacting-systems model.
ideas.rationale: Maps cleanly onto Memoria's governance vocabulary: 'engines' (deterministic devices that parse/extract) vs 'agents' (judge, but sandboxed) vs the PI (the only true intentional agent who approves). The agent/device distinction is a clean conceptual frame for the engines-write/agents-judge/PI-approves split and the MCP-only sandbox (agents are deliberately denied device capabilities).

ideas.idea: Data-centred / entity-relationship modelling makes the semantics of and relationships between data items explicit, and offers rigorous techniques (normalization, ER) to detect redundant or hidden entities not obvious in the 'real world' (e.g. User distinct from Owner).
ideas.rationale: Memoria's typed-relations graph, claim/note entities, and record-linkage entity dedup are essentially an ER model of the research domain. The insight that disciplined data modelling surfaces non-obvious entities and de-duplicates supports the pre-file similarity gate and record-linkage dedup design. Borrow the modelling discipline, not a specific notation.

ideas.idea: A model is 'made from' its primitives, not a model 'of' them; declare the perspective (weltanschauung) and abstraction level explicitly as part of the model.
ideas.rationale: Useful design hygiene for Memoria's ADRs and projection engine: a projected canvas is a disposable view 'made from' notes/claims at a declared abstraction level — naming the perspective explicitly avoids confusing the projection with the underlying graph. A conceptual aid, not a feature.

ideas.idea: Task-based modelling (MUSE, GOMS-style structure charts) as the primary domain model for UI design.
ideas.rationale: Task models are device-dependent, unstable across users (Draper's critique cited in the paper), and centered on prescriptive workflow decomposition — at odds with Memoria's data/claim-centred, single-PI, emergent-use design. The object/data-centred lessons are the transferable ones.

file: Jacobs et al. - 2021 - How machine-learning recommendations influence clinician treatment selections the example of antide.md
title: How machine-learning recommendations influence clinician treatment selections: the example of antidepressant selection
one_line: A within-subject factorial experiment showing that ML recommendations did not improve clinician treatment accuracy, that incorrect recommendations actively lowered accuracy, and that feature-based explanations made overreliance worse rather than better.

ideas.idea: Incorrect ML recommendations significantly lower expert accuracy versus a no-recommendation baseline (overreliance/automation bias).
ideas.rationale: Direct evidence for Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' gate: a wrong discovery-relevance score or wrong contradiction flag surfaced confidently can drag the PI below their unaided baseline. Argues the structural review gate must let the PI see a no-suggestion baseline and not anchor on the engine's pick.

ideas.idea: Explanations (especially simple feature-based ones) can EXACERBATE overreliance, not fix it; less information in an explanation is not always better.
ideas.rationale: Memoria plans to show warrants/aspects/uncertainty alongside claims. This warns that a tidy feature-importance blurb attached to a wrong claim can lower PI judgment. Favor showing provenance + uncertainty flags + dissenting evidence (contradictions dashboard) over a single persuasive rationale.

ideas.idea: Subjective metrics (confidence, perceived utility) do not track objective accuracy and cannot predict tool success; evaluate with realistic tasks against expert-consensus ground truth.
ideas.rationale: Memoria evaluation (L5 quality) should not lean on the PI's felt-usefulness ratings of triage/discovery; needs held-out ground-truth concordance, mirroring this paper's psychopharmacologist-consensus design.

ideas.idea: Show a top-N list with calibrated scores (stability/dropout) rather than a single highlighted pick.
ideas.rationale: Supports presenting triage/discovery results as a ranked candidate set with uncertainty rather than one auto-accepted answer, reducing single-recommendation anchoring.

ideas.idea: Higher ML familiarity correlated with LOWER reliance on the recommendation.
ideas.rationale: The PI is ML-literate; expect skeptical, low-reliance use. Memoria should optimize for an expert who interrogates suggestions (good provenance, easy override) rather than one who defers.

ideas.idea: Online vignette experiment with simulated/manipulated ML outputs and Bonferroni-corrected repeated-measures ANOVA.
ideas.rationale: Multi-participant clinical study methodology; Memoria is single-researcher and not running human-subjects trials. Useful as cautionary evidence, not a buildable mechanism.

file: Jacobson et al. - 2026 - A multi-agent human-LLM collaborative framework for closed-loop scientific literature summarization.md
title: A Multi-Agent Human-LLM Collaborative Framework for Closed-Loop Scientific Literature Summarization (Elhuyar)
one_line: Elhuyar is a human-in-the-loop multi-agent pipeline (filter, extract, consensus-score, fit models, report) that turns scattered literature into structured numerical datasets and fitted equations, with reliability enforced via iterative consensus scoring and confidence-tiered human review.

ideas.idea: Iterative Consensus Scoring (ICS): extract each data point k times under identical input, score confidence by agreement count; auto-filter low scores, flag mid scores for human review, auto-accept high scores.
ideas.rationale: Directly buildable cheap-model reliability layer for Memoria's deterministic-ingest claim/aspect extraction. Confidence-tiered routing (auto-drop / route-to-gate / auto-accept) is exactly the structural review gate triage Memoria needs, and self-consistency over k local-model runs fits qwen2.5:7b economics.

ideas.idea: Two-stage extraction: cheap yes/no filtering questions BEFORE numeric extraction, because LLMs hallucinate more on specific value extraction than on high-level yes/no document questions.
ideas.rationale: Maps onto Memoria's pre-file similarity/relevance gate: run cheap boolean relevance/eligibility checks (does this paper contain X?) to prune before the expensive atomic-claim extraction, improving faithfulness and saving local-model cost.

ideas.idea: Explicit 'Data Definition' contract from the user: required vs preferred variables, accepted units, conversion rules, and discard-if-false filtering criteria, compiled into the extractor prompt.
ideas.rationale: A PI-authored structured extraction schema is a clean Memoria capture form / profile-compiled artifact; turns open-ended extraction into a deterministic, auditable spec the engine fills.

ideas.idea: Confidence-tiered human inspection (low=auto-filter, middle=flag for inspector, high=accept) to minimize human workload while keeping a human in the loop.
ideas.rationale: Restates Memoria's governance ethos precisely; gives a concrete threshold policy for what reaches the PI approval gate versus what engines handle silently.

ideas.idea: Synthetic evaluation harness: fictional materials with known generating equations + LLM-written mini-papers (some untargeted noise) to measure extraction precision/recall and filter accuracy before deployment.
ideas.rationale: A controllable, ground-truth-bearing eval for Memoria's extraction/filter engines without needing labeled real corpora; lets quality tests run on cheap synthetic data and ablate each component (filter / ICS).

ideas.idea: Response agent grounded strictly in provided data ('use only the provided data as evidence'), separating LLM narrative from non-LLM numerical model fitting.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's faithfulness-over-flash and deterministic-ingest stance: keep math/aggregation deterministic, restrict LLM to grounded summarization. Worth adopting as a prompting/architecture discipline.

ideas.idea: Whole-document-as-context single-shared-LLM with isolated per-agent prompts (no cross-contamination).
ideas.rationale: Stateless per-agent calls over a shared local model fit Memoria's MCP-only sandbox and single-model economics; a reasonable architecture pattern, though Memoria's agents get no file access so context must be passed via MCP.

ideas.idea: Automated unit conversion with fallback to discard papers reporting incompatible/ambiguous units.
ideas.rationale: Sensible normalization-or-discard rule for record-linkage/entity-dedup and numeric claim extraction; cheap deterministic guardrail.

file: Ji et al. - 2022 - Survey of hallucination in natural language generation.md
title: Survey of Hallucination in Natural Language Generation
one_line: Comprehensive taxonomy of NLG hallucination (intrinsic vs extrinsic), its data and training/inference causes, and a catalog of measurement metrics (IE/QA/NLI/LM-based) and mitigation methods across summarization, dialogue, QA, data-to-text, and MT.

ideas.idea: Intrinsic (contradicts source) vs extrinsic (unverifiable from source) hallucination distinction, with the note that traditional overlap metrics (ROUGE/BLEU) miss hallucination.
ideas.rationale: Gives Memoria a principled label set for its uncertainty flags and faithfulness checks on extracted claims/summaries: 'contradicts the paper' (intrinsic) vs 'not stated in the paper' (extrinsic). Intrinsic flags can feed the contradictions dashboard; extrinsic flags gate notes at the review gate.

ideas.idea: NLI-based faithfulness scoring: a faithful summary/claim must be entailed by the source; score = entailment probability between source and generated text. SUMMACConv applies NLI at sentence granularity.
ideas.rationale: A buildable, cheap, local check for Memoria: run an NLI/entailment model from each extracted atomic claim against its source span before the note is written. Sentence-level (SUMMAC-style) granularity matches Memoria's claim-sentence units and pinpoints which span is unsupported.

ideas.idea: QA-based faithfulness (generate questions from the summary, answer them from the source, compare answers) and IE-triple-based checking (subject,relation,object mismatch).
ideas.rationale: Useful secondary verification signals for claim extraction; IE-triple matching aligns with Memoria's typed-relations/knowledge-graph extraction. Borrow rather than adopt because of error-propagation from the QG/QA/IE models and local-model cost; use as an optional gate check, not the primary one.

ideas.idea: Numeral/quantity hallucination is under-addressed and high-stakes; re-rank candidate summaries by verification score of quantity entities.
ideas.rationale: Memoria extracts quantitative atomic claims; a dedicated numeral-faithfulness check (verify numbers/units against the source span) is a concrete, high-value uncertainty flag, echoing Elhuyar's numeric-extraction caution.

ideas.idea: Source-reference divergence and dedup: heuristically paired data and unfiltered duplicates cause hallucination; instance-level filtering loses word-level signal.
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's record-linkage entity dedup and pre-file similarity gate, and argues for span/word-level (not whole-note-level) faithfulness scoring so partially-supported notes aren't wholesale discarded.

ideas.idea: Human cognitive perspective for metrics: weight named-entities/numbers more than function words; check-worthy detection.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's faithfulness scoring should prioritize entity/number errors (most salient to the PI) over stylistic drift; cheap heuristic for ranking which flags reach the gate.

ideas.idea: Extrinsic-hallucination fact-checking against world knowledge (Wikipedia/web, with source-trust weighting like PageRank).
ideas.rationale: Web-scale world-knowledge fact-checking with source-trust meta-signals is off-Memoria-domain: Memoria's 'source' is the cited paper corpus, not the open web, and the local/offline constraint plus single-researcher scale make web fact-checking infrastructure out of scope.

ideas.idea: RL/multi-task/controllable-decoding training-time mitigations (slot-consistency reward, entailment auxiliary task, reduced sampling randomness).
ideas.rationale: These require training/fine-tuning generation models; Memoria uses off-the-shelf local models with deterministic ingest and no model training. Out of scope versus inference-time gating.

file: Ji et al. - 2026 - ClawArena benchmarking AI agents in evolving information environments.md
title: ClawArena: Benchmarking AI Agents in Evolving Information Environments
one_line: A benchmark testing persistent-assistant agents on three coupled challenges (multi-source conflict reasoning, dynamic belief revision, implicit personalization) with a hidden-ground-truth scenario design and a Composite Reliability Score that rewards consistency, not just accuracy.

ideas.idea: Three coupled capabilities for a persistent assistant: multi-source conflict reasoning (judge source reliability, don't naively aggregate), dynamic belief revision (revise rather than accumulate when new evidence invalidates old), implicit personalization (learn preferences from corrections).
ideas.rationale: These are exactly Memoria's claim-supersession-over-time, contradictions dashboard, and learning-to-rank-triage problems framed as evaluable capabilities. Belief revision = supersession; conflict reasoning = the argument graph / contradictions surface. Gives Memoria an evaluation framing for its core engines.

ideas.idea: Dynamic belief revision difficulty is governed by update DESIGN (does the update force re-interpretation of a prior claim?) not update VOLUME; revision is scored only when contradictory evidence actually arrives.
ideas.rationale: Sharp design insight for Memoria's supersession engine: the hard cases are updates that flip a previously-true claim (partial vs full fix), not sheer paper count. Test set for supersession should be built around contradiction-forcing updates, and the system should explicitly mark when a new paper invalidates an earlier claim.

ideas.idea: Hidden-ground-truth, six-layer scenario design: agent sees only noisy/partial/contradictory traces; correctness verified against a never-shown Layer-0 truth with a contradiction map and answer provenance.
ideas.rationale: A clean way to build Memoria eval fixtures for contradiction detection and supersession: construct a synthetic corpus with a known truth + contradiction map, expose conflicting paper claims, verify the engine recovers the right resolution. Borrow the construction pattern, not the multi-channel chat framing.

ideas.idea: Composite Reliability Score = (TCR + Robustness)/2, where Robustness rewards sustained success streaks and penalizes clustered failures (Success Cohesion x Failure Dispersion).
ideas.rationale: Behavioral-consistency-over-time metric is relevant to Memoria's nightly discovery loop and triage quality: a discovery engine that intermittently collapses is worse than its mean accuracy suggests. Borrow the consistency idea as a monitoring signal, though the exact streak formula is benchmark-specific.

ideas.idea: Skill overlay (MetaClaw): a repository of procedural skills distilled from prior failure trajectories, retrieved and injected into the prompt per round without weight changes, improving robustness without changing accuracy.
ideas.rationale: Aligns with Memoria's skill governance / profile-compiled approach: prompt-injected, governed skills distilled from failures are compatible with the MCP-only no-fine-tuning sandbox. Borrow the in-context skill-injection pattern; Memoria already governs skills/profiles.

ideas.idea: Implicit personalization scored only in silent-exam rounds (apply learned preference with no reminder); preferences are brittle and decay as checkers tighten.
ideas.rationale: Relevant to Memoria learning the PI's relevance/triage preferences from corrections; the brittleness finding warns that learned preferences need re-validation. But Memoria favors explicit PI-authored profiles/forms over silently inferred preferences, so adopt only as a caution.

ideas.idea: Multi-channel chat/workspace harness benchmarking across many agent frameworks (OpenClaw, Claude Code, etc.) and 18 models with framework x model interaction analysis.
ideas.rationale: Multi-framework, multi-user-collaboration enterprise-workspace setting is off-Memoria-scale (single researcher, single local runtime). The cross-framework leaderboard provides nothing buildable for Memoria's architecture.

file: Jiang et al. - 2022 - PromptMaker prompt-based prototyping with large language models.md
title: PromptMaker: Prompt-based Prototyping with Large Language Models
one_line: A CHI case study of industry practitioners prototyping ML features via natural-language prompts, surfacing benefits (democratized, fast, grounding) and four challenges: reverse-engineering goals into prompts, example-sourcing, non-deterministic debugging, and evaluating prompt effectiveness.

ideas.idea: Evaluate prompts against a curated canonical 'test set' of inputs (including extreme caricatures and known-groundtruth cases) and track a quality score across iterations; test the same set across prompt variants.
ideas.rationale: Directly buildable for Memoria's profile-compiled engine prompts (extraction, triage, classification): keep a versioned regression test set of representative papers/claims with expected outputs, score each prompt revision, and gate prompt changes on it. Matches deterministic-ingest discipline.

ideas.idea: Non-determinism debugging: re-run multiple times to separate signal from sampling noise; low-temperature confirm then raise temperature to probe.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Elhuyar's ICS multi-run consensus and argues Memoria's local-model extraction should run low/zero temperature for reproducibility and use repeated runs to detect instability, keeping ingest deterministic.

ideas.idea: Structured few-shot mode with prefix labels and double-bracket [[variable]] placeholders that auto-concatenate examples into a reusable, generalizing prompt template.
ideas.rationale: A clean pattern for Memoria's compiled-profile prompt templates: typed placeholders + labeled few-shot rows make extraction/classification prompts reusable and PI-editable via a structured capture form rather than freeform text.

ideas.idea: Prompts as shareable, modular 'boundary objects' in a gallery that teammates borrow and remix.
ideas.rationale: The value here is cross-collaborator grounding and reuse in a team; Memoria is explicitly single-researcher (no co-PI required, the PI is the only user). The social-glue/boundary-object benefit does not apply.

ideas.idea: Prompt-based prototyping democratizes ML authoring for non-experts (designers/PMs) and lowers the access barrier.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's user is an ML-literate PI, not a non-expert needing democratized access; the broaden-who-can-build framing is irrelevant to a single expert researcher's knowledge system.

ideas.idea: Mental-model shift: treat the LLM as a most-likely-continuation engine over training data, not a human-like agent that 'understands' requests; work backwards from goal to a prompt that matches training-data phrasing.
ideas.rationale: A useful design reminder for whoever authors Memoria's extraction/judgment prompts; explains why faithfulness gates are needed (the model continues plausibly, it does not comprehend), but it is guidance rather than a component to build.

ideas.idea: No-code integration of prompts as endpoints into existing visual tools (e.g., Figma plugin / web app embedding).
ideas.rationale: Echoes Memoria's PI-direct-access rule that every feature must be reachable from the Obsidian UI directly; the principle of embedding LLM capabilities into the user's native authoring environment transfers, though the specific Figma tooling does not.

file: Andriushchenko et al. - 2025 - AgentHarm a benchmark for measuring harmfulness of LLM agents.md
title: AgentHarm: A Benchmark for Measuring Harmfulness of LLM Agents
one_line: A benchmark of 110/440 explicitly malicious multi-step agentic tool-use tasks showing leading LLMs often comply without jailbreaks and that chat jailbreaks transfer to the agent setting while preserving capability.

ideas.idea: Rubric-based scoring with manually-written deterministic checks (was the right function called, in the right order, with the right argument value) and an LLM judge used ONLY for narrow well-specified sub-checks, never to grade the whole output.
ideas.rationale: Directly maps to Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge' governance and faithfulness-over-flash constraint. Memoria's claim-extraction / structural-review gate can use the same pattern: deterministic field checks first, narrow LLM semantic judge only for specific spans (e.g. 'is this sentence an atomic claim', 'does this warrant support that claim'), avoiding whole-output LLM grading bias/calibration problems.

ideas.idea: Separate a 'capability/correctness' score from a 'refusal/abstention' score, computing the correctness score only on non-refused cases, so that abstention never inflates or hides quality.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's triage/discovery scoring and extraction QA can decouple 'engine abstained / low-confidence' from 'engine produced a wrong note', preventing uncertainty flags from masking quality regressions in dashboards.

ideas.idea: Synthetic, side-effect-free proxy tools that let agents self-correct (hallucinated filename -> 'file does not exist' message -> retry) for safe, deterministic, cheap evaluation in a standardized framework (Inspect).
ideas.rationale: Memoria's MCP-only sandbox already denies file/terminal; a synthetic-tool eval harness is a clean way to regression-test agent behavior over Memoria's MCP surfaces deterministically and cheaply (matches local+cheap constraint) without touching the real vault.

ideas.idea: Canary string in the repo plus a withheld private test split to detect/avoid benchmark contamination.
ideas.rationale: Useful hygiene if Memoria ever builds an internal eval set for claim-extraction or ranking quality; a held-out, never-API-exposed split guards against local-model overfitting, but it's a minor operational practice not a core feature.

ideas.idea: The specific harm taxonomy (fraud, cybercrime, terrorism, etc.) and the finding that chat-jailbreak templates transfer to agents.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is a single-researcher local tool with no untrusted users and an MCP-only sandbox that already denies file/terminal/code execution; adversarial-misuse red-teaming of frontier-model refusals is off-domain for the threat model.

file: Anil et al. - 2022 - Exploring length generalization in large language models.md
title: Exploring Length Generalization in Large Language Models
one_line: An empirical study showing transformers fail to generalize from short to long instances under finetuning (preferring non-sequential shortcut solutions), while few-shot in-context scratchpad prompting enables variable-length template matching that does extrapolate.

ideas.idea: Few-shot scratchpad / chain-of-thought prompting where the model learns a step template from a few short exemplars and applies it to much longer inputs ('variable length template matching').
ideas.rationale: Relevant to Memoria's local-model extraction pipelines: when an engine must process a long paper or a long claim chain on qwen2.5:7b, giving a few short worked exemplars of the per-step transformation (e.g. sentence -> atomic claim -> uncertainty flag) is more robust than asking for the whole document at once. A general prompting heuristic, not a buildable component.

ideas.idea: Decompose a task into explicit sequential per-step state-tracking sub-steps rather than expecting a single parallel pass, because models bias toward non-generalizing shortcut solutions.
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's deterministic, decomposed ingest design: prefer many small typed extraction steps (claim, relation, supersession) each with explicit intermediate state over one monolithic LLM call, improving faithfulness on small local models.

ideas.idea: Finetuning transformers on length-generalization tasks (parity, variable assignment) and analysis of positional encodings / EOS / distractor tokens / computational-graph-depth as the difficulty axis.
ideas.rationale: Memoria does no model training and uses off-the-shelf local models; the synthetic algorithmic-reasoning training analysis is off-domain for a markdown knowledge vault.

file: Arias et al. - 1997 - Enhancing communication, facilitating shared understanding, and creating better artifacts by integra.md
title: Enhancing communication, facilitating shared understanding, and creating better artifacts by integrating physical and computational media (extraction empty)
one_line: The markdown extraction contains no body text (only stripped image placeholders and empty headings), so no ideas can be assessed from this file.

ideas.idea: No extractable content: the file is 36 lines of empty headings and omitted-picture placeholders with no prose.
ideas.rationale: Nothing to adopt or borrow because the extraction failed to capture any text; the original (a 1997 CSCW paper on integrating physical and computational collaborative media) is also multi-user/co-located collaboration oriented, which contradicts Memoria's single-researcher scope even if recovered.

file: Arora et al. - 2022 - Ask me anything a simple strategy for prompting language models.md
title: Ask Me Anything: A Simple Strategy for Prompting Language Models
one_line: AMA reformats task inputs into open-ended question-answer prompts via reusable functional prompt-chains, then aggregates multiple noisy prompt votes with weak supervision (modeling prompt accuracies and dependencies, not majority vote), letting a 6B open model match few-shot GPT3-175B.

ideas.idea: Weak-supervision aggregation of multiple noisy prompt outputs: learn each prompt's accuracy and the dependency structure among prompts (graph-structured inverse covariance) with no labeled data, instead of majority vote.
ideas.rationale: Directly buildable for Memoria's claim-extraction and discovery-relevance scoring on cheap local models: run several imperfect prompt variants of a classifier (claim-sentence type, relevance score, contradiction detection), then combine with WS rather than naive voting. WS accounts for correlated errors among similar prompts, which raises faithfulness without any training data or labels - ideal for a single-researcher, local, label-poor setting.

ideas.idea: Reusable task-agnostic 'functional' prompt-chains (question() then answer()) applied across all inputs rather than per-example hand-tuned prompts.
ideas.rationale: Matches Memoria's compiled-profile / deterministic-ingest philosophy: define a small library of fixed, reusable extraction prompts that operate over any paper, instead of bespoke prompts per paper. Cheap to maintain and version under skill governance.

ideas.idea: Open-ended QA prompt format outperforms restrictive 'output True/False' formats because it aligns with the next-token pretraining distribution (open-ended structures ~1000x more frequent in pretraining corpus), plus a final step that MAPS the open-ended answer to a valid output class.
ideas.rationale: Concrete, cheap accuracy lift for Memoria's local-model classifiers (uncertainty flags, MASSW aspect tagging, keyphrase candidates): prompt as open-ended questions and add an explicit answer->category mapping step, rather than forcing constrained tokens. Especially valuable for qwen2.5:7b-class models where the same gains were observed.

ideas.idea: Use the model recursively to transform inputs into the effective format (reformat-then-answer) rather than crafting one perfect prompt.
ideas.rationale: Reduces prompt-engineering brittleness in Memoria's pipelines; a useful design stance but secondary to the WS aggregation and QA-format ideas.

ideas.idea: Conditional-entropy information-flow metric H(y|y-hat) to diagnose whether the prompt set or the aggregator is the bottleneck.
ideas.rationale: A handy offline diagnostic for tuning Memoria's ensemble extractors (is adding another prompt-chain helping, or is aggregation losing information), but it's an analysis tool rather than a shipped feature.

file: Auger - 2013 - Speculative design crafting the speculation.md
title: Speculative Design: Crafting the Speculation
one_line: A design-theory essay arguing speculative/critical design works by carefully crafting a 'perceptual bridge' (grounding fictional artifacts in familiar, plausible, near-future contexts) to provoke reflection on technology, illustrated through art/design case studies.

ideas.idea: The 'perceptual bridge': anchor an unfamiliar/speculative proposal in familiar, mundane, contemporary detail so the audience can relate to and reason about it.
ideas.rationale: Weakly applicable to Memoria's PI-facing surfaces: dashboards, projected canvases, and the contradiction/argument-graph views land better when novel structure is grounded in the PI's familiar vocabulary and existing notes rather than presented as abstract graph jargon. A UX framing insight, not a buildable mechanism.

ideas.idea: Speculative design as critique - using artifacts to provoke discourse and surface what one does NOT want, decoupled from commercial/market constraints.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is a faithfulness-oriented knowledge tool, not a provocation/art-discourse instrument; deliberately ambiguous or fictional artifacts contradict the deterministic, evidence-grounded design pillars.

ideas.idea: Verisimilitude / 'design faction' techniques (blurring fact and fiction so the concept passes as real, e.g. the Audio Tooth Implant hoax).
ideas.rationale: Directly contradicts Memoria's core constraint of faithfulness, claim provenance, and uncertainty flagging; fabricating plausible-but-false artifacts is the opposite of the system's purpose.

file: Ghafarollahi and Buehler - 2024 - SciAgents automating scientific discovery through multi-agent intelligent graph reasoning.md
title: SciAgents: Automating Scientific Discovery through Multi-Agent Intelligent Graph Reasoning
one_line: A multi-agent LLM system (Ontologist, Scientist 1/2, Critic, Planner, Assistant) that samples random paths through a 33k-node ontological knowledge graph built from ~1000 papers to generate, expand, critique, and novelty-score scientific research hypotheses.

ideas.idea: Random/heuristic path sampling through a knowledge graph (modified Dijkstra with randomness factor + random waypoints, embedding-based heuristic) instead of shortest path, to surface diverse cross-domain concept connections
ideas.rationale: Memoria already has a typed relation graph and discovery loop; a randomized-waypoint walk over the claim/note graph is a cheap, deterministic-seedable mechanism for the nightly proactive discovery loop to propose non-obvious cross-paper connections beyond nearest-neighbor similarity. Adapt the algorithm (it's just NetworkX-scale), but not the bio-materials specifics.

ideas.idea: Structured hypothesis schema with fixed JSON aspects (hypothesis, outcome, mechanisms, design_principles, unexpected_properties, comparison, novelty), each expanded by a dedicated per-aspect prompt
ideas.rationale: Mirrors Memoria's MASSW-style paper aspects and atomic-claim extraction; the fixed-key JSON-with-per-field-expansion pattern is a clean template for engine outputs that the structural review gate can validate field-by-field. Borrow the decomposition discipline, not the materials-science fields.

ideas.idea: Critic agent that reviews strengths/weaknesses and surfaces the most impactful testable questions; adversarial agent interactions mirroring peer review
ideas.rationale: Aligns with Memoria's 'agents judge' governance role and contradictions/argument-graph work. A critic profile that flags weaknesses and uncertainty is reusable, but Memoria's critic must be MCP-only (no tool execution) and produce candidates for PI approval, not autonomously finalize.

ideas.idea: Semantic Scholar API tool to score novelty/feasibility of a generated idea against existing literature, pruning ideas too similar to prior work
ideas.rationale: Directly maps to Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and discovery relevance scoring: scoring a candidate note/claim against the existing corpus before it is written. Use the local corpus + embeddings for the dedup half; the external-API novelty half is optional given single-researcher offline-first constraints.

ideas.idea: Ontologist agent that converts graph nodes/edges into explicit natural-language definitions and relationship interpretations
ideas.rationale: Useful pattern for rendering Memoria's typed relations into human-readable warrants on the projected canvas, but should be a deterministic projection/engine step rather than a free-running agent.

ideas.idea: Fully autonomous group-chat-manager orchestration where agents self-organize with shared memory and no predefined order
ideas.rationale: Contradicts Memoria's deterministic ingest + structural review gate ('engines write, agents judge, PI approves'). Unbounded self-organizing agent chats with full shared memory are non-auditable and over-scaled for a single researcher; the pre-programmed pipeline variant is the better fit.

ideas.idea: Generating thousands of speculative hypotheses at scale to build a large ideation database
ideas.rationale: Memoria values faithfulness over flash and PI-approved curation; mass-generating un-vetted speculative hypotheses would flood the vault and the approval gate. Out of scope for single-researcher scale.

file: Gholizadeh HamlAbadi et al. - 2025 - Agent-to-agent (A2A) protocol integrated digital twin system with AgentIQ for multimodal AI fitness.md
title: Agent-to-Agent (A2A) Protocol Integrated Digital Twin System with AgentIQ for Multimodal AI Fitness Coaching and Personalized Well-Being
one_line: An architectural blueprint (unimplemented) for a five-layer multimodal digital-twin fitness coach using Google's A2A protocol, MCP tool invocation, NVIDIA AgentIQ/LangSmith observability, and embodied XR avatar feedback.

ideas.idea: Short-term memory (last K frames) + long-term memory (exponential moving average) split feeding a combined context vector for downstream agents
ideas.rationale: The STM/LTM separation with an EMA-decayed long-term store is a lightweight, deterministic memory-management pattern Memoria could borrow for session vs. persistent context, though Memoria's LTM is the markdown vault itself rather than a numeric EMA.

ideas.idea: MCP tool invocation with mandatory logging of (tool, input, params) and output hash for auditability/reproducibility
ideas.rationale: Memoria is already MCP-only for agents; logging every MCP call with an output hash for an audit trail directly strengthens the agent sandbox and the read-only inspector. Cheap and aligned with governance.
