ideas.idea: Agent Card (.well-known/agent-card.json) advertising each agent's skills, modalities, endpoints for dynamic discovery and capability-based routing
ideas.rationale: Maps loosely to Memoria's compiled profiles / skill governance: a declarative manifest of what each profile (retriever, scout, critic) can do. Borrow the declarative-capability idea, but Memoria has no need for HTTP/JSON-RPC inter-agent networking at single-user scale.

ideas.idea: Observability/profiling (token-level traces, latency, inter-agent hops, trajectory and ragas evaluators) over agent workflows
ideas.rationale: Memoria's content-light diagnostics plane and static HTML admin reports would benefit from per-run token/latency/tool-trace logging; the trajectory-evaluator concept (verify tool-call sequences) is a useful test idea. Borrow the metrics, not the NVIDIA-specific tooling.

ideas.idea: Distributed A2A inter-agent messaging over JSON-RPC/HTTP, embodied XR avatars (Audio2Face/Omniverse), multimodal sensor fusion (OpenPose, openSMILE, wearables)
ideas.rationale: Entirely off-domain (real-time fitness coaching, GPU-heavy XR) and contradicts Memoria's single-researcher, local, cheap-model, text-vault constraints. Networked multi-agent protocols add non-auditable surface with no benefit at single-user scale.

file: Gilbert et al. - 2025 - Could transparent model cards with layered accessible information drive trust and safety in health A.md
title: Could transparent model cards with layered accessible information drive trust and safety in health AI?
one_line: A News & Views commentary arguing that health-AI 'Model Cards'/nutrition labels must use layered, progressively-deepening information linked to verifiable, auditable underlying data to avoid becoming a vehicle for unverified marketing claims.

ideas.idea: Layered information disclosure: a simple high-level summary that links down through successively deeper, cross-verifiable layers to the raw evidence (wiki-style)
ideas.rationale: Directly applicable to Memoria's surfaces: dashboards/spaces show a high-level claim or aspect summary that must link down to the atomic claim, its source sentence, and the citekey/PDF. This 'every summary is traceable to verifiable source' principle is the heart of faithfulness-over-flash and the structural review gate.

ideas.idea: Standardized, fixed-layout label so users can do side-by-side comparison and become familiar with where each field lives
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's structured capture forms and static HTML admin reports / read-only inspector: a consistent fixed schema per note-type (paper card, claim card) makes the PI's review faster. Borrow the standardized-layout discipline.

ideas.idea: Risk of 'superficial/sham transparency' where a clean summary masks unverified or misleading underlying content unless independently audited
ideas.rationale: A direct warning for Memoria's engine-generated summaries: a polished claim summary that isn't linked to and checked against its source is a faithfulness failure. Reinforces that the review gate must verify summary-to-source linkage, not just presence of a citation.

ideas.idea: Mandatory linkage/integration with external regulatory labels (IFU, ISO 15223 symbols, EU AI Act, medical device law)
ideas.rationale: Regulatory/compliance framing for multi-stakeholder commercial health-AI deployment; irrelevant to a single-researcher local knowledge vault with no patients or procurement.

ideas.idea: Color-scale graphical 'nutrition label' ratings for fast consumer decisions on AI ethics/sourcing
ideas.rationale: Aimed at lay-consumer rapid trust signaling; Memoria's user is the expert PI who needs the underlying evidence, not a simplified color rating. The paper itself cautions these shouldn't substitute for real information.

file: Gottweis et al. - 2025 - Towards an AI co-scientist.md
title: Towards an AI co-scientist
one_line: A Gemini-2.0 multi-agent 'co-scientist' (Generation, Reflection, Ranking, Proximity, Evolution, Meta-review, Supervisor) that uses a generate-debate-evolve loop with an Elo tournament and test-time-compute scaling to produce novel, verifiable, wet-lab-validated research hypotheses in a scientist-in-the-loop paradigm.

ideas.idea: Deep verification review: decompose a hypothesis into constituent assumptions, then into decontextualized sub-assumptions, and independently check each for correctness to find invalidating elements
ideas.rationale: This is essentially atomic-claim decomposition + per-claim uncertainty checking, which Memoria already does for ingest; applying it as a verification pass over generated/extracted claims (break a claim into sub-assumptions and flag the unsupported one) is a directly buildable engine for the contradictions dashboard and claim supersession.

ideas.idea: Scientist-in-the-loop paradigm: system never autonomously finalizes; the expert specifies goals/constraints, supplies own hypotheses into the tournament, and reviews/approves outputs
ideas.rationale: Exactly Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' governance and PI-direct-access rule. Validates the core design; the PI's manual reviews feeding back into ranking is the gate pattern Memoria should keep.

ideas.idea: Meta-review agent that synthesizes recurring critique patterns across all reviews/debates and appends them to other agents' prompts (learning without fine-tuning/backprop)
ideas.rationale: Memoria can't fine-tune cheaply and wants determinism; appending distilled recurring-issue feedback into a profile's compiled prompt is a low-cost self-improvement loop compatible with compiled profiles. Borrow as a periodic, PI-reviewable prompt-amendment step rather than an always-on autonomous loop.

ideas.idea: Reflection agent's multi-mode reviews: initial (no tools, fast-discard), full (with literature search), observation (does the hypothesis explain long-tail prior results), simulation review
ideas.rationale: A tiered review pipeline (cheap local triage first, expensive grounded check only on survivors) maps onto Memoria's pre-file similarity gate -> deeper structural review, and respects cheap/local-model-first cost discipline. Borrow the cascade; the 'observation review' (does this claim better explain existing findings?) is a strong idea for the argument graph.

ideas.idea: Proximity agent: builds a similarity graph of hypotheses for clustering, de-duplication, and diverse-tournament scheduling
ideas.rationale: Directly equals Memoria's record-linkage entity dedup + BERTopic clustering MCP; using a proximity graph to dedupe near-identical generated/extracted claims before they reach the gate is buildable now and prevents vault bloat.

ideas.idea: Elo-based tournament with pairwise multi-turn debate comparisons to rank hypotheses, with Elo concordant with quality (validated against GPQA)
ideas.rationale: Memoria's learning-to-rank triage and discovery relevance scoring could use pairwise/Elo ranking for prioritizing discovery candidates for PI attention. Borrow pairwise-comparison ranking; the costly multi-turn LLM-debate-per-match and test-time-compute scaling is too expensive for a single local GPU, so cap match counts.

ideas.idea: Explicit default generation criteria: alignment, plausibility, novelty (grounded, not just synthesis), testability, safety, with a dedicated safety/ethics review of every hypothesis
ideas.rationale: A clean, adoptable rubric for Memoria's structural review gate to score candidate notes/claims against before approval; novelty-vs-mere-synthesis and testability flags fit the claim-extraction schema directly.

ideas.idea: Evolution agent that only creates NEW hypotheses (never mutates top-ranked ones) so improvements must re-win the tournament, protecting existing quality
ideas.rationale: An immutability/append-only insight that fits Memoria's claim supersession model: don't overwrite an approved claim; create a candidate successor that must pass the gate. Borrow the append-then-compete discipline.

ideas.idea: Massive test-time-compute scaling and asynchronous worker-queue orchestration on Gemini 2.0 across hundreds of research goals
ideas.rationale: Cloud-frontier-model, compute-heavy scaling contradicts Memoria's local qwen2.5:7b / single-GPU, single-researcher constraints; the async-worker framework is over-engineered for one PI's nightly batch. Keep the agent roles, drop the scale.

file: Govers et al. - 2026 - Narratives and perspectives how AI summaries steer users' opinions and engagement on social media.md
title: Narratives and Perspectives: How AI Summaries Steer Users' Opinions and Engagement on Social Media
one_line: A 144-participant CHI study showing that AI percentage/agreement summaries amplify conformity to majority views while AI narrative summaries create false perceptions of balance in polarized threads, with design recommendations to mitigate these steering effects.

ideas.idea: AI narrative summaries that present opposing arguments side-by-side make polarized debates be perceived as balanced, dampening (or distorting) the reader's judgment of the true distribution
ideas.rationale: Critical caution for Memoria's contradictions dashboard and claim-aspect summaries: an even-handed two-sided summary can mislead the PI into thinking the literature is balanced when it is lopsided. Memoria should surface the actual evidence weight/count (how many papers support vs. contradict), not just a balanced prose synthesis.

ideas.idea: Displaying agreement/disagreement percentages (proportion of support) measurably shifts users toward the majority — quantified consensus is a strong steering signal
ideas.rationale: Memoria can show support/contradiction counts on the argument graph, but this paper warns such numeric framing biases the reader. Borrow as a design constraint: present counts with provenance and avoid a single dominant 'X% agree' framing that could anchor the PI prematurely.

ideas.idea: Summaries can foreground certain arguments / bury minority-but-valid positions due to majority weighting, brigading, or opaque ranking
ideas.rationale: Direct warning for Memoria's learning-to-rank triage and discovery relevance scoring: ranking can systematically hide minority/contrarian claims. The structural review gate and inspector should let the PI see what was down-ranked and why, preserving dissenting claims rather than silently suppressing them.

ideas.idea: Using a single high-quality LLM (GPT-4.5) to generate controlled, consistent synthetic discussion content for experiments
ideas.rationale: Methodologically useful for Memoria's own test fixtures: generating consistent synthetic papers/claims with controlled properties to test ingest/clustering/gate pipelines deterministically. Borrow as a testing technique.

ideas.idea: Social-media-specific findings (conformity, spiral of silence, toxicity deterring engagement, willingness-to-engage measures, political polarization)
ideas.rationale: The substantive domain (multi-user social-media opinion dynamics, engagement, toxicity) is off-domain for a single-researcher knowledge vault with no social feed or audience; only the summary-bias cautions transfer.

file: Healey and Enns - 2012 - Attention and visual memory in visualization and computer graphics.md
title: Attention and Visual Memory in Visualization and Computer Graphics
one_line: A psychophysics survey of preattentive processing, feature hierarchies, and visual-memory limits (change/inattentional blindness, attentional blink) and how they inform visualization design.

ideas.idea: Preattentive visual features (hue, luminance, orientation, size, motion) pop out in <250ms and can draw attention to targets independent of distractor count
ideas.rationale: Memoria's Obsidian dashboards (contradictions, gates, spaces) can encode the single most important signal (e.g. contradiction status, uncertainty flag) in a preattentive channel like hue/luminance so the PI spots it at a glance without serial scanning.

ideas.idea: Feature hierarchy: strong features (luminance, hue) mask weaker ones (shape, curvature); the most important attribute must get the most salient channel
ideas.rationale: Direct guidance for ranking visual encodings in projected canvas / dashboards: encode claim-supersession or contradiction (high priority) in hue, secondary metadata in shape/size, so secondary values never hide the patterns the PI cares about.

ideas.idea: Conjunction search is slow — encoding two data attributes in two features the user must jointly find produces interference
ideas.rationale: Cautionary constraint for dashboard design: don't force the PI to find notes that are simultaneously 'contradicted AND stale' by two separate visual channels; precompute and encode that conjunction as its own preattentive mark.

ideas.idea: Change blindness / inattentional blindness — viewers miss significant changes across an interrupt or when attention is elsewhere
ideas.rationale: Relevant to nightly proactive-discovery surfacing: new claims/contradictions appearing in a dashboard the PI already scanned may be missed; use bottom-up transients (Nowell-style fade-in of new clusters) so new discovery items attract attention rather than silently appearing.

ideas.idea: Postattentive amnesia — previewing a novel display gives no search benefit because contents can't be committed to long-term memory
ideas.rationale: An argument that novel visualizations need preattentive guidance, but Memoria's surfaces are reused and learned over time; the deeper engagement/aesthetics/NPR-rendering material is off-domain for a single-researcher markdown tool.

ideas.idea: Ensemble coding — viewers extract rapid summary statistics (mean size, average) of a set without individual elements
ideas.rationale: Interesting for real-time data streams but Memoria deals with discrete notes/claims, not dense glyph fields; no direct build.

ideas.idea: Saliency models (Itti-Koch) and gaze prediction / NPR attention direction / perceptual rendering budgets
ideas.rationale: Eye-tracking, rendering-budget, and photorealism techniques assume a graphics pipeline and hardware Memoria does not have and does not need.

file: Hearst - 2009 - Information visualization for search interfaces (ch 10).md
title: Information Visualization for Search Interfaces (Search User Interfaces, Ch. 10)
one_line: A survey of decades of attempts to visualize text search (query specification, term-hit displays, faceted navigation, cluster 'starfields', 3D) with the recurring empirical finding that text/faceted views beat fancy spatial visualizations.

ideas.idea: Faceted navigation: multiple hierarchical category facets, selecting a category narrows results and updates other facets' counts (query previews), with fluid refine/expand and no-empty-results
ideas.rationale: Directly buildable as an Obsidian space/Base over Memoria's typed metadata (citekey, paper aspects, claim-type, uncertainty, relations): faceted filtering with live counts is the empirically validated way for a single researcher to navigate a claim/note collection, and beats every spatial alternative in the chapter.

ideas.idea: Query previews — show the count of results before a filter is applied
ideas.rationale: Cheap, high-value: pre-file similarity gate and triage surfaces should show how many notes/claims a candidate filter would match before the PI commits, exactly the validated win Tanin/Plaisant report.

ideas.idea: TileBars: per-result glyph showing document length plus per-term/per-facet hit density across subtopic segments (TextTiling), so term overlap within a passage signals relevance
ideas.rationale: Adaptable to claim-level provenance: a compact glyph beside a paper note showing where the PI's query concepts co-occur across the paper's sections/claims would aid relevance triage; grayscale-vs-color and overlap-as-relevance are reusable insights.

ideas.idea: Empirical verdict that document-cluster 'starfield'/2D-3D spatial layouts and node-link maps consistently underperform textual/faceted views and confuse users (adjacency falsely read as relevance)
ideas.rationale: A negative result Memoria should heed: it argues AGAINST building a projected-canvas spatial map as the primary navigation surface; useful as a guardrail but offers nothing to build. (The discriminating-term suggestions from cluster maps are the one salvageable bit.)

ideas.idea: Cluster maps surface useful discriminating terms even when the spatial layout itself fails (Hornbaek: query-term ideas came from the map 11% of the time)
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's keyphrase tag candidates / BERTopic clustering MCP as a term-suggestion source rather than as a spatial display — extract the labels, drop the starfield.

ideas.idea: Read Wear / scrollbar hit-location visualization for showing query-term positions within a long document
ideas.rationale: A within-note minimap of where claims/query-terms/contradictions sit could aid the read-only inspector; lightweight and PI-accessible in Obsidian.

ideas.idea: Textually-enhanced thumbnails and image thumbnails of documents in result lists
ideas.rationale: Studies show thumbnails alone are error-prone and inflate false relevance; Memoria's results are claims/notes, not web pages, so rendered-page thumbnails add cost without value.

ideas.idea: Visual Boolean query builders (Venn/VQuery, filter-flow, magic lenses)
ideas.rationale: The chapter reports these never caught on vs. category-label selection; faceted selection (adopted above) is the validated path, so the Boolean-diagram variants are superseded.

file: Heer and Bostock - 2010 - Crowdsourcing graphical perception using mechanical turk to assess visualization design.md
title: Crowdsourcing Graphical Perception: Using Mechanical Turk to Assess Visualization Design
one_line: Validates Amazon Mechanical Turk for graphical-perception experiments by replicating Cleveland-McGill and Stone-Bartram, and derives design defaults (position>length>area; gridline alpha~0.2; chart height plateau ~80px; gridlines >=8px apart).

ideas.idea: Concrete perceptual-encoding rankings and defaults: position beats length beats angle/area for quantitative comparison; squarified-treemap 1:1 aspect ratios are actually WORST for area judgment
ideas.rationale: Settles encoding choices for any quantitative chart Memoria renders (dashboards, admin HTML reports): prefer position/length encodings for claim counts/scores over area/pie, and don't trust treemaps for magnitude — usable without any user study.

ideas.idea: Gridline alpha ~0.2 safe default and gridlines spaced >=8px; chart height >80px gives no accuracy gain on a 0-100 scale
ideas.rationale: Drop-in styling defaults for Memoria's static admin reports / projected canvas charts; saves the PI from re-deriving sensible chart parameters.

ideas.idea: Crowdsourcing methodology: qualification tasks for comprehension + verifiable questions to deter gaming; higher pay speeds completion but does not improve quality; 'streakers vs samplers' completion distribution
ideas.rationale: Memoria is explicitly single-researcher and local; recruiting crowd workers for evaluation contradicts its scale and privacy posture, so the MTurk operational machinery is inapplicable.

ideas.idea: Verifiable-question / qualification-gate pattern to ensure responders understood the task before trusting their output
ideas.rationale: The abstract pattern — gate an actor with a comprehension check and reject grossly-wrong outputs outright — maps onto Memoria's structural review gate and LLM-judge quality checks (e.g. require an engine/agent to pass a verifiable self-check before its extraction is accepted), even though the human-crowd context is rejected.

file: Hightower and Borriello - 2001 - Location systems for ubiquitous computing.md
title: Location Systems for Ubiquitous Computing
one_line: A taxonomy and survey of physical/symbolic location-sensing systems (GPS, Active Badge/Bat, Cricket, RADAR, vision, RFID) classified along axes like accuracy/precision, scale, recognition, and cost.

ideas.idea: Physical vs. symbolic location distinction, and augmenting a physical position with an external database to derive symbolic meaning (room, activity)
ideas.rationale: Off-domain: Memoria has no spatial-sensing or location-awareness requirement; the abstraction does not map to claims/notes.

ideas.idea: Report accuracy AND precision together as an error distribution (e.g. Gaussian, sigma, plus dependencies) rather than a single number
ideas.rationale: The only transferable nugget: Memoria's discovery relevance scores, triage ranks, and dedup/record-linkage confidences should be reported as calibrated distributions/uncertainty (accuracy + how-often), echoing the paper's call for honest error reporting — fits Memoria's faithfulness-over-flash and uncertainty-flag pillars.

ideas.idea: Sensor fusion: statistically merge multiple independent estimators with different error distributions to beat any single one; the more independent, the better
ideas.rationale: Analogous to fusing multiple weak relevance/entity-match signals (keyphrase overlap, embedding similarity, citation overlap) in the learning-to-rank triage and record-linkage dedup; independence-improves-fusion is a reusable principle.

ideas.idea: Localized computation for privacy — the located object computes its own position so no infrastructure learns its location unless it publishes
ideas.rationale: Privacy-by-local-computation is already a Memoria axiom (local-only, MCP-only sandbox), so this adds nothing new; and the spatial framing is irrelevant.

ideas.idea: GUID + external-database lookup for recognition/identity resolution
ideas.rationale: Memoria already has a citekey/Zotero backbone and record-linkage entity dedup serving this role; the badge/RFID framing is superseded for its domain.

file: Hill et al. - 1992 - Edit wear and read wear.md
title: Edit Wear and Read Wear
one_line: Introduces 'computational wear' — accumulating and displaying the history of edit and read activity directly on a document (e.g. scrollbar marks showing time spent reading each region and how often each line was edited), so usage history becomes a navigable, informative part of the artifact.

ideas.idea: Computational wear: record per-region read time and per-line edit frequency and render it as visual 'wear' marks on the document/scrollbar
ideas.rationale: Highly aligned with a personal single-researcher system: Memoria can accumulate the PI's own read/edit/revisit history on notes and claims and surface it as wear marks in the Obsidian read-only inspector — which claims the PI dwells on, which paragraphs get re-edited — turning usage into a navigation and salience signal at zero modeling cost.

ideas.idea: History of activity as a first-class, persistent attribute of the artifact rather than ephemeral logs
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's provenance/supersession tracking: attach read/edit wear as durable metadata on notes and feed it into triage/relevance ranking (frequently-revisited claims rank higher), consistent with the discovery-relevance and learning-to-rank machinery.

ideas.idea: Wear as an implicit recommendation signal aggregated across many users/readers
ideas.rationale: The multi-reader collaborative-filtering interpretation assumes a community of users; Memoria is single-PI, so cross-user aggregation does not apply — only the self-history variant is adopted above.

file: Sun et al. - 2023 - Recitation-augmented language models.md
title: Recitation-Augmented Language Models (RECITE)
one_line: Proposes a recite-and-answer paradigm where an LLM first samples a relevant passage from its own parametric memory and then answers, improving closed-book QA without an external corpus.

ideas.idea: Recite-then-answer: have the model generate a relevant passage from parametric memory before answering, as an intermediate grounding step.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is corpus-grounded by design (Zotero/citekey backbone, faithfulness over flash, contradictions/supersession). Inventing passages from a 7B local model's weights instead of retrieving from the vault is exactly the hallucination/bias risk Memoria's structural gate exists to prevent; the paper itself warns RECITE amplifies model biases vs RAG.

ideas.idea: Self-consistency over multiple sampled recitations with majority voting to improve robustness.
ideas.rationale: The general insight (sample several candidate extractions, then aggregate/vote) is reusable for claim/aspect extraction in ingest where a single qwen2.5:7b pass is noisy — multi-sample + agreement could raise extraction confidence and feed an uncertainty flag, without adopting recitation itself.

ideas.idea: Passage-hint diversification: deduplicate by section-title-derived hints to force diverse, non-overlapping evidence before aggregating.
ideas.rationale: The dedup-by-structural-hint trick maps loosely onto Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and record-linkage dedup: using section-path identifiers to detect near-duplicate evidence/notes before merging is a cheap, deterministic heuristic worth adapting.

ideas.idea: Recite-vs-retrieve-vs-ground-truth ablation showing BM25 retrieval remains a strong baseline over model recitation.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's choice to keep a deterministic lexical/retrieval path (BM25-class) in the retriever profile rather than trusting generative recall; useful evidence for the retriever design but not a buildable feature.

file: Sundar - 2020 - Rise of machine agency a framework for studying the psychology of human–AI interaction (HAII).md
title: Rise of Machine Agency: A Framework for Studying the Psychology of Human–AI Interaction (HAII)
one_line: A communication-theory framework (HAII-TIME) arguing AI media affect users via a cue route (machine heuristic, transparency) and an action route (control, collaboration, ground-truthing), and that machine agency must be negotiated with human agency.

ideas.idea: Reactive personalization (ask user assent before acting) is preferred over proactive personalization (auto-pushing recommendations).
ideas.rationale: Directly validates Memoria's structural review gate and 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' model: the discovery loop should surface candidates for PI assent rather than silently writing notes. This is the psychological grounding for keeping the gate, not auto-filing.

ideas.idea: User control + awareness ('Algorithmic Experience'): users should see what the algorithm tracks, corroborate it, and direct its future behavior.
ideas.rationale: Maps onto Memoria's PI-accessible surfaces — the read-only inspector, dashboards/gates, and learning-to-rank triage should expose why a paper was ranked/surfaced and let the PI correct it, satisfying the awareness+control hallmarks the paper identifies.

ideas.idea: Ground-truthing: let the user feed corrections back as training signal so each person's circumstances refine the system's inferences.
ideas.rationale: This is exactly the loop for learning-to-rank triage and discovery relevance scoring — PI approve/reject decisions at the gate become labeled feedback that tunes future ranking. Single-researcher scale makes this personal ground-truthing especially apt.

ideas.idea: Machine heuristic / automation bias: users over-trust outputs framed as algorithmic and become less vigilant (e.g., for fake-news flags).
ideas.rationale: A design caution for Memoria: claim extraction, contradiction flags, and uncertainty markers must be presented so the PI stays critical (show provenance + confidence), not as authoritative machine verdicts — informs UI framing of the contradictions dashboard.

ideas.idea: Transparency of operation reduces 'folk theories'/algorithmic anxiety and increases trust.
ideas.rationale: Supports making the projection/ranking engines auditable (static HTML admin reports, inspector) so the PI isn't reverse-engineering why notes were filed; partial because the paper is multi-user-product oriented while Memoria is single-user.

file: Talukder et al. - 2026 - Towards automated ontology generation from unstructured text a multi-agent LLM approach.md
title: Towards Automated Ontology Generation from Unstructured Text: A Multi-Agent LLM Approach
one_line: A controlled study showing that decomposing LLM ontology construction into artifact-driven roles (Domain Expert → Manager → Coder → QA) with front-loaded planning beats single-pass generation, and that gains come from externalized planning, not the downstream bug-fixing loop.

ideas.idea: Artifact-driven multi-agent pipeline (DAG of roles, each emitting a consumable, traceable artifact: requirements doc → implementation plan → code → QA findings).
ideas.rationale: Strong template for Memoria's ingest/structuring pipeline under the MCP-only sandbox: separate extraction (engine), planning/judging (agent), and writing, with each stage producing an auditable artifact the structural gate can review. Matches 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' and gives provenance for free.

ideas.idea: Front-loaded planning is the dominant quality driver; collapsing planning and implementation into one step ('illusion of thinking') drops constraints and produces unanswerable structures.
ideas.rationale: Directly argues against single-prompt claim/relation extraction on a small local model. Memoria should plan structure first (which typed relations/claims a note needs) then emit them, freeing the 7B model's context for faithful encoding — a concrete reliability win at single-researcher scale.

ideas.idea: Competency-Question-driven evaluation: derive CQs as functional requirements, then auto-test the artifact by generating SPARQL and scoring answerability.
ideas.rationale: Adapt as a self-test for Memoria's structured output: generate a few 'can this note answer X?' probes per ingested paper and verify the extracted claims/relations actually support them — a deterministic-ish quality check feeding the gate, though Memoria isn't building OWL.

ideas.idea: Dual evaluation: strict SPARQL (formal queryability) plus fault-tolerant vector-RAG topological probe (2-hop traversal over the graph) to separate 'knowledge absent' from 'query-formulation failure'.
ideas.rationale: The insight that retrieval failure ≠ knowledge absence, and that a graph-traversal probe measures connectivity, is useful for evaluating Memoria's argument graph / typed-relation graph health (disconnected/star fragments signal poor structuring).

ideas.idea: Heterogeneous LLM-judge panel (5 model families) with Krippendorff's alpha and separation of generation vs judging prompts to mitigate evaluation circularity.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's quality (L5) tier uses an LLM judge; the safeguards — distinct judge prompt, multiple judges, inter-rater agreement reporting, and noting that redundancy/global-comparison judgments are least reliable — transfer, though a single-researcher local setup can't run 5 prod models so this is partial.

ideas.idea: Redundancy is fundamentally harder for LLMs than local pattern application because it needs global state comparison across the evolving artifact.
ideas.rationale: Validates Memoria's record-linkage entity dedup and pre-file similarity gate as deterministic components rather than trusting the model to avoid duplicate entities/claims — the paper empirically shows LLMs introduce slightly-varied duplicates instead of reusing existing ones.

ideas.idea: Autonomous remediation pathologies: bug-fixer fixates on the reported line, suffers 'state amnesia' after history-clearing, and hallucinates syntax/bloat; bounded iteration with a high cap is mostly wasted compute.
ideas.rationale: Concrete warning for any Memoria agent self-repair loop: prefer first-pass correctness + a tight bounded retry, and surface unrecoverable cases to the PI gate rather than burning local-GPU cycles. Reinforces keeping fixes deterministic where possible.

ideas.idea: Demonstrated at 30B-class local model for privacy-sensitive, resource-constrained deployment.
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's local/cheap-model posture; 30B is above the test-env qwen default but the paper shows the planning-first decomposition is what makes a modest local model usable — the architecture compensates for model size.

file: Tam et al. - 2021 - Improving and simplifying pattern exploiting training.md
title: Improving and Simplifying Pattern Exploiting Training (ADAPET)
one_line: ADAPET improves few-shot PET by decoupling label losses (BCE over the full vocabulary) and adding a label-conditioned MLM objective, beating GPT-3 and iPET on SuperGLUE with 32 labels and no unlabeled data.

ideas.idea: Cloze/pattern-verbalizer reformulation of classification tasks for few-shot learning without large models.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's claim-sentence classification and keyphrase tagging run on small/local models with scarce labels; pattern-verbalizer framing is a cheap way to get few-shot classifiers without fine-tuning a big model, fitting the cheap-local-model constraint.

ideas.idea: Dense supervision via decoupled label losses (penalize incorrect class tokens across the whole vocab) + label conditioning, removing the need for task-specific unlabeled data.
ideas.rationale: This is a training-time fine-tuning method for ALBERT-class masked LMs. Memoria does not train/fine-tune models (deterministic ingest, off-the-shelf local generative models); implementing ADAPET's objective is out of scope and superseded by instruction-tuned LLM prompting.

ideas.idea: Few-shot setups should avoid assuming task-specific unlabeled data, since acquiring it can be as expensive as labeling.
ideas.rationale: A useful constraint reminder for single-researcher Memoria: don't design classifiers/rankers that presuppose a large in-domain unlabeled pool; favor methods that work from the PI's handful of gate decisions.

file: Tam et al. - 2022 - Parameter-efficient prompt tuning makes generalized and calibrated neural text retrievers.md
title: Parameter-Efficient Prompt Tuning Makes Generalized and Calibrated Neural Text Retrievers
one_line: Shows that parameter-efficient prompt tuning (P-Tuning v2) of dense/late-interaction retrievers matches fine-tuning in-domain while substantially improving zero-shot cross-domain and cross-topic generalization, attributed to better confidence calibration and query-length robustness.

ideas.idea: Parameter-efficient prompt tuning (tune ~0.1% of params) generalizes better out-of-domain than full fine-tuning for neural retrievers.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's retriever/scout profile faces a cross-topic distribution shift (a single PI's reading spans many fields, like OAG-QA's 87 topics). If Memoria ever adapts a dense retriever, PE prompt tuning is the right knob — cheap, generalizes — but Memoria more likely uses off-the-shelf embeddings, so this is an option not a commitment.

ideas.idea: Confidence calibration (low ECE) is a proxy for, and driver of, out-of-domain retrieval generalization.
ideas.rationale: Directly usable: Memoria's discovery relevance scoring and learning-to-rank triage should be calibrated (predicted relevance ≈ true relevance) and ECE-monitored, so the proactive discovery loop's confidence is trustworthy at the gate rather than over/under-confident on off-domain papers.

ideas.idea: Fine-tuning biases retrievers to the training query/doc length (DPR biased to ~100-word docs); PE methods that don't touch positional embeddings are more query-length robust.
ideas.rationale: Useful diagnostic for Memoria's retriever: paper abstracts vs short note-titles vs long claim sets have very different lengths; a length-robustness check (and not over-tuning to one length regime) protects retrieval quality across the vault's heterogeneous text.

ideas.idea: Neural dense retrievers underperform BM25 on cross-domain generalization unless specifically helped.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces keeping a BM25/lexical baseline in Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and retriever as a robust fallback, since a single researcher's interests drift across domains where a fixed dense model may degrade.

ideas.idea: OAG-QA cross-topic academic retrieval dataset (87 fine-grained topics) as a generalization benchmark.
ideas.rationale: A released benchmark dataset, not a mechanism; Memoria evaluates against the PI's own vault, not a public multi-topic leaderboard, so adopting the dataset offers nothing buildable.

file: Su et al. - 2025 - BRIGHT a realistic and challenging benchmark for reasoning-intensive retrieval.md
title: BRIGHT: A Realistic and Challenging Benchmark for Reasoning-Intensive Retrieval
one_line: A retrieval benchmark of 1,384 realistic queries across 12 domains where relevance requires multi-step reasoning (shared theorem/algorithm/principle) rather than lexical or semantic overlap, showing SOTA dense retrievers collapse from ~59 to ~18-24 nDCG@10 and that LLM-generated reasoning steps used as queries recover up to +12 points.

ideas.idea: Reasoning-intensive retrieval as a distinct 'level 3' regime: relevance defined by shared underlying principle/theorem/algorithm, not surface overlap, so dense+BM25 retrievers fail badly.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's whole premise is connecting claims/notes by shared warrants, theories, and methods (argument graph, typed relations). This paper proves naive embedding similarity will miss exactly the cross-paper connections Memoria most wants. It justifies why the pre-file similarity gate and discovery scorer must go beyond cosine similarity and reason about why two papers relate.

ideas.idea: Query expansion via LLM reasoning traces: prompt 'identify the essential problem; reason step-by-step about what relevant docs contain; draft an answer', then retrieve with that expanded query (+up to 12.2 nDCG@10, BM25 benefits most).
ideas.rationale: Directly buildable in the nightly discovery loop and retriever/scout profile: have the local LLM (qwen2.5:7b) write a short reasoning trace / hypothetical relevant-passage for each PI interest before hitting the vector/BM25 index. Cheap, local, deterministic-friendly, and the finding that BM25 (which Memoria can run locally) gains the most is ideal for a single-researcher local stack.

ideas.idea: LLM reranking of top-k beats trained cross-encoders here; an MS-MARCO-trained MiniLM cross-encoder actually HURT (relevance fell as k grew) because it doesn't transfer to reasoning relevance.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's learning-to-rank triage should not lean on an off-the-shelf MS-MARCO cross-encoder for connecting scholarly claims; an LLM-judge reranker over a small candidate set fits the 'agents judge' governance model. Borrow the two-stage retrieve-then-LLM-rerank shape, but keep k small for cost.

ideas.idea: Citation-grounded relevance labels: a doc is 'positive' only if cited in an accepted/upvoted answer AND unanimously confirmed by annotators to supply a critical concept needed to reason through the query.
ideas.rationale: Maps to Memoria's citekey backbone and structural review gate: 'a note is relevant to a claim if it is actually cited/used as a warrant, verified at the gate' is a cleaner relevance signal than topical similarity. Borrow the principle for relation/edge quality, not the crowd-annotation machinery (single-PI scale).

ideas.idea: Robustness to pretraining leakage: continuing to train on the corpus (without query->doc mappings) barely moved scores, showing the task needs reasoning, not memorized in-domain text.
ideas.rationale: Benchmark-integrity finding about model contamination; nothing to build for a single-researcher local vault.

ideas.idea: Long-context retrieval over unsplit web pages with reduced pool is still hard (recall@1 <= 27.8); passage splitting matters.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's deterministic-ingest choice to split papers into atomic claims/passages rather than embed whole PDFs; weak but supports the chunking design.

file: Sun et al. - 2022 - BBTv2 towards a gradient-free future with large language models.md
title: BBTv2: Towards a Gradient-Free Future with Large Language Models
one_line: Improves Black-Box Tuning by inserting deep continuous prompts at every transformer layer and optimizing them with a divide-and-conquer CMA-ES (one low-dim random projection + optimizer per layer), matching full fine-tuning on few-shot NLU without any gradients.

ideas.idea: Gradient-free deep-prompt optimization (CMA-ES in a random low-dim subspace per layer) to adapt a frozen black-box LLM via inference-API calls only.
ideas.rationale: Memoria runs local open models (qwen2.5:7b) it fully controls and prefers prompt/skill engineering over any training; it never adapts a remote black-box API. The whole motivating scenario (Language-Model-as-a-Service, no weight access) is exactly what Memoria's local-first design avoids.

ideas.idea: Divide-and-conquer for high-dimensional derivative-free optimization, exploiting residual-connection additivity to decompose into per-layer subproblems.
ideas.rationale: Niche optimization technique with no surface in Memoria's ingest/retrieval/governance pipeline; no component does black-box continuous optimization.

ideas.idea: Match the random-projection variance to the model's embedding/hidden-state distribution (model-specific normal sigma) for cross-model generalization and faster convergence.
ideas.rationale: Internal to the BBT method; irrelevant to a markdown-vault knowledge system.

file: Sun et al. - 2022 - Black-box tuning for language-model-as-a-service.md
title: Black-Box Tuning for Language-Model-as-a-Service
one_line: Introduces the LMaaS setting and Black-Box Tuning: optimize a frozen LLM's continuous input prompt using CMA-ES in a random low-dim subspace (exploiting PTMs' low intrinsic dimensionality), beating manual prompts, in-context learning, and even gradient-based prompt/full tuning in few-shot NLU.

ideas.idea: Black-box continuous-prompt tuning via derivative-free CMA-ES over inference-API outputs only (no gradients, no weight access).
ideas.rationale: Solves a problem Memoria doesn't have: Memoria owns its local models and tunes behavior through prompts/skills/profiles, never via gradient-free weight-equivalent optimization against a remote API.

ideas.idea: Mixed-task batching on the server so one frozen LLM serves many tasks by swapping per-task prompts (deployment efficiency of a single shared model).
ideas.rationale: Conceptually echoes Memoria's compiled-profiles model: one base model, many task-specific compiled prompt/skill bundles rather than per-task model copies. Useful framing only; Memoria already commits to this and gains nothing concrete from the LMaaS mechanics (single-user, no batching pressure).

ideas.idea: Loss-shaping insight: cross-entropy / hinge over candidate label-word logits gives far more signal than negative-accuracy in few-shot, low-data settings.
ideas.rationale: Specific to training/optimizing a classifier; Memoria's claim-sentence classification and aspect extraction use prompted local LLMs, not DFO over reward signals.

ideas.idea: Exploit low intrinsic dimensionality of large PTMs (a few hundred parameters suffice to adapt) for cheap adaptation.
ideas.rationale: Adaptation-theory point with no buildable surface in Memoria; the system does not perform any parameter adaptation.

file: Sun et al. - 2022 - NSP-BERT a prompt-based few-shot learner through an original pre-training task--next sentence predi.md
title: NSP-BERT: A Prompt-Based Few-Shot Learner Through Next Sentence Prediction
one_line: Repurposes BERT's abandoned Next-Sentence-Prediction head as a sentence-level prompt mechanism: cast classification/entailment/entity-linking as 'does candidate label-sentence follow the input?', avoiding fixed-length mask spans and enabling variable-length label/entity descriptions for zero/few-shot.

ideas.idea: Sentence-level prompting that scores variable-length candidate descriptions (vs token-level MLM masks of fixed length), enabling entity linking / typing where candidate descriptions differ in length.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's record-linkage entity dedup and entity/relation extraction must compare an entity mention against variable-length candidate canonical descriptions. The NSP framing (score 'does this candidate description match this mention in context?') is a clean local-classifier pattern, though Memoria would realize it via a prompted local LLM rather than a BERT NSP head.

ideas.idea: Two-stage prompt: stage 1 surfaces the target span at end of sentence A for context, stage 2 supplies the candidate sense/description in sentence B; big gains on entity linking/typing.
ideas.rationale: A concrete disambiguation template Memoria's entity-dedup / record-linkage gate can imitate: first anchor the ambiguous mention with surrounding context, then test each candidate canonical entity. Cheap and deterministic-friendly for the pre-file similarity gate.

ideas.idea: Samples-contrast answer mapping: for entailment-style tasks where most IsNext probs cluster near 1, rank a batch by score and split by the dev-set label distribution rather than thresholding each sample alone.
ideas.rationale: Assumes a known label prior and batch-relative calibration over a test set; Memoria classifies items as they arrive (streaming ingest) and at single-researcher scale lacks the labeled dev distributions this trick needs.

ideas.idea: Pre-training corpus, not just model size or prompt method, is the decisive determinant of few-shot quality (continued pretraining on RoBERTa's corpus beat prompt-method changes); demand equal-corpus baselines for fair comparison.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's model-selection discipline (validated qwen2.5:7b / qwen3:14b): for the extraction/classification engines, the base model's training data dominates quality, so swapping prompt tricks is secondary to picking the right local checkpoint. A guiding heuristic, not a feature.

ideas.idea: Reusing a pre-existing pretraining head (NSP) instead of a freshly initialized classifier head preserves zero-shot capability into few-shot (ablation: re-init heads degrade and destabilize results).
ideas.rationale: BERT-head-architecture specific; Memoria uses generative prompted models with no fine-tuned heads, so there is no head to preserve or reinitialize.

file: Sun et al. - 2022 - Paradigm shift in natural language processing.md
title: Paradigm Shift in Natural Language Processing
one_line: A survey arguing that NLP tasks are increasingly solved by reformulating them into one of seven paradigms (Class, Matching, SeqLab, MRC, Seq2Seq, Seq2ASeq, (M)LM), with (M)LM, Matching, MRC, and Seq2Seq emerging as unifying paradigms a single PTM can serve.

ideas.idea: MRC paradigm as a universal reformulation: cast NER, relation extraction, coreference, entity linking, ABSA, event extraction as (context, question, answer-span) — interpretable because the answer is a grounded span, and generalizes zero-shot to unseen relations via relation-specific questions.
ideas.rationale: Directly applicable to Memoria's entity/relation extraction and argument-graph construction: ask the local LLM relation-specific questions ('Find the method this claim uses', 'Which prior claim does this contradict?') and require the answer to be an extracted span. Span-grounding gives the faithfulness/traceability Memoria's structural review gate demands (every extracted relation points to source text) and supports zero-shot new relation types without retraining.

ideas.idea: Matching / textual-entailment as a unifying paradigm: reformulate classification and relation judgments as 'does premise entail hypothesis?'; reuse Entailment/Contradiction/Neutral output directly without a verbalizer.
ideas.rationale: This is the natural backbone for Memoria's contradictions dashboard and claim supersession: testing whether a new claim entails, contradicts, or is neutral to an existing claim is exactly an NLI/entailment formulation. The note that entailment output needs no verbalizer (cleaner than (M)LM prompting) is a concrete design choice for the contradiction/warrant engine.

ideas.idea: (M)LM / prompt-based tuning taxonomy: prompts (manual / mined / paraphrased / model-generated / gradient-learned) and verbalizers (manual / searched / knowledge-base-refined) as the design space.
ideas.rationale: A useful checklist for Memoria's claim-sentence classification and aspect extraction prompts: prefer manual + knowledge-base-refined verbalizers (deterministic, inspectable) and avoid gradient-learned/continuous prompts (no training in Memoria). Borrow the taxonomy as a prompt-engineering guide, not the tuning machinery.

ideas.idea: Cross-task nearest-neighbor domain adaptation for entailment models (match instance/class representations across source and target domains to transfer with limited annotation).
ideas.rationale: Memoria's contradiction/relation classifier will face domain drift across the PI's subfields with almost no labels; the idea of anchoring to class-representation prototypes (few PI-curated exemplars per relation type) to steer a general entailment model is adaptable to single-researcher scale via the structured-capture forms.

ideas.idea: Seq2Seq as the most flexible unifying paradigm for structured/complex output, but with auto-regressive inference latency as the cost.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's extraction engines do emit structured output (claims, typed relations) generatively; the explicit latency caveat is a useful constraint reminder for the local stack (favor smaller models / bounded outputs for nightly batch work). Insight only.

ideas.idea: Compound paradigms: complex tasks (multi-hop QA) decompose into Matching (find relevant docs) + MRC (select span).
ideas.rationale: Validates Memoria's pipeline decomposition: discovery/retrieval (Matching) then claim/relation extraction (MRC) as composable stages with a gate between, rather than one monolithic prompt. Architectural framing.

file: Ackerman - 2000 - The intellectual challenge of CSCW the gap between social requirements and technical feasibility.md
title: The Intellectual Challenge of CSCW: The Gap Between Social Requirements and Technical Feasibility
one_line: A foundational CSCW essay arguing there is an enduring 'social-technical gap' between the nuanced, contextual, ambiguous flexibility human activity requires and the rigid, explicit behavior systems can support, and proposing 'first-order approximations' (especially augmentative/critic architectures) as the way to design around it rather than to close it.

ideas.idea: The social-technical gap: systems cannot fully mechanize nuanced, context-dependent human judgment, so designers should aim for tractable approximations with known trade-offs rather than full automation.
ideas.rationale: Directly underwrites Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' governance and structural review gate: claim extraction/relations/triage are deliberately approximations surfaced for human adjudication, not autonomous decisions. This essay is the canonical justification for keeping the PI in the loop instead of pretending the pipeline is 'correct'.

ideas.idea: Critic / augmentative architecture: many small, separable 'critics' that watch over the user's shoulder and make suggestions/warnings without taking action; user can turn them on/off.
ideas.rationale: Maps almost exactly onto Memoria's MCP-only agent sandbox (agents never act, only advise), contradictions dashboard, pre-file similarity gate, and discovery relevance scoring as independent advisory signals. The 'hundreds of small critics, no single accurate agent' framing legitimizes Memoria's many-small-engines-plus-gate design over one monolithic autonomous agent.

ideas.idea: Augmentation over delegation: prefer architectures that 'do not invoke the gap' — they provide advice/resources the user can judge, rather than mechanizing the social environment.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces the ADR rule that everything must be PI-accessible and judgeable in the Obsidian UI; engines surface evidence/scores the PI evaluates, they do not silently file or delete.

ideas.idea: Provide a secondary communication/backchannel so users can negotiate norms, exceptions and breakdowns the system cannot encode.
ideas.rationale: For a single-researcher system there is no group to negotiate with, but the insight maps to giving the PI override/annotation surfaces at the gate (structured capture forms, approval/rejection with reasons) so the rigid pipeline's exceptions are handled by the human rather than forced into the schema.

ideas.idea: Categories are political and people re-categorize over time; rigid role/category schemes break because users tailor and re-tailor them.
ideas.rationale: Cautions Memoria's typed-relation vocabulary, MASSW aspects and tag taxonomy to stay PI-editable and evolvable (gate-vs-space vocabulary already shows this churn); avoid hard-coding a closed ontology the PI cannot bend.

ideas.idea: Critical-mass / buy-in problem for CSCW systems (need many simultaneous users to be useful).
ideas.rationale: Memoria is explicitly single-researcher; multi-user adoption dynamics, floor/session/concurrency control and group reward structures are out of scope by design constraint.

ideas.idea: Reframe the field as a Simonian 'science of the artificial' with a study-design-theory spiral.
ideas.rationale: Meta-disciplinary positioning argument; offers no buildable mechanism for Memoria beyond the iterative-design ethos already implicit in the ADR process.

file: Acquisti et al. - 2015 - Privacy and human behavior in the age of information.md
title: Privacy and Human Behavior in the Age of Information
one_line: A Science review synthesizing behavioral-economics evidence that privacy preferences are uncertain, context-dependent, and malleable (manipulable via defaults, framing, interface cues), concluding that transparency-and-control policies alone backfire and protection must not rely on rational individual choice.

ideas.idea: Defaults are powerful: people stick to default settings and read them as implicit recommendations; default visibility/opt-in-vs-opt-out drives behavior more than stated preferences.
ideas.rationale: Direct UX guidance for Memoria's gate and capture forms: choose conservative, faithfulness-preserving defaults (e.g. default to 'needs review' / not-auto-published, uncertainty flags on by default) because the PI will mostly accept defaults. A single-user system, but the default-as-recommendation effect still governs how the PI interacts with the approval gate.

ideas.idea: Control paradox: giving users more granular control can increase disclosure/oversharing and create a false sense of safety; transparency mechanisms are easily rendered ineffective.
ideas.rationale: Warns against over-engineering fine-grained knobs in the structural review gate / inspector. More controls do not equal better outcomes; Memoria should keep the gate simple and the defaults safe rather than offload judgment onto a wall of toggles the PI won't tune.

ideas.idea: Malicious / dark-pattern interface design and cue manipulation (casual vs formal UI changes disclosure).
ideas.rationale: Adversarial-manipulation framing assumes a commercial entity with interests opposed to the user; irrelevant to a local single-PI vault with no third-party data extraction.

ideas.idea: Privacy is fundamentally about boundary management between public/private audiences across contexts (contextual integrity).
ideas.rationale: Memoria is a local, single-user, non-sharing system (memoria-private is hands-off production for one PI); there is no audience-boundary or disclosure-to-others problem to manage, so this core theme has no surface in the architecture.

ideas.idea: Policy should protect 'real people who are naive, uncertain, vulnerable' with baseline protections not requiring rational choice.
ideas.rationale: Regulatory/public-policy conclusion aimed at firms and governments; no Memoria mechanism to build from.

file: Agrawal et al. - 2022 - Large language models are few-shot clinical information extractors.md
title: Large Language Models are Few-Shot Clinical Information Extractors
one_line: Shows that a general LLM with carefully guided prompts plus lightweight deterministic 'resolvers' can do zero/few-shot clinical NER, span/coreference, and relation extraction competitively with supervised models, and that its outputs can be distilled via weak supervision into a small local task-specific model.

ideas.idea: Guided prompt design + resolver pattern: a one-shot example that fixes the OUTPUT FORMAT (content may even be wrong) steers the LLM toward a structured output, so a tiny deterministic post-processor (<10 LOC) can map it into the schema.
ideas.rationale: This is the core recipe for Memoria's deterministic-ingest constraint: keep the cheap local LLM (qwen2.5:7b) doing the linguistic judgment while a small deterministic resolver enforces the atomic-claim / relation / MASSW-aspect schema. The format-vs-content separation (and the finding that example correctness barely matters, only format) is directly buildable into claim extraction and typed-relation extraction prompts.

ideas.idea: Resolver complexity measured in lines-of-code as a proxy; cleaner prompts shrink the resolver.
ideas.rationale: A concrete engineering discipline for the ingest pipeline: invest prompt effort to minimize brittle regex/post-processing, which aligns with 'deterministic ingest, faithfulness over flash' and reduces the surface the structural review gate must police.

ideas.idea: Treat LLM+resolver as a LABELER (weak supervision) and distill into a smaller PubMedBERT-style model that runs locally and can transfer to data you cannot send to an API; distillation can even boost accuracy.
ideas.rationale: Strong fit for Memoria's local/cheap-model preference and 16GB GPU constraint: use a one-time labeling pass (even from a larger model) to train a small local extractor/classifier for claim-sentence classification or status tagging, removing per-document API cost and keeping data on-vault.

ideas.idea: Bias toward outputting a non-empty answer even when none exists (LLM invents a medication when the true list is empty), mitigated by chaining (first ask 'does X exist?') or by sequence-tagging.
ideas.rationale: Directly relevant failure mode for atomic-claim extraction and contradiction detection: Memoria's engines must guard against hallucinated claims/relations. Adopt the 'gate existence before extraction' chaining and/or tagging approach to keep faithfulness, which the structural review gate then double-checks.

ideas.idea: Multi-prompt sampling (several 1-shot prompts) to estimate when the model is UNCERTAIN, important for high-stakes extraction.
ideas.rationale: Operationalizes Memoria's 'atomic claims WITH uncertainty flags' pillar: sample-disagreement across prompts is a cheap, local way to populate the uncertainty flag rather than trusting a single generation.

ideas.idea: Build small expert-annotated benchmarks by re-annotating a public dataset (CASI) to evaluate extraction under data-use constraints.
ideas.rationale: Memoria can mirror this by maintaining a small PI-curated gold set of papers/claims to regression-test the ingest engines (claim extraction, relation typing) locally, supporting the 'verify the inference, not just the file' discipline.

ideas.idea: Use an LLM-edit endpoint for abbreviation/acronym expansion, picking the answer by highest character overlap with candidates.
ideas.rationale: A clean trick for entity normalization / record-linkage dedup in Memoria (e.g. citekey/author/venue disambiguation): generate-then-match-by-overlap is a deterministic resolver well-suited to the pre-file similarity gate, though it must run on a local model.

ideas.idea: Specific clinical domain framing (medications, i2b2, MIMIC) and the use of OpenAI GPT-3 APIs.
ideas.rationale: Off-domain (clinical EHR) and cloud-API-dependent; Memoria ingests scientific papers locally. The transferable content is the method, not the clinical task or the hosted-model assumption.

file: Ajith et al. - 2024 - LitSearch a retrieval benchmark for scientific literature search.md
title: LitSearch: A Retrieval Benchmark for Scientific Literature Search
one_line: Introduces a 597-question benchmark of realistic natural-language literature-search queries (GPT-4-rewritten inline citations plus author-written questions) and shows instruction-tuned dense retrievers (GritLM) far outperform BM25 and commercial search engines, with modest gains from LLM reranking and little benefit from indexing full text.

ideas.idea: Instruction-finetuned dense retrievers (GritLM, E5, Instructor) with task-specific instruction strings ('Given a research query, retrieve the title and abstract of the relevant paper') strongly outperform BM25 (+24.8% recall@5) on natural-language literature questions.
ideas.rationale: Directly informs Memoria's retriever/scout profile and discovery relevance scoring: prefer an instruction-tuned embedding retriever over lexical BM25 for the PI's question-shaped discovery queries. GritLM-7B is a local, open model that fits the cheap/open-model constraint and the 16GB GPU.

ideas.idea: Indexing only title+abstract beats indexing full paper text for current embedding models (context-length and training-length mismatch make full text hurt more than help).
ideas.rationale: Concrete, cost-saving guidance for Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and nightly discovery loop: embed title+abstract (and perhaps extracted atomic claims) rather than whole PDFs, which is cheaper and empirically better with small local encoders.

ideas.idea: LLM reranking (vanilla listwise over top-100) gives a real but modest lift (~4-5% recall@5) on top of a strong dense retriever.
ideas.rationale: Worth a local-model rerank stage in the learning-to-rank triage, but the small marginal gain plus token cost means it is a tunable add-on, not core; Memoria should gate it on whether a local rerank model is cheap enough.

ideas.idea: One-hop / referral reranking (retrieve citing papers, then add their references as candidates) helps only when the query pipeline leaks the citing source; a diversified corpus prevents this 'shortcut'.
ideas.rationale: Citation-graph expansion is a usable discovery tactic for Memoria (follow citations of top hits to surface targets), but the paper's caution that it can be a gameable shortcut argues for using it as a recall booster, not a relevance signal the triage learns from.

ideas.idea: Commercial search engines (Google Search/Scholar, Elicit) perform poorly (recall@5 ~17-43%) on specific literature questions.
ideas.rationale: Justifies Memoria building its own local retriever over the PI's curated corpus instead of delegating discovery to external scholarly search; the local instruction-tuned retriever is competitive and keeps the workflow on-vault.

ideas.idea: Generate evaluation queries by prompting an LLM to rewrite inline-citation paragraphs into search questions, then filter by word-overlap with target title and manually annotate specificity/quality.
ideas.rationale: A cheap recipe for Memoria to auto-generate a local retrieval/triage eval set from the PI's own already-ingested papers (each inline citation -> a query whose target is the cited note), enabling regression testing of the retriever/scout profile without manual query writing.

ideas.idea: Stratify evaluation by query specificity (broad ~20 targets vs specific ~5 targets) and by question quality.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's discovery relevance scoring should likewise report recall at different k for broad vs narrow PI queries rather than a single number, giving a more honest picture of the retriever for the triage gate.

ideas.idea: Full LitSearch dataset is ML/NLP-paper-specific and English-only.
ideas.rationale: The specific corpus/domain is not Memoria's (the PI's own field/vault is), so the dataset itself is not adopted; only the construction method and retriever findings transfer.

file: Akyürek et al. - 2022 - On measuring social biases in prompt-based multi-task learning.md
title: On Measuring Social Biases in Prompt-Based Multi-Task Learning
one_line: Demonstrates that for the same semantic input, a multi-task prompted LLM (T0) is markedly more biased in a task FORMAT it saw in training (QA) than in a novel format (NLI), introduces a hand-written NLI bias benchmark (BBNLI), and suggests format/prompt choice is itself a bias lever.

ideas.idea: Input ENCODING/format (QA vs NLI), not just content, materially changes model output behavior even when accuracy is held roughly constant; familiar training-shaped formats trigger more spurious/stereotyped associations.
ideas.rationale: Practical prompt-engineering caution for Memoria's extraction/classification engines: the framing of a claim-classification or relation prompt can shift the local model toward lazy/spurious answers independent of correctness. Worth testing alternative framings when an engine shows systematic error, even though Memoria's tasks are not social-bias tasks.

ideas.idea: Models are reluctant to abstain ('unknown'/'neutral') and instead force a definite choice, inflating error in ambiguous cases.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces the Agrawal 'don't-invent-answers' lesson for Memoria: ingest prompts for atomic claims and contradictions must explicitly permit and reward an 'insufficient evidence / neutral' output, feeding the uncertainty flags rather than forcing a spurious claim or relation.

ideas.idea: Sensitivity to surface artifacts: order of entity mentions and premise length significantly shift answers.
ideas.rationale: Warns Memoria's contradiction/relation engines that A-vs-B claim comparisons can be order-sensitive; mitigate by symmetrizing (run both orderings and aggregate), a cheap robustness step before the structural review gate sees the result.

ideas.idea: Debiasing idea: hold out 'popular' task formats from training so the model meets them only zero-shot, reducing learned bias.
ideas.rationale: Concerns training a multi-task LLM from scratch; Memoria uses off-the-shelf local models and does not control their training mixture, so this lever is unavailable.

ideas.idea: BBNLI / BBQ social-bias benchmarks and bias-score metric for measuring stereotype amplification.
ideas.rationale: Social-bias measurement of generative LMs is off-domain for a single-PI scientific-knowledge vault; Memoria's faithfulness concern is factual/claim accuracy, not demographic stereotype scoring, so the benchmarks and metric have no surface here.

file: Chamberlain et al. - 2006 - Towards a framework for integrating agile development and user-centred design.md
title: Towards a Framework for Integrating Agile Development and User-Centred Design
one_line: A 2006 field study of three teams combining UCD with Scrum/XP, yielding five integration principles (user involvement, collaboration/culture, prototyping, lifecycle, project management) centered on resolving the designer-developer power struggle and feedback-cadence mismatch.

ideas.idea: Separate an upfront research/abstraction phase from the iterative build phase so 'understand before code' is not crushed by sprint pressure (Project I's design-before-Scrum, Project M's 'business analysis' sprint).
ideas.rationale: Loosely mirrors Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves': ingestion/abstraction (claim extraction, aspects) runs as a deliberate upfront pass before fast write cycles, rather than mixing extraction and projection. Only a faint analogy, not a buildable mechanism.

ideas.idea: Designers must 'feed the developers' prototypes on a cadence that works for everyone; the core failure was mismatched iteration speeds (paper prototype fast, dev prototype slow).
ideas.rationale: Maps to Memoria's pipeline-stage cadence: fast deterministic ingest vs slower L5 quality/judgment passes. Worth keeping the insight that stage cadence mismatch causes abandonment, but it is a generic process observation.

ideas.idea: A balancing role/mechanism to keep disciplines from making decisions at each other's expense (the designer-vs-developer power struggle).
ideas.rationale: Memoria is single-PI with no competing human disciplines; the governance gate already centralizes authority in the PI. No multi-stakeholder power balancing is needed.

ideas.idea: Active customer/user as team member rather than passive bystander; proxy users on the team.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's 'user' is the PI directly via the Obsidian UI (PI direct-access rule); there is no proxy-user or multi-team coordination problem to solve.

file: Chamoun et al. - 2026 - SciPaths forecasting pathways to scientific discovery.md
title: SciPaths: Forecasting Pathways to Scientific Discovery
one_line: A benchmark and annotation schema that decomposes a target scientific contribution into NECESSARY 'enabling contributions' (each with a functional role, rationale, and a grounding in prior work or an 'unmapped' marker), separating 'what is needed' from 'which prior work realizes it'.

ideas.idea: Constructive-necessity criterion for a dependency: include an enabling contribution only if removing it would prevent the target from being realized in its claimed form ('if I had to realize this tomorrow, what would I still need?').
ideas.rationale: Directly sharpens Memoria's typed relations / argument graph: a 'depends-on'/'enables' edge should pass a counterfactual-necessity test, not mere co-citation. Gives the structural review gate a concrete acceptance rule for dependency edges between claims/notes.

ideas.idea: Atomic, abstracted, functional, causal rewrite of a contribution as [object] + [key property] + [what it enables], avoiding paper-specific names.
ideas.rationale: A schema for Memoria's atomic-claim extraction: it pushes claims to be reuse-oriented and name-free, improving dedup (record-linkage) and cross-paper matching. Concrete template the claim engine can target.

ideas.idea: Functional role taxonomy for a dependency edge: Core Method/Algorithm, Conceptual Framework, Data Source, Training Data, Model Initialization, Evaluation Protocol, Implementation/Tooling.
ideas.rationale: A ready-made controlled vocabulary for typing relations in Memoria's relation graph, far more useful than a generic 'cites' edge. Slots straight into the typed-relations design.

ideas.idea: Explicit 'unmapped' / NONE outcome: a needed building block that no single prior work cleanly realizes is a valid, recorded result, not a forced grounding.
ideas.rationale: Mirrors Memoria's faithfulness-over-flash and uncertainty flags: the system should record 'no clean source' rather than hallucinate a citation. Also surfaces research gaps for the discovery loop (what is unmapped = candidate to look for).

ideas.idea: Citation-intent filtering (USES/EXTENDS via an ACL-ARC-trained classifier) plus high-precision LLM verification to keep only functional-reuse citations, not background/comparison.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's claim-sentence classification and pre-file similarity gate can adopt citation-intent typing so that relation edges are weighted by functional reuse. The ACL-ARC classifier dependency is heavier than needed at single-PI scale, hence borrow not adopt.

ideas.idea: Embed contribution descriptions and cluster to consolidate the 'same' reused contribution across papers.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's BERTopic clustering / record-linkage dedup, applied at the claim/contribution level rather than the note level. Technique already in scope; SciPaths confirms applying it to contributions.

ideas.idea: Silver pipeline: a frontier LLM drafts pathways with a critic that selects on necessity/sufficiency/relevance/evidence, but final inclusion always stays with the human expert.
ideas.rationale: Exactly Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' gate: an LLM proposes dependency decompositions, a critic ranks, the PI approves. Validates the generate-critic-approve governance shape with measured agreement gains.

ideas.idea: Strict semantic one-to-one matching (LLM judge + Hungarian alignment) as the evaluation metric, with cap on multiple-credit for broad predictions.
ideas.rationale: Useful as an internal QA metric for Memoria's claim/relation extraction (did the engine recover the PI-approved set?), but full bipartite-matching eval harness is more than a single-PI tool needs day-to-day.

ideas.idea: Backward reasoning ('what prerequisites would make this target feasible') as a distinct capability from related-paper retrieval or idea generation; frontier models are weak at recovering core-method dependencies.
ideas.rationale: Informs Memoria's discovery loop: do not assume the LLM can infer dependency structure; ground it in actual extracted claims + citations. A cautionary design constraint rather than a component.

file: Chancellor et al. - 2016 - #thyghgapp instagram content moderation and lexical variation in pro-eating disorder communities.md
title: #thyghgapp: Instagram Content Moderation and Lexical Variation in Pro-Eating Disorder Communities
one_line: A large-scale (2.5M post) study showing pro-eating-disorder communities evade Instagram tag moderation via ever-more-complex lexical tag variants, measured by Levenshtein edit distance, momentum, co-occurrence clustering, and log-likelihood-ratio topical contrast.

ideas.idea: Detect lexical variants of a canonical 'root' term via broad regex over edit operations (lengthening, permutation, deletion, substitution) followed by two-rater binary validation (Cohen's kappa) to prune spurious matches.
ideas.rationale: Maps onto Memoria's keyphrase tag-candidate generation and record-linkage entity dedup: cheap regex/edit-distance candidate generation + a judgment pass to confirm. Useful for collapsing spelling/format variants of the same tag or entity (e.g. author/method name variants).

ideas.idea: Group variant strings into a 'tag chain' under a canonical root tag treated as the canonical form.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces canonicalization in Memoria's dedup: pick a canonical surface form and link variants to it, which is exactly what entity/keyphrase normalization needs.

ideas.idea: Edit distance alone is insufficient to decide semantic equivalence ('anatips' vs 'anaaaaa' are equidistant but semantically distinct), so combine NLP candidate generation with human annotation.
ideas.rationale: Confirms Memoria's pre-file similarity gate should not rely on string/embedding distance alone; a judgment step is required before merging. A design caution, already aligned with the gate philosophy.

ideas.idea: Spectral clustering of a tag co-occurrence graph + open-coded human themes; log-likelihood-ratio to contrast which co-occurring terms distinguish two corpora.
ideas.rationale: LLR contrast is a cheap, deterministic technique Memoria could use to characterize what distinguishes two note/claim sets (e.g. contradicting clusters, or a topic before/after a supersession). BERTopic already covers clustering, so only the LLR-contrast idea is additive.

ideas.idea: Content-moderation-as-intervention findings (banning drives communities to the periphery, increases vulnerability).
ideas.rationale: Domain-specific to multi-user social-platform governance; Memoria is a single-PI knowledge vault with no community moderation surface. Off-domain.

file: Chandrasekaran et al. - 1999 - What are ontologies, and why do we need them.md
title: What Are Ontologies, and Why Do We Need Them
one_line: Extraction failed: the markdown file contains only repeated IEEE Xplore licensing boilerplate and no actual paper content, so no ideas could be assessed.

ideas.idea: No extractable content: the .md file is 14 lines of 'Authorized licensed use limited to: The University of Iowa...' IEEE boilerplate with zero body text, tables, or figures.
ideas.rationale: Nothing to adopt or borrow because the paper body was not captured in the extraction; the file needs re-extraction before it can be judged. As-is it offers Memoria nothing.

file: Chen - From copilots to colleagues a survey of autonomous research agents.md
title: From Copilots to Colleagues: A Survey of Autonomous Research Agents
one_line: A survey proposing an L1-L5 autonomy taxonomy for research agents, four architecture patterns (single-agent loop, multi-agent, hierarchical orchestration, tool-augmented), a 17-system feature matrix, and six open problems (cognitive loops, context, novelty eval, reproducibility, safety, cost).

ideas.idea: L1-L5 autonomy taxonomy distinguishing scope of delegated decisions and duration of unsupervised operation; L3 = multi-step with human checkpoints, L4 = bounded full autonomy with human evaluating final output.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's gate deliberately holds agents at ~L3 (engines/agents act, PI approves at checkpoints before notes are written). The taxonomy is a useful vocabulary to articulate why Memoria caps autonomy at PI-checkpointed L3 rather than L4 autonomous writing.

ideas.idea: Supervisor-worker hierarchical pattern with context isolation: a primary agent holds high-level state and spawns sub-agents in isolated contexts to prevent 'context pollution' (Claude Code example).
ideas.rationale: Directly applicable to Memoria's profile-compiled agents (retriever/scout, judge): isolate sub-task contexts (e.g. per-paper extraction) so one paper's content cannot bleed into another's judgment, and keep a coordinator holding vault-level state. Buildable with the existing MCP-only sandbox.

ideas.idea: Structured-artifact communication (MetaGPT SOPs): agents exchange typed documents/specs rather than free-form chat, reducing ambiguity and hallucination.
ideas.rationale: Matches Memoria's deterministic-ingest + structured-capture-forms philosophy: agents should emit structured records (claims, relations, aspects) into the gate, not prose. Reinforces the structured-output discipline already central to Memoria.

ideas.idea: Adversarial / debate / self-play evaluation: a separate reviewer agent attempts falsification, reproduction failure, or finding prior work that subsumes a claim ('adversarial evaluation' for novelty).
ideas.rationale: Strong fit for Memoria's contradictions dashboard and structural review gate: a critic agent that tries to falsify or find subsuming/superseding claims operationalizes claim supersession and contradiction detection. 'Engines write, a critic challenges, PI approves.'

ideas.idea: Persistent structured knowledge across sessions via external knowledge graphs that preserve causality, contradiction, temporal ordering, and evidential support (vs flat vector stores).
ideas.rationale: This is essentially Memoria's thesis: the Obsidian vault + typed relations + argument graph + supersession IS the persistent structured memory the survey says agents lack. Validates the relation-graph-over-RAG design choice.

ideas.idea: Reflexion/ExpeL-style experiential learning: extract reusable natural-language 'insights' from success/failure trajectories and store for retrieval, without gradient updates.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's learning-to-rank triage and discovery-relevance scoring can capture PI accept/reject decisions as stored heuristics to improve future ranking, a lightweight no-train feedback loop. Borrow the mechanism, scoped to triage rather than open agent self-modification.

ideas.idea: Reproducibility/determinism problems: non-deterministic LLM outputs, prompt sensitivity, and model-version drift undermine repeatable agent behavior; remedy with deterministic modes and agent 'cards' specifying exact config.
