ideas.rationale: Directly supports Memoria's deterministic-ingest constraint and profile-compilation: pin prompts/models, prefer deterministic settings for the ingest path, and version agent profiles so a re-run reproduces. Concretely justifies design pillars Memoria already holds.

ideas.idea: Cost/accessibility: achieve high reliability with smaller cheaper (open) models through better tool design and scaffolding; adaptive compute allocation (more tokens on hard problems).
ideas.rationale: Validates Memoria's local + cheap/open-model preference (qwen2.5:7b default): invest in tool/scaffold quality and tiered compute (cheap local for wiring, prod model only for L5 quality) instead of expensive frontier calls everywhere.

ideas.idea: Cognitive-loop problem: agents perseverate on failed strategies and fail to recognize when they are stuck.
ideas.rationale: A failure mode Memoria's nightly discovery loop and triage must guard against (e.g. re-surfacing the same rejected candidates); argues for storing PI rejections so the loop does not perseverate. Design caution rather than a component.

ideas.idea: Fully autonomous L5 'self-bootstrapping research communities' / agent populations setting their own agendas and peer-reviewing each other.
ideas.rationale: Contradicts Memoria's single-PI, PI-approval-gated, MCP-only-sandbox constraints; multi-agent self-directed publishing with no human gate is explicitly out of scope and the survey itself flags it as unsafe/aspirational.

ideas.idea: Domain-specific closed-loop autonomous labs with robotic execution as the near-term L5 path.
ideas.rationale: Off-domain: Memoria is a literature/knowledge vault, not a wet-lab or experiment-execution system. No physical-experiment loop in scope.

file: Qin et al. - 2023 - ToolLLM facilitating large language models to master 16000+ real-world APIs.md
title: ToolLLM: Facilitating Large Language Models to Master 16000+ Real-World APIs
one_line: A tool-learning framework (ToolBench dataset, DFSDT decision-tree reasoning, neural API retriever, ToolEval) that fine-tunes LLaMA to call 16k+ REST APIs at near-ChatGPT level.

ideas.idea: Neural dense retriever (Sentence-BERT over instruction + tool-doc embeddings) to shortlist relevant tools/APIs from a large pool, beating BM25/Ada and even improving over the oracle set by finding better-fitting substitutes.
ideas.rationale: Memoria already plans a retriever/scout profile and a pre-file similarity gate; the insight that a contrastively-trained dense retriever over (query, capability-doc) pairs can shortlist from a large pool and outperform a hand-picked set supports compiling agent skill/tool selection via embedding similarity rather than enumerating every MCP tool in the prompt.

ideas.idea: DFSDT (depth-first search decision tree) over reasoning traces with explicit retract/give-up nodes to escape error-propagation loops that trap linear CoT/ReACT.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's discovery loop and structural-review agents do multi-step judging; allowing an agent to abandon a faulty path and expand a diversified alternative (vs. committing to one chain) is a cheap robustness gain for nightly discovery/triage, though full tree search is overkill at single-researcher scale.

ideas.idea: ToolEval: LLM-as-judge automatic evaluator with pass-rate and pairwise win-rate metrics, validated at ~80-87% agreement with humans.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's 'agents judge' governance and L5 quality testing can reuse the pass-rate (did the run satisfy the instruction within budget) + pairwise-preference pattern for evaluating engine/agent outputs locally, but must run on the cheap local model not ChatGPT.

ideas.idea: Massive instruction-tuning dataset construction by sampling API combinations and prompting ChatGPT (self-instruct style) to fabricate instructions + solution paths.
ideas.rationale: Memoria does not fine-tune models or build SFT datasets; it relies on off-the-shelf local models (qwen2.5:7b) with deterministic ingest, so the whole data-construction/training pipeline is out of scope.

ideas.idea: Generalization to unseen APIs purely from documentation provided in-context (zero-shot tool adaptation).
ideas.rationale: Relevant only to a 16k-tool marketplace agent; Memoria's MCP-only sandbox exposes a small fixed, curated tool surface, so unseen-API generalization is not a problem it has.

file: Qu et al. - 2024 - “Looking” into attention patterns in extended reality an eye tracking-based study.md
title: “Looking” into Attention Patterns in Extended Reality: An Eye Tracking–Based Study
one_line: An eye-tracking study comparing user attention patterns (fixations/saccades, ML prediction of attention-control ability) in an AR vs. VR Sudoku-helper app, finding AR and VR are not interchangeable.

ideas.idea: Transformer (MVTS) on time-series gaze data with unsupervised mask-recovery pretraining on a large external dataset, then fine-tuning on a small task dataset for user-trait prediction.
ideas.rationale: Memoria has no sensor/gaze input, does no model pretraining/fine-tuning, and serves text/markdown knowledge, not biometric XR signals; the technique is off-domain.

ideas.idea: Categorizing UI content into 'useful' (task-relevant guidance) vs. 'relevant-but-distracting' overlays and measuring attention allocation to each.
ideas.rationale: Tangentially echoes Memoria's signal-vs-noise triage, but the operationalization is gaze-based AR overlay design with no transfer to a markdown vault / dashboard surface; nothing concretely buildable.

ideas.idea: Domain-gap finding: an ML model trained on simulated (VR) data transfers poorly to the real (AR) target.
ideas.rationale: A generic train/test-distribution-shift caution already covered by Memoria's preference for live local validation; no Memoria-specific mechanism to adopt.

file: Qu et al. - 2026 - CORAL towards autonomous multi-agent evolution for open-ended discovery.md
title: CORAL: Towards Autonomous Multi-Agent Evolution for Open-Ended Discovery
one_line: An autonomous multi-agent evolution framework where long-running coding agents explore in parallel and coordinate through a shared filesystem-based persistent memory (attempts/notes/skills) with heartbeat-driven reflection, setting SOTA on optimization tasks.

ideas.idea: Shared persistent memory structured as a plain filesystem with three root folders: attempts/ (scored solutions+evals), notes/ (markdown observations/reflections), skills/ (reusable SKILL.md + executable artifacts), accessed via a CLI and organizable into subfolders by agents.
ideas.rationale: Strongly resonant with Memoria's Obsidian markdown-vault-as-memory and skill governance: notes-as-markdown and a skills/ folder with SKILL.md descriptions match Memoria's existing skill model and 'engines write, agents judge' substrate. But Memoria's MCP-only sandbox forbids agents direct Bash/filesystem write access, so the access path must be mediated by the structural-review gate, not a raw CLI.

ideas.idea: Heartbeat mechanism: periodic triggers (per-iteration reflection, consolidation after N attempts, stagnation-triggered redirection) that inject prompts to make the agent record notes, distill notes into skills, and pivot when scores plateau.
ideas.rationale: Directly buildable as Memoria's nightly/proactive discovery cadence: a stagnation-triggered 'redirect' and a periodic 'consolidate notes into reusable patterns' step map onto the discovery loop and claim/relation consolidation, giving the loop explicit memory-formation and anti-drift behavior at single-researcher scale.

ideas.idea: A 'what NEVER worked' folder documenting failed approaches so agents avoid revisiting dead ends across runs.
ideas.rationale: Cheap and high-value: Memoria can keep a negative-results / superseded-approaches note so the discovery loop and triage don't re-surface or re-propose rejected directions; complements the claim-supersession and contradictions machinery.

ideas.idea: Coordination through shared memory only (no peer-to-peer messaging protocol); agents influence each other purely by reading/writing artifacts, yielding emergent technique diffusion.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is single-researcher and largely single-pipeline, so multi-agent horizontal parallelism is not a goal; but the stigmergic 'communicate via the vault, not via direct messages' pattern is the right cheap coordination model if Memoria ever runs multiple engines/agents concurrently against the same vault.

ideas.idea: Local verification before external evaluation (agents run/compile candidates locally to catch failures before consuming an expensive eval), shown to correlate with higher improvement rates.
ideas.rationale: Maps to Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and deterministic-ingest validation: cheap local checks (schema/lint/dedup) before invoking an expensive LLM judge or writing a note conserves budget and matches faithfulness-over-flash.

ideas.idea: Isolated per-agent worktrees with symlinks to shared state, evaluator separation (grader hidden behind an API), and resource/health management.
ideas.rationale: These safeguards are for many long-running frontier-model coding agents under compute pressure; at Memoria's single-researcher local scale with an MCP-only sandbox the orchestration overhead isn't warranted, though evaluator-hidden-behind-API echoes Memoria's gate.

ideas.idea: Relies on frontier models (Claude Opus / MiniMax M2.5) for complex coding-agent workflows; authors note local deployment is hard.
ideas.rationale: Memoria prefers cheap/open local models (qwen2.5:7b); the full autonomous-evolution loop assumes frontier capability that Memoria deliberately does not require.

file: Rabinovich and Anaby-Tavor - 2025 - On the robustness of agentic function calling.md
title: On the Robustness of Agentic Function Calling
one_line: A benchmark showing LLM function-calling accuracy degrades under meaning-preserving query rephrasings and when the toolkit is expanded with semantically related tools, and that exact-match evaluation overstates failures.

ideas.idea: Toolkit-expansion stress test: adding a few semantically-related tools (the realistic output of a top-K shortlister) measurably degrades function selection and slot-filling (wrong function, wrong number of functions, parameter hallucination).
ideas.rationale: Directly actionable for Memoria's retriever/scout + MCP tool selection: the more confusable tools the shortlister surfaces, the worse the agent's tool choice, so Memoria should keep the per-task exposed MCP toolset small/disambiguated and test agent behavior with near-duplicate tools present rather than only the oracle tool.

ideas.idea: Evaluation-methodology weakness: exact-match scoring on argument values (e.g., named entities like 'Miami, FL' vs. 'Miami, Florida') falsely flags correct calls; semantic-similarity / LLM-as-judge matching is more faithful.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's record-linkage entity dedup and any internal eval of extracted-claim/field correctness must use normalization + semantic matching, not literal string equality, or it will mis-score correct outputs exactly as this paper documents; reinforces a concrete dedup/eval design rule.

ideas.idea: Robustness as semantic consistency: equivalent inputs (paraphrases) should yield equivalent structured outputs; the agent's stochastic context-sensitivity means unrelated context changes flip outputs.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's deterministic-ingest and faithfulness goals benefit from a paraphrase-consistency check on extraction engines (does claim/aspect extraction stay stable under reworded input?), a cheap local regression test, though Memoria favors determinism over fixing model stochasticity wholesale.

ideas.idea: Building the benchmark by generating related-but-distinct tool definitions with a code model and filtering near-duplicates via embedding cosine-similarity over a concatenated tool 'signature'.
ideas.rationale: The signature-concatenation + cosine-threshold dedup recipe is reusable for Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and tool/skill dedup (detect when a candidate tool/skill/note is a near-duplicate of an existing one), at single-researcher scale.

ideas.idea: Distraction by large/irrelevant context degrades reasoning (cited motivation for future non-relevant-tool experiments).
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's principle of minimal, curated context windows and projection of only relevant notes; argues against dumping the whole vault into an agent prompt.

file: Rao and Callison-Burch - 2026 - BibTeX citation hallucinations in scientific publishing agents evaluation and mitigation.md
title: BibTeX Citation Hallucinations in Scientific Publishing Agents: Evaluation and Mitigation
one_line: A 931-paper benchmark showing search-enabled frontier LLMs still produce heavily error-prone BibTeX (only 50.9% fully correct), and a deterministic Zotero/CrossRef retrieval tool (clibib) in a two-stage revise pipeline that raises fully-correct entries to 78.3% with 0.8% regression.

ideas.idea: Deterministic citation retrieval (clibib): resolve a DOI/arXiv-ID/persistent identifier against the Zotero Translation Server (CrossRef fallback) to fetch the publisher-deposited BibTeX with zero model involvement, bypassing LLM parametric memory entirely.
ideas.rationale: This is Memoria's exact Zotero/citekey backbone: bibliographic metadata must come from deterministic identifier resolution, never from an LLM. Adopt as the canonical ingest path for citation fields, eliminating a whole class of hallucination in a faithfulness-first, deterministic-ingest system.

ideas.idea: Two-stage architecture (generate/baseline, then revise against authoritative records) beats single-stage tool-loop: +8.0pp accuracy, far lower regression (0.8% vs 4.8%) because separating search from revision avoids corrupting already-correct fields.
ideas.rationale: Maps cleanly onto Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' gate: an engine produces a candidate, then a separate deterministic/judge stage revises only against authoritative ground truth, and the very low regression rate is exactly the property the structural-review gate needs to safely auto-correct without breaking good fields.

ideas.idea: Six-way field-level error taxonomy (Correct / Missing / Fabricated / Partial / Substituted / N-A), distinguishing Fabricated (from noise) vs. Substituted (wrong real record).
ideas.rationale: A ready-made schema for Memoria's claim/field uncertainty flags and the contradictions/quality dashboard: classifying an extracted value as missing/partial/substituted/fabricated gives the inspector and gate a precise, auditable error vocabulary instead of a binary correct/wrong.

ideas.idea: Version-aware ground truth: one work has multiple legitimately-different citable versions (arXiv preprint, proceedings, journal); citing a different valid version is not a hallucination, and fields must be resolved per-version.
ideas.rationale: Directly informs Memoria's record-linkage / entity-dedup and claim-supersession: the same paper across preprint/published versions must be linked as one entity with version-specific metadata, preventing false-duplicate and false-contradiction flags between versions.

ideas.idea: Canonical field resolution with explicit per-field provenance + majority-vote/priority rules across multiple authoritative sources (OpenAlex, DBLP, PubMed, Zotero), plus a venue synonym table for normalization.
ideas.rationale: A concrete multi-source reconciliation recipe Memoria can implement for entity dedup and metadata canonicalization, with per-field provenance matching Memoria's audit/inspector requirements; the synonym-table normalization is the deterministic layer before any LLM-judge escalation.

ideas.idea: Citation-tier finding: accuracy drops 27.7pp from popular to recent/post-cutoff papers, proving heavy reliance on parametric memory even with search enabled.
ideas.rationale: A strong empirical justification for Memoria's stance that ingest/discovery must not trust model memory for recent papers (the PI's research frontier is exactly the post-cutoff regime); reinforces deterministic-retrieval-first and the proactive discovery loop pulling fresh authoritative records.

ideas.idea: Two-stage evaluation: deterministic rule-based matching first (resolved 93.6% of fields), LLM-as-judge only for the residual fuzzy cases, with human spot-check (Cohen's kappa).
ideas.rationale: Matches Memoria's cost-discipline: resolve the bulk deterministically/locally and reserve the LLM judge for the small ambiguous residual, with the PI as the human spot-check at the approval gate; keeps the local cheap model load minimal.

file: Wang et al. - 2026 - Sibyl-AutoResearch autonomous research needs self-evolving trial-and-error harnesses, not paper gen.md
title: Sibyl-AutoResearch: Autonomous Research Needs Self-Evolving Trial-and-Error Harnesses, Not Paper Generators
one_line: A framework (and file-backed SIBYL system) arguing autonomous research must convert trial signals into later behavior via auditable 'conversion units', evidence-maturity gates, routed memory, and a claim registry that blocks pilot signals from being upgraded into paper claims by prose alone.

ideas.idea: Evidence-maturity ladder (execution completion -> pilot signal -> analysis-ready -> paper-ready -> audited claim) with explicit gates that allow/downgrade/block a claim based on artifacts, validation, negative evidence, and objections
ideas.rationale: This is a near-direct articulation of Memoria's claim-supersession + structural review gate. A claim/note should carry a maturity label and the structural gate should refuse to promote weakly-supported claims; aligns with 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves'.

ideas.idea: Validated claim registry consumed by the writer: claims have maturity labels, artifact links, and validation status; a note/paper is generated from the registry, not from whatever narrative is most polished
ideas.rationale: Memoria already extracts atomic claims with uncertainty flags; making the registry the single source that surfaces/dashboards read from (rather than free prose) is directly buildable and enforces faithfulness over flash.

ideas.idea: Routed memory vs. textual memory: normalize reflections into typed issue categories with severity/decay/source-links and route lessons to the specific role that needs them (planner check, critic objection, validation contract) rather than leaving them as long-context text
ideas.rationale: Memoria's profiles/skills could carry typed, role-scoped lesson overlays. The single-researcher scale means 'roles' map to Memoria's profiles (retriever/scout, judge); adopt the routing+decay+source-link discipline, not the multi-agent debate machinery.

ideas.idea: Trial-to-behavior conversion as an AUDITABLE unit (signal at t, trace path, behavior change at t+k must be linkable to artifacts) — memory effectiveness measured by behavior change, not volume
ideas.rationale: Useful evaluation lens for Memoria's discovery/triage loop and gate: a stored lesson or a flagged contradiction should be tied to a later observable change (a re-ranked discovery, a downgraded claim). Adapt as a lightweight provenance/audit trace in the inspector, not the full 8-event audit apparatus.

ideas.idea: Claim-evidence graph linking each prose claim to tables/figures/logs/configs/negative evidence; a reader can reconstruct why a later action changed
ideas.rationale: Mirrors Memoria's typed-relations + argument-graph-with-warrants. Borrow the explicit prose-claim -> artifact backlinks; Memoria's domain is papers not experiments, so 'artifacts' become source spans/citekeys/contradicting notes.

ideas.idea: Pre-narrative validation gate / deterministic fixers + 'self-heal' for recurring process failures (duplicate files, stale numbers, source-to-paper numeric cross-check before writing)
ideas.rationale: Analogous to Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and record-linkage dedup. Borrow the principle that integrity checks (dedup, stale-supersession, numeric/source consistency) run BEFORE a note is written, not after; the deterministic source-to-paper cross-check maps to deterministic ingest.

ideas.idea: Reviewer scores are poor progress metrics; optimizing a single review score creates a brittle objective — prefer multi-dimensional labels + protected integrity constraints + hidden injected failures
ideas.rationale: Cautions Memoria's learning-to-rank triage and discovery relevance scoring against collapsing to one optimized number; keep multi-signal scoring and a protected-integrity baseline. Governance table (claim-evidence gates, negative-evidence reporting, periodic memory audit) reinforces Memoria's governance posture.

ideas.idea: Self-evolving harness that rewrites its own gates/prompt-overlays/scheduler policy from recurring failures (agent-harness co-evolution)
ideas.rationale: Contradicts Memoria's profiles-compiled + structural-review-gate + skill-governance model where the PI approves changes; autonomous self-modification of gates is exactly the 'self-evolution drift' risk and violates 'PI approves'. Keep harness changes human-gated.

ideas.idea: Multi-agent role separation with GPU scheduler, dependency layers, and bounded experimental trial-and-error (running code/experiments)
ideas.rationale: Off-domain for Memoria (it ingests/organizes literature, it does not run ML experiments) and the MCP-only sandbox forbids agents from executing code/terminal; the heavy multi-agent+compute apparatus is out of scope at single-researcher scale.

file: Wattenberg and Kriss - 2006 - Designing for social data analysis.md
title: Designing for Social Data Analysis
one_line: A design study of the NameVoyager baby-name visualization arguing that exploratory data analysis tools succeed when they support social, shareable exploration — via instant keystroke filtering, smooth animated transitions, application-state pointers ('BookMarks'), and road-less-traveled navigation.

ideas.idea: Application-state pointers / 'BookMarks': a short serializable token that captures the exact view state so a user can paste it to jump another user (or themselves) to that state, mimicking URL-sharing
ideas.rationale: Memoria's disposable projection engine + projected canvas + spaces could emit a shareable/bookmarkable state token so the PI can save and return to a specific projected view; a single-researcher analogue of 'discovery transfer' for self-resumption, not social sharing.

ideas.idea: Instant, incremental filter-as-you-type (overview first, zoom and filter, details on demand) with no submit button, demonstrating the query mechanism by reacting per keystroke
ideas.rationale: Reinforces a known-good interaction for Memoria's read-only inspector / dashboards: live incremental filtering over notes/claims rather than form-submit search. Standard Shneiderman mantra, partially applicable.

ideas.idea: Road-less-traveled navigation: track which items were 'visited' and gray them out to push the eye toward unexplored territory (inverting social-navigation popularity cues into a staleness cue)
ideas.rationale: Directly relevant to Memoria's proactive discovery loop: surface UNread/under-explored papers and de-emphasize already-visited ones, a single-researcher coverage aid. Borrow the staleness-as-cue idea for the discovery surface.

ideas.idea: Smooth animated transitions and color interpolation to preserve context and make state changes legible
ideas.rationale: Minor UX polish for Memoria's projected canvas transitions; cheap and aids the PI's sense of continuity between projected views. Low priority.

ideas.idea: Social data analysis: design for spectators, group dialogue, Bartle player types (explorer/achiever/socializer/killer), common-ground-but-unique-perspective to spark multi-user conversation
ideas.rationale: Memoria is explicitly single-researcher (no multi-user/social layer); the entire social/spectator framing and player taxonomy do not apply.

ideas.idea: Stacked/additive time-series visualization for hierarchical, summable series (ThemeRiver-style)
ideas.rationale: Memoria's data is claims/notes/relations, not large collections of additive numeric time series; the specific visualization technique is off-domain.

file: Webson and Pavlick - 2022 - Do prompt-based models really understand the meaning of their prompts.md
title: Do Prompt-Based Models Really Understand the Meaning of Their Prompts?
one_line: Across 30+ NLI templates and 13 target-word sets, models (up to GPT-3 175B and instruction-tuned T0) learn nearly as fast from irrelevant or pathologically misleading prompts as from instructive ones, and are far more sensitive to the choice of answer target words ('yes'/'no') than to the meaning of the instruction.

ideas.idea: Models are highly sensitive to the choice of LM target/answer words (e.g. 'yes'/'no' vastly outperforms 'agree'/'disagree' or arbitrary 'cat'/'dog'), and target-word choice overrides template meaning
ideas.rationale: Direct, buildable lesson for Memoria's local-model classifiers (claim-sentence classification, MASSW aspects, keyphrase/tag candidates running on qwen2.5:7b): fix verbalizers/answer tokens to high-frequency natural labels and rank-classify over them rather than relying on instruction wording. Cheap reliability win on small models.

ideas.idea: Prompt instruction semantics matter far less than assumed; pathological/irrelevant instructions can match instructive ones — so don't over-invest in elaborate instruction wording
ideas.rationale: Tempers Memoria's prompt-engineering effort for engines: invest in output-format/verbalizer design and few-shot exemplars over ornate task descriptions. Caveat (per the 2023 follow-up) that larger/RLHF models behave differently.

ideas.idea: Evaluate prompt robustness by deliberately injecting irrelevant/misleading/null templates and checking that performance tracks instruction correctness (necessary-but-not-sufficient sensitivity test)
ideas.rationale: A concrete QA harness Memoria can borrow for validating its extraction/classification profiles on the local model: a pathological-prompt control set flags when a profile is exploiting spurious cues rather than following the instruction. Adapt at single-researcher scale as a small fixed control battery.

ideas.idea: Including answer choices / target hints in the input can HURT performance on some tasks
ideas.rationale: Counterintuitive tuning detail worth testing when designing Memoria's classification prompts; informs whether to append option lists (contrast with FLAN's OPTIONS finding — test empirically on the local model).

ideas.idea: Ethical framing: an 'illusion of instruction-following' can mislead lay users into trusting outputs more than warranted
ideas.rationale: Valid but a general caution, not a Memoria mechanism; Memoria's faithfulness/uncertainty-flag posture already addresses the substance, and the single-PI user is not a 'lay user' being deceived.

file: Webson et al. - 2023 - Are language models worse than humans at following prompts It's complicated.md
title: Are Language Models Worse than Humans at Following Prompts? It's Complicated
one_line: A human-subjects rebuttal to the 2022 paper showing humans (like models) reliably ignore IRRELEVANT prompts and default to the task, but unlike models faithfully FOLLOW misleading prompts — so 'human-likeness' is a poorly-validated gold standard and intuitions about human behavior must be empirically checked.

ideas.idea: Empirically validate assumptions about the human/expected baseline before using it as an evaluation gold standard; researchers' intuitions about correct behavior routinely fail to bear out
ideas.rationale: Methodological caution for Memoria's evaluation/benchmark work and its LLM-judge gate: don't assume a presupposed 'correct' behavior; calibrate the judge/triage criteria against actually-observed PI judgments rather than an idealized notion. Adapt to single-PI: use the PI's own approval decisions as the calibration signal.

ideas.idea: Distinguishing irrelevant vs. misleading instructions: tolerating irrelevant context while still following genuinely misleading instructions is the desired behavior ('feature not a bug' that models default to the task under irrelevant prompts)
ideas.rationale: Refines Memoria's prompt-robustness control battery (from the 2022 paper): the goal for extraction engines is robustness to irrelevant boilerplate but correct deference to genuine instruction changes — a useful target distinction when designing local-model prompts.

ideas.idea: Few-shot label feedback lets humans rapidly recover the true task even from a misleading instruction; behavior is heterogeneous (strict vs. pragmatic interpreters)
ideas.rationale: Supports preferring a few well-chosen exemplars over instruction wording when configuring Memoria's classification profiles; the heterogeneity point cautions against a single rigid prompt. Minor, reinforces the FLAN/Webson-2022 exemplar lesson.

ideas.idea: GPT-4 (RLHF/large) behaves more human-like on misleading prompts than smaller supervised-finetuned models
ideas.rationale: Memoria runs cheap/local models (qwen2.5:7b) by constraint, so the frontier-model-specific behavior is not actionable; relevant only as a reason to expect MORE spurious-cue exploitation from Memoria's small models, already covered.

ideas.idea: MTurk crowdsourcing protocol details (speeder exclusion via response-time floor, control items, IRB)
ideas.rationale: Human-subjects experimental machinery; Memoria is single-researcher with no crowdsourcing, so not applicable.

file: Wei et al. - 2022 - Finetuned language models are zero-shot learners.md
title: Finetuned Language Models Are Zero-Shot Learners (FLAN)
one_line: Instruction tuning — finetuning a 137B LM on 60+ NLP datasets verbalized as natural-language instruction templates with held-out task clusters — substantially improves zero-shot generalization to unseen task types, with benefits emerging only at large scale and depending critically on diverse instruction phrasing and number of task clusters.

ideas.idea: Phrase every task as a natural-language instruction and write multiple (≈10) diverse templates per task, including some that 'turn the task around', to reduce sensitivity to any single prompt
ideas.rationale: Directly informs how Memoria authors prompts for its local extraction/classification engines: use several paraphrased instruction templates per engine and aggregate/average to cut prompt-brittleness, and consider template-ensembling for robustness. Buildable without any training.

ideas.idea: OPTIONS suffix / classification-with-options: append the explicit list of output classes and rank-classify over them so the model knows the desired output space (fixes probability-mass dilution across synonymous answers)
ideas.rationale: Concrete, immediately adoptable for Memoria's claim-sentence classification and MASSW-aspect tagging on qwen2.5:7b: present a closed option set and score via rank classification for deterministic, parseable outputs — supports deterministic ingest. (Test against Webson-2022's 'options can hurt' caveat.)

ideas.idea: Few-shot exemplars complement instructions, especially for large/complex output spaces, and reduce variance across templates (lower prompt sensitivity)
ideas.rationale: For Memoria's structured-output engines (claim extraction, keyphrase candidates), a few in-context exemplars stabilize output format on a small local model and cut template variance; cheap and aligned with deterministic ingest goals.

ideas.idea: Instruction tuning is a way to USE labeled data to make one generalist model perform many unseen tasks (vs. one specialist per task)
ideas.rationale: Strategic insight: a single instruction-tuned local checkpoint could serve Memoria's many extraction/classification engines rather than many bespoke models — relevant when selecting/finetuning the local model, though Memoria currently prefers off-the-shelf instruction-tuned models (qwen2.5:7b is already FLAN-lineage).

ideas.idea: Instruction-tuning benefits emerge only with sufficient scale (>~8B); at small scale it HURTS held-out generalization (capacity consumed by the tuning mixture)
ideas.rationale: A caution against Memoria itself instruction-tuning a small local model from scratch — its hardware (16GB / 7B-class) sits near/below the threshold where DIY instruction tuning helps; better to consume an already-instruction-tuned model. Recorded as a reason NOT to build, hence reject-for-adoption.

ideas.idea: Held-out-task-cluster evaluation protocol (a dataset is 'unseen' only if no dataset from its cluster was in training) to measure true cross-task generalization
ideas.rationale: An evaluation method for training generalist LMs; Memoria does not train models and operates at single-researcher scale, so the cluster-holdout protocol is not applicable.

file: Li et al. - 2026 - Source or it didn't happen a multi-agent framework for citation hallucination detection.md
title: Source or It Didn't Happen: A Multi-Agent Framework for Citation Hallucination Detection
one_line: CiteTracer reframes citation verification as taxonomy-aligned, field-level adjudication and builds a four-stage cascade (structured extractor -> cheapest-first evidence cascade over 8 bibliographic connectors -> deterministic field matcher -> class-specialist judger agents) that closes most cases with rules and reserves LLM calls for the ambiguous remainder.

ideas.idea: Deterministic-first cascade: a rule matcher with field-specific normalizers (case folding, DOI canonicalization, page-range/author-order normalization, venue-abbreviation tables) closes the easy majority (61.7%) with NO LLM call, and only the residual ~35% reaches LLM judgers.
ideas.rationale: This is exactly Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge' + deterministic-ingest philosophy: cheap deterministic checks first, LLM only on ambiguity. Directly applicable to the record-linkage entity-dedup and pre-file similarity gate to keep local-model cost low.

ideas.idea: Per-field taxonomy with three auditor-facing classes (REAL / POTENTIAL / HALLUCINATED) instead of a binary verdict, where POTENTIAL buffers genuinely ambiguous cases (name variants, non-academic sources, unindexable peripheral fields) for human routing.
ideas.rationale: Mirrors Memoria's uncertainty-flag and structural-review-gate design: a third 'route to PI' bucket fits the approval gate better than a forced binary. Memoria should give its claim/entity verifiers an explicit POTENTIAL/abstain class rather than valid-or-invalid.

ideas.idea: Class-specialist judger panel with a router (Valid/Potential/Hallucinated judges) rather than one monolithic LLM judge, because each class needs different evidence thresholds and a single judge becomes miscalibrated.
ideas.rationale: Memoria compiles profiles/skills; routing residual cases to small specialized prompts (each with its own threshold and worked examples) suits a cheap local model (qwen2.5:7b) better than one giant prompt. Borrow the routing+specialist pattern, not the frontier-model stack.

ideas.idea: Memory cache stage seeded from an offline DBLP mirror and updated with every newly verified REAL citation, queried first at near-zero cost (long-term-memory layer for the agent).
ideas.rationale: A single-researcher vault accumulates the same citations repeatedly; a local verified-citation cache (keyed by citekey/DOI) makes the Zotero/citekey backbone self-reinforcing and avoids re-querying external APIs. Fits local-first, deterministic constraints.

ideas.idea: Layout-aware reference extraction: OCR as a high-recall block proposer, then a vision-LLM re-parse over the cropped reference image to repair character-level errors and merge column/page-split entries into structured fields.
ideas.rationale: Memoria parses papers locally with a citekey backbone; the crop-and-reparse trick to fix author/venue/identifier OCR errors is useful for robust local reference extraction, but the vision-LLM dependency is heavier than Memoria's cheap-model preference, so borrow the idea (OCR-proposer + targeted reparse) not the specific 235B model.

ideas.idea: Strict JSON-only parser prompt with an explicit 'et al.' truncation-marker rule so downstream code can detect intentionally partial author lists.
ideas.rationale: Concrete, reusable prompt-engineering detail for Memoria's deterministic ingest: preserving truncation markers prevents false 'missing author' contradictions in the dedup/claim layer.

ideas.idea: Precision-first design treating a wrong HALLUCINATED verdict on an honest citation as the primary failure to avoid (false-positive cost prioritized).
ideas.rationale: Aligns with Memoria's 'faithfulness over flash' and the gate's bias toward not writing wrong notes; the verifier should abstain to POTENTIAL rather than emit a confident wrong flag the PI must clean up.

ideas.idea: Synthetic benchmark built from real seeds with controlled per-field LLM mutation operators plus a deterministic post-processor enforcing each operator's field schema, with round-trip diff audits as QC.
ideas.rationale: Memoria needs evaluation harnesses for its claim-extraction/dedup engines; the seed+controlled-mutation+round-trip-audit recipe is a clean way to generate ground-truth test sets locally without multi-user data.

ideas.idea: Frontier-model multi-agent stack (DeepSeek-OCR, Kimi K2.5, Qwen3-VL-235B) with 3x nested parallelism and 8 live scholar-API connectors.
ideas.rationale: Contradicts Memoria's single-researcher, local + cheap-model, MCP-only sandbox constraints; 235B vision models and massive parallel external-API fan-out are out of scope. Keep the architecture, drop the heavyweight runtime.

file: Licklider - 1960 - Man-computer symbiosis.md
title: Man-Computer Symbiosis
one_line: Licklider's foundational 1960 vision of humans and computers in a cooperative partnership where machines handle routine formulative/clerical work so humans can focus on judgment and goals — but this vault file contains only IEEE-Xplore watermark lines and no actual paper text.

ideas.idea: The man-computer symbiosis thesis (machine handles routinizable preparatory/clerical work; human sets goals, hypotheses, and judgments) — known from the canonical paper, but NOT present in this extraction, which is empty save for download watermarks.
ideas.rationale: The extracted markdown contains no paper content (only repeated 'Authorized licensed use limited to: The University of Iowa' watermark lines), so there is no in-file idea to adopt or borrow; the file is unusable as-is and should be re-extracted before any judgment. Conceptually the symbiosis framing echoes Memoria's 'engines do clerical extraction, PI judges' division, but that cannot be sourced from this file.

file: Liu and Chilton - 2022 - Design guidelines for prompt engineering text-to-image generative models.md
title: Design Guidelines for Prompt Engineering Text-to-Image Generative Models
one_line: A CHI'22 empirical study of ~5500 VQGAN+CLIP generations deriving design guidelines for SUBJECT-in-the-style-of-STYLE prompts (keywords matter more than connecting words; try 3-9 seeds; 100-500 iterations suffice; concrete subjects and figurative styles fare better).

ideas.idea: Connecting/function words and prompt rephrasings are statistically insignificant; content keywords (subject, style) drive output quality.
ideas.rationale: A weak, indirect lesson for Memoria's prompt engineering: for its local-model extraction/judging prompts, invest tokens in the load-bearing content slots rather than phrasing variants. Off-domain (image gen), so borrow only the general prompt-design insight.

ideas.idea: Failure modes from lexical ambiguity (mola the art style read as mola the fish; 'action painting' read as a person in action) — model latches onto the more Internet-frequent meaning of a polysemous term.
ideas.rationale: Relevant to Memoria's keyphrase tag candidates and entity dedup: polysemous author/venue/topic terms can be mis-resolved by a cheap model the same way. Reinforces the need for the disambiguation/POTENTIAL escalation seen in the citation paper, but no concrete mechanism to adopt here.

ideas.idea: Systematic probing of a model via a constrained keyword/prompt grid to map its knowledge and biases (e.g., Western vs non-Western style coverage).
ideas.rationale: Methodological: Memoria could probe its local model's domain coverage with a fixed keyword grid before trusting it on a topic. Useful as evaluation methodology, not a buildable feature.

ideas.idea: Text-to-image specific guidelines (seed counts, iteration counts, style/subject abstractness interactions, VQGAN+CLIP hyperparameters).
ideas.rationale: Entirely off-domain for a text-based academic-knowledge system; Memoria does no image generation, so the hyperparameter and style guidance is inapplicable.

file: Liu et al. - 2019 - RoBERTa a robustly optimized BERT pretraining approach.md
title: RoBERTa: A Robustly Optimized BERT Pretraining Approach
one_line: A careful replication showing BERT was undertrained: train longer on more data with bigger batches, drop next-sentence-prediction, use full-sentence packing, dynamic masking, and byte-level BPE to reach SOTA on GLUE/SQuAD/RACE — establishing RoBERTa as a strong general encoder backbone.

ideas.idea: RoBERTa as a robust pretrained encoder for downstream sentence/sentence-pair classification (GLUE-style tasks).
ideas.rationale: Memoria does claim-sentence classification, MASSW aspect tagging, and contradiction/entailment-style relation typing — all sentence and sentence-pair classification tasks where a fine-tuned RoBERTa (or a small distilled variant) is a cheap, deterministic, local, non-generative classifier that fits the no-cloud, faithfulness-first constraints better than an LLM judge.

ideas.idea: Sentence-pair entailment/NLI capability (MNLI, RTE, STS-B) as a learned-similarity backbone.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's contradiction dashboard, claim supersession, and pre-file similarity gate need pairwise semantic-relation scoring; an NLI-tuned encoder gives a deterministic supporting/contradicting signal between claims without invoking a generative model. Borrow the encoder, not the training recipe.

ideas.idea: Byte-level BPE (50K) that encodes any input with no unknown tokens, no language-specific preprocessing.
ideas.rationale: Useful tokenization insight for robustly handling messy PDF-extracted text (math, identifiers, non-English author names) in Memoria's ingest; relevant if any local embedding/classifier is trained or fine-tuned.

ideas.idea: Large-scale pretraining recipe details (1024 V100 GPUs, 160GB corpus, 500K steps, large-batch optimization, dynamic masking, NSP ablation).
ideas.rationale: Memoria will never pretrain an encoder; the GPU-cluster training methodology is irrelevant under single-researcher, local-hardware constraints. Only the resulting off-the-shelf model is usable.

file: Liu et al. - 2020 - Keep the phone in your pocket enabling smartphone operation with an IMU ring for visually impaired.md
title: Keep the Phone in Your Pocket: Enabling Smartphone Operation with an IMU Ring for Visually Impaired People
one_line: A UbiComp accessibility system: a single-IMU finger ring lets visually impaired users perform 15 subtle on-any-surface gestures to operate a pocketed phone, with SVM-based touchdown detection and gesture recognition at 95.5% accuracy.

ideas.idea: Two-stage deterministic-then-classifier pipeline: a fast SVM touchdown detector segments gesture starts before a second SVM classifies the 15 gestures, plus debounce rules (ignore events within 50ms, require 10 consecutive positive frames).
ideas.rationale: Only an abstract methodological echo of Memoria's cheap-gate-then-classifier ingest pattern (e.g., similarity gate before claim extraction); the debounce/consecutive-confirmation idea for noisy event streams is mildly transferable to deduplicating near-duplicate discovery hits. The wearable/sensing substance is off-domain.

ideas.idea: Participatory command/gesture elicitation: focus groups to derive a versatile command set, ease-of-use scoring, then map commands to gestures by idiom + semantic fit + score.
ideas.rationale: A faint design-process parallel to Memoria's PI-direct-access requirement (design surfaces around the single user's real workflow), but this is generic HCI method, not a Memoria-buildable mechanism.

ideas.idea: IMU ring hardware, on-body gesture sensing, accessibility for visually impaired smartphone use, frequency/battery tradeoffs.
ideas.rationale: Wholly off-domain: Memoria is a text/markdown research-knowledge system with no wearable, sensor, or mobile-accessibility component. None of the sensing or hardware contribution applies.

file: O'Leary et al. - 2018 - “Suddenly, we got to become therapists for each other” designing peer support chats for mental heal.md
title: “Suddenly, we got to become therapists for each other”: Designing Peer Support Chats for Mental Health
one_line: A two-week CHI field experiment comparing prompt-guided vs. unguided peer-support mental-health chats, finding guided chats produce 'deep' problem-solving while unguided chats are 'smooth' but burdensome, and surfacing the depth-vs-smoothness and guidance-as-scaffold tradeoffs.

ideas.idea: Structured prompt scaffolding (expressive/reflective prompt pairs) mitigates the burden of initiating/maintaining an open-ended task by providing explicit 'moves' to pick from.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's structured capture forms and guided gate review could borrow the move-by-move scaffold idea: rather than a blank note, offer the PI explicit prompts (e.g. 'state the claim', 'state the warrant', 'note the contradiction') to reduce blank-page friction. The therapeutic content is irrelevant; the scaffolding-reduces-initiation-cost insight transfers.

ideas.idea: 'Deep vs. smooth' deductive coding scheme plus inductively-discovered codes (reciprocity, continuity) as a quality lens on interactions.
ideas.rationale: This is a domain-specific qualitative coding rubric for therapy sessions; Memoria's claim-sentence classification and aspect extraction are about scholarly content, not affective session quality. No transfer.

ideas.idea: 'Design beyond the session' — carry topics/strategies forward across sessions and nudge at the next session's start; show trends over time.
ideas.rationale: Loosely supports Memoria's continuity-over-time stance: surfacing carried-forward open questions/threads at the start of a work session and showing claim/contradiction trends maps to the discovery loop and dashboards. Adapted only as a cross-session continuity nudge, not the chat mechanic.

ideas.idea: Adaptive guidance via NLP that detects topic shift and offers appropriate scaffolds.
ideas.rationale: Speculative future-work for affective chat steering; Memoria is single-PI deterministic ingest, not a real-time conversational adaptation system. Off-domain and contradicts the engines-write/agents-judge model.

file: OpenAI et al. - 2024 - GPT-4 technical report.md
title: GPT-4 Technical Report
one_line: OpenAI's capability/safety report for GPT-4, notable for predictable-scaling loss prediction, calibration analysis, hallucination/factuality evals, and a model-assisted safety pipeline using rule-based reward models (RBRMs) — GPT-4 LLM-judges scoring outputs against a written rubric.

ideas.idea: Rule-Based Reward Models (RBRMs): zero-shot LLM classifiers that take (prompt, output, human-written rubric) and classify the output into rubric categories (e.g. desired refusal / undesired refusal / disallowed content / safe).
ideas.rationale: This is exactly Memoria's 'agents judge' pattern. The structural review gate can use an LLM-as-rubric-judge: feed (engine-extracted note, source, written governance rubric) and have a local model classify pass/needs-revision/reject. The rubric-driven-classifier mechanism is directly buildable for the gate, claim-faithfulness checks, and pre-file similarity decisions — with a cheap local model standing in for GPT-4.

ideas.idea: Model calibration (predicted confidence ≈ probability of correctness), with the finding that RLHF post-training degrades calibration.
ideas.rationale: Memoria flags claims with uncertainty. Borrow the calibration concern: any extraction confidence score the engine emits should be checked against actual correctness, and we should not over-trust a heavily instruction-tuned local model's stated confidence. Informs how uncertainty flags are surfaced, not a buildable component.

ideas.idea: Hallucination is intrinsic and unfixed; high-stakes use needs human review or grounding with additional context.
ideas.rationale: Directly validates Memoria's faithfulness-over-flash + PI-approval architecture: ground every claim in the cited source, require human approval before notes are written. Reinforces the existing gate design rather than adding new mechanism.

ideas.idea: Predictable scaling: fit a power-law on small cheap runs to predict a large run's loss/capability before training.
ideas.rationale: Memoria trains nothing and runs at single-researcher scale on a fixed local model; pre-training scaling laws are irrelevant to its design.

ideas.idea: Adversarial red-teaming by domain experts to find high-risk failure modes before deployment.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's MCP-only sandbox and skill governance already embody a 'limit the blast radius' stance; the insight that targeted adversarial probing finds failures generic tests miss can inform how we stress-test engines/gate prompts. Lightweight adaptation, not the org-scale program.

file: Oppenlaender - 2024 - A taxonomy of prompt modifiers for text-to-image generation.md
title: A taxonomy of prompt modifiers for text-to-image generation
one_line: An ethnographic study yielding a six-type taxonomy of text-to-image prompt modifiers (subject term, style modifier, image prompt, quality booster, repeating term, magic term) and a five-step iterative prompt-writing workflow.

ideas.idea: A typed taxonomy of prompt components, each playing a distinct functional role (define / modify / solidify / vary / exclude), with weights to mix or negatively exclude terms.
ideas.rationale: Memoria compiles agent/engine prompts. Borrow the decompose-a-prompt-into-typed-functional-slots idea for prompt engineering of the extraction/judge engines: a template with explicit slots (task definition, style/format constraints, quality directives) is more maintainable than monolithic prompts. The image-art specifics don't apply; the slot-typing discipline does.

ideas.idea: Inductive taxonomy development to saturation (stop when no new categories appear) from a corpus of artifacts.
ideas.rationale: Mirrors Memoria's MASSW-style aspect schema and tag-candidate generation: a defensible way to derive a closed category set (e.g. relation types, claim-sentence classes) is to inductively grow it from real notes until saturation. Methodological transfer only.

ideas.idea: 'Magic terms' / repeating terms as folk-theory prompt hacks that exploit model latent-space quirks.
ideas.rationale: These are non-deterministic, model-specific aesthetic tricks contradicting Memoria's deterministic-ingest and faithfulness goals; injecting 'surprise' into extraction prompts is actively harmful here.

ideas.idea: Prompt engineering as an iterative trial-and-error skill learned from a community.
ideas.rationale: Single-PI, governed, compiled-profile system; Memoria deliberately avoids ad-hoc trial-and-error prompting in favor of versioned, reviewed prompts. No adoption.

file: Ouyang et al. - 2022 - Training language models to follow instructions with human feedback.md
title: Training language models to follow instructions with human feedback (InstructGPT)
one_line: InstructGPT: aligning GPT-3 to user intent via SFT on labeler demonstrations + a reward model on ranked-output comparisons + PPO, showing a 1.3B aligned model beats 175B GPT-3 on human preference, plus a detailed per-output quality/harm rubric and inter-annotator agreement methodology.

ideas.idea: A structured labeler metadata rubric scoring each model output on an overall Likert quality plus binary flags (fails-to-follow-instruction, hallucination, satisfies-constraint, harmful-advice, expresses-opinion/moral-judgment).
ideas.rationale: Directly buildable as Memoria's gate/judge rubric: each engine-produced note/claim can be scored by an LLM-judge on analogous binary flags (faithful-to-source? hallucinated? satisfies-extraction-constraint? expresses-unwarranted-claim?). This concretizes the 'agents judge' rubric and feeds the structural review gate.

ideas.idea: Closed-domain faithfulness framing: output should contain no information not present in the input; measured as a hallucination rate (21% vs 41%).
ideas.rationale: This is precisely Memoria's atomic-claim-extraction contract — a claim must be grounded in the source. Adopt the closed-domain hallucination-rate as an evaluation metric for the extraction engine, gating notes that introduce ungrounded content.

ideas.idea: Reward-model-from-pairwise-comparisons + PPO RLHF training pipeline.
ideas.rationale: Memoria trains no models and uses fixed local models (qwen2.5:7b); building an RM + RL pipeline is far out of single-researcher scope and contradicts the no-training constraint. The alignment outcome matters, the training machinery doesn't.

ideas.idea: Inter-annotator agreement (~72-77%) and held-out-labeler generalization as a way to validate that a preference signal is reliable and not overfit to one rater.
ideas.rationale: Memoria has one PI, so multi-annotator agreement isn't literal, but the insight that a judging rubric should be checked for consistency (e.g. re-judge stability across runs, or PI-vs-engine agreement on a sample) transfers to validating the gate's LLM-judge before trusting it.

ideas.idea: Alignment tax — optimizing for one objective regresses others; mitigate by mixing the original objective back in (PPO-ptx).
ideas.rationale: General design caution for Memoria: tuning a judge/extraction prompt to maximize one quality (e.g. faithfulness) can regress another (e.g. recall of nuanced claims). Borrow the watch-for-regressions-on-a-held-out-set discipline when iterating prompts.

ideas.idea: Small-aligned beats large-unaligned: a 1.3B instruction-tuned model preferred over 175B base.
ideas.rationale: Validates Memoria's local/cheap-model bet: a well-prompted/instruction-tuned small local model can suffice for the task if the task is well-specified, reducing reliance on cloud-scale models. Supports the qwen2.5:7b default.

file: O’Brien et al. - 2014 - Standards for reporting qualitative research a synthesis of recommendations.md
title: Standards for Reporting Qualitative Research (SRQR): A Synthesis of Recommendations
one_line: A 21-item reporting-standards checklist (SRQR) for qualitative research, synthesized from 40 sources, covering title/abstract, problem/question, design/methods (incl. reflexivity, sampling, trustworthiness techniques), results, and discussion — a structured schema for what a qualitative study report must contain.

ideas.idea: A 21-item structured reporting schema with per-item definitions and a 'rationale' requirement (justify each methodological choice, not just name it).
ideas.rationale: Directly usable as a paper-aspect schema for Memoria's MASSW-style aspect extraction on qualitative/empirical papers: the SRQR items (approach/paradigm, sampling strategy, trustworthiness techniques, reflexivity, limitations) are extractable fields the ingest engine can populate. Gives a concrete, citable checklist of what to pull from a qualitative paper into structured note fields.

ideas.idea: Reflexivity item (S6): record how researcher characteristics/assumptions influenced the findings.
ideas.rationale: Useful as an extraction target (flag whether a paper reports researcher positionality) and as a methodological-quality signal feeding triage/relevance scoring — a paper that omits it is weaker evidence. Adapted as a metadata flag, not a core mechanism.

ideas.idea: 'Techniques to enhance trustworthiness' (member checking, audit trail, triangulation) as named, checkable methods rather than vague claims.
ideas.rationale: Maps to Memoria's uncertainty-flagging and evidence-grounding: when extracting claims from qualitative work, capture which trustworthiness techniques back a finding so the argument graph can weight warrants by methodological support. Transfer as a claim-warrant attribute.

ideas.idea: Distinguish reporting completeness from methodological rigor — a checklist for the former, explicitly not judging the latter.
ideas.rationale: Sharp design principle for Memoria's gate: separate 'is the note complete/well-formed' (a structural check the engine/gate can enforce) from 'is the underlying science good' (a judgment the PI makes). Reinforces the engines-write / agents-judge-structure / PI-approves-substance split.

ideas.idea: Checklist-as-planning-tool (use the standards before and during the study, not just at write-up).
ideas.rationale: This is advice to authors conducting studies; Memoria ingests already-published papers and doesn't run the PI's own qualitative studies, so the planning-phase use has no hook in the system.

file: Crothers et al. - 2023 - Machine generated text a comprehensive survey of threat models and detection methods.md
title: Machine Generated Text: A Comprehensive Survey of Threat Models and Detection Methods
one_line: A cybersecurity-framed survey cataloguing NLG abuse threat models and the full landscape of machine-generated-text detection methods (feature-based, neural/RoBERTa fine-tuning, zero-shot, human-aided), with attention to fairness, robustness, and explainability.

ideas.idea: Feature-based statistical detectors (Zipf/lemma-frequency fit, n-gram and POS-tag overlap, supermaximal repeated substrings, Gunning-Fog/Flesch readability, coreference consistency) as robust, cheap, explainable signals.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and structural review gate could borrow these as a cheap, local, deterministic 'is this note/claim plausibly machine-boilerplate or low-information' heuristic before LLM judgment; they run without a model and are explainable, matching the local/cheap constraint.

ideas.idea: Combining statistical + neural features in parallel ensembles to harden against adversarial attacks and improve cross-architecture transfer.
ideas.rationale: Memoria has no adversary trying to evade it; ingested papers are not attacker-crafted text. Adversarial-robustness engineering is off-scope for a single-researcher trusted corpus.

ideas.idea: Detection as binary classification of machine vs human text; RoBERTa fine-tuning as SOTA; model-attribution to identify which generator produced text.
ideas.rationale: Memoria ingests vetted academic papers via Zotero/citekey backbone; provenance is known. Detecting/attributing machine text is not a Memoria task.

ideas.idea: Prompt injection awareness: NLG agents that consume untrusted text can be hijacked to leak their prompt or deviate.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's MCP-only sandbox and structural review gate: ingested paper text is untrusted input to extraction agents, so engines (not agents) should do deterministic parsing and agents must never gain file/terminal access. A useful threat-model lens for the ingest pipeline, not a new feature.

ideas.idea: Human-aided detection with human agency/oversight (GLTR-style) as a trustworthy-AI principle; the 'engine flags low-confidence segments, human reviews them' pattern.
ideas.rationale: Mirrors Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' gate: surface low-confidence extractions to the PI rather than auto-committing. Borrow the targeted-human-review interaction pattern, not the detection use-case.

ideas.idea: Explainability requirement for flagging decisions (SHAP on RF/XGBoost) so decisions are human-interpretable and not perceived as arbitrary suppression.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's gate/dashboards should give the PI a human-readable rationale for why a claim was flagged/superseded/deduped, not opaque scores; the interpretability-over-flash principle aligns.

file: Cui et al. - 2022 - Prototypical verbalizer for prompt-based few-shot tuning.md
title: Prototypical Verbalizer for Prompt-based Few-shot Tuning (ProtoVerb)
one_line: Builds a verbalizer for prompt-based few-shot classification by learning class prototype vectors from training instances via contrastive learning (InfoNCE), beating search/soft verbalizers under extreme data scarcity and even boosting untuned PLMs.

ideas.idea: Class prototypes learned from a handful of labelled instances via contrastive InfoNCE loss, usable as a classifier head that needs no model fine-tuning ('non-tuning' use of a fixed PLM).
ideas.rationale: Directly applicable to Memoria's claim-sentence classification and MASSW aspect tagging: the PI can label a few examples and a prototype head over a frozen local embedding model classifies new sentences cheaply, no LLM fine-tune, matching local/cheap constraints and single-researcher cold-start.

ideas.idea: Prototypes as centroids capture class-level (not entity-level) semantics; ProtoVerb beats manual verbalizers as examples grow, and prototype vectors can be discretized back to nearest vocabulary words for inspection.
ideas.rationale: The discretization trick gives an inspectable, read-only explanation of what each label 'means' to the system, fitting Memoria's read-only inspector and faithfulness-over-flash ethos; centroid-summarization also echoes record-linkage/dedup centroids.

ideas.idea: Demonstrated robustness to a few noisy/mislabelled samples relative to search/soft verbalizers.
ideas.rationale: Single-researcher labelling will be small and occasionally inconsistent; a method that degrades gracefully under label noise is valuable for the PI-driven, low-volume training regime.

ideas.idea: Ensemble prototype verbalizer with a manual verbalizer (standard-scale then average logits) to combine prior knowledge with learned data.
ideas.rationale: Lets Memoria seed a classifier with PI-authored label words and refine with examples over time, a pragmatic warm-start for triage/aspect tagging without retraining.

ideas.idea: Full prompt-based tuning of a RoBERTa/BERT backbone (the tuned-PLM variants and templates).
ideas.rationale: Memoria prefers frozen local models (qwen2.5:7b) and deterministic pipelines; maintaining fine-tuned task-specific PLMs adds training infrastructure that conflicts with the local/cheap, low-ops posture. The non-tuning prototype path is the part worth borrowing.

file: Dang et al. - 2022 - How to prompt Opportunities and challenges of zero- and few-shot learning for human-AI interaction.md
title: How to Prompt? Opportunities and Challenges of Zero- and Few-Shot Learning for Human-AI Interaction in Creative Applications of Generative Models
one_line: An HCI position paper proposing four design goals and concrete UI sketches for prompting interfaces (formulating, combining, applying, representing prompts) in creative writing with LLMs.

ideas.idea: End-user programming: users define reusable tools ad hoc via natural-language prompts and build a personal library of generative tools for reuse across contexts.
ideas.rationale: Maps onto Memoria's compiled profiles / skill governance: the PI authors prompt-defined operations (e.g. an 'extract counter-arguments' skill) once and reuses them. Borrow the reusable-prompt-as-tool-library framing, but governed by Memoria's skill-governance and MCP sandbox, not free-form runtime tool creation.

ideas.idea: Parse a free-text prompt into editable structured parameters (detected task + params shown as dropdowns), and offer pre-validated 'building-block' prompt components.
ideas.rationale: Aligns with Memoria's structured capture forms and Obsidian-UI-direct principle: give the PI a structured, editable surface over prompts/queries rather than a raw text box, improving repeatability of discovery/triage queries.

ideas.idea: Prompt chaining / AI-Chains: decompose a complex task into a sequence of sub-prompts, and a narrative-tree UI for exploring branching outputs.
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's multi-step ingest/extraction pipelines and the disposable projection engine; decomposing extraction into auditable sub-steps fits 'engines write, agents judge'. The branching-exploration canvas idea suits projected canvas, but is secondary.

ideas.idea: Asynchronous prompt execution embedded in the document: insert a prompt anywhere, it triggers an async LLM call, user keeps working and is notified when ready.
ideas.rationale: Matches Memoria's nightly proactive discovery loop and gate model: long local-model calls run in background and surface results to the PI later, rather than blocking. A concrete interaction pattern for the Obsidian surfaces.

ideas.idea: Represent saved prompts as toolbar symbols/annotations applied to text selections; split prompt-view vs text-view editor.
ideas.rationale: This is creative-writing-tool UI polish; Memoria is a research-knowledge system, not a co-writing tool. Symbol toolbars over prose don't serve claim/relation curation and would be UI surface bloat.

ideas.idea: Challenges noted: prompts generalise poorly across models (lock-in), computational delay, inherited model bias.
ideas.rationale: Validates Memoria's deterministic-ingest and local-model choices: prompt brittleness across models argues for pinning prompts to a known local model and faithfulness checks at the gate rather than trusting raw generation.

file: Davis - What is a knowledge representation.md
title: What Is a Knowledge Representation?
one_line: Classic AI essay arguing a knowledge representation plays five roles simultaneously — surrogate, set of ontological commitments, fragmentary theory of intelligent reasoning, medium for efficient computation, and medium of human expression — each imposing distinct, sometimes conflicting demands.

ideas.idea: A representation is a surrogate with imperfect fidelity; all sufficiently broad reasoning over the world eventually errs, so the goal is choosing a representation that minimizes error for the task at hand.
ideas.rationale: Directly underwrites Memoria's atomic-claims-with-uncertainty-flags and contradictions/supersession machinery: the claim store is an admittedly-lossy surrogate, so uncertainty flags and an explicit 'this can be wrong' stance are principled, not optional. Adopt as a design axiom for the claim model.

ideas.idea: Selecting a representation = making ontological commitments ('a strong pair of glasses') that bring some aspects into focus and blur others; commitment accumulates in layers (technology -> task scheme -> instantiation).
ideas.rationale: Memoria's typed-relations vocabulary, MASSW aspects, claim-sentence classes, and 'spaces' are exactly such layered ontological commitments. The essay justifies treating the relation/aspect schema as a deliberate, documented choice (ADR-worthy) rather than an incidental data model, and warns to choose it consciously per task.

ideas.idea: A representation is NOT a data structure: the representational content is the correspondence to the world plus the constraints that correspondence imposes (e.g., a family-membership net cannot cycle).
ideas.rationale: Tells Memoria to enforce semantic constraints on its argument graph and typed relations (e.g., supersession is acyclic, 'supports'/'contradicts' have meaning and validity rules) at the structural review gate — constraints are the representation, so the gate should validate them, not just store edges.

ideas.idea: Sanctioned vs recommended inferences: a representation must say not only what can be inferred but which inferences are worth making, else the reasoner drowns in legal-but-useless conclusions.
ideas.rationale: Frames Memoria's discovery relevance scoring, learning-to-rank triage, and argument-graph warrants as the 'recommended inferences' layer — the system should bias toward inferences the PI cares about (relevance/warrants) rather than enumerating all graph-derivable links. Justifies ranking/triage as core, not cosmetic.

ideas.idea: Combine representations by reconciling their theories of intelligent reasoning, not just wiring their mechanisms together; and beware claims that a new representation is 'just' formally equivalent to logic.
ideas.rationale: Guidance for integrating Memoria's heterogeneous structures (BERTopic clusters, KG entities, claim graph, embeddings): integrate at the level of what reasoning each supports (topical neighborhood vs entailment vs identity), not by flattening all into one store. Useful design caution; less directly buildable than the adopt items.

ideas.idea: A representation is a medium of human expression — pragmatic ease of saying things matters ('how easy?', not just 'can we?').
ideas.rationale: This is Memoria's PI-direct-access / Obsidian-UI principle in 1993 terms: the claim/relation vocabulary and capture forms must be things the PI can express without heroic effort. Adopt as a usability test for any schema added to the vault.

file: Davis et al. - 2017 - Creative sense-making quantifying interaction dynamics in Co-creation.md
title: Creative Sense-Making: Quantifying Interaction Dynamics in Co-Creation
one_line: Proposes a cognitive framework and video-coding tool that scores moments of human/AI co-creation on a clamped<->unclamped (free-energy) continuum to quantify turn-taking and sense-making dynamics over time.

ideas.idea: Continuous time-series coding of interaction (the 'sense-making curve'), then analyze via integrals/derivatives and stock-market-style trend detection to classify collaboration regimes (mutual clamp, dominant clamp, turn-taking).
ideas.rationale: Memoria is asynchronous PI-plus-engines, not real-time embodied co-creation; there is no continuous dyadic interaction stream to quantify. The whole apparatus targets a problem Memoria does not have.

ideas.idea: Free-energy / clamped-vs-unclamped framing: an agent invests effort (unclamped exploration) to refine its generative model and reduce future surprise, then settles (clamped) on a stable shared meaning structure.
ideas.rationale: Evocative but not operational for Memoria; the system's 'surprise' is already handled concretely by contradiction detection, novelty/relevance scoring, and supersession. Re-casting these in free-energy terms adds no buildable mechanism.

ideas.idea: Delphi-method iterative refinement of a coding scheme + inter-rater reliability (Fleiss' kappa, Krippendorff's alpha) to validate that a qualitative rubric is reproducible.
ideas.rationale: Marginally useful: when Memoria defines a labelling rubric for claim-sentence classes or aspect tags, the iterate-rubric-until-agreement discipline and reporting agreement could validate that the engine's labels match PI intent. Borrow the validation methodology only, not the cognitive theory.

ideas.idea: Behavioral markers map observable signals to hidden cognitive states (hesitation -> perceptual unclamp, etc.) as a transparent proxy.
ideas.rationale: Domain-specific (embodied play/drawing) and not transferable to a text-knowledge system; Memoria infers from text/claims, not bodily hesitation markers.

file: Zou et al. - 2024 - DOCBENCH a benchmark for evaluating LLM-based document reading systems.md
title: DOCBENCH: A Benchmark for Evaluating LLM-based Document Reading Systems
one_line: A benchmark of 229 real PDFs and 1,102 QA pairs across 5 domains and 4 question types (text-only, multi-modal, meta-data, unanswerable) for evaluating end-to-end LLM document-reading systems, revealing large gaps vs. human performance on long, multi-modal, and unanswerable cases.

ideas.idea: Four orthogonal question-type axes for stressing a document reader: text-only, multi-modal (tables/figures), meta-data (page numbers, word counts, term-frequency), and unanswerable.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's deterministic ingest + claim extraction can be regression-tested with a similar typed QA suite over the PI's own papers; meta-data and unanswerable categories in particular probe faithfulness, which Memoria prizes over flash. Adapt the taxonomy, not the dataset.

ideas.idea: Explicit 'unanswerable' category to measure fidelity — penalizing systems that hallucinate answers not grounded in the document.
ideas.rationale: Directly maps to Memoria's faithfulness-over-flash constraint and its uncertainty flags on atomic claims. An eval that rewards 'not stated in this paper' is exactly the discipline the structural review gate enforces; build unanswerable probes into the claim-extraction QA harness.

ideas.idea: Location -> Extraction -> Calculation failure decomposition for multi-modal (table/figure) questions, showing systems fail at distinct stages.
ideas.rationale: Memoria parses tables/figures during local ingest; decomposing multi-modal extraction errors into locate/extract/compute stages gives a concrete diagnostic for where the deterministic parser vs. the LLM aspect-extractor is failing, feeding the content-light diagnostics plane.

ideas.idea: LLM-as-judge (GPT-4) scoring with answer-type-specific criteria embedded in the prompt, achieving 98% agreement with humans vs. ~55% for string matching.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's L5 quality tests already need a judge; the type-conditioned rubric (Y/N vs numeric vs abstractive) is a reusable pattern. But Memoria prefers local/cheap models, so adopt the rubric design while validating agreement on qwen2.5:7b/qwen3:14b rather than depending on GPT-4.

ideas.idea: Difficulty-graded synthetic QA generation (Easy/Medium/Hard) and the finding that asking the model to emit graded items raises generation quality.
ideas.rationale: Useful trick if Memoria auto-generates self-test QA over ingested papers; the difficulty-laddering prompt improves item quality cheaply. Marginal, hence borrow.

ideas.idea: Parse-then-read pipeline (fitz extract text+image blocks, denote images as [image i], feed to an open LLM) outperforms some proprietary black-box systems on multi-modal and unanswerable questions.
ideas.rationale: This validates Memoria's core architecture: deterministic local parse feeding an open model beats opaque all-in-one systems, and gives better faithfulness on unanswerable cases. Confirms the parse-then-read + local-model design choice rather than introducing new mechanism.

ideas.idea: Proprietary all-in-one document systems overfit and lose fidelity on unanswerable questions (GPT-4 system 37% vs its own parse-then-read pipeline 70%).
ideas.rationale: Cautionary insight: heavy product-level optimization can degrade faithfulness. Reinforces Memoria's separation of deterministic engines from judging agents and its refusal of black-box cloud document systems.

ideas.idea: Multi-domain document corpus (Academia, Finance, Government, Laws, News) as the evaluation scope.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is single-domain (academic research papers, Zotero/citekey backbone). Finance/Government/Laws/News breadth is off-target; only the Academia slice and the question-type axes are relevant.

ideas.idea: Benchmarking 22 systems / 15 base LLMs by max file size and context length across web UI vs API access modes.
ideas.rationale: A comparative leaderboard of commercial systems is not a Memoria need; Memoria runs a fixed local stack and cares about its own regression suite, not ranking vendors.

file: Efrat and Levy - 2020 - The turking test can language models understand instructions.md
title: The Turking Test: Can Language Models Understand Instructions?
one_line: An early (pre-instruction-tuning) probe showing GPT-2 fails to follow natural-language instructions of even trivial complexity, proposing instruction-following as a benchmarking paradigm and a strict generalization of few-shot.

ideas.idea: Instruction paradigm is a strict generalization of few-shot: instructions can carry negative/forbidden signals ('do NOT write why questions') that examples alone cannot express implicitly.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's compiled profiles and skill prompts run on cheap local models (qwen2.5:7b); the insight that explicit prohibitions/negative constraints belong in the instruction text (not just exemplars) is directly useful when writing extraction/classification prompts for claim-sentence classification and aspect extraction.

ideas.idea: Lenient evaluation relaxations (prefix rule, introduction rule, oracle generation algorithm) to grade model outputs charitably when the model rarely emits EOS or wraps answers in preamble.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's deterministic ingest must parse small-model outputs that often include preambles ('Here are the claims:') or trailing junk; a prefix/introduction-style tolerant parser plus post-hoc selection of the best decoding setting is a practical robustness pattern for the engine layer.

ideas.idea: Models are hyper-sensitive to tiny instruction changes (nth word vs nth character produced totally different failure patterns); per-task manual instruction refinement (pick the wording the model does best on).
ideas.rationale: Justifies Memoria's profile-compilation step empirically tuning prompt wording per engine task against held-out papers, rather than assuming a clean instruction 'just works' on a 7B model.

ideas.idea: Core empirical result that a 1.5B LM cannot follow instructions at all (2% on nth-word).
ideas.rationale: Superseded: GPT-2 predates instruction tuning/RLHF; modern instruction-tuned 7B models Memoria targets handle these tasks fine, so the headline finding has no bearing on current design.

ideas.idea: Turking tasks (have the model generate valid SNLI/SQuAD-style annotation examples) as a probe.
ideas.rationale: Synthetic-data-generation benchmarking is off-domain for Memoria, which extracts from real papers rather than fabricating dataset examples.

file: Engelbart - Augmenting human intellect a conceptual framework - 1962 (AUGMENT,3906,) - doug engelbart institute.md
title: Augmenting Human Intellect: A Conceptual Framework
one_line: Engelbart's foundational 1962 vision of the H-LAM/T system: augmenting (not replacing) an individual's intellect through co-evolving artifacts, language, methodology, and training, with interlinked working records that mature alongside thought.

ideas.idea: Augment rather than automate: the goal is a better match between human intelligence and complex problems via tools, not making the machine think for the human ('intelligence amplification', not artificial replacement).
ideas.rationale: This is precisely Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' philosophy and the structural review gate — the system amplifies the single PI's reasoning and keeps the human in the loop, never autonomously deciding. Engelbart is the canonical citation for this design stance.

ideas.idea: Working record as an 'ever-more-detailed, interlinked structure which represents the maturing thought behind the design' — notes/specs that grow and cross-link as understanding deepens.
ideas.rationale: Directly describes Memoria's typed-relation graph, argument graph, and claim supersession over time: the vault is the externalized, interlinked maturing thought-structure of the PI, exactly Engelbart's interlinked working lists.

ideas.idea: Rapid, low-friction rearrangement of the working record ('compose trial drafts of rearranged excerpts', cheaply restructure when the tangle gets too complex) is what unlocks accommodating more complexity.
