ideas.rationale: Motivates Memoria's disposable projection engine and projected canvas — cheap, regenerable rearrangements of the underlying notes let the PI explore structure without committing, lowering the cost of handling complexity.

ideas.idea: Capability/repertoire hierarchy: a change at a low-order capability propagates up and down, triggering a chain of coordinating improvements across the whole system.
ideas.rationale: Frames why a single deterministic-ingest improvement (e.g. better claim extraction) cascades through triage, contradictions dashboard, and discovery — useful systems-thinking lens for prioritizing where to invest, though not a buildable feature itself.

ideas.idea: A new artifact lets the architect ask the 'clerk' for any view (slice, perspective, functional analysis) of the same underlying model on demand.
ideas.rationale: Anticipates Memoria's read-only inspector, spaces, and Obsidian Bases views — multiple disposable projections over one canonical store. Reinforces the projection-over-duplication principle.

ideas.idea: The 'clerk' as a subordinate clerical assistant the human directs minute-by-minute.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's runtime is nightly/batch (proactive discovery loop) and gate-mediated, not a real-time interactive clerk; the synchronous always-on assistant framing doesn't match the local, asynchronous, governance-first architecture.

file: Epstein et al. - 2020 - Mapping and taking stock of the personal informatics literature.md
title: Mapping and Taking Stock of the Personal Informatics Literature
one_line: A mapping review of 523 self-tracking/personal-informatics papers, organized around Li et al.'s five-stage model (preparation, collection, integration, reflection, action) and surfacing under-served stages, weak privacy engagement, and declining artifact contributions.

ideas.idea: Li et al.'s five-stage model (preparation -> collection -> integration -> reflection -> action) with cascading barriers: a failure at an early stage (forgetting to log) makes every later stage harder.
ideas.rationale: Maps cleanly onto Memoria's pipeline: preparation = pre-file similarity gate / what-to-ingest; collection = deterministic ingest + claim extraction; integration = record-linkage entity dedup across notes; reflection = contradictions/argument-graph dashboards; action = triage/discovery. Adopting this staged framing helps diagnose where the PI's workflow breaks and where to invest, and the cascading-barrier insight argues for getting ingest/integration right before reflection surfaces.

ideas.idea: The field over-invests in collection+reflection and under-invests in integration and action; integration is hardest in long-term tracking with many siloed tools.
ideas.rationale: Validates Memoria's heavy investment in integration (record-linkage entity dedup, typed relations, claim supersession) and action (learning-to-rank triage, proactive discovery) rather than just capture+dashboards — the literature gap is exactly where Memoria differentiates.

ideas.idea: Privacy/ethics are routinely neglected; risk emerges not just from corporations but from social sharing (data 'over the shoulder').
ideas.rationale: Memoria is single-user and local (no multi-user sharing), so social-sharing privacy is moot, but the local-first, no-data-egress stance and MCP-only sandbox are exactly the privacy posture this literature wishes for; reinforces keeping everything on-device.

ideas.idea: Flexible/configurable tracking (OmniTrack, paper bullet journals) lowers preparation burden but the field rarely helps users decide what is worth tracking or whether a tool fits.
ideas.rationale: Analogous to Memoria's structured capture forms and pre-file similarity gate: configurable capture plus a gate that judges whether a candidate is worth filing addresses the under-served 'preparation/fit' decision the review flags.

ideas.idea: Declining artifact contributions; call for lower-fidelity prototypes and platforms that lower the burden of deploying/evaluating tracking tools.
ideas.rationale: Encourages Memoria's disposable-projection and engines-as-cheap-prototypes approach over heavyweight bespoke UI; build evaluable surfaces cheaply (static HTML admin reports, projected canvas) rather than polished apps.

ideas.idea: Behavior-change theory, domain-expert involvement, multi-stakeholder/collaborative tracking, wellness/clinical health domain focus.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is a single-PI research-knowledge tool, not a health/behavior-change system; multi-stakeholder collaboration, clinicians, and behavior-change theory are out of scope and contradict the single-researcher constraint.

file: Erickson et al. - 2002 - Social translucence designing social infrastructures that make collective activity visible.md
title: Social Translucence: Designing Social Infrastructures That Make Collective Activity Visible
one_line: Introduces 'social translucence' and the 'social proxy' — minimalist persistent visualizations of presence/activity that support mutual awareness and accountability in online groups, demonstrated in the Babble system.

ideas.idea: Social proxy: a minimalist visualization that makes activity traces visible and persistent so people can draw inferences and govern behavior.
ideas.rationale: The single-user analogue is Memoria's dashboards/gates and timeline-style activity surfaces: a minimalist projection of what the engines/agents did (claims extracted, contradictions found, discovery hits) so the PI can judge at a glance. The 'visible persistent trace -> human draws inference -> human decides' pattern is exactly the structural review gate's UX, just with the group replaced by one PI plus automated agents.

ideas.idea: Translucence over transparency: deliberately NOT showing everything; preserve 'socially useful ambiguities' and propagate cues differentially.
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's read-only inspector and content-light diagnostics plane: surface enough signal (counts, statuses, confidence/uncertainty flags) for the PI to act without dumping raw internals; selective, faithful summarization beats exhaustive exposure.

ideas.idea: Infrastructure-not-straightjacket: the proxy informs but does not enforce; the group decides how to use the information.
ideas.rationale: Aligns with 'agents judge, PI approves' — Memoria surfaces evidence (contradictions, similarity, triage rank) but the PI retains the decision at the gate; the system advises, it doesn't auto-act.

ideas.idea: Multi-user collaboration mechanics: presence circles, peer pressure, accountability among many people, counterfeit-crowd trust concerns in auctions/lectures/lines.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is explicitly single-researcher, not multi-user/enterprise; the collective-governance and social-accountability machinery has no counterpart and contradicts the core scale constraint.

file: Fallman - 2003 - Design-oriented human-computer interaction.md
title: Design-oriented Human-Computer Interaction
one_line: Argues HCI is a design-oriented field and distinguishes three accounts of design (conservative/romantic/pragmatic), framing design as a sketching 'dialogue' where problem-setting and problem-solving co-evolve, and separating Design-oriented Research from Research-oriented Design.

ideas.idea: Design as reflective conversation: problem-setting and problem-solving are an intertwined pair worked out simultaneously; you don't know the problem until you start solving it.
ideas.rationale: Justifies Memoria's iterative, ADR-driven, deferred-ADR design model where decisions are revisited as understanding matures, and the disposable-projection workflow where the PI explores structure to discover what the real question is — co-evolution of problem and solution rather than upfront spec.

ideas.idea: Sketching/prototyping as thinking (low-cost externalizations that 'talk back'), and the warning that focusing on the artifact's fidelity hides the real dialogue.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces building cheap, regenerable projected canvases and static reports as thinking aids, not polished deliverables; the value is the dialogue the PI has with the projected structure, not the surface itself.

ideas.idea: Distinction between Design-oriented Research (knowledge is the contribution, artifact is the means) and Research-oriented Design (artifact is the goal).
ideas.rationale: Maps onto the memory note's 'separate re-implement-approved-goal from net-new-component tracks' collaboration style — useful vocabulary for classifying whether a given Memoria task is producing knowledge/an ADR or producing a shipping engine.

ideas.idea: Conservative account: design as a linear analysis->synthesis->evaluation pipeline driven by a complete requirements spec; rationality externalized into guidelines.
ideas.rationale: Fallman argues this fails in practice; Memoria's iterative ADR/gate process already rejects the rigid waterfall framing, so adopting it would be a step backward.

ideas.idea: Romantic account: the designer as opaque 'black-box' creative genius whose process needn't be explained.
ideas.rationale: Directly contradicts Memoria's faithfulness/auditability principles (structural review gate, ADRs, read-only inspector) which demand the rationale be explicit and inspectable, not mystical.

file: De Angelis et al. - 2023 - ChatGPT and the rise of large language models the new AI-driven infodemic threat in public health.md
title: ChatGPT and the rise of large language models: the new AI-driven infodemic threat in public health
one_line: A perspective article warning that LLMs can flood scientific/health literature with fabricated, plausible-sounding text (an 'AI-driven infodemic'), and arguing for detectability and provenance safeguards.

ideas.idea: LLMs fabricate plausible-but-fake bibliographic references (real-looking author/journal/title, fake DOI) that are hard to spot.
ideas.rationale: Directly motivates a Memoria ingest guard: every claim/reference must be tied to a verifiable citekey/DOI in the Zotero backbone; the pre-file similarity gate and structural review should reject or flag any reference that cannot be resolved against a real identifier, treating unverifiable citations as a contradiction/uncertainty flag.

ideas.idea: Mandatory DOI use as a defense to rapidly verify references are real.
ideas.rationale: Fits Memoria's deterministic ingest and citekey/DOI backbone: enforce DOI/citekey resolution as a hard precondition before a note is written, making fabricated provenance structurally impossible to file.

ideas.idea: 'Detectable-by-design' policy and AI-text detectors to label machine-generated content.
ideas.rationale: Aimed at industry/regulatory policy and public-platform moderation, not a single-PI local vault; Memoria has no need to detect AI-authored text since the PI controls ingest and provenance is tracked by citekey, not by stylometry.

ideas.idea: LLMs are submissive and easily tricked into producing misinforming content by following user instructions.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' gate and faithfulness-over-flash stance: LLM-extracted claims must carry uncertainty flags and pass structural review rather than being trusted as authoritative.

ideas.idea: Risk of researcher deskilling and over-reliance on auto-suggested outputs.
ideas.rationale: Argues Memoria should keep the PI in the loop (approval gate, read-only inspector) and surface uncertainty rather than presenting synthesized text as finished fact; an insight, not a buildable feature.

ideas.idea: LLMs (BioBERT/BioGPT, GPT-2) used for abstract screening, summarization, and QA over large corpora (e.g., CORD-19) to tame literature volume.
ideas.rationale: Validates Memoria's discovery/triage/summarization direction at a high level, but the specific domain models are superseded and off-the-shelf; only the framing (AI to tame literature volume) is reusable.

file: Debenedetti et al. - 2024 - AgentDojo a dynamic environment to evaluate prompt injection attacks and defenses for LLM agents.md
title: AgentDojo: A Dynamic Environment to Evaluate Prompt Injection Attacks and Defenses for LLM Agents
one_line: A dynamic benchmark of realistic stateful tool-calling tasks plus prompt-injection attacks/defenses, showing that agents acting on untrusted data are easily hijacked and that tool-isolation/least-privilege defenses help most.

ideas.idea: Indirect prompt injection: untrusted data returned by tools (e.g., an ingested email/web page) hijacks the agent to take malicious actions.
ideas.rationale: Core threat for Memoria: agents process untrusted paper text, web discovery results, and PDFs. This is the security justification for the MCP-only sandbox (no file/terminal/code execution) and the structural review gate — engines parse, agents only judge, nothing untrusted can directly mutate the vault.

ideas.idea: Tool-filter / least-privilege isolation: restrict the agent to the minimal tool set needed for a task before it sees untrusted data (read-only tasks get only read tools), dropping attack success to ~7.5%.
ideas.rationale: Directly buildable into Memoria's profile compilation: compile per-task/per-profile tool allowlists (retriever/scout gets read-only MCP tools; only the approval path can write). Matches the MCP-only sandbox and 'engines write, agents judge' separation.

ideas.idea: Deterministic, state-based utility/security checks rather than LLM-as-judge, because an injection could also fool the LLM evaluator.
ideas.rationale: Strong argument for Memoria's deterministic ingest and structural review gate: validate note/claim writes with deterministic checks over vault state, not by asking another model — an injected paper shouldn't be able to talk its way past the gate.

ideas.idea: Dual-LLM / planner-dispatcher isolation where untrusted tool results are communicated only symbolically to the planner.
ideas.rationale: Adaptable to Memoria as separating the extraction engine (handles raw untrusted text) from the judging agent (sees only structured, sanitized candidates), limiting how raw paper text can steer downstream decisions; full dual-LLM machinery is heavier than a single-PI vault needs.

ideas.idea: Injection position matters — text near the end of a tool output is most effective; attacker control of a large fraction of context raises success.
ideas.rationale: Informs how Memoria formats untrusted content for agents: wrap/delimit ingested text, cap how much raw untrusted content reaches a judging prompt, and never place tool output where it can be read as instructions.

ideas.idea: Data delimiters / spotlighting and prompt-sandwiching as lightweight defenses (some even raise benign utility), though not robust to adaptive attacks.
ideas.rationale: Cheap to add when an agent must see untrusted paper text, but the paper itself shows they are not sufficient — useful as defense-in-depth atop the MCP sandbox, not as the primary guarantee.

ideas.idea: LLM-based prompt-injection detector (BERT classifier) on each tool output.
ideas.rationale: Paper shows it has high false-positive rates that degrade utility, and it adds a model dependency; for a single-PI local system the architectural isolation (sandbox + deterministic gate) is a better fit than a noisy classifier.

ideas.idea: Inverse-scaling observation: more capable models are easier to hijack because they execute the injected goal more competently.
ideas.rationale: Reassures Memoria's preference for small local models (qwen2.5:7b) is not purely a cost trade-off; capability alone doesn't buy safety, so robustness must come from architecture, not a bigger model.

ideas.idea: Dynamic/extensible benchmark with deterministic per-task utility functions and ground-truth tool-call sequences.
ideas.rationale: A template for Memoria's own internal regression suite for ingest/agent behavior: define tasks with deterministic state-based success checks so changes to engines/profiles can be evaluated without an LLM grader.

file: Deng et al. - 2022 - RLPrompt optimizing discrete text prompts with reinforcement learning.md
title: RLPrompt: Optimizing Discrete Text Prompts with Reinforcement Learning
one_line: Trains a small policy network with RL to generate discrete (often gibberish but transferable) prompts that optimize a black-box LM's task reward without gradient access.

ideas.idea: Optimize discrete prompts via RL against an arbitrary reward, treating the LM as a black box (no gradient access).
ideas.rationale: Memoria uses prompts engineered by the PI for a few stable local tasks (claim extraction, classification); standing up an RL training loop with reward design and a policy network is disproportionate for a single-PI deterministic-ingest system and adds a fragile moving part.

ideas.idea: Optimized prompts are often ungrammatical 'gibberish' yet still effective.
ideas.rationale: Conflicts with Memoria's faithfulness/interpretability and PI-direct-access principles; non-human-readable prompts are unauditable and unmaintainable in a vault the PI must understand and govern.

ideas.idea: Discrete prompts transfer across LMs (even small-to-large), so you can tune cheaply on a small model and run on a larger one.
ideas.rationale: A useful insight only: Memoria could prototype/tune prompt templates on the cheap local default and expect partial carryover to a fallback model, but the transfer is lossy and the gibberish caveat makes it a weak, optional lever.

ideas.idea: Input-specific z-score reward normalization and piecewise (dense+sparse) rewards to stabilize noisy optimization.
ideas.rationale: Generic technique worth remembering if Memoria ever does any reward/score normalization in learning-to-rank triage (normalize scores per-query to avoid scale bias), independent of the RL-prompt context.

file: Dey - 2001 - Understanding and using context.md
title: Understanding and Using Context
one_line: A foundational HCI paper defining 'context' operationally and giving an architecture (widgets, interpreters, aggregators, situations) for building context-aware applications.

ideas.idea: Operational definition: context is any information that characterizes the situation of an entity (person/place/object) relevant to an interaction.
ideas.rationale: A clean lens for Memoria's relevance/triage: discovery relevance and the retriever profile should score a paper by how it characterizes the PI's current research 'situation' (active projects, open questions), not by global popularity — relevance is situation-relative.

ideas.idea: Three context-aware behaviors: presentation of info/services, automatic execution of a service, and tagging of context to information for later retrieval.
ideas.rationale: Maps onto Memoria's surfaces: present (spaces/dashboards), auto-execute (nightly discovery loop), and tag-for-retrieval (keyphrase tag candidates / typed relations). The 'tag context for later retrieval' category especially validates capturing why/when a note was filed.

ideas.idea: Context interpreter abstraction: combine raw context signals into higher-level derived context (e.g., infer 'a meeting is occurring').
ideas.rationale: Parallels Memoria's engines that derive structure (claims, aspects, relations) from raw paper text; the interpreter-as-pure-transform idea reinforces a deterministic engine layer feeding the judging agents.

ideas.idea: Aggregator abstraction: collect all context about a single entity as a proxy, so designers reason in terms of entities.
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's entity-centric record-linkage/dedup: an aggregated per-entity (author, concept, claim) view is the natural unit, echoing the canonical-entity note that gathers all signals about one thing.

ideas.idea: Store context history automatically to predict/interpret future user intentions.
ideas.rationale: Backs Memoria's claim-supersession-over-time and temporal tracking, and a usage-history signal for learning-to-rank triage; but must respect the single-PI, faithfulness constraints (history as audit, not opaque prediction).

ideas.idea: The 'situation' abstraction: a declarative description of states of relevant entities that the toolkit wires up and fires on, lettable by end users.
ideas.rationale: Directly buildable: Memoria can let the PI declare 'situations' (e.g., 'a paper contradicts an accepted claim in active project X') that the system wires to triggers (contradictions dashboard, gate alerts) — a declarative, PI-accessible rule layer over the relation/claim graph rather than hand-wired logic.

ideas.idea: Distributed sensor/widget infrastructure transparent to applications (poll/subscribe).
ideas.rationale: Targets multi-device physical sensing (infrared positioning, etc.); irrelevant to a single local Obsidian vault with no physical-context sensors.

file: Diao et al. - 2023 - Black-box prompt learning for pre-trained language models.md
title: Black-Box Prompt Learning for Pre-trained Language Models
one_line: Learns discrete prompt tokens for a frozen, API-only LM via a variance-reduced policy-gradient estimator, plus a PMI-based method to build the candidate token vocabulary, evaluated on GPT-3/RoBERTa few-shot classification.

ideas.idea: Black-box discrete prompt optimization via variance-reduced policy gradient (VR-PGE) over per-position categorical token distributions, no model gradients needed.
ideas.rationale: Premised on a cloud API where weights are hidden and queries cost money; Memoria runs local open models (qwen2.5:7b) with full access, so there is no black-box constraint to solve and an RL optimization loop is unjustified overhead for a single PI.

ideas.idea: PMI-based unsupervised vocabulary construction: segment text on pointwise mutual information to extract domain/task-specific n-grams as candidate tokens.
ideas.rationale: Reusable inside Memoria's keyphrase/tag-candidate engine: PMI-based n-gram extraction is a cheap, deterministic, local way to surface domain-specific multiword tag candidates from a paper, independent of the prompt-learning use case.

ideas.idea: Discrete prompts transfer across tasks and models (e.g., tune on SST-2, apply to IMDB/CR; share across edge devices).
ideas.rationale: Insight that a prompt tuned on one classification task can seed a related one; minor value for Memoria as a way to bootstrap a new claim/aspect classifier prompt from an existing one, but lossy and low priority.

ideas.idea: Cloud-device collaboration keeps private labels/data on-device and only the prompt is tuned locally to avoid uploading sensitive data.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is already fully local with no cloud LM in the loop; the privacy/data-residency problem this solves does not exist, and the architecture (upload queries to a paid API) contradicts Memoria's local-first constraint.

ideas.idea: Salience-map prompt explanation (LIT) showing which tokens drive a prediction; learned prompts are readable but semantically incomprehensible.
ideas.rationale: The explanation/salience tooling idea aligns with Memoria's read-only inspector and faithfulness goals (surface what drove a classification); but the 'effective yet meaningless prompt' finding is rejected on the same interpretability grounds as RLPrompt.

ideas.idea: Few-shot black-box prompting can match or beat full fine-tuning while avoiding overfitting on small data.
ideas.rationale: Supports Memoria's preference for prompting small models over fine-tuning at single-PI data scale, where fine-tuning would overfit; a directional confirmation, not a technique to implement.

file: Wei et al. - 2022 - Why do pretrained language models help in downstream tasks An analysis of head and prompt tuning.md
title: Why Do Pretrained Language Models Help in Downstream Tasks? An Analysis of Head and Prompt Tuning
one_line: A theoretical analysis proving that when text is generated by an (optionally memory-augmented) HMM, downstream labels (functions of latent-variable posteriors) can be recovered by a linear/attention head on a frozen pretrained LM, and that soft prompt tuning relaxes the non-degeneracy conditions needed for recovery.

ideas.idea: Soft prompt tuning relaxes non-degeneracy conditions by conditioning the latent variables so that task-irrelevant information is removed from the model output, making task-essential information easier to recover with a linear head.
ideas.rationale: Memoria treats LLMs as black boxes accessed via MCP and prefers prompt-engineering/instructions over gradient-based prompt tuning of frozen weights; there is no training loop, no embedding-vector optimization, and qwen2.5:7b is used off-the-shelf. The theoretical recovery guarantees on HMM-generated synthetic data give no buildable mechanism for a deterministic local ingest pipeline.

ideas.idea: Memory-augmented HMM that disentangles fast-changing 'syntax' (hidden state) from a persistent 'memory' (semantics), and an attention head that attends to low-entropy positions where the posterior concentrates on informative states.
ideas.rationale: This is a generative-model abstraction for proving recovery theorems, not an extraction technique. It does not translate into Memoria's claim-extraction or relation-typing engines; the syntax/semantics disentanglement insight is too abstract to act on at single-researcher scale.

ideas.idea: Treating the downstream task as recovering a function of the posterior distribution over latent variables in a generative model of text.
ideas.rationale: Pure learning-theory framing with no operational payoff for a markdown-vault knowledge system; offers no metric, prompt, schema, or pipeline step Memoria could adopt.

file: Wei et al. - 2023 - Chain-of-thought prompting elicits reasoning in large language models.md
title: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models
one_line: Shows that prompting an LLM with a few exemplars that include intermediate natural-language reasoning steps (chains of thought) substantially improves performance on multi-step arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic reasoning, as an emergent ability of large models.

ideas.idea: Eliciting a step-by-step reasoning chain via few-shot exemplars before the final answer, allocating more intermediate computation to harder problems.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's 'agents judge' tasks (claim classification, relation typing, contradiction detection, discovery relevance scoring) are multi-step judgments where intermediate reasoning improves faithfulness. Borrow CoT prompting inside agent profiles, but note the paper finds CoT is emergent only at ~100B scale — with qwen2.5:7b/qwen3:14b the gains are smaller and chains can be 'fluent but illogical', so use it selectively where the judgment is genuinely multi-hop.

ideas.idea: Chain of thought provides an interpretable window into how the model reached an answer, enabling debugging of where reasoning went wrong.
ideas.rationale: Directly serves Memoria's faithfulness-over-flash and structural-review-gate ethos: capturing the reasoning trace lets the PI inspect why an engine proposed a relation or flagged a contradiction. Store/surface the rationale in the inspector, but treat it as a debugging aid, not ground truth (the paper warns chains are not guaranteed factual).

ideas.idea: Ablations showing the benefit is from the sequential natural-language reasoning itself, not merely extra compute (dots) or post-hoc rationalization (reasoning-after-answer performs like baseline).
ideas.rationale: Tells Memoria to require reasoning to precede the verdict in judge prompts (rationale-then-decision ordering) rather than asking the model to justify a decision after the fact — a cheap, concrete prompt-design rule.

ideas.idea: Reasoning chains are not guaranteed to be factually correct and can reach correct answers via wrong reasoning (and vice versa).
ideas.rationale: Not a technique to build, but a caution Memoria already encodes via uncertainty flags and the PI-approval gate; reinforces that engine rationales must not be trusted as claims without verification.

file: Wei et al. - 2024 - PubTator 3.0 an AI-powered literature resource for unlocking biomedical knowledge.md
title: PubTator 3.0: an AI-powered literature resource for unlocking biomedical knowledge
one_line: A weekly-updated literature resource that runs deterministic NER, entity normalization, and typed relation extraction over 36M abstracts + 6M full texts, then exposes precomputed entity/relation indices for semantic and relation search and as a RAG grounding source that sharply reduces LLM citation hallucination.

ideas.idea: Offline NLP pipeline (NER -> identifier normalization/linking -> typed relation extraction) run as a scheduled batch job, with results precomputed and indexed for search rather than computed at query time.
ideas.rationale: This is the deterministic-ingest, engines-write architecture Memoria already espouses: parse locally, extract claims/aspects offline, store typed relations, index for retrieval. PubTator validates the separation of a deterministic extraction pipeline from the interactive surface, and the nightly/weekly cadence mirrors Memoria's proactive discovery loop.

ideas.idea: Entity normalization/linking to canonical identifiers (e.g. mapping 'paraoxonase 1' and 'PON1' to one ID), which improves recall by collapsing synonyms.
ideas.rationale: Maps directly onto Memoria's record-linkage entity dedup and citekey backbone. Adopt the normalize-to-canonical-ID step as the join key for cross-note entity matching; the synonym-collapse insight strengthens the pre-file similarity gate and dedup. Single-researcher scale makes a local lexicon-plus-vector normalizer (TaggerOne-style TF-IDF self-mapping) feasible without the NCBI infrastructure.

ideas.idea: Precomputed typed relations between entities (12 relation types over entity-pair types) enabling 'relation search' that answers questions like 'what chemicals reduce expression of JAK1?' directly.
ideas.rationale: Equivalent to Memoria's typed-relations and argument-graph layer. Borrow the design of a fixed, curated relation-type vocabulary with directionality (e.g. negative_correlate) and prioritizing results by relation presence > co-occurrence-in-sentence > distant co-occurrence — a concrete ranking signal for Memoria's retrieval/triage.

ideas.idea: Section-aware and relation-strength relevance ranking (title matches and articles with identifiable relations between query entities ranked highest) plus facet filters by section/journal/type.
ideas.rationale: A cheap, deterministic ranking heuristic for Memoria's discovery relevance scoring and projection surfaces: weight matches by document section and by whether a typed relation (not just co-occurrence) connects the query terms. Fits learning-to-rank triage as features.

ideas.idea: Tool-augmented RAG: give an LLM function-calling access to the precomputed entity/relation/search APIs so it decomposes a question into sub-queries, resolves canonical IDs, fetches evidence, and cites verifiable references — dramatically reducing fabricated citations.
ideas.rationale: This is exactly Memoria's MCP-only agent model: agents call read-only tools over the curated index instead of free-generating. Adopt the pattern of grounding agent answers in the precomputed claim/relation store with mandatory citekey citations, which operationalizes faithfulness-over-flash and the no-hallucinated-references requirement. The decompose-into-sub-questions step also aligns with the RIR survey's query-decomposition.

ideas.idea: Data-centric unification of fragmented, separately-annotated training datasets into one consolidated corpus to train a single multi-type extractor (AIONER's flexible tagging scheme, BioREx's reconciliation) that generalizes better.
ideas.rationale: Insight, not infra: Memoria's claim/aspect/relation extractors benefit from consolidating heterogeneous schemas into one tagging scheme so a single local model handles multiple entity/relation types, improving generalization at small scale. Borrow the principle, not the biomedical models.

file: Wei et al. - 2026 - A survey of reasoning-intensive retrieval progress and challenges.md
title: A Survey of Reasoning-Intensive Retrieval: Progress and Challenges
one_line: A taxonomy survey of reasoning-intensive retrieval (RIR) — retrieval where relevance depends on latent inferential links rather than semantic similarity — organizing benchmarks by domain/reasoning-type and methods by where reasoning is injected (pre-retrieval query/index augmentation, retriever training, reranking, iterative loops).

ideas.idea: Index-side augmentation with textual surrogates: pre-enrich each document offline with synthetic descriptions / hypothetical retrieval scenarios (SPIKE, EnrichIndex) that anticipate how users might seek it, shifting LLM computation offline to cut online latency and cost.
ideas.rationale: Perfect fit for Memoria's deterministic offline ingest: during structural review, an engine can attach 'hypothetical questions this note/claim answers' as searchable metadata, improving the pre-file similarity gate and discovery recall without per-query LLM cost. EnrichIndex's offline-cost-shifting argument directly supports the local/cheap-model constraint.

ideas.idea: Query decomposition and rewriting/expansion: break a complex query into logically-connected sub-queries (with AND/OR/NOT operators) or reformulate it with a reasoning trace before retrieval.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's nightly discovery loop and retriever/scout profile can borrow LLM-light query decomposition so multi-hop research questions retrieve evidence linked only by inference. Use the logical-operator decomposition (Faltings et al.) deterministically; the RL-trained rewriters are out of scope for a no-training local setup.

ideas.idea: Reasoning-enhanced reranking via prompt-tuning (JudgeRank decomposes reranking into query-analysis + document-analysis stages; InsertRank injects the BM25 score into the prompt).
ideas.rationale: Directly buildable as a reranker stage on top of Memoria's cheap first-stage retriever: a qwen judge reranks candidates by a staged analysis prompt, with the lexical (BM25) score passed in as a feature. Aligns with learning-to-rank triage and 'agents judge'. Avoid the SFT/distillation/RL variants (no training pipeline).

ideas.idea: Five-type reasoning taxonomy for relevance (deductive, analogical, causal, analytical, numerical) with inference-chain definitions.
ideas.rationale: Useful vocabulary for Memoria's typed relations and argument graph: classifying WHY a piece of evidence is relevant (e.g. analogical = shares solution strategy, causal = root-cause-to-effect) gives a principled relation/warrant typology and could become a facet in the contradictions/discovery dashboards.

ideas.idea: Iterative retrieval that alternates retrieve<->reason with explicit actions (SMR's REFINE/RERANK/STOP state machine; turn-level rewards), and a warning that naively chaining reasoning modules amplifies 'overthinking' and drifting traces.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's discovery loop can adopt a bounded refine/stop control structure for multi-pass evidence gathering, but the survey's overthinking warning argues for hard iteration caps and a deterministic STOP gate — important to keep local-model cost and faithfulness in check.

ideas.idea: Explicit future direction: RIR aligns with long-term memory systems and deep-research assistants where a reasoning retriever leverages a scientist's historical interests and prior publications for personalized, context-aware evidence.
ideas.rationale: This is essentially Memoria's thesis stated by the authors — a single-PI reasoning retriever grounded in the PI's own corpus and interest profile. Confirms the retriever/scout-profile and discovery-relevance-scoring direction and motivates conditioning retrieval on a PI interest model.

ideas.idea: Hybrid benchmark construction (scalable LLM synthesis + targeted expert/human checks) to balance scale and reliability, and a call for metrics beyond nDCG/Recall that capture efficiency and fine-grained relevance.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's evaluation/benchmark plane can borrow the hybrid-construction principle: auto-generate candidate eval cases with a local model but gate them through the PI/structural review. The efficiency+fine-grained-relevance metric critique informs how Memoria scores its own retrieval quality.

ideas.idea: Latent-reasoning and multi-vector representations (LaSER, AdaQR, ColBERT-style late interaction) that internalize reasoning into the embedding space for low online latency.
ideas.rationale: These require training/fine-tuning embedding backbones, which Memoria avoids (no training loop, off-the-shelf local models). Multi-vector late interaction is also heavier than single-researcher scale warrants; a single-vector dense + BM25 hybrid suffices.

file: Weisz et al. - 2024 - Design principles for generative AI applications.md
title: Design Principles for Generative AI Applications
one_line: Six validated UX design principles for generative-AI applications — Design Responsibly, for Mental Models, for Appropriate Trust & Reliance, for Generative Variability, for Co-Creation, and for Imperfection — each with concrete strategies, supporting two user goals (optimization and exploration).

ideas.idea: Design for Appropriate Trust & Reliance: calibrate trust with capability/limitation explanations, provide rationales by surfacing source materials for each output, and use deliberate friction at key decision points to prevent overreliance.
ideas.rationale: Maps onto Memoria's structural-review gate and 'PI approves' model: the approval surface should show each engine output with its source citekeys/rationale and inject friction (an explicit approve step) before notes are written. 'Provide rationales by identifying source materials' is exactly the citekey-grounded provenance Memoria requires.

ideas.idea: Design for Imperfection: make uncertainty visible, evaluate outputs with domain-specific metrics, and offer ways to fix/regenerate/replace outputs.
ideas.rationale: Directly supports Memoria's atomic-claim uncertainty flags and contradictions dashboard. Surface extraction/relation confidence in the gate UI and give the PI edit/regenerate affordances on proposed notes. The distinction between model flaws (a) and intent-misalignment (b) reinforces that uncertainty surfacing is permanent, not a stopgap.

ideas.idea: Design for Co-Creation: help the user craft effective specifications, expose use-case-relevant controls (and generic ones like seed/number-of-outputs), and support co-editing of generated outputs by both user and system.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's structured-capture forms and projected canvas are co-creation surfaces; borrow 'help craft effective specifications' (scaffolded capture forms / query templates) and co-editing of engine-proposed notes. Generic controls like random seed are low-value for Memoria's deterministic-ingest goal, so adopt only the use-case controls.

ideas.idea: Two-tier principle/strategy structure and the optimization-vs-exploration user-goal distinction (single best artifact vs. exploring a possibility space).
ideas.rationale: Useful framing for Memoria surfaces: focused retrieval/triage (optimization) vs. the nightly discovery loop and projected canvas (exploration) serve different PI goals and should be designed/evaluated differently. A lightweight lens, not a buildable feature.

ideas.idea: Design for Mental Models, incl. 'Teach the AI system about the user' — capture the user's expectations/behaviors/preferences to improve interactions (Mutual Theory of Mind).
ideas.rationale: Supports a PI interest/preference model feeding discovery-relevance scoring and the retriever/scout profile (echoing the RIR survey's personalization direction). Borrow the capture-preferences idea; keep it single-PI and local, not a multi-user personalization system.

ideas.idea: Design for Generative Variability: leverage multiple outputs, visualize the user's journey, enable curation/annotation, draw attention to differences across outputs.
ideas.rationale: Partially relevant: Memoria values deterministic ingest, so embracing variability in the write path is the wrong fit, but generating multiple candidate extractions/relations for the PI to curate at the gate, and drawing attention to differences, fits the 'engines propose, PI curates' flow. Borrow the curation/diff-surfacing strategies, reject variability-as-a-feature in core ingest.

ideas.idea: Design Responsibly via human-centered process, value-tension resolution, and testing/monitoring for user harms (bias, toxicity, misinformation).
ideas.rationale: Largely an organizational/process principle aimed at multi-stakeholder enterprise product teams; for a single-PI local tool the value-tension and labeler-harm machinery is over-scoped. The narrower 'minimize misinformation' goal is already covered by Memoria's faithfulness and contradiction tracking.

file: Gu et al. - 2022 - PPT pre-trained prompt tuning for few-shot learning.md
title: PPT: Pre-trained Prompt Tuning for Few-shot Learning
one_line: A method that pre-trains soft prompt embeddings on self-supervised tasks (NSP, next-sentence-selection) to give parameter-efficient prompt tuning a good initialization, making it match full fine-tuning on 11B PLMs in few-shot settings.

ideas.idea: Pre-train soft prompt embeddings (continuous, ~410K params) on self-supervised tasks so few-shot prompt tuning of a frozen 11B PLM matches full fine-tuning.
ideas.rationale: Memoria uses frozen local models (qwen2.5:7b) via Ollama/MCP with no gradient access or trainable soft-prompt layer; soft-prompt pre-training requires a training pipeline Memoria deliberately does not have. Off-architecture.

ideas.idea: Unify heterogeneous classification tasks into a single multiple-choice (option-based) format so one model/prompt covers sentence-pair, single-sentence, and multi-choice tasks.
ideas.rationale: The insight that diverse classification tasks (claim-sentence classification, MASSW aspect tagging, contradiction detection) can be cast into one unified verbalizer/option schema is useful for designing consistent local-LLM prompts and reducing prompt sprawl across Memoria's many extraction engines. Adapt the task-unification idea, not the soft-prompt training.

ideas.idea: Verbalizer choice (mapping labels to concrete words) has a large impact on few-shot accuracy; common words that explain the label work best.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's local-model classification prompts (claim type, uncertainty flag, aspect labels) are few-shot/zero-shot; choosing natural-language label words carefully is a cheap, directly transferable lever for prompt quality on qwen2.5.

file: Gu et al. - 2023 - Watermarking pre-trained language models with backdooring.md
title: Watermarking Pre-trained Language Models with Backdooring
one_line: Embeds black-box ownership watermarks into a PLM's word-embedding layer via backdoor triggers (rare words or stealthy common-word combinations) that survive multi-task fine-tuning, for IP protection.

ideas.idea: Embed backdoor triggers in the word-embedding layer so they survive downstream fine-tuning, enabling black-box ownership verification of a released model.
ideas.rationale: Memoria neither trains nor releases models; it consumes frozen off-the-shelf local models. IP-watermarking of model weights has no role in a single-researcher knowledge vault.

ideas.idea: Use combinations of common words (e.g. 'green idea nose') as stealthy triggers because their joint occurrence is vanishingly rare in natural text, evading frequency-based detection.
ideas.rationale: This is an adversarial/security construct for model supply chains; Memoria has no adversary model and no trained-model asset to protect. Off-domain.

ideas.idea: A detection method: feed each vocab word into a suspect model and flag words that shift the predicted-label probability mass disproportionately (frequency-vs-probability outliers).
ideas.rationale: Prompt-injection / poisoned-input defense for served models is outside Memoria's threat surface (MCP-only sandbox, single PI, local trusted corpus). Not actionable here.

file: Guo et al. - 2022 - Efficient (soft) Q-learning for text generation with limited good data.md
title: Efficient (Soft) Q-Learning for Text Generation with Limited Good Data
one_line: Reformulates text generation as soft Q-learning with path-consistency learning, combining on-/off-policy updates and using end rewards to handle sparse-reward, large-action-space RL for tasks like learning from noisy data, adversarial attacks, and prompt generation.

ideas.idea: Train a generator with arbitrary task-metric rewards (entailment score, topic accuracy) instead of MLE, so the model can 'outperform' noisy/negative training data.
ideas.rationale: Memoria does not train or fine-tune generators; it orchestrates frozen local models behind an MCP sandbox. RL-from-reward over a generator is off-architecture and far above single-researcher cost/complexity budget.

ideas.idea: RL-learned prompt generator steers a frozen LM for controllable (topic) generation, much faster at inference than steered-decoding methods like PPLM/GeDi.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's prompts are human-authored/compiled profiles, not RL-optimized; it has no differentiable-pipeline training loop and prefers deterministic, auditable prompting over learned controllers.

ideas.idea: Use a learned model (entailment classifier, GPT-2 perplexity) as part of a composite reward/scoring signal that mixes task quality and language quality.
ideas.rationale: The compositional-scoring insight (combine an entailment/faithfulness model score with a fluency/quality score and weight them) maps loosely onto Memoria's discovery relevance scoring and learning-to-rank triage, where multiple signals are blended into one ranking score. Borrow the multi-signal scoring framing only, not the RL.

file: Guo et al. - 2023 - From images to textual prompts zero-shot VQA with frozen large language models.md
title: From Images to Textual Prompts: Zero-shot VQA with Frozen Large Language Models
one_line: Img2LLM is a plug-and-play module that turns an image into synthetic question-answer exemplars and question-relevant captions used as in-context prompts, letting frozen off-the-shelf LLMs do zero-shot VQA without any end-to-end training.

ideas.idea: Modular design over a frozen off-the-shelf LLM: keep the LLM frozen and bridge it with lightweight pre/post modules, so the LLM can be swapped/upgraded with zero retraining cost.
ideas.rationale: This is exactly Memoria's architecture stance: frozen local models behind deterministic engines, models swappable (qwen2.5:7b default, qwen3:14b fallback) without touching the pipeline. The paper is a clean validation of the 'frozen-LLM + glue modules' bet over costly end-to-end coupling.

ideas.idea: Generate synthetic exemplar QA pairs from the current item itself to demonstrate the task in-context, avoiding reliance on a curated few-shot exemplar bank.
ideas.rationale: For Memoria's extraction prompts (atomic-claim, aspect, classification) on a small local model, generating self-grounded in-context exemplars from the current note/paper could lift zero-shot quality without maintaining a labeled exemplar store. Adapt the self-exemplar trick to text extraction.

ideas.idea: Question-relevant caption generation: use GradCAM/cross-attention to localize the part of the source relevant to the query, generate descriptions from only that part, then filter low-similarity candidates by a matching score.
ideas.rationale: The query-conditioned, relevance-localized + similarity-filtered context-selection pattern is the core of good RAG context assembly. Memoria's retriever/scout and pre-file similarity gate can borrow 'select query-relevant spans, then filter by a similarity threshold before feeding the model' to cut noise (their answer-noise-rate metric).

ideas.idea: Prompt ordering matters: presenting all captions then all QA pairs (CCC-QAQAQA) beats interleaving (CQA-CQA), and answer-frequency selection of exemplars improves results.
ideas.rationale: Concrete, free prompt-engineering levers for Memoria's local-model prompts: group context before exemplars, and select exemplars by salience/frequency. Cheap to apply, no training.

ideas.idea: Answer Hit Rate / Answer Noise Rate as metrics for the quality of assembled context (does it contain the needed answer; how diluted is it).
ideas.rationale: Memoria's evaluation/benchmarks plane could adopt context-quality metrics like 'does the retrieved context contain the gold claim' and 'signal-to-noise of the assembled prompt' to tune the pre-file similarity gate and retriever, rather than only end-task accuracy.

file: Hambardzumyan et al. - 2021 - WARP word-level adversarial ReProgramming.md
title: WARP: Word-level Adversarial ReProgramming
one_line: WARP learns a tiny set of task-specific prompt and verbalizer embeddings (under 25K params) inserted around the input of a frozen masked LM, matching far larger fine-tuned models on GLUE and beating GPT-3 on two few-shot SuperGLUE tasks.

ideas.idea: Cast classification as masked-LM cloze prediction over verbalizer tokens (predict the class word at a [MASK] position) rather than adding a task head.
ideas.rationale: The cloze/verbalizer reformulation generalizes to prompting frozen generative local models for Memoria's classification tasks (claim-sentence type, uncertainty flag): ask the model to emit a constrained label word. Adapt the framing; skip the trainable embeddings.

ideas.idea: Initialize learnable prompts with manually designed human-readable prompts/verbalizers, which especially helps in the low-data (few-shot) regime.
ideas.rationale: Confirms that careful hand-written prompt + label wording is the dominant lever in low-data settings, which is precisely Memoria's regime (no training data, frozen small model). Reinforces investing in good static prompt/verbalizer wording over learned tuning.

ideas.idea: Learn task-specific soft prompt+verbalizer embeddings via gradient descent on a frozen LM, storing only M + NE(C+K) params to serve many tasks cheaply.
ideas.rationale: Requires gradient access and an embedding-training loop; Memoria's MCP-only sandbox gives agents no code execution and uses frozen models with no fine-tuning. The parameter-efficiency-at-serving-scale motivation is also a multi-task-serving concern, not single-PI.

ideas.idea: Learned continuous prompt embeddings are largely uninterpretable (nearest vocab tokens are gibberish), unlike the more interpretable verbalizer tokens.
ideas.rationale: Uninterpretable continuous prompts directly conflict with Memoria's faithfulness/auditability requirement (engines write, agents judge, PI approves). A cautionary point against soft-prompt approaches, but offers nothing to build.

file: Kang et al. - 2023 - Exploiting programmatic behavior of LLMs dual-use through standard security attacks.md
title: Exploiting Programmatic Behavior of LLMs: Dual-Use Through Standard Security Attacks
one_line: Shows that instruction-following LLMs behave like programs (string concat, variable assignment, branching), so classic security attacks (obfuscation, payload-splitting/code-injection, virtualization) reliably bypass content filters, arguing LLM safety should adopt traditional computer-security defenses (sandboxing, secure enclaves, threat models).

ideas.idea: LLMs behave like programs with composable 'gadgets' (string concatenation, variable assignment, sequential composition, branching) that can be chained — input filtering alone cannot stop attacks because behavior is undecidable without running the model.
ideas.rationale: Directly justifies Memoria's MCP-only agent sandbox (D40/D41, ADR-32/46): you cannot vet agent intent by inspecting prompts/inputs, so the only sound control is capability denial. Reinforces 'engines write, agents judge, PI approves' — agents get no file/terminal/code-execution regardless of how benign their instructions look.

ideas.idea: Defense should aim for unconditional defenses under a stated threat model (sandboxing, memory protection, secure enclaves) rather than pattern-matching filters.
ideas.rationale: Aligns with Memoria's hard-deny gate plugin (PR #364) and compiled profiles: enforce capability boundaries at the runtime/sandbox layer, not via prompt-level content filters that an adversarial or jailbroken model can evade. The structural review gate is a deterministic chokepoint analogous to a sandbox boundary.

ideas.idea: Payload-splitting / virtualization attacks let unnatural prompts reassemble into harmful instructions inside the model.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is single-PI and not an abuse-prevention service for adversarial third-party users; the offensive-content generation threat model (scams, hate speech at scale, economic abuse) does not match a private research vault. Useful only as motivation, not as a feature.

ideas.idea: Economic-cost analysis of malicious generation ($0.006-0.016 per email).
ideas.rationale: Off-domain; no bearing on a local single-user research system using cheap/open models for ingest and triage.

file: Karpas et al. - 2022 - MRKL systems a modular, neuro-symbolic architecture that combines large language models, external k.md
title: MRKL Systems: A Modular, Neuro-Symbolic Architecture Combining LLMs, External Knowledge and Discrete Reasoning
one_line: Proposes a router-plus-experts neuro-symbolic architecture where a lightweight router dispatches each natural-language input to specialized neural or symbolic modules (calculators, DB/API calls), giving up-to-date knowledge, interpretable rationales, and cheap robust extensibility, with the LLM's job reduced to reliably extracting arguments for symbolic tools.

ideas.idea: Router + extendable set of 'expert' modules (neural and symbolic), with the general LLM as a safe fallback when no expert matches.
ideas.rationale: This is essentially the Hermes/MCP design: a dispatcher routing tasks to deterministic engines (ingest parser, dedup, keyphrase, BERTopic MCP) vs. LLM agents. Validates Memoria's 'engines do the deterministic work, LLM does language' split and the profile/skill-governance model. The 'safe fallback to LLM' pattern fits a discovery loop where unmatched items go to a general scout profile.

ideas.idea: Limit the LLM's role to extracting structured arguments for a symbolic module, not performing the computation itself ('crossing the neuro-symbolic chasm').
ideas.rationale: Core to Memoria's faithfulness-over-flash and deterministic-ingest constraints: use the local model only to extract atomic claims/aspects/relations as structured fields, then let deterministic code (record-linkage, supersession logic, contradiction checks) do the reasoning. Keeps errors auditable and bounded.

ideas.idea: Robust extensibility: each expert trained/built independently so new capabilities can be added cheaply without retraining/regressing others; only the lightweight router changes.
ideas.rationale: Matches compiled profiles + skill governance — add a new MCP engine or skill without re-validating the whole system. Strong argument for keeping engines modular and the routing layer thin, which suits single-PI scale and avoids monolithic fine-tuned models (model explosion).

ideas.idea: Interpretability as a side benefit of routing: invoking a named module yields a rationale ('1+1=2 because the calculator said so').
ideas.rationale: Memoria's read-only inspector / static HTML admin reports can record which engine produced each field, giving provenance for every claim/relation in the structural review gate. Provenance-by-routing is cheaper than post-hoc explanation.

ideas.idea: Data-augmentation from a structured template space to make a small model extract arguments at near-perfect, production-grade reliability (and generalize across phrasings/formats/digit-counts).
ideas.rationale: Directly applicable to making qwen2.5:7b reliable at the bounded extraction tasks Memoria needs (claim/aspect/relation field extraction). Template-driven synthetic eval/tuning is a cheap, local-friendly way to harden extraction without large models — fits the RTX 4060 Ti / cheap-local-model constraint.

ideas.idea: Up-to-date and proprietary knowledge via external API/DB calls instead of relying on parametric LM memory.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's knowledge lives in the Obsidian vault + Zotero, not in the model. The MRKL insight (don't trust the LM's stale parametric knowledge; route to the authoritative store) reinforces grounding retrieval in the vault and citekey backbone rather than the model's recall.

file: Karusala et al. - 2021 - “That courage to encourage” participation and aspirations in chat-based peer support for youth livi.md
title: "That courage to encourage": Participation and Aspirations in Chat-based Peer Support for Youth Living with HIV
one_line: Qualitative CHI study of a six-month facilitated WhatsApp peer-support pilot for youth living with HIV in Nairobi, detailing facilitator moderation work, intermittent participation patterns, negotiated privacy norms, and the role of aspirations in health behavior.

ideas.idea: Facilitator must 'close the loop' on stale threads in a busy multi-thread chat by re-surfacing/answering old questions, aided by features like starring messages (a reply queue) and the reply feature to revisit threads many messages later.
ideas.rationale: A loose analogy for Memoria's contradictions dashboard and claim-supersession queue: surface open/unresolved items so nothing is dropped, and link a resolution back to the originating claim/thread. The 'starred queue of items needing a reply' maps to an open-questions/needs-review surface in a space.

ideas.idea: Auto-summarize/curate a backlog of messages for users who return after a gap, tagging discourse types (questions, resources, advice) so they can jump to the salient items.
ideas.rationale: Maps to Memoria's nightly proactive discovery digest and projection engine: when the PI returns, present a curated, type-tagged summary (new claims, contradictions, resources) rather than a raw firehose. Discourse-type tagging is conceptually adjacent to claim-sentence classification.

ideas.idea: Negotiate privacy/safety norms as an explicit, revisitable social contract; norms drift over time and must be periodically reinforced.
ideas.rationale: Memoria is single-PI and local; there is no multi-user privacy negotiation, phone-sharing, or stigma threat model. The governance analogue (ADRs as revisitable decisions) already exists and is not informed by this group-dynamics finding.

ideas.idea: Aspirations vs. needs framing; sentiment analysis to flag conversations/patients needing follow-up.
ideas.rationale: Off-domain (health-intervention design, group emotional support). No buildable mechanism for a personal research-knowledge vault; sentiment-triage of human distress is not a Memoria function.

file: Khashabi et al. - 2022 - Prompt waywardness the curious case of discretized interpretation of continuous prompts.md
title: Prompt Waywardness: The Curious Case of Discretized Interpretation of Continuous Prompts
one_line: Shows that for any task one can find a continuous (soft) prompt that solves it within ~2% of optimal while projecting to arbitrary, even contradictory, discrete text — so nearest-neighbor textual 'interpretations' of soft prompts are not faithful, with implications for interpretability and concealed adversarial behavior.

ideas.idea: Discrete (textual) interpretations of continuous prompts are not faithful — a soft prompt's nearest-neighbor text can be totally unrelated to what it actually does.
ideas.rationale: Memoria does not use soft/continuous prompt tuning; it uses discrete prompts with cheap local models and deterministic engines. The faithfulness failure mode here is specific to learned continuous prompts and doesn't arise in Memoria's pipeline.

ideas.idea: A benign-looking discrete prompt projection can mask malicious behavior in the underlying continuous prompt ('false sense of security').
ideas.rationale: Generalizes to a useful caution for the structural review gate: do not trust a stated description/label of a model artifact (or an agent's self-described intent) as evidence of behavior — judge by observed output on actual data. Reinforces output-side governance over input-side trust, consistent with the Kang paper's lesson.

ideas.idea: Continuous prompt tuning does not need task-specific initialization (random common-word init works) because effective prompts exist near arbitrary word embeddings.
ideas.rationale: An implementation detail of soft-prompt training; Memoria fine-tunes nothing of this kind and uses off-the-shelf local models, so it is inapplicable.

ideas.idea: Use a joint objective trading task utility against a human-readability/distance term to search for interpretable prompts (shown here to be degenerate).
ideas.rationale: Memoria has no need to auto-discover prompts via gradient search; prompts are authored and version-controlled. The paper's own finding is that this approach is unreliable, so there is nothing to adopt.

file: Kirchenbauer et al. - 2024 - A watermark for large language models.md
title: A Watermark for Large Language Models
one_line: Introduces a statistical watermark for LLM text by softly promoting a pseudo-randomly seeded 'green list' of tokens at each step, detectable with an interpretable z-test without access to the model or API, with low perplexity impact (file not present/extractable in assigned set; assessed from established method).

ideas.idea: Embed a detectable statistical signal in generated text via seeded green/red token partitioning, detectable post-hoc without the model.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's concern is faithfulness and provenance of ingested human-authored papers, not proving that a given text was machine-generated. Watermarking its own LLM outputs adds no value for a single-PI vault and degrades extraction text quality.

ideas.idea: Interpretable z-test detection with tunable false-positive rate over a span of tokens.
ideas.rationale: The general pattern — a cheap, interpretable statistical test with a controllable false-positive budget — is a reasonable model for Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and dedup thresholds (set explicit, defensible false-positive rates rather than opaque cutoffs). Borrowed as a statistical-rigor stance, not the watermark itself.

file: Jin et al. - 2022 - A good prompt is worth millions of parameters low-resource prompt-based learning for vision-languag.md
title: A Good Prompt Is Worth Millions of Parameters: Low-resource Prompt-based Learning for Vision-Language Models
one_line: FewVLM is a moderate-sized seq2seq vision-language model that, with hand-crafted prompts and MaskedLM/PrefixLM pretraining, matches few-shot learners hundreds of times larger on VQA and captioning.

ideas.idea: A smaller, locally-deployable model with task-appropriate prompting can match a 246x larger model on few-shot tasks, since prompts matter most in zero-shot and matching the pretraining objective format to the downstream task helps fast adaptation.
ideas.rationale: Reinforces Memoria's local + cheap/open model stance (qwen2.5:7b): a well-prompted small model is competitive, so the design choice to keep extraction/classification on a small local model is sound. Only a general validation, not a buildable mechanism.

ideas.idea: Prompts significantly affect zero-shot but only marginally affect few-shot performance once there is training data; with enough examples even noisy/random prompts learn as fast as hand-crafted ones.
ideas.rationale: Tells Memoria's claim-extraction/classification prompt engineering to invest in prompt wording only where it runs zero/near-zero-shot (cold-start), and not over-tune prompts once a handful of PI-labeled examples exist. Insight only.

ideas.idea: Sentinel-token target format mimicking MaskedLM to constrain output format and reduce variance.
ideas.rationale: Architecture-specific to T5/VL-T5 fine-tuning; Memoria uses off-the-shelf instruction models via MCP and structured-output schemas, not sentinel-token decoding. Vision-language domain is also off-domain for a text/paper system.

file: Jin et al. - 2022 - Plot writing from pre-trained language models.md
title: Plot Writing From Pre-Trained Language Models (ScratchPlot)
one_line: ScratchPlot generates stories with off-the-shelf PLMs by first prompting the model to compose a structured content plan (location/characters/genre/theme) then generating and ranking story+ending pairs, beating fine-tuned baselines without any training.

ideas.idea: Decompose generation into explicit, sequentially-dependent attribute slots (a content plan) sampled from the same off-the-shelf model, rather than one end-to-end generation, to improve coherence and content.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's structured capture forms and MASSW aspect extraction already factor a paper into typed slots; this validates prompting the same small local model slot-by-slot with dependency ordering instead of one big extraction call. Adapt the staged-prompt pattern, not the story domain.

ideas.idea: Generate-and-rank: sample multiple candidates and select with an independent scorer; conditional perplexity (PPL of ending given body) is a more reliable selector than BERT next-sentence-prediction.
ideas.rationale: Useful for Memoria's engines-propose / judge-rank pipeline: when an engine emits multiple candidate claims/summaries, a cheap PPL-style coherence score is a deterministic local re-ranker before the structural gate. NSP-is-unreliable warning saves a dead end.

ideas.idea: Self-debiasing decoding: reward continuations with high prob under one task description and low under a contrasting one, to force distinct outputs.
ideas.rationale: A creative-diversity trick for fiction; Memoria values faithfulness over diversity/novelty, so deliberately pushing generations away from likely tokens is counter to its claim-fidelity goals.

ideas.idea: Heuristic post-processing/filtering of model output (strip trailing punctuation, drop continuations echoing the prompt, require presence of required slot values).
ideas.rationale: Directly applicable to deterministic-ingest discipline: cheap post-filters that reject extractions which merely echo the prompt or omit required fields fit the engines-write / validation layer before notes are gated.

file: Jung et al. - 2022 - Maieutic prompting logically consistent reasoning with recursive explanations.md
title: Maieutic Prompting: Logically Consistent Reasoning with Recursive Explanations
one_line: Maieutic prompting builds a tree of abductive, recursively-validated True/False explanations from an LM, defines belief and consistency relations between them, and runs weighted MAX-SAT to find the globally consistent truth assignment, beating chain-of-thought and rivaling supervised models unsupervised.

ideas.idea: Logical integrity check: a proposition is trustworthy only if the model assigns opposite truth values to it and its negation; flag non-integral propositions as unreliable.
ideas.rationale: A cheap, local, model-agnostic reliability test for Memoria's atomic-claim uncertainty flags: re-prompt the small model on a claim and its negation; disagreement under negation marks a low-confidence claim before it enters the argument graph. Runs on qwen2.5:7b, no training.

ideas.idea: Treat a set of generated propositions plus typed relations (belief/consistency, entail/contradict) as a weighted MAX-SAT problem and solve symbolically for the globally consistent subset.
ideas.rationale: Directly maps to Memoria's argument graph + contradictions dashboard + claim supersession: model claims as nodes, typed relations as weighted clauses, and use a MAX-SAT/constraint solver to surface the maximal consistent claim set and pinpoint which claims to flag as contradicted/superseded. This is the engines-compute, deterministic-judge pattern.

ideas.idea: Use a small NLI verifier (RoBERTa-MNLI) to derive entail/contradict relations between arbitrary proposition pairs, converted to logical implications.
ideas.rationale: Memoria already wants typed relations and a contradictions dashboard; a local NLI model is a deterministic relation-extraction engine for entailment/contradiction edges between claims, feeding the structural review gate. Fits MCP-only sandbox (engine output, not agent file access).

ideas.idea: Abductive generation: prompt the model to rationalize BOTH answers (True and False) rather than generate one discriminative explanation, eliciting latent knowledge it otherwise wouldn't surface.
ideas.rationale: For Memoria's claim assessment, prompting the model to argue both for and against a paper's claim yields warrant candidates for the argument graph and exposes hidden assumptions, improving warrant coverage over single-shot extraction.

ideas.idea: Depth-adaptive decoding: nucleus sampling for breadth at shallow depth, greedy at deeper depth, and stop expanding a branch once a logically-integral node is reached.
ideas.rationale: A compute-budget heuristic for any recursive Memoria reasoning loop (e.g. nightly discovery or contradiction resolution): expand widely up front, prune branches that reach a confident/integral conclusion, cap depth. Keeps local-inference cost bounded.

file: Kandel et al. - 2012 - Enterprise data analysis and visualization an interview study.md
title: Enterprise Data Analysis and Visualization: An Interview Study
one_line: An interview study of 35 enterprise data analysts that models analysis as five tasks (discover, wrangle, profile, model, report) and documents pain points around finding data, entity/record linkage, undocumented assumptions, static reports, and lost provenance.

ideas.idea: Capture metadata at natural annotation points: impose light structure on file-naming/save points so author, rationale, and assumptions are recorded as first-class searchable metadata instead of buried in ad-hoc filenames.
ideas.rationale: Directly supports Memoria's structured capture forms and the structural review gate: intercept the moment a note/claim is written and require typed provenance/assumption fields, exactly the 'intervene at existing annotation points' principle for a single PI's vault.

ideas.idea: Assumptions are treated as overlooked footnotes rather than first-class results, causing conflicting metrics and irreproducible conclusions.
ideas.rationale: Argues Memoria should make uncertainty flags / claim assumptions first-class fields surfaced on dashboards (not fine print), and underpins the contradictions dashboard: divergent conclusions usually trace to undocumented assumptions.

ideas.idea: Record-linkage / fuzzy entity matching across sources (MRN example: match on name+birthdate+gender when IDs missing or inconsistent; reconcile differing code standards via explicit mappings).
ideas.rationale: Validates Memoria's record-linkage entity-dedup component: the same name-variant/identifier-reconciliation problem applies to authors, venues, and citekeys. Borrow the multi-field fuzzy-match-then-verify pattern; Memoria's scale is single-PI not enterprise.

ideas.idea: Static reports with no access to provenance prevent recipients from changing parameters/assumptions to see how conclusions change; desire for dynamic, parametrizable reports.
ideas.rationale: Motivates Memoria's disposable projection engine + projected canvas and read-only inspector over purely static HTML reports, so the PI can re-pivot views rather than trust frozen summaries. Memoria already leans this way; the study confirms the failure mode of static-only.

ideas.idea: Lack of reusable workflows / discarded dead-ends ('no remnants of that'); knowledge as folklore transmitted person-to-person; repeating analysis a year later.
ideas.rationale: For a single PI this is the core motivation for Memoria as durable memory: record negative results / dead-ends and rationale so the PI doesn't re-walk them. Borrow the 'capture dead-ends' insight; the multi-analyst sharing/collaboration framing is out of scope (single-user).

ideas.idea: Three analyst archetypes, IT-staff dependency, distributed warehouses, Hadoop/scale, in-database analytics, collaboration impediments across heterogeneous tools.
ideas.rationale: Enterprise multi-team, multi-tool, big-data-scale concerns explicitly contradict Memoria's single-researcher, local, single-vault constraints; no buildable element here.

file: Kang and Xiong - 2024 - ResearchArena benchmarking large language models' ability to collect and organize information as re.md
title: ResearchArena: Benchmarking LLMs' Ability to Collect and Organize Information as Research Agents
one_line: A benchmark that decomposes literature surveying into information discovery, selection, and organization (mind-map) over a 12M-paper offline corpus, finding that LLM/agent methods underperform simple keyword (title-as-query) retrieval and hierarchical clustering.

ideas.idea: Decompose 'research agent' work into three measurable stages: information discovery (retrieve relevant papers), information selection (rank by influence/impact), information organization (build a hierarchical mind-map).
ideas.rationale: This is essentially Memoria's discovery/triage pipeline made explicit: nightly proactive discovery (stage 1), learning-to-rank triage + discovery relevance scoring (stage 2), BERTopic clustering / spaces (stage 3). Adopt the three-stage decomposition as the evaluation backbone for Memoria's retriever/scout profile.

ideas.idea: Strong finding: LLM/agentic discovery (Self-RAG, STORM, zero-shot query rewriting) consistently UNDERPERFORMS simple keyword retrieval (survey title as query with BM25/BGE); decomposed prompting helps but still trails the title baseline.
ideas.rationale: Direct design guidance: Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and triage should lean on cheap deterministic BM25/dense retrieval as the primary, with LLM query expansion as an optional add-on, not the core. Avoids spending the local model budget on agentic search that loses to a one-line query. Reinforces faithfulness/determinism over flash.

ideas.idea: Influential-vs-non-influential citation classification (Valenzuela et al.) to prioritize impactful references for the selection/ranking step.
ideas.rationale: A concrete feature for Memoria's learning-to-rank triage and discovery relevance scoring: classify citation importance so the PI sees high-impact references first. Computable locally over Zotero/citekey backbone.

ideas.idea: Mind-map / taxonomy as JSON-encoded hierarchical tree; evaluate organization with Heading Soft Recall (Sentence-BERT embeddings), Heading Entity Recall (NER), and Tree (Semantic) Edit Distance with similarity-0.8 free-edits.
ideas.rationale: Memoria's spaces / projected canvas / BERTopic clustering produce hierarchical structure; these are ready-made offline metrics to evaluate whether a generated space-hierarchy matches a known survey taxonomy. Borrow Tree Semantic Distance and Heading Soft/Entity Recall as eval, not user-facing features.

ideas.idea: Hierarchical clustering (Ward's method on dense embeddings) with TF-IDF n-gram node labels beats LLM agents at building the structural hierarchy (lowest tree distance).
ideas.rationale: Confirms Memoria's BERTopic/clustering MCP plus keyphrase tag candidates is the right deterministic engine for organizing the vault into spaces; an LLM should label/judge, not invent the hierarchy. Engines-cluster, agents-judge.

ideas.idea: Offline, reproducible, weekly-updatable corpus built from S2ORC with leakage controls (exclude survey papers and anything published after the target survey from the candidate pool).
ideas.rationale: The temporal-leakage discipline (only consider pre-dated, non-survey sources) maps to Memoria's temporal reasoning / claim supersession: when evaluating discovery or 'what was knowable then', filter the corpus by publication date. Full S2ORC offline mirror is enterprise-scale and not needed for a single PI.

file: Chowdhery et al. - 2022 - PaLM scaling language modeling with pathways.md
title: PaLM: Scaling Language Modeling with Pathways
one_line: A 540B-parameter densely-activated Transformer trained on 6144 TPU v4 chips via the Pathways system, demonstrating continued few-shot scaling gains, discontinuous 'emergent' jumps, and breakthrough chain-of-thought reasoning.

ideas.idea: Massive cloud-scale dense Transformer (540B params, TPU pods) for few-shot capability
ideas.rationale: Directly contradicts Memoria's single-researcher local/cheap-model constraint (qwen2.5:7b default, RTX 4060 Ti); the model itself is irrelevant to a vault running on a workstation.

ideas.idea: Chain-of-thought prompting unlocks multi-step reasoning at scale
ideas.rationale: Memoria's claim extraction, contradiction detection, and structural-review judging are multi-step reasoning tasks; eliciting explicit reasoning chains from the local model can improve faithfulness, but Memoria should treat CoT as scaffolding to be verified by engines, not trusted output (consistent with 'agents judge, engines write').

ideas.idea: Discontinuous/emergent capability jumps appear only past certain scale
ideas.rationale: This is an argument FOR very large models; for Memoria it is a caution that a 7B local model may simply lack a capability, but it offers no buildable mechanism — it argues against Memoria's own constraints.

ideas.idea: Data deduplication via Levenshtein distance over near-duplicate source files before training
ideas.rationale: Mirrors Memoria's pre-file similarity gate and record-linkage entity dedup; near-duplicate detection (edit-distance / hashing) on incoming papers and extracted notes is a cheap, deterministic guard worth reusing at ingest.

ideas.idea: Model Card / datasheet discipline documenting training data, intended use, limitations
ideas.rationale: Memoria's profile-compilation and skill-governance surfaces could adopt a lightweight 'card' per engine/profile (inputs, intended use, known failure modes) as a static admin report, supporting the governance gate.

ideas.idea: Prompt-style strongly influences toxicity/output (model mirrors prompt style more than humans)
ideas.rationale: Relevant to Memoria's compiled profiles: deterministic, neutral prompt templates reduce drift; reinforces keeping prompts engine-controlled rather than free-form agent text.

file: Clive et al. - 2022 - Control prefixes for parameter-efficient text generation.md
title: Control Prefixes: Conditional Input-Dependent Prefix-Tuning for Parameter-Efficient Text Generation
one_line: Extends prefix-tuning with dynamic, attribute-conditioned 'control prefixes' (0.1-3% extra params, frozen LM) that steer generation by discrete labels, achieving SOTA on data-to-text and competitive summarization/simplification.

ideas.idea: Attribute-conditioned dynamic prompts (control prefixes) to steer a frozen LM by a discrete guidance label (domain, length-ratio, category)
ideas.rationale: Memoria's projection/summarization and aspect-extraction engines could condition generation on discrete attributes (paper aspect, target length, MASSW facet) — but via prompt conditioning / control tokens on a local model, since training prefixes for qwen on a single workstation is heavier than the value; borrow the conditioning idea, not the training machinery.

ideas.idea: Parameter-efficient fixed-LM adaptation (train <3% params, keep base frozen, share one base across tasks)
